       Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ
1.       INTRODUCTION
         This document details the process, findings and recommendations of the Environmental and Social
         Impact Assessment undertaken for the Rogun Hydropower Plant in Tajikistan. The ESIA is divided
         into three volumes:
          Volume 1: Environmental and Social Impact Assessment
          Volume 2: Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Annexes
          Volume 3: Environmental and Social Management Plans

1.1.     PROJECT PROPONENT
1.1.1.   This Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) has been prepared for the Project
         Management Group for Energy Facilities Construction under the President of the Republic of
         Tajikistan by WSP UK Limited (hereafter referred to as ‘WSP’) for the Rogun Hydropower Project
         (HPP) (hereafter referred to as ‘the Project’).
1.1.2.   The Government of the Republic of Tajikistan established:
             Rogun Open Joint Stock Company (OJSC) (hereafter referred to as ‘JSC Rogun’) to manage
             construction and operation of the HPP.
          Directorate of Flooding Zone of Rogun HPP (DFZ) for the preparation of the impoundment area,
           and organize the resettlement of project affected persons (PAPs) in coordination with other
           government ministries and agencies; and
          Project Management Group for Energy Facilities Construction under the President of the
           Republic of Tajikistan (PMG) to liaise with potential financial lenders for the Project.

1.2.     INTRODUCTION TO THE PROJECT
1.2.1.   The Project was first conceived in the Soviet Union in the 1950s and 1960s, as part of the regional
         development of the republics that are now independent states in Central Asia. The original purpose
         of the Project was to provide water regulation for irrigation schemes in Tajikistan, Uzbekistan and
         Turkmenistan, as well as a power supply for the Soviet Central Asian republics. Site investigation for
         the Project started in 1967. Figure 1-1 presents a chronology of events in the development of the
         Project.
1.2.2.   Due to economic and political changes in Tajikistan, the current purpose of the Project is to generate
         electricity to overcome critical shortages of electricity throughout the winter months and to meet
         future energy demand in the Republic of Tajikistan, while respecting the water needs of downstream
         riparian countries.
1.2.3.   The Project will also have collateral benefits including:
          Downstream flow regulation potential – the addition of storage capacity provided by the Project
           reservoir will alleviate shortages in downstream areas during dry years;
          Electricity production for export – electricity generated by the Project will be exported to
           neighboring countries;



         ROGUN HYDROPOWER PROJECT – UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT                     PUBLIC |
         WSP
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: DATE                                                     June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                   Page | 1-1
           Flood routing – the Vakhsh cascade, including the Nurek dam, is not designed to withstand the
            Probable Maximum Flood (PMF) for the Vakhsh River. The Project is designed to withstand the
            PMF and will therefore provide flood retention capacity and protection to Nurek and downstream
            dams; and
           Retention of sediments – retention of the high sediment load which would otherwise fill the Nurek
            reservoir. The Project will effectively extend the life of the Nurek HPP and Vakhsh cascade by
            over 100 years.
1.2.4.    The Project is located approximately 100 kilometers (km) east of Dushanbe on the Vakhsh River.
          The river is intensively developed and hosts five operational HPP developments, of which the
          Project is the uppermost. The Vakhsh hydropower cascade currently includes the Nurek HPP,
          located 70 km downstream from the Project location and downstream of the Nurek, Baipaza,
          Sangtuda 1, Sangtuda 2 and Golovnaya dams.
1.2.5.    Once complete, the height of the dam wall will be 335 meters (m) and a total of six 630 megawatt
          (MW) turbines will have a generation capacity of 3,780 MW and an average annual output of over
          17,000 gigawatt (GW) hours. The Project has an expected life of 110 years, dependent on the
          sediment inflow. The reservoir will take approximately 16 years to fill to a maximum storage capacity
          of 13.3 km3 and will flood an area of 170 km2 at the full supply level (FSL).
1.2.6.    The installation and commissioning of the turbines is being completed incrementally. As of 2023, two
          400 MW turbines have been installed and are currently operational. This phase of the Project is
          referred to as Stage 1 throughout the ESIA. Stage 1 power generation is currently limited to 200 MW
          per turbine, due to the current reservoir levels, providing a combined capacity of 400 MW of power
          to the grid.
1.2.7.    Stage 1 turbines have been installed with temporary impellers. The impellers will ultimately be
          replaced by Voith (Austria) as part of the Lot 1 Engineering, Procurement and Construction (EPC)
          contract, increasing the capacity of each turbine from 400 MW to 630 MW.
1.2.8.    The Project has a number of associated facilities, these are described in Volume 1, Chapter 3:
          Project Description of this ESIA.
1.2.9.    Construction of the Project (hereafter referred to as Stage 2) is ongoing. According to the most
          recent construction schedule, the main dam expected to be completed in 2032 with full inundation of
          the reservoir area expected by 2038 (correct as of March 2024).
1.2.10.   A large numbcloser of Project Affected Persons (PAPs) have already been or will need to be
          relocated because of the Project. Resettlement is managed by the State Enterprise of the
          Directorate of Flooding Zone (DFZ) and is undertaken in phases aligned with the inundation levels of
          the reservoir.
1.2.11.   The resettlement program includes a total of 69 villages from the Rogun, Nurobod, and Rasht
          districts affecting over 50,000 PAPs, with almost half from the Rasht district.
1.2.12.   In 2014, 2,697 PAPs (under Resettlement Action Plan (RAP) 1) were resettled from eight villages in
          Rogun City and Nurobod District. A total of 16,919 PAPs are in the second phase of resettlement
          (RAP 2 2017-2026).
1.2.13.   Further RAPs (RAPS 3, 4, and 5) will be developed by DFZ. All resettlement activities are due for
          completion by 2032. The total number of resettled PAPs throughout the Project life cycle may be


          ROGUN HYDROPOWER PROJECT – UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT                PUBLIC |
          WSP
          Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: DATE                                                June 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                              Page | 1-2
somewhat higher than current estimates, due to natural population growth among affected
communities.

1.2.16.   Ongoing construction also involves a number of other contracts pending appointment and full
          mobilization of EPC Contractors for all four lots.
1.2.17.   More detailed descriptions of the current and future Project, the resettlement program, component
          specifications and Project activities undertaken to date are included in Volume 1, Chapter 3:
          Project Description.

1.3.      APPLICABLE LENDER STANDARDS
1.3.1.    Several financial institutions (hereafter referred to as ‘Lenders’) are likely to be involved in the
          Project. These include the World Bank (WB), Asian Infrastructure Investment Bank (AIIB), Asian
          Development Bank (ADB), European Investment Bank (EIB), Islamic Development Bank (IsDB), and
          the OPEC fund. A full list of Lenders is provided in Volume 1, Chapter 2: Legislation and
          Standards.
1.3.2.    The Lenders’ policies, standards and frameworks detail the E&S performance requirements that are
          conditional on a Lender providing funding to a project. The World Bank and AIIB provided Technical



          ROGUN HYDROPOWER PROJECT – UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT                   PUBLIC |
          WSP
          Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: DATE                                                   June 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 1-6
         Assistance funding in 2022 to allow Tajikistan to undertake project preparation activities including
         preparation of this ESIA, as well as other technical procurement and E&S studies and activities.
1.3.3.   The World Bank and AIIB approved financing in 2024, contingent upon satisfactory completion of
         the ESIA and other E&S instruments as well as other requirements.
1.3.4.   . The overarching E&S framework used in this ESIA is the WB Environmental and Social Framework
         (ESF) including:
          Vision for Sustainable Development which sets out the aspirations of the World Bank for a
           more environmentally and socially sustainable future.
          WB E&S Policy for Investment Project Financing which details the mandatory requirements
           that apply to the Bank.
          Environmental and Social Standards (ESS): a set of 10 standards which together with their
           annexes set out the mandatory requirements on Borrowers for managing E&S risks on their
           projects.
1.3.5.   The principles of the Lenders’ E&S policies are, with a few exceptions, materially consistent with the
         WB ESF and compliance with the WB ESF substantially meets the requirements of other Lenders’
         policies and standards. In a few cases where other Lenders have specific requirements beyond
         those of the World Bank, these have been addressed as appropriate.
1.3.6.   The ESF is supported by several relevant guidance and best practice notes prepared by the World
         Bank Group that inform the ESIA process. Collectively, these and other accepted guidelines
         comprise Good International Industry Practice (GIIP) for E&S performance, which the ESF requires
         to be taken into account in impact assessments. Additional information on the ESF is provided in
         Volume 1, Chapter 2: Legislation and Standards.
1.3.7.   In addition to the above, the laws of Tajikistan, including international agreements to which
         Tajikistan is a party, will apply to the Project.

1.4.     PREVIOUS ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL STUDIES KEY FINDINGS
1.4.1.   From 2011 to 2014, the WB provided financing to assess the technical, environmental and social
         aspects of the Project. In 2014, an ESIA, an Environmental and Social Management Plan (ESMP), a
         Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF), a Resettlement Action Plan (RAP) for phase 1 of the
         resettlement program, a Livelihood Restoration Plan (LRP) and a Techno-Economic Assessment
         Study (TEAS) were prepared, disclosed for public review and comment, and adopted.
1.4.2.   The 2014 ESIA was carried out for regulatory purposes to adhere to national legislation on
         environmental protection and ESIA procedure (No. 464), in addition to the World Bank Operational
         Policies (OPs), given the participation of the World Bank in the project assessment studies between
         2011-2014. The scope of the 2014 ESIA and TEAS assessed the design, construction and full
         operation up to FSL 1290m.
1.4.3.   Throughout preparation of the 2014 ESIA, the WB E&S Panel of Experts (hereafter referred to as
         ‘2014E&S PoE’), provided advice to the Government of the Republic of Tajikistan . In their final
         report, the 2014 E&S PoE concluded that the final draft ESIA ‘is of acceptable international
         standard, and subject to some comments on key issues raised in the present report the Panel
         agrees with the overall conclusion and recommendations made in the ESIA’.

         WSP
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: DATE                                                    June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page | 1-7
1.4.4.   In 2021, the WB reviewed the ongoing construction activities to:
          evaluate the adequacy and performance of the mitigation and management measures in the
           2014 ESMP;
          assess whether the E&S impacts were being managed to acceptable levels; and
          evaluate the extent to which Rogun JSC and its contractors were implementing the requirements
           of the 2014 ESMP.
1.4.5.   In 2023, the WB and AIIB approved Technical Assistance grants to support PMG in updating the
         2014 ESIA and other E&S instruments and undertaking additional studies that are required to
         ensure the Project’s technical and E&S planning and execution complies with the 2018 ESF and
         Good International Industry Practice (GIIP). The WB and AIIB approved the financing for the Project
         in December 2024.
1.4.6.   In 2023, a newly constituted E&S PoE, whose role is to provide independent advice regarding the
         measures to enhance the overall environmental and social outcomes of the Project, produced two
         reports appraising the Project against each of the applicable WB ESF ESS. The E&S PoE reports
         highlighted several key issues, recommendations on organizational arrangements and E&S capacity
         building measures that would require consideration within the scope of the ESIA, RPF, Stakeholder
         Engagement Plan (SEP) as well as additional E&S studies. The E&S PoE findings have been
         considered within the scope of the ESIA.

1.5.     ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT SCOPE
1.5.1.   This ESIA is an update to the 2014 ESIA for the Project and includes new information and new
         analysis required to address the issues and concerns raised in recent studies and subsequent to the
         completion of the 2014 ESIA. The aim of the ESIA update is to ensure the Project E&S planning and
         execution comply with the Applicable Standards, including GIIP, as described in Volume 1, Chapter
         2: Legislation and Standards.
1.5.2.   The updated ESIA incorporates an impact assessment methodology, characterization of baseline
         conditions to reflect current conditions, an updated E&S impact assessment, a Cumulative Impact
         Assessment (CIA) and, amongst other studies, a Carbon Footprint Analysis (CFA), Climate Change
         Risk Assessment (CCRA) and Minimum Flows Study, which are further described in Section 1.6.
1.5.3.   Other key E&S instruments, including management measures, are described in detail in Section 1.6.

1.6.     REPORTING APPROACH AND STRUCTURE
1.6.1.   This ESIA builds on the findings and information presented in previous studies, input from the
         current ESPOE, and additional reports, studies and data etc prepared and collected since the issue
         of the 2014 Rogun ESIA. . This will enhance and address the key E&S issues resulting from ongoing
         construction and development of the Project, as well as updates to the Applicable Standards.
1.6.2.   The ESIA update is informed by several key documents which include (but not limited to):
          ESIA for Rogun Hydro Power Plant (2014) consisting of three Volumes (ESIA, Technical
           Annexes and an ESMP);
          LRP for Stage 1 (2015).
          Phase 1 RAP (2014)

2.       LEGISLATION AND STANDARDS
2.1.1.   This chapter summarizes the international agreements, Lender standards and Tajik national laws
         and regulations that are applicable to the Project.

2.2.     INTRODUCTION
2.2.1.   The ESIA is required to align the Project E&S performance to comply with several standards,
         including the international agreements that Tajikistan is party to, the international Lender E&S
         policies and guidelines that apply to the Project (hereafter referred to as ‘the Applicable Standards’)
         and Tajik national legislation and regulations. The relevant standards include but are not limited to:
          Tajikistan law, including international agreements to which Tajikistan is a party;
          The World Bank (WB) ESF (2018), the WB Group (WBG) General Environmental, Health, and
           Safety (EHS) Guidelines (2007); the Industry Sector EHS Guidelines which provide guidance on
           EHS issues in specific industry sectors; and relevant Operational Policies, including 7.50;
          The European Investment Bank (EIB) Environmental and Social Framework (ESSF), consisting
           of the E&S policy (2022) and revised E&S standards (2022); and the EIB Environmental, Climate,
           and Social (ECS) Guidelines on Hydropower Development (2019);
          Asian Development Bank (ADBlabor code): Safeguard Policy Statement (SPS) (2009) and
           associated safeguard documents;
          Asian Infrastructure Investment Bank (AIIB): Environmental and Social Framework (2019, 2021,
           2022, and 2024); and
          Other environmental and social standards, objectives and goals prescribed by the Islamic
           Development Bank, the Eurasian Development Bank (EDB); the Saudi Fund for Development
           (SFD) and the Kuwait Fund for Arab Economic Development (KFAED).
2.2.2.   The overarching E&S framework used in this ESIA is the WB ESF. The principles of the Lenders’
         E&S policies are, with a few exceptions, materially consistent with the WB ESF and compliance with
         the WB ESF substantially meets the requirements of other Lenders’ policies and standards. In a few
         cases where other Lenders have specific requirements beyond those of the World Bank, these have
         been addressed as appropriate

2.4.     NATIONAL ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL REGULATORY
         FRAMEWORK
         ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
2.4.1.   Three laws establish the requirements for impact assessment in Tajikistan:
          The Law on Environmental Protection (2011);
          The Law on Ecological Expertise (2012); and
          The Law on Environmental Impact Assessment (2017).
2.4.2.   The Laws on Environment Protection, Environmental Impact Assessment and Ecological Expertise
         requires the Government to approve a list of activities for which a complete EIA is mandatory.
2.4.3.   The Law on State Ecological Expertise (SEE) defines the principles and procedures for
         environmental impact assessment to prevent proposed activity causing harm to the environment.
         The Law also regulates social, economic and other consequences of the implementation of the
         proposed project.
2.4.4.   The SEE examines the compliance of proposed project activities with environmental legislation and
         standards to ensure that probable social, economic and ecological consequences are scientifically
         considered, findings are justified, comprehensive and objective and the development is in
         accordance with the law. The SEE must conclude that economic and other activities can be
         implemented in accordance with existing environmental standards with sufficient environmental
         protection and mitigation measures. Financing of programs and projects is allowed only after a
         positive SEE finding.
2.4.5.   An Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) study is a component of the SEE, as set out in the 2011
         amendments to the Environmental Protection Law and in the Law on the State Ecological Expertise
         (2012).
2.4.6.   The EIA is the responsibility of the Project and must analyze the short- and long-term environmental,
         genetic, economic, and demographic impacts and consequences. SEE for all investment projects is
         the responsibility of the Committee for Environmental Protection (CEP) and its regional offices,
         under the Government of Tajikistan. All civil works must be assessed with impacts and proposed
         mitigation which is reviewed and monitored by CEP.
2.4.7.   Conducting the EIA study is the responsibility of the project proponent. The Procedure for carrying
         out the EIA (Government Resolution No. 532 of November 1, 2018) establishes general
         requirements for the contents of the EIA documentation.
2.4.8.   The current guidelines for EIA do not require a preliminary assessment (screening) of a proposed
         project to decide whether an EIA is required as the list of objects and activities for which an EIA is
         required is detailed. In addition, the guidelines do not define the scope of the EIA.
2.4.9.   Table 2-2 provides a list of the relevant laws on the protection of the environment, with a summary
         of the objectives and the relevance to the Project.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |

         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 2-6

                               articles: Freedom of Thought (Article 30), Right to Health (Article 39), Environmental Protection    Project.

 The Law on Dehkan Farms      This Law provides the legislative basis for the establishment and operation of privately owned       Safeguards the rights of certain
 (2016)                       commercial farms known as Dehkan farms. According to the Law of 2009, Dehkan farms                   farms and farmers and is especially
                              carried out activities without any legal status, but the new Law allows Dehkan farms to obtain       relevant to resettlement.
                              the status of legal entities. It also clarifies and fixes the rights of members of Dehkan farms as
                              land users. The law improves the management of Dehkan farms and defines the rights and
                              duties of their members. It allows farmers to legally erect field camps on land as temporary
                              buildings, which makes it possible to significantly improve productivity during the agricultural
                              season. The law requires Dehkan farms to take measures to improve soil fertility and improve
                              the ecological status of lands, make timely payments for water and electricity and provide
                              statistical information to government agencies.

 The Labor Code (2016)      The Labor Code prohibits discrimination in employment and sets the minimum age of                       Safeguards the rights of workers,
                            employment, as well as the conditions under which minors can work and establishes rules for             women and children. Incorporated
                            the employer-employee relationship and for protection of workers. The Code prohibits forced             into the Project labor management
                            labor and underage labor.                                                                               practices.
                            The Code establishes rules for minimum wages, leave, overtime and has provisions for
                            pregnant women and caregivers for children. It also sets the rules for settling disputes between
                            workers and employers.
                            The Labor Code stipulates requirements for occupational health and safety. It establishes the
                            right of workers to workplaces that are protected from exposure to dangerous and harmful
                            factors. Employers are required to tell workers of the risks and hazards of their jobs and
                            requires employers to provide appropriate personal protective equipment. Employers are
                            required to provide workers with compulsory social insurance against accidents, disease or
                            injuries that may result from undertaking the work.
                            Workers the right to refuse to undertake work that violates labor protection requirements. In
                            addition, workers engaged in hazardous working conditions are entitled to free medical and
                            preventative care, additional paid leave and other benefits and compensation. In case of
                            disability or death, employers must provide compensation in multiples of average annual
                            earnings. Employers must train workers in performing their work safety and must provide
                            collective and personal protection of workers.

            The Law on Conformity Assessment.

2.6.     CENTRAL ASIAN WATER MANAGEMENT AGREEMENTS
2.6.1.   Prior to the independence of Tajikistan in 1991, the Soviet Union had concerns about the water
         crisis in the Aral Sea and allocated water distribution limits among the former Soviet Republics of the
         Aral Sea Basin, establishing a series of protocols and resolutions.
2.6.2.   Water distribution limits for the Amu Darya Basin were established by the Scientific and Technical
         Council of the Ministry of Water Resources of the Soviet Union on March 12, 1987. On September
         10, 1987, Protocol 566 was formally endorsed in Moscow by the riparian states of the Amu Darya
         Basin, now known as Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan. Protocol 566 is still
         used today as a reference document for discussions on water allocation and water-sharing
         principles between the nations of the Amu Darya Basin.
2.6.3.   Table 2-3 outlines the water distribution limits in the Amu Darya Basin.

         Table 2-3 - Water distribution limits in the Amu Darya Basin

          Country                   Maximum allocation (millions m3 / year)          Share Percentage (%)

          Kyrgyzstan                400                                              0.7

          Tajikistan                9,500                                            15.4

          Uzbekistan                29,600                                           48.1

          Turkmenistan              22,000                                           35.8

          Total for basin           61,500                                           100




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: ESIA-Rev09-English                                   June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                              Page | 2-18
2.6.4.   After the independence of the former Soviet Republics, a joint declaration was issued on October
         12, 1991 regarding compliance and continuity in the allocation of water to the riparian states. The
         Almaty Agreement was signed on February 18, 1992 as an “agreement on cooperation in joint
         management, use and protection of interstate sources of water resources” to maintain and adhere to
         the sharing of the transboundary water resources set out in Protocol 566 for the Amu Darya, in
         addition to establishing an Interstate Commission for Water Coordination (ICWC) and designating it
         as the body responsible for the definition of seasonal allocations in line with the annual agreements.
2.6.5.   The Department of Energy and Water Resources is responsible for representing the Republic of
         Tajikistan at the ICWC and BVO Amu Darya, in addition to reaching agreements with water
         allocations. Formal agreements between the Republics of Tajikistan and Uzbekistan are in place to
         manage expectations and obligations of both countries in the consideration of issues of mutual
         concern.
2.6.6.   A Memorandum (dated 2022) between the government of the Republic of Tajikistan and the
         government of the Republic of Uzbekistan sets out the intentions of cooperation with regard to the
         generation of energy from Rogun HPP; including the long-term export of energy from Tajikistan to
         Uzbekistan during the summer months, under mutually beneficial terms.
2.6.7.   To date, Afghanistan has not been formally involved in discussions regarding the allocation of the
         waters of the Amu Darya.
2.6.8.   Transboundary impacts that extend to countries beyond Tajikistan are discussed in Volume 1,
         Chapter 24 of this ESIA.

2.7.     INTERNATIONAL STANDARDS AND GUIDELINES
         INTERNATIONAL OBLIGATIONS
2.7.1.   In addition to national legislation and regulations on environmental issues, Tajikistan is also
         signatory to several international treaties focused on environmental issues:
          Vienna Convention for the Protection of the Ozone Layer, 1996, as updated by Montreal Protocol
           on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer;
          UN Convention to Combat Desertification (CCD), 1997;
          UN Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD), 1997, as updated by Cartagena and Nagoya
           Protocols;
          Ramsar Convention (joined 2000);
          Bonn Convention on the Conservation of Migratory Species of Wild Animals (joined 2001), as
           updated by Bukhara Deer Memorandum, 2002;
          UN Framework Convention on Climate Change, 1998, with related update Kyoto Protocol,
           accessed on December 29, 2008, and entered into force on March 29, 2009;
          Stockholm Convention on Persistent Organic Pollutants (ratified 2007);
          Aarhus Convention (UNECE Convention on Access to Information, Public Participation in
           Decision Making and Access to Justice in Environmental Matters) (joined 2001), as updated by
           Kiev Protocol on Pollutant Release and Transfer Registers to the Convention on Access to
           Information, on May 21, 2003;
          Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora, 2016.
          UNESCO Convention Concerning the Protection of the World Cultural and Natural Heritage
           (joined 1992);
          Rotterdam Convention on Prior Informed Consent (PIC) procedure on September 28, 1998,
           ratification pending;
          The United Nations Convention to Combat Desertification (1997); and


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: ESIA-Rev09-English                                    June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 2-19
          Basel Convention on the Control of Transboundary Movements of Hazardous Wastes and their
           Disposal (2016).
2.7.2.   In addition, Tajikistan has ratified several core labor-related conventions and standards of the
         International Labor Organization (ILO), including:
            Tripartite Consultation (International Labor Standards) Convention (2014);
            Labor Inspection Convention (2009);
            Convention for the Safeguarding of the Intangible Cultural Heritage (2006);
            Convention on the Rights of the Child (CRC) (1993);
            Employment Policy Convention (1993);
            Occupational Safety and Health Convention (1981);
            Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination Against Women (1979);
            International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights (1976);
            Forced Labor (C029) and Abolition of Forced Labor (C105);
            Minimum Age (C138) and Worst Forms of Child Labor (C182);
            Discrimination (C111);
            Freedom of Association and the Right to Organize (C087);
            Right to Organize and Collective Bargaining (C098); and
            Equal Remuneration (C100).

         WORLD BANK ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL FRAMEWORK
2.7.3.   The policies and standards of the various Lenders to the Project are summarized in this section. The
         E&S framework used as the overarching framework in this ESIA is the WB Environmental and Social
         Framework (ESF) including:
          Vision for Sustainable Development which sets out the aspirations of the World Bank for a more
           Environmentally and Socially sustainable future;
          WB E&S Policy for Investment Project Financing which details the requirements that apply to the
           Bank; and
          Environmental and Social Standards (ESS): a set of 10 standards which together with their
           annexes set out the mandatory requirements on Borrowers for managing E&S risks on their
           projects.
2.7.4.   The underpinning principles of the Lenders’ E&S policies are materially consistent with the WB ESF.
         The assumption is that, in most cases, compliance with the WB ESF will result in effective
         compliance with other lenders policies and standards. The implementation of this “common
         approach” is consistent with ESS1.
2.7.5.   In general, the environmental and social assessment will consider the requirements of OP 7.50 for
         projects on international waterways.
2.7.6.   Table 2-4 outlines the WB ESSs and their relevance to the Project. Table 2-5 outlines the key gaps
         between National Tajik legislation and WB ESS requirements.

2.7.7.   The WB ESF states that Borrowers and projects are also required to apply the relevant
         requirements of the WB Group EHSGs. These are technical reference documents with general and
         industry specific examples of GIIP. The standards referenced in this section are included for the
         purposes of the consideration of best practice, it does not imply financing of the Project by all banks.
         EUROPEAN INVESTMENT BANK (EIB)
2.7.8.   The EIB ESSF is an overarching policy framework that focuses on sustainable and inclusive
         development, committing to a just and fair transition and supporting the transition to economies and
         communities that are climate and disaster-resilient, low-carbon, environmentally sound and more
         resource efficient.
2.7.9.   The EIB ESSs set out standards and obligations in ten areas and identifies key pieces of EU
         legislation that should be applied. As the Project is located outside the jurisdiction of the EU and
         European Free Trade Area (EFTA), the EIB ESSs specify that the Project should comply with the
         core principles and essential procedural elements laid down by the relevant EU legislation. There
         are 10 ESSs, including:
            Standard 1: Environmental and Social Impacts & Risks;
            Standard 2: Stakeholder Engagement;
            Standard 3: Resource Efficiency & Pollution Prevention;
            Standard 4: Biodiversity and Ecosystems;
            Standard 5: Climate Change;
            Standard 6: Involuntary Resettlement;
            Standard 7: Vulnerable Groups, Indigenous Peoples & Gender;
            Standard 8: Labor Rights;
            Standard 9: Health, Safety & Security; and
            Standard 10: Cultural Heritage.

         ASIAN DEVELOPMENT BANK (ADB)
2.7.10. The ADB SPS (2009) and associated safeguarding documents aim to avoid, limit and compensate
        for negative project impacts.
2.7.11. The ADB SPS builds on three previous safeguard policies:
          The environment;
          Involuntary resettlement; and
          Indigenous peoples.
2.7.12. The SPS brings the three safeguard policies into a single policy that enhances consistency and
        coherence and more comprehensively addresses environmental and social impacts and risks.
2.7.13. The SPS aims to promote the sustainability of project outcomes by:
          Avoiding adverse impacts of projects on the environment and affected people, where possible;
          Minimizing, mitigating, and/or compensating for adverse project impacts on the environment and
           affected people when avoidance is not possible; and
          Helping borrowers/clients to strengthen their safeguard systems and develop the capacity to
           manage environmental and social risks.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: ESIA-Rev09-English                                    June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 2-32
         ASIAN INFRASTRUCTURE INVESTMENT BANK (AIIB)
2.7.14. The AIIB Environmental and Social Framework (ESF) (2019, 2021, 2022 and updated in July 2024)
        aims to create environmentally and socially sustainable developments for the Bank and its clients
        through the integration of good environmental and social planning, alongside management of risks
        and impacts, to feed into preparation, decision-making and implementation phases on supported
        projects.
2.7.15. The framework includes three key documents:
          Environmental and Social Policy;
          Environmental and Social Standards; and
          Environmental and Social Exclusion List.
         ISLAMIC DEVELOPMENT BANK (ISDB)
2.7.16. The IsDB aims at bettering lives, delivering impact, boosting recovery, tackling poverty, building
        resilience and driving green economic growth.
2.7.17. The IsDB has made the 17 UN SDGs a key factor in their investment decision-making, as the high-
        level SDGs and the 169 specific targets, encompassing the social, economic and environmental
        dimensions of development and these aspirations for human dignity, is in line with the principles and
        objectives of development from an Islamic perspective.
         SAUDI FUND OF DEVELOPMENT (SFD)
2.7.18. The SFD was established as a governmental institution to provide support in the form of soft loans
        to finance development projects in developing countries. SFD has defined its goal as unifying efforts
        with leading global organizations and contributing to sustainable development.
2.7.19. Through the unlimited support of the Government of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia (KSA), and as
        defined in its law, the SFD’s stated objective is to ‘contribute to financing development projects in
        the developing countries by granting them the necessary loans, the technical aids necessary for
        financing studies and institutional support.’
2.7.20. The SFD has played a significant role in enhancing renewable energy in Asia and has signed six
        loan agreements for renewable energy in Pakistan and Nepal, all of which relate to four hydropower
        plant developments. Since its establishment, the SFD has made important contributions through its
        loans towards mitigating climate change, such as by supporting various solar and hydropower
        projects. By the end of 2021, it funded 15 development loans for the renewable energy sector in
        nine developing countries, worth a total of $650.6 million.
2.7.21. According to the SFD and UN Development Programme (UNDP) joint workshop on climate change
        (June 27, 2023), SFD officials highlighted the Fund’s commitment to assisting sustainable
        development through clean energy, afforestation, and greening initiatives in developing countries.
2.7.22. The SFD provide loans for developing countries, which they define as countries with limited
        economic and financial resources and are characterized by being:
            Less developed;
            Structurally impoverished;
            In need of fundamental development assistance;
            Landlocked and non-coastal; and
            Facing economic and social challenges, such as lack of natural resources and high population
             growth rates.


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: ESIA-Rev09-English                                    June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 2-33
2.7.23. SFD loans are directed towards the following economic and social sectors within developing
        countries:
            Developing basic infrastructure by expanding transport and communication networks;
            Strengthening protection against diseases and epidemics by supporting the healthcare sector;
            Developing human resources;
            Providing energy for all;
            Ensuring food and water security;
            Preserving the environment and public health;
            Providing adequate housing for low-income groups; and
            Developing industry and mining.

         KUWAIT FUND FOR ARAB ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT (KFAED)
2.7.24. The KFAED was established to assist Arab and other developing countries to prosper economically.
        No geographic, ethnic, religious, caste or cultural barriers restrict access to the Fund's loans and
        assistance; and the Fund provides technical assistance to finance the cost of feasibility studies.
2.7.25. The KFAED supports projects that have a strong public sector element and are of a developmental
        nature, and assistance may be provided to the following sectors: infrastructure, agriculture,
        irrigation, transport, communications, energy, water supply, sewage treatment, education and
        health.
2.7.26. In a news article dated June 26, 2023, the KFAED stated the importance of encouraging joint efforts
        with the Group members contributing to investments in the energy sector to combat the impacts of
        climate change and its challenges.

2.8.     GOOD INTERNATIONAL INDUSTRY PRACTICE (GIIP)
         WORLD BANK GROUP (WBG) ENVIRONMENTAL, HEALTH AND SAFETY (EHS)
         GUIDELINES
2.8.1.   The WBG has developed EHS Guidelines as technical reference documents containing industry-
         specific examples of GIIP that are referred to in the WB ESF. The WBG requires Borrowers to apply
         the relevant measures of the EHS Guidelines.
2.8.2.   The General EHS Guideline (2007) includes guidelines for environmental controls during facility
         construction and operation (air and water emissions, hazardous materials management, noise,
         contaminated land, etc.) and occupational and community health and safety during construction and
         operation.
2.8.3.   The WBG has also outlined Industry Sector Guidelines providing industry-specific examples GIIP.
         The Industry Sector Guidelines are designed to be used together with the General EHS Guidelines.
2.8.4.   The principle EHS Industry Sector Guidelines relevant to the Project, include but are not limited to:
            EHS Guidelines for Construction Materials Extraction (2007).
            EHS Guidelines for Electric Power Transmission and Distribution (2007).
            EHS Guidelines for Toll Roads (2007).
            EHS Guidelines for Waste Management Facilities (2007).
            EHS Guidelines for Water and Sanitation (2007).
            EHS Guidelines for Crude Oil and Petroleum Product Terminals (2007).




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: ESIA-Rev09-English                                   June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                              Page | 2-34
         WORLD BANK - GOOD PRACTICE HANDBOOK - ENVIRONMENTAL FLOWS FOR
         HYDROPOWER PROJECTS (2018)
2.8.5.   The WB has produced a good practice handbook, written in collaboration with the IFC, to provide
         guidance to practitioners for a consistent approach to assess and manage hydropower project
         impacts on downstream river ecosystems and people through the assessment and provision of e-
         flows.

         WORLD BANK GUIDANCE ON HYDROPOWER OPERATION AND MAINTENANCE
         (2020)
2.8.6.   The WB has produced a handbook to provide guidance to raise awareness among utility managers,
         decision makers, and other stakeholders of the benefits of developing robust operation and
         maintenance (O&M) strategies for all existing hydropower plants and those under development.’
2.8.7.   The handbook was written in collaboration with the International Hydropower Association (IHA) with
         financial support from the Global Water Security & Sanitation Partnership (GWSP) and the Swiss
         State Secretariat for Economic Affairs (SECO).
2.8.8.   The handbook outlines practical steps and recommendations to prepare ad hoc O&M strategies that
         will help to extend the life expectancy of assets, while securing hydropower production in a cost-
         effective way and preventing high-cost expenditures for rehabilitating facilities that have not been
         maintained properly.
         WORLD BANK GOOD PRACTICE NOTE ON DAM SAFETY (2020)
2.8.9.   The good practice note provides additional guidance on the application of relevant requirements of
         the ESF, in particular in utilising a risk management approach to the application of dam safety
         requirements. The document provides guidance on guidance on compliance requirements, a risk
         management approach to dam safety, risk analysis tools, quality of information and capacity as well
         as application to World Bank operations and associated procedural aspects.
         WORLD BANK – ESSENTIAL ELEMENTS FOR ENSURING THE SAFETY OF DAMS
         AND DOWNSTREAM COMMUNITIES POLICY NOTE (2023)
         This policy note provides guidance to policy makers and practitioners on the essential elements for
         establishing regulatory regimes for assuring the safety of dams and downstream communities.
         The policy note summarizes country case studies; provides a comparative analysis of the legal,
         institutional, and regulatory requirements; and recommends legal, institutional, technical, and
         financial elements relevant for different countries.
         EIB – ENVIRONMENTAL, CLIMATE AND SOCIAL GUIDELINES ON HYDROPOWER
         DEVELOPMENT (2019)
2.8.10. The EIB has also published Guidelines on Hydropower Development which set out the EIB’s
        objectives for investments in hydropower projects, establishing sector-specific standards and criteria
        which promoters should meet. They also summarize best practice recommendations for integrating
        social, biodiversity, natural resource management and climate considerations into hydropower
        projects.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment               PUBLIC |
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: ESIA-Rev09-English                                 June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                            Page | 2-35
         IFC – GOOD PRACTICE NOTE - ENVIRONMENTAL, HEALTH, AND SAFETY
         APPROACHES FOR HYDROPOWER PROJECTS (2018)
2.8.11. The IFC authored a guidance document on best practices for managing environmental, health, and
        safety (EHS) risks for hydropower projects to minimize their environmental impact, ensure the safety
        of workers and communities, and promote sustainable development. The document was written by
        the IFC with support from Golder Associates and the World Bank.
2.8.12. It sets out guidelines on:
          Environmental Management:
            •   Watershed Management;
            •   Habitat Conservation; and
            •   Instream Flow.
          Occupational Health and Safety (OHS):
            •   Construction Safety; and
            •   Operational Safety.
          Community Health and Safety:
            •   Dam Safety; and
            •   Public Health.
          Performance Indicators and Monitoring:
            •   Environmental Monitoring; and
            •   OHS Monitoring.
         INTERNATIONAL COMMISSION ON LARGE DAMS (ICOLD) – WORLD DECLARATION
         ON DAM SAFETY (2019)
2.8.13. The document sets ICOLDs declaration that dam safety is of ICOLDs highest priority. The changing
        conditions of dam safety are highlighted to include aspects such as lack of experience in dam safety
        management and operations and climate change.
2.8.14. Overarching pillars of dam safety are recognized and include, amongst others, the structural
        integrity of dams being the keystone to dam safety, the importance of routine surveillance,
        maintenance, monitoring, emergency planning and training as well as a comprehensive dam safety
        approach.
         INTERNATIONAL HYDROPOWER ASSOCIATION (IHA)
2.8.15. The IHA is a non-profit membership association, operates in over 120 countries, and includes
        leading hydropower owners and operators, developers, designers, suppliers, and consultants. The
        IHA has played a leading role in improving understanding of good and best practices in hydropower
        development and operations.
2.8.16. The IHA published the Hydropower Sustainability Standard, a global certification scheme outlining
        sustainability expectations for hydropower projects around the world, and managed by the
        Hydropower Sustainability Alliance (HSA).



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment              PUBLIC |
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: ESIA-Rev09-English                                June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                           Page | 2-36
2.8.17. The IHA has several assessment tools and guidelines used to design, develop, and assess
        hydropower projects including:
         The Hydropower Sustainability Guidelines on GIIP
         Hydropower Sustainability Standard - a framework designed to ensure that hydropower projects
          are developed and operated in a sustainable manner. It encompasses twelve ESG topics and a
          robust assessment and certification process. It aims to balance the benefits of hydropower with
          the need to protect communities and the environment, promoting responsible growth in the
          sector;
         The Hydropower Sector Climate Resilience Guide - practical and systematic guidance for
          hydropower engineers, operators and project owners to develop climate resilient projects; and
         The Carbon Calculator for Reservoirs (g-res tool) – a tool used to predict greenhouse gas
          emissions from a reservoir.

Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                     June 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ
3.       PROJECT DESCRIPTION
3.1.1.   This chapter provides a description of the Project location, components, associated facilities,
         activities and organization for the construction and operational phases.

3.2.     PROJECT OVERVIEW
3.2.1.   The Rogun HPP Project has been under construction for many years, most recently since 2018 with
         work currently ongoing. As of May 2025, the status of the Project is:
            A dam crest at 1,110 meters above sea level (masl) (Stage 1: Dam Construction);
            A reservoir at elevation 1,075 masl with a surface area of 12 square kilometers (km2) (Stage 1:
             Reservoir Creation);
            An underground powerhouse with two operating turbines (Units 5 and 6) with a combined
             generation capacity of 400 MW. In 2025, these will be upgraded to a capacity of 630 MW each.
            Three high pressure water outlets, referred to as High Level Outlet (HLO) one and two and
             remote tunnel spillways (RTS), headrace tunnels and four diversion tunnels;
            A gas insulated substation and five transmission lines that connect the Project to the national
             grid, Dushanbe and other countries;
            Approximately 70 km of underground roads;
            Approximately 26 km of aboveground roads, mostly unpaved;
            Approximately 15 km of water tunnels;
            Permanent offices and site buildings;
            Temporary construction areas including borrow pits, spoil storage, concrete batching, logistics
             and laydown areas, offices and other construction facilities;
            One main construction camp for over 20 construction contractors involving approximately 20000
             workers (95% of which are from Tajikistan) on the lower terrace between the reservoir and
             Rogun City. This camp will be flooded by 2026/2027 and contractors will be relocated to new
             locations; and
            A program to resettle villages and households that are located within the footprint of the reservoir
             inundation zone. To date, about 10,000 Project Affected Persons (PAPs) have been resettled
             with a choice of moving to host communities or new settlements.
         It is clear, therefore, that the project is unique in the sense that the project location is a highly
              disturbed, brownfield, construction site. This feature is, therefore, taken into account in the
              description of the baseline conditions and impact assessment which are presented in
              subsequent chapters.
3.2.2.   Once construction of the Rogun HPP is completed, the Project will comprise:
            A dam with a crest at 1,300 masl by 2032, and an overall height of 335 m (Stage 2: Dam
             Construction);




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                            June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                    Page | 3-1
            A reservoir with the water level progressively raised until 2038 when the full supply level (FSL) is
             reached at 1,290 masl. The reservoir will have total surface area of 170 km², a length of
             approximately 70 km and capacity of approximately 13.3 km3 (Stage 2: Reservoir Creation);
            A surface spillway, two mid-level and three high-level tunnels to protect the dam and allow safe
             passage of floodwaters up to the Probable Maximum Flood (PMF);
            An underground powerhouse and transformer room in excavated caverns on the left bank. The
             powerhouse will contain six turbines, having a maximum generation capacity of 3,780 MW;
            Approximately 5-6 km of above ground surface roads;
            Six transmission lines connecting the Project to the national grid and other countries;
            Reinstatement of the 55 km bare earth road on the left bank to connect with the new bridge that
             will span the Surhkhob River to alleviate severance of the left bank villages due to the reservoir;
             and
            Continuation of the resettlement program until about 2032, by which time about 50,000 people in
             69 villages will have been resettled into either new or existing settlements.
3.2.3.   Once complete, the Project will be the largest installation in Central Asia, with a generation capacity
         of 3,780 MW and an average annual generation of over 17,000,000 MW-hour (17,000 gigawatt-hour
         (GWh) or 17 billion Kilowatt-hour (KWh). The Project has an expected life of 110 years, dependent
         on the sediment inflow. Furthermore, by intercepting sediment upstream of Nurek, it will extend the
         lifespan of the Nurek hydropower plant by 100 years or more.
3.2.4.   Further information regarding the Project components is provided in Table 3-7.
         PROJECT ORGANIZATION

3.2.6.   Approximately 20 small non-EPC contractors undertake various preparatory and maintenance works
         and are supervised by Rogun JSC.


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                        June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page | 3-3
3.2.7.   As of May 2025, there are approximately 20,000 people employed for Project construction and
         resettlement planning and implementation. About 95% of the workforce is Tajik and the remainder
         expatriate.
3.2.8.   The current Project organizational structure is presented in Figure 3-1.
3.2.9.   When international financing becomes available, the organizational structure will remain except for:
             A Project Management Consultant (PMC) will be appointed to replace the Employers
              Representative to supervise all technical and Environmental and Social Health and Safety
              (ESHS) performance for all contractors on site across Lots 1-4. They will report to Rogun JSC.
             An EPC Contractor will be appointed to complete the Lot 4 works. Following the EPC
              appointment, the Ariana and EMZ contracts will be terminated;
             The Lot 1 EPC Contractor, Voith, will mobilize to the site to install and commission the new
              turbines.
3.2.10. Construction of the Project and resettlement program will be completed in 2032.
         ESHS RESPONSIBILITIES
3.2.11. The responsibility for ESHS management is divided between three organizations on site:
             Project Management Group (PMG) for Energy Facilities Construction: under the President of
              Tajikistan has been appointed to coordinate all parties involved in Rogun HPP, including liaison
              with international lenders.
             Employer/Owner: OJSC Rogun HPP (aka Rogun JSC): responsible for ongoing operation of
              Rogun HPP and for construction of the Project. Rogun JSC provides supervision of Lot 3 EPC,
              Lot 4 early works contractors, other smaller contractors and of the Employer’s Representative,
              Tractebel ELC. Rogun JSC is ultimately accountable for all parties’ compliance with Tajikistan
              Law and, in future, International Standards.
             Employers Representative (Tractebel) provides overall supervision of E&S compliance
              monitoring for Lot 4 and recently expanded to cover all lots and the two major early works
              contractors.

3.2.14. Off-site, the State Enterprise Directorate for Flood Zone (DFZ) is responsible for developing and
        implementing the resettlement and livelihood restoration program, in addition to managing and
        overseeing the left bank works. They employ two main contractors, one for construction at new
        resettlement sites and another for demolition of relocated villages. DFZ also employs the State
        Forestry Agency to undertake land clearance works in advance of flooding, and another contractor
        who transports property of resettled households to designated resettlement sites.

Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                                           Page | 3-8
3.3.     PROJECT LOCATION AND CONTEXT
3.3.1.   The Republic of Tajikistan is a landlocked nation in Central Asia, with an area of 142,326 km²
         (54,952 sq. mi). Dushanbe is the country’s capital and largest city. On 9 September 1991, Tajikistan
         declared itself an independent sovereign nation from the Soviet Union.
3.3.2.   Tajikistan is divided into four administrative regions or provinces. These regions are:
            Sughd province.
            Khatlon province.
            Gorno-Badakhshan Autonomous province.
            Region of Republican Subordination.
3.3.3.   The Region of Republican Subordination is divided into seven districts (rayons), namely Fayzabad,
         Rogun, Nurobod, Rasht, Tavildara, Tajikabod and Jirgital. The Project will directly affect Rogun,
         Nurobod and Rasht districts. See Figure 3-2.
3.3.4.   The Project is located approximately 100 km east of the Tajik capital of Dushanbe on the Vakhsh
         River within the Amu Darya basin. The Vakhsh River is one of the two main tributaries of the Amu
         Darya, which is the largest river in Central Asia with a total length of 2,540 km. The Amu Dayrya
         originates at the confluence of the Pyanj and Vakhsh rivers and drains a catchment area of
         543,739 km². The Amu Darya basin is shared between Kyrgyzstan, Afghanistan, Tajikistan,
         Turkmenistan, and Uzbekistan.
3.3.5.   Four right bank tributaries (Kafirnigan, Surhan, Sherabad and Zeravshan) and one left bank tributary
         (Kunduz) flow into the Amu Darya within its middle reach. Currently, the Zeravshan does not reach
         the Amu Darya since its water is being used for irrigation within the territory of Uzbekistan. Further
         downstream towards the Aral Sea the river has no more tributaries. It is fed largely by water from
         melted snow, thus maximum discharges are observed in summer and minimum ones in January-
         February.

3.3.6.   One of the tributaries of Vakhsh River, the Kizil-Su, originates in Kyrgyzstan, flows through the
         Pamir-Alai mountains of Tajikistan and joins the Muk-Su, forming the Surkhob River. The Vakhsh
         River is formed by the confluence of Surkhob with Obihingou and then joins the Pyanj River after
         520 km to form the Amu Darya. The Pyanj and Amu Darya form the border between Tajikistan and
         Afghanistan.
3.3.7.   The drainage area of the Vakhsh River in Tajikistan is 31,200 km2. Most of the river runs through
         mountainous territory. At the Project site, about 340 km upstream of the confluence with the Pyanj,
         the river flows through a narrow, 400 to 500 m deep V-shaped gorge with gradients of the valley
         sides up to 50°.
3.3.8.   In its upper reaches, the Vakhsh River flows through a narrow valley with a steep gradient but, on a
         few sections the valley is wider and almost flat, and here the river has formed extensive floodplains.
         The floodplain near Komsomolobod and another near Novobod is located within the flooding zone of
         the reservoir. The confluence of the Surkhob and Obihingou tributaries will also be submerged by
         the reservoir.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                      June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                              Page | 3-9
Figure 3-2 - Overview of Project Location

3.3.9.   The Vakhsh River is intensively developed and accommodates five existing hydropower dams; of
         which the Project is the furthest upstream, in a cascade that includes the Nurek HPP, located 70 km
         downstream from the Project and, downstream of Nurek, the Baipaza, Sangtuda 1, Sangtuda 2 and
         Golovnaya Dams. The Rogun HPP is located at the upstream end of the cascade. Key features of
         the hydropower dams are presented in Table 3-5).

         Table 3-5 - Vakhsh Cascade Hydropower Dams
                                                                       Sangtuda     Sangtuda
                            Rogun             Nurek         Baipaza                                  Goluvnaya

                          construction   since 1979,       operation   operation    operation    since 1963,
                                         now under         since       since 2009   since 2011   now under
                                         rehabilitation    1986                                  rehabilitation
          Live Storage    10,300         4,200             87          18           5            4

3.3.10. Planning for the Shurob HPP began in the Soviet era to locate the HPP on the Vakhsh River
        between Rogun HPP and the Nurek reservoir. However, the scheme is on hold until Rogun HPP is
        constructed.
3.3.11. Although the Vakhsh River lies within Tajikistan, the Amu Darya basin is shared between
        Kyrgyzstan, Afghanistan, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan, and Uzbekistan and is one of the two main
        tributaries of the Aral Sea. On February 18, 1992, the Almaty Agreement (previously Protocol 566)
        was ratified to manage the water allocation and water-sharing principles between the nations of the
        Amu Darya basin, except for Afghanistan, which is not party to the Agreement.
         PROJECT HISTORY
3.3.12. The Project has a long history and was first proposed under the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics
        (USSR) in 1959. In 1978, a technical design of a 335 m dam and six power units of 630 MW each
        installed in an underground power plant was prepared. The final construction decision was granted
        in 1980, and the main construction work began in 1982. Construction of the main components of the
        underground works and a 45 m hydraulic cofferdam progressed until 1990 when work was
        suspended due to political changes and the independence of Tajikistan. In 1993, there was a flood
        during which the diversion tunnels were blocked, and the cofferdam was destroyed.
3.3.13. The Government of Tajikistan announced the construction of the Project to resume in May 2008.
        Limited construction and maintenance activities were resumed until 2011, when a Techno-Economic
        Assessment Study (TEAS) was written by Coyne et Bellier, which was reviewed by the 2014 Dam
        Safety Panel of Experts (DSPoE) and found to be of sound design. An overview of previous studies
        and key findings is presented in Volume 1, Chapter 1 of this ESIA.
3.3.14. The Project is being constructed in two stages (Figure 3-3), with details of each stage discussed in
        Sections 3.4 and 3.7. Stage one construction commenced in 2014 and is almost complete.

3.3.17. After the Project receives financing in 2025, early works contracts will be terminated, and all works
        will be completed under EPC contracts based on FIDIC (Fédération Internationale des Ingénieurs-
        Conseils) and World Bank Standard Procurement Documents (SPDs).
3.3.18. Most of the existing ongoing accommodations and work camps for existing contractors are on
        construction site 1 (see Figure 3-5). This area will be flooded in 2026/2027, before which
        accommodation and work camps will be relocated to new areas within Construction Site 2 and Area
        5.

3.4.     PROJECT COMPONENTS
3.4.1.   This section describes the built components of the Project and the areas immediately surrounding the dam and reservoir. The Project components
         considered in the ESIA are summarized in Table 3-7.

         Table 3-7 - Rogun HPP Project Components

          Component                 Description

          Lot 1

          Powerhouse and Turbines    Total Installed Capacity: 3,780 MW.
                                     Number of Turbine Units: 6.
                                     Located in a sedimentary complex constituted of sandstone and siltstone.
                                     Powerhouse Cavern: 220 x 20 x 70 m.
                                     Transformer Cavern: 220 x 20 x 70 m.
                                    Powerhouse and transformer caverns will have 200 m vertical by 800 m horizontal by 12 m diameter tunnel/shaft for ventilation. At
                                    present, temporary equipment provides ventilation, which is poor. Under the Lot 4 contract, permanent forced-air equipment will be
                                    installed (forced-air in, natural exhaust), expected completion in approximately 2025.

          Lot 2

          Dam (Stage 2) and         The Stage 2 Dam consists of an earth-rock-filled dam. The cofferdams form part of the main embankment body. It has an inclined
          Reservoir                 loam core, with upstream and downstream fine and coarse filter zones (or a transition zone below the upstream minimum operating
                                    level). The shells consist of granular alluvium with rockfill placed on the outside of the shell. Information regarding the dam is
                                    presented in Section 3.7.
                                     Total land area under the reservoir – 16,823.25 hectares:
                                     Agricultural – 8,036.23 hectares.
                                     Irrigated – 3,43.67 hectares.

                            Forests and bushes – 727.26 hectares.

 Crushing Facilities       There are six crushing facilities currently under operation:
                            One closed area crusher facility with 1 million m3 / year aggregate production capacity.
                            Five open area crusher facilities with an aggregate production of between 12 to 15 m3/month each.


                           RCC Crushing Plant – Left Bank Upstream ~1198 m asl
                           The system is fed by alluvium type material (Labydors) taken from stockpile area at Quarry No.15. The Crushing Plant has been set
                           with screens of predetermined dimensions suitable to produce aggregates [0-5 mm], [5-20 mm] and [20-40 mm] as designed for the
                           RCC mixes to be used for the Main Dam RCC Pad.


                           Main mechanical components of the Crushing Plant are:
                            Feeder – METSO Nordberg VF661 – Initial Screening.
                            Jaw Crusher - METSO Nordberg C140 – Primary Crushing.
                            2-story screening - METSO Nordberg CVB2661 – Primary Crushing.
                           Cone-shaped Crusher - METSO Nordberg GP500S – Secondary Crushing.3-Story Screening - METSO Nordberg TS5.3 (x2) –
                           Secondary Crushing.

 Lot 3

 Headrace Tunnels          The primary purpose of headrace tunnels is to convey water from the reservoir or intake structure to the powerhouse, to drive turbines
                           to produce electricity. Once all six turbine units are operational in the powerhouse, the maximum discharge capacity is 1,626 m 3/s.
                           Construction of the tunnels involves drilling and blasting with the excavated tunnel lined with reinforcing steel bar grids and concrete.

 Diversion Tunnels         Diversion tunnels for diverting the river during construction or repair of other hydraulic structures are part of the hydropower complex.



Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                                                                                PUBLIC |
Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                                                                                      June 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                                                                             Page | 3-16
 Component                 Description

                           There are currently four diversion tunnels operating on site known as DT-1, DT-2, DT-3 and DT-4.
                           DT-1 and DT-2 will work under a maximum head of 120 m, and their maximum discharge should preferably not exceed 1,600
                           m3/s/tunnel.
                           A maximum head of 120 m is tolerated in tunnels during the construction period. This value can be overpassed by 30 m, i.e., 150 m, in
                           extreme conditions such as high floods or seismic events. The design of the dam and reservoir accounts for the possibility for this to
                           occur such that permanent structures are not compromised.
                           Construction of the tunnels is as described for the headrace tunnels.

 Lot 4

 Spillways                 High Level Outlet 1 (HLO-1)
                           HLO-1 spillway consists of an outlet, 600 m long and 12 m in diameter pressurized tunnel equipped with maintenance gate and radial
                           service gate followed by a 200 m long free surface flow tunnel before entering a vertical vortex shaft which is followed by a 350 m long
                           tunnel.
                           High Level Outlet 2 (HLO-2)
                           HLO-2 spillway consists of an outlet, some 310 m long 12 m in diameter pressurized tunnel equipped with maintenance gate and
                           radial service gate which is followed by a 14 m diameter 3.2 km long free surface flow tunnel that is followed by a chute type structure.
                           HLO-1 and HLO-2 remain in operation until they lose functionality due to siltation and are replaced by Remote Tunnels Spillways.
                           Remote Tunnel Spillways (RTS)
                           The RTS features an ogee-shaped outlet structure equipped with radial gate, followed by a roughly 4 km long horseshoe-shaped
                           tunnel, 13 m in width and height, that is terminating in a stilling basin.
                           The discharge capacity per RTS is higher than HLOs 2,400 m3/s, to cope with Probable Maximum Floods (PMF). All flood releasing
                           facilities are being constructed to the right abutment with dimensions of about 2 km long and 1.5 km wide.

 Other Project Components – responsibilities shared between EPC Contractors

 Transportation Routes     A single gate provides access to the Project site.




Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                                                                              PUBLIC |

 Component                 Description

                           There are approximately 70 km of underground transport routes; most of which will be converted to water tunnels or plugged as the
                           reservoir rises. Six km of underground roads will remain after construction.
                           Currently there are 26 km of surface roads, most unpaved. Approximately 5-6 km of this will remain after construction.
                           Concrete and other supplies arrive from Dushanbe on public roads.

 Power Supply (Back up)    A back-up power supply is necessary beyond the construction phase for electricity usage for operational site facilities. A direct power
                           source from the 6 x 630 MW turbines will be used as power supply in normal circumstances. Back-up power is currently supplied by
                           the existing turbines 5 and 6, diesel generators and a direct power source from Nurek HPP.

 Current accommodation     As of May 2025, approximately 20,000 people are engaged for construction activities with peak employment likely during the period
                           between 2025-2028. Of the current workforce, approximately 2,800 are office workers, made up of administrative, manager and
                           support workers (cleaners, canteens, shops) who work 8am to 5pm, approximately 14,000 work 12-hour shifts in construction, on a 15
                           day on/off schedule with some night shifts (depending on the work program and individual contractors). Each shift includes 1 hour for
                           lunch.
                           Except for some WeBuild employees and a few small camps, all accommodation is on Construction Site 1 (and will only be used as
                           long as the non-EPC contractors are on site. Current accommodation sites will be demolished and rehabilitated before being flooded
                           in 2026/2027.
                           Buses transport workers to and from their workplaces to their accommodation on Construction Site 1. Most of the contractors have
                           their own buses, which collect workers from designated locations around half an hour before the start of their shift and transfer them to
                           their construction site area. The designated pickup locations vary depending on the contractor, but usually, contractors collect workers
                           in either Rogun City or by the worker accommodation camps. Approximately 300-400 live outside of Rogun city within 30km and are
                           transported to and from their villages.
                           Some contractors, workers and office staff hire accommodation in Rogun City (approximately 2 km uphill from the main worker
                           accommodation area). Rogun JSC provides accommodation for their staff in four tower block buildings in Rogun City.
                           Each contractor is responsible for collecting sanitary waste, with liquids from sewage going into sumps which drain into the ground
                           and solids periodically removed for disposal to the Rogun Municipal Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP). Rogun JSC is in the
                           process of designing and installing water treatment facilities for all of Construction Camp 1.
                           Photos of on-site accommodation camps are presented in Figure 3-6, Figure 3-7 and Figure 3-8.

 Future accommodation      Before Construction Camp 1 is flooded in 2026/2027, all four EPC contractors will design and construct accommodations and other

 Medical Facilities        Most major contractors provide medical facilities on-site. For the contractors that do not have their own medical facility, there are
                           central Rogun Medical Centers that can be utilized by all workers. For any serious injuries or operations, workers are transported off-
                           site to the closest medical centers in Rogun City or to a hospital in Dushanbe.
