 Borrow Pits / Quarries /   Three quarries are currently operational on the Rogun site by Lot 2 contractor WeBuild, but under the subcontractor TGEM working
 Batching plants            within the quarries.
                            There are currently up to 6 batching plants operating on site, these are managed by WeBuild, TGEM, Ariana, MEZ, and TGM.
                            Quarry 15
                            Extracted material for use in construction of the dam wall such as sand and gravel – alluvium sedimentation.
                            As of November 2023, a total of 46 million m3 of material was extracted. Of this, 20 million m3 has been used (mostly in construction
                            of the dam). The remaining 26 million m3 of extracted material is stored in three open stockpiles, comprising of sand and gravel
                            (alluvium material). This is transported using a 7 km covered conveyor to the batching plants.
                            Another 5 million m3 was extracted from the Right Bank area until May 2024. Approximately, 5 million m 3 of extracted material will be
                            moved to the Left Bank stockpiles, as the Quarry 15 and stockpiles will be inundated by the reservoir with no rehabilitation.
                            During the Soviet era, approximately 10 million m3 of stone, sand and gravel material was extracted from Quarry 15a, this area is now
                            inundated by the reservoir and crushed for use in the batching plant. Five million m 3 of material remains and is stored in two stockpiles
                            on the Left and Right Bank and is planned for use in the batching plants.

Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                                                                                      June 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                                                                             Page | 3-20
 Component                 Description

                           Quarry 17
                           Quarry 17 is extracting Loam (clay and sand) material and is located downstream of the Dam, with use of a small stockpile area. A
                           total of 20 million m3 of material will be extracted, of which; 7 million m3 of fine material for dam core backfilling, and the remaining 13
                           million m3 used for a later stage of dam construction.
                           As of November 2023, a total of 1 million m3 of fine material has been extracted at Quarry 17. This is expected to be operational until
                           2029/2030. Quarry 17 is not within the inundation zone for the reservoir, and there are no current plans to rehabilitate or use the land
                           beyond operation.
                           Quarry 26
                           Quarry 26 is located on the Kishrog River right bank in the downstream area of the dam, 2.5 km from the site. Extracting rock mass
                           material for use in the dam construction is undertaken via blasting, and is divided into 3 sections:
                            Quarry 26 is no longer operational.
                            Quarry 26A is currently operational.
                            Quarry 26B designated for any additional material if required.
                           A total of 15 million m3 of material to be extracted from Quarry 26A, with approximately 3 million m3 material already extracted.
                           Quarry 26 is outside the inundation zone, and there are no current plans for future uses or rehabilitation of the area.
                           The working Quarry 26A has a height of up to 210 m. The quarry is planned to be developed by 10 m high benches and a slope of 70º
                           between the 6 m wide berms. Material is quarried from borrow pits using blasting techniques.

 Stockpiles and material   There are several stockpiles on site used to store extracted and processed materials. Locations of stockpiles are shown in Figure 3-5.
 transfer
                           Materials from upstream quarries and stockpiles are transported to the dam and to stockpiles downstream of the dam by a 7 km
                           covered conveyor. A new 8-9 km covered conveyor (called the ‘flying belt’) transports materials from the downstream quarry and
                           stockpiles to the dam site. The existing conveyor has reduced traffic by about 100 trucks per hour and the new conveyor will also
                           reduce traffic further.
                           Spoil from the ongoing tunnelling which is not suitable for use in the dam is transported by truck to piles in locations within the future
                           reservoir footprint that will be flooded. A spoil heap has been established in Area 5 to take aggregate that is out of geotechnical
                           specification.

 Fuel Storage Areas        Rogun JSC has an old storage area in Construction Site 1 for its own and minor contractor use and includes:
                            Seven underground diesel storage reservoirs/tanks.
                            Three above-ground diesel and petrol storage reservoirs/tanks.
                            The major contractors each have their own fuel storage areas.
                           Selected photos of the Fuel Storage Areas are presented in Figure 3-7.
                           These areas will be rehabilitated prior to being flooded.

 Concrete Batching         1,000 tonnes of cement arrive per day from Dushanbe which equates to about 30 trucks per day.
 Facilities
                           There are eight concrete batching plants on-site with an effective capacity of 40 m3/h.
                           There are approximately 25 concrete mixers with an average capacity of 6 m 3/h.
                           A disused but not decommissioned batching plant is located in Construction Site 2.

 Power Supply (non-grid)   Power supply is necessary throughout the construction phase for electricity usage on site. Prior to 2018, a direct power source from
                           diesel generators and a direct power source to Nurek HPP was used. Following the completion of the Stage 1 Dam in 2018, the
                           operation of the existing Unit 5 and 6 turbines allow direct power from Rogun HPP, although for some construction activities, diesel
                           generators are required as detailed below:

 Waste Storage Areas       As construction works are undertaken in four main work packages, each contractor provides its own waste storage areas and disposal
                           facilities.



Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                                                                        PUBLIC |
Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                                                                              June 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                                                                     Page | 3-23
 Component                 Description

                           LOT2 Construction – WeBuild Contractor
                           Facilities for waste storage areas within LOT2 construction are managed by the contractor WeBuild, and include areas at the tank
                           farm, maintenance yard and garage, warehouses, crushed-ore storage, and a washing facility.
                           The domestic waste storage area is constructed of concrete brick, fitted with concrete flooring, however, there is no roofing, and hence
                           no protection from rain, wind or snow.
                           Hazardous waste is stored in the workshop service area prior to uplift by the waste contractor. The storage area is constructed from
                           concrete and is covered with corrugated roofing.
                           Waste is stored within open-top intermediate bulk containers (IBCs) or on top of wooden pallets within the concrete cubicles. Signs
                           and labelling of waste are noted within the storage area.
                           LOT3 Construction – TGEM Contractor
                           The storage area shown in Figure 3-4 is for uncontaminated construction waste
                           Hazardous waste is stored in metal barrels fitted with lids, which in turn are kept inside larger containers.
                           LOT4 Early Works Construction - Sadd Ariana Co. Contractor
                           Hazardous wastes are removed from site by a waste contractor for disposal at a certified waste disposal site; and
                           Septic tanks are installed on site for the collection of sewage waste.
                           Non-hazardous waste / construction debris
                           All contractors reportedly had their non-hazardous wastes transported by licensed hauler to licensed landfills
                           To the north of Construction Site 1 rock rejects interspersed with a number of small loads of construction debris are being stored.
                           Rogun JSC is in the process of designing a repository for construction debris and other non-hazardous waste.
                           Downstream of Construction Site 5 is a small expanse of rock piles interspersed with truckloads of construction debris.
                           Hazardous waste
                           A licensed hauler takes away hazardous waste for recycling (batteries and some other wastes), for treatment or disposal at facilities in
                           Dushanbe.
                           Medical waste

                                     In the past, medical waste from the HPP’s central clinic went to Obigarm, where there was no proper management, so it was burned
                                     with local medical waste. Since early 2023 the Rogun hospital has had a new UNICEF-financed facility for medical wastes including
                                     an ampule crusher and dry sump for crushed-glass storage and an incinerator with 1m3 capacity and burn temperature of 700-1000
                                     degrees centigrade. Medical waste accumulates and is burned every one or two days, and medical waste from the Rogun HPP site is
                                     brought three to four times per week. Rogun contributes only a small part of what they receive.

          Sewage Disposal (non-      Each contractor’s sewage waste is managed in ponds / sumps. Liquid is generally infiltrated into the ground and solids are reportedly
          municipal)                 removed by a hauler for treatment in the Rogun Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP).
                                     A treatment system plant to treat sanitary water from Construction Camp 1 is now operational. It will be removed before flooding in
                                     2027 and relocated for use elsewhere. The HPP also has a small plant that will be sufficient through to full operation.

          Left Bank Road             The filling of the reservoir will submerge the unpaved road connecting 15 villages in the Nurabad district and one village in the Rogun
                                     district on the left bank; affecting a population of approximately 8,000 people. In addition, seven pedestrian bridges, including vehicle
                                     bridges, will also be submerged by 2028. The loss of the bridges and road will result in loss of access to the facilities and amenities
                                     that are available in the larger villages located on the right bank, including access to hospitals.
                                     To alleviate the severance, reinstatement of the left bank road will be funded by the AIIB. The works will be managed by the DFZ and
                                     constructed by the Ministry of Transport and local contractors. The reinstatement will involve a 55-km bare earth road to join the new
                                     International Bridge currently under construction.
                                     Figure 3-10Error! Not a valid result for table. shows the route of off-site roads and bridges, including the right-and left bank roads.
                                     An assessment of the potential impacts of the left bank road inundation and reinstatement is considered in Volume 2, Annex 17-3:
                                     Severance Impacts of this ESIA and will be managed as described in Volume 3, A11: Ancillary Works Plan.

3.5.     RESETTLEMENT
3.5.1.   Resettlement is managed by the Directorate of Flooding Zone (DFZ) and, to date, has been funded
         by the Government of the Republic of Tajikistan. In future, some resettlement activities may be
         financed by one or more International Lenders and, as such, the program is considered a core
         Project component.
3.5.2.   The effects of resettlement are considered in Volume 1, Chapter: 17: Socio-economics of this
         ESIA. The Project funding to households to be resettled comprises a grant issued by DFZ to
         facilitate the establishment of new dwellings in resettlement areas and reestablish the PAP’s primary
         source of income. A loan from either the Ministry of Labor or Ministry of Agriculture is also available
         if required by the PAP to enhance their level of income or access additional resources to improve
         their livelihood. Livelihood restoration for PAPs in the form of apprenticeships are also being funded
         by the Project.
3.5.3.   The physical infrastructure required for the establishment of new cities or the expansion of existing
         towns is funded nationally, is constructed to National Requirements and assessed for compliance
         with Tajik law by the Committee for Environmental Protection. Infrastructure provision and
         replacement is managed by DFZ and has generally been adequate to maintain and improve living
         standards for most resettled households. Generally, PAPs construct their own homes on a
         designated plot of land.
3.5.4.   Resettlement is planned in phases that are aligned with the inundation levels of the reservoir.
         According to data from DFZ as of October 2024, resettlement of households in the flooding zone is
         due for completion by 2032. This will include 69 villages from the Rogun, Nurobod, and Rasht
         districts affecting an estimated 50,267 PAPs.
3.5.5.   Table 3-8 presents resettlement figures by Resettlement Action Plan (RAP) 1 and impoundment
         levels and Table 3-9 presents a summary of villages during past, ongoing, and future resettlement
         phases.

         PHASE 1 RESETTLEMENT
3.5.6.   Resettlement of eight villages, 326 households and 2,697 people was guided by RAP 1 that was
         developed in 2014, and a Livelihood Restoration Plan 1 (LRP 1) created in 2015. A World Bank
         audit of Phase 1 resettlement concluded that the program met the principles established in 2014
         RPF, 2014 RAP 1, and LRP 1 and was in line with the World Bank requirements (WB OP 4.12).
         PHASE 2 RESETTLEMENT
3.5.7.   Initially, all resettlement after completion of RAP 1 was referred to as Phase 2. DFZ subsequently
         developed RAP 2 to cover the period 2018-2026 and is in the process of implementing that RAP.
         This RAP 2 for 2018-2026 is being updated as part of the ESIA process and will be compliant with
         the new ESS5 requirements.
3.5.8.   To guide ongoing and future resettlement, a Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF), including a
         Livelihood Restoration Framework, were adopted in 2024. The RPF calls for at least three additional
         RAPs to guide the remainder of the resettlement program which will be completed by 2032.
3.5.9.   RAP 2 is in the process of being implemented. It covers about 1,710 households (more than 16,919
         people), of which approximately 500 households (over 4,600 people) have already been resettled,
         and the remainder are due for resettlement by end of 2025.
3.5.10. The updated RAP 2 will apply retroactively to households resettled since 2017 and to those that will
        be resettled through 2026. Relocation of villages under RAP 2 is illustrated by Figure 3-11.

         Figure 3-11 – Resettlement of Villages Under RAP 2 (Source: DFZ data)




         MULTIPLE DISPLACEMENT IMPACTS
3.5.11. People in some villages that will be affected by resettlement for the Project, may also be impacted
        by other projects, including:
               Elements of the CASA-1000 rural electrification project, which required land to be acquired in
                Tursunzoda and Rudaki;
               M41 Obigarm-Nurobod road realignment project – (ADB & EBRD) required land acquisition
                in Rogun city and Nurobod district;
               Dushanbe-Tursunzoda-Uzbekistan border road project (ADB) required land acquisition, in
                particular, in Tursunzoda City; and
               Dushanbe-Bokhtar road project (ADB & JICA) required land acquisition in Rudaki district.
3.5.12. The effects of multiple displacement will be assessed within this ESIA where information is
        available. Where applicable projects may be developed in the future there will be a requirement to
        ensure these impacts are assessed in future ESIAs for these specific projects.
Figure 3-12 - Areas to be Resettled
         IDENTIFICATION AND PREPARATION OF RESETTLEMENT SITES
3.5.13. The State Committee on Land Management and Geodesy are responsible for identifying suitable
        land for resettlement sites. Meaningful engagement is undertaken with PAPs to understand their
        needs and desires when identifying such locations. The land is requested via the local authorities
        and must be approved by the Jamoats and local community before resettlement can proceed.
3.5.14. Before the land acquisition for the development of resettlement sites can begin, the site must be
        approved by government ministries and agencies with appropriate competences (e.g., the Ministry
        of Environmental Protection confirms that the site is not vulnerable to, and will not cause, significant
        environmental risks). Once all necessary approvals have been granted, relocation to the site can
        begin.
3.5.15. Resettlement sites are selected in areas where the earthquake risk is low and where there is
        available agricultural and pastureland of sufficient quality to prevent pressure on local resources due
        to the additional population. All new and existing residents have the right to unrestricted use of
        common pasture lands. As an example, in Tursunzoda, additional common pastureland for host
        communities was also provided to accommodate pre-existing population growth. The initial
        allocation of land was inadequate resulting in the need for additional land to be made available.
3.5.16. Under RAP 2, five new resettlement sites have been identified in Nurobod region in June 2023,
        besides those already in use. In December 2024 these are allocated plots which are uninhabited
        and unnamed. All new sites consist primarily of agricultural fields and pastureland. These new sites
        will accommodate approximately 2,600 land plots (320 hectares).
         PUBLIC INFRASTRUCTURE AND SERVICES IN RESETTLEMENT SITES
3.5.17. DFZ’s mandate is to ensure that the construction of all public infrastructure (i.e., water supply, health
        centers, education facilities, roads, electricity lines, etc.) and utility services in resettlement sites is
        complete before settlers permanently move in. There have been instances when households
        relocated before all infrastructure was in place.
3.5.18. Construction of these facilities is the responsibility of the relevant ministry. When resettled people
        move to host communities, the existing infrastructure is expanded or improved. Connection to
        utilities is provided at no cost at new resettlement sites. New infrastructure is required to meet
        national building standards.
3.6.     ASSOCIATED FACILITIES
3.6.1.   As defined in WB ESS1, the term “Associated Facilities” means facilities or activities that are not
         funded as part of the project and are:
               (a) directly and significantly related to the project;
               (b) carried out, or planned to be carried out, contemporaneously with the project; and
               (c) necessary for the project to be viable and would not have been constructed, expanded or
                conducted if the project did not exist.
3.6.2.   For facilities or activities to be associated facilities, they must meet all three criteria. The associated
         facilities applicable to the Project are described in Table 3-10.
3.6.3.   For the Rogun HPP, the associated facilities are directly and significantly related to the Project and
         would not have been constructed without the Project being developed. These facilities are either
         under construction or will be constructed in the future as part of the overarching Project
         development.
3.6.4.   Associated facilities that are identified in future may require an assessment in compliance with
         International Standards prior to the commencement of construction depending on the funding entity.
         Where an ESIA is undertaken, the Rogun HPP should be included in the cumulative effects
         assessment.
3.6.5.   As indicated in Table 3-10, ESIA have been done for some of the associated facilities; but not for
         others.
Table 3-10 - Details of the Associated Facilities

3.7.     DAM CONSTRUCTION
3.7.1.   Excavation on both the left and right banks, along with the elevation of the dam, requires the
         mobilization of a sizable fleet of vehicles and construction equipment. This fleet is required to
         relocate substantial quantities of material, including spoil and construction materials for the dam, as
         well as for transport workers and other construction supplies.

         and commissioning of all turbines will be done progressively with increasing dam height and

         the final units (1 and 2) installed at 1200 masl in 2028.

3.8.     COMMISSIONING
3.8.1.   Commissioning of the operational components will be conducted by the Lot Construction contractors
         prior to handover to the Rogun JSC operations team.
3.8.2.   Commissioning will entail a stepwise series of tests to ensure components are functioning within
         design specifications including:
              Pre-commissioning tests - tests undertaken prior to any component being flooded;
              Pre-commissioning tests - tests undertaken after a component being flooded;
              Initial test run; and
              Full/Final test run.
3.8.3.   Commissioning will comprise a series of checks to sign off the grouting works, confirm that all anti-
         corrosion painting has been completed and that. Readiness tests prior to flooding of components
         such as the upstream and downstream feeder tunnels headraces, will be undertaken, including a
         check on the cleanliness, to ensure all components are free from obstructions and debris and that
         water can flow freely.
3.8.4.   Once complete, all mechanical operating equipment, such as brakes, pressure units and runners,
         will be tested for operational readiness.
3.8.5.   Ventilation equipment will be tested and calibrated to ensure operational functionality prior to any
         turbines being operated.
3.8.6.   Redundant tunnels to be flooded will be completed and sealed prior to commissioning and tunnels
         which are for emergency use will be tested for operation of gates, penstocks, etc., to ensure
         functionality.
3.8.7.   Once all pre-filling tests have been completed the tunnels to be flooded will be filled gradually to
         allow for the testing of emergency equipment for closure of systems before being filled, and to
         ensure leak detection systems are operating within design parameters. Tunnels will be also checked
         for leaks and deformation using mechanical means during this filling period. The turbines will be also
         checked and tested to ensure they are operational and produce the required outputs.
3.8.8.   Once filling is complete an initial test run of the system can be undertaken using manual control
         mechanisms to ensure components are operating satisfactorily before automation. Once complete,
         the system can be synchronized with the national electricity grid.
3.8.9.   A full automated test run can be initiated, to ensure all systems are fully operational at commercial
         levels prior to handover.

3.9.     RESERVOIR FILLING
3.9.1.   The filling of the reservoir has been optimized based on the following mandatory criteria (TEAS
         2014):
             1. The discharges from the Nurek HPP cannot change from the existing quantities.
             2. The quantity of water used to fill the Rogun reservoir must remain within the annual allocation of
                water to the Vakhsh River as agreed by the Basin Water Organization (BVO “Amu Darya”) of
                the ICWC and cannot exceed the agreements made within the Almaty Agreement (Protocol
             566) and the Nukus declaration (Annual Maximum allocation for Tajikistan is 9.5 million m3 /
             year (0.095 km3) from the Amu Darya basin). The justification for this figure as well as further
             information on flow rates in the Nurek HPP is set out in Volume 1: Chapter 13: Water, of this
             ESIA.
3.9.2.   The estimated time to fill the Rogun reservoir is between nine and 22 years based on a simulation
         for low and high fill scenarios respectively that is described in Volume 2, Annex 13-3: Water
         Resources Modelling of this ESIA. The staged filling of the Rogun reservoir will be undertaken over
         an extended period as the annual volume of water available for filling the reservoir is dependent on
         the differential between the water allocation to Tajikistan as per Protocol 566 and water
         consumption, especially during periods of lower precipitation and snowpack formation.
3.9.3.   The Republic of Tajikistan intends to make use of its full water allocation of 9.5 km3 per year in terms
         of Protocol 955 to fill the Rogun reservoir.
3.9.4.   A minimum environmental water requirement (EWR) was calculated to ensure that sufficient water is
         released from Rogun HPP to maintain intake flows to Nurek HPP to generate electricity and
         maintain ecological integrity of the aquatic ecosystem in the reach of the Vakhsh River between
         Rogun HPP and Nurek HPP. The simulation showed that, during the filling of the Rogun reservoir,
         sufficient water is released from Rogun HPP to meet the minimum EWR and the EWR modelling is
         discussed in Volume 2, Annex 31-1: Minimum Environmental Flows of this ESIA.
3.9.5.   Once the Rogun HPP is fully operational, the Rogun reservoir will continue to rise following the end
         of construction. As the dam reaches staged levels, the reservoir will rise each summer, and recede
         each winter, rising higher each year until FSL 1290 masl, which under average flow regimes is
         expected to occur in 2038. The incremental filling of Rogun reservoir is shown in Table 3-15.

         Table 3-15 - Reservoir Inundation Levels


          Date               Impounding Elevation (m asl)          Storage Volume (km3)

          1 Jan 2025         1,075                                 0.56

          15 Oct 2026        1,100                                 0.86

          15 Aug 2028        1,175                                 2.68

          15 June 2032       1 230                                 6.16

          15 Nov 2038        1 290                                 13.30

         *Reservoir Inundation Levels from Volume 2, Annex 13-3; Water Resource Modelling of this ESIA, and
         subject to sufficient progress of construction of the dam embankment and infrastructure.
3.9.6.   The pattern of the monthly average flow and range of flow is shown in the Box and Whisker plot in
         Figure 3-15. The plot shows that flows during the winter period do not vary significantly and range
         from 0.3 to 0.9 km3/month. The annual streamflow total is variable ranging from 13 km3/yr to 26
         km3/yr with an average of 20 km3/yr (634 m3/s). The highest streamflow occurs during the month of
         July.
         Figure 3-15 - Box and Whisker Plot of monthly Streamflow Record at Rogun HPP




         ELECTRICITY GENERATION
3.9.7.   During the reservoir filling period, the Rogun HPP will operate as a storage HPP to provide a
         constant and reliable supply of electricity that is always available, especially during adverse weather
         conditions, to meet grid baseload demand and is not subject to interruptions, other than for
         scheduled maintenance.
3.9.8.   In summary, water accumulated in the reservoir is channeled into the penstock that funnels the
         water to spin the turbines that power a generator to produce electricity.
3.9.9.   The total actual electricity production of the Stage 1 Dam between November 2018 and October
         2024 is presented in Table 3-16. The zero output in June 2024 was due to mud infiltration of the
         powerhouse following a mud slide upstream of the powerhouse. The zero output in August 2024
         was due to annual maintenance.
3.9.10. The installation and commissioning of the turbines is being completed incrementally. Since late
        2019, turbines unit No. 5 and No.6 have operated at reduced capacity using temporary runners2.
        The runners will be replaced in 2024 or early 2025, increasing the capacity of the existing turbines
        from 400 MW to 630 MW each. The current regime of turbines unit No. 5 and No.6 operating at a
        reduced capacity allows for a monthly average release of flow downstream of the Rogun HPP based
        on the requirements of the Nurek HPP operating regime and the maintenance of outflows for the




         2 Runners are the rotating element of the Francis turbine that convert the energy of the flowing water into

             mechanical energy.
cascade that vary with the seasons.

3.10. JOINT OPERATION
3.10.1. Rogun HPP will operate as a storage hydropower facility and the key assumption for the operational
        design is to maintain the existing outflows from Nurek HPP to ensure compliance with the
        transboundary agreements, and any change to this regime can only be undertaken in agreement
        with neighboring countries.
3.10.2. The joint operation of Nurek HPP and Rogun HPP is planned to not impact flows downstream of
        Nurek HPP. The seasonal water balance between the two reservoirs has been modelled (Refer to
        Volume 2, Annex 13-3 (Water Resource Modelling) based on the annual average statistics and
        maintenance of the downstream regime from Nurek HPP. Rogun HPP will effectively be the
        regulating hydropower facility for the cascade and, as such, Nurek HPP will no longer experience
        significant seasonal drawdown. This will be achieved by the storage of water in the Rogun reservoir
        during the summer months that will be released during the winter months to achieve an annual
        seasonal shift of up to 4.5 km3 of water.
3.10.3. The water resources modelling showed that flows released from Rogun reservoir during the summer
        months are reduced as water is stored in the reservoir to maintain the releases during winter. During
        the low-flow winter months, the discharge from Rogun reservoir is higher than the natural flows into
        Rogun reservoir.
        Figure 3-16 - Plot of Average Monthly Inflows and Outflows from Rogun Reservoir




3.10.4. Although Rogun HPP will act as the main regulating reservoir, the Nurek HPP will retain some
        regulating capacity to ensure the cascade can operate effectively, as well as ensure maintenance of
        the outflows from the Nurek dam downstream. The minimum reservoir levels for both Rogun HPP
        and Nurek HPP when operating together are 1185 m asl and 857 m asl respectively.
3.10.5. The Rogun reservoir has been designed to accommodate the Probable Maximum Flood (PMF)
        having a 100-year return period and the operation of the release facilities would allow the safe
        management of the PMF, protecting the downstream riparian areas. The diversion and spillway
        structures have been designed to protect the dam wall regardless of the construction stage and are
        described in the TEAS report (2014):
          The cofferdam crest at 1035 masl protects the construction site with diversion tunnel (DT) 1 and
            DT2 diverting the river flow. The capacity of the tunnels is 2900 m3/s with a water level at 1033
            masl;
          The Stage 1 dam protects the site and DT1, DT2 and DT3 divert the river with a water level at
            1100 masl and the combined capacity of the three tunnels is 7100 m3/s;
          At reservoir levels between 1110 masl and 1145 masl, DT2 and DT3 divert the river and the DT2
            discharge can be limited to 1800 m3/s by closing some of its gates;
          At reservoir levels between 1145 and 1185 masl, DT2, DT3 and the remote spillway divert the
            river;
          Above 1185 masl, the river diversion and flood discharge is ensured by final spillways: OP3; run-
            of-stream (RS) and open spillway structure (OSS) that have a combined discharge capacity of
            7100 m3/s at water elevation 1290 masl.
3.10.6. The potential for excessive water release from the Project due to dam failure or overtopping was
        analyzed in a dam break analysis undertaken in 2018 by Stucky for the Stage 1 dam wall height of
        1100 masl. A further dam break analysis will be done for the final dam wall height of 1290 masl.
3.10.7. The DSPoE report (Dam Safety Panel of Experts (DSPoE), 2023) concluded that the maximum
        outflow discharge from Rogun HPP is 5400 m3/s to allow safe passage of flows through downstream
        dams. The proposed infrastructure to alleviate the flows of a PMF include a spillway on the left bank
        and potential right and left bank bypass tunnels; and the required discharge capacity will be
        confirmed one the configuration is finalized.
3.10.8. The sensitivity of the PMF flood discharge to sedimentation of the reservoir has also been
        highlighted by the DSPoE in that a 10% loss of reservoir capacity of sedimentation results in a PMF
        water level at 1297 masl and a 20% loss of capacity results in a water level at the top of the dam
        wall.
3.10.9. These risks are further considered in Volume 1, Chapter 23: Major Accidents and Disasters of
        this ESIA.
         ELECTRICITY GENERATION
3.10.10. As a storage hydropower scheme, the Rogun HPP is designed to reliably generate a guaranteed
         minimum output, that is generated with a specified level of availability, especially during winter when
         electricity demand is high. If necessary, it can also operate as a peaking plant, adjusting output to
         meet fluctuating demand, given the ability to shut down and start up the generating turbines.
3.10.11. By 2039 the Rogun HPP is expected to generate electricity more than 17 billion kWh per year.
         Rogun HPP will significantly enhance security of supply in Tajikistan by generating an average of
         30% of domestic electricity demand between 2020 and 2050.
3.10.12. The Rogun HPP substation has capacity to regulate the maximum 3,780 MW output of the Rogun
         HPP once fully operational.
3.10.13. Electricity generation that is surplus to domestic demand will be exported to neighboring countries
         via the CASA-1000 project. The CASA-100 initiative aims to establish a unified market for electricity
         and allow for trade between Central and South Asia. Key infrastructure comprises:
          A 470 km, 500 kV AC line from Datka (Kyrgyz Republic) to Sugd (Tajikistan);
          A 115 km, 500 kV AC line in Tajikistan between Regar and Sangtuda;

          A 1300 MW AC-DC converter station at Sangtuda;

          An 800 km HVDC (High Voltage Direct Current) line from Sangtuda to Nowshera (Pakistan);

          A 1300 MW DC-AC converter station at Nowshera; and

          A back-to-back HVDC substation in Afghanistan.

3.10.14. Construction is expected to be completed by end 2025 and surplus summer hydroelectricity will be
         transmitted from Kyrgyzstan and Tajikistan to Afghanistan and Pakistan. The CASA-1000
         transmission lines will convey approximately 1300 MW of electricity.
3.10.15. Rogun HPP is expected to export approximately 70% of electricity generated at full capacity to
         neighboring countries, with Kazakhstan and Uzbekistan the primary export destinations. The
         intention is to replace fossil-fuel fired electricity generation with cleaner electricity sources.
         OPERATIONS AND MAINTENANCE ACTIVITIES
3.10.16. The expected operational lifespan of the hydropower plant is 110 years, dependent on the rate of
         sediment accumulation in the reservoir. The lifespan will include several major maintenance cycles
         that will provide the opportunity to upgrade the plant and equipment to increase the operational
         efficiency.
3.10.17. In general, activities undertaken on-site during the operational phase include:
          Monitoring of various parameters, including water levels, flow rates, temperatures, pressures, and
            generator performance;
          Inspection of turbines, generators, transformers, switchgear, and other critical components for
            wear, damage, or abnormal conditions;
          Checking the proper functioning of control systems; and

          Checking for blockages, debris, and structural damage in the intake area, penstock, and
            associated waterways.
3.10.18. Scheduled maintenance activities include:
          Undertaking routine maintenance based on manufacturer recommendations and historical data;

          Replacing worn or damaged components, such as bearings, seals, and control system parts;

          Identifying and resolving unexpected issues or equipment failures; and

          Implementing refurbishment or upgrade projects to extend the lifespan of plant and improve
            efficiency or capacity.
3.10.19. Rogun JSC will be the operator of the hydropower plant and will likely subcontract the operation and
         maintenance (O&M) of equipment and systems to specialist contractors.

      Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                  Page | 4-0
4       ANALYSIS OF ALTERNATIVES

4.1     INTRODUCTION
4.1.1   The WB ESF (2018), and specifically ESS1 Assessment and Management of Environmental and
        Social Risks and Impacts requires an ‘Analysis of Alternatives’ as part of an ESIA.
4.1.2   ESS1 requires the ‘Analysis of Alternatives’ to include:
         A systematic comparison of feasible alternatives to the proposed project site, technology, design,
          and operation, including the “without project” situation, in terms of their potential environmental
          and social impacts;
         An assessment of the feasibility of mitigating the environmental and social impacts; the capital
          and recurrent costs of alternative mitigation measures, their suitability under local conditions and
          the institutional training, and monitoring requirements for the alternative mitigation measures for
          all alternatives; and
         Quantification of the environmental and social impacts of the alternatives to the extent possible
          and the economic values where feasible.
4.1.3   The Analysis of Alternatives enables E&S risks to be considered in the early stages of project
        planning, as well as the later stages of design and implementation, and describes the basis of
        selection of preferred alternatives at all stages of the Project.
4.1.4   At the highest level, a "without project" scenario is included to identify the need for the Project as
        well as the key collateral benefits of the Project. Alternatives to the location and key design features
        of the Project, sources of energy technology for Tajikistan, dam wall height and reservoir inundation
        levels and the effect on the riparian environment, the location for resettlement of affected
        households and fill and Project costs are discussed to demonstrate the potential benefits as well as
        adverse effects the proposed alternatives may have on environmental and social receptors.
4.1.5   In 2011, the Government of Tajikistan used funding from the WB to conduct a Technical and
        Economic Assessment Study (TEAS) for the Project. The objectives of the study were to evaluate
        the technical and economic feasibility of different operational regimes and Project designs.
4.1.6   This chapter has been informed by the TEAS Phase II Report – Volume 1: Summary – Chapter 3.2:
        Selection of Site, Powerhouse, Dam Type and Alternative, April 2014; and the 2014 ESIA.
4.1.7   The ‘without project Scenario’ has been strengthened with recent information on the energy security
        and demand situation of Tajikistan and recent trends in fossil fuel usage and increased greenhouse
        gas (GHG) emissions.
4.1.8   The assessment presented in this chapter discusses the “without project” scenario and alternatives,
        including the Project location, type of structure and design with various dam heights. It provides a
        comparison of E&S impacts of various dam height alternatives and the economic and financial cost
        of the Project.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                    PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                          May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page | 4-1
4.2     WITHOUT PROJECT SCENARIO
4.2.1   The ‘without Project’ scenario typically considers the potential environmental and social adverse and
        beneficial effects of not developing the Project and retaining the site with original features.
4.2.2   The Rogun HPP Project has been in development since the 1970s in this location and therefore this
        assessment considers the scenario against the changes which have occurred to date, including the
        existing 630 MW HPP, the ongoing construction of the 3,780 MW HPP, the historical legacy and
        building facilities present on site and resettlement that has been completed to date. It considers the
        social, financial, and environmental effects of continuing the Project versus the effects of cancelling
        the Project.
4.2.3   Non-completion of the Project would require an estimated US$818 million of additional costs for
        decommissioning and mitigation of risks to the Vakhsh cascade, and additional US$2.8 billion to
        replace the generation from Rogun (to meet the domestic demand), and result in significant lost
        opportunities to decarbonize the power sectors in Central and South Asia, as well as forgone
        revenues from electricity exports.
4.2.4   The Government of Tajikistan is highly unlikely to abandon the Project and will continue constructing
        it with available resources, which is the practice since 2007. However, should decommissioning of
        the Project be required at this stage to due to insufficient funding, investments would be required to
        mitigate risks of damage of the civil works and generating plants downstream, consisting of:
         (i) at least US$500 million for Project decommissioning exclusive of the cost to safely
          decommission the 100 m-high dam; and
         (ii) US$318 million for additional spillways at Nurek HPP to improve flood protection of Vakhsh
          cascade.
4.2.5   In addition to the cost of decommissioning, capital investments of US$2.8 billion would be required
        in 2022-2040 to construct new generation capacity (primarily solar PV and wind) to replace energy
        expected from the Project. Moreover, abandoning the Project would potentially result in US$2.9
        billion lower export revenues for Tajikistan (compared to the best available alternatives of solar PV
        and wind). The revenues from exports are estimated to be higher compared to the scenario without
        the Project completion because the Project is estimated to have more competitive cost for firm
        baseload electricity and balancing services compared to replacement projects (solar PV and wind)
        that would need to be constructed to replace supply from Rogun.
        FLOOD PROTECTION
4.2.6   The existing HPPs in the Vakhsh river cascade are not designed to withstand the Probable
        Maximum Flood (PMF). The 2014 TEAS studies estimate that, if the Rogun HPP Project was not
        constructed, there would be significant investment required for immediate implementation of large
        upgrading works to the existing spillway structures of the cascade to achieve protection against the
        PMF. This is described in Volume 1, Chapter 3 (Project Description).
        SEDIMENT CONTROL
4.2.7   The Vakhsh River has a high sediment output. A benefit of the Project is that the retention of
        sediments by the Rogun reservoir effectively increases the life span of the downstream Nurek HPP
        reservoir by up to 110 years or more. Without the Project, the Nurek reservoir would fill with
        sediment and lose its useful capacity for power generation much sooner.



        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                         May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 4-2
         RELIEF IN DROUGHT YEARS
4.2.8    The Amu Darya River is one of the main tributaries feeding to Aral Sea, and the Vakhsh River is a
         tributary to the Amu Darya.
4.2.9    The Amu Darya catchment drains from five countries namely: Afghanistan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan,
         Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan. Approximately 80% of the Amu Darya annual flow volumes are
         estimated to be generated in Tajikistan, with approximately 8% generated in Afghanistan; 6% in
         Uzbekistan; 4% in Turkmenistan; and 2% in Kyrgyzstan (Pöyry, 2014). These countries have an
         intercountry agreement which allocates Tajikistan an annual share of Amu Darya’s water.
4.2.10   The Project will use Tajikistan’s allocation of water from the Amu Darya, as provided in the Nukus
         Declaration, Protocol 566 agreement until the reservoir reaches its full capacity of FSL 1,290 m asl
         by the year 2033. The Project is expected to store the Vakhsh River flow in summer, which will
         consequently reduce flow to Amu Darya during flood period (March to May) and increase flow in
         drought period of winter.
         EMPLOYMENT AND ECONOMIC GROWTH
4.2.11   Without the Project, the existing infrastructure in the reservoir area (see Volume 1, Chapter 3)
         would be unaffected. However, the investment that has gone into the Project (both in the Soviet
         period and more recently), including construction of the HPP and connection roads, resettlement
         operations and the construction of associated infrastructure, would be lost.
4.2.12   As outlined in Volume 3, A3: Livelihood Restoration Plan (LRP), future employment opportunities
         in the area associated with the Project, including investment in new and expanded settlements
         where people have resettled or will be resettled in the future would not exist, and the expected
         economic benefits for the region, the nation, and local communities would be lost.

4.3      ENERGY SECURITY IN TAJIKISTAN
4.3.1    The International Energy Agency (IEA) 2022 review states that approximately 92% of the country’s
         electricity generation is supplied by hydropower (2020). This results in a seasonal variability in the
         generation and supply of electricity, with a surplus during the summer and a shortage during the
         winter. This reflects the pattern of river flow, which is highest in late spring and summer and lowest
         in autumn and winter. The rural poor and remote regions, representing around 70% of the
         population, are most affected by this seasonal variation.
4.3.2    The IEA also stated that many existing generation facilities in Tajikistan are reaching the end of their
         design lifespans. To maintain existing capacity, it is estimated that approximately 80% of the
         country’s HPPs will need to be rehabilitated by 2030. Nurek HPP is undergoing rehabilitation and
         restoration of three power generating units to improve efficiency and strengthen the safety of the
         dam.
4.3.3    When fully operational, the Rogun HPP Project will have a total installed capacity of 3,780 MW and
         will produce enough electricity to eradicate winter shortages. In 2024, the total installed capacity of
         Tajikistan was 5,810 MW, therefore; the Project will increase Tajikistan’s installed capacity by
         approximately 65%. The Project will provide enough energy for domestic consumption, with surplus
         energy exported during summer months to neighboring countries via the CASA-1000 transmission
         line (refer to Volume 1, Chapter 24: Summary of Transboundary Cumulative Impact
         Assessment for information regarding this project).



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                         May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 4-3
        INCREASED USE OF FOSSIL FUELS
4.3.4   To reduce volatility in the country’s power generation and meet year-round demand, the
        Government of Tajikistan has developed, and continues to develop, alternative energy sources and
        diversify energy production.
4.3.5   According to the IEA, since 2014 an increasing amount of the country’s power demand has been
        supplied by the combustion of fossil fuels, including oil and coal (refer to Figure 4-1). Tajikistan has
        significant coal reserves, and both the Government and large industries have turned to coal as a fuel
        to partially resolve severe electricity shortages during winter months and industrial processes.
        Consequently, Tajikistan’s annual carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions have risen sharply in recent years
        (refer to Figure 4-2).

        Figure 4-1 - Total energy supply by source, Tajikistan 2000-2020




        Source: https://www.iea.org/countries/tajikistan

        Figure 4-2 - Annual CO2 Emissions for Tajikistan




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                         May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 4-4
        Source: https://ourworldindata.org/co2/country/tajikistan#what-are-the-country-s-annual-co2-emissions

4.3.6   It is estimated that hydropower has an emissions intensity of approximately 24 gCO2e/kWh
        generated (International Hydropower Association, 2021). Compared to gas (CCGT) at 348
        gCO2e/kWh and Coal (IGCC) at 760 gCO2e/kWh (International Finance Corporation, 2008) this is
        substantially better. This also is much lower than Tajikistan’s current grid average at 106
        gCO2e/kWh (UNFCCC, 2021) as shown in Table 4-1.
        Table 4-1 – Indicative emissions intensities of various generation sources
         Energy Generated                             Emissions Intensity (gCO2/kWh)
         Hydropower                                              24
         Tajikistan Grid Mix (2021)                              106
         Coal (IGCC)                                             760
         Gas (CCGT)                                              348

4.3.7   Given an estimated generation when fully operational of 17,000 GWh per year, this Project performs
        substantially better than alternatives when considering impact over the Project’s lifetime. Using the
        indicative figures in Table 4-1, annual emissions from hydropower would be 0.41 MtCO2e/yr
        compared to 12.9 MtCO2e/yr from coal. This is further described in Volume 1, Chapter 9:
        Greenhouse Gases of this ESIA.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                            PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                                  May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                          Page | 4-5
        COST OF ELECTRICITY
4.3.8   About 26% of the Tajikistan population lives below the National Poverty Line (ADB 2023). Electricity
        is crucial for poverty alleviation, economic growth and improved living standards, and the
        Government has therefore prioritized providing uninterrupted and cost-efficient power.
4.3.9   The 2014 TEAS for the Project analyzed the Least Cost option for meeting the domestic demand
        between 2014 and 2050 (TEAS, Phase II: Economic Analysis, March 2014). The assessment
        established that the economic benefits of the Project exceed those of other feasible HPPs (run-of-
        river) and thermal plants (both coal and natural gas).

4.4     RENEWABLE ENERGY ALTERNATIVES
4.4.1   The 2014 TEAS noted that wind and solar energy production cannot make up the forecast shortfall
        in energy supply in Tajikistan and, as a technology, it remains more expensive than other sources
        although the cost has declined steadily in recent years (ADB, 2016). The Project is designed to
        significantly increase the supply of renewable energy, thereby reducing reliance on fossil fuels and
        supporting the region's low-carbon development goals. The Project, along with other large-scale
        hydropower projects, is expected to displace gas and coal-fired generation and provide stability
        services to further enhance the development of variable renewable energy sources like solar and
        wind.
4.4.2   According to IEA, there is potential for wind and solar energy to complement hydropower in
        Tajikistan, with limited implementation of renewable technology in some regions.
4.4.3   As of 2024, there is less than 1 MW of solar energy generation in operation. The estimated potential
        for solar energy in Tajikistan is approximately 25 billion kWh / year. Tajikistan does have favorable
        conditions for abundant solar energy exploitation and could satisfy up to 10-20% of energy demand,
        particularly in remote mountainous regions far from centralized power grids. At present, there is
        ongoing feasibility studies for a proposed 200 MW solar power plant in the Sughd region. The
        Government of Tajikistan has set a target to develop more than 1 GW of solar capacity by 2030.
4.4.4   As of 2024, there is less than 1 MW of wind energy generation in operation. While the estimated
        potential of wind is relatively unknown, energy production is promising, with varying forecasts
        estimated at 30-100 billion kWh per year.
4.4.5   Expected unit costs of hydroelectric power from Rogun remain highly competitive with solar and
        wind, and when combined with the ability to provide a stable baseload supply, makes
        hydroelectricity a more viable option for Tajikistan's energy needs.

4.5     ALTERNATIVE PROJECT SITES
4.5.1   The Rogun dam site was selected by Hydroprojekt Tashkent and has not been re-considered since
        1981. It is understood three sites were originally considered, and the main reasons for the location
        of the Project include:
         Topography: the very narrow valley at the Project site allows for the construction of a high dam
          with a minimum quantity of fill material and minimum loss of land; and
         Seismicity: The NW-SE trending Ionakhsh and Gulizindan faults run from the Vakhsh fault 10km
          north of the Rogun Dam to Nurek Dam in the south. After the impoundment at Rogun, these
          faults will be subject to both the Rogun and Nurek impound. The active Ionakhsh Fault along the



        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                       May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 4-6
            river axis upstream of the Project site precludes the implementation of a dam core across it; and
            moving the dam axis would place the dam in a tectonically less stable, and thus higher risk, area.

4.6     ALTERNATIVE DAM DESIGN
4.6.1   The 2014 TEAS considered several types of dams including:
           Clay Core Rockfill dam
           Impervious Core Embankment dam;
           Concrete Arch dam;
           Gravity Roller Compacted Concrete (RCC) dam;
           Concrete Face Rockfill dam (CFRD);
           RCC arch gravity dam or RCC arch dam;
           Rockfill dam with earth core dam with upstream concrete block cutting the heel; and
           Rockfill dam with earth core dam with downstream concrete block cutting the toe.
4.6.2   The 2014 TEAS study includes an assessment and comparison of the above-mentioned dam types,
        as presented in Table 4-2.
4.6.3   An impervious core embankment dam was selected as the preferred dam type based on sensitivity
        to movements along fault lines, risk from salt fill of Ionakhsh fault, where salt moves into the fault,
        deforming the rock and resulting in potential hazard, rock quality, sensitivity to overtopping and the
        relative complexity of constructing the dam in stages when compared to other dam types.

        ALTERNATIVES FOR DAM HEIGHT
4.6.4   The 2014 TEAS recommended the FSL 1290 m asl as the preferred alternative for the Project. The
        Assessment of Alternatives remains ongoing during both the design and construction phases of the
        Project, incorporating design changes for FSL 1290 m asl since the 2014 TEAS study. Volume 1,
        Chapter 3 of this ESIA includes the most recent design of the Project.
4.6.5   The 2014 TEAS evaluated three different dam heights, Full Supply Levels (FSL) and installed
        capacities for each height as presented in Table 4-3, and the subsequent E&S impacts of the three
        options to determine the most appropriate.

        Table 4-3- Alternatives Considered for Dam Height and Capacity

         Alternative                                    FSL 1290              FSL 1255         FSL 1220

         Dam crest level (m asl)                        1,300                 1,265            1,230

         Dam height (meters)                            335                   300              265

         Total reservoir capacity (Million cubic        13.3                  8.55             5.22
         meters)

         Reservoir active storage (Million cubic        10.3                  6.45             3.93

        ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACTS OF FSL 1220, 1255 AND 1290
4.6.6   The 2014 assessment was for the three dam height options, i.e., FSL 1220, FSL 1290 and FSL
        1255. These dam height options differ by their reservoir size and installed capacity, although from
        geological, seismic and climate change perspectives there is no notable difference between the
        alternatives.
        ENERGY PRODUCTION
4.6.7   The average annual energy production for each alternative:
         FSL 1220 - 10.1 TWh (terawatt hour) (10,100,000 MWh)
         FSL 1255 - 12.4 TWh (12,400,000 MWh)
         FSL 1290 - 14.4 TWh (14,400,000 MWh)
4.6.8   The FSL 1290 option generates an additional two (2) TWh/yr of electricity production compared to
        the FSL 1255 alternative.
        RESERVOIR SEDIMENTATION
4.6.9   The largest reservoir would retain the most sediment and provide the extended life to both Rogun
        and Nurek HPPs.


        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                       PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                             May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                     Page | 4-9
4.6.10   As discussed in the TEAS, the annual sediment input to the reservoir could be between 62 and 100
         hm3. Considering a 62 hm³/yr. sediment inflow, the life expectancy would be 80, 120 and 200 years
         for FSL 1220, 1255 and 1290 respectively. With 100 hm³/yr. sediment inflow, the life expectancy
         would be 45, 75 and 115 years respectively for the three dam heights.
4.6.11   From an economic perspective, the FSL 1290 option provides the longest life span, most efficient
         energy production and longest regional energy export potential. However, as of September 2024,
         the design life of the FSL 1290 is projected to be 110 years. This could be extended by maintenance
         to reduce the impact of sedimentation on the hydropower plant.
         FLOOD RISK
4.6.12   According to the 2014 TEAS, the existing Vakhsh cascade was not designed to withstand the
         Probable Maximum Flood (PMF). The FSL 1220 alternative for Rogun would not be able to protect
         the downstream cascade in the event of a PMF and substantial investment to the existing cascade
         would be required for this purpose. However, both the FSL 1290 and the FSL 1255 alternatives
         allow for the safe evacuation for the entire cascade should a PMF event occur.
4.6.13   The construction of the Project will improve flood routing capacity for the area downstream of the
         Vakhsh cascade. This positive effect could be increased by appropriate flood management. The
         inclusion of Rogun HPP in the cascade would also reduce risks of floods of lower magnitude, but
         with a higher probability of occurrence. Under the intended operating mode, the flood routing
         capacity of floods of an occurrence probability of 1/10 or 1/100 would be the same for FSL 1220 and
         FSL 1255 since the winter drawdown of the Project would be 4.4 km3 in both cases. However, the
         FSL 1290 option will offer a greater flood retention potential including preventive drawdown.
         ECONOMIC
4.6.14   The recommended FSL 1290 provides the total installed capacity with the highest benefits from a
         least-cost planning and economic perspective. The TEAS found that ultimately the FSL 1290 at full
         production met the winter energy demand of Tajikistan and allowed export sales of summer
         electricity. This alternative provides an increase in the operating lifetime for the dam of 115 years
         (now 110 years) over 75 years for FSL 1255.
4.6.15   Once the reservoir reaches FSL 1290, it is expected approximately 70% of electricity generated at
         Rogun HPP will be exported to the Central Asian nations developing energy relations between
         countries’ energy systems and bolstering regional energy security and cooperation. Additionally, the
         construction of CASA-1000 transmission lines between Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Afghanistan and
         Pakistan will facilitate clean power export in nearby South Asian countries alleviating existing
         electricity shortages in their regions and contributing to sustainable economic growth.
         RESETTLEMENT
4.6.16   The reservoir size will have a direct effect on the magnitude of the socio-economic impact, including
         the loss of additional agricultural land and increased resettlement.
4.6.17   The increase in reservoir size has a considerable effect on resettlement (For the definition of the
         resettlement, all villages below the respective FSL of the reservoir and a few villages above FSL
         which are expected to be affected by flood levels and accessibility have been considered) and loss
         of agricultural land, as calculated in the TEAS (2014):




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                        May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 4-10
          FSL 1220: a resettlement of 13,000 PAPs or 1,825 households; loss of 971 ha of land used for
           agriculture;
          FSL 1255: a resettlement of 18,000 PAPs or 2,433 households; loss of 1,409 ha of agricultural
           land; and
          FSL 1290: a resettlement of 42,000 PAPs or 6,065 households; loss of 3,337 ha of agricultural
           land.
4.6.18   Since 2014, natural population growth has increased the estimated number of potential Project
         Affected Parties (PAPs). It is currently estimated that the FSL 1290 alternative will displace over
         50,000 PAPs, which is likely to increase further in the future due to the high birth rate in the area.
         The total number of displaced households is currently estimated at 6,788. This is not expected to
         increase significantly, as the construction moratorium which has been in place in the flooding zone
         in Rogun City and Nurobod District since Project initiation has now been extended to cover Project-
         Affected Households (PAHs) in Rasht District.
4.6.19   The WB ESS5 recommends ‘avoiding resettlement, or when unavoidable, exploring all options
         including project design alternatives to minimize displacement of people’. As outlined in the 2014
         TEAS studies, resettlement is unavoidable for all dam alternatives, with the technical studies
         showing both FSL 1255 and 1290 dams being feasible, with the recommended alternative for FSL
         1290.
4.6.20   A higher number of resettled PAPs would result with the FSL 1290 alternative, but this is considered
         acceptable for the power generation and flood attenuation capabilities of this option. Resettled
         households would receive improved public facilities, such as health centers, schools, water, and
         power. All affected households would have a choice of different livelihood restoration options,
         through the Ministry of Labor, Migration and Employment of the Population (MLMEP) support or
         Volume 3, A3: Livelihood Restoration Plan (LRP) prepared for the Project. In summary, under all
         alternatives resettled households would suffer no reduction in their standards of living.
         RESETTLEMENT SITE SELECTION
4.6.21   According to Resolution No.47, The State Committee on Land Management and Geodesy is
         responsible for identifying suitable land for resettlement sites. The land is requested via the
         Hukumats and must be approved by the Jamoats and local community before resettlement can
         proceed.
4.6.22   Before the land acquisition for the development of resettlement sites can begin, the site must be
         approved as safe and suitable by government ministries and agencies with appropriate
         competencies (e.g., the Committee for Environmental Protection (CEP) confirms that the site is not
         vulnerable to, and will not cause, significant environmental risks). Once all necessary approvals
         have been granted, relocation to the area can begin.
4.6.23   Site selection is aligned with central government planning for social and economic development.
4.6.24   Sites are selected in areas where the additional population will not cause strain on local resources,
         earthquake risk is low and where there is sufficient agricultural and pastureland of sufficient quality
         to accommodate new arrivals and/or access to alternative livelihoods. All new and existing residents
         have the right to unrestricted use of common pasture lands. In Tursunzoda, it was not possible to
         provide new pasturelands adjacent to the settlements due to the more urbanized nature of the area,




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                        May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 4-11
         however pastureland in another district was allocated. In Namuna village (Tursunzoda) there are
         also plans to provide 0.03 ha Presidential land plots to resettled households.
4.6.25   Five new resettlement sites have been identified in Nurobod District, besides those already in use.
         All new sites consist of agricultural fields and pastureland. According to the planning documents
         these new sites will accommodate around 2,600 land plots over an area of 320 ha.
4.6.26   These new resettlement sites are at an early stage of selection and evaluation by multiple
         government ministries to identify the exact size and location. Once agreed, the CEP will undertake
         an assessment of environmental and social risks of developing the site.
         EFFECTS ON THE RIPARIAN ENVIRONMENT
4.6.27   The Project will allow storage of water and transfer flow from the high flow season (summer,
         vegetation period) to the low flow season (winter, non-vegetation period) for maximizing winter
         energy output.
4.6.28   As described previously, the Government of Tajikistan intend to fill the Rogun reservoir using part of
         the share allocated to Tajikistan under current agreements and practices. Between 2005 and 2011,
         Tajikistan was not using its full share for irrigation from the Amu Darya (particularly from the Vakhsh
         river), with an average of 1.2 km3 of its share unused. This unused share is considered sufficient to
         fill the reservoir without reducing current irrigation in Tajikistan.
4.6.29   Nurek HPP currently acts as the main regulating dam in the Vakhsh cascade. Once constructed,
         Rogun HPP will take over this role and the reservoir will manage flows downstream of Nurek, which
         will retain the ability to regulate flows from Rogun. In this respect, whilst Rogun will be the main dam
         regulating volumes of water downstream, Nurek will fine-tune the releases such that flows remain
         the same as currently experienced.
4.6.30   The 2014 TEAS study concludes that the different proposed Rogun dam alternatives will not impact
         the seasonal flow pattern downstream of Nurek, and the operation of the Project will strictly comply
         with existing agreements and practices on allocation of water shares and, at FSL 1255 and FSL
         1290, will have a large unused live storage capacity. This will create an opportunity to cooperate
         with the entire Amu Darya basin, creating greater storage capacity that will create the potential for
         increased availability of water to riparian countries during dry years.
4.6.31   Three main operational patterns were identified for the determining the Minimum Environmental
         Flow required for the Project (Pöyry 2014):
         ▪   Operation pattern 1: consists in applying the same water allocation and water use patterns
             currently in use. This would be achieved by operating Rogun dam with the same principles that
             are currently applied to Nurek, and by operating Nurek as a run-of-river dam for hydropower
             purposes only;
         ▪   Operation pattern 2: aimed at optimizing winter energy production, which would lead to an
             additional shift of flows from the high flow season to the low flow season. This would have
             consequences on water availability during the summer months and for irrigation.; and
         ▪   Operation pattern 3: aims at maximizing the water allocation for all the users in the entire basin,
             especially during the dry years. This requires a framework to be developed and implemented for
             the Project under different scenarios of water use.
4.6.32   The outcomes of the 2014 evaluation recommended the continued application of Operation pattern
         1 which would result in minimal changes in the flow regime downstream of Nurek dam. However,


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                         May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page | 4-12
         adverse climatic conditions could lead to deviations from the envisaged operation pattern, as
         described in Volume 1, Annex 13-1: Minimum Environmental Flows of this ESIA.
4.6.33   Subsequent studies for this ESIA have focused on updating the MEF study with the most recent
         information and consistent with the Good Practice Handbook for Environmental Flows for
         Hydropower Projects to determine the resolution for the E-Flow assessment (World Bank, 2018).
         BIOLOGICAL
4.6.34   The higher the FSL, the larger the area submerged and the extent of the effect on vegetation and
         fauna is greater. The inundation has an impact on the biodiversity of floodplain habitat and limited
         area of woodland habitat, both of which are considered to be Natural Habitat as per ESS61. Natural
         habitats (ESS6) are areas composed of viable assemblages of plant and/or animal species of
         largely native origin, and/or where human activity has not essentially modified an area’s primary
         ecological functions and species composition. Estimates of habitat loss within the Project AoI are
         provided in Table 4-4.

         Table 4-4- Habitat types within the Project Area of Influence at 1290m asl

          Habitat type                             Area (ha)                          Relative coverage within AoI (%)

          Pasture                                  15650                              55.6

          Agriculture                              5850                               20.8

          River/floodplain                         4220                               15

          Settlements                              2150                               7.6

          Woodland                                 283                                1

          TOTAL                                    28153                              100

4.6.35   Following updated studies in 2023, a no net loss strategy has been developed (Volume 3, A5: No
         Net Loss Framework) to deliver no net loss for the small areas of floodplain and juniper woodland
         habitats that will be submerged. An estimate of habitat loss for juniper woodland for each Project
         alternative is presented in Table 4-5, and estimates of habitat loss for floodplain habitats for each
         Project alternative is presented in Table 4-6 .
