6.7      SUMMARY OF KEY ISSUES
         Resettlement Issues
6.7.1.   The stakeholder engagement process to date has yielded valuable information regarding RAP
         implementation, GRM implementation, resettlement impacts including on vulnerable groups, and
         land allocation and compensation for PAPs. The following key issues and themes identified across
         the process so far are:
         ▪     Level of engagement with communities not affected by the resettlement program;
         ▪     Impacts of the resettlement program;
         ▪     Land allocation and compensation; and
         ▪     Infrastructure, utilities, and services.
         Engagement with Communities not Affected by the Resettlement Program
6.7.2.   Engagement to date conducted by DFZ has primarily focused on communities directly impacted by
         the resettlement program. However, there has been limited engagement undertaken with other
         potentially affected communities, including within the buffer zone.
         Impacts of the Resettlement Program
6.7.3.   Challenges associated with the construction of houses in relocation settlements have been
         highlighted by resettled persons during consultation meetings. This includes poor quality roads, the
         availability, and transportation, of construction materials (such as sand and gravel), and accessibility
         issues. DFZ addressed these issues with specific resettled communities by providing machinery for
         communities to use, including an excavator for transporting sand and gravel, ensuring suitable
         transportation provisions, and enhancing access roads (including providing iron sheets for channel
         crossings). The GRM provides a channel for future complaints to be raised and aims to ensure that
         all Project-related issues are resolved in a timely manner.
6.7.4.   A small number of women affected by resettlement, who are the heads of their household, were
         concerned over the provision of support to construct their new homes. However, the Jamoats have
         arranged for local contractors in the communities to provide construction assistance to women-
         headed households throughout the construction of their new homes. Ongoing engagement with
         vulnerable groups will be facilitated by DFZ to ensure minimum impact on these groups.
         Land Allocation and Compensation
6.7.5.   During consultation meetings, PAPs questioned the land plot allocation process and economic
         impact reimbursement. Concerns have been raised regarding compensation amounts, adjustments
         (e.g., in line with changing item costs and inflation), and the payment schedule. In 2024 the one-time
         transitional allowance paid to resettlers was increased to account for inflation (for the first time since
         it was introduced in 2009). With regard to land issues, some PAPs have expressed concern about
         the amount of agricultural near the towns and villages to which they have resettled. In some cases,
         agricultural land may not be available in the immediate resettlement area. DFZ and its resettlement




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                              PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                                   June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                          Page |Use
                                                                                                                Official 6-22Only
         consultants have been engaging with resettled communities to provide up-to-date information on the
         Project, land acquisition process and RAP studies, and to identify compensation and resettlement
         assistance requirements (please see RAP 2).
         Infrastructure, Utilities and Services
6.7.6.   At some of the relocation sites, communities who were initially relocated under the first stages of
         RAP 2 experienced issues with water and electricity supply. However, these issues have since been
         addressed and no recent reports of insufficient utilities have been raised during recent engagement.
6.7.7.   Some resettled communities have also raised concerns over the availability and construction
         progress of public infrastructure (e.g., schools, roads, mosques, cemeteries, libraries, sports fields)
         and utilities (e.g., electricity, drinking water, irrigation water) in the new relocation settlements.
         Follow up consultations and discussions with district authorities concerning infrastructure
         development have ensured that affected communities remain informed about that construction
         progress and utilities at relocation sites. In some instances, temporary facilities have been provided
         when construction delays have meant new facilities are not ready in time for resettlers (e.g. the
         provision of temporary classrooms).
         General Attitude towards the Project
6.7.8.   Overall, while there have been complaints and issues relating to the resettlement process and other
         matters, resettled communities, host communities and local stakeholders are also positive about the
         Project. Residents and businesses are positive towards the benefits that the Project will bring,
         including stable electricity supply, particularly during winter months, improved road conditions and
         updated public infrastructure services.
         Further information will be provided to potentially affected persons during continuous engagement
         and during the ESIA disclosure period. Following SEP implementation, the stakeholder engagement
         process will also continue throughout the construction and operation of the Project.
         Non-Resettlement Related Issues
6.7.9.   In addition to engagement associated with the resettlement process, the engagement that has been
         undertaken with CSOs, NGOs, local and national authorities, Riparian Governments and
         International Finance Institutions has also contributed to an enhanced understanding of the Project.
         key themes and issues. Key themes and concerns raised during consultations with Riparian
         governments and CSOs included:
         ▪   Potential reduction of downstream water flows resulting in adverse effects for livelihoods and
             economies dependent on adequate summer water flows for agriculture and managed winter
             flows to prevent flooding;
         ▪   Concerns over water quality due to potential land contamination associated with the Rogun HPP
             operations;
         ▪   Concerns over the seismic and other risks that could lead to downstream flooding;
         ▪   Plans for a cascade failure emergency warning system; and
         ▪   Concerns over the appropriate management of gender issues and working conditions during the
             construction activities of the Rogun HPP.

         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page |Use

6.8      STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PROGRAM
6.8.1.   In addition to the previous stakeholder consultation and engagement undertaken for the Project (as
         summarized in Section 6.7), PMG and DFZ will disclose relevant information about the Project
         during the disclosure period (dates to be determined), and ongoing as the Project evolves.
6.8.2.   The Project will have one DFZ-assigned District Livelihood and Engagement Officer (DLEO) and
         one supporting Engagement Officer (one male and one female) for each affected district (Rasht,
         Rogun City, Nurobod, Faizobod, Tursunzoda, Danghara, and Rudaki) to ensure regular consultation
         (visiting, and leading consultations in, each village on a monthly basis) and interaction with affected
         local communities. The appointment of female DLEOs on the Project will facilitate women in the
         local community directly approaching the DLEOs in relation to any concerns. The DLEOs will report
         to DFZ’s Senior Community Relations Manager, who will report to PMG’s Stakeholder Engagement
         Lead. PMG’s Stakeholder Engagement Lead will have overall responsibility for;
         ▪   All external stakeholder (including the general public) engagement activities;
         ▪   The implementation and management of the Project Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM); and
6.8.3.   Monthly reporting on all Project-related grievances to PMG senior management.
6.8.4.   The Project will use different consultation approaches and methods for different stages of the Project
         activities and stakeholder groups. The stakeholder engagement activities that will be undertaken
         throughout the Project lifecycle are presented in Table 6-4 below.

6.9      BENEFIT SHARING CONSULTATION
6.9.1.   The Project will implement a Benefit Sharing Program to maximize the development impact of the
         dam for stakeholders living in the AoI. The program will focus on community development and
         poverty alleviation, complementing the livelihood restoration activities planned in the LRPs to
         accompany the RAPs. Consultation with communities that are directly and indirectly affected by the
         dam alongside broader members of civil society to discuss benefit sharing opportunities will be
         undertaken (see the Project SEP for full details on consultations under the Benefit Sharing
         Program).

6.10     CITIZEN ENGAGEMENT
6.10.1. Citizen engagement, as defined by the WBG, involves two-way interaction between citizens and
        the government to identify methods for the inclusion of citizens and to assess priorities for
        intervention, design, and implementation of the Project and to improve its outcome3. Citizen
        engagement plays a critical role in advocating for the transparency and accountability of public
        institutions and the effectiveness of solutions implemented. Further details on citizen engagement
        as part of the Project are presented within the accompanying SEP.

6.11     GRIEVANCE MECHANISM
6.11.1. A Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) has been developed to ensure the timely and successful
        resolution of stakeholder concerns, as well as to monitor the Project’s relationship with
        stakeholders throughout the Project lifecycle. The GRM includes the processes of receipt,
        acceptance, investigation, resolution, and closure of operational issues, concerns, problems, or
        grievances (after this, referred to as "grievances").
6.11.2. This GRM is only for the external stakeholders affected by the Rogun HPP, not the Rogun HPP
        employees or workers. A separate internal GRM for employee and labor-related grievances is in
        place for use by employees of PMG, Rogun JSC, DFZ, and contractors. The worker GRM is
        detailed in the Labor Management Procedures (LMP).
6.11.3. Purpose and Principles of the Grievance Mechanism
         The purpose of the present GRM is to:
         ▪    Ensure that issues raised by stakeholders are addressed in a credible, predictable, transparent,
              and fair way;
         ▪    Provide prompt resolution to grievances;
         ▪    Ensure that concerns and grievances of vulnerable groups, who often lack access to legal
              processes and grievance procedures, are addressed; and
         ▪    Increase accountability to stakeholders.




         3 Strategic framework for mainstreaming citizen engagement in World Bank Group operations (2014): engaging with

             citizens for improved results: https://documents.worldbank.org/en/publication/documents-
             reports/documentdetail/266371468124780089/strategic-framework-for-mainstreaming-citizen-engagement-in-world-
             bank-group-operations-engaging-with-citizens-for-improved-results



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                              PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                                    June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                           Page | 6-35
        The GRM is based on the following fundamental principles:
        ▪   Accessibility: The GRM must be readily available to all external stakeholders, considering
            confidentiality, legal obligations, and the vulnerability of individuals.
        ▪   Accountability: Ensure that members of the affected community know who is responsible for
            the GRM.
        ▪   Awareness: Stakeholders must know the GRM and receive report on its implementation
            regularly.
        ▪   Communication: Provide grievants with prompt and continuing updates on the progress in
            addressing the grievance, including the reasons for any delays.
        ▪   Cultural sensitivity: Existing lines of communication using the Mahalla and Jamoat
            representative systems will be used for raising and resolving grievances, and communications
            will be in Tajik and Russian.
        ▪   Effective and efficient: Address grievances effectively, promptly, discretely, objectively,
            sensitively, responsively, and transparently.
        ▪   Freedom from reprisal: Participants in the GRM must be free from reprisal, whether from
            authorities, Rogun HPP, DFZ, communities, or other community members.
        ▪   Impartiality: Those who are responsible for addressing grievances must not have any reason
            to favour one solution over another, or one party over another, and no outside parties must be
            permitted to interfere with the grievance resolution process.
        ▪   No cost: Grievances must be resolved at no cost to the party that originated the issue or
            concern.
        ▪   No limitation in civil or legal rights: Participation in a GRM shall not prevent or hinder a
            grievant’ s right to other judicial, administrative, civil, or traditional remedies.
        ▪   Confidentially: All grievances shall be treated confidentially throughout the process, with no
            one not involved in addressing the grievance receiving any information at all.
        ▪   Quick responses: Address grievances as quickly as possible, with a maximum of 30 days
            unless there is no alternative.

6.12    PROJECT GRIEVANCE MECHANISM PROCEDURE
6.12.1. PMG and DFZ have initiated a formal project-specific grievance (complaint) procedure to receive
        the affected communities’ and stakeholders concerns and views. The complaints procedure is
        being used as a tool to assist the timely and successful resolution of stakeholder concerns, as well
        as to monitor the Project’s relationship with the affected community during the different phases of
        the Project.
6.12.2. The GRM process follows a two-tiered process:


            •   Tier 1 - Tier 1 is the initial process where the Rogun HPP and the grievant are in direct
                consultation. Tier 1 of the grievance mechanism is an internal process where the
                grievances are addressed and resolved directly by the Rogun HPP in consultation with
                the grievant.



        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                       June 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                              Page | 6-36
             •   Tier 2 - The grievance procedure moves to the Tier 2 level if no mutually satisfactory
                 resolution is found. The Tier 2 process involves third parties, where the participants
                 agree on the process, the parties involved, and the remedies available.

6.12.3. Details of the grievance procedure have been disseminated in English, Russian and Tajik.
        Information about the existence of the grievance mechanism will be readily available to all
        communities in all Jamoat offices, the offices of PMG, Rogun JSC, and DFZ, at the HPP
        construction site gate, and in affected municipality offices. Additionally, GRM posters will be
        displayed at key locations within the Project affected communities.
6.12.4. It must be underscored that participation in a GRM shall not prevent or hinder a complainant’s right
        to other judicial, administrative, civil, or traditional remedies. The grievant can stop participating in
        the GRM at any time and elect to follow other remedies.


6.12.5. Grievance Channels
6.12.6. The Project grievance procedure (see Figure 6-3) builds on the existing communication methods
        and channels used by communities to raise grievances. The District Livelihood and Engagement
        Officers (DLEO’s), supported by the Senior Grievance Manager (SGM), are responsible for
        managing all resettlement-related grievances, whilst the PMG Stakeholder Engagement Lead
        manages all other grievances. Affected community members can raise Project-related grievances
        through the Jamoats or the DLEOs. All Project-related grievances communicated to Jamoats are
        reported to the DLEOs who are responsible for recording the grievance in the logbook. The DLEOs
        share the GRM with the heads of the Jamoats and explain the process, ensuring the Jamoats
        record all the necessary information which is then communicated to the DLEO to be formally
        recorded. The SGM monitors and oversees this process.
6.12.7. The PMG Stakeholder Engagement Lead is responsible for collecting and managing grievances
        from all other stakeholders, such as NGOs or Riparian representatives. All sensitive grievances,
        such as those relating to sexual harassment, are directed to and managed by a Third-Party
        Sensitive GRM Officer. Figure 5 presents a summary of the different grievance communication
        channels for all the Project stakeholders. Further information on the specific roles and
        responsibilities associated with grievance management is detailed in Table 6-5.
6.12.8. Tier 1 Approach
6.12.9. Tier 1 of the GRM is an internal process where grievances are addressed and resolved directly by
        the Rogun HPP in consultation with the grievant. The underlying assumption of the Tier 1 process
        is that the grievance will be addressed to the satisfaction of all parties.
6.12.10. The Tier 1 approach includes:
             •   A unilateral resolution, where Rogun HPP provides an acceptable solution.
             •   A bilateral approach, where Rogun HPP and the grievant resolve the grievance through
                 discussion or negotiation.
             •   Appeal to a higher Rogun HPP management level if a grievance cannot be resolved.

6.12.11. The Tier 1 GRM will be performed according to the following steps:
6.12.12. Step 1: Grievance Uptake


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                         June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page | 6-37
        Any verbal and written complaints can be raised through the contact details that follow. Anonymous
        complaints can be raised as well. “Complaint/Comment” boxes have been placed in all Jamoat
        offices, the offices of PMG, Rogun JSC, and DFZ, at the HPP construction site gate, and in
        affected municipality offices. These boxes are checked weekly (for DFZ) by the heads of Jamoat
        and sent via photograph or phone call to the DLEO for recording and management and for PMG
        and Rogun JSC by the respective Engagement and Grievance Managers. Posters have been
        prepared and displayed at key locations within the Project affected communities.
        Complaints and grievances can be raised via the following channels during the construction phase:
             •    Grievance Mechanism Paper Form: Stakeholders can fill grievance forms (see Annex
                  4) that will be posted with the Complaint/Comment boxes as well as distributed by
                  Jamoat Heads and made available in affected settlements, affected municipality offices
                  and public venues (mosques, schools, libraries, teahouses).
             •    Face to Face: Stakeholders can voice their grievance to any DLEO, Jamoats or to
                  assigned personnel of DFZ at local offices. In practice, they may also communicate with
                  Contractor supervisors or with any representatives of PMG, DFZ, or Rogun JSC (who
                  will receive training on handling such communications).
             •    Online: Stakeholders can submit forms to esia.rogun.hpp@gmail.com
             •    Company website: https://energyprojects.tj/index.php/en/rogun-hpp
             •    By telephone at: +992 37 235 74 16 or +992 37 235 74 17.
             •    By post: Grievance Manager, Project Management Group for Energy Facilities
                  Construction. 5/1 Shamsi str., 6th floor, 734064, Dushanbe, Tajikistan.
6.12.13. Step 2: Sort and Process:
6.12.14. The DLEOs categorize and sort DFZ grievances, while the EGMs will categorize and sort for PMG
         and Rogun JSC. They determine the relevance of the grievance, categorize the grievance, identify
         the grievance type, and allocate the redress process and responsibilities for resolving. A broad
         grievance categorization is used to ensure that the grievance management procedure is "fit-for-
         purpose" and easy to use. Table 6-5 indicates typical grievance categorizations, with some
         examples.4

        Table 6-5: Grievance Categorization

         Category                                            Example of Grievance

         Relatively minor and one-time                       •   Minor damage to a building or structure.
         problems related to Rogun HPP                       •   Rogun HPP equipment causes minor damage to an
         impacts.                                                individual's livestock or crop.

         Relatively minor but repetitive                     •   Destruction of landscape, local greenery, not
         problems related to Rogun HPP                           removing rubbish or waste.
         impacts.                                            •   Noise and dust complaints.

6.12.15. Step 3: Acknowledge and Follow Up.
6.12.16. The DLEOs contact the complainant within 3 working days following complaint registration to
         explain the Project response process for the grievance. If the grievance is not well understood or if




        5 (International Finance Corporation, 2012, p. 30)




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                             PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                                   June 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                          Page | 6-39
        additional information is required, clarification will be sought from the complainant by the DLEOs
        during this step.
6.12.17. Step 4: Verification, Investigation and Action
6.12.18. All grievances are verified via obtaining information about the grievance to verify its legitimacy and
         the facts surrounding it. Depending on the nature of the grievance, the process may include site
         visits, document reviews, a meeting with the grievant and meetings with others (both project-
         related and non-project-related) who may have knowledge or otherwise assist in resolving the
         problem. The grievant must agree to any such consultation. If the resolution is mutually acceptable,
         the SGM, assisted by the relevant DLEO, will discuss the proposed remediation with the grievant
         and develop an agreed timeline for the remediation process.
6.12.19. The DLEOs prepare a grievance resolution form which includes the nature of the grievance, date of
         its submission, actions implemented to resolve the grievance and date of implementation, or
         proposed actions to be implemented to resolve the grievance along with the timeline for their
         completion. The grievance resolution form is submitted within 20 days of receiving the grievance.
         The DLEOs have 10 working days to investigate and respond to the complaint. If the case requires
         a more complex investigation, updated information will be provided to the complainant explaining
         the actions required to resolve grievance, and the likely timeline.
6.12.20. Upon senior management and SGM approval, the outcomes of the grievance resolution form are
         communicated to the complainant by the DLEOs in accordance with the preferred method of
         communication specified. The grievance resolution form must be signed‐off by the DLEOs or SGM
         and the complainant.
6.12.21. When the grievance resolution form identifies proposed actions to be implemented; the DLEOs
         monitor and follow up to ensure that such actions have been implemented in accordance with the
         timeline proposed within the grievance resolution form.
6.12.22. Upon resolving the grievance, including the completion of required actions, a grievance closeout
         form is prepared to be signed off by the DLEOs and the complainant that will detail the solution that
         was implemented to resolve the grievance.
6.12.23. The DLEOs ensure that the grievance forms, grievance resolution forms and grievance closeout
         forms are always updated and maintained onsite (i.e. DFZ offices, community hubs in affected
         settlements, municipality offices etc.).
6.12.24. The grievance mechanism allows submission of anonymous complaints and sexual exploitation
         and abuse/sexual harassment (SEA/SH) complaints by community members. Senior management
         at DFZ/PMG also monitor the way in which grievances are being handled by their staff and ensure
         they are properly addressed within deadlines specified on their website. The DLEOs keep a
         confidential grievance log of all grievances. The DLEOs response to grievances is monitored by the
         SGM, who advises on corrective actions, such as awareness raising, further training and
         disciplinary measures. In case of receiving SEA/SH complaints, an allocated Gender Focal Point
         addresses and raises the issue with the dedicated Third-Party Sensitive GRM Officer. The Third-
         Party Sensitive GRM Officer investigates the complaint and identify appropriate corrective
         measures. The case is referred to a relevant service provider (i.e. authorities) in severe cases.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                        June 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 6-40
6.12.25. Where required or requested by the complainant, investigations are escalated to Senior
         Management for resolution, with support from the DLEOs, and the involvement of a civic
         representative, such as a Jamoat Head.
6.12.26. If the grievance is not resolved to mutual satisfaction, there will be further consultation toward
         finding an acceptable resolution. The objective is to resolve all grievances at this stage. However, if
         the appeal process is unsuccessful or broader external consultation is required to resolve the
         grievance, the Tier 2 approach will be implemented (subject to the grievant approval).
6.12.27. The grievant may also opt to seek other legal or procedural remedies. The GRM will not prevent
         the grievant from seeking any such alternative remedies or approaching a court or any other party
         for assistance.
6.12.28. A summary of the Tier 1 approach is presented in Figure 6-1.

         Figure 6-1- Tier 1 GRM Approach




6.12.29. Tier 2 Approach
         If the Tier 1 process does not resolve the grievance to the complainants or Rogun HPP's
         satisfaction or if broader consultation is necessary, the grievance procedure will enter the external
         Tier 2 process. The Tier 2 resolution process involves a third party or parties. The Tier 2 process
         will be subject to recognized national regulatory, cultural and independence requirements. The Tier
         2 approach is implemented on a case-by-case basis. An overview of the Tier 2 approach is
         presented in Figure 7.
         The grievant and the Rogun HPP or DFZ must formally agree to enter the Tier 2 process and on
         the participants to the Tier 2 process. A third party should be neutral, well-respected, and
         acceptable to the Rogun HPP and the grievant. The third parties may include public defenders,
         legal advisers, local or international NGOs, technical experts, Jamoat and village leaders or any


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                        June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 6-41
        such acceptable institution. In the case of a Tier 2 procedure, the Rogun HPP legal team will be
        consulted.
        On initiation of the Tier 2 approach, the grievant, the Rogun HPP, and the third party develop a
        roadmap with associated time frames, venues and associated procedures. The participants
        determine the nature of the process. This process can include facilitation, mediation, arbitration,
        resolution by the third party, or any procedure deemed acceptable by the participants. There must
        be agreement on the fees and payment aspects. Regarding the "no-cost" principle, grievances
        must be resolved at no cost to the grievant. The Tier 2 process must be documented, transparent
        and inclusive.
        After agreement on the roadmap and associated aspects, the third party driven Tier 2 procedure is
        implemented. The outcomes can lead to an agreement or fail to achieve a mutually acceptable
        resolution.
            •   If the outcome is acceptable to the grievant and the Rogun HPP, the General Director
                and the grievant complete and sign a grievance resolution agreement. This action
                closes out the grievance and should be logged as such.
            •   The grievant or Rogun HPP may choose not to be bound to or not to accept the
                outcomes of the Tier 2 approach. In such a case, Rogun HPP or the grievant may seek
                other remedies, including those under the Tajikistan legal framework. In this event,
                Rogun HPP will terminate the grievance procedure and log the action as terminated.

        Figure 6-2 - Tier 2 GRM Approach




6.12.30. Grievance Management Timeframes
6.12.31. Every effort will be made to solve each complaint within 10 working days, but for more complex
         investigations may take longer. The complainant is informed in writing of the reasons for any delay


        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                      June 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                             Page | 6-42
        past 10 days and updated on a fortnightly basis of progress on a delayed grievance. The time
        frames indicated in Table 6-5 will be followed in the administration of the GRM. The DLEOs must
        ensure that all parties stakeholders are aware of these time frames.

        Table 6-6 - Grievance Resolution Time Frames

         Action                                             Time frame                       Responsible

         Register grievance.                                Within two working days of       DLEO
                                                            submission.

6.12.32. Contractors’ Role in Implementing the Grievance Redress Mechanism
        All contractors are required to implement the GRM procedures if they receive a grievance,
        escalating to the relevant DLEO or PMG Stakeholder Engagement Lead as needed.
        All worker grievances are covered in the separate Worker Grievance Mechanism, including in the
        Labor Management Procedures.

6.13    MONITORING AND REPORTING
6.13.1. The planned engagement program presented in Figure 6-3 is set out in the SEP, alongside the
        GRM and how this can be used by stakeholders. Additionally, the budget (approximately USD
        368,000) required to carry out the engagement program and detailed description of the key roles
        and responsibilities for implementation is set out within the SEP. The PMG Stakeholder
        Engagement Lead, Senior Grievance Manager (SGM) and DLEOs are reviewing the SEP regularly
        to ensure that all the consultation activities are implemented and are in accordance with the
        planned schedule.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                        June 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 6-43
6.13.2. In terms of the monitoring and review of the stakeholder engagement program, the PMG
        Stakeholder Engagement Lead, assisted by the SGM and PMG Social Specialists, is responsible
        for producing progress updates on the SEP within semi-annual reports submitted to the World
        Bank. The reports summarize stakeholder interactions and consultations, grievances and
        decisions, new stakeholders, partnership progress, and plans for the next period.

6.14    ROLES AND RESPONSIBILITIES
6.14.1. Stakeholder engagement for a project of this scale requires a well-resourced team with
        experienced staff for effectively implementing and managing stakeholder engagement activities
        and grievances. Therefore, a number of additional roles that require specialist expertise have been
        created to ensure that there is sufficient capacity to conduct continuous and effective coordination
        and engagement.
6.14.2. The PMG Stakeholder Engagement Lead and the SGM are periodically assessing stakeholder
        engagement activities and ensuring that all consultations and disclosures are properly accounted
        for.
        ▪     The DLEOs, supervised by the SGM, are responsible for all community-related and
              resettlement stakeholder engagement activities and GRM implementation and management.
        ▪     The Rogun JSC Stakeholder Engagement Lead is responsible for all Project worker related
              stakeholder engagement and GRM implementation and management.
        ▪     The PMG Stakeholder Engagement Lead is responsible for all other external stakeholder
              engagement activities and GRM implementation and management.

6.15    GRIEVANCE RECORDKEEPING AND MONITORING
        GRIEVANCE RECORDKEEPING
6.15.1. The grievance management procedure must be recorded accurately6. Any grievances with
        discrepancies or lacking interventions must be highlighted and submitted for managerial
        intervention. The DLEO’s, SGM, Rogun JSC, and PMG Stakeholder Engagement Lead will maintain
        grievance logs to ensure that each grievance is individually referenced and properly tracked, and
        actions recorded are followed. The log will only be accessible to these designated individuals and
        will contain the following information:


            •   Grievance reference number
            •   Name and contact details of the person receiving the grievance.
            •   Name, gender and contact details of grievant (unless the complaint has been submitted
                anonymously).
            •   Date of the submission of the grievance.
            •   Date of the response to the grievance.
            •   Location of the grievance.
            •   Details of the grievance.
            •   Grievance Category
            •   Re-occurrence
            •   Reporting action and schedule (target and achieved).
            •   Agreed Response by Date.
            •   Response provided.
            •   Final outcome.
            •   Final Response Date.
            •   Status of grievance.
            •   Escalation of grievance to managerial review.
            •   Escalation of grievance to Tier 2 level.
            •   Date when the resolution is implemented (if any).
            •   Was the grievance closed out or not.
            •   Comments.

6.15.2. Confidential grievances or grievances submitted anonymously will be accessed only by authorized
        personnel. Anonymous grievances will include the selected option of communication method




        6 This includes recording complaints by gender, where the grievant is not anonymous.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment              PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                    June 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                           Page | 6-48
        (whether the complainant would want the response via phone, to a different email, or to a different
        postal address).
6.15.3. Sensitive grievances, such as those relating to SEA/SH, will be referred immediately to the Sensitive
        GRM Officer, who will maintain a separate grievance logbook that will not be accessible to anyone
        else).


        GRIEVANCE MONITORING
6.15.4. The grievance management procedure will be monitored continuously, with monthly progress
        reports submitted to management. The overall effectiveness of the grievance management
        procedure will be assessed on a six-monthly basis. Aspects to be considered include Project
        stakeholder feedback on the use and effectiveness of the procedure, awareness of the grievance
        management procedure, unresolved grievances and adherence to process time frames.
6.15.5. PMG may consider revising the mechanism depending on the stakeholder feedback. If many
        grievances are unresolved, PMG may reconsider its approach rather than only increasing efforts to
        resolve individual grievances. If many similar grievances are recorded, it may indicate that the PMG,
        DFZ, and/or Rogun JSC need to undertake appropriate management interventions.
6.15.6. PMG may consider involving an external group or NGO in evaluating the impact of the grievance
        management procedure. Such involvement can add to the independence and robustness of the
        GRM.
6.15.7. All grievances will be stored in the grievance logbook when first recorded and updated with key
        findings and actions during assessment and after being resolved. If the same or similar grievance is
        submitted, it can be attached to a previously closed grievance and re-opened.


6.15.8. Grievance Reporting
6.15.9. The DFZ and Rogun JSC SGMs report to their respective managers on a monthly basis on the
        operation of the GRM. This includes (but is not limited to):
           ▪    number of grievances received by gender,
           ▪    number resolved to the grievance’s satisfaction by gender,
           ▪    number not resolved at the end of the month by gender,
           ▪    number not resolved after 30 days by gender, and
           ▪    the number that will go to Tier 2 or are referred to the legal system by gender.
6.15.10. DFZ and Rogun JSC review the respective reports and submit them to PMG. PMG is including in its
         quarterly reports to Lenders a summary of the GRM’s operation.
6.15.11. PMG and OJSC finalized the establishment of a GRM for workers in January 2025.


6.16    WORLD BANK GRIEVANCE REDRESS SERVICE
6.16.1. Through the World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS), the World Bank accepts grievances
        from individuals and communities who believe a project has negatively impacted them. Complaints
        may be submitted in English, Russian, or Tajik. Note that additional processing time will be needed



        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                       June 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                              Page | 6-49
        for grievances not in English. GRS grievances are reviewed promptly to deal with concerns related
        to the project as soon as possible.
6.16.2. A grievance must clearly state the adverse impacts the World Bank-supported project has caused or
        is likely to cause. The grievance should contain as much supporting documentation and
        correspondence as possible. The grievant may also indicate the desired outcome of the grievance.
        The grievance should describe the complainant or the assigned representative's identity and provide
        contact information as a final step.
6.16.3. Grievances may be submitted to the World Bank GRS through any of the following channels:


            •   Online: https://wbgcmsgrs.powerappsportals.com/en-US/new-complaint/
            •   By email: grievances@worldbank.org
            •   By hand: The World Bank,1818 H Street, Northwest Washington, DC 20433, USA
            •   By post: Grievance Redress Service, MSN MC10-1018, 1818 H Street, Northwest,
                Washington, DC 20433, USA
            •   Through the World Bank Tajikistan Country Office in Dushanbe:
                Business Center "Sozidanie" 3rd floor
                48 Ayni Street
                Dushanbe
                Tajikistan.
                Tel: +992 48 701 5810.
6.16.4. Monitoring, Reporting, and Responsibilities
6.16.5. Successful stakeholder engagement depends on systematic dialogue, performance monitoring and
        adapting to changed circumstances and stakeholder information needs. Monitoring of interaction
        with the stakeholders, including the operation of the GRM, will be carried out on an ongoing basis.
6.16.6. The PMG Stakeholder Engagement Lead, SGMs, and DLEOs review this SEP regularly to ensure
        that all the consultation activities are implemented and are in accordance with the planned schedule.
            •   The DLEOs, supervised by the SGM, are responsible for all community-related
                stakeholder engagement activities and GRM implementation and management.
            •   The PMG Stakeholder Engagement Lead are responsible for all other stakeholder
                engagement activities and GRM implementation and management.

6.16.7. The PMG Stakeholder Engagement Lead and the SGM periodically assess stakeholder
        engagement activities and ensure that all consultations and disclosures are properly accounted for.
6.16.8. The PMG Stakeholder Engagement Lead, assisted by the SGM and PMG Social Specialists, is
        responsible for the general compilation of progress reports and SEP results and their synthesis in
        quarterly reports that are submitted to the lenders. The reports include a summary of stakeholder
        interactions and consultations, new grievances, resolved and unresolved grievances, new
        stakeholders, partnership progress, and plans for the next period.
6.16.9. Record Keeping and Monitoring

         proof of participation are uploaded to DFZ’s and PMG’s internal filing systems.

6.16.12. Routine Evaluation
6.16.13. Various indicators and validation methods are used to evaluate the stakeholder engagement
         process. The following questions must be considered during the external evaluation process:
             •    Are the Rogun HPP strategy, policy, and principles of engagement aligned with the
                  requisite standards?




        7 Full grievance management responsibilities are detailed in Table 6-3.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                                PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                                      June 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                             Page | 6-51
            •    Is there a stakeholder identification and analysis process?
            •    What is the effectiveness of the stakeholder engagement process?
            •    Is information disclosure comprehensive and transparent?
            •    Are free and prior informed consultation principles applied, particularly in the RAP
                 processes?
            •    Are vulnerable groups receiving specific support to participate in the various processes?
            •    Is the grievance mechanism compliant with international standards and functioning
                 effectively?
            •    Is there an effective communication and feedback process between the Company and
                 the Project stakeholders?
6.16.14. External evaluations will be carried out by an independent stakeholder engagement specialist at a
         minimum every three years.
6.16.15. Internal Reporting
6.16.16. The DFZ SGMs, assisted by the DLEOs, report to the PMG Stakeholder Engagement Lead on all
         community stakeholder engagement consultations and grievances on a monthly basis. Additionally,
         both the Rogun JSC Stakeholder Engagement Lead and the third-party Sensitive Grievance
         Manager also report on a monthly basis to the PMG Stakeholder Engagement Lead on
         consultations and grievances received.
6.16.17. The PMG Stakeholder Engagement Lead produces monthly summary reports for the PMG on all
         responsibilities included within this SEP. The monthly reports include stakeholder engagement
         information, grievance information and management of the issues and grievances. The stakeholder
         engagement team (DLEOs and PMG Stakeholder Engagement Lead) are using an internal reporting
         template for monthly reports as set out by the PMG Stakeholder Engagement Lead, which covers all
         work areas, e.g., socio-economic projects delivered, quantitative and qualitative information about
         stakeholder engagements and platforms and grievances, RAP progress updates, etc. PMG is
         responsible for producing quarterly reports, based on the updates provided by the PMG Stakeholder
         Engagement Lead, that will be submitted to the lenders, if requested.

6.17.2. Please note the following points regarding the above budgeting:
                •      Grievance Committees are comprised of representatives from DFZ, PMG, affected Jamoats,
                       SGM and the appropriate DLEO (all hired staff members). Therefore, no additional budget is
                       included for these committees.

                •      Standalone costs for public consultation (e.g. public meetings and focus groups) are limited
                       for refreshments as it is expected that venues will be provided for free by the Jamoats.

                •      The purchase of further vehicles is not included.

                •      Monitoring and evaluation of the SEP has not been provided within the breakdown as these
                       are included within job descriptions of the additional staff required (and therefore covered in
                       the staffing costs).

6.18    COMPLIANCE WITH NATIONAL LEGISLATION
6.18.1. Through the development and implementation of the SEP under the updated 2025 ESIA and SEP,
        stakeholder engagement is meeting national EIA requirements regarding disclosure, consultation,
        and access to information.

6.19    ESIA AND SUPPLEMENTARY DOCUMENTATION DISCLOSURE
6.19.1. PMG, supported by DFZ and Rogun JSC, is disclosing relevant information about the Project during
        implementation. The ESIA and supplementary documentation are being disclosed with sufficient
        time allowed for review and consultation and thereafter will remain in the public domain. The
        disclosed information includes:
        ▪      ESIA (including the ESMP);
        ▪      Non-Technical Summary (NTS) of the ESIA;
        ▪      SEP and GRM; and
        ▪      RLRF.
6.19.2. Consultation and disclosure materials, including the relevant management plans, will be adapted
        depending on the point of interest of the different stakeholder groups. The ESIA report will be
        available for review and comment by stakeholders through a variety of mechanisms outlined in the
        SEP. All documents will be prepared in English, Russian and Tajik. During the disclosure period,
        consultation will be undertaken with those likely to be affected by the Project, as outlined in the SEP.
        Further information on the disclosure process is set out in the SEP.

6.20    POST DISCLOSURE OF ESIA AND SUPPLEMENTARY DOCUMENTATION
6.20.1. Following completion of the disclosure period, feedback gained throughout will inform the continued
        development of the Project through updating the ESIA and relevant mitigation measures and



        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                          PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                                June 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                       Page | 6-54
        management plans. The disclosure documentation (such as the ESIA and SEP) will be updated to
        capture the feedback gained throughout the disclosure period.
6.20.2. The full program for disclosure activities is provided within the SEP document and the agenda for
        undertaking the program is being shared with the Project Lenders for comment prior to its
        implementation in the first week of the disclosure period.
6.20.3. After the disclosure period, feedback from the engagement sessions presented within the SEP will
        be circulated by the PMG to relevant departments of the supporting and implementing agencies, and
        the mitigation measures contained within the relevant management plans will be updated as
        appropriate.

8        AIR QUALITY
8.1.1.   This chapter describes the assessment of potential impacts of the Project activities on air quality
         (AQ) in line with the requirements of the World Bank’s Environmental and Social Standards (ESS) 1:
         Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts.
8.1.2.   A description of ambient and indoor air quality conditions is presented in Volume 2, Annex 7.1:
         Baseline Conditions Report and a summary is provided in Volume 1, Chapter 7: Baseline of this
         ESIA. An assessment of the potential Project activities on ambient and indoor air quality conditions
         for the construction phase is presented in Section 8.6. For the operational phase, only indoor air
         quality is considered in Section 8.7; given that limited outdoor works will be undertaken during the
         operational phase. Where necessary, mitigation measures for the avoidance of impact, impact
         minimization and/or long-term management of activities are included.
8.1.3.   An Environmental and Social Impact Assessment for Rogun Hydro Power Plant was undertaken by
         Poyry and completed in 2014. The 2014 ESIA report discusses the findings of the assessment
         within the Project Area of Influence (AoI). The air quality assessment is focused on emissions
         comprising dust1 and fine particulates, collectively known as total suspended particulate (TSP) and
         exhaust gases including nitrogen oxides (NOx) and Particulate Matter (PM10) generated by quarries,
         construction activities and transportation activities.

8.2      LEGISLATIVE FRAMEWORK, POLICY AND GUIDANCE
         NATIONAL REQUIREMENTS
8.2.1.   Article 7 of the Law of the Republic of Tajikistan on the Protection of Atmospheric Air (2012)
         mandates the Government to establish, among other, maximum permissible concentrations for
         ambient pollutants.
8.2.2.   Government Decree No. 297 of June 26, 2023 ‘Standards for Maximum Permissible Concentrations
         of Pollutants in the Atmospheric Air and the Level of Harmful Physical and Other Impacts on it;
         provides the maximum permissible concentration for a long list of AQ determinants. The maximum
         permissible concentration is also known as Maximum Allowable Concentration (MAC) and one-time
         exceedance (allowable concentration) and daily average MAC values relevant to the Project are
         shown in Table 8-1.




         1 Solid particles either suspended in air or settled on surfaces (deposited dust).

         INTERNATIONAL LENDER REQUIREMENTS
8.2.3.   The World Bank Group (WGB) Environmental and Social Standards (ESS) applicable to air quality
         management include:
          WBG ESS1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts -
           with- with the objective to identify, evaluate and manage the environment and social risks and
           impacts of the project;
          WBG ESS2: Labor and Working Conditions – with the objective to promote safety and health at
           work;
          WBG ESS3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention – with the objective to avoid or
           minimize adverse impacts on human health and the environment by avoiding or minimizing
           pollution from project activities; and
          WBG ESS4: Community Health and Safety – with the objective to anticipate and avoid adverse
           impacts on the health and safety of project-affected communities during the project life cycle.
8.2.4.   In addition to the above, examples of general and industry-specific Good International Industry
         Practice (GIIP) are provided in:
          WBG Environmental Health and Safety (EHS) Guidelines - General EHS Guidelines: Introduction
           (2007) – Section 1.1 provides guidance for management of emission limits and ambient air
           quality to ensure that relevant ambient quality guidelines and standards are not exceeded. This is
           done by applying national legislated standards, or in their absence, the current WHO Air Quality
           Guidelines or other internationally recognized sources.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                    PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                    June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page | 8-2
         WORLD HEALTH ORGANIZATION
8.2.5.   The WBG General EHS Guidelines make specific reference to the World Health Organization
         (WHO) Air Quality Guidelines recommended levels and interim targets for common air pollutants in
         ambient air, namely: PM10 (particles less than 10 microns (µm) in diameter), PM2.5 (particles less
         than 2.5 µm in diameter), O3, CO, NO2, and SO2.
8.2.6.   The WHO Guidelines aim to reduce health impacts of air pollution but are neither standards nor
         legally binding criteria. The provision of interim targets is intended for use in areas where pollution is
         high, providing targets for incremental reductions in air quality standards to meet the 2021
         guidelines. Annual and peak season2 averaging periods are applicable to the assessment of long-
         term exposure, whilst averaging periods of 24-hours and less including, 8-hour, 1-hour, 15-minutes
         and 10-minutes, are applicable to the assessment of short-term exposure and are applicable to both
         public and workers. The WHO interim targets and 2021 Guidelines are presented in Table 8-2.
8.2.7.   The WHO Air Quality Guideline values presented in the WBG General EHS Guidelines are taken
         from the 2005 version of the WHO Air Quality Guidelines and although generally aligned, there are
         some discrepancies between the two data sources. These are noted in Table 8-2 (see footnote to
         table), and the 2021 WHO Guideline values have been used in this assessment.
8.2.8.   Air quality monitoring undertaken on site is done to monitor exposure of workers to particulates and
         exhaust emissions in indoor areas; and does not provide ambient baseline data. The airshed is
         considered non-degraded based on the surrounding land-use but it is not possible to distinguish the
         Project contribution from other sources of emissions to air, due to the absence of historic air quality
         monitoring data.




         2 The World Health Organization defines ‘peak season’ as the six consecutive months of the year with the highest six-

           month running-average Ozone concentration; usually over the warmer months

          PM2.5 (µg/m3)        Annual                   35                25              15           10                5

                               24-hour(a)               75                50             37.5          25               15

          PM10 (µg/m3)         Annual                   70                50              30           20               15

                               24-hour(a)              150               100              75           50               45

          O3 (µg/m3)           Peak                    100                70               -            -               60
                               season(b)

                               8-hour(a)               160               120               -            -              100

          NO2 (µg/m3)          Annual                   40                30              20            -               10

                               24-hour(a)              120                50               -            -               25

                               1-hour                    -                 -               -            -              200(c)

                               10-minute                 -                 -               -            -              500(c)

          SO2 (µg/m3)          24-hour(a)              125                50               -            -               40

                               10-minute                                   -               -            -              500(c)

         (b) - Average of daily maximum 8-hour mean O3 concentration in the six consecutive months with the highest six-month
         running- average O3 concentration
         (c) – Guideline values that were not updated in the WHO Air Quality Guidelines in 2021 and are still valid.

         PROJECT AIR QUALITY STANDARDS
8.2.9.   Ambient air quality monitoring is currently undertaken by contractors as part of their contractual
         obligations and monitoring is done at locations where activities result in airborne particulates, where
         fixed plant and equipment is operated and at quarries and borrow pits.
8.2.10. The current Project ambient air quality standards (AAQS) are detailed in the Air Quality and Dust
        Monitoring and Management Plan (AQDMMP) as shown in Table 8-3.

8.2.11. In addition to the Project AAQS, trigger values for dust require that the mean concentration of TSP
        does not exceed 6000 µg/m3 (10-minute value) and 2000 µg/m3 (8-hour value) at workplaces and
        work areas and 500 µg/m3 at any sensitive receptor within 100 m of the site boundary.
8.2.12. The AQDMMP includes current Occupational Health and Safety standards (OHS) for dust levels at
        receptors based on Tajikistan national standards as shown in Table 8-4.

                                                        < 6.0 (10-minutes)
          Working Areas                   mg/m3
                                                        < 2.0 (daily average)

8.2.13. The sample frequency stated in the current AQDMMP is:
         One air quality sample per month or frequently during periods of intensive machinery use or when
          responding to previous exceedance; and
         One dust concentration sample per week or frequently during times when there is no rainfall or
          upon visual observation results.
8.2.14. The averaging period is not stated in the AQDMMP and a review of the ambient air quality
        monitoring determined that measurement durations and frequencies undertaken by the contractor
        are not aligned with the averaging period required by the Project standards, and the data is not
        directly comparable.
8.2.15. Similarly, a review of the indoor air quality monitoring undertaken by the Republican Military Mine
        Rescue Service (RMMRS) determined that monitoring is done using hand-held devices on a

        aligned with the averaging period required by Project standards and the data is not directly
        comparable.

8.3      ASSESSMENT METHODOLOGY
8.3.1.   The assessment methodology is described in Volume 1, Chapter 5: ESIA Methodology of this
         ESIA.
         AREA OF INFLUENCE
8.3.2.   The Area of Influence (AoI) for the air quality assessment has been informed by:
         ▪     Desk-based review of publicly available information;
         ▪     The location of the nearest residential areas, community and recreational facilities;
         ▪     Stakeholder engagement; and
         ▪     Site observations.
8.3.3.   For the construction phase, the AoI comprises the entire construction site given the dispersed
         location and multiple working areas where activities generate sources of emissions to air which have
         the potential to cause air quality impacts. The AoI for the construction phase extends to 2 km from
         the location of the source given that large particles are deposited within a short time and distance;
         but finer particles remain suspended for longer and can be carried considerable distances before
         settling.
8.3.4.   The AoI for the operational phase extends to the site, the Pasi Muhraho village and Rogun City but,
         following completion of the construction phase, vehicle movements will be limited and there are no
         other major sources of emissions to air.
8.3.5.   No other village is located within the Project AoI. The village of Sidoni Nav, located on the left bank
         to the north of the construction site, is approximately 3 km from the crushing plant at the northern
         end of Quarry 17 and, with the distance and prevailing wind direction, it is unlikely that dust
         generated at the crusher would reach the village.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                                       PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                                       June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                                     Page | 8-7
         IMPACT MAGNITUDE
8.3.6.   The assessment of magnitude is based on available data and consideration of the duration, extent,
         reversibility, and likelihood of the impact. The definitions of these key criteria and how they are
         considered to define magnitude are presented in Volume 1, Chapter 5 of this ESIA.
8.3.7.   The categories of impact magnitude are described in Table 8-6. Due to the absence of a baseline
         AQ data set, no data is available that would allow for a distinction between natural variability in AQ
         determinants and the Project contribution to ambient AQ.
8.3.8.   To determine impact magnitude, activities undertaken during the construction and operational
         phases generate particulates and/or emissions that either meet or exceed the relevant Project
         ambient air quality standard for outdoor, and the air quality standard for indoor areas within the
         tunnels and caverns. Consequently, a limited number of categories are described relative to the
         number of categories identified in Volume 1, Chapter 5: Methodology of this ESIA.

         Table 8-6 – Impact Magnitude Category

          Magnitude           Description
          Category

          Large               Particulate and other air quality determinants in outdoor and indoor areas exceed the
                              Project AQ standard for relevant long-term and short-term averaging periods.

          Very Small          Particulate and other air quality determinants in outdoor and indoor areas are within the
                              Project AQ standard for relevant long-term and short-term averaging periods.

          Beneficial          N/A


         RECEPTOR SENSITIVITY
8.3.9.   In the context of this Project, the identified receptors include:
          Workers on site in proximity to activities undertaken during the construction and operational
           phases. The sensitivity of the receptors is categorized as high.
          Residents of local communities are exposed to air quality, where the elderly, children, pregnant
           women and people with pre-existing health conditions are at heightened risk of adverse health
           effects. The sensitivity of residents of local communities is high.
8.3.10. However, the local community receptors identified in Table 8-7 are at least 1 km from the nearest
        location on the construction site where emissions to air are generated; as such, mitigation measures
        to reduce the impact to workers will effectively reduce the impact on residents of local communities.
8.3.11. The location of areas where receptors applicable to the assessment are identified in Table 8-7.

8.3.12. The village of Sidoni Nav located on the left bank to the north of the site has been scoped out of the
        assessment as the village is outside of the Project AoI.
8.3.13. During a typical construction 12-hour shift, workers in outdoor areas are exposed to pollutants
        resulting from activities such as blasting, crushing, mixing, vehicles moving materials along
        unsurfaced roads, dust blown from stockpiles and exhaust emissions from plant and equipment.
        Workers in indoor areas are exposed to pollutants resulting from the operation of plant and
        equipment in underground areas and vehicle movements through access tunnels.
8.3.14. During the operational phase, workers will spend limited time in outdoor areas such as the
        substation site and around the reservoir area, and most of the operational and maintenance (O&M)
        activities will occur within the turbine hall, powerhouse, workshops, offices and ancillary buildings.

8.4      POTENTIAL IMPACTS AND EFFECTS
8.4.1.   The impact assessment is informed by an understanding of current site conditions based on site
         visits, visual observations of the working practices and monitoring data. The assessment determined
         where there is potential for adverse air quality impacts and to recommended mitigation measures to
         reduce the potential impact on the identified receptors. The assessment considers potential impacts
         on the receptors due to changes in ambient air quality (outdoor) and on the air quality within caverns
         and tunnels (indoor) for both the construction and operational phases.
8.4.2.   Meteorological conditions on site are described in Volume 2, Annex 7.1: Baseline Conditions
         Report of this ESIA. In summary, in the Vakhsh Valley region, the prevailing wind direction is west
         to east, with peak recorded wind speeds of up to 10 m/s. Dust storms are common in lowland areas
         between April and November, due to the aridity of the region situated in the ‘global dust belt’ which
         extends from the Sahara to the Gobi.
8.4.3.   The sources of emissions to air and the locations of the sources are indicated in Table 8-9.

8.5      SITE AIR QUALITY
8.5.1.   Air quality conditions on site are detailed section 2 of Volume 2, Annex 7.1: Baseline Conditions
         Report of this ESIA.
8.5.2.   Potential sources of emissions to air are shown in Figure 8-1 and Figure 8-2 for the upstream and
         downstream areas respectively.



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                       PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                       June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                    Page | 8-11
Figure 8-1 - Upstream Area - Stockpiles, Plant, Conveyors and Transport Routes




Figure 8-2 - Downstream Area – Stockpiles, Plant, Conveyors and Transport Routes




Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment        PUBLIC |
Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                        June 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                     Page | 8-12
8.5.3.   Site air quality conditions were observed during a site visit undertaken in May 2023 to verify the site
         monitoring program and concluded that:
         ▪   The construction area on the left bank generates significant dust, which is visible as a cloud
             above the site when viewed from a distance;
         ▪   A substantial amount of fugitive dust is generated due to the movement of construction traffic on
             dry and dusty haul roads within the site, primarily on the left bank—this would prevail in summer
             and early autumn dry conditions. Most above-ground roads are unpaved, and watering of road
             appears to be inconsistent across the site, with left bank roads better (but not adequately)
             watered, but the right bank access road to the site being extremely dusty and poorly watered,
             resulting in large fugitive dust emissions when used by vehicles;
         ▪   Fugitive dust is generated by handling of dusty materials, and evidently from trucks dumping
             materials down the side of embankments on a regular basis;
         ▪   Borrow pits, spoil storage areas and stockpiles are uncovered and observed as major sources of
             fugitive dust under windy conditions, although there are relatively few workers present to be
             exposed;
         ▪   Trucks carrying materials are not covered, but exposed materials nevertheless appear to be a
             relatively minor contributor to fugitive dust across the site.
         ▪   Fugitive dust and particulates are generated by underground vehicles, contributing to very poor
             air quality in the tunnels. In many locations, there is poor or no mechanical ventilation. In
             underground roads where daylight is not visible, and where no natural ventilation is possible,
             visible dust is present despite water ingress into tunnels from pipe work and other sources,
             which would be expected to have a mitigating effect;
         ▪   A high intensity of diesel-powered vehicle movement in the tunnels continue to emit PM2.5, NO2
             and CO resulting in poor underground air quality. A deep haze in the air of many tunnels and in
             the powerhouse and other underground workplaces (especially where penstock and intake
             tunnels and shafts are being excavated) is likely to be a combination of humidity, diesel
             emissions, and fine particulates, which is considered almost certainly to present a significant
             health hazards to those who experience chronic exposure—this includes powerhouse staff and
             also construction staff since construction has been ongoing for nearly a decade and will continue
             until 2029; and
         ▪   Visible dust generation above ground and the deep haze underground demonstrate that more
             comprehensive management is required to improve current conditions at the site, and in
             particular to reduce risks for those working on site (particularly underground), those now residing
             in nearby Construction Camp 1, the settlement located in Construction Site 2, and future worker
             accommodation in Construction Site 2 and Area 5 downstream of the dam.
         ▪   General site air quality conditions are shown in Figure 8-3.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |

         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page | 8-13
