         CONSTRUCTION AIR QUALITY MONITORING
8.5.4.   The monitoring of air quality is undertaken at several outdoor locations:
          Upstream cofferdam (dust, CO, NO2, NO, SO2, H2S, CH4, C3H8 and SiO2);
          Downstream Coffer Dam (dust, CO, NO2, NO, SO2, H2S, CH4, C3H8 and SiO2);
          Gas insulated substation (GIS) yard at T4 portal (dust);
          Main Dam Core (dust, CO, NO2, NO, SO2, H2S, CH4, C3H8 and SiO2);
          Main Dam Core (middle) (dust);
          Main Dam Core (left side) (dust);
          Quarry No. 26 (CO, NO2, NO, SO2, H2S, CH4, C3H8 and SiO2);
          GG2 (CO, NO2, NO, SO2, H2S, CH4, C3H8 and SiO2);
          Asphalt Plant (CO, NO2, NO, SO2, H2S, CH4, C3H8 and SiO2);
          CP-2 (CO, NO2, NO, SO2, H2S, CH4, C3H8 and SiO2);
          CP-3 (CO, NO2, NO, SO2, H2S, CH4, C3H8 and SiO2); and
          Quarry No. 17 (CO, NO2, NO, SO2, H2S, CH4, C3H8 and SiO2).
8.5.5.   The outdoor sample locations are shown in Figure 2-3 of Volume 2, Annex 7.1: Baseline
         Conditions Report of this ESIA.


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment             PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                             June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                          Page | 8-15
8.5.6.   The monitoring of air quality is undertaken at several indoor locations:
          Tunnel 3A (CO, NO2, NO, SO2, H2S, CH4, C3H8 and SiO2);
          Entrance of Tunnel 4 (CO, NO2, NO, SO2, H2S, CH4, C3H8 and SiO2);
          Tunnel 22 (CO, NO2, NO, SO2, H2S, CH4, C3H8 and SiO2); and
          Tunnel 5 (CO, NO2, NO, SO2, H2S, CH4, C3H8 and SiO2).
8.5.7.   The indoor sample locations are shown in Figure 2-4 of Volume 2, Annex 7.1: Baseline
         Conditions Report of this ESIA.
8.5.8.   An audit of the monitoring program undertaken in 2023 concluded that the sampling program is
         designed to indicate the presence of air pollutants and localized elevated concentrations and is fit for
         purpose. However, the monitoring program in its current form does not provide adequate data as:
          Spot samples do not represent air pollutant concentrations for the same exposure/ averaging
           periods as the Project Standards for both ambient and occupational exposure;
          The sampling period is not a full year and therefore may not represent pollutant concentration
           fluctuations caused by seasonality and all meteorological conditions; and
          The instrumentation used is not able to sample for the exposure periods required to assess
           against the Project standards.
8.5.9.   The results of the outdoor and indoor monitoring indicate the presence of air pollutants and localized
         elevated concentrations. However, given the short-term nature of monitoring, the findings should be
         considered indictive only. The following indicative exceedances were recorded in the dataset, and
         these are further discussed in Volume 2, Annex 7.1: Baseline Conditions Report:
          SiO2 at the Upstream Cofferdam in November and December 2023;
          SiO2 at the Downstream Cofferdam in December 2023;
          SiO2 at the Entrance of Tunnel 4 in December 2023; and
          CO at the Entrance of Tunnel 5 in January 2024.
8.5.10. A short sampling program was undertaken by WSP during the 2023 audit and all monitored
        pollutants were detected at the construction site, including plumes of visible dust observed at some
        of the areas visited. This monitoring program is summarized in Volume 1, Chapter 7: Baseline of
        this ESIA

8.6      IMPACT ASSESSMENT - CONSTRUCTION
8.6.1.   The main construction site extends over an area of several square kilometers and, at the main dam,
         the left-bank, the right-bank and downstream of the main dam site the activities undertaken include
         blasting, demolition works, excavation works, crushing plants, concrete batching plants, earthmoving
         activities, material transfer including covered conveyors, open stockpiles and drilling.
         TSP (DUST, PM10, PM2.5)
8.6.2.   Activities that generate Total Suspended Particulates include:
          Blasting, demolition, excavation, crushing, concrete batching, earth moving and dumping,
           material transfer, stockpiles and drilling activities (main dam, left bank, right bank and Area 5).



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                    PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                    June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 8-16
          Vehicle and plant movements on roads (main dam, left bank, right bank, downstream, Area 5 and
           access tunnels).
8.6.3.   Wind entrained dust (coarse particulates) from construction sources does not generally travel over
         distances greater than 250 m of the source. Fine particulates will be dispersed over greater
         distances particularly in the dry summer months. Dispersion is impacted by the topography within
         and surrounding the AoI as hilly and mountainous regions can lead to reduced wind speed or wake
         effects which may cause the particles to be deposited. In wet conditions in the winter months,
         particle mobilization by the wind is decreased.
8.6.4.   Vehicles travelling along unpaved roads generate higher quantities of particulates than from paved
         roads. The speed of the vehicle and the moisture content of the surface material also affects the
         generation of dust. Dust entrained by vehicles is generally considered for receptors located within 50
         m of roads3. The public and site roads are a combination of paved and unpaved surfaces.
8.6.5.   Most roads within the tunnels are paved and water leaking from pipes in the tunnels has been
         observed to dampen the road surface with a resulting reduction in fugitive particulates and engine
         exhaust emissions as vehicles pass through the tunnels. In addition, the short tunnels have daylight
         at both ends within one km of the entrance or exit, providing natural ventilation with little visibility
         impairment.
8.6.6.   During the 2023 site visit, poor visibility was noted in the tunnels, the impaired visibility indicating
         that the concentration of particulates and other pollutants likely exceed Project standards. Poor
         indoor air quality was cited in the 2014 ESIA with limited measures in place to manage the issue.
         Workers spend substantial amounts of time in underground spaces and tunnels, some workers are
         within tunnels for most of their 12-hour shift.
         Impact Assessment
8.6.7.   The receptors potentially impacted by this source are the site workers and residential receptors as
         defined in Section 8.3. The sensitivity of these receptors is high.
8.6.8.   Air quality monitoring undertaken to date includes a trigger value for total suspended particulate
         matter (TSP) of 6 mg/m3 (10-minute value) and 2mg/m3 (8-hour value) at workplaces and work
         areas and 500 μg/m3 at any sensitive receptor within 100m of the site boundary.
8.6.9.   At the time of monitoring, localized concentrations of dust generated by construction activities were
         below the trigger value; however, SiO2 concentrations exceed the Project standard for both outdoor
         and indoor areas, with indicative exceedances noted for one to two months at the end of 2023. Due
         to the proximity of the site workers to the emission sources, the magnitude of the potential impact is
         large as the 24-hour Project standard is exceeded, albeit that the workers will not be present for the
         full 24-hour period. The significance of the effect is major.
8.6.10. The village of Pasi Muhraho is located adjacent to the road used by heavy and light construction
        vehicles moving between Construction Site 1 or Rogun City to the site. In the absence of AQ
        monitoring data, the magnitude of the potential impact of suspended particulates on receptors within
        the village is large and the significance of the effect is major.




         3 IAQM Guidance on the Assessment of Dust from Demolition and Construction, 2024.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                     PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                     June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page | 8-17
8.6.11. Site worker accommodation in Construction Site 1 and Rogun City is a minimum of 1 km from the
        nearest working area on the construction site. Given that larger suspended particles are known to
        settle within a short distance of the source and smaller suspended particles remain airborne
        depending on the wind speed and the presence of vegetation, it is unlikely that, due to the distance,
        AQ at these locations would be potentially impacted by suspended particulates generated on site.
        The magnitude of the impact is very small, the significance of the effect is minor, and no specific
        mitigation is proposed.
8.6.12. The demolition of structures on Construction Site 1 prior to inundation of the lower terrace will
        generate suspended particulates that may potentially impact on the WeBuild worker accommodation
        at Rogun City. An asbestos survey as described in Volume 3, B14: Outline Demolition
        Management Plan of this ESIA is required to protect worker health; however, most of the structures
        on site are prefabricated and the demolition process will be of short duration. The magnitude of the
        impact is very small, and the significance of the effect is minor.
        Mitigation Measures
8.6.13. Mitigation measures to minimize the impact of particulates on workers and the village of Pasi
        Muhraho include:
         Undertake daily observations of visible dust at the Pasi Muhraho village and minimize dust on the
          paved road through the Pasi Muhraho village using water sprayers daily and more frequently
          during dry and windy periods;
         Fit watering system or dust extraction systems to crushing and mixing plant and consistently use
          systems to suppress airborne particulates;
         Use water cannons consistently in areas where spoil is dumped over the cliff and over large
          unpaved areas, especially during dry and windy periods;
         Use water sprayers consistently on roads on site and wet the load on open tip trucks prior to
          transport;
         Do not locate any activities that generate particulates adjacent to village community facilities or
          residential receptors;
         Ensure that workers wear appropriate PPE including face coverings during the shift if workers will
          be in areas that may exceed AQ standards for short periods of time. The face covering must
          show a NIOSH certification mark or the CE certification mark or equivalent;
         Ensure that roads within the tunnels are paved or kept sufficiently damp to minimize entrainment
          of particulates by vehicle movements; and
         If the mitigation measures are deemed to be insufficient, for example, dust levels are observed to
          be high, additional mitigation e.g. increased frequency of wetting of surfaces shall be undertaken.
        Residual Effect Significance
8.6.14. With the implementation of the mitigation measures, the significance of the effect on the receptors,
        both workers and residents, can be reduced to moderate.
8.6.15. As moderate effects are considered significant, monitoring of AQ at sensitive receptor, including site
        locations where workers are undertaking activities and at Pasi Muhraro residences and at




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
        Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                   June 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page | 8-18
        Construction Site 1 worker accommodation, is required to ensure that the mitigation measures are
        sufficient to meet Project standards. The requirement for monitoring is described in Section 8.8.
        EXHAUST EMISSIONS (NO2, SO2, PM10, PM2.5, CO)
8.6.16. Diesel, petrol and limited quantities of oil are used on site to power generators, plant and light and
        heavy vehicles both for site works and the transportation of workers. The major components of
        exhaust emissions include combustion products and fine particulates such as CO, NO2, SO2, PM10
        and PM2.5.
8.6.17. The receptors potentially impacted by this source are site workers and residents of local
        communities that have a high sensitivity.
        Impact Assessment
8.6.18. On-site monitoring undertaken to date indicates that concentrations of these parameters are within
        the Project standard. The exception to this is CO, where an exceedance was recorded during a
        single month at one location. Particulate matter (PM10 and PM2.5) from vehicle exhaust emissions is
        not monitored. However, given the lack of a robust AQ monitoring set to assess against the Project
        standards, the magnitude of the impact is considered large. The significance of the effect is major.
8.6.19. Several schemes have been implemented on site to reduce the combustion emissions. Most of the
        generator sets have been gradually replaced by generators powered by electricity received from the
        power line from Nurek HPP and in 2023 seven diesel-fueled generators remained on site. The
        covered conveyor belt transporting aggregate from Quarry 26a to the main dam site has
        considerably reduced vehicle exhaust emissions in recent years.
8.6.20. Within the short tunnels, natural ventilation provides limited fresh air at both ends of the tunnels.
        However, within the longer tunnels, the powerhouse and transformer caverns, there is no ducting to
        introduce clean air or to exhaust stale air that is recirculated by the fans that have been installed.
        The magnitude of the potential impact is large, and the significance of the effect is major.
8.6.21. The magnitude of the potential impact of exhaust emissions from vehicles on the road between the
        site and Construction Site 1 or Rogun City that pass the village of Pasi Muhraho is large given the
        number of vehicle movements and the significance of the effect is major.
        Mitigation Measures
8.6.22. The following measures have been identified to further reduce the impact of exhaust emissions on
        indoor air quality:
         Ensure that generators, plant and vehicles are maintained in accordance with manufacturer’s
          requirements to reduce excessive emissions. The maintenance of all vehicles and plant must be
          documented to ensure that it aligns with manufacturer instructions and GIIP supported by daily
          plant and vehicle inspections. This requirement should be included in the site vehicle and plant
          maintenance plans;
         Ensure that plant and equipment is not idled but switched off when not in use;
         Enforce the site vehicle speed limit of 20 km/hr in the tunnels to minimize exhaust emissions;
         Install mechanical ventilation of sufficient capacity to ensure sufficient delivery of fresh air to the
          working faces of the tunnels and removal of stale air;




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
        Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                      June 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                   Page | 8-19
         Ensure that roads within the tunnels are paved or kept sufficiently damp to minimize entrainment
          of particulates by vehicle movements;
         Where practicable, continue to replace fossil-fueled machinery with electrically operated plant
          and equipment; and
         Ensure that workers wear appropriate PPE including face coverings during the shift if workers will
          be in areas that may exceed AQ standards for short periods of time. The face covering must
          show a NIOSH certification mark or the CE certification mark or equivalent.
8.6.23. The following mitigations apply to outdoor areas:
         Ensure that generators, plant and vehicles are maintained in accordance with manufacturer’s
          requirements to reduce excessive emissions. The maintenance of all vehicles and plant must be
          documented to ensure that it aligns with manufacturer instructions and GIIP supported by daily
          plant and vehicle inspections. This requirement should be included in the site vehicle and plant
          maintenance plans;
         Ensure that plant and equipment is not idled but switched off when not in use;
         Enforce the site vehicle speed limit of 40 km/hr above ground to minimize exhaust emissions;
         Ensure that workers wear appropriate PPE including face coverings during the shift if workers will
          be in areas that may exceed AQ standards for short periods of time. The face covering must
          show a NIOSH certification mark or the CE certification mark or equivalent; and
         Ensure that vehicles travelling between the site and Construction Site 1 or Rogun City adhere to
          the speed limit.
        Residual Effect Significance
8.6.24. With the implementation of the mitigation measures, the significance of the effect on the receptors,
        both workers and residents, can be reduced to moderate.
8.6.25. As moderate effects are considered significant, monitoring of AQ at sensitive receptors, including
        site locations where workers are undertaking activities and at Pasi Muhraro residences and at
        Construction Site 1 worker accommodation, is required to ensure that the mitigation measures are
        sufficient to meet Project standards. The requirement for monitoring is described in Section 8.8.
        OZONE EMISSIONS
8.6.26. There is potential for ozone (O3) to be emitted from electrical equipment including the switchgear,
        cables, transformers and breakers, which may have implications for site workers. The sensitivity of
        the site workers is high.
        Impact Assessment
8.6.27. There is temporary ventilation equipment in place in the powerhouse and transformer caverns, but
        this is considered inadequate. While the indoor ozone levels are unknown, the deep haze in many
        tunnels, the powerhouse and other underground workplaces strongly suggests that the magnitude of
        the potential impact is large and the significance of the effect is major.
8.6.28. Transformers will also contain sulfur hexafluoride (SF6) as an insulating gas and the assessment of
        the potential impact of SF6 on the atmosphere is described in Volume 1, Chapter 9 Greenhouse
        Gases of this ESIA.



        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
        Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                 June 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                              Page | 8-20
        Mitigation Measures
8.6.29. At present, there is insufficient information to comprehensively assess the risks and define a
        monitoring program for ozone. However, given the known health risks due to ozone, Rogun JSC and
        the relevant contractors must undertake a risk assessment to characterize the air quality within the
        turbine hall, powerhouse, tunnels and other interior workplaces for which they are responsible.
8.6.30. Mitigation measures to reduce the potential impact of ozone on workers in the underground work
        areas and tunnels can be categorized as conditional on the outcome of the risk assessment. Should
        the risk assessment indicate that the AQ within these areas does not comply with Project AQ
        standards, then a permanent ventilation system must be installed in the turbine hall, powerhouse,
        tunnels and other interior workplaces with sufficient capacity to ensure that the interior AQ meets
        Project AQ standards.
8.6.31. Mitigation measures that must be implemented as GIIP include:
         Ensure that generators, plant and vehicles are maintained in accordance with manufacturer’s
          requirements to reduce excessive emissions. The maintenance of all vehicles and plant must be
          documented to ensure that it aligns with manufacturer instructions and GIIP supported by daily
          plant and vehicle inspections. This requirement should be included in the site vehicle and plant
          maintenance plans;
         Ensure that plant and equipment is not idled but switched off when not in use;
         Enforce the site vehicle speed limit of 20 km/hr in the tunnels to minimize exhaust emissions;
         Install mechanical ventilation of sufficient capacity to ensure sufficient delivery of fresh air to the
          working faces of the tunnels and removal of stale air;
         Ensure that roads within the tunnels are paved or kept sufficiently damp to minimize entrainment
          of particulates by vehicle movements;
         Where practicable, continue to replace fossil-fueled machinery with electrically operated plant
          and equipment; and
         Ensure that workers wear appropriate PPE including face coverings during the shift if workers will
          be in areas that may exceed AQ standards for short periods of time. The face covering must
          show a NIOSH certification mark or the CE certification mark or equivalent.
        Residual Effect Significance
8.6.32. With implementation of GIIP, the significance of the effect can be reduced to moderate. However,
        as moderate effects are considered significant, monitoring of AQ at sensitive receptors is required
        to ensure that the mitigation measures are sufficient to meet Project standards. The requirement for
        monitoring is described in Section 8.8.
8.6.33. Pending the contractor assessments and implementation of the mitigation measures, the
        significance of the residual effects could be reduced to minor.
        LEFT BANK ROAD
8.6.34. The progressive inundation of the reservoir will flood roads and bridges that connect the villages
        located on the left and right banks of the Vakhsh river; and villages located on the tributaries will
        also be affected. The timing of the inundation and the impact on roads and bridges is described in
        Volume 1, Chapter 3: Project Description of this ESIA.The The left bank road will be reinstated as


        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
        Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                      June 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                   Page | 8-21
         part of the reprovision of existing infrastructure as a bare ground road to prevent severance of
         communities, as detailed in Volume 2, Annex 17-3: Severance Impacts of this ESIA. The route of
         the left bank road is shown in Figure 8-4 and the road alignment will pass close to or through
         several villages along the route. The distance of the road to the villages is unknown; however,
         village residents have been identified as receptors with a high sensitivity.

         Figure 8-4 - Proposed Left Bank Road Alignment




8.6.35. The left bank road construction and rehabilitation activities that potentially impact on air quality
        include:
          Earth moving equipment and vehicle exhaust emissions.
         Impact Assessment
8.6.36. The construction and rehabilitation activities will generate dust, finer particulate matter and exhaust
        emissions in the immediate vicinity of the works and, given the earth moving equipment and site
        vehicles operating at any one stretch of the road, the local air quality may be impacted.
8.6.37. Wind entrained dust (coarse particulates) from construction sources does not generally travel over
        distances greater than 250 m of the source. Fine particulates will be dispersed over greater
        distances particularly in the dry summer months and dispersion is also determined by the
        topography as hilly and mountainous regions can lead to reduced wind speed or wake effects which


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                    PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                    June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 8-22
         may cause the particles to be deposited. Dust entrained by vehicles is generally considered for
         receptors located within 50 m of roads4. In wet conditions in the winter months, particle mobilization
         by the wind is decreased. Vehicles travelling along unpaved roads generate higher quantities of
         particulates than from paved roads. The speed of the vehicle and moisture content of the surface
         material also affects the generation of dust.
8.6.38. As the road is a bare ground road, it will follow the contours of the landscape and the requirement
        for road cuttings will be limited to maintain the road grade. Where the road intersects the Vakhsh
        river tributaries, five new bridges will be constructed.
8.6.39. During the construction phase, the potential for local AQ determinant concentrations to exceed
        Project standards and the magnitude of the potential impact is very small and the significance of
        the effect is minor.
         Mitigation Measures
8.6.40. Although the significance of the effect is minor, mitigation measures to minimize the impact of the
        road construction activities are required as GIIP:
          Ensure that maintenance of all vehicles and plant aligns with manufacturer instructions and daily
           plant and vehicle inspections are undertaken. This requirement should be included in the site
           vehicle and plant maintenance plans;
          Ensure that the speed limit is limited to between 10 and 20 km/h in areas where construction
           works are taking place;
          Ensure that completed sections of the road are kept sufficiently damp to minimize entrainment of
           particulates by construction vehicle movements; and
          Ensure that workers wear appropriate PPE including face coverings during the shift if workers will
           be in areas that may exceed AQ standards for short periods of time. The face covering must
           show a NIOSH certification mark or the CE certification mark or equivalent.
         Residual Effect Significance
8.6.41. With the implementation of the mitigation measures the significance of the residual effect is
        negligible.
8.6.42. Management of construction traffic should follow the measures identified in Volume 3, A9a On-site
        Traffic Management Plan of this ESIA.

8.7      IMPACT ASSESSMENT - OPERATIONS
8.7.1.   As discussed in Section 8.3, the assessment for the operational phase includes potential impacts in
         indoor area where activities will include:
          Routine operation and maintenance (O&M) - activities within the turbine hall and powerhouse
           may include functional checks on the turbine, gearbox inspections and lubrication, hydraulic
           systems inspection and oil change and sensor checks and recalibration; and
          Reactive maintenance – activities may include repairs and replacement of mechanical and
           electrical parts undertaken on a scheduled basis or as necessary.


         4 IAQM Guidance on the Assessment of Dust from Demolition and Construction, 2024.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                    PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                    June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 8-23
8.7.2.   The potential impacts on receptors, including workers and residents of the Pasi Muhraho village and
         Rogun City are also considered.
8.7.3.   During the operational phase, dust generated by O&M activities undertaken outdoors will be de
         minimis and are scoped out of the assessment.
         TSP, EXHAUST EMISSIONS AND OZONE EMISSIONS
8.7.4.   The primary changes from the construction to the operational phase include:
          A substantially smaller workforce of approximately 800 personnel is required to operate and
           maintain the HPP;
          A substantially smaller number of vehicles will be required, with mostly buses and light vehicles
           traversing the site; and
          Most energy demand can be met by renewable energy generated on site.
8.7.5.   Workers will undertake O&M activities within indoor areas such as the turbine hall, powerhouse and
         access tunnels. The sensitivity of workers as a receptor is high. The sensitivity of workers in outdoor
         areas and the residents of the adjacent village of Pasi Muhraho and of Rogun City is high.
         Impact Assessment
8.7.6.   Although far fewer vehicles will utilize the access tunnels relative to the construction phase,
         suspended particulates, exhaust emissions and ozone emissions have the potential to exceed the
         Project standards and the magnitude of the potential impact on workers that may be in the tunnels is
         large and the significance of the effect is major.
8.7.7.   Following completion of the construction phase, vehicle movements due to Project activities will be
         substantially reduced on roads passing the Pasi Muhraho village and within Rogun City, and the
         entrainment of particulate matter will be minimized due to the reduction in the number of vehicles
         and the roads being paved. The potential impact of suspended particulates and exhaust emissions
         on residents during the operational phase is scoped out of the assessment.
         Mitigation Measures
8.7.8.   The following mitigation measures have been identified to ensure that the air quality within indoor
         areas is maintained:
          Ensure that vehicles are maintained in accordance with manufacturer’s requirements to reduce
           excessive emissions. The maintenance of all vehicles must be documented to ensure that it
           aligns with manufacturer instructions supported by daily vehicle inspections. This requirement
           should be included in the vehicle maintenance plans;
          The roads within the tunnels must be paved or kept sufficiently damp to minimize entrainment of
           particulates by vehicle movements;
          Ensure that workers wear appropriate PPE including face coverings during the shift if workers will
           be in areas that may exceed AQ standards for short periods of time. The face covering must
           show a NIOSH certification mark or the CE certification mark or equivalent; and
          Enforce the 20 km/h speed limit within the tunnels.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                  June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 8-24
         Residual Effect Significance
8.7.9.   With the implementation of the mitigation for suspended particulates, exhaust emissions and ozone
         emissions, the significance of the effect can be reduced to moderate.
8.7.10. As moderate effects are considered significant, the OHS Plan must include an AQ monitoring
        program, implemented to assess the risk of air emissions on workers and identify the requirement
        for mitigation measures, including mechanical ventilation of the powerhouse and transformer room
        to maintain sufficient exchange of fresh air to comply with Project standards.
         LEFT BANK ROAD
8.7.11. The alignment of the left bank road is not yet finalized, but where it passes close to villages, the
        residents of the villages have a high sensitivity.
8.7.12. Once completed, the unpaved road surface will be a source of dust and finer particulate matter due
        to passing vehicles, especially during the dry season and in windy conditions. Dust entrained by
        vehicles is generally considered for receptors located within 50 m of roads5. Finer particulate matter
        may be deposited further away depending on the wind speed and direction, vegetation and
        topography. Particulates generated by engine exhaust emissions tend to settle out of suspension
        within 250 m from the source.
         Impact Assessment
8.7.13. The left bank road will be used by residents for access between villages and to travel to the larger
        centers located on the right bank. As an access road serving small villages and isolated dwellings,
        the road will not carry substantial volumes of traffic; however, the use of the road has the potential to
        impact on village residents depending on the final alignment.
8.7.14. Given the number of vehicles that will utilize the left bank road at any one time, it is unlikely that any
        of the Project standards for AQ determinants will be exceeded. The magnitude of the impact is very
        small, and the significance of the effect is minor, and no mitigation is proposed.
8.7.15. The left bank road will be the responsibility of the Jamoat and will be maintained in compliance with
        local requirements.

8.8      MONITORING AND ADAPTIVE MANAGEMENT
8.8.1.   Site-wide E&S standards and guidelines will be established by Rogun JSC in Volume 3, B6:
         Outline Air Quality Management Plan of this ESIA. The document will specify the Project AQ
         standards for sensitive receptors, identify the overarching requirements for AQ management,
         alignment with other Project management plans, and the responsibilities and coordination required
         for implementation, monitoring and reporting to Rogun JSC.
8.8.2.   For the construction phase, the EPC Contractor and all sub-contractors will each prepare a detailed
         AQ management plan that will be reviewed and approved by the Engineer's Representative and/or
         PMG. For the operational phase, the detailed management plan will be prepared and implemented
         by Rogun JSC as the operator of Rogun HPP.
8.8.3.   The detailed management plans will include, but not be limited to, the mitigation measures identified
         in this chapter. In the absence of representative AQ measurements to quantify the AQ baseline,


         5 IAQM Guidance on the Assessment of Dust from Demolition and Construction, 2024.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                    PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                    June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 8-25
         mitigation measures identified in this ESIA may not be sufficient and residual effects may remain
         significant. This has been addressed by including a requirement for further measures to be
         implemented, specifically for AQ monitoring to be undertaken to check AQ relative to the Project
         standards.
8.8.4.   The AQ monitoring to be undertaken requires a comprehensive and systematic monitoring program
         to be developed and implemented for the construction phase. The monitoring program should be
         developed by AQ specialists with expertise and experience of preparing monitoring plans for large
         construction sites having multiple types of activities that result in emissions to air.
8.8.5.   Specific requirements for AQ monitoring include, but are not limited to:
          Use of diffusion tubes for sampling, where appropriate, to collect long-term data for Project AQ
           determinants at locations of sensitive receptors. Air quality monitoring must include all
           determinants in Table 8-5;
          Use of Type I or Type II hand-held air quality monitoring equipment that is maintained, serviced
           and calibrated according to manufacturer instructions;
          Use of an accredited laboratory for the analysis of AQ samples; a robust chain of custody
           procedure should be documented and tested;
          Ensure that the averaging period/s used for monitoring of AQ determinants align with the Project
           standard (short-term or long-term) to ensure that the monitoring data and Project standards are
           directly comparable; and
          Commission an annual review of the monitoring system by a suitable qualified and experienced
           specialist to ensure that the ambient and indoor AQ meets Project standards, and that either the
           Project standards and/or the monitoring done by contractors is amended, as necessary to
           continually improve the monitoring program.
8.8.6.   PMG must instruct the contractor to stop work where monitoring indicates that Project standards are
         constantly exceeded for one or more AQ determinants. Work can restart when PMG or the
         representative is satisfied that AQ concentrations do not pose a hazard to workers; either through
         the ventilation of the workspace or workers using the specified PPE.
8.8.7.   All contractors are required to monitor for AQ and implement measures to reduce emissions,
         including dust, when concentrations exceed Project standards.
8.8.8.   If the monitoring indicates that Project standards for indoor AQ are exceeded, contractors will be
         required to install forced-air ventilation systems of sufficient capacity to maintain indoor AQ in
         compliance with Project standards.

         mitigation, is presented in Table 8-10 and Table 8-11 respectively.

9        GREENHOUSE GASES
9.1.1.   This chapter considers the impact of Greenhouse Gas (GHG) emissions during the Project
         construction and operation stages on global GHG.
9.1.2.   The approach to carbon management within any ESIA is to provide a clear and proportional
         assessment of GHG emissions that includes practical mitigation measures. This is achieved by
         proposing for inclusion only those emission sources that are expected to be significant, that are
         within the Project’s direct control or are expected to materially affect the assessment outcome.
9.1.3.   A comprehensive description of GHG baseline conditions are presented in Volume 2, Annex 7.1:
         Baseline Conditions Report and a summary is provided in Volume 1, Chapter 7: Environmental
         and Social Baseline of this ESIA. An assessment of the potential impacts due to GHG emissions is
         presented in Section 9.8. Mitigation measures for the avoidance of impact, impact minimization and
         long-term management are included in Section 9.9.
9.1.4.   Previous Project reports have not specifically considered the assessment of GHG emissions, and to
         date, the methane emissions produced by the reservoir have not been calculated, neither has any
         calculation of potential carbon savings from replacing fossil fuels with hydropower.

9.2      POYRY (2014) ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
         FOR ROGUN HYDOR POWER PLANT
9.2.1.   The 2014 ESIA report touched on the issue of GHG emissions, stating that:
            Under conditions where large amounts of biomass are submerged or eutrophication occurs due
             to high trophic productivity or the stock of organic material in the reservoir, high oxygen
             consumption results due to the breakdown of the biomass and the deeper layers of the reservoir
             become anoxic. This can lead to an emission of methane from the reservoir, predominantly in
             large reservoirs in moist tropical areas; and
            Hydropower can reduce GHG emissions by reducing the amount of CO2 emissions from the
             combustion of fossil fuels to produce electricity in thermal power plants.
9.2.2.   No calculation of GHG emissions from the Project was undertaken as part of the 2014 ESIA report.

9.3      LEGISLATIVE FRAMEWORK, POLICY AND GUIDANCE
9.3.1.   This section identifies the relevant legislation, policy and guidance for this GHG assessment.
         THE LAW OF THE REPUBLIC OF TAJIKISTAN ON ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION
9.3.2.   The Law does not refer to GHG emissions and GHG emissions are currently not controlled by
         legislation.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Vol.1          PUBLIC
                                                                                                 September 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page 9-1
         NATIONALLY DETERMINED CONTRIBUTION OF THE REPUBLIC OF TAJIKISTAN
         (FAO, 2022)
9.3.3.   Tajikistan submitted its updated Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) in June 2022, with input
         from various sectors that include revised unconditional and conditional emissions reduction targets
         for 2030 and increases the focus on adaptation. Tajikistan's updated NDC commits to a 50-60%
         reduction in emissions by 2030 compared to 1990 levels, conditional on international support. The
         country also set an unconditional emissions reduction target of 60-70% by 2030 compared to 1990
         levels.
         THE NATIONAL ACTION PLAN OF THE REPUBLIC OF TAJIKISTAN FOR CLIMATE
         CHANGE MITIGATION (FAO, 2003)
9.3.4.   The National Action Plan (NAP) 2003 is a national-wide policy aiming at implementing the
         commitments of the Republic of Tajikistan concerning the UN Framework Convention on Climate
         Change (UNFCCC). Tajikistan's NAP includes the following measures on reduction of GHG
         emissions and enhancing of natural sinks of carbon:
            Enhancement of energy efficiency in relevant sectors of the national economy;
            Application of effective technologies and use of energy sources that promotes high rates of
             economic growth and reduce or limit GHG emissions;
            Protection and enhancement of natural sinks and reservoirs of GHG emissions;
            Promotion of sustainable forest management practices, afforestation and reforestation;
            Promotion of sustainable forms of agriculture;
            Research on promotion, development and use of new and renewable energies together with
             advanced and environmentally sound technologies; and
            Encouragement of appropriate reforms in relevant sectors to promote measures to limit or
             reduce GHG emissions.

9.4      GHG GUIDANCE
9.4.1.   The approach to the calculation of GHG emissions is aligned with the guidance in:
            Greenhouse Gases from Reservoirs Caused by Biogeochemical Processes (World Bank,
             2017): This document by the World Bank acts as a technical note to provide guidance on how to
             assess GHG emissions from reservoirs at an early stage of the preparation process. It
             recommends the use of GHG Reservoir tool (G Res tool) which has been developed by the
             United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO) and the
             International Hydropower Association (IHA).
            GHG Protocol Corporate Standard (Greenhouse Gas Protocol, 2015) : The GHG Protocol
             Corporate Accounting and Reporting Standard provides requirements and guidance for
             companies and other organizations preparing a corporate-level GHG emissions inventory. The
             standard covers the accounting and reporting of seven greenhouse gases covered by the Kyoto
             Protocol – carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4), nitrous oxide (N2O), hydrofluorocarbons
             (HFCs), perfluorocarbons (PFCs), sulphur hexafluoride (SF6) and nitrogen trifluoride (NF3).




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Vol.1          PUBLIC
                                                                                                 September 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page 9-2
            Guidelines for Estimating Greenhouse Gas Emissions of Asian Development Bank
             Projects (ADB, 2017): The ADB guidance document puts forth general methodologies to
             estimate GHG emissions during project appraisal/development stage. The methodologies
             included are consistent with the approaches used by International Finance Institutions (IFI) and
             are based on the Clean Development Mechanism (CDM) methodologies and Japan Bank for
             International Cooperation’s Guidelines for Measurement, Reporting and Verification of GHG
             Emission Reductions (Japan Bank for International Cooperation’s J-MRV) (JBIC, 2018).

9.5      SCOPING
         SCOPE OF THE GHG ASSESSMENT
9.5.1.   The GHG assessment is not restricted by geographical area but instead includes “any increase or
         decrease in emissions as a result of the Project wherever they occur.” This normally includes:
            Construction emissions from the Project, including the transport of materials to and from the site
             and their manufacture;
            Biogenic emissions - which originate from the decomposition of organic materials like plants and
             soil. Such emissions often arise from submerged vegetation in reservoirs, causing organic
             material underwater to decompose and release gases like methane (CH4); and
            Decommissioning emissions.
9.5.2.   In accordance with the GHG Protocol, the potential sources of emissions for each scope are set
         out as per Table 9-1, including justification of each being scoped in or out of the assessment.

         Table 9-1 - Scope of the GHG Assessment

9.6      ASSESSMENT METHODOLOGY
         GENERAL APPROACH
9.6.1.   GHG emissions should be quantified following an internationally recognized methodology. The GHG
         Protocol: A Corporate Accounting and Reporting Standard (Greenhouse Gas Protocol, 2015) details
         the accounting and reporting of seven greenhouse gases covered by the Kyoto Protocol – carbon
         dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4), nitrous oxide (N2O), hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs), perfluorocarbons
         (PCFs), sulphur hexafluoride (SF6) and nitrogen trifluoride (NF3).
9.6.2.   For reporting purposes and to avoid double counting of emissions, the GHG Protocol divides GHG
         emissions into three separate ‘scopes’:
          Scope 1 Direct GHG emissions - from sources owned or controlled by the Project e.g. from a
           turbine;
          Scope 2 Electricity indirect GHG emissions - GHG emissions from generation of purchased
           electricity by the Project; and
          Scope 3 Other indirect emissions - emissions that result from Project activities but occur from
           sources not owned or controlled by the Project e.g. transport of fuels or materials. Scope 3
           emissions are considered an optional reporting category and can often be difficult to quantify.
9.6.3.   In accordance with the GHG Protocol, Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions for the Project are quantified and
         reported in this ESIA.
9.6.4.   To estimate GHG emissions, activity data (e.g. fuel consumption, electricity usage, transportation
         distances) should be multiplied by the corresponding emissions factors sourced from established
         databases, such as the UK Government GHG Conversion Factors for Company Reporting. The
         resulting emissions are converted into CO₂ equivalents (CO₂e) by applying the global warming
         potential (GWP) values as defined by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC).
9.6.5.   Construction emissions have been calculated and aligned to the GHG Protocol’s corporate reporting
         scopes, ensuring consistency with global reporting standards. Additionally, biogenic emissions have
         been assessed using the G-res tool, which is specifically designed to evaluate emissions associated
         with biogenic processes in hydropower reservoirs. Together, these methodologies offer a robust
         approach to capturing both construction and biogenic emissions accurately. Further detail of the
         approach to each aspect is given in the following sections.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Vol.1                  PUBLIC
                                                                                                         September 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                        Page 9-4
          Construction materials
9.6.6.    Emissions from the manufacturing of construction materials used on site are one of the major
          contributors to the total GHG emissions. These emissions form part of the Scope 3 construction
          emissions.
9.6.7.    The quantities of materials used in this assessment have been taken from Bill of Quantities (BOQs)
          for Lot 1, Lot 2, Lot 3 and Lot 4 and are summed to arrive at total quantity for each material type.
          Given the limited information in the BOQ for mechanical and electrical components, the assessment
          focusses primarily on civil infrastructure elements with appropriate uplift applied to account for other
          components. The assessment has been based on the following key elements that are the largest
          contributors of GHG emissions on civil infrastructure projects: excavation and embankment (dam
          construction), reinforced concrete and steel manufacturing.
9.6.8.    Emissions Factors for Embankment Sand and Rocks were taken from the research paper (Cuihong
          Song et. al., 2018), highlighting the emissions of different components of the dam structure. Factors
          for all the remaining assets listed are taken from Bath Inventory of Carbon and Energy (ICE) V3.0
          database (Bath ICE, 2019).
9.6.9.    The corresponding assumptions while extracting relevant material quantities and choosing emission
          factors have been listed in the Assumptions and Limitations section of this chapter.
9.6.10.   Construction materials have been assigned appropriate modelled materials using professional
          judgement and best practice. The emissions from the construction stage of the Project are assessed
          using the quantities and emission factors listed in Table 9-2.

          MATERIAL TRANSPORTATION
9.6.11.   The GHG emissions associated with the transportation of construction materials to the Project Site
          should be quantified as part of the overall carbon assessment. This includes evaluating the distance
          materials are transported, the modes of transportation used (e.g., trucks, rail, shipping), and the
          specific materials being delivered. Emission Factors for each transportation mode should be applied
          to the relevant activity data (e.g., tonne-km travelled) to estimate total emissions. Identifying
          opportunities for reducing these emissions may involve optimizing logistics, consolidating deliveries,
          and selecting lower-carbon transport options.
9.6.12.   The transport distances for Concrete and Steel have been assumed to be sourced from Primary
          Suppliers, as per Table 3-12 of Volume 1, Chapter 3: Project Description. The transport
          emissions have been assessed based on Average Laden load for journey to the Site, and Empty
          Laden load for journey away from the Site.
