          SITE EMISSIONS
9.6.13.   Site activity data from 2023 has been used to quantify emissions from fuel used on site (diesel and
          gas) and electricity. The total litres of fuel and annual electricity consumption (kWh) have been
          multiplied by an appropriate emissions factor for fuel and electricity to give an estimated total carbon
          emissions per year. The annual emissions have then been scaled for the 10-year construction
          period.
9.6.14.   Whilst the assessment outlined above focuses on civil infrastructure, it is important to account for
          emissions from other construction elements where data to assess was more limited. Based on
          previous life cycle assessments (EDP, n.d.)), it is estimated that approximately 70% of emissions
          are associated with civil assets. As such, an uplift has been included to account for emissions from
          mechanical and electrical components including turbines, transformers, pumps and cabling.



          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Vol.1                   PUBLIC
                                                                                                           September 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                         Page 9-6
          BIOGENIC EMISSIONS
9.6.15.   Emissions originating from biological processes which include the release of carbon dioxide (CO2)
          and methane (CH4), and other gases from organic material decomposition, primarily from plant
          matter and soil organic carbon. In the context of this Project, biogenic emissions assessed include
          the carbon released from both the decaying vegetation that was cleared and submerged vegetation,
          as well as from the organic content in soils disturbed or flooded due to the vegetation clearance and
          loss of vegetation during the Project construction.
9.6.16.   To assess GHG emissions from reservoirs at an early stage of the preparation process, it is
          recommended that the GHG Reservoir tool (G-Res Tool) (World Bank, n.d.) developed by the
          United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO) and the International
          Hydropower Association (IHA) is used to assess impact.
9.6.17.   The G-res Tool is a specialized assessment tool designed to estimate carbon footprint of reservoirs,
          including emissions of CO2 and CH4 released from reservoirs, particularly those associated with
          hydropower projects.
9.6.18.   Further information on the methodology for G-Res Tool, the required data inputs for the three
          categories and the data used to populate the G-res Tool is given in Appendix in Section 9.9.
          ASSUMPTIONS AND LIMITATIONS
          Construction materials and material transportation
9.6.19.   Given the high-level nature of design information at this time, aligned to RICS (2023), an uplift of 6%
          has been made to the construction emissions to account for uncertainly to the assessment during
          design and construction.
9.6.20.   The assessment has focused on civil infrastructure assets where more detailed information was
          available. To account for emissions from other areas (e.g. mechanical and electrical components)
          an uplift factor has been used based on previous studies which show civil infrastructure elements
          account for approximately 70% of construction emissions.
9.6.21.   For civil elements, only those materials which were quantifiable in terms of volume/weight (e.g.
          concrete (m3), steel (tonnes)), have been included in this assessment. Relevant density factors have
          been used to convert the quantities to arrive at final emission numbers.
9.6.22.   Any material quantity provided in terms of length (e.g. drainage (m); filling grouting (m2) etc.) or
          number of units (e.g. site investigations; stationery plant etc.) have been excluded from this
          assessment. The emissions from the excluded material items have the potential to increase
          emissions further, however it is not expected these elements to be significant and they are captured
          by the 6% uplift due to uncertainty.
9.6.23.   The diameter for the perforated drainage pipe (500mm) is assumed to be internal diameter, with
          thickness as 15mm and material composition as steel.
9.6.24.   Data for the dam structure has been taken from the BOQ and consists of the following materials,
          and the emission factors for these have been used in calculations:
             Sand for embankment, alluvium shell, filter and transition drains;
             Rock stones for rockfill shell and riprap; and
             Concrete core.


          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Vol.1           PUBLIC
                                                                                                   September 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page 9-7
9.6.25.   The embodied carbon does not account for emissions associated with on-site installation processes
          such as construction energy and fuel consumption, temporary works, and other on-site activities, are
          not calculated in this assessment. This exclusion is due to limited data on construction-phase
          installation activities and emissions specific to these.
9.6.26.   In the absence of specific Environmental Product Declarations outlining materials, specifications,
          and transport categories, the emission factors chosen for construction emissions and transport
          distances are taken from internationally recognized databases and are appropriate for the stage of
          assessment in the absence of data from specific manufacturers.
9.6.27.   The operational phase, which would include emissions from routine maintenance and repair
          activities, is scoped out from this assessment. Emissions from this category would be insignificant
          compared to those during the construction phase and it would be expected that with time, as the
          construction industry decarbonizes, that emissions from this phase would become less with time.
          Similarly, these impacts would be expected to be insignificant compared to the biogenic impacts,
          referenced below.
9.6.28.   Emissions from the end-of-life stage, including decommissioning activities and any restoration of
          disturbed land, are also excluded from the current assessment. Given the long lifespan of the
          project, it is likely that any decommissioning activities would take place at time when construction
          impact has been decarbonized and thus the impact at this stage would be minimal.
9.6.29.   This assessment serves as an indicative assessment due to limited data provided for materials and
          transport distances. All the data used in assessment is considered up to date and was provided by
          the Project site teams; however no independent verification of the raw data was done.
          Biogenic Emissions
9.6.30.   To obtain information on soil organic content, Geographical Information Systems (GIS) mapping was
          conducted using soil grid profiles from the International Soil Reference and Information Centre
          (ISRIC). ISRIC offers a high-resolution global dataset, SoilGrids, which provides predictive soil
          properties and classifications across multiple depths. This dataset enables detailed spatial analysis
          of soil organic carbon and other critical soil parameters, enhancing the accuracy and depth of
          environmental assessments.
9.6.31.   It is assumed that the site area has undergone significant modification in the past due to activities
          such as grazing, agricultural practices, and urban development in the surrounding regions, leading
          to a reduction in soil organic matter compared to undisturbed conditions, as observed through GIS
          mapping and site observations, which also indicate a higher presence of minerals and bare rock,
          with soil mineral content calculated as the remainder after deducting estimated organic content,
          potentially not fully reflecting the site's soil diversity.
9.6.32.   Organic soil carbon stock for the pre-impoundment land cover area is assessed up to a depth of 30
          cm, which is where the highest concentration of carbon typically resides and limited data capability
          within the Soil Grid mapping.
9.6.33.   At maximum inundation, the reservoir is assumed to reach a height of 1,290 meters above sea level
          (m asl) with the dam's base at 1,000 m asl obtained as detailed in Volume 1, Chapter 3: Project
          Description of this ESIA.




          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Vol.1           PUBLIC
                                                                                                   September 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page 9-8
9.6.34.   While the G-res tool estimates emissions based on average values derived from typical operational
          conditions, it assumes that real-world conditions will align with these parameters. The tool does not
          account for the potential impact of thermal stratification, which could influence water temperature
          layers in reservoirs and affect energy generation efficiency. This limitation means that variations in
          thermal stratification, which might occur due to seasonal changes or environmental factors, may not
          be reflected in the emissions estimate. However, the tool provides a reliable baseline estimate, with
          total emissions expected to remain close to the predicted value over the project lifecycle, assuming
          typical operational conditions and uniform thermal mixing.

9.7       BASELINE – CURRENT CONDITIONS
9.7.1.    To assess the impact of the GHG emissions generated from the Project, a ‘baseline’ comparison of
          Tajikistan’s national emissions has been selected to provide context to the impact of the Project.
          The total GHG emissions in the country was 13.974 million tCO2e in 2016 (including land use, land
          use change and forestry) (Government of the Republic of Tajikistan, 2022). Tajikistan’s major
          emission sources are from agriculture (50%), energy (47%), industrial process and product use
          (12%) in 2016. Waste and land use, land-use change and forestry make up the remaining emission
          contributions for the country.
9.7.2.    Tajikistan’s current grid average emissions factor per kilowatt-hour of electricity produced is 106
          gCO2e/kWh (UNFCCC, 2021). This will be used as a counterfactual to assess the impact of the
          proposed development against the equivalent power requirement being generated at Tajikistan
          current average.
9.7.3.    For construction emissions, it has been assumed that there has been no construction previously and
          as such, any emissions from construction activities are an increase on a baseline of zero. While this
          is not an accurate assumption, it is a more conservative one.
9.7.4.    With regards to the biogenic assessment, the ‘do minimum’ and ‘do something’ scenarios are
          described below:
          ‘DO MINIMUM’ SCENARIO
9.7.5.    The ‘Do Minimum’ scenario comprises of “without project” baseline emissions associated with the
          current operations of 2x400MW units generating power.
          Pre-impoundment soil organic carbon
9.7.6.    Soil Organic Carbon (SOC) is the carbon stored within soil organic matter, which is composed of
          decomposing plant and animal materials, microorganisms, and stable organic compounds that resist
          further decomposition. SOC is a key indicator of soil health, as it helps improve soil structure, water
          retention, and nutrient availability, supporting plant growth and sustaining ecosystems (IPCC, 2016)
9.7.7.    In the context of reservoir and land-use changes, assessing SOC is crucial for several reasons:
             Carbon Emissions: When land is flooded for reservoir creation, previously dry soils rich in
              organic carbon become submerged. This process can disrupt the natural carbon cycle, as
              anaerobic (oxygen-limited) conditions in submerged soils may trigger the decomposition of
              SOC, releasing greenhouse gases such as CH₄ and CO₂ into the atmosphere. Estimating SOC
              in the reservoir area helps predict potential emissions and understand the reservoir’s impact on
              climate change.




          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Vol.1           PUBLIC
                                                                                                   September 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page 9-9
                 Baseline for Carbon Stock Loss: By assessing SOC levels in land destined for flooding,
                  researchers establish a baseline for the amount of carbon that might be lost due to land-use
                  change. This baseline aids in comparing pre- and post-impoundment conditions, helping
                  measure how much organic carbon has shifted or emitted as greenhouse gases after the
                  reservoir’s creation.
                 Land-Use Change Impact: SOC levels differ significantly across habitat types—forests,
                  grasslands, and agricultural fields, for example, vary in their carbon densities. Understanding
                  SOC by habitat type in a reservoir area helps anticipate and mitigate the environmental
                  impacts of converting various landscapes to water bodies.
9.7.8.    To assess SOC stock across the project area, we utilized soil data from the SoilGrids database,
          available on the ISRIC (International Soil Reference and Information Centre) website. This database
          provides SOC values and bulk density estimates for various land cover types up to a depth of 30
          cm. The process involved the following steps:
             Mapping SOC: SOC values from SoilGrids were mapped across different land cover types in
              the project area, yielding average SOC stock estimates (t/ha) for each type.
             Conversion to Soil Organic Matter (SOM): Soil Organic Carbon (SOC) is key fraction of SOM,
              typically accounting for about 58% of total SOM content ( E.V. Shamrikova et al., 2022). SOC
              stock was converted to soil organic matter by applying a standard conversion factor of 1.724.
              This conversion provided an estimate of SOM, which accounts for the total organic material
              within the soil.

 9.7.9.

                                   Soil Organic Matter = Soil Organic Carbon * 1.724


             Calculation of Bulk Mass: The soil's bulk mass is calculated by multiplying bulk density by the
              specified depth (30 cm). This step provided the soil's total mass within the 30 cm depth profile,
              which is essential for estimating the overall SOM percentage in the soil.

          Table 9-4 – Relative Landcover coverage based in the catchment area

                                Habitat Type               Relative Coverage (%)

                                Croplands                  18.6%

                                Bare Areas                 15.1%

                                Forest                     3.8%

                                Grassland/Shrubland        29.9%

                                Permanent Snow / ice       32.2%

                                Settlements                0.2%

                                Water Bodies /             0.1%
                                Drained Peatlands




          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Vol.1           PUBLIC
                                                                                                   September 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page 9-10
             Deriving SOC Percentage: To determine the percentage of organic content in the soil, the
              SOM value has been divided by the calculated bulk mass. This resulted in the organic
              percentage for each land cover type, which helps evaluate soil carbon stocks across the
              reservoir’s future inundation area.

          Table 9-5 - Relative % distribution of Minerals and Organics based on different Habitat types

                          Habitat Type                             % of Mineral Soil

                          Cropland/Agriculture                     5.0%

                          Urban/Built-up                           15%

                          Grassland/Pasture                        49%

                          Wetland/Flooded Areas                    0.4%

                          Forest/Woodland                          1%

                          Water Bodies / Drained peatlands         30%


9.7.10.   As seen in pictures and through site surveys, the percentage of organic content in the project area is
          relatively low across different habitat types. The site has been heavily modified since the 1960s due
          to agricultural activities and grazing practices, which have significantly altered the land cover.
          Further exploration of the site confirmed that extensive modifications were made for various
          purposes, contributing to the reduction in organic carbon levels in the soil.
9.7.11.   For the purpose of this assessment, and in line with the G-Res Tool methodology, soils are
          categorized as either mineral soils or organic soils. While all soils contain some level of organic
          content, organic soils are specifically defined as those with a high carbon content. In the case of the
          Rogun reservoir catchment and analysis using Google Earth Engine confirms that the buffer area
          around the reservoir shows relatively low organic soil land cover present. Therefore, the percentage
          of organic soils in the different land cover types have been assumed to be zero for the purposes of
          this assessment.
          ‘DO SOMETHING’ SCENARIO
9.7.12.   The ‘Do something’ scenario assesses the emissions from the whole project – both construction and
          biogenic emissions.
9.7.13.   As part of Phase 2, the 2 x 400 MW (“without project”) will be upgraded to 2 x 630 MW along with
          the additional 4 x 630MW units. Hence, the total power generated in the “with project” would be
          3,780 MW (6 x 630 MW).




          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Vol.1           PUBLIC
                                                                                                   September 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page 9-11
          Soil Carbon Content under Impounded Area
9.7.14.   Soil carbon content under the impounded area refers to the total amount of organic carbon stored in
          the soil layers (typically the top 30 cm) of land that will be submerged by a reservoir once it’s filled.
          When land is inundated, the submerged soil becomes anaerobic (lacking oxygen), which slows
          down the decomposition of organic material but can still result in emissions over time. For
          reservoirs, calculating the SOC within the impounded area helps estimate potential greenhouse gas
          emissions due to the new water coverage. It also serves as a baseline for carbon stock loss in areas
          previously used for agriculture, forestry, or natural vegetation.
9.7.15.   The G-res Tool, developed for assessing GHG emissions from reservoirs, requires SOC values (in
          kgC/m²) to estimate these emissions based on the amount of carbon stored in the soil and the
          anticipated anaerobic conditions after impoundment.
9.7.16.   To calculate SOC for the impounded area, data from the SoilGrids database (ISRIC) can be used to
          estimate SOC concentration, bulk density, and soil depth. The methodology involves converting
          SOC concentration (usually in %) into SOC stock (kgC/m²) with the following steps:
          Calculate SOC Stock: Using the formula



                   (SOC) Stock (kgC/m2) = Bulk Density (g/cm3) * depth (cm) * SOC % * 1/10000



9.7.17.   This approach provides SOC in kg of carbon per m² for the top 30 cm of soil.
             Summing SOC across Land Types: Aggregate SOC values across habitat types in the
              impounded area to provide the total SOC stock. This approach, based on methods detailed by
              ISRIC, helps estimate carbon stocks accurately, especially for assessing land inundation
              impacts in reservoirs (ISRIC, 2024; G-res Tool Methodology).
9.7.18.   The calculation will involve assessing soil samples collected to a depth of 30 cm- where majority of
          SOC is typically found. The formula used for this calculation will incorporate the bulk density of the
          soil, which is necessary to convert the SOC concentration (kgC/m3) to a stock value across the
          specific depth (IPCC, 2006). This approach ensures a comprehensive evaluation of the impacts of
          reservoir inundation on SOC and overall soil health, supporting effective land management and
          climate resilience strategies.
          Total Soil carbon content under Impounded area (kgC/m2) = 7.746 kgC/m2

9.8       ASSESSING SIGNIFICANCE
9.8.1.    The Project impacts have been assessed against baseline conditions that include embedded
          mitigation (i.e. mitigation that is an inherent component of the Project design). The significance of
          the effect indicates the requirement for additional mitigation. The post-mitigation outcome has also
          been assessed to determine the significance of the residual effect.




          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Vol.1             PUBLIC
                                                                                                     September 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page 9-12
         Sensitive Receptors
9.8.2.   The impact of GHG emissions relate to their contribution to global warming and climate change.
         These impacts are global and cumulative in nature, with every tonne of GHG contributing to impacts
         on natural and human systems. The receptor is therefore the global atmosphere. As there is only
         one receptor ‘the climate’, it has a consistent sensitivity (high) no matter the location of the
         emissions source.
         Significance Criteria
9.8.3.   There are currently no agreed thresholds for what level of GHG emissions generated in new
         development is considered to be ‘significant’ for ESIA. The significance of GHG emissions is
         assigned with reference to the magnitude of emissions, their context, guidance (such as the Institute
         of Environmental Management and Assessment (IEMA, 2022)), and the use of experience and
         professional judgement. As climate change impacts are global in nature, it is not possible to link an
         overall global effect with a specific project and determine the specific environmental-climate impact.
9.8.4.   Although the IEMA Guidance is specific to UK, it is considered as a best practice approach to
         assess GHG emissions and significance of projects, irrespective of their geographical location. It
         provides a scale of significance by which to assess the current Project. A development’s emissions
         should be based on its net impact over its lifetime, which may be beneficial, adverse, or negligible.
         The evaluation of significance should not just focus on GHG emissions, or the magnitude of those
         emissions, but whether the Project contributes to reducing GHG emissions relative to a comparable
         baseline consistent with a trajectory towards net zero by 2050. Significance of GHG impacts for the
         Proposed Scheme is assessed in line with IEMA Guidance as set out in Table 9-6 -
9.8.5.   Whilst there is no scale for ‘receptor sensitivity’ or ‘impact magnitude’ for GHG emissions following
         those described in Table 5-2 Impact Significance Table in Chapter 5 of this ESIA, the level of
         impact (e.g. major, moderate, minor, negligible, positive) is aligned between this and IEMA and
         experience and professional judgement has been used to determine the overall impact of
         significance.

         Table 9-6 - Significance of GHG Impacts

          Level of Effect    Significance Criteria                                                         Significance

                             The GHG impacts are not mitigated or are only compliant with do-
                             minimum standards set through regulation, and do not provide further
          Major adverse      reductions required by existing local and national policy nor make a          Significant
                             meaningful contribution to the country’s trajectory towards net zero.

                             The GHG impacts are partially mitigated and may partially meet the
                             applicable existing and emerging policy requirements but would not fully
          Moderate
                             contribute to decarbonisation in line with local and national policy goals,   Significant
          adverse
                             falling short of fully contributing to the country’s trajectory towards net
                             zero.

                             The GHG impacts are fully consistent with applicable existing and
                             emerging policy requirements and good practice design standards; they         Not
          Minor adverse
                             are fully in line with measures necessary to achieve the country’s            significant
                             trajectory towards net zero.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Vol.1                    PUBLIC
                                                                                                           September 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                         Page 9-13
          Level of Effect    Significance Criteria                                                       Significance

                             The GHG impacts are reduced through measures that go well beyond
                             existing and emerging policy and design standards for projects of this      Not
          Negligible
                             type, such that radical decarbonisation or net zero is achieved well        significant
                             before 2050.

                             The net GHG impacts are below zero, causing a reduction in
                             atmospheric GHG concentration, whether directly or indirectly,

                             compared to the without-project baseline, substantially exceeding net

9.9      POTENTIAL IMPACTS
9.9.1.   The GHG emissions from the Project have been calculated and contextualized with various other
         power generating sources and against Tajikistan’s annual carbon emissions and NDC. The resultant
         construction and biogenic emissions are discussed in this section.
         CONSTRUCTION (SCOPE 3)
9.9.2.   The construction emissions from material extraction, manufacturing and production and
         transportation together account for 6.60 MtCO2e.

9.9.3.   The majority of emissions generated in the construction phase are from the embankment which
         represented approximately 3 MtCO2e. This is expected as the total volume of sand, stone and
         concrete needed for the shell and core of the embankment equated to over 72 million m3 of material.
         BIOGENIC EMISSIONS (SCOPE 1)

         recognized that metrics such as emissions/m2/year simplify complex dynamics, including depth
         variation, stratification, and year-to-year changes, for practical purposes. However, this metric
         represents the average conditions and provides an accessible means of assessing the reservoir's
         overall GHG footprint.

9.9.5.   This analysis evaluates the GHG emissions associated with reservoir impoundment by comparing
         pre- and post-impoundment emission metrics. The assessment focuses on three key indicators:
            Emissions per square meter of reservoir area;
            Total annual reservoir emissions; and
            Cumulative lifetime emissions.
9.9.6.   Understanding these metrics provides insights into how the reservoir’s creation affects GHG
         emissions on both an immediate and long-term basis.
         EMISSIONS PER M² OF RESERVOIR (GCO₂E/M²/YR)
9.9.7.   The emissions intensity per square meter is estimated at 64 gCO₂e/m²/yr under post-impoundment
         conditions, compared to negligible pre-impoundment emissions (0 gCO₂e/m²/yr). This indicates that
         the net GHG footprint of the Rogun reservoir can be fully attributed to the creation and operation of
         the impoundment. The pre-impoundment baseline contributes only a minor negative offset (–30
         tCO₂e/yr), reflecting the already modified state of the land.
         TOTAL BIOGENIC RESERVOIR EMISSIONS
9.9.8.   Post-impoundment, the project is expected to generate approximately 10,908 tCO₂e/yr of biogenic
         emissions. Over a 100-year project lifetime, this equates to a cumulative total of about 1.09 MtCO₂e.
         The pre-impoundment baseline is negligible (–30 tCO₂e/yr, or –3,040 tCO₂e over the lifetime),
         meaning that the reservoir contributes a net increase of roughly 10,938 tCO₂e/yr, or 1.09 MtCO₂e
         over 100 years. While this represents a substantial quantity of carbon released, the net footprint is
         considered modest relative to the project’s renewable electricity generation potential (17,000
         GWh/yr), equivalent to about 0.64 gCO₂e/kWh.
9.9.9.    As noted earlier, the post-impoundment emissions are estimated at 64 gCO₂e/m²/yr. This trend is
         consistent with many reservoir projects, where the inundation of soils and biomass creates
         anaerobic conditions that enhance methane (CH₄) and carbon dioxide (CO₂) production. In contrast,
         the pre-impoundment land within the reservoir footprint was already heavily modified, primarily used
         for grazing, with much of the natural vegetation removed and soils disturbed. As a result, baseline
         emissions were minimal, with the small negative value suggesting a minor sink or offset under
         certain land cover conditions. While limited, some emissions during land clearing may still result




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Vol.1             PUBLIC
                                                                                                    September 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page 9-15
          from the release of soil organic carbon previously stored underground, which becomes oxidized
          when exposed (Fearnside, P.M., 2004) and (Amin, M.A.., et al., 2021).
9.9.10.   Once the reservoir is filled during the post-impoundment phase, emissions from land clearing and
          biomass removal cease. At this stage, the primary source of emissions is the anaerobic
          decomposition of submerged soils and organic material, which produces methane (CH₄) and, to a
          lesser extent, carbon dioxide (CO₂). While the initial release of carbon from inundated biomass can
          be significant, the rate of soil carbon release gradually stabilizes over time. A portion of the organic
          carbon transported into the reservoir becomes buried in sediments, leading to long-term carbon
          sequestration, which partially offsets decomposition-related emissions (McGuire, K.J., et al., 2013)
          and (Fearnside, P.M., 2006). For the Rogun project, post-impoundment emissions are estimated at
          approximately 10,908 tCO₂e/yr, which corresponds to the net biogenic footprint of the reservoir
          after accounting for pre-impoundment baseline conditions.
9.9.11.   Over the 100-year lifetime of the project, the reservoir is projected to generate cumulative biogenic
          emissions of approximately 1,093,828 tCO₂e under post-impoundment conditions. By comparison,
          the pre-impoundment baseline represents a negligible contribution of about -3,040 tCO₂e, which
          reflects the limited emissions potential of the previously disturbed, grazing-dominated land. The
          higher post-impoundment emissions are primarily linked to the long-term anaerobic decomposition
          of flooded soils and organic matter, whereas the small pre-impoundment value reflects the land’s
          already modified state. Importantly, a portion of organic carbon transported into the reservoir is
          buried in sediments, contributing to long-term sequestration that partially offsets ongoing emissions
          (McGuire, K.J., et al., 2013) and (Fearnside, P.M., 2006). In summary, while the transition from pre-
          to post-impoundment results in a net increase in emissions attributable to the reservoir, the overall
          footprint remains relative lower when compared to its electricity generation potential. (McGuire et
          al., 2013; Fearnside, 2006).
9.9.12.   The breakdown of carbon dioxide and methane emissions by Project phase is presented in Table
          9-9.

          Table 9-9 - Emissions Breakdown (CO2 and CH4)

          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Vol.1              PUBLIC
                                                                                                      September 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                   Page 9-16
9.9.13.   The assessment of GHG emissions focuses on the overall emissions rate, the breakdown of
          emissions by gas type (CO₂ and CH₄), and the emissions per unit area. Each metric provides insight
          into how the impoundment influences the reservoir's GHG footprint, particularly examining the
          contributions of CH₄, which can significantly impact the climate due to its higher global warming
          potential compared to CO₂.
9.9.14.   The reservoir’s CH₄ and CO₂ emissions are relatively low compared to global benchmarks across
          different pathways as shown in Figure 9-1. The assessment of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions
          from the Rogun reservoir considers overall emissions rates, the breakdown between carbon dioxide
          (CO₂) and methane (CH₄), and emissions per unit area. While CO₂ is the dominant gas emitted,
          CH₄ remains important due to its significantly higher global warming potential. Compared with global
          benchmarks, the Rogun reservoir’s emissions are relatively low. CH₄ diffusive emissions are
          estimated at 16 gCO₂e/m²/yr, placing the project near the lower quartile of global distributions and
          indicating limited methane production or effective diffusion mitigation. CH₄ bubbling emissions are
          minimal at about 1 gCO₂e/m²/yr, suggesting that sediment conditions and low organic inputs restrict
          bubble formation and release. CH₄ degassing emissions are reported as zero, although this reflects
          a lack of data on water column stratification and discharge rather than a confirmed absence,
          highlighting the need for additional monitoring. CO₂ diffusive emissions are estimated at 44
          gCO₂e/m²/yr, positioning the reservoir near the median of global benchmarks and reflecting the
          continued decomposition of organic matter within the flooded area. Taken together, these results
          suggest that the Rogun reservoir has a relatively low climate impact compared to global averages,
          though further monitoring particularly of potential degassing pathways is recommended to fully
          characterize its GHG profile.

          Figure 9-1 - CH4 and CO2 emissions compared to worldwide reservoirs




9.9.15.   Whilst CO2 emissions fall by approximately 58% a year, CH4 emission increase by approximately
          116%. Although CO2 emissions are higher, the potency of CH₄ (25-28 times that of CO₂ over 100
          years) elevates the climate impact due to this increase in global warming potential. The overall
          reduction is a positive shift, lowering the CO₂-specific footprint of the reservoir. The substantial rise
          in CH₄ is a result of organic material submerged by the impoundment. Underwater, organic matter
          decomposes anaerobically, leading to CH₄ production—a potent greenhouse gas. This effect is
          typical in reservoirs but can vary depending on factors like organic load, depth, and water
          temperature. This increase offsets the benefits gained from CO₂ reduction and adds significant
          climate impact due to methane’s higher global warming potential. Despite the small overall change


          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Vol.1              PUBLIC
                                                                                                      September 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                   Page 9-17
          per area, CH₄ emissions per area rise significantly post-impoundment. This finding aligns with the
          overall CH₄ increase, reinforcing that CH₄ production has become a dominant contributor to
          emissions following impoundment. Post-impoundment dynamics significantly influence CH4
          emissions due to changes in environmental conditions. The flooding of organic matter, such as
          vegetation and soil, creates a rich carbon source that decomposes under anaerobic conditions,
          producing methane. Impoundment often leads to stratification, where warmer, oxygen-rich surface
          waters are separated from cooler, methane-rich bottom layers by a thermocline. Seasonal variations
          further impact these dynamics; for example, strong summer stratification can lead to methane
          buildup, while mixing during cooler seasons or events like water release can result in sudden,
          significant methane emissions. These processes highlight the need for a comprehensive
          understanding of post-impoundment conditions to accurately quantify CH4 emissions.
9.9.16.   As shown in Figure 9-2, 82% of post-impoundment CH₄ emissions arise from diffusion, indicating
          surface-driven release likely influenced by temperature, wind, and organic matter. Degassing
          contributes 12%, suggesting some CH4 is released through deeper water discharges, potentially
          from stratified layers. Bubbling accounts for 7%, indicating limited CH4 escape from sediments.
          Overall, diffusive emissions dominate, while degassing and bubbling play smaller but notable roles.

          Figure 9-2 - Post-impoundment CH4 emissions




9.9.17.   Figure 9-3 shows the graph illustrating the annual net GHG emissions per square meter over the
          lifespan of a reservoir, showing a steep decline in emissions in the initial years post-impoundment.

          Figure 9-3 - Annual net GHG emissions trajectory through the lifecycle of the reservoir




9.9.18.   Emissions start high, above 600 gCO₂e/m²/yr, likely due to rapid CH₄ release from the anaerobic
          decomposition of submerged organic material, but they decrease sharply within the first 20 years



          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Vol.1          PUBLIC
                                                                                                  September 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page 9-18
          and gradually stabilize below zero over time. This trend suggests that while reservoirs have a
          significant GHG impact in the early years, their long-term emissions footprint is much lower thus
          giving a carbon reduction over the Project’s lifespan.
9.9.19.   The black line in Figure 9-3, representing the 100-year integrated net GHG level, provides a
          baseline that balances this initial surge with the subsequent decline, giving a more comprehensive
          view of the reservoir's climate impact over a century. This pattern emphasizes the potential
          effectiveness of early-stage mitigation strategies, such as pre-impoundment vegetation removal, to
          reduce the high initial emissions and lower the reservoir’s cumulative GHG impact in the long run.

          Figure 9-4 - Annual Total GHG emissions by emission pathways




9.9.20.   Figure 9-4 illustrates the annual total GHG emissions (gCO₂e/m²/yr) from the reservoir over a 100-
          year period, disaggregated by key emission pathways: CH₄ degassing, CH₄ bubbling, CH₄ diffusive,
          and CO₂ diffusive emissions. The graph demonstrates that GHG emissions are highest immediately
          following impoundment, primarily driven by the rapid decomposition of submerged biomass and
          organic matter. During this initial stage, CH₄ degassing and CO₂ diffusive emissions are the
          dominant contributors, reflecting intense microbial activity and the release of gases from deeper,
          anoxic layers of the water column and from the decomposition of organic carbon. As the reservoir
          ages, total emissions decline significantly, stabilizing after approximately 60 to 80 years. CH₄
          degassing shows the steepest decline within the first two decades, likely due to the depletion of
          easily degradable organic material and reduced methane production in deeper waters as thermal
          stratification becomes less pronounced or as management strategies mitigate its formation. In
          contrast, CH₄ bubbling and diffusive emissions persist at lower rates over a longer period, indicating
          a gradual release of methane from sediments where decomposition continues at a slower rate. CO₂
          diffusive emissions also decline sharply after the first few years, suggesting that carbon
          mineralisation and microbial respiration in the surface waters diminish as organic inputs decrease
          over time. By the later decades of the reservoir’s life (post-60 years), total GHG emissions stabilise
          at significantly lower levels, with residual contributions mainly from CH₄ bubbling and diffusion. This
          long-term emission profile underscores the importance of early-stage management to limit peak
          emissions, particularly by minimising the amount of organic material inundated during reservoir filling
          and addressing thermal stratification that drives CH₄ degassing. These mitigation strategies can
          substantially reduce the reservoir’s climate impact in its most emission-intensive years.



          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Vol.1           PUBLIC
                                                                                                   September 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page 9-19
          Summary of Impacts
9.9.21.   As a final step, to help better understand the summary of impacts related to the hydropower
          generation capacity of the reservoir, the results of the construction assessment were added with the
          biogenic emissions in the G-res tool to help identify an emissions intensity over the project life cycle.
9.9.22.   The combination of the construction emissions and biogenic emissions give an estimated emissions
          intensity of approximately 4.5 gCO2e/kWh for the proposed scheme. This is substantially lower than
          the current grid average emission factor for Tajikistan of 106 gCO2e/kWh.

9.10      MITIGATION MEASURES
9.10.1.   GHG mitigation for the Project are discussed for the project construction and operational phases.
          CONSTRUCTION PHASE
9.10.2.   To reduce the carbon footprint of the Project, the following mitigation measures for the construction
          phase have already been implemented:
                 Green air ventilation system installed - an automated ventilation system capable of
                  optimizing jet fan speed based on real-time pollutants measurements to provide continuous
                  monitoring of pollutants (CO2, NO2) and ventilation speed adaptation to reduce energy
                  consumption by 6,023 kWh/day; and emissions by 337.28 kg CO2e/day (123 tCO2e/annum).
                 Installation of the remaining 6.02 km of the electrically controlled conveyor belt system to
                  transfer crushed materials from crusher plants to silos near the dam area. The system is
                  expected to result in annual energy savings of 9 million kWh and annual CO2e savings of
                  13,500 tonnes. (1 Ton = 0.99 tonnes; values in tons have been converted to tonnes in order
                  to maintain uniformity throughout the assessment.)
                 Connection of a direct power source was already solicited from the Nurek Power Station.
                  The external power feeds into a 16,000kVA transformer substation which subsequently
                  phases down the infeed power into usable electricity power source. In this way, the direct
                  power feed effectively replaces the use of 12 generator sets of 1,200kVA. This is expected to
                  result in 101 million kWh energy savings and 71,600 tCO2e savings annually.
                 Energy efficient lighting has been installed at various locations of the site. LED lighting is
                  expected to result in annual energy savings of 239,919 kWh for the main offices and camp
                  area and 460,776 kWh for the workshop area.
                 The installation of energy efficient water hoses, energy and water efficient water guns
                  significantly reduces consumption of on-site water resources for dust suppression. Energy
                  savings also result from reduced water truck mileage. These measures are expected to lead
                  to annual energy savings of 76,000 kWh and annual savings of 69 tCO2e.
                 Transportation emissions have been reduced and are expected to result in energy savings of
                  6 million kWh per year and CO2e savings of 904 tonnes per year by:
                  • Deploying only vehicles that will get better fuel mileage;
                  • Ensuring regular and proper vehicles’ technical inspection and maintenance;
                  • Re-siting expat camps next to Main Office to cut down travelling; and
                  • Using multiple-passenger vans or bus to transport staff.




          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Vol.1             PUBLIC
                                                                                                     September 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page 9-20
9.10.3.   To reduce these emissions further, it is recommended that all engines are turned off when not in use
          to be implemented through the CEMP.
          BIOGENIC EMISSIONS
9.10.4.   The recommended GIIP measures to reduce the generation and release of methane and other
          GHGs to the atmosphere include:
                 Hydropower projects like this Project, must consider alternative project design configurations
                  to minimize emissions wherever possible:
                  • Forestry Agency, under the guidance and supervision of the Directorate for Flooding Zone
                    (DFZ), is obliged to clear of vegetation all the areas to be flooded before each inundation
                    stage.
                  • Implement mechanical mixing systems, air bubble plumes, or controlled water flow
                    management to disrupt thermal stratification and maintain oxygen levels throughout the
                    water column. This prevents anaerobic conditions in deeper layers, reducing methane
                    emissions. Regular monitoring of water temperature, dissolved oxygen, and methane
                    concentrations should guide mixing operations to target hotspots effectively. Where
                    feasible, utilize renewable energy sources to power mixing systems and ensure long-term
                    sustainability.
                  • This involves carefully controlling how water is released from the reservoir to avoid the
                    build-up of methane in the deeper layers. By releasing water from the surface, where
                    there are fewer greenhouse gases, and occasionally lowering the water level, it helps mix
                    the water and adds oxygen. This process reduces the chance of organic material breaking
                    down in low-oxygen areas, which would release methane. By preventing the formation of
                    anaerobic (low oxygen) zones where methane is produced, optimized flow management
                    helps reduce biogenic emissions, particularly methane, from the reservoir.
                  • The permanent water intakes under construction will be located at quite higher level. This
                    is considered as design factor for GHG emissions and climate change.
9.10.5.   EIB requires that all hydropower projects considered as large hydropower projects, undertake direct
          and continuous monitoring of CO2e emissions during operation. The assessment is based on
          available Scope 1-3 data. To manage these emissions, it is recommended that an ongoing
          monitoring and mitigation plan (GHG Reduction Plan) of operational GHG sources is put in place.
          Whilst the mitigation measures listed above help to mitigate emissions, there is always more that
          can be undertaken to reduce GHG emissions further. Table 9-10 provides further opportunities
          which exist and could be included in the GHG Reduction Plan.

9.11.5.    A summary of the direct GHG emissions over the Project’s construction and operation are presented
           in Table 9-14. The total emissions over the assumed 100-year lifespan is 7.69 MtCO2e.

           Table 9-14 - Total GHG emissions over the Project’s lifetime

 9.11.6.   Phase 2 of construction will finish in 2032. Assuming an even split of emissions each year of
           construction (assuming 10 years), the annual impact of the construction equates to 0.660 MtCO2e.
           This represents approximately 4.7% of Tajikistan’s annual emissions (based on the 2016 baseline).
           This is a substantial impact for one project and provides a risk of Tajikistan not meeting is short-term
           NDC carbon targets. As such, the impact of the scheme aligned to IEMA’s criteria, before mitigation,
           is Moderate Adverse for construction emissions.
 9.11.7.   The assessment of Biogenic Emissions shows overall emissions of approximately 1.09 MtCO2e over
           the 100-year project lifespan. However, compared to the pre-impoundment scenario (-3,040 tCO2e
           over the 100years), this represents a net increase of about 1.09 MtCO2e.. Whilst this indicates as
           overall increase in emissions over the project lifetime, as discussed in Sections 9.8.8 – 9.8.21, there
           is a net increase in emissions initially and it will be approximately 30-40 years before the net benefit
           of this improvement is seen. As such, this will also put pressure on short-term NDC targets;
           however, over the long-term, it is well positioned to contribute positively to the transition toward a
           net-zero future, resulting in a classification of Minor Adverse impact.
 9.11.8.   Whilst the summary above is based on the construction impact, and changes to biogenic emissions,
           it is widely recognized that hydropower dams, over their entire project lifecycle, emit significantly
           less carbon compared to other electricity sources like coal and gas. Although hydropower projects
           can have initial emissions associated with construction and reservoir creation, their operational
           phase typically has minimal emissions due to the renewable nature of water flow as an energy
           source.
 9.11.9.   To demonstrate this, the assessment has shown how the proposed scheme compares against the
           current grid average emissions factor for Tajikistan. With an intensity of approximately 4.5
           gCO2e/kWh, the scheme is substantially better than the current grid average of 106 gCO2e/kWh.


           Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Vol.1            PUBLIC
                                                                                                     September 2025
           Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page 9-25
           This equates to an annual difference – based on the projected generation of 17,000 GWh per year –
           of 1.76 MtCO2e per year.
9.11.10.   Table 9-15 shows how the proposed scheme compares to gas (CCGT) at 348 gCO2e/kWh and Coal
           (IGCC) at 760 gCO2e/kWh (International Finance Corporation, 2008). This also is much lower than
           Tajikistan’s current grid average at 106 gCO2e/kWh (UNFCCC, 2021).

           Table 9-15 - Indicative emissions intensities of various generation sources
            Energy Generated                                    Emissions Intensity (gCO2e/kWh)

            The Proposed Scheme                                 4.5

            Tajikistan Grid Mix (2021)                          106

            Coal (IGCC)                                         760

            Gas (CCGT)                                          348

9.11.11.   However, although this is significantly better than the baseline, it is acknowledged that that this
           scheme is to enhance grid capacity rather than replacing less clean forms of energy generation. As
           such, although the scheme is much better than alternatives, there will still be a net increase in
           emissions over the lifetime of the project. As such, an overall impact of Minor Adverse has been
           given.
9.11.12.   A full summary of the GHG Impact Assessment is provided in Table 9-16.
           RESIDUAL EFFECTS
9.11.13.   The construction phase of hydropower projects will always lead to an increase in emissions. Whilst
           there is a substantial amount of emissions from the construction phase – primarily associated with
           the construction of the embankment – mitigation measures, those both implemented already and
           proposed, will help to reduce this impact over the 10-year construction period.
9.11.14.   Regarding biogenic emissions, as with most hydropower dams, the project is anticipated to
           contain biomass which will be submerged. Although it is shown that there will be a negative impact
           at first, over the 110-year lifespan of the project, there will be a net benefit to biogenic emissions
           over the do-minimum baseline. The project will work to reduce any residual emissions. Where
           practicable, monitoring of methane emissions can be undertaken to understand the long-term effects
           on carbon and carbon equivalent emissions.
9.11.15.   Implementation of these mitigation measures will aid the reduction of emissions. Whilst the impact
           during construction is likely to remain Moderate for that stage of the project, through the remaining
           lifespan of the project, recognizing that the alternative of using coal or gas for electricity generation,
           the overall impact of the project is expected to be Minor Adverse.

9.12      APPENDIX
          G-RES TOOL METHODOLOGY
9.12.1.   The G-res Tool follows methodologies aligned with the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change
          (IPCC) Guidelines for national Greenhouse Gas Inventories (IPCC, 2019). To produce accurate
          estimates, the G-res tool requires comprehensive data inputs, categorized into three main groups:
             Landscape data – provides a baseline of the natural conditions before the area was flooded to
              create the reservoir. The input includes data on prior land use, vegetation types, soil carbon
              content, and the total carbon stored in biomass.
             Reservoir data – encompasses the characteristics of the reservoir post-construction, such as its
              area, depth, water quality, sediment accumulation, and fluctuations in water levels.
             Reservoir Services Data – captures operational aspects, focusing on how the reservoir is used
              and managed for functions like hydropower, irrigation, or flood control.
9.12.2.   The following section outlines the methodology used by the G-Res tool and provides a description of
          the data needed for input within the tool.

          Table 9-17 - Landscape Data Description for G-Res Tool

           Landscape Data                  Description

           Catchment Area (km2)            The catchment area upstream of the reservoir, measured in (km2). This
                                           area analysis is crucial to understand upstream impacts on hydrology,
                                           nutrient flow, and organic matter input, which influence reservoir emissions
                                           and water quality.

           Population in the Catchment     The population within the catchment area upstream of the dam. This
           (persons)                       provides an estimate on the total number of individuals residing in the
                                           catchment area for understanding the potential social impacts and the
                                           demand for resources in the affected communities.

           Land cover of the Catchment     Represents percentage of the land cover within the catchment area

                                           9. Drained Peatland
                                           For each identified category, the percentage of the total catchment area (in
                                           km2) is calculated and entered into the assessment. Additionally, for certain
                                           land cover categories, it is essential to specify the Land Use intensity,
                                           indicating whether the land is ‘unmanaged’ (low intensity, natural land

 Pre-impoundment land cover      Pre-impoundment land cover refers to the land cover characteristics and
 in the reservoir area (% of     types present in an area before the construction of a dam and subsequent
 mineral and organic soils)      flooding that creates a reservoir. This includes all natural and human-

                                 impoundment. For each land cover category, the corresponding percentage
                                 of mineral and organic soils within the impounded area, as well as the
                                 percentage of organic soil that is drained.

                                 should be indicated on a similar scale ranging from ‘unmanaged to heavily
                                 managed’ land.

9.12.4.   The catchment area (km2) represents the total defined as the total area of land that contributes
          surface water flow to the reservoir. At the proposed Rogun dam site the Vakhsh drains a catchment
          area of approximately 30,390 km² and at this location the river flows through a narrow, 400 to 500 m
          deep V-shaped gorge with gradients of the valley sides up to 50° (Pöyry, 2014).
9.12.5.   Population within the Catchment Area - This indicator represents the population residing within
          the catchment area upstream of the dam providing an estimate of the total number of individuals
          living in the area, which is important for understanding potential social impacts as well as the
          demand for resources in affected communities. The estimated population within the catchment area
          is 304,338 persons.
9.12.6.   Land Cover of the Catchment Area - The land cover within the catchment area upstream of the
          reservoir was assessed to provide an overview of habitat types, land use intensity, and their
          relevance to greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. Understanding this distribution is critical for
          evaluating potential carbon fluxes under the G-Res Tool framework, as different habitat types have
          varying emission or sequestration potentials.
          The analysis indicates the following land cover distribution:
           Permanent Snow/Ice (32.2%) – These areas are largely inert with respect to GHG fluxes,
            contributing minimally to biogenic emissions. However, glacier retreat could expose new surfaces
            that may gradually increase organic matter inputs downstream.
           Grassland/Shrubland (29.9%) – These ecosystems can serve as moderate carbon sinks but
            may release emissions when inundated, particularly from soil organic matter decomposition.



          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I              PUBLIC
          Project No.: 70097413 | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                               September 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                     Page | 9-34
            Croplands (18.6%) – Agricultural land use is associated with relatively higher GHG emissions,
             especially due to organic matter inputs, soil disturbance, and potential nutrient runoff that can
             drive methane and nitrous oxide production in the reservoir.
            Bare Areas (15.1%) – With limited vegetation or soil organic matter, these contribute minimally to
             direct GHG emissions, although erosion from these areas may supply particulate matter to the
             reservoir.
            Forest (3.8%) – While forests are generally strong carbon sinks, inundation of forested areas
             during reservoir filling can lead to significant decomposition of biomass and elevated methane
             emissions in the short to medium term.
            Settlements (0.2%) – Although limited in area, settlements may contribute local sources of
             organic waste and nutrients, indirectly affecting reservoir emissions.
            Water Bodies and Drained Peatlands (0.1%) – Peatlands, even in small areas, can be
             important hotspots for methane and carbon dioxide emissions when disturbed or inundated.
9.12.7.    Soil Classification within the Catchment - For the purposes of this assessment, and in line with
           the G-Res Tool methodology, soils are categorized as either mineral soils or organic soils. While all
           soils contain some level of organic content as noted in Table 9-5, organic soils are specifically
           defined as those with a high carbon content. In the case of the Rogun reservoir catchment and
           analysis using Google Earth Engine confirms that the buffer area around the reservoir shows
           relatively low organic soil land cover present. Accordingly, for the G-Res Tool assessment, %
           organic soils in the different land cover types have been assumed to be zero for the purposes of this
           assessment.
9.12.8.    A remote sensing exercise was undertaken using readily available mapping data, to accurately
           map/classify habitat polygons across the Project and for a buffer distance of 2km using the industry
           leading ESRI GIS software. This process underwent ongoing iteration following site visits and review
           of satellite imagery to assist with defining the boundaries of the various habitat types; in particular
           those of greatest sensitivity.
9.12.9.    As mentioned earlier in the do something scenario (Section 9.4) and as visible upon site surveys,
           the site seems to be heavily modified for agriculture, grazing and other purposes. To reflect this, the
           land use intensity % across Cropland, Forest, and Settlement has been taken as 75%.
9.12.10.   River length before impoundment (m) - This value represents the length of the river channel
           between the upstream river inlet and the dam site before the reservoir was impounded (i.e., before
           the dam was built and the area was flooded). This has been assumed as 70,000 m.

9.12.11.   The location and region-specific characteristics of the project such as latitude and longitude have
           been obtained from a review of the Google Earth engine to accurately reflect the properties.
9.12.12.   Tajikistan, including the Rogun area, generally falls within a continental climatic zone. The climate is
           characterized by significant variations in temperature across seasons, with cold winters and hot, dry
           summers. Precipitation levels vary across different regions but are generally low, with most areas
           experiencing limited rainfall, mainly during the winter and early spring months. As per the Koppen-



           Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I                     PUBLIC
           Project No.: 70097413 | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                                      September 2025
           Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                            Page | 9-36
           Gieger Climate Classification referenced in the G-res tool, the climatic zone in Tajikistan has been
           assumed to be temperate with cold summers and dry winters.
9.12.13.   The annual discharge from main intake value represents the total volume of water withdrawn from
           the reservoir through its main intake. This value expressed in (m3 /sec) reflecting the typical flow
           released through the intake. For G-res tool input, we have assumed a long-term average discharge
           rate through the intake/outlet accounting for season variations and operational constraints and
           typically required by the tool for estimating methane degassing calculations. The annual discharge
           values have been referenced from the doc. Ref. No. 41106439-REP-00001 Rogun Hydropower
           Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Vol. 2, Annex 13-3 Water
           Resource Modelling.

           Figure 9-5 - Maximum flow through turbines as Rogun reservoir is constructed and number
           of turbines increase




9.12.14.   Figure 9-5 represents the maximum flow through the turbine for the 100-year operational lifecycle of
           the reservoir. To help calculate the long-term average, we have used weighted average method
           from the start of the first turbine. The below periods and durations have been calculated based on
           the graph provided above.




           Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I          PUBLIC
           Project No.: 70097413 | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                           September 2025

            Time Period            Duration (in years)               Average discharge over the time period (m3/s)

            2025-2028              4                                 340

            2029-2032              4                                 850

            2032-2124              92                                1475

9.12.15.   To estimate the long-term average annual discharge from the main intake over the reservoir's 100-
           year operational life, a weighted average approach was applied. This method accounts for changes
           in flow capacity over time as additional turbines become operational. Based on defined operational
           periods and corresponding discharge rates, the calculation ensures a representative average value
           that reflects both early-stage and full-capacity operations.
           Weighted average formula
           Weighted average = Summation of (annual discharge * no. of years) / Total no. of years

           Weighted average = (340 * 4) + (850*4) + (1475 * 92) / 100

           Weighted average = (1360 + 3400 + 135700) / 100 = 1405 m3/s (approx.)

           As per calculations above, we have assumed the annual discharge from main intake as 1405
           m3/sec.
9.12.16.   The "impoundment year" refers to the specific year when the flooding of land, creating the reservoir,
           was completed. It is assumed that the reservoir will be fully completed at its operating level by 2038.
9.12.17.   The Project has been in construction for many years, most recently since 2018 with work currently
           ongoing. The current status of the Project (as of September 2024) includes: A reservoir at elevation
           1,065 m asl with a surface area of 12 square kilometers (km2). The reservoir will be raised to 1,110
           m asl by 2027 and progressively raised to 1,290 by 2039.
9.12.18.   A reservoir with a full supply level (FSL) at 1,290 m above sea level (asl), a total surface area of 170
           km², an overall length of approximately 70 km and capacity of approximately 13.3 cubic kilometers
           (km3).
9.12.19.   Water Intake Elevation (m a.s.l.) – This input refers to the elevation above sea level (in meters) at
           which water is withdrawn from the reservoir through the main intake structure. This is calculated by
           the difference between the maximum water level and the water intake depth. The maximum water
           level is 1,290 m and the water intake depth of 110 m.
           Water Intake Elevation = Water Level – Water Intake Depth
           Water Intake Elevation = 1290 – 110 = 1180 m a.s.l.
9.12.20.   Water Intake Depth (m) - This input refers to the vertical distance between the reservoir surface (at
           the chosen operating level—either full supply or mean level) and the intake point where water is
           withdrawn. This has been taken as 110 m.




           Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I           PUBLIC
           Project No.: 70097413 | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                            September 2025
           Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page | 9-38
9.12.21.   Wind speed data is not available from the World Bank Climate Change Knowledge Portal. However,
           Meteoblue presents observed climate data for the DRS (Meteoblue, 2022) with data obtained from a
           weather station approximately 72km away (at 1251 m asl) from Project site shown in Figure 9-6.
           This data is provided as an indication of the wind speeds at the Project site. Wind speeds are
           typically lowest in July and August, and highest in March.

           Figure 9-6 - Wind speed data for Dehauz, 72km far from the Rogun Project site




9.12.22.   At the time of writing this report no direct flow measurement downstream of Rogun is available to
           WSP. However, the data used in Coyne et Bellier et al. (2014) and the World Bank (2023) can be
           considered a good approximation to the flow conditions downstream of Rogun before the
           construction of the dam. The data sources available are:
              Rogun dam inflows for the period from 1932 to 2008 (Coyne et Bellier et al., 2014); and
              Nurek HPP inflows for the period from 2009 to 2019 (World Bank, 2023).
9.12.23.   Mean Global Horizontal Radiance (GHR) refers to the average amount of total solar radiation
           received per unit area by a horizontal surface at the Earth's surface, typically measured in kilowatt-
           hours per square meter per day (kWh/m²/day). It represents the total solar energy that strikes a
           horizontal plane and accounts for direct sunlight as well as diffused radiation scattered through the
           atmosphere. The Mean Global Horizontal Radiance (kWh/m2/d) though not available on the Global
           Solar Atlas website, has information on Global Horizontal Irradiance (GHI) used similarly can be
           used for reservoir modelling and greenhouse gas emissions (Solargis, 2023).
9.12.24.   The temperatures experienced across Tajikistan are moderated by the mountainous terrain.
           Average monthly temperatures are slightly warmer in comparison to the rest of Tajikistan as
           presented in




           Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I          PUBLIC
           Project No.: 70097413 | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                           September 2025
           Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 9-39

9.12.26.   Seasonal temperatures range from an average of -6.9°C in winter (December, January, February)
           and 15.9°C in the summer (June, July, August). The average minimum temperature in winter is -
           11.0°C and average maximum summer temperature is 23.1°C.
           Reservoir Services data

           Table 9-23 - Reservoir Services data for G-Res Tool assessment

            Allocation of Reservoir Services Data      Value

             Hydroelectricity                          Primary – 80% allocation Installed Capacity – 3,780 MW
                                                       Total Annual Generation – 17,000 GWh

            Environmental Flow                         Secondary Allocation – 15%

            Flood Control                              Tertiary Allocation – 5%

9.12.27.   To assess the distribution of services provided by the reservoir, the G-Res Tool methodology
           applies proportional allocation based on the primary intended use and associated benefits. For
           Rogun HPP, three main services were identified: hydroelectricity generation, environmental flow
           provision, and flood control.
            Hydroelectricity was determined to be the primary service, representing 80% of the total
             allocation. This is supported by the reservoir’s substantial installed capacity of 3,780 MW and an
             average annual generation of 17,000 GWh. The dominance of hydroelectricity reflects the
             project’s design objective and the significant role it plays in meeting regional energy demands.




           Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I            PUBLIC
           Project No.: 70097413 | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                             September 2025
           Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                   Page | 9-41
            Environmental flows were categorized as a secondary service, with an allocation of 15%. This
             recognizes the operational measures in place to maintain downstream ecological integrity,
             including sustaining aquatic habitats and ensuring minimum flow requirements.
            Flood control was identified as a tertiary service, receiving 5% allocation. While not the primary
             driver of reservoir construction, flood attenuation remains an important co-benefit, providing
             protection to downstream communities and infrastructure.
9.12.28.   Currently, there is an underground powerhouse on the site with two operating turbines (Units 5 and
           6) with a combined generation capacity of 400 MW. These will be upgraded in 2024 / early 2025,
           bringing their capacity to 630MW each. Turbine Units 1 to 4 will be progressively installed from 2025
           to 2029, at which point the full generation capacity will reach 3,780 MW.
