10       CLIMATE CHANGE
10.1.1. This chapter assesses climate change from a dual perspective, considering the vulnerability of the
        Project to current and future climate risks; and the potential for transition risks to affect the Project,
        specifically those of policy and regulatory changes. The climate risk and vulnerability assessment is
        presented from Section 2 to Section 10.9 and considers how future climate change may impact the
        physical assets and operations of the Project. Unlike other ESIA topic assessments, it does not
        consider the impact of the Project on the environment. The assessment of transition risks is
        presented from Section 10.10 and considers risks or opportunities related to a transition to a low-
        carbon economy.
10.1.2. A description of baseline climate change conditions is presented in Volume 2, Annex 7.1: Baseline
        Conditions Report and a summary is provided in Volume 1, Chapter 7: Baseline of this ESIA.
        The assessment is in line with the requirements of WB ESS3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution
        Prevention and Management.

10.2     CLIMATE RISK AND VULNERABILITY ASSESSMENT
10.2.1. The Climate Risk and Vulnerability Assessment (CRVA) is an assessment of the potential impacts
        which may occur to the Project due to climate change variables. This considers how future climate
        trends may adversely impact the construction and operation of the Project components (the
        ‘receptors’) which are yet to be constructed. The scope of the CRVA does not extend to the
        consideration of impacts outside of the immediate HPP infrastructure. Any impacts such as those to
        downstream riparian countries will be considered within Volume 1, Chapter 13: Water and / or
        Volume 1, Chapter 24: Summary of Transboundary Cumulative Impact Assessment. The
        CRVA is presented in Section 10.7 and utilizes future climate trend analysis (Section 10.6) as well
        as project specific risk mitigation measures to determine the risk rating. Where necessary, additional
        mitigation measures for the avoidance of impact, impact minimization and long-term management
        are included in Section 10.8.
10.2.2. The approach to the CRVA is to consider climate change risks through an assessment of the climate
        hazards and provides a high-level overview as to how the Project may be at risk from future climate
        change; and through an assessment of the risks to the Project due to the transition to a lower-
        carbon economy.
10.2.3. The assessment of the climate-related risks which may impact the Project is based on forecast
        climate data for relevant climate variables, and the exposure and vulnerability of the Project to these
        climate variables. The assessment considers the Project specific design and mitigation measures
        that improve the Project resilience.
10.2.4. The hydrology and climate change study is underway. A hydrological geodatabase has been
        developed and thematic maps were produced. The flood hydrographs, previously evaluated as part
        of the TEAS studies, have been reevaluated for return periods up to 10,000 years, revealing slightly
        lower results. The risks of glacial lake (GLOF) and landslide dam outburst floods (LDOF) have been
        assessed, showing that GLOF events are not considered critical for Rogun dam and flood safety
        and that there is a potential for large LDOF events in the Vakhsh catchment, but not significant
        enough to be the critical flood type. A catchment model is being developed, which will be used to
        evaluate the impact of climate change on the key hydrologic process drivers for mid- and end-of-


         ROGUN HYDROPOWER PROJECT – UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT                     PUBLIC |
         WSP
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                     May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                   Page 10-1
        century horizons. The sediment yield under current conditions and climate change scenarios is still
        under evaluation, and will be an input for a comprehensive sedimentation study.

10.3    CRVA LEGISLATIVE FRAMEWORK, POLICY AND GUIDANCE
        NATIONAL STRATEGY
10.3.1. The National Climate Change Adaptation Strategy of the Republic of Tajikistan for the period up to
        2030 outlines the country’s commitment to the Paris Agreement (Government of Tajikistan, 2019).
        The Strategy notes that Tajikistan is one of the most vulnerable countries to the impacts of climate
        change due to its geographical location, diversity of climatic conditions, economic structure and
        demographic interests. Priority sectors for development in Tajikistan include energy and the Strategy
        notes that the country has a strong dependence on hydroelectric power.
10.3.2. Climate change risks may impact hydropower performance though changes in water availability
        affecting hydropower production.
10.3.3. Prioritized adaptation options that are relevant to hydropower in the Strategy include:
         Investing in climate-resilient infrastructure to enhance hydropower resilience and productivity; and
         Strengthening adaptation and planning tools for long-term hydropower generation, addressing
          changes in water and energy availability.
        INTERNATIONAL STANDARDS
10.3.4. The CRVA aligns with the World Bank Environmental and Social Standards (ESS) including:
         ESS1, paragraph 35 which requires an ESIA to consider potentially significant project related
          risks associate with climate change adaptation and resilience issues; (World Bank, 2018)
         ESS3, paragraph 13 where impacts of climate change should be assessed; (World Bank, 2018)
          and
         ESS4, paragraph 2 where the considerations relating to climate change should be considered.
          (World Bank, 2018)
10.3.5. The CRVA aligns with European Investment Bank (EIB) Environmental and Social Standard 5:
        Climate Change (EIB, 2022). The objective of Standard 5 is to promote “the alignment of projects
        supported by the EIB with the goals and principles of: (i) the Paris Agreement and (ii) the
        Sustainable Finance Action Plan”. Standard 5 notes that climate change mitigation and adaptation
        must be considered and addressed in the decision-making process of the Project. In relation to
        physical climate risk, a Project should be assessed to consider its “alignment with climate-resilient
        development pathways,1 and identify options to reduce physical climate risks to the project, its
        natural environment and the people that may be affected by it” and identify “options to reduce
        transition risks”.
10.3.6. Standard 5 outlines the approach for CRVA to align with the EIB Group Climate Bank Roadmap
        (EIB Group, 2020) and have consistency with the EU Taxonomy ‘Do No Significant Harm’ (DNSH)




        1
         Climate-resilience development pathways are defined as: “Trajectories that strengthen sustainable development and efforts to eradicate
        poverty and reduce inequalities while promoting fair and cross-scalar adaptation to and resilience in a changing climate” (IPCC, 2018)


        ROGUN HYDROPOWER PROJECT – UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT                                                PUBLIC |
        WSP
        Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                                                May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                                              Page 10-2
        principle (Official Journal of the European Union, 2023). The EIB Group Climate Bank Roadmap is
        based on:
         Promoting green finance through investing in projects which accelerate low carbon transition;
         Investing in projects that simultaneously support the transition and improve socio-economic
          development;
         Supporting Paris-aligned projections, considering both low carbon and climate resilience; and
         Ensuring projects align with finance and socially sustainable policies, and that their success can
          be monitored and reported for accountability.
10.3.7. The criteria for EU Taxonomy DNSH to climate change adaptation include:
         Screening of the activity to identify the physical climate risks that may affect the performance of
          the economic activity during its expected lifetime;
         A CRVA to assess the materiality of the physical climate risks on the economic activity; and
         An assessment of adaptation solutions that can reduce the identified physical climate risk.
10.3.8. The International Hydropower Association (IHA) has produced a Climate Resilience Guide for the
        Hydropower Sector (IHA, 2019) which has informed the identification and assessment of climate
        change risks and, where required, suggest additional mitigation measures for the Project.

10.4    CRVA ASSESSMENT METHODOLOGY
10.4.1. The CRVA is aligned with EU Taxonomy ‘DNSH’ criteria (Official Journal of the European Union,
        2023).
10.4.2. An initial screening of climate hazards (such as changing temperature, heavy precipitation, glacial
        lake outburst) was undertaken to identify the climate-related hazards which are relevant to the
        Project and its geographical location. The chronic (long-term) and acute (event-driven) hazards
        identified in the DNSH criteria have been used to inform the screening process.
10.4.3. The climate projection data contained in this Chapter has been obtained from the World Bank
        Climate Change Knowledge Portal (World Bank Group, 2021) to inform the CRVA. Climate
        projections have been presented for the Districts of Republican Subordination (DRS) in Tajikistan.
10.4.4. Projections are provided for Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSP) 2-4.5, as the middle-of-the-
        road scenario, equivalent to 2-degree warming; and SSP5-8.5, as the fossil fuel development
        scenario, equivalent to 4-degree warming. The use of two climate projection scenarios provides
        context for the uncertainties inherent in climate science and projection modelling. SSP5-8.5 has
        been used to assess the risk to the Project as a conservative, precautionary approach. A
        precautionary approach is used in CRVAs to identify potential risks where there is scientific
        uncertainty. Climate projections have been provided for 20-year incremental periods, including the
        2030s, 2050s and 2090s, using global climate data. The current global climate model is known as
        Coupled Model Intercomparison Project (CMIP6).
10.4.5. The CRVA considers hazard, exposure and vulnerability to determine the overall risk rating. These
        have five levels, namely very low, low, moderate, high and very high which correspond to the
        terminology detailed in Table 10-1.

10.4.6. Climate-related hazards which are screened out will not be assessed further. Those that are
        screened in will be considered in the risk assessment.
10.4.7. Firstly, the risk rating is obtained by assessing the ‘magnitude of impact’ which is determined by
        assessing the hazard likelihood and the exposure, as presented in Table 10-2. The rating for the
        hazard likelihood is determined using future climate projection data and professional judgement to
        assign a rating to the receptor (the Project component at risk). The exposure rating is determined
        through literature review and professional judgement.

        Table 10-2 - Matrix for Impact Magnitude

          Magnitude of impact                                          Exposure

                                           Very Low        Low         Moderate         High       Very High
                                              (1)           (2)          (3)             (4)          (5)

          Hazard           Very High         Low         Moderate         High       Very High     Very High
          (Likelihood         (5)             (5)          (10)           (15)         (20)          (25)
          of event)
                              High           Low         Moderate         High          High       Very High
                               (4)            (4)          (8)            (12)          (16)         (20)

                           Moderate          Low            Low        Moderate         High          High
                             (3)              (3)            (6)         (9)            (12)          (15)

                              Low          Very Low         Low           Low        Moderate       Moderate
                               (2)            (2)            (4)           (6)         (8)            (10)

                           Very Low        Very Low      Very Low         Low           Low           Low
                              (1)             (1)           (2)            (3)           (4)           (5)

10.4.8. Secondly, the overall risk rating is determined, where the magnitude of impact (described above)
        and the vulnerability is assessed. The risk rating matrix is presented in Table 10-3. The vulnerability
        rating considers the probability of the receptor being impacted by the climate hazard and any known
        embedded or committed risk mitigation measures that have been incorporated into the design to
        incorporate resilience to climate change.
10.4.9. Mitigation measures may include technological and engineering solutions (termed ‘grey’ measures),
        nature-based solutions (‘green’ measures) or soft measures which include policy, legal, social,
        management and financial measures.

                                                (1)             (2)          (3)             (4)           (5)

          of impact             (5)              (5)          (10)           (15)           (20)           (25)

                               High             Low         Moderate         High           High         Very High
                                (4)              (4)          (8)            (12)           (16)           (20)

                             Moderate           Low            Low         Moderate         High           High
                               (3)               (3)            (6)          (9)            (12)           (15)

                                Low          Very Low          Low            Low         Moderate       Moderate
                                 (2)            (2)             (4)            (6)          (8)            (10)

                             Very Low        Very Low       Very Low          Low           Low            Low
                                (1)             (1)            (2)             (3)           (4)            (5)

10.4.10. Where the overall risk rating is categorized as very low or low, it is considered that the receptor is
         not materially affected by the climate hazard and no additional mitigation measures are required.
10.4.11. Where the overall risk rating is categorized as moderate, high or very high, it is considered that there
         is a potential for a material effect to the Project and additional mitigation measures may be required.
         Additional mitigation measures are detailed in Section 10.8.
10.4.12. Supporting information used to undertake the CRVA is provided in Volume 2, Annex 10-1: Climate
         Change of this ESIA This includes a summary of previous reports made available to inform the
         CRVA and a hydropower literature review to inform the sensitivity of Project receptors.
         ASSUMPTIONS AND LIMITATIONS
10.4.13. The World Bank Knowledge Portal climate projections have been used to infer future changes in a
         range of climate variables that may pose a risk to the Project from climate change. At the time of
         writing, these represent the most up-to-date representation of future climate.
10.4.14. There are inherent uncertainties associated with climate projections and they are not predictions of
         the future. It is possible that future climate will differ from the future climate against which the Project
         has been assessed, depending on global emissions over the next century. The use of two climate
         projection scenarios (SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5) across short-, medium- and long-term time frames
         have been used to develop a baseline against which climate change risk has been assessed.
10.4.15. Any further research, analysis or decision-making should take account of the uncertainties
         associated with climate projections. It is also important to note that the analysis is based on selected
         observational data, the results of climate model ensembles and a selected range of existing climate
         change research and literature available at the time of assessment. Any future decision-making
         based on this analysis should consider the range of literature, evidence and research available at
         that time and any changes to this.
10.4.16. The CRVA considers the risk posed to the Project receptors from projected changes in climate. The
         scope of the CRVA does not extend to modelling or calculations to inform design specifications or
         inputs.
10.4.17. Identification of the embedded and committed mitigation measures for the Project is informed by
         review of the design, previous studies, committed future studies and available management plans. It
         is assumed that the recommendations of previous studies and management plans will be
         implemented and updated during the construction and operation of the Project. Whilst the outcomes
         of future studies are unknown, the assumption has been made that the studies will be undertaken by
         competent experts and the recommended design specifications and construction and operational
         processes will be incorporated into the Project. The CRVA should be reviewed and updated as the
         outcomes of future studies are embedded into the Project.
10.4.18. Provision of detailed design measures and the implementation of measures is outside of the scope
         of the CRVA.
10.4.19. The CVRA is a qualitative assessment, with the risk grading based on analysis of the climate
         hazards (climate projection data), understanding of the Project and literature review. Professional
         judgement is used to apply risk ratings to the assessment categories.
10.4.20. The CRVA should be considered an iterative tool which will require review and update as the Project
         design develops, as further studies are completed and as additional mitigation measures are
         identified and implemented.

10.6    CRVA CLIMATE PROJECTIONS
10.6.1. Climate projections have been derived from the World Bank Climate Change Knowledge Portal for
        the Project area (World Bank, 2020). Projections have been presented for the DRS, using CMIP6
        data for SSP2-4.5 (representing a middle-of-the-road scenario, equivalent to 2-degree warming) and
        SSP5-8.5 (representing a fossil fuel development scenario, equivalent to a 4-degree warming) over
        the 20-year intervals covering the 2030s (2020 to 2039), 2050s (2040 to 2059) and 2090s (2080 to
        2099).
10.6.2. The climate projection data shows the 50th percentile, with the 10th and 90th percentiles in brackets
        to demonstrate the range in the modeled data. Seasonal data has been used for each of the time
        periods, with the summer season covering the months of June, July and August; and the winter
        season covering December, January and February.
        Temperature
10.6.3. Compared to the baseline of 1995-2014, the projected change in seasonal temperature for the DRS
        for the 2030s, 2050s and 2090s time periods under SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5 scenarios shows an
        increase in temperature. Under the SSP2-4.5 scenario the temperature increase ranges from 1.09°C
        (2030s winter) to 3.21°C (2090s summer) for the 50% percentile ‘central estimate’ (taken as the
        median value of predicted change). Under the SSP5-8.5 scenario there is an increase in
        temperature in the range of 1.15°C (2030s winter) to 6.27°C (2090s summer).
10.6.4. For both scenarios, the projected air temperature increases are greatest for the summer months.
        During the winter months, air temperatures are also expected to increase, albeit to a lesser extent.
        Data are presented in Table 10-5.

        Table 10-5 - DRS Projected Change in Average Seasonal Temperature (°C) under SSP2 and
        SSP5 for the 2030s, 2050s and 2090s (baseline period 1995-2014)

          Year                                       SSP2-4.5                    SSP5-8.5

          2030s           Summer                     +1.21                       +1.51
                                                     (+0.58 to +2.08)            (+0.51 to +2.39)

                          Winter                     +1.09                       +1.15
                                                     (+0.11 to +1.95)            (+0.36 to +2.01)

          2050s           Summer                     +2.01                       +2.65
                                                     (+1.09 to +3.25)            (+1.74 to +4.02)

                          Winter                     +1.87                       +2.30
                                                     (+0.72 to +2.89)            (+1.19 to +3.62)

          2090s           Summer                     +3.21                       +6.27
                                                     (+1.93 to +4.63)            (+4.49 to +8.64)

                          Winter                     +2.97                       +5.49
                                                     (+1.57 to +4.40)            (+3.69 to +8.41)
10.6.5. As well as an increase in average temperatures, projections indicate an increase in extreme
        temperatures (i.e. the temperature experienced during heatwaves). Table 10-6 shows the projected
        change in maximum daily maximum temperature for summer in each of future time slices relative to
        the reference period (1995-2014) for the DRS. Table 10-6 shows that the by the 2090s daily
        maximum temperature in summer may be 3.03°C hotter under SSP2-4.2, and potentially 6.38°C
        hotter under SSP5-8.5.

         Table 10-6 - DRS Projected Change in Maximum of Daily Max-Temperature (°C) during
         Summer under SSP2 and SSP5 for the 2030s, 2050s and 2090s (baseline period 1995-2014)

          Year                                       SSP2-4.5                     SSP5-8.5

          2030s            Summer                    +1.10°C                      +1.34
                                                     (-0.68 to +2.66)             (-0.10 to +3.05)

          2050s            Summer                    +2.12                        +2.59
                                                     (+0.33 to +3.66)             (+1.17 to +4.38)

          2090s            Summer                    +3.03                        +6.38
                                                     (+1.49 to +4.40)             (+4.06 to +8.55)



10.6.6. Periods of extreme temperature can increase the risk of fire outbreak, with high temperatures, low
        humidity and dry conditions conducive to fire weather (Jones et al., 2020). Projection data for
        Tajikistan suggest that by the 2030s, there will be minimal increase in the land fraction exposed to
        wildfire (0.1%). By the 2050s and 2075 (the furthest extent of the projection data) this will increase
        slightly to 0.2% (Climate Analytics , 2023).
         Precipitation
10.6.7. Projection data shows that summer precipitation rates will decrease (under the 50th percentile in
        both SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5), but winter precipitation rates will increase. Under the SSP2-4.5
        scenario precipitation decreases from -2.38mm (2030s summer) to -17.5mm (2090s summer) for the
        50% percentile. Under the SSP5-8.5 scenario the decrease in precipitation is projected to be from -
        11.5mm (2030s summer) to -34.5mm (2090s summer).
10.6.8. Winter increases in precipitation under SSP2-4.5 range from 13.6mm in the 2030s to 30.9mm in the
        2090s (50th percentile); and from 8.5mm to 72.7mm by the 2090s.
10.6.9. Annually, there is an increase in the DRS due to winter precipitation increases far outweighing
        summer precipitation decreases.

         Table 10-7 - DRS Projected Change in Summer, Winter and Annual Precipitation (mm) under
         SSP2 and SSP5 for the 2030s, 2050s and 2090s (baseline period 1995-2014)

          Year                                    SSP2-4.5                         SSP5-8.5

          2030s          Summer                   -2.38                            -11.51
                                                  (-43.70 to +27.98)               (-45.55 to +31.07)
          Year                                    SSP2-4.5                         SSP5-8.5

                       Winter                     +13.66                           +8.58
                                                  (-35.54 to +54.02)               (-52.95 to +65.12)

                       Annual                     +17.11                           -6.43
                                                  (-188.01 to +186.26)             (-203.62 to +199.77)

          2050s        Summer                     -10.20                           -12.30
                                                  (-49.97 to +34.70)               (-51.52 to +29.97)

                       Winter                     +24.33                           +30.92
                                                  (-31.66 to +81.37)               (-28.65 to +97.13)

                       Annual                     +26.85                           +37.18
                                                  (-188.95 to +238.29)             (-181.11 to +252.99)

          2090s        Summer                     -17.50                           -34.52
                                                  (-65.73 to +32.61)               (-90.65 to +23.22)

                       Winter                     +30.94                           +72.75
                                                  (-46.09 to +91.01)               (-26.62 to +153.8)

                       Annual                     +32.34                           +42.70
                                                  (-224.9 to +271.52)              (-277.45 to +342.30)

10.6.10. Water stress in the Project area is projected to remain low in the future (data available for the 2080s
         under a the SSP5-8.5 scenario). Projected drought data is not available, however, the Project is in
         an area of low water depletion with low to medium variability of water supply (World Resources
         Institute, 2024).
10.6.11. Given the projection data showing increasing temperatures, it is anticipated that there will be
         seasonal changes as more precipitation will fall as rain and less as snow. This has the potential to
         increase annual and inter-annual variability, leading to greater average flow volumes and greater
         variability.
10.6.12. As the country’s mountain glaciers melt at increasing rates due to higher temperatures, in the short
         to medium term this could lead to excess waterflows and cause severe flooding and associated
         hazards such as landslides and mudslides. This will occur for several decades whilst the glaciers
         deplete. In the longer term, reduced glaciers are likely to reduce the regularity of waterflows, where
         annual flow volumes will decrease due to extensive glacier recession and may result in the drying of
         some watersheds.
         Wind
10.6.13. Due to the complexities of modelling global changes in wind, there is low confidence in the accuracy
         of the results from existing models of future wind conditions under climate change. The site of the
         Project is identified as having a ‘very low’ storm hazard risk (Swiss Re, 2020). Changes in summer
        and winter wind speeds in the vicinity of the Project are projected to increase by two to four meters
        per second, however the granularity of the data is low (Copernicus , 2024).
10.6.14. The retreat of glaciers can increase the incidence of dust storms however these are concentrated
         within the lowland areas.
10.6.15. Wind can generate wave action on reservoirs which increase with wind strength.
        Solid Mass
10.6.16. Climate projection data is not available for solid mass variables, although change in precipitation
         and temperature have a direct relationship to avalanche, mudflows and landslides. Projected
         increases in extreme precipitation events combined with warm spells could increase avalanching
         and landslides with climate change.
        FUTURE CLIMATE TRENDS

         described in Table 10-8. The increasing or decreasing direction of the climate trend has been
         derived from the climate projection data.

10.7    RISK ASSESSMENT
10.7.1. The CRVA utilizes the climate projection data to assess the change in climate trends across the time
        periods relevant to the Project.
10.7.2. An assessment of risk, considering the climate hazard, exposure of the Project and vulnerability of
        the Project, is undertaken using the methodology described in Section 10.4, and terminology
        detailed in Table 10-1. The components of the Project (receptors) are assessed against each of the
        screened-in climate hazards. The Project receptors align with the descriptions provided in Volume
        1, Chapter 3: Project Description of this ESIA, and comprise:
           Construction Assets and Temporary Works,
           Energy Generation,
           Dam (Stage 2) and Reservoir,
           Powerhouse, transformers, turbines,
           Spillway (High level outlets and Remote tunnel) and Headrace Tunnel,
           Access roads and replacement bridges,
           Permanent offices and maintenance facilities,
           Transmission lines, and
           Human health.
10.7.3. The CRVA describes potential impacts associated with the climate hazards and Project receptors
        (‘Potential Impact(s)’ column). Existing, or committed mitigation measures are known through
        documentation review and engagement with DSPOE and are listed in the ‘Embedded and
        Committed Mitigation Measures’ column.
10.7.4. Where the overall risk rating is very low or low, it is considered that no additional mitigation
        measures are required beyond those stated in the ‘Embedded and Committed Mitigation Measures’
        column of the CRVA. Where moderate-, high- or very high-risk ratings are identified, then additional
        mitigation recommendations are provided in Section 10.8.
10.7.5. The CRVA presented in Table 10-9 is for the construction phase. This considers the climate
        projections for the 2030s in line with the anticipated completion of the construction phase by 2032.
10.7.6. Table 10-10 presents the CRVA for the operation phase. This assessment is based on the 2090s
        climate projections in line with a precautionary approach.

10.7.7. During the construction phase, the risks to the Project from climate change are generally considered to be very low or low based on the change in climate hazards in the short-term (2030s) and the embedded and committed
        mitigation measures currently in place. The exception to this relates to solid mass climate related variables. Recommendations for additional risk mitigation measures are detailed in Section 10.8.
10.7.8. The CRVA presented in Table 10-10 is for the Operation phase.

10.7.9. During operation phase, the majority of risks posed to the Project by climate change are considered to be very low or low. However high risks have been identified in relation to water and solid mass climate-related variables,
        and moderate risks in relation to solid mass, temperature, water and wind climate-related variables. It is also important to note that climate-related risks on the same receptor as a result or consequence of multiple climate
        hazards may also occur on the Project. For example, increasing sedimentation over time may exacerbate impacts of GLOF events or hydrological variability as the reservoirs capacity changes, impacting energy production.
It is considered that the mitigation measures identified (embedded and committed as well as additional measures) would be sufficient to manage these combined impacts, however when applying and developing additional
measures, the combined nature of climate-related risks should be taken into account. Recommendations for additional risk mitigation measures are detailed in Section 10.8.
10.8    CRVA MITIGATION AND ENHANCEMENT MEASURES
10.8.1. Where the CRVA identified medium or high risks (no very high risks were identified), additional
        mitigation measures (beyond those noted in the ‘Embedded and Committed Mitigation Measures’
        column of the CRVA) have been identified to further reduce the risk to the Project from climate
        hazards.
10.8.2. The additional mitigation measures identified are high level recommendations and it will be the
        responsibility of the design team to develop detailed measures and engineering solutions, as
        necessary, and the DSPOE (or other such party) to develop and implement applicable management
        plans, monitoring and inspection regimes.
10.8.3. A summary of the recommended additional mitigation measures is provided in Table 10-11 with
        further information provided below.

10.8.4. Due to the iterative and developing nature of additional studies and management plans for the
        Project, the CRVA has noted that there is a commitment to undertake more detailed hydrology,
        GLOF, landslide-dam-break, sedimentation and climate change studies which will further inform the
        design, construction and operation of the Project. These studies must be undertaken by competent
        experts, and the findings of these studies must be incorporated into the Project in order to provide
        increased operational safety and provide resilience to the effects of future climate change.
10.8.5. Similarly, the development and implementation of measures outlined in management and monitoring
        plans (including the Landslide Management and Monitoring Plan), emergency procedures and
        plans, and the Occupational Health and Safety Management Plan must all include measures which
        account for the effects of climate change. For some aspects (landslides, rockfalls, sedimentation),
        the impacts of climate change would not change the nature of the situation, but occurrences may
        become more frequent or intense. As such, recommendations to ensure the effective and safe
        operation of the Project will need to be sufficient within the monitoring and management plans.
        Where climate change may result in hazards such as wildfire, heat stress, storms and flooding, the
        management and emergency preparedness plans should include measures to manage the risk to an
        acceptable level.
10.8.6. Developing a regular inspection regime which embeds measures to reduce the impacts of climate
        hazards on access roads and permanent buildings should be implemented. The inspection regime
        may include ensuring drainage infrastructure is not blocked, that buildings are in a good state of
        repair to minimize storm damage and ensuring access roads are accessible. The frequency of such
        inspection and subsequent repair or maintenance works should be considered in line with seasonal
        weather variations, prior to forecast events and after the occurrence of an event.
10.8.7. In addition to the above additional mitigation measures, the following good practice
        recommendations are made to enhance the resilience of the Project.
         The CRVA should be considered as an iterative tool which will require review and update as the
          Project design develops, as further studies are completed, as additional mitigation measures are
          identified and implemented and as climate projection data is updated. Throughout the operational
          lifetime of the Project, the CRVA should also be periodically updated as the impacts of actual
          weather events are realized.
         A list of severe and extreme weather-related incidents should be maintained and the impact to
          the Project recorded against the weather event. This may include windspeeds which resulted in
          damage to buildings or increased wave height; temperature which resulted in degradation of
          deformation of materials, precipitation volumes which result in flooding. By recording such data,
          thresholds can be identified which, when exceeded, require maintenance or emergency
          measures to be initiated.
         Weather forecasts should be routinely checked for extreme events to allow time to react and put
          into place any measure to ensure the Project assets and employees / contractors are not unduly
          disrupted by such weather events.
         Management, monitoring and preparedness plans should be periodically reviewed and updated
          as the impacts form extreme or sever weather events are realized. Where necessary additional
          measures to manage risks posed by a changing climate and extreme weather events should be
          made to the plans.
         Calculations which are relied on for the operation of the Project should be periodically reviewed to
          ensure they are sufficient to accommodate climate change projections.
         When Project components are replaced during the planned replacement program, replacement
          components should be sufficient to accommodate future climate change projections for the
          lifetime of the component.

10.9    RESIDUAL EFFECTS
10.9.1. Once the additional mitigation measures are implemented, the impacts that have a risk rating of
        high and moderate prior to mitigation would be downgraded to low or very low and there would be
        no residual effects.
10.9.2. This should be confirmed through review and update of the CRVA as the Project progresses to
        confirm that the mitigation measures have been carried out through the Project processes, and
        implemented through management plans as recommended.

10.10 TRANSITION RISK - LEGISLATIVE FRAMEWORK, POLICY AND
      GUIDANCE
10.10.1. The approach followed for this assessment is the European Investment Bank (EIB) Standard 5 –
         Climate Change (EIB5) which sets out the requirement for projects to assess, manage and monitor
         project-related transition climate risks. EIB5 aims for the Transition Risk Assessment to build
         resilience and adaptive capacity in response to current and future climate change-induced impacts.
10.10.2. The EIB defines transition risks in line with the Taskforce for Climate-related Financial Disclosure
         Framework (TCFD) (TCFD, 2024). TCFD includes recommendations of the disclosures to be
         considered and the themes of a transition risk assessment, namely; policy, technology and market
         changes used to facilitate the change to a lower carbon economy. Transition risks may pose varying
         levels of financial and reputation risks on the Project.

10.11 TRANSITION RISK – ASSESSMENT METHODOLOGY
10.11.1. The purpose of the transition risk assessment is to assess climate-related risks and opportunities on
         the Project’s existing and proposed assets, arising from the transition to a lower carbon economy,
         under a high and a low emission scenario.
10.11.2. A current scenario is based on the Project construction phase that is ongoing and anticipated for
         completion in 2032 with gradual reservoir filling until full capacity in 2038. The future scenarios
         assess the transition risks against a high and low emission scenario when the Project is in full
         operation in the 2030 decade.
10.11.3. The assessment of transition risks follows the steps set out by the TCFD Framework:
         Screening of the policy, technological and market risks and opportunities relevant to the Project;
         Assessment of relevant scenarios for the Project; 2023 has been assigned for the current
          baseline, and future scenarios include a 2030 high emission, and a 2030s low emission scenario;
          and
         A likelihood and impact rating are assigned to each relevant risk, giving a final risk rating (low /
          medium / high). The likelihood of the risk is based on its occurrence within Tajikistan or the
          renewable energy industry. The impact is based on an assessment of the scale of outcome for
          any given risk. Wider context of transition risk is provided by national and sector commentaries
          available via the IEA and World Bank.
        ASSESSMENT LIMITATIONS
10.11.4. The national policy framework in Tajikistan is, in common with other national Governments, a
         dynamic document, reflecting ongoing international commitments to climate mitigation and
         adaptation targets. The current assessment uses known policy commitments and objectives, as well
         as wider market details available.

10.12 TRANSITION RISK ASSESSMENT
10.12.1. In the context of climate change, transition risks arise due to the necessary changes that are
         required during the shift to a lower carbon economy, including policy and market changes,
         technological advances, reputational risks to higher emitting operations and a lack of resilience to
         climate impacts and future climate projections.
10.12.2. Several transition risks and opportunities relevant to the Rogun HPP have been identified, including
         policy and legal, technology and market risks in line with the EIB (and subsequent TCFD) guidance.
10.12.3. The following subsections detail:
         Risk: in line with EIB guidance;
         Impact: specific impact on the Rogun HPP;
         Context: review of data received on aspects and process of the construction, operation and
          maintenance of the Rogun HPP;
         Current scenario: the risk rating for the 2023 construction period;
         Future scenario: the risk rating for the 2030 operation period; and
         Summary: outlining the requirements for management.
        POLICY AND LEGAL CHANGES
        Risk 1
         Enhanced GHG and climate reporting obligations and monitoring for future reporting obligations.
        Impact on Rogun HPP
         Resources will be needed to record full GHG emissions from construction, operation and
          maintenance, including recording offset emissions of the Rogun HPP; as well as monitoring and
          understanding future reporting obligations.
        Context
10.12.4. Tajikistan is a member of the IEA’s EU4Energy Program, a collaborative program designed to
         support countries in implementing sustainable energy policies and energy sector development. The
         program is made up of five components to assist Eastern and Central Asian countries in reporting
         on energy sources and demand.
10.12.5. Of relevance to the Rogun HPP are Component 1: enhanced energy data management and
         Component 3: generate an improved energy legislative and regulatory framework and implemented
         policy recommendations as well as promote investments in key energy infrastructure strategic
         projects. This suggests that reporting of GHG emissions may become more stringent, whilst also
         being influenced by surrounding countries following commitments to reduce overall national
         emissions.
 10.12.6. The FP040 project by the Green Climate Fund (2023) aims to protect Tajikistan’s hydropower from
          climate risks. There are three elements to the project:
           Adopting best international practices;
           Re-training Tajik hydropower operators; and
           Assessing and managing climate risks.
 10.12.7. The funds show progress for Tajikistan in developing a framework for efficient management of
          hydropower projects.
 10.12.8. It is likely that Tajikistan’s requirement to report at a country level will feed down to a project level,
          and reporting requirements will increase under the low emission scenario. The regulatory landscape
          for climate and carbon in Tajikistan is not considered to be a leader in policy frameworks, therefore
          the risk remains low for the high emission scenario.
          Current Scenario

                                                             2023

                                                      Current Scenario

                       Likelihood                           Impact                              Risk

                          Low                                Low                                Low

          Future Scenario

                                                            2030s

                        Low Emissions Scenario                                High Emissions Scenario

             Likelihood          Impact             Risk            Likelihood        Impact             Risk

                High                Low             Low              Medium             Low              Low

          Summary
 10.12.9. The assessment of enhanced GHG and climate reporting obligations and monitoring for future
          reporting obligations has revealed low risks in both scenarios. Hydropower resources are at the core
          of Tajikistan’s Energy Sector and an integral part of its targets set out in its Nationally Determined
          Contribution (NDC). More stringent future reporting of GHG emissions is more likely to focus on
          fossil-fuel generating assets rather than renewable generation.
10.12.10. Rogun JSC, as operators of Rogun HPP, should maintain monitoring regimes for the reporting of
          GHG emissions ensuring that existing and future reporting requirements can be met.
          Risk 2
           Exposure to litigation and mandates on existing services.
          Impact on Rogun HPP
           Periodic monitoring and strategic horizon scanning will be required to understand the Project’s
            exposure to environmental litigation; carbon reduction requirements, climate resilience, water
             management and land use requirements and changes in social expectations regarding resource
             sharing and conservation.
          Context
10.12.11. The Tajikistan Development Strategy by 2030 explicitly seeks to expand hydropower generation as
          one of the targets to increase overall electricity production. Alongside this generation target is
          regulation in respect of both water resource efficiency and maintenance or enhancement of
          biodiversity. In developing the Rogun HPP these two elements need to be suitably accounted for in
          both construction and operation of the scheme. The financial risk is that more rigorous and precise
          measures will be required following updates to regulations, particularly under a low emission
          scenario, where protection of water resources and biodiversity is likely to be more stringent.
          “The Water Codex of the Republic of Tajikistan contains provisions for particular uses of water for
          which there is a charge and for compensation of damage to water sources (Tajikistan Ministry of
          Justice 2020)”.
          “The Law of the Republic of Tajikistan on Protection and Use of Flora defines principles of protection
          and rational use of flora, determines the legal, economic and social basis in this area and is directed
          on preserving and protecting flora (Tajikistan Ministry of Justice 2018)”.
10.12.12. The Program for Reforming the Water Sector of Tajikistan for 2016-2025 contains adaptation
          measures through the development of a long-term strategy for the use and protection of water
          resources, development of seasonal and annual plans for the distribution and management of water
          resources in river basins, restoration of irrigation infrastructure and improvement of conditions for its
          maintenance and operation, and introduction of new water-saving technologies (Tajikistan Ministry
          of Justice 2015). It notes that hydropower plays an important role in the efficient functioning of all
          sectors of the economy. It also highlights that the use of water in power generation does not impact
          either resource availability, or overall water quality.
10.12.13. Based on the recommendations provided in the Biodiversity assessment (summarized in Section
          8.11 of this report) being implemented, it is understood that all residual impacts on biodiversity will
          be reduced to where the significance of the effect is minor. The exception to this is the alteration of
          the Vakhsh River into a deep lake, where the significance remains as a moderate effect. The
          transition risk is therefore assessed as low under the 2030 low emissions scenario and low under
          the 2030 high emissions scenario.
          Current Scenario

                                                            2023

                                                      Current Scenario

                      Likelihood                           Impact                              Risk

                          Low                                Low                                Low
          Future Scenarios

                                                         2030s

                       Low Emissions Scenario                              High Emissions Scenario

             Likelihood         Impact            Risk           Likelihood        Impact            Risk

              Medium              Low             Low             Medium            Low              Low

          Summary
10.12.14. The assessment of exposure to litigation and mandates on existing services has revealed low risk in
          the low emission scenario and low risk in the high emission scenario. Rogun JSC as operators of
          Rogun HPP will need to monitor changes to regulatory requirements as they are published and
          ensure compliance is maintained.
          Risk 3
           Policy changes associated with write-offs, asset impairment and early retirement of existing fossil
            fuel vehicles and machinery due to policy changes.
          Impact on Rogun HPP
           An increase in the requirement for the replacement of vehicles and machinery with lower carbon
            options.
          Context
10.12.15. The number of vehicles during operation will be small, with access required only for maintenance
          and servicing. The majority of these will be light vehicles with HGVs only required if components
          need replacing. Crane use is likely to be limited to replace turbine units.
10.12.16. Under a low emission scenario there may be a requirement to replace operational vehicles with
          lower carbon alternatives; however, due to the low number involved, the risk remains low in both the
          high and low emission scenarios.
          Current Scenario

                                                          2023

                     Likelihood                          Impact                             Risk

                          Low                             Low                               Low
          Future Scenario

             Likelihood         Impact            Risk            Likelihood       Impact            Risk

               Medium            Low              Low                Low            Low              Low

          Summary
10.12.17. The assessment of policy changes associated with write-offs, asset impairment, and early retirement
          of fossil fueled vehicles and machinery due to policy changes has revealed low risks in both
          scenarios. Rogun JSC as operators of Rogun HPP will be required to monitor policy changes to
          allow any required replacement of assets can be factored into planned replacement regimes.
          TECHNOLOGY CHANGES
          Risk 1
           Technological advances causing existing products to become obsolete and substituted with lower
            emissions options.
          Impact
           Costs of replacing assets, plant and machinery and other equipment.
           Extra resource needed for replacing assets plant and machinery and other equipment.
          Context
10.12.18. There are several energy-dependent assets within the Project, namely; the powerhouse, turbines,
          power supplies, permanent maintenance facilities and site offices. Hydropower is a well-established
          technology and the means of energy generation (conversion of potential energy into electricity) will
          not substantially change in future. Innovation in optimizing components within the powerhouse or
          turbine can be incorporated within planned major maintenance periods (where relevant).
          Construction
10.12.19. It is understood that the contractor team is currently working to replace diesel engines used during
          construction with electrical engines. Five new electrical engines were commissioned for the
          construction of the Rogun HPP in 2021. A high capacity conveyor belt, or ‘Flying Belt System’, has
          also being installed on site to transport construction material around the site, replacing diesel
          vehicles.
          Operation
10.12.20. The entirety of the assets will be powered by the Project; however, there will be two diesel back-up
          generators on standby should supply shortages impact the HPP.
10.12.21. The opportunity to substitute to lower emission assets is low, making the overall risks under each of
          the future scenarios also low.
          Current Scenario

                                                                 2023

                                                            Current Scenario

                         Likelihood                              Impact                            Risk

                               Low                                Low                              Low



          Future Scenario

                                                                 2030s

                              Low Emissions Scenario                              High Emissions Scenario

             Likelihood              Impact               Risk           Likelihood       Impact            Risk

                 Low                   Low                Low               Low            Low              Low

          Summary
10.12.22. The assessment of technological advances causing existing products to become obsolete and
          substituted with lower emissions options has revealed low risks in both scenarios. Rogun JSC as
          operators of Rogun HPP should ensure replacement of assets with lower emission options can be
          factored into planned replacement regimes.
          Risk 2
           Updates and innovations within hydropower production are driven by policy changes.
          Impact
           Policy driven technology updates mean extended periods of maintenance to replace key assets.
            This results in loss of revenue due to impaired, or no, energy generation.
          Context
10.12.23. Hydropower is well established as an energy source, though there are several innovation trends
          focused on increasing the resilience and capacity of hydropower plants. The five main themes when
          understanding the development of hydropower are:
                        i.      Hydropower flexibility;
                       ii.      Hydropower digitalization;
                       iii.     Energy storage and variable speed turbines;
                     iv.        Generators with current-controlled rotors; and
                       v.       Fish-friendly hydropower technologies.
10.12.24. Under a low emissions scenario, it is possible that the policy transition will be more stringent thereby
          putting pressure on technological developments to achieve a lower carbon scenario. As such, under
          a low emission scenario the risk would be Medium.
10.12.25. Under a high emissions scenario, it is likely the Project design will be capable of meeting current
          and projected policies and targets, given the current political landscape in Tajikistan.
          Current Scenario

                                                           2023

                                                     Current Scenario

                      Likelihood                          Impact                              Risk

                          Low                               Low                               Low


          Future Scenario

                                                           2030s

                        Low Emissions Scenario                               High Emissions Scenario

             Likelihood         Impact             Risk            Likelihood        Impact            Risk

               Medium           Medium           Medium             Medium            Low              Low

          Summary
10.12.26. The assessment of updates and innovations within hydropower production driven by policy changes
          has revealed medium risk in the low emission scenario and low risk in the high emission scenario.
          Rogun JSC as operators of Rogun HPP will need to ensure that upgrades and innovation are
          suitably planned to minimize the financial, and loss in energy production, impacts.
          MARKET CHANGES
          Risk 1
           Inability to reconnect to Central Asian Power System (CAPS).
          Impact
           Network balancing challenges due to inability to export power outputs in periods of higher
            domestic demand.
          Context
10.12.27. Prior to 2009, electricity network resilience was facilitated through Tajikistan’s connection to the
          CAPS. This was particularly important in meeting winter peak demand periods, through parallel
          operation with Uzbekistan. Without a re-connection to this system, management of the output from
          the Rogun HPP will be challenging given no means of exporting surplus power in this scenario.
          There is potential for difficulties in load management, causing network imbalances and potential
          need to curtail output from the scheme. This would mean inefficient operation and make integration
          of other renewable generation (solar and wind) harder to achieve.
10.12.28. Existing infrastructure provides export capacity between Tajikistan and Uzbekistan/Kyrgyzstan and
          on to Kazakhstan. This capacity is greater than the amount of generation capacity currently required
          to meet existing power purchase agreements with Kazakhstan. This has been secured through
          completion of the first phase of synchronization work with the CAPS. Full synchronization is
          anticipated for completion in 2027.
10.12.29. The CASA – 1000 project is developing network transmission infrastructure in Tajikistan,
          Afghanistan and Pakistan, interconnected with Kyrgyzstan. This will ultimately facilitate network
          resilience in the region and enable power flows from the Rogun HPP to neighboring countries.
10.12.30. Given uncertainty in availability of funding and therefore in timescales of delivery of the CASA-1000
          project, there is a medium risk under a low emission scenario. The risk under a high emission
          scenario is medium due to the scenario analysis that there will be a more relaxed transition to a
          lower carbon economy under a high emission scenario.
          Current Scenario

                                                           2023

                                                    Current Scenario

                      Likelihood                          Impact                             Risk

                        Medium                             Low                               Low

          Future Scenarios

                                                          2030s

                        Low Emissions Scenario                              High Emissions Scenario

             Likelihood          Impact           Risk            Likelihood        Impact             Risk

               Medium            Medium          Medium            Medium           Medium            Medium

          Summary
10.12.31. The assessment of uncertainty in market signals has revealed medium risk in the low emission
          scenario and medium risk in the high emission scenario. Rogun JSC as operators of Rogun HPP will
          need to monitor the market status to ensure any potential risks are identify early and measures can
          be implemented in a timely manner. Current infrastructure capacity, and planned synchronization
          with the CAPS, should ensure sufficient cross-border export capacity to enable all existing power
          purchase agreements and further capacity agreements as more generation capacity comes on line.

11       NOISE AND VIBRATION
11.1.1. The assessment of potential noise and vibration impacts generated by Project activities, was
        undertaken in line with the requirements of the WB ESS1: Assessment and Management of
        Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts.
11.1.2. A description of ambient noise baseline conditions is presented in Volume 2, Annex 7.1 – Baseline
        Conditions Report and a summary is provided in Volume 1, Chapter 7 of this ESIA.
11.1.3. In 2014, an environmental and social assessment of the Rogun hydropower plant was undertaken
        by Poyry. The ESIA report identified several sources of noise and vibration from construction
        activities:
          The use of explosives, vehicle movements and the operation of machines; and
          The use of equipment in the turbine hall and the movement of vehicles through the access roads
           in the tunnels.
11.1.4. The ESIA identified mitigation measures with the objective of reducing noise levels to comply with
        Tajik and other applicable noise limits. Mitigation measures included, among others:
             Use adequate and well-maintained construction and transportation equipment including state-of-
             the-art built in systems (muffler) to reduce the noise. The contractor must develop a maintenance
             program to ensure to keep noise within legally permitted limits;
            Instruct the workforce to avoid unnecessary noise;
            Use adequate and state of the art techniques for blasting, which do not exceed the exposition
             time to the noise;
            Workers exposed to excessive noise must be equipped with PPE (e.g. ear protectors) and have a
             limited exposure period;
            The quarry must be located a sufficient distance to any populated area;
            Separate installation areas, for example mechanical workshops etc. from areas that are used by
             people for temporary housing and recreation; distance should be at least 10 m;
            Noise and vibration associated with the use of explosives needs to be monitored with a maximum
             sound pressure level of 136 dB;
            Avoid any noise intensive activities such as metalworking, blasting (in quarries) etc. during
             nighttime; and
            Avoid transporting of material (rock, concrete, etc.) during night, if they have to pass villages.
11.1.5. The ESIA recommended compliance with noise standards set by international organisations such as
        WHO or with Tajik legislation. Several management and monitoring plans to facilitate
        implementation of the mitigation measures were proposed.

11.2     LEGISLATIVE FRAMEWORK, POLICY AND GUIDELINES
11.2.1. National legislation and international lender standards applicable to noise and vibration is described
        in Volume 1, Chapter 2: Legislation and Standards of this ESIA.
         TAJIKISTAN NATIONAL LEGISLATION
11.2.2. In Tajikistan, control of noise and vibration falls under the wider category of ‘environmental pollution’
        in terms of the Law of the Republic of Tajikistan on Environmental Protection No. 760 (2011), as
        amended:

                                                                                                            PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                     September 2025
         Project Management Group for Energy Facilities Construction under the President of the Republic of Tajikistan
                                                                                                         Page | 11-1
         Article 27 refers to the limits on noise and vibration to protect health and the environment; and
         Article 52 requires individuals and legal entities to prevent harmful industrial noises and
          vibrations.
11.2.3. The Tajikistan noise standards are stipulated in Sanitary Norm 2.2.4/2.1.8.562-96 for noise levels at
        workplaces, residential and public buildings and for work activities and sensitive receptors. Sound
        levels and equivalent sound levels for typical work activities and workplaces are presented in Table
        11-1.

11.2.5. Clause 383: Time and Activity Control of Sanitary Norm 2.2.4/2.1.8.562-96 specifies several
        restrictions to specific construction activities:
         Construction work from 22:00 to 06:00 on sites located at a distance of 100 m from residential
          areas will be prohibited;
         Construction work on sites located at 150 m from critical areas, such as schools or medical
          facilities, is prohibited from 18:00 to 08:00; and
         Noisy construction activities (such as the use of crushers) near residential areas or important
          facilities are prohibited.
        INTERNATIONAL LENDER REQUIREMENTS
11.2.6. World Bank Environmental and Social Standards (ESS) that are applicable to noise management
        include:
         World Bank ESS2: Labour and Working Conditions – conditions of work that are likely to be
          hazardous include, among others, noise and vibration levels that are damaging to health;
         World Bank ESS3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention – the objective to avoid or
          minimize adverse impacts on human health and the environment by avoiding or minimizing
          pollution, including noise and vibration; and
         World Bank ESS4: Community Health and Safety - adverse impacts on the health and safety of
          project-affected communities during the project life cycle from both routine and nonroutine
          circumstances should be avoided.


        2 L
          eq   an average decibel value without a frequency weighting for a non-constant noise

              max, the maximum sound level, during a measurement period or a noise event

11.2.7. In addition to the above, examples of general and industry-specific Good International Industry
        Practice (GIIP) are provided in:
         WB Environmental Health and Safety (EHS) Guidelines - General EHS Guidelines: Introduction
          (2007) – Section 1.7 provides detailed guidance for management of noise beyond the site
          boundary. Section 2.3 provides guidance on occupational exposure to noise;
         WB Good Practice Note: Environmental, Health and Safety Approaches for Hydropower Projects
          (2018) – noise and vibration is included as a source of pollution to be managed during both the
          construction and operation phase.
        Noise Level Guidelines
11.2.8. WB General EHS Guideline document provides noise levels for measured noise impacts from a
        project facility or operations. The Guideline states that the maximum increase in background levels
        should not exceed 3 dB at the nearest receptor location off-site or the noise levels in Table 11-3
        should not be exceeded.

        Table 11-3 - Noise Level Guidelines

          Receptor                                          1 Hour Threshold LAeq4

          Industrial, commercial                            Daytime and night-time: 70dB

11.2.11. The WHO Noise Guidelines, updated in 2018, provide recommended average sound pressure levels
         for outdoor exposure to, among others, road traffic noise. The WHO strongly recommends that the
         noise level generated by road traffic is below 53 dB Lden and below 45 dB Lnight during the daytime
         and nighttime hours respectively.
11.2.12. Directive 2000/14/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 8 May 2000 on the
         approximation of the laws of the Member States relating to the noise emission in the environment by
         equipment for use outdoors, as amended, specifies the permissible sound power level for specified
         construction equipment including compaction machines, dozers, loaders, graders and excavators;
         and the guaranteed sound power level of equipment subject to noise marking6, including concrete or
         mortar mixers.
11.2.13. Directive 2002/44/EC of 25 June 2002 on the Minimum Health and Safety Requirements Regarding
         the Exposure of Workers to the Risks Arising from Physical Agents (Vibration) aims to provide a
         minimum level of protection based on timely detection of adverse health effects arising or likely to
         arise from exposure to mechanical vibration, especially musculo-skeletal disorders to ensure worker
         health and safety.
        PROJECT NOISE LIMITS
11.2.14. For the construction phase, the noise limits are the Tajikistan maximum permissible sound levels for
         typical work activities and workplaces as presented in Table 11-1 and the Tajik maximum
         permissible sound levels for private and public buildings and residential areas, as presented in
         Table 11-2.
11.2.15. For the operational phase, the Project noise limits are the WB Guideline noise level in Table 11-3 for
         outdoor areas and the noise limits for working locations in Table 11-4 for indoor areas.
11.2.16. The Project noise limits are an amalgamation of the Tajikistan noise standards and World Bank
         EHSGs levels and are presented in Table 11-5.

         VIBRATION
11.2.17. Construction activities that generate vibration impacts include:
          Blasting;
          Operation of earth moving equipment; and
          HGV movements.
11.2.18. No measurements of vibration were taken on the site visit in May 2023 and none of the contractors
         monitor vibration as part of their environmental or occupational health monitoring.
11.2.19. Impacts due to vibration have been scoped out of the assessment as the geology of the area,
         described in Volume 1, Chapter 12: Geology and Soils of this ESIA, is a mix of igneous and
         sedimentary rock and, considering the combination of the geological characteristics, the
         management of blasting activity under Tajikistan regulatory requirements, the exponential
         attenuation of blast vibration with distance7 and that there are no buildings on the Project site within
         500 m of a blast site, the blasting activities undertaken site are unlikely to impact off-site receptors.
11.2.20. In addition, vehicles passing the village of Pasi Muhraho may cause vibration but as the vehicles
         travelling on the road passing the village are relatively light, it is unlikely that the vibration resulting
         from vehicle movements will impact on the houses and other structures within the village.

11.3     ASSESSMENT METHODOLOGY
11.3.1. The approach to the assessment has been to combine the primary data collected during a site visit
        in May 2023 and personal observations made during visits (primary data) with a review of historic
        data and information provided by the Contractors and other Project literature (secondary data).
11.3.2. The assessment methodology is described in Volume 1, Chapter 5: Methodology of this ESIA.
         AREA OF INFLUENCE
11.3.3. The Area of Influence (AoI) extends to the distance at which the noise level generated at source is
        not likely to exceed the background noise level, including:
          Locations around the construction site with notable noise sources including the quarries, borrow
           pits, stockpiles, batching/mixing plant, conveyor and dam wall working face; and
          Indoor areas including tunnels used as haul roads and caverns where construction activities are
           undertaken.



         7
          Vibration can be described in the form of a general exponential equation: A=kW bDn, where:
          A is a measure of the peak vibration amplitude (or velocity);
          W is the explosive charge weight per delay;
          D is the distance from the blast; and
          k, b, and n are constants determined by a given site or blasting procedure.


                                                                                                            PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                     September 2025
         Project Management Group for Energy Facilities Construction under the President of the Republic of Tajikistan
                                                                                                           Page | 11-6
        IMPACT MAGNITUDE
11.3.4. The magnitude of the potential impact considers the duration, extent, reversibility and likelihood of
        the impact. The impact magnitude categories are described in Table 11-6 and reflect that activities
        undertaken during the construction and operational phases either meet or exceed the Project noise
        limits; or may result in a 3dBA increase in background noise levels.

        Table 11-6 - Impact Magnitude Categories

          Impact Magnitude      Construction and Operational Phase

                                The Project noise limit is exceeded for a given activity or location or the
          Large                 background noise level is exceeded by more than 3 dBA at a specific
                                location

                                The Project noise limit is not exceeded for a given activity or location or the
          Very Small            background noise level is not exceeded by more than 3 dBA at a specific
                                location

        RECEPTOR SENSITIVITY
11.3.5. The receptors impacted by noise include workers on site and residents of local communities around
        the site where noise levels may be elevated due to construction or operational activities. Sensitivity
        is also a function of land use where:
         Commercial or industrial uses are not considered noise sensitive as activities within these areas
          are generally compatible with higher noise levels; and
         Residential and institutional uses, including worker accommodation and facilities, are considered
          noise sensitive, especially at nighttime.
11.3.6. For both the construction and operational phase, the sensitivity of workers on site and residents of
        local communities is considered high given that noise may induce medical conditions, such as
        hearing loss and tinnitus, stress, sleep disturbance and cardiovascular problems. Activities
        undertaken in designated outdoor or indoor areas may also be disturbed or disrupted by noise levels
        and the sensitivity of these receptors is described in Table 11-7.




                                                                                                           PUBLIC |

        EFFECT SIGNIFICANCE
11.3.9. As described in Volume 1, Chapter 5: Methodology of this ESIA, the significance of effects is a
        combination of the receptor sensitivity and the impact magnitude. Where the significance of the
        effect is moderate or major, the effect is considered significant, and mitigation measures are
        required. Where the significance is minor or negligible, the effect is considered not significant, and
        no mitigation measures are proposed.
