11.4.2. The activities that generate potential noise impacts during the operational phase include:
         Transformer operation;
         Vehicle movements; and
         Small plant and equipment operation.
11.5    CONSTRUCTION PHASE
11.5.1. Sound intensity follows an inverse square law and noise levels decrease by approximately 6 dB for
        a doubling of distance from a point source over open terrain8. Consideration of potential noise
        impacts on receptors uses the attenuation of noise with distance to determine the impact magnitude.
        BLASTING
11.5.2. Potential noise impacts arise from blasting activity currently undertaken at Quarry 26/26a, at the
        main dam site and at specific locations of the left bank and right bank. No blast noise measurements
        were recorded during the site visit in May 2023; but the literature notes that noise from a blast may
        impact human receptors.




        8 In acoustics, the inverse-square law in a field is understood as sound radiating into space from a source uniformly in all

        directions and the sound pressure level decreases 6 dB, on an unweighted scale, each time the distance from the point
        source is doubled. OSHA Technical Manual (OTM) Section III: Chapter 5 Noise. https://www.osha.gov/otm/section-3-
        health-hazards/chapter-5#whatisnoise

                                                                                                           PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                     September 2025
        Project Management Group for Energy Facilities Construction under the President of the Republic of Tajikistan
                                                                                                                      Page | 11-10
        Impact Assessment
11.5.3. On the construction site, workers have a high sensitivity in indoor and outdoor areas where noise is
        generated.
11.5.4. The procedure for blasting in open areas and tunnels is described in Annex 10 – Drilling and Blasting
        Management Subplan of the Occupational Health and Safety Management Plan (WeBuild, 2020). The
        measures already in effect to minimise the potential impact of noise from blasting on workers include:
        ▪   Restricting open air blasting times to shift changeover and workforce break times (usually
            between 12:00-13:00 and 18:00-19:00) to ensure that a minimal workforce is exposed to the
            noise;
        ▪   Evacuating workers to more than 50 m from the blast zone at least 30 minutes prior to the blast
            to ensure that exposure to the blast noise is minimised;
        ▪   Ensuring that all acoustic barriers are in place; and
        ▪   Ensuring that all workers undertaking activities associated with blasting and workers in the
            vicinity of the blast site have appropriate Personal Protection Equipment (PPE) including
            appropriate hearing protection.
11.5.5. The timing of blast activities to minimise the number of workers in indoor and outdoor areas who are
        exposed to the blast and the evacuation of workers from the blast zone effectively avoids the
        potential impact of excessive noise on workers who would be exposed. The evacuation distance
        reduces the noise impact magnitude to very small and the residual impact is minor, and no new
        mitigation is proposed.
11.5.6. The worker accommodation currently located within Construction Site 1 is approximately 2000 m
        from the nearest site of blasting activity at the main dam site, worker accommodation at
        Construction Site 2 is at least 1500 m from the main dam site and worker accommodation in Rogun
        City is approximately 2000 m from the main dam site.
11.5.7. The village of Pasi Muhraho is approximately 2000 m from the nearest blasting activity at the main
        dam site and Rogun City residential areas and the Hospital are at least 2200 m from the main dam
        site when measured to the entry checkpoint on the Rogun City boundary.
11.5.8. Given the exponential decrease of noise levels with distance, where noise levels decrease by 6 dB
        with a doubling of the distance from the noise source, the potential impact of blasting at the main
        dam site and Quarry 26/26a is effectively attenuated by the distance and topography between the
        location of noise sources and the receptor locations. The magnitude of blast noise impact on
        residents is very small, the significance of the effect is minor.
        Mitigation Measures
11.5.9. The following mitigation measures have been identified to further minimize the noise due to blasting
        activity on site should grievances regarding blasting noise be received from workers or the local
        community:
        ▪   A maximum level for air blast overpressure of 115 dB (Lin Peak9) shall be exceeded for no more
            than 5 % of blasts over a 12-month period; and


        9 Lin Peak (Linear Peak) is the maximum level of air pressure fluctuation measured in decibels without frequency

        weighting.

                                                                                                           PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                     September 2025
        Project Management Group for Energy Facilities Construction under the President of the Republic of Tajikistan
                                                                                                                  Page | 11-11
         ▪   Ground vibration (peak particle velocity) shall neither exceed 5 mm/second on 9 out of 10
             consecutive blasts, nor exceed 10 mm/second at any time. (Standard for Responsible Mining,
             2018).
11.5.10. The mitigation measures will form part of the requirements of the JSC Rogun Volume 3, B9 Outline
         Blasting and Explosives Management Plan of this ESIA that will be used by contractors to prepare
         detailed management plans.
         EARTH MOVING EQUIPMENT AND VEHICLE MOVEMENTS
11.5.11. There are 26 km of surface roads on the construction site, the majority of which are unpaved, that
         are primarily used by dump trucks to haul spoil from the crusher plant, stockpile areas, dump sites
         and the main dam site.
11.5.12. The site roads are also used by buses to transport shift workers commuting from site
         accommodation and surrounding villages to work locations. A fleet of smaller vehicles is used by
         contractor personnel to move around the site.
11.5.13. On the construction site, earth moving equipment, concrete mixer trucks and dump trucks are
         concentrated at the left and right bank stabilization works and the main dam (cofferdam) site and in
         routes connecting the various Project components.
11.5.14. The sensitivity of workers in outdoor areas where heavy earth moving equipment and construction
         vehicles are in operation throughout an 8-hour shift is high.
11.5.15. Locations opposite the left bank stabilisation site where receptors have a high sensitivity to noise
         include the Pasi Muhraho village, worker accommodation at Construction Site 2, worker
         accommodation in Construction Site 1, worker accommodation in Rogun City, the Hospital,
         residential areas and the clinics operated by JSC Rogun and WeBuild. The short-term noise
         measurement at Construction Site 1 in May 2023 noted noise from construction traffic on the site
         road network, private vehicles and general settlement noise.
11.5.16. During the site visit in May 2023, the noise level measurement of 60 dB(Leq) taken approximately 3m
         from the façade of one of the accommodation buildings at Construction Site 1 exceeded the Project
         standard of 45 dB(A) which is usually the nighttime limit but applied in this instance to take into
         consideration that shift workers sleep during daytime.
11.5.17. The magnitude of the potential impact of noise generated by the movement of heavy equipment and
         construction vehicles is large and the significance of the effect is major.
         Mitigation Measures
11.5.18. Given the limited accessibility on the left bank and right bank and the layout of the stabilization
         works site, the installation of acoustic barriers is not feasible. The following mitigation measures
         have been identified to reduce the potential impact of noise from heavy earth moving equipment and
         construction vehicles on the site:
          Ensure that earth moving equipment and vehicles are well maintained and servicing and repairs
           are recorded;
          Install noise attenuation devices such as mufflers and acoustic panels to minimise engine noise,
           replace noisy exhaust systems and turn off equipment and vehicles not in use;



                                                                                                            PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                     September 2025
         Project Management Group for Energy Facilities Construction under the President of the Republic of Tajikistan
                                                                                                       Page | 11-12
         Ensure that the speed limits of 40 km/h aboveground and 20 km/h tunnels are enforced on the
          site roads;
         Install sound dampening mats in the equipment operator cabins to reduce noise levels,
          particularly while the machine is operating at full-throttle, to meet Project limits for drivers (70 dB);
         Ensure that plant and equipment procured from European suppliers complies with Directive
          2000/14/EC;
         Provide workers in outdoor or indoor areas who are exposed to a noise level exceeding 70dB
          with appropriate hearing protection, especially where exposure is for the duration of an 8-hour
          shift; and
         Install acoustic barriers and insulation, where appropriate, at sensitive receptor locations,
          particularly at worker accommodation to ensure that sleeping quarters meeting the Project
          nighttime standard for residential areas is met during day and night.
11.5.19. All noise mitigations will be incorporated into Volume 3, B2: Outline Noise and Vibration
         Management Plan of this ESIA that will be prepared by JSC Rogun and used by contractors to
         prepare detailed management plans.
11.5.20. Site-wide requirements for onsite and offsite traffic movements will be described in Volume 3, On-
         Site Traffic Management Plan and Volume 3, Off-Site Traffic Management Plan of this ESIA
         respectively; and these requirements will also be relevant to the preparation of the detailed
         management plans to be prepared by contractors.
        Residual Effect Significance
11.5.21. With the implementation of the mitigation measures, the significance of the effect of potential noise
         on site workers, Pasi Muhraho village, worker accommodation in Construction Site 1, worker
         accommodation in Rogun City, the Hospital and residential areas and the WeBuild clinic will be
         reduced to moderate.
11.5.22. Given that a moderate effect is significant, further measures to be implemented include:
         Monitor noise levels at all high-sensitivity receptor locations, including the Hospital, to check
          ambient noise levels relative to the Project noise limits;
         Install acoustic barriers and insultation, where appropriate, at sensitive receptor locations, to
          ensure that Project standards are met; and
         Provide workers in areas exposed to noise levels that frequently or consistently exceed the
          Project noise limits for outdoor and indoor areas periodic medical hearing checks.
        CRUSHER AND CONVEYOR OPERATION
11.5.23. Crusher plants are operated at Quarry 15, Quarry 17 and Quarry 26 on the left bank.
11.5.24. Receptors having a high sensitivity to noise include the workers on site, Pasi Muhraho village
         adjacent to Construction Site 2, worker accommodation in Construction Site 1; worker
         accommodation in Rogun City, the Hospital and residential areas during nighttime (23:00 – 07:00).
11.5.25. There are no receptors located within 2500 m of the crusher plant at Quarry 15.
        Impact Assessment
11.5.26. There are five crusher plants in operation at Quarry 17. The distance between two adjacent crushers
         on Quarry 17 is approximately 300 m and given the logarithmic addition of airborne noise levels from


                                                                                                           PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                     September 2025
        Project Management Group for Energy Facilities Construction under the President of the Republic of Tajikistan
                                                                                                      Page | 11-13
         multiple sources the overall noise level from the crushers operating independently or concurrently
         would not be substantially different.
11.5.27. A covered conveyor of approximately 4 km in length has been installed to transport aggregate from
         the crusher at Quarry 15 and crushers at Quarry 17 to the entrance of Tunnel 4.
11.5.28. Spot noise measurements of up to 20 minutes duration were taken at an operational crusher during
         the site visit in May 2023. The short-term measurement taken 30 m from the crusher was 89 dB
         LAeq,T and 85 dB LAeq,T at 50m from crusher. Both measurements exceed the project standard of
         70 dB(A).
11.5.29. The sensitivity of outdoor areas where workers are present during working hours is high, the
         magnitude of the potential impact of noise from operation of the crusher plants is large and the
         significance of the effect is major.
11.5.30. The main conveyor systems on the site include:
          Quarry 15 crusher plant to the KT9 tunnel entrance – approximately 4000 m;
          Quarry 17 stockpiles to the KT9 tunnel entrance – approximately 500 m; and
          Quarry 22 crusher plant to the terrace above the main dam site, the ‘flying conveyer’ –
           approximately 1400 m.
11.5.31. Spot noise measurements were also taken at 2 m from the conveyor with a resulting noise level of
         81 dB(A); and a noise level of 77 dB(A) at 10 m, both noise levels exceeded the Project standard.
11.5.32. The closest receptor to the crushers and conveyor in Quarry 17 is the worker accommodation in
         Construction Site 1 located at least 1500 m to the south-west. Given the distance between the
         locations, the magnitude of the potential impact is very small, the significance of the effect is minor,
         and no mitigation is proposed.
11.5.33. The village of Pasi Muhraho, worker accommodation at Construction Site 2 and worker
         accommodation in Rogun City, residential areas, the Hospital and WeBuild clinic are all a minimum
         of 2000 m to the south-west of the nearest Quarry 17 crushers and conveyor, and the magnitude of
         the potential impact is very small, and the significance of the effect is minor and no mitigation is
         proposed.
11.5.34. Quarry 26/26a is located to the south-south-east of the main dam site and there are two crusher
         plants in operation. The worker accommodation in Construction Site 1, Construction Site 2 and the
         village of Pasi Muhraho, are approximately 3500 m to north and north-north-east respectively. Given
         the exponential attenuation of noise with distance, noise from the Quarry 26/26a crusher is unlikely
         to impact on the worker accommodation at Construction Site 1 or Construction Site 2, the village of
         Pasi Muhraho or at sensitive locations in Rogun City. With the distance of these locations from
         Quarry 26/26a, the magnitude of the potential impact of noise from the crusher plant is very small
         and the significance of the effect is minor, and no mitigation is proposed.
         Mitigation Measures
11.5.35. The installation of acoustic barriers around the crusher plant site is not feasible and the following
         mitigation measures have been identified:
          Ensure that the crusher plant is well maintained, use noise attenuation devices such as mufflers
           and acoustic panels within the equipment to minimise noise;


                                                                                                            PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                     September 2025
         Project Management Group for Energy Facilities Construction under the President of the Republic of Tajikistan
                                                                                                       Page | 11-14
          Follow manufacturers operating instructions for the start-up and shutdown procedures, and
           efficiently feeding the primary, secondary and tertiary crushers;
          Ensure that workers do not stay in areas where noise levels exceed the Project noise limit for
           longer than necessary during an 8-hour shift;
          Follow manufacturers operating instructions for the start-up and shutdown procedures and
           efficient loading of the conveyor;
          Ensure that the conveyor is well maintained and install noise attenuation devices such as
           acoustic panels within the equipment to minimise noise; and
          Ensure that no worker is exposed to a noise level exceeding 70dB without having appropriate
           hearing protection.
         Residual Impact Significance
11.5.36. With the implementation of the mitigation measures, the magnitude of the potential impact will
         reduce to medium and the significance of the effect to moderate.
11.5.37. Given that a moderate effect remains significant, further measures to be implemented include:
          Monitor noise levels at locations of high-sensitivity receptors to check ambient noise levels
           relative to the Project noise limits; and
          Provide workers in areas exposed to noise levels that exceed the Project noise limits for outdoor
           and indoor areas periodic medical hearing checks.
         TUNNELS
11.5.38. There are 70 km of underground roads in tunnels, of which 6 km of underground roads will remain
         during the operational phase. The tunnels are used for vehicle access to the site and the daily traffic
         count at the entrance of T3 tunnel is approximately 35 vehicles per hour. There is also plant and
         equipment, including ventilation fans, operating in the tunnels.
11.5.39. Workers are in the tunnels to manage vehicle movements and rest areas and facilities for workers
         are in the tunnels. The sensitivity of workers in the tunnels during a 12-hour shift is high.
         Impact Assessment
11.5.40. There is currently limited data available on flows of vehicles on-site. However, the daily traffic flow at
         the entrance of T3 tunnel is approximately 852 vehicles per day. 86 dB LAeq,T at approximately 10
         m from main construction activities and this level exceeds the Project standard of 70 dB(A). The
         magnitude of the potential impact of the noise levels in the tunnel on workers during a 12-hour shift
         is large and the significance of the impact is major.
         Mitigation Measures
11.5.41. The following mitigation measures have been identified to minimise traffic noise in the tunnels:
          Enforce the speed limit of 20 km/h in the tunnels for all vehicles;
          Ensure that vehicles are well maintained and install noise attenuation devices such as mufflers
           and silencers;
          Enclose noisy machinery with sound-absorbing material to reduce the nose when operating;
          Switch off machinery in the tunnels when not in operation; and
          Ensure that no worker in the tunnel is exposed to a noise level exceeding 70dB without having
           appropriate hearing protection.


                                                                                                            PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                     September 2025
         Project Management Group for Energy Facilities Construction under the President of the Republic of Tajikistan
                                                                                                       Page | 11-15
        Residual Effect Significance
11.5.42. With the implementation of the mitigation measures, the significance of the effect will be reduced to
         moderate. Given that a moderate effect remains significant, further measures to be implemented
         include:
         Monitor noise levels within the tunnels to check noise levels relative to the Project noise limits;
          and
         Provide workers in areas exposed to noise levels that exceed the Project noise limits for indoor
          areas periodic medical hearing checks.
        CONSTRUCTION SITE 1 DEMOLITION
11.5.43. Building and structures on Construction Site 1 will be demolished and all scrap materials will be
         cleared from the site by 2027 prior to flooding of the lower terrace as the height of the reservoir
         increases. New worker accommodation facilities will be built on Construction Site 2, and all workers
         will be relocated to the new accommodation facilities prior to demolition works commencing.
        Impact Assessment
11.5.44. Many of the structures are constructed with a steel frame and corrugated iron cladding and can be
         readily dismantled and removed without generating excessive noise. The abandoned structures may
         contain concrete slabs, but it is likely that these will remain in situ.
11.5.45. Detailed management plans will be prepared by contractors for the demolition activities and will
         comply with the JSC Rogun requirements described in Volume 3, B4: Outline Demolition
         Management Plan of this ESIA and the JSC Rogun requirements described in Volume 3, B2:
         Outline Noise and Vibration Management Plan of this ESIA.
11.5.46. The nearest sensitive receptor to Construction Site 1 is the worker accommodation in Rogun City
         that is on a terrace above the Construction Site 1, approximately 400 m from the closest structure on
         Construction Site 1 that will be demolished.
11.5.47. Another sensitive receptor is the Pasi Muhraho village adjacent to Construction Site 2. Although the
         layout of Construction Site 2 is not finalised, the placement of mechanical workshops, machine
         shops, waste separation yards, washdown and parking areas relative to the Pasi Muhraho village is
         critical to comply with Project noise limits.
11.5.48. The sensitivity of the area of worker accommodation in Rogun City and the Pasi Muhraho village is
         high, the magnitude of the potential impact is small, and the significance of the effect is moderate.
        Mitigation Measures
11.5.49. The following mitigation measures have been identified:
         Dismantle steel frame structures and corrugated iron cladding to minimise noise levels;
         Establish noise barriers around concrete structures that require heavy earth-moving equipment
          for demolition;
         Ensure that earth moving equipment and vehicles are well maintained, use noise attenuation
          devices such as mufflers and acoustic panels to minimise engine noise and turn off equipment
          and vehicles not in use;
         As far as possible, plan the deconstruction and demolition work to sequentially move away from
          the Rogun City boundary; and

                                                                                                           PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                     September 2025
        Project Management Group for Energy Facilities Construction under the President of the Republic of Tajikistan
                                                                                                      Page | 11-16
         Plan the routes used by construction vehicles to minimise the impact on the Pasi Muhraho village
          adjacent to Construction Site 2.
        Residual Impact Significance
11.5.50. With the implementation of the mitigation measures, the magnitude of the potential impact small
         and the significance of the effect is minor.

11.6    OPERATIONAL PHASE
        VEHICLE MOVEMENTS
11.6.1. The receptors that have a high sensitivity to noise from vehicle movements include outdoor areas
        where workers are present and residents Pasi Muhraho village and Rogun City, the Hospital and
        general residential areas.
11.6.2. Given that the 6km of underground roads that remain in the operational phase will not require
        workers to be stationed in the tunnel to manage traffic movements, there will be no impact on
        workers from the movement of vehicles through the tunnels and this is scoped out of the operational
        phase assessment.
11.6.3. Under normal conditions, Rogun HPP will operate uninterrupted over a 24-hour period, and a
        system of shift work will result in vehicle movement twice a day. The Project operational fleet is
        mostly light vehicles with HGV for maintenance work and specialised vehicles such as ambulances
        and fire engines. Workers will not be stationed alongside roads during their shifts and the impact of
        noise is scoped out of the assessment.
        Impact Assessment
11.6.4. Given the workforce of approximately 600 personnel during the operational phase, the number of
        vehicle movements on site and between the site, Rogun City and surrounding villages will be
        substantially reduced when compared to the construction phase. Personnel will likely reside in
        Rogun City and the majority will commute to site using the shuttle bus service; the minority will
        commute by private vehicle. Specialised vehicles will be used to transport oversized and heavy
        loads to site in compliance with regulatory requirements.
11.6.5. The magnitude of the potential impact of vehicle noise on workers and residents of Pasi Muhraho
        and Rogun City is very small, and the significance of the effect is minor.
        Mitigation Measures
11.6.6. Although the potential impact is minor, the following mitigation measures have been identified to
        further minimise the impact of vehicle noise:
         Enforce the site speed limit of 40 km/h, particularly in areas where workers congregate at shift
          changeover, around bus shelters, and at the entrance to the Rogun site;
         Enforce the speed limit along the route that passes between Rogun City and the village of Pasi
          Muhraho;
         Ensure vehicles are mechanically well maintained to reduce engine noise;
         Monitor noise levels at location of high-sensitivity receptors in Rogun City and Pasi Muhraho to
          check that noise levels comply with the Project noise limits; and
         Install acoustic fencing or similar noise barriers at sensitive receptors, such as residential,
          institutional and educational locations in the village of Pasi Muhraho, if necessary.

                                                                                                           PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                     September 2025
        Project Management Group for Energy Facilities Construction under the President of the Republic of Tajikistan
                                                                                                      Page | 11-17
        Residual Effect Significance
11.6.7. The implementation of the mitigation measures will reduce the significance of the effect to
        negligible.
        OTHER NOISE SOURCES
        Transformer Room
11.6.8. As described in Volume 1, Chapter 3: Project Description of this ESIA, an underground
        transformer room will accommodate the five transformers that will connect the turbines to the
        aboveground substation. With Rogun HPP currently operating at reduced capacity, transformers on
        site are operating below the maximum load capacity.
        Impact Assessment
11.6.9. Personnel working in high voltage areas are required to adhere to occupational health and safety
        (OHS) regulations, including limits on exposure to noise levels. Compliance with OHS regulations
        will effectively reduce the potential impact of exposure to noise level through the mandatory use of
        hearing protection and limits on the duration of exposure. The magnitude of the impact is very
        small, and the significance of the effect is minor, and no further mitigation is proposed.
11.6.10. Noise from the transformer room will effectively be contained in the underground location and
         potential impacts from offsite noise have been scooped out of the assessment.
        Turbine Hall
11.6.11. As described in Chapter 3: Project Description of this ESIA, Rogun HPP will operate six turbines
         in an underground turbine hall (also referred to as the powerhouse). Currently there are two turbines
         in operation at a reduced capacity; and these will be upgraded to match the boilerplate capacity of
         the four new turbines to be installed.
        Impact Assessment
11.6.12. As for the transformer room, compliance with OHS regulations will effectively reduce the potential
         impact of exposure to noise level through the mandatory use of hearing protection and limits on the
         duration of exposure and no mitigation is proposed for workers.
11.6.13. Regular noise monitoring is currently undertaken in the turbine hall. Measurements indicate that
         noise levels are 75-80 dB, and this exceeds the Project standard. The source of noise has been
         identified as the construction work undertaken in the nearby tunnels. However, the specific turbine
         model for the Rogun HPP is unknown, but Franci’s turbines typically operate within a sound level
         range of 81.95-104 dB, and, at the lower level, the noise level may exceed the Project standard. The
         magnitude of the potential impact is large, and the significance of the effect is major.
        Mitigation Measures
11.6.14. The following mitigation measures have been identified to minimise noise levels within the turbine
         hall:
         Implement the mitigation measures identified for noise from construction activities undertaken in
          tunnels as described in Section 11.5.41;
         Ensure that the turbines commissioning process includes a noise analysis under different intake
          flows to optimise the noise levels; and


                                                                                                           PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                     September 2025
        Project Management Group for Energy Facilities Construction under the President of the Republic of Tajikistan
                                                                                                      Page | 11-18
          Ensure that no worker in the turbine hall is exposed to a noise level exceeding 70dB without
           having appropriate hearing protection.
         Residual Effect Significance
11.6.15. With the implementation of the mitigation as described for construction activities in tunnels, the
         significance of the effect is reduced to moderate. As a moderate effect is considered significant,
         further mitigation is required, including:
          Noise monitoring as described in Section 11-7 is required until the construction activities are
           completed; and
          The requirement for a noise monitoring program described in Section 11-7 includes the
           operational phase.
11.6.16. Noise from the turbines will effectively be contained in the underground location and potential
         impacts from noise on offsite locations have been scoped out of the assessment.
         Spillway
11.6.17. The Rogun HPP is designed to discharge water through the turbines with surplus water stored in the
         reservoir until it reaches the full supply level, when the excess water is then discharged.
11.6.18. The Climate Risk and Vulnerability Assessment (CRVA) done in Volume 1, Chapter 10: Climate
         Resilience of this indicates that, over the next century, summer precipitation is expected to
         decrease but winter precipitation will increase significantly, and the total annual precipitation is
         expected to increase as a result. Temperatures are expected to generally increase and as a result,
         more precipitation is expected to occur as rain and less as snow. Glaciers in the upper catchment of
         the Project are expected to continue melting with increased melt water flooding in the next few
         decades. This has the potential to increase annual and inter-annual variability, leading to greater
         average flow volumes and greater variability.
11.6.19. The Dam Safety Panel of Experts (DSPOE) has considered spillway alternatives in the context of
         maximum probable flood (MPF) event management and proposed that two mid-level tunnels and
         one mid-level outlet, complemented by one surface spillway will provide acceptable protection at
         Rogun HPP against the MPF at the reservoir full level of 1290 masl.
         Impact Assessment
11.6.20. Depending on the annual filling rate, the Rogun reservoir is expected to take between 9 and 22
         years to fill. During the filling phase, in the event of a flood, water will pass through right-bank mid-
         level diversion tunnels and outlet. Once the reservoir has reached full capacity, the spillway will
         convey water from extreme events that cannot be released via the mid-level tunnels, outlet. In
         combination, the tunnels, outlets and spillway will be capable of discharging 7100 m3/s at a water
         level of 1290 masl.
11.6.21. During the operational phase, the spillway will only operate during extreme conditions and workers
         will not be in proximity to the spillway during a flood event. As such, the potential impact of noise
         from spillway water flow on sensitive receptors has been scoped out of the assessment.

11.7     MONITORING AND ADAPTIVE MANAGEMENT
11.7.1. Site-wide E&S standards and guidelines will be established by JSC Rogun in Volume 3, B2:
        Outline Noise and Vibration Management Plan of this ESIA. The document will specify the

                                                                                                            PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                     September 2025
         Project Management Group for Energy Facilities Construction under the President of the Republic of Tajikistan
                                                                                                       Page | 11-19
        Project noise limits for sensitive receptors, identify the overarching requirements for noise
        abatement techniques, alignment with other Project management plans, the responsibilities and
        coordination required for implementation, monitoring and reporting to JSC Rogun.
11.7.2. For the construction phase, the EPC Contractor and sub-contractors will each prepare detailed
        noise management plan that comply with the site-wide E&S standards and guidelines established by
        JSC Rogun. The detailed plans will be reviewed and approved by the Engineer's Representative
        and/or PMG. For the operational phase, the detailed management plan will be prepared and
        implemented by JSC Rogun as the operator of Rogun HPP.
11.7.3. In the absence of representative noise measurements to quantify the noise level at notable noise
        source locations, mitigation measures identified in this ESIA may not be sufficient and residual
        effects may remain significant. This has been addressed by including a requirement for further
        measures to be implemented, specifically for noise monitoring to be undertaken to check noise
        levels relative to the Project standards.
11.7.4. The noise monitoring to be undertaken requires a comprehensive and systematic program to be
        developed and implemented for the construction and operational phases. The monitoring program
        should be developed by acoustic specialists with expertise and experience of preparing monitoring
        plans for large construction sites having multiple types of noise-generating activities.
11.7.5. Specific requirements for noise monitoring include:
         Sampling should be carried out using a Type 1 sound level meter (SLM) that meets all
          appropriate IEC standards and is subject to annual calibration by an accredited laboratory;
         The following acoustic indices should be recoded and reported: LAeq (T), LAIeq (T), statistical
          noise level LA90, LAFmin and LAFmax, octave band or 3rd octave band frequency spectra; and
         The SLM should be located approximately 1.5 m above the ground and no closer than 3 m to any
          reflecting surface.
11.7.6. An assessment of vibration must be done that is aligned with GIIP such as Directive 2002/44/EC
        that requires:
         Daily exposure limit values standardized to an eight-hour reference period shall be 1.15 m/s2; and
         Daily exposure action value standardised to an eight-hour reference period shall be 0.5 m/s2.
11.7.7. The JSC Rogun requirements for a noise and vibration monitoring program are described in Volume
        3, B2: Outline Noise and Vibration Management Plan of this ESIA.

        mitigation, is presented in Table 11-10 and Table 11-11 respectively.

12        SOILS AND GEOLOGY
12.1.1.   This chapter presents the impact assessment for Soils and Geology for the Project. Soils and
          geology impacts are described in Section 12.4 using the assessment methodology described in
          Volume 1, Chapter 5: ESIA Methodology of this ESIA. A summary of the results of the impact
          assessment is also presented in Section 12.5.
12.1.2.   Significant adverse effects of moderate or higher have been reassessed following mitigation.
          Mitigation already set out in Volume 1, Chapter 3: Project Description of this ESIA, has been
          incorporated into the initial assessment.

12.2      LEGISLATIVE FRAMEWORK, POLICY AND GUIDANCE
12.2.1.   The Land Code of the Republic of Tajikistan (327 of 1996), as amended on 19-07-2022, sets out the
          provisions for safeguarding and the effective use of land, land use rights and allocation of land, and
          provides measures to reduce deterioration of land to support ecological function.
12.2.2.   Article 51 of the Land Code sets out the aims and objectives for managing land and preventing
          degradation from economic activities and developments including requirements to:
             ensure the improvement and restoration of land that is being degraded or disturbed;
             create a mechanism for accounting and verification of the ecological condition of lands, as well
              as provide land users with ecological standards approved by orders established by the
              Government of the Republic of Tajikistan.
12.2.3.   Article 52 of the Code sets out the requirements on land users to:
             Ensure an appropriate site layout to avoid or reduce potential land degradation
             Restore and improve soil fertility, as well as other properties of the land;
             Protect land from water and wind erosion, mudflows, waterlogging, swamping, compaction,
              salinisation, pollution by industrial wastes, chemical and radioactive substances and other
              degradation;
             Maintain agricultural lands to reduce overgrowth (e.g. with weeds, trees and bushes), and other
              types of degradation to the cultural value or soils structure;
             Appropriate stripping, storage and preservation of topsoil during construction;
             Restoration and/or rehabilitation of soil fertility and properties of the land.
12.2.4.   Article 52 also requires all works to ensure timely restoration of soils or where this is not possible,
          conservation of lands shall be considered in any orders established by the Government of the
          Republic of Tajikistan
12.2.5.   Article 53 of the Code sets out the overarching requirements on developers regarding use of the
          land. The article requires all developments to be undertaken in a sustainable manner, and where
          degradation of the land occurs as a result of construction, commissioning or operation of any
          structures, techniques or technologies, requires appropriate measures to be implemented in
          compliance with environmental protection, sanitary protection measures or any special requirements
          as detailed in orders by the Government of the Republic of Tajikistan.
12.2.6.   Articles 54 and 101 of the code establish the polluter pays principle with levies and fines for
          developers polluting or otherwise degrading land quality during construction or operation of facilities.



          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
          Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                               November 2024
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page | 12-1
           INTERNATIONAL LENDER REQUIREMENTS
12.2.7.    Lenders’ environmental and social standards (ESS) detail specific requirements relating to the
           protection of and prevention of degradation to soils and geology.
12.2.8.    World Bank ESS3 sets out the guidance for managing raw materials appropriately and sustainably.
           Paragraph 5 sets out the need to identify technically and financially feasible options for using raw
           materials efficiently, as well as requiring the incorporation of cleaner production measures where
           appropriate.
12.2.9.    Paragraph 10 of ESS3 sets out the additional requirements to meet GIIP as described in the World
           Bank Environmental, Health and Safety (EHS) Guidelines to reduce use where the project is
           expected to be a significant user of raw materials.
12.2.10.   Paragraphs 11 to 14 of ESS3 set out the requirements for minimising the potential for pollution to
           the environment including pollution to land. It requires the developer to identify historic pollution and
           as well as the party responsible for the pollution, and to ensure remediation to GIIP or National
           requirements whichever is most stringent. It also requires the developer to use GIIP to minimize
           emissions in line with the mitigation hierarchy.
12.2.11.   Paragraphs 19 to 20 of ESS3 identify the key requirements for the management of chemicals and
           other hazardous materials to minimize the potential for pollution to soils. In addition, Section D
           (paragraphs 21 to 25) set out in detail the requirements where pesticides or herbicides are planned
           to be used.
12.2.12.   In general, all lender Environmental and Social Standards/Guidelines including ADB, AIIB, and EIB
           are broadly consistent with the World Bank ESS. Key differences between lender standards and the
           World Bank Environmental and Social Framework (ESF) are:
              Many of the standards advocate, and the EIB Environmental and Social Standards (2022)
               require, the application of the Circular Economy in terms of material use and prevention of
               pollution.
              The AIIB ESF Framework (2024) explicitly requires an Asbestos Management Plan to be
               developed for Projects for the removal and disposal of asbestos materials.
              The EIB requires the implementation of BAT and the EU IPPC BREF (BAT Reference) notes, as
               appropriate, for assessing technically and financially feasible alternatives to minimize pollution.
12.2.13.   Typical Good International Industry Practise (GIIP) is set out in the World Bank EHS General and
           associated Sector Specific Guidelines. Key measures relating to Soils and Geology are:
              Section 1.5 of the General EHS guidelines provides detailed GIIP for managing Hazardous
               Materials for the prevention of pollution to soils, including appropriate handling and storage of
               solid and liquid materials (e.g. chemicals, fuels, oils), and the incorporation of secondary
               containment (e.g. use of bunded double skinned tanks etc.).
              Section 1.8 of the General EHS guidelines provide a detailed description for the identification,
               management, treatment and remediation of Contaminated land, including the requirement for a
               contaminated land risk assessment, and GIIP remediation requirements.
12.2.14.   Sector specific guidelines of key relevance to Soils and Geology with specific requirements are:
              For quarries and other areas of waste rock (e.g. Quarry 26A) the Environmental, Health and
               Safety Guidelines for Mining (2007) are relevant which includes specific requirements for


           Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
           Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                              November 2024
           Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 12-2
              managing the potential for pollution from Acid Rock Drainage (ARD) and Acid Mine Drainage
              (AMD) as well as for managing waste rock dumps and potential drainage and pollution due to
              leaching.
             For all borrow pit areas (e.g.) the Environmental, Health, and Safety Guidelines for Construction
              Materials Extraction (2007) provide GIIP for managing leachate and waste materials areas for
              extracting gravels, clay and other construction materials.
             For all fuel storage areas, the Environmental, Health, and Safety Guidelines for Retail Petroleum
              Networks (2007) provide GIIP for underground and above ground storage tanks for Fuels and
              oils to prevent pollution to soils.

12.3      ASSESSMENT METHODOLOGY
12.3.1.   The assessment methodology for describing the magnitude of impact, sensitivity of receptors, and
          significance of effects, is described in Table 12-1 to Table 12-3.
12.3.2.   The assessment of the Project has been undertaken primarily through a desk-based study using
          available information relating to geology and soils.
12.3.3.   The baseline data has been informed by the following resources:
             Aerial imagery – OS Maps and Google Earth;
             Desk-based review of existing publicly available information;
             Existing Project information provided by PMG, Rogun JSC and Primary Contractors; and
             Site visit observations and stakeholder engagement.
          DETERMINING SIGNIFICANT EFFECTS
12.3.4.   The significance of each effect is assessed based on the magnitude of change due to the Project,
          and the sensitivity of the affected receptor to the change.
12.3.5.   The determination of magnitude is based on available data and professional judgement. The most
          important considerations are duration, extent, reversibility, and likelihood. The definitions of these
          key criteria for Soils and Geology, and how they are considered to define magnitude, are presented
          in Table 12-1.

          Table 12-1 – Magnitude criteria
           Magnitude    Criteria

           Large           Change is likely to cause a direct adverse permanent or long-term (> 10 years) effect on
                            a local to regional scale
                           Change at a regional to National scale, periodic (multiple occurrence) to constant impact,
                            likely to adversely affect the quality/value of the soil, but recovery is predicted in the
                            medium term (i.e. 3 -10 years).
                           Permanent changes to geological features of regional /national importance
                           Nationally significant or transboundary contaminant release, soil degradation or
                            subsidence event

           Medium          Change at a local to regional scale, periodic (multiple occurrence) to constant impact,
                            likely to adversely affect the quality/value of the soil, but recovery is predicted in the
                            medium term (i.e. 3 -10 years).
                           Local direct adverse, permanent or long-term / permanent effects.
                           Permanent impacts to geology at a local to regional scale
                           Local to regional contaminant release to soil with the potential for migration to receptors



          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                       PUBLIC |
          Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                   November 2024
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                    Page | 12-3
           Magnitude     Criteria
                                Local to regional soil degradation or subsidence

           Small                Change likely to adversely affect the quality/value of the soil but reversible in the short
                                 term (< 3 years), at a local scale.
                                Local, small spillage causing localised impact, or localised soil degradation or subsidence
                                Reversible, localised impacts to geology e.g. borrow pits

           Very Small           Receptor change is essentially indistinguishable from natural background variation or
                                 baseline conditions that existed before the commencement of the Project. Could occur
                                 over any duration or geographic extent.

           Beneficial           Impacts leading to positive effects on soil of any duration or scale



12.3.6.   The sensitivity of the affected receptor is assessed on a scale of high, medium and low as defined in
          Table 12-2.

          Table 12-2 – Sensitivity criteria
           Sensitivity       Criteria

           High                  Highly fertile soils suitable for arable agriculture
                                 Soils that are structurally prone to compaction or erosion, highly leachable and/or prone
                                  to contamination
                                 Soils which host shallow aquifers relied upon for potable abstraction, or essential for
                                  river baseflow
                                 Soils of specific characteristics (e.g. pH, carbon content, mineralogy) that supports or
                                  could support specific critical habitats
                                 Regionally or nationally significant geology (e.g. important economic, structural, or
                                  cultural geological form)

           Medium                Soils used for limited agricultural production, grazing or subsistence agriculture
                                 Soils that are less structurally prone to compaction or erosion, moderately leachable
                                 Soils of specific characteristics (e.g. pH, carbon content, mineralogy) that support or
                                  could support natural habitats
                                 Soils which host shallow aquifers relied upon for non-potable abstraction, or essential
                                  for river baseflow

           Low                   Soils of low fertility which are not used and unsuitable for agriculture
                                 Soils that are robust to physical disturbance and/or impermeable to contamination
                                 Contaminated made-ground soils at brownfield sites
                                 Soils not able to support sensitive or important habitats

          The sensitivity of the soils and geology in different areas of the Project with regard to these impacts
          is presented in Table 12-3.
