12.4      POTENTIAL IMPACTS AND EFFECTS
          CONSTRUCTION PHASE
12.4.1.   The impacts to soils and geology as a result of the Project, and the mitigation measures to be
          implemented, are described below under the following headings:
             Soil Loss and Degradation: Impacts to soils from clearance, storage, and other disturbance
             Soil Loss: Impacts to soils from inundation
             Soil Loss: Impacts from vehicle movements
             Soil and aquifer loss: Impacts from excavations and quarrying, and abstractions
             Contamination: Impacts to soils from demolition works
             Contamination: Impacts to soils and geology from historic pollution
             Contamination: Impacts to soils and geology from leaks and spills
12.4.2.   Where applicable the impacts under each of the above headings are described for both the Main
          Rogun HPP Construction Site and Other Areas (e.g. inundation zone, left bank roads and bridges,
          new resettlement areas, etc).



          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                         PUBLIC |
          Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                     November 2024
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                      Page | 12-5
          Soil Loss and Degradation: Impacts to soils from clearance, storage, and other disturbance
12.4.3.   Construction activities have the potential to remove completely or affect the structural integrity of
          soils through stripping of top and subsurface soils, storage of materials in stockpiles, erosion of soil
          from stockpiles and other construction disturbance areas (e.g. roads, road embankments and
          bridges exposed to wind and water erosion), as well as through compaction of subsoils.
12.4.4.   The main sources of impact to soil structure and integrity during construction will be:
             Removal of topsoils in new areas for construction of ancillary works using mechanical diggers.
             Compaction Rollers.
             Temporary access roads and vehicles including trucks etc.
             Material laydown areas, and construction areas
             Temporary stockpiles of soils
             Temporary accommodation areas (e.g. for roads and other works)
             Improperly managed wastewater.
          Impact Assessment
          Main Rogun HPP Construction Site.
12.4.5.   Within the historic construction area of the Rogun HPP much of the topsoil and subsoil was stripped
          during the early part of construction in the 1980s and have either been removed or reused in the
          project area during that period. As such remaining topsoil is very limited and is mainly comprised of
          low value non-agricultural soils. Compaction of underlying subsoils is extensive and has been
          undertaken over many decades, and therefore remaining integrity is likely to be limited.
12.4.6.   The sensitivity of soils in this area is considered to be Low, with further expansion of construction in
          this area limited due to progressive inundation. Any magnitude of impact is likely to be Small in the
          main construction area.
12.4.7.   Downstream of the dam (Construction Area 5) proposals for new accommodation areas and
          construction camps are being developed, with some accommodation being constructed (Nov 2024).
          These areas contain some soils of value for landscaping and for re-use. Soils are typically in this
          area of low agricultural potential but retain natural seedbanks for other uses, with some soils
          capable of supporting limited crops. Given the potential for some soils of higher value in this area,
          taking a precautionary approach the sensitivity of soils in Construction area 5 is considered to be
          Medium.
12.4.8.   Construction is ongoing downstream of the dam; however, adequate stripping and storage of
          topsoils and subsoils was not observed on-site at the time of the visit. Stripping each layer in order
          and storing soils in separate stockpiles according to type is crucial to ensure structural integrity and
          intrinsic value on reuse can be retained.
12.4.9.   Given the speed at which the development of Construction Area 5 is taking place a precautionary
          approach to the assessment has been taken. Canteen facilities and accommodation for Lot 3 is
          already well advanced, and further construction for other lots is in development. The construction
          area will be in place for between 5 and 10 years with some potential for areas to remain beyond
          construction (e.g. maintenance facilities and storage areas).




          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
          Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                              November 2024
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 12-6
12.4.10.   Any stockpiles are likely to be required for at least 1 year before re-use can be completed. Where
           stockpiles are not managed effectively integrity could be compromised in the medium term (3-5
           years) lengthening recovery on re-use.
12.4.11.   Once soils have been stripped compaction of subsoils is likely to occur with some hardstanding
           being laid down (tarmac and concrete) and remaining areas hardened and compacted through truck
           movements. The downstream construction area itself, however, is local in scale, and therefore a
           Medium magnitude has been applied.
12.4.12.   The significance of the effects is likely to be Moderate for downstream of the Dam in areas of
           Construction Area 5 subject to new development and Negligible for other areas of the main
           construction site.
           Other Areas – (Left bank roads and bridges, new resettlement areas etc.)
12.4.13.   Outside of the main construction area, within the inundation zone, soils are more heterogenous and
           comprise some areas of high fertility soils. The exact distribution of these soils and depths is not
           clearly mapped, given the steepness of the slopes, seismic activity, and high potential for small
           scale landslides, although soils maps of the areas and site observations confirm the presence of
           some High and Medium value agricultural soils.
12.4.14.   Works are ongoing outside of the main construction site to support delivery of the Project, including
           replacing roads and bridges on the left bank. Resettlement is also ongoing with sites for newly
           resettled people being identified on an ongoing basis. Many of the designs and specific locations are
           in development or unknown at present. Soils in these areas are already disturbed/lost so have Low
           sensitivity, though pockets of soils remain that have Medium sensitivity.
12.4.15.   The earthmoving activities to create temporary laydown areas for construction materials and
           construction site infrastructure (e.g. temporary offices, worker accommodation, etc) have the
           potential to damage soil structure through compaction. Soil compaction typically increases density
           and soil strength and reduces soil porosity and soil hydraulic properties, affecting yields. In sloping
           landscapes, compaction may also lead to increased runoff and erosion.
12.4.16.   Compaction effects on surface topsoils (up to 45cm) and sub-soils can result in erosion and below
           average crop yields over a longer period of time (up to 10 years), although mechanical loosening of
           subsoils can be undertaken to promote structural recovery and improved vegetation cover to reduce
           erosion.
12.4.17.   Taking a precautionary approach and using the worst-case scenario of construction of the left Bank
           Roads occurring at a regional scale, the Magnitude of the effect is considered to be Medium. The
           significance of the effect is therefore Moderate for productive agricultural soils outside of the main
           construction area and Minor for Low sensitivity soils in other areas including areas already disturbed
           by construction activities.
           Mitigation and Enhancement Measures
12.4.18.   Before any Contractor mobilizes into areas not previously used for construction, they will be required
           to develop Construction Method Statements that shall be reviewed and approved by the PMC and
           the HSE Manager prior to any commencement of work. These will include (but are not limited to):
              Sequence of Operation,
              Requirements for Site Mobilisation including equipment,



           Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
           Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                              November 2024
           Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 12-7
              Full description of Construction activities including design and good construction management
               practises proposed for the work,
              Identification of relevant ESMPs applicable to the site, and how procedures and designs will
               accommodate ESMP requirements,
               Layouts including laydown areas, stockpile location and design, office areas, storage and waste
                   areas etc.,
              Location of known sensitive receptors,
              Detailed construction methods for the permanent facilities including methods for topsoil stripping
               and stabilisation methods for stockpiles in situ for over 4 months,
              Definition and method statement for drainage requirements around all permanent structures
               including roads, road embankments and bridges to reduce rainfall runoff erosion and any
               potential stream erosion,
              Waste Management requirements and details on waste disposal, supply chain and disposal
               locations for different waste streams (as outlined in Volume 3, B3: Outline Non-hazardous and
               Hazardous Waste Management Plan)
              Management of contaminated materials, either on-site or offsite, as directed by the PMC
              Wastewater management and disposal requirements
              Soil surveys for new construction areas, including stockpile locations and stockpile protection
               measures
              Conceptual plans for site rehabilitation including regrading, replanting and any restoration works
               (Volume 3, B7: Outline Site Rehabilitation Plan).
12.4.19.    At any new greenfield land occupied by the Project, either at new construction areas or outside of
           the main construction site (i.e. for all roads, bridges, new transmission lines, etc.), a pre-construction
           survey (detailed in Volume 3, A11: Ancillary Works Plan) shall be completed by suitably qualified
           staff to identify soil types and areas of potential high sensitivity soil types. The pre-construction
           survey must be completed prior to submission of construction method statements, and any
           mitigation measures identified and incorporated into the ESMP for the works prior to
           commencement of any construction or clearance works.
12.4.20.   In advance of construction works the area to be prepared will be marked using wooden pegs and
           coloured tapes/ribbons. Areas for storage of topsoil storage and other special areas will be
           designated and marked out using tape/ribbons of different colours. The exact colour coding will be
           defined in the Construction Method statement to be developed prior to any clearance or construction
           work commencing.
12.4.21.   Existing areas of contamination identified during preconstruction surveys, recorded in Rogun JSC’s
           inventory of contaminated areas, or identified by Contractors shall be removed and disposed of as
           hazardous waste prior to general topsoil stripping and compaction. Excavated material will be
           sampled and analysed in a laboratory for potential contaminants. Where materials are identified as
           unsuitable for reuse these will be stored in designated waste areas for disposal. Suitable,
           uncontaminated material will be stored in designated areas, segregated from topsoil stockpiles, and
           used for backfilling and ground restoration.
12.4.22.   To prevent loss of the soil resource, typical construction methods for these types of works require
           stripping of topsoils which are stored in stockpiles over the short term, typically up to 4 months. The
           larger area of construction disturbance on the left bank will likely require stripping over a more




           Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
           Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                              November 2024
           Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 12-8
           extensive area. On completion of the construction activities, soils are typically reused to restore
           damaged areas.
12.4.23.   Topsoils and subsoils shall be stripped in separate layers and stored in separate stockpiles to
           prevent mixing.
12.4.24.   Once topsoil has been stripped, a vibratory roller will be used to compact the soils in defined areas
           as required. These areas will be defined in the site layouts and method statements. Other areas will
           be left free from compaction.
12.4.25.   Topsoil stockpiles will be designed to be stable, with slopes of not more than 2m high with 45-
           degree slopes. Stockpiles shall not be located within 10m of a waterbody, as far as practicable.
           Topsoil stockpiles must be designed and located so as not to impede general runoff and flow within
           drainage lines.
12.4.26.   Stockpiles being stored for longer durations (over 4 months) shall require a stockpile management
           plan to be developed, which shall detail the long-term stabilisation and management requirements,
           as well as detailed monitoring and remediation measures.
12.4.27.   Stockpiles being stored for longer durations (over 4 months) will be stabilised as appropriate (using
           technical or vegetative means) to prevent erosion and degradation of the resource.
12.4.28.   Topsoil stockpiles will be monitored to ensure that they remain viable and are not subject to
           excessive erosion pressures. If practicable during the storage period, the exposed surfaces will be
           temporarily vegetated
12.4.29.   Training to be provided to workers and supervisors on the importance of soil rehabilitation and
           correct procedures to safeguard soil stockpiles
12.4.30.   Land rehabilitation shall commence when directed by the PMC and as soon as practicable after the
           completion of construction in an area, including regrading as necessary, spreading of stored topsoil
           as appropriate, protection from erosion, seeding or planting of vegetation, protection from grazing
           (with fencing if necessary) until vegetation is self-sustaining. A Rehabilitation / Reinstatement Plan
           shall be developed to guide all rehabilitation activities.
12.4.31.   The effects of compaction on replaced soils can be ameliorated through soil loosening and allowing
           natural freeze thaw cycles to take place, especially in soils with high agricultural potential, with yields
           returning to normal in the short to medium term (1 to 3 years depending on the soil types).
12.4.32.   A final inspection shall be undertaken of rehabilitated areas to verify that requirements of the
           rehabilitation program have been met.
           Residual Effects
           Implementation of these measures will reduce the potential effects to Minor significance.
           Soil Loss: Impacts to soils from inundation
           Impact Assessment
12.4.33.   Within the inundation zone soils are heterogenous, comprising a range of soil types with varying
           potential to support agricultural crops. Inundation to form the reservoir will result in effective soil loss
           across approximately 170km2. Although not all soils will technically be lost, they will be removed
           from potential for arable production indefinitely.



           Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                     PUBLIC |
           Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                 November 2024
           Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page | 12-9
12.4.34.   Flooding of the inundation zone will also impact soils and subsoils, where wave action and runoff will
           result in erosion of soils in the drawdown zone (area between the summer high-water level and
           winter low-water level).
12.4.35.   Whilst volumes of high and medium sensitivity soils capable of supporting crops are not considered
           to be large in terms of total soil in the inundation zone, they have been used by local communities
           for subsistence food production. Many of those relying on the soils that are to be inundated are
           project affected people (PAP) from communities being resettled. Further detail regarding this impact
           is presented in Volume 1, Chapter 17: Socio-Economics of this ESIA.
12.4.36.   Specific mapping of soils at a local and habitat level has not been undertaken due to the
           heterogeneity and steepness of the terrain, and with the area being subject to minor landslides. As
           such a precautionary approach has been taken to assessing the potential impact of soil loss from
           inundation with a Medium magnitude applied as a result of across a regional scale.
12.4.37.   The significance of the effect is therefore considered to be Major for High Sensitivity soils and
           Moderate for Medium sensitivity soils.1 All other soils would be Minor.
           Mitigation and Enhancement Measures
           There is no mitigation for the loss of soils in the inundation zone. The stripping and translocation of
           High potential soils in the inundation zone for translocation to higher ground, or resettlement sites,
           would be both prohibitively costly and impractical.
           Residual Effects
12.4.38.   Since no mitigation of the impact is possible the residual effects remain unchanged. The significance
           of the loss of soil through inundation remains Major for High Sensitivity soils and Moderate for
           Medium sensitivity soils. All other soils would be Minor.
           Soil Loss: Impacts from vehicle movements
12.4.39.   Vehicles move daily to and from the site during construction for deliveries, mobile plant, and worker
           transport. This may cause significant erosion, especially in very dry periods and in locations where
           the soil is fragile. Sensitivity of soils is rated Low to Medium in light of the significant disturbance to
           soils in construction and other disturbed areas.
12.4.40.   Outside of the main construction site vehicle movements between local villages and Rogun are
           frequent. Main roads are generally paved and currently in good condition, other smaller roads are
           generally degraded and in need of repairs, or are compacted with no hardstanding.
12.4.41.   Further out from the main construction site, vehicles are less frequent and comprise local traffic
           mainly in cars. Construction work for the Left Bank Roads and Bridges, and resettlement introduces
           more frequent and larger vehicles sometimes along the more degraded roads.
12.4.42.   Vehicle movements have the potential to introduce erosion to soils and rocks in close proximity to
           the roads, especially where these roads are not well marked, or new roads are required.




           1 Sensitivity criteria defined in Table 12-2




           Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                    PUBLIC |
           Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                November 2024
           Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page | 12-10
           Impact Assessment
12.4.43.   Vehicle movements are generally constrained to designated access tracks to reduce the impact on
           soils. However, outside of the main construction site and Rogun City, especially with work on the
           Left Bank and also for resettlement, there is potential for vehicles to move around the site or beyond
           approved access routes.
12.4.44.   Access tracks and construction areas are damped down with water in the drier months to reduce the
           potential for soil loss through vehicle entrainment of dust.
12.4.45.   Given the short duration of the works in any given area outside of the main construction site, and the
           well-established network of access routes on the Rogun HPP site, the magnitude of the impact is
           considered to be Small, with significance from Negligible to Minor in more sensitive soil areas.
           Mitigation and Enhancement Measures
12.4.46.   To reduce impacts further, especially to highly sensitive receptors, it is recommended that the
           following additional measures are incorporated:
              Develop a Construction Method Statement for the construction of roads, that shall be reviewed
               and approved by the PMC and the HSE Manager prior to any commencement of road building.
               This will include as aspects listed in section 12.4.18.
   A Traffic Management Plan (Volume 3, A9) shall be implemented to manage construction traffic. This
    shall include, but not be limited to:
           •   Identification and demarcation of approved access routes.
           •   Segregation of incoming and outgoing traffic to prevent risk of collisions or overuse.
           •   Off-road driving will be prohibited except for in emergency situations.
           •   Regrading of access routes where these are required for longer durations.
           •   Where access routes are subject to wind erosion and monitoring of these routes shows
               damping with water is insufficient, consideration shall be given to alternative means such as
               chemical binding or other measures.
12.4.47.   Further detail regarding requirements for the Traffic Management Plan are provided in Volume 1,
           Chapter 22: Traffic and Transport of this ESIA.
           Residual effects
12.4.48.   With the implementation of these measures the residual effects are considered to be Negligible.
           Soil and aquifer loss: Impacts from excavations and quarrying, and abstractions
           Main Rogun HPP Construction Site.
12.4.49.   Excavations are required for the main construction site and potentially for construction materials in
           the form of borrow pits for the construction of roads, bridges, quarries and borrow pits.
12.4.50.   The extraction of materials from these sites can permanently impact the geology, hydrogeology,
           topography and soil condition of the area. Deeper excavations can change hydrogeological aquifers
           and routing of water through the aquifers, as well as require dewatering. Compaction of soils in the
           surrounding area from vehicle movements where this is not managed appropriately can render
           subsoils unable to support natural drainage properties.




           Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
           Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                            November 2024
           Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                            Page | 12-11
12.4.51.   Siting of the borrow pits shall be informed by site geology and avoidance of sensitive receptors (e.g.
           communities, surface water courses, critical habitat). Impacts to surface waters would be primarily
           through any run-off or discharge from dewatering. These impacts are covered under in Volume 1,
           Chapter 13: Water of this ESIA.
12.4.52.   Impacts to groundwater, and in particular underlying aquifers, increase where the depth of the
           borrow pit lies in close proximity to, level or below the top of the aquifer. As the exact depth of the
           borrow pits is not yet defined, a precautionary approach to the assessment assumes excavations to
           this level. Given the potential for shallow aquifers to be present which could be used either for
           irrigation or an informal source of potable water in some villages the direct impacts on aquifers from
           this type of construction activity needs to be carefully considered.
12.4.53.   Where excavations breach the aquifer or are at a depth to allow free movement of water upwards
           and downwards into the aquifer, dewatering may be required, which will reduce aquifer capacity for
           the duration of the excavation and use.
           Impact Assessment
12.4.54.   In the main construction area at Rogun HPP all borrow and quarry areas are clearly defined and
           additional facilities are not expected to be required. The main quarries and borrow pits operating
           downstream of the dam are clearly demarcated and lie on the left bank of the Vakhsh river.
           Underlying geology suggests that aquifers of significant quality and quantity are more likely to be
           associated with the Right Bank. Those on the left bank are superficial and not able to be used for
           potable water supply. Given the Low sensitivity of the area, and that extensive excavation has
           already taken place over many years, the magnitude of impact is assessed as being Small. The
           significance of the effect for these areas of the main construction site is considered to be Minor.
12.4.55.   New areas of excavation may be required to support the development of the other supporting
           infrastructure works, and in particular the Left Bank Roads and Bridges which are required to
           replace those being lost through inundation. Areas of excavation are expected to be contained
           entirely within the construction areas and are assumed to be relatively small areas in terms of the
           amount of surface area disturbed.
12.4.56.   Where borrow pits are required, these are likely to be small in number spaced out along the route of
           the roads.
12.4.57.   Abstractions in other areas are likely to be of short duration for production of concrete materials etc.,
           and of limited volumes.
12.4.58.   The soils in new areas of disturbance are given a Medium sensitivity. The magnitude of this impact
           is assessed as Medium, leading to a Moderate significant effect for areas where soils and geology
           are of the highest sensitivity.
           Mitigation and Enhancement Measures
12.4.59.   To reduce impacts further, especially to highly sensitive receptors, it is recommended that the
           following mitigation measures are incorporated:
              Remediation measures shall be implemented immediately following construction, with guidance
               being taken from the Outline Site Rehabilitation Plan (Volume 3, B7).
              Where borrow pits are required an Excavation Spoil and Fill Storage Management Plan (Volume
               3, B1) will be developed and implemented which details the Project approach to site selection,



           Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
           Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                              November 2024
           Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                              Page | 12-12
    usage and rehabilitation. Though no international GIIP guideline for borrow pits is available,
    several recent projects2 provide best practice guidance for the siting, operation and restoration of
    borrow pits. Mitigation measures may include (but are not limited to):
    • For Site definition:
         o    Minimum setback of 100m from environmentally sensitive areas and residences;
         o    Excavation and construction areas must be delineated with tape or other clear markers or
              barriers;
         o    Inclusion of adequate space for all activities;
         o    Estimated quantity and type of resources to be extracted;
         o    Defined Sequence of Operation;
         o    Location and definition of stockpile locations;
         o    Location and procedures for waste management;
         o    Type, source and quantity of water required for operation;
         o    Site operating procedures; and
         o    Consultation requirements with the adjacent communities and stakeholders.
    •    For Construction:
         o    All excavations are to be designed to reduce footprint and depth as far as practicable.
              Excavations shall not go below the depth of the water table where possible;
         o    Retain as much vegetation as possible to maintain slope stability;
         o    Topsoil to be stripped, retained and preserved for restoration;
         o    Excavated material shall be stripped in layers and stored separately to avoid mixing;
         o    Construct ditches to divert water runoff away from site;
         o    Define the stockpile management practices;
         o    Optimise of internal-traffic routing to maximise distances to the closest sensitive
              receptors; and
         o    Design separate footpaths to segregate pedestrians and vehicles.
    •    For Operation:
         o    Limit the sediment from runoff by using sand traps or sediment ponds;
         o    Limit pit depth above the water table; and
         o    Limit the formation of ponds and accumulation of standing water to avoid vector larvae.
    •    For Closure:
         o    Use the overburden for area recovery and revegetation;
         o    Use non-conforming material for backfilling;
         o    Re-grade perimeter berms and stockpiles;
         o    Reuse topsoils on top of the overburden for restoration purposes;
         o    Restore natural drainage;




2 High Speed Rail (West Midlands - Crewe) Environmental Statement Volume 5: Technical appendices Borrow pits restoration strategy

                  o   Remove all waste materials, temporary structures and equipment;
                  o   Re-establish vegetation for next land use and to minimise erosion; and
                  o   Restore the gradient of the land to prevent fall-from-height risk.


              In other areas outside of the Main Rogun HPP Construction site, where borrow pit excavations
               may breach the aquifer depth, or water for abstraction may be required from groundwater
               sources, community engagement during the Pre-construction Survey Process shall identify
               whether there is the potential for formal or informal use to be present. If boreholes are present,
               the location, depth, use and estimated volumes of water from private boreholes and hand dug
               wells shall be calculated, and a method for monitoring and establishing any project impacts on
               the availability of water for communities will be developed. Adaptive management measures will
               be adopted in the event of Project impacts.
              Where impacts are predicted, liaise and work with communities to identify potential mitigation for
               loss of water resources and the timeframes to achieve this. This must be integrated into the
               Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP), and implemented before the impacts occur. Where there
               are potential impacts, frequency of engagement shall be determined using a risk-based
               approach to enable the early identification of issues and solutions.
           Residual effects
12.4.60.   The implementation of these measures will reduce impact magnitude to Very Small. The residual
           significance of the effect will therefore be Minor.
           Contamination: Impacts to soils from demolition works
12.4.61.   The main impacts to soils from demolition works are related to contamination. On the main
           construction site buildings and historical infrastructure dating back to the 1980s are either
           abandoned or where still in use, degraded.
12.4.62.   A preliminary assessment of contaminated lands was completed in 2023 to identify the main
           sources of contamination on the HPP construction site. These are primarily associated with:
              Tank Farms (both disused and those in current use);
              Workshops;
              Abandoned/disused sewage facilities (septic tanks); and
              Old buildings and facilities such as concrete batching facilities, especially those containing
               Asbestos.
12.4.63.   In 2025, JSC Rogun will conduct a more detailed site survey and develop a remedial plan, to be
           implemented prior to inundation by EPC or other Contractors.
12.4.64.   Outside of the main construction site at Rogun HPP, risk from demolition is associated with the
           removal of structures at existing villages which need to be resettled. Buildings in these villages have
           been constructed using locally available and/or repurposed materials which may contain asbestos.
           Contaminated land in these areas may also be present from older vehicles and other sources of
           pollution. The specific type and quantity will vary locally.
12.4.65.   Removal and demolition of this infrastructure has the potential, especially in the case of asbestos, to
           pollute soils over a long duration, with a risk for some types of pollution (e.g. oils and chemicals)
           infiltrating local aquifers, or where inundation occurs locally polluting the reservoir.



           Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
           Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                             November 2024
           Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                             Page | 12-14
           Impact Assessment
           Main Rogun HPP Construction Site.
12.4.66.   The Rogun HPP main construction site contains a legacy of buildings and infrastructure constructed
           in the 1980s. Legislation in the soviet era allowed (and still allows) for the use of asbestos containing
           materials in buildings. These buildings have been used and/or repurposed over many years, with the
           potential to damage the integrity of any Asbestos Containing Materials (ACMs), including roofing,
           walls, pipes, and water tanks amongst others. Historic and disused tanks and concrete batching
           plants also have the potential to contain asbestos materials.
12.4.67.   In addition, removal of infrastructure including fuel tanks, septic tanks, batching plants, and old
           workshops, has the potential to introduce pollutants such as VOCs, oils and grease, toxic and heavy
           metals, as well as general detritus and old sludges (fuels, sewage etc) into the surrounding soils and
           infiltrate into any local groundwater.
12.4.68.   Whilst much of the area where these buildings and infrastructure are located is due to be flooded,
           any pollutants will remain in the soils and retain capability of polluting the water column, with the risk
           being higher immediately after flooding and diminishing in time through attenuation of the pollutants.
12.4.69.   Although the sensitivity of the soils and geology in this area is Low, it is considered that the scale of
           the construction site as well as the potential for legacy impacts in any soils to last beyond the
           lifetime of the reservoir constitute a Large magnitude.
12.4.70.   For the main construction area, the significance of the effect is therefore considered to be Major as
           a result of hazardous substances in buildings potentially being released during demolition works.
           Other Areas (inundation zone)
12.4.71.   In the inundation zone, the main concern from demolition relates to the villages being resettled.
           Currently, PAPs living in the inundation zone are allowed to salvage materials from their existing
           houses to support the construction of their new homes in new resettlement areas.
12.4.72.   Once asbestos containing materials, coatings, pipework etc are removed, there is a risk of fibres
           contaminating soils locally. The re-use of these materials risks damaging them even further
           potentially giving rise to long term effects in the new locations they are taken to. Removal of other
           infrastructure where present (e.g. garage structures, domestic gas/oil storage structures etc) may
           also cause leaks and spills to occur into the local soils.
12.4.73.   Demolition works in the inundation zone associated with resettlement is the responsibility of the DFZ
           through their contractors. These are typically local contractors with varying degrees of awareness of
           such issues, especially in relation to asbestos.
12.4.74.   The resettlement programme, and therefore demolition works, is occurring over a regional scale and
           over many years, although the actual effects on soils may be very local and spread over large areas.
           The magnitude of the impact is therefore considered to be Medium.
12.4.75.   Soils in these areas are heterogenous some of which are high quality agricultural soils, although it is
           noted that in the inundation zone these will be lost due to flooding. Similarly in new resettled areas
           are likely to be of Medium to High sensitivity as the resettlement programme tries to move affected
           people to areas where agricultural activity can be established. Moving older infrastructure and/or
           damaged asbestos materials to these locations may therefore cause pollution to the new soils.



           Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
           Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                               November 2024
           Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 12-15
12.4.76.   Taking a precautionary approach the significance of the effect is considered to be Major for the high
           sensitivity soils reducing to Moderate for medium sensitivity soils.
           Mitigation and Enhancement Measures
12.4.77.   To reduce impacts further, especially to highly sensitive receptors, the following additional measures
           are to be incorporated. For the main Rogun HPP construction site, the remedial plan to be
           developed by the PMC (in support of PMG) is to be implemented by the contractors undertaking the
           works. The plan shall include (but is not limited to):
              Location of buildings to be demolished;
              Location of existing services (gas, electricity, water etc);
              Ground conditions and locations of hazardous materials;
              Drawings/plans of the buildings including site access and egress, traffic and pedestrian routes
               etc.;
              Roles, responsibilities and training required and identification of special job requirements (e.g.
               qualifications for asbestos surveys or other specialist jobs);
              Demolition method statements including the sequence of works and full details on methods
               being used to undertake the works including:
               •   Mobilisation & site set-up.
               •   Traffic management
               •   Termination, disconnection and capping off of live services.
               •   Vehicle movements.
               •   Asbestos removal
               •   Soft strip (removal of fixtures, furniture etc and salvageable materials using hand held tools).
               •   Hard or hand demolition followed by vehicular plant attendance and operation.
               •   Hot cutting operations (cutting, burning or welding operations).
               •   General Housekeeping and maintenance.
               •   Completion and de-mobilisation.
              Consideration of HSE requirements relevant to demolition activities, including dust and air quality
               monitoring requirements and PPE. These will be aligned with the overarching Occupational
               Health and Safety Plan for the Project;
              Training required;
12.4.78.   For demolition in the inundation zone associated with resettlement, the DFZ (or its Contractors shall
           develop a remedial plan approved by the PMC or develop this plan jointly with the PMC.
12.4.79.   Rogun JSC shall arrange for sitewide asbestos surveys across the whole of the main construction
           site, using appropriately qualified personnel / contractors to identify structures where asbestos is
           and/or is potentially present. If suitably qualified asbestos removal specialists cannot be found within
           Tajikistan, international contractors should be used. The method for asbestos analysis should
           conform to the ISO 22262-2:2014 standard, or equivalent;
12.4.80.   Once the survey has been completed an Asbestos Register and Asbestos Management Plan shall
           be developed by the party undertaking the survey, as part of the Demolition Plan. The Asbestos
           Management Plan will form part of the overarching Hazardous Materials Management Plan (HMMP)
           (as outlined in Volume 3, B5). The asbestos register shall include (but is not limited to):
               •   All known and presumed ACMs in all buildings;


           Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
           Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                             November 2024
           Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                             Page | 12-16
               •   The type of ACMs present;
               •   How much asbestos there is and its condition, including dates of the original where possible
                   and last inspection dates;
               •   Risk assessment of the potential for each ACM to release fibres and likelihood of disturbance
                   during the day-to-day running of the building. The risk assessment should cover as a
                   minimum:
                   o   the information gathered on the location, amount and condition of the ACMs
                   o   whether there is easy access to the ACMs
                   o   whether people work near an ACM in a way that is liable to disturb it
                   o   the numbers of people who use the area where the ACMs are
                   o   if demolition, maintenance, refurbishment or other work on the premises is likely to be
                       carried out where the ACMs are.
              where asbestos is presumed to be located note should be made where the surveyor has been
               unable to access areas (these locations should be kept to a minimum)
              photographs to confirm location and for routine condition monitoring
              specific actions that need to be taken and dates these are required to be completed, and by
               which party
              evidence of actions being completed
              due date for the next condition assessment.
              Training requirements for recognition of Asbestos will be provided to all staff, including DFZ and
               their contractors, by the party undertaking the asbestos survey. Where specialist surveys are
               required for resettlement, DFZ will liaise with Rogun JSC and PMC to identify and use personnel
               with requisite qualifications, to manage asbestos risk during the resettlement process.
              No ACMs or potential ACMs shall be reused. Where ACMs or potential ACMs are found, this
               hazardous waste shall be disposed of in line with the recommendations of the asbestos survey
               and Asbestos Management Plan. The disposal requirements of asbestos in the Asbestos
               Management Plan will be aligned with the Hazardous Materials Management Plan and
               overarching Non-hazardous and Hazardous Waste Management Plan.
           Residual effects
12.4.81.   The implementation of these measures will reduce the significance of the effect to Minor.
           Contamination: Impacts to soils from historic pollution
12.4.82.   Activities which disturb the soil have the potential to mobilise contamination that is already present
           within the ground. Contamination may be in the form of hazardous chemicals or microbes. Whilst a
           walkover survey of the current construction area has been undertaken using theconceptual site
           model (Volume 2, Annex 13-1: Contaminated Land), it is possible that further contamination may
           be present in the construction areas that has not yet been identified.
12.4.83.   In the inundation zone and for other works outside of the main Rogun HPP construction area (e.g.
           left bank roads and bridges etc., historic contamination may be present that has not yet been
           identified. Sources of potential historic contaminated materials include:
              Areas around construction equipment including tank farms, concrete batching areas, materials
               storage, waste facilities, septic tanks etc.
              Areas around roads and extensively used dirt tracks


           Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
           Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                            November 2024
           Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                            Page | 12-17
              Abandoned and modern agricultural fields and livestock areas (especially where land application
               of manure occurs)
              Cemeteries and burial grounds
              Waste dumps and livestock burial pits (formal and informal).
           Impact Assessment
           Main Rogun HPP Construction Site.
12.4.84.   Within the main Rogun HPP construction site historic pollution has been identified across
           widespread areas most notably associated with abandoned infrastructure such as the tanks farms,
           septic tanks and disused waste dump areas and infrastructure locations (see Volume 2: Annex 12.1
           of this ESIA).
12.4.85.   Construction activities can mobilise pollutants from local soils into wider areas, or waterbodies if not
           managed appropriately. This is especially the case where historic contamination is located
           downstream of the dam which indirectly could impact downstream sections of the Vakhsh river
           between Rogun and Nurek HPP.
12.4.86.   Whilst the sensitivity of the soils and geology in this area is Low to Medium, historic contamination
           is widespread across the site, with pollution types likely to persist in the long term or permanently
           without some intervention. The majority of affected areas will also be inundated by 2038, which
           would potentially allow leaching of contaminants.
12.4.87.   The magnitude is therefore considered to be Large, with significance Moderate for the main Rogun
           HPP construction area.
           Other Areas
12.4.88.   In other areas where construction may occur (e.g. Left Bank Roads and Bridges, new transmission
           lines, new resettlement areas etc)., sources of pollution are typically from local spills from vehicles,
           local septic tanks and other types of domestic pollution. Pollution around local roads which typically
           have not been graded or in poor repair can also cause localised pollution from vehicles.
12.4.89.   There is also potential for informal livestock burial sites in and around local villages. Whilst not
           official, or technically prohibited, these types of sites are not uncommon, and there is potential for
           these to cause microbial contamination in a localised area.
12.4.90.   Construction in these areas has the potential to mobilise pollutants more widely in soils of Medium
           to High sensitivity or allow infiltration into shallow aquifers.
12.4.91.   Given that design details for some of these works is in development, or some areas have yet to be
           defined a precautionary approach to the assessment has been made. The magnitude is considered
           to be Small due to the localised nature of pollution types and extent. The significance is therefore
           Minor for medium sensitivity soils rising to Moderate for the most sensitive soil types.
           Mitigation and Enhancement Measures
12.4.92.   New tank farms will be designed and constructed in accordance with GIIP standards
12.4.93.   A Hazardous Materials Management Plan (HMMP) shall be developed and implemented which
           includes measures related to the management of historic pollution. To reduce impacts further in




           Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
           Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                              November 2024
           Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                              Page | 12-18
           areas outside of the main Rogun HPP construction site, especially to highly sensitive receptors, the
           following measures will be incorporated:
              Sources of historic contamination will be identified and mapped as part of the Pre-construction
               Survey Process. This will include soil testing to identify chemical and biological contamination,
               and stakeholder engagement to identify any sources of contamination that are not officially
               registered, e.g., informal waste dumps or burial pits.
              Contaminated areas and known livestock burial sites shall be avoided where practicable through
               microrouting of the roads and bridges, any new transmission lines and or micrositing of
               construction areas and new resettlement areas.
              Where avoidance is not possible, materials shall be excavated and disposed of at registered
               hazardous material facilities. If livestock burial sites are identified and need to be excavated, this
               shall only be undertaken in consultation with the Veterinary Control Services of Tajikistan and
               with strict adherence to their requirements. Remediation shall be completed before construction
               activities can commence.
              For all contaminated soil and other contamination existing as a result of historical activities, the
               remedial plan will require this source of contamination to be isolated from reservoir water with an
               impermeable cap or by removal off-site to a facility licensed to treat or dispose the specific type of
               hazardous substances that caused the contamination. If no such facilities exist, JSC Rogun will
               take steps to store the contaminated media/materials until such a facility can be developed. Such
               storage will be designed to prevent releases to the environment and approved by the PMC.
           Residual effects
12.4.94.   With the implementation of these measures the residual effects are considered to be Minor for the
           main construction site and Negligible for the other areas.
           Contamination: Impacts to soils and geology from leaks and spills
12.4.95.   Construction activities have the potential to introduce contaminants to soils through a variety of
           sources, which can pollute soils once construction is complete, as well as leach into groundwater
           aquifers.
12.4.96.   The main sources of leak and spill risk during construction will be:
              Vehicles: mobile plant, delivery vehicles, and worker / site visitor transport.
              Vehicle maintenance facilities
                 Stationary plant and generators.
              Refuelling of vehicles and stationary plant
              Storage, use and transportation of fuel and lubricant.
              Storage, use and transportation of paints.
              Storage, use and transportation of cleaning chemicals.
              Storage, use and transportation of other chemicals (e.g., chemical grouting)
              Aggregate materials storage areas and stockpiles.
              Quarrying and material extraction.
              Concrete batching areas including material storage.
              Sanitary facilities (including septic tanks, temporary toilets and washing facilities).
              Hazardous waste storage: chemical containers (e.g., oil drums, paint and varnish tins).
              Contaminated materials (e.g., used spill kits, used medical supplies, contaminated soil).


           Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                    PUBLIC |
           Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                November 2024
           Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page | 12-19
             Impact Assessment
             Main Rogun HPP Construction Site.
 12.4.97.    Fuel and chemical storage is required both on-site within the construction area, and at workers’
             camps. Hazardous waste is also stored on site throughout construction for disposal at registered
             facilities. In some areas, the hazardous materials and waste storage areas are well designed and
             monitored, while other areas are less robust in application of GIIP. Vehicle maintenance facilities in
             some areas are also very well designed and managed with others managed less stringently.
             Existing management measures to prevent leaks and spills on-site include provision of spill kits etc.,
             were observed for areas where known potential pollutants are present.
 12.4.98.    Water flowing through material stockpiles and concrete batching areas, as well as from vehicle
             washout areas were observed in some areas to be discharging directly into soils across open
             ground (Volume 2, Annex 12-1: Contaminated Land) of this ESIA, as well as through open ditches
             directly into the downstream Vakhsh river. Dumping of spoil and poor housekeeping on site were
             also observed in some areas.
 12.4.99.    Although the soils in this area are generally Low in sensitivity, the likelihood of contaminants
             continuing to be discharged to soils and polluting new areas as construction progresses, is certain
             and on a frequent basis, given the continuance of these practises, at least in the short to medium
             term. The magnitude of the impact is therefore considered to be Large, and significance of the effect
             is considered to be Moderate.
 12.4.100. It is also important to consider this type of pollution in-combination effects from historic pollution and
             demolition works which also give rise to significant contamination effects in soils. Implementation of
             the mitigation to reduce these effects across all types of potential contamination is considered to be
             imperative in the immediate term.
             Other areas (e.g. Left Bank Roads and Bridges etc)
12.4.101. Given the speed of construction along the new roads and at bridge locations, with construction in
            place for only a short period (up to 6 months) in any single location, it is likely that some spills will
            occur from materials and fuels in particular. The scale however will be local and of short duration.
12.4.102. Assuming a precautionary approach with a large spill (e.g. > 1000 L) with the potential to contaminate
            agricultural topsoils of high value receptors, the magnitude of the effect is considered to be Medium.
            The significance of the effects is likely to be Moderate for Medium sensitivity receptors and Major for
            High sensitivity.
             Mitigation and Enhancement Measures.
              To reduce impacts further, especially to highly sensitive receptors, it is recommended that
               construction method statements be developed that include all aspects and mitigation measures
               listed in section 12.4.18.
              Furthermore, a Hazardous Materials Management Plan (HMMP) shall be compiled that:
                   • Describes the process for purchasing including requirements and audit of purchase
                       inventories,
                   • Identifies substitutions to less hazardous materials where practicable,
                   • Sets out the requirements for Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for the management
                       of secondary containment structures, including integrity inspections and the removal of any
                       accumulated fluid, such as rainfall, to ensure that the containment capacity is maintained.


             Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                     PUBLIC |
             Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                 November 2024
             Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 12-20
                •  Sets out the method for ensuring access to hazardous materials storage areas shall be
                   restricted.
                • Sets out the design requirements for secondary containment for fuel storage tanks and for
                   the temporary storage of other fluids such as lubricating oils and hydraulic fluids which is at
                   least 110% of the largest container or 25% of the total volumes of fuel and fluids stored
                   (whichever is greater).
                • Sets out the procedures for ensuring refuelling and other fluid transfer only in areas with
                   impervious surfaces, or where this is not possible (e.g. temporary construction camps)
                   mandate the use of drip trays to avoid spills during refuelling.
                • Sets out the training and awareness for transfer and handling of fuels and chemicals and
                   the response to spills for workers involved in the handling of fuels and chemicals.
                • Details any specific personal protective equipment (PPE) required to respond to an
                   emergency and training on its use.
                • Details the siting for spill kits, portable spill containment and clean up equipment where
                   fuel and chemical handling and takes place.
                • Identifies any hazardous substances present in or on building materials (e.g., PCBs,
                   asbestos containing flooring or insulation, electrical components containing mercury). If
                   present, ensure contaminated building materials are decontaminated or properly managed
                   and disposed of in line with the waste management plan.
                • Identify training requirements for personnel to remove potentially hazardous materials in
                   building elements. Such materials should only be removed by specially trained personnel,
                   and according to protocols detailed in the Hazardous Materials Management Plan
                   (HMMP).
             The HMMP will cover all hazardous materials expected to be encountered including all
              hazardous wastes.
          Residual Effects
12.4.103. Implementation of these mitigation measures at all sites will reduce the potential effects to Minor
         significance.
          OPERATION PHASE
12.4.104. Impacts from ongoing operational activities at Rogun have the potential to result in leaks and spills
         resulting in the contamination of soils.
12.4.105. In addition, the Rogun Reservoir could potentially influence dam stability through impacts on
         geological features in close proximity to the dam and reservoir (i.e. the salt wedge and geological fault
         lines). These potential impacts are safety risks that have been addressed in the geotechnical and
         engineering design reports for the project, and are mentioned only briefly in this chapter for
         completeness.
          Contamination: Impacts to soils from leaks and spills
12.4.106. Operation activities have the potential to introduce contaminants to soils through a variety of sources,
         which can pollute soils once construction is compete as well as leach into groundwater aquifers.
12.4.107. The main sources of leak and spill risk during the operational phase will be:

             Vehicles: mobile plant, delivery vehicles, and worker / site visitor transport.
             Vehicle maintenance facilities


          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
          Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                             November 2024
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                             Page | 12-21
             Stationary plant and generators.
             Storage, transportation and use of fuel and lubricants
             Storage, transportation and use of paints.
             Storage, transportation and use of cleaning chemicals.
             Storage, transportation and use of any other chemicals used during maintenance.
             Surface and below ground transformers and switch gear containing oils.
             Sanitary facilities (including septic tanks, temporary toilets and washing facilities).
             Hazardous waste storage: chemical containers (e.g., oil drums, paint and varnish tins).
             Contaminated materials (e.g., used spill kits, used medical supplies, contaminated soil).
12.4.108. Fuel and chemical storage is required on-site within the operational HPP.

12.4.109. Although the soils in this area are generally Low in sensitivity, the likelihood of contaminants
         continuing to be discharged to soils and pollute potentially new areas during the operational phase, is
         Likely and on a Periodic basis. The magnitude of the impact is therefore considered to be Small, and
         significance of the effect is considered to be Negligible.
12.4.110. The operation of the Hydropower plant will require use of fuels and other potentially contaminative
         substances which may be toxic and mobile within the environment. There is a potential for release of
         contaminants to ground which would have an impact on soils and an indirect impact on human health
         and the local environment.
12.4.111. Volumes of chemicals and materials are likely to be low, and maintenance activities carried out in
         specialists’ facilities designed to GIIP which are retained following construction. Soils in this area are
         considered to be of Low sensitivity. Taking the precautionary approach the magnitude of the effect is
         considered to be Small with a significance of Negligible..
          Mitigation and enhancement measures
 12.4.112. To control potential contamination impacts in the Operational Phase, regular environmental audits
          shall be undertaken, and where systems are deficient in their adherence to GIIP requirements the
          applicable Operational Plans and Procedures must be reviewed, revised, and reissued to prevent
          recurrence of pollution incidents. All Plans and Procedures will undergo regular revision throughout
          the life of the project.
          Potential impacts on geology and dam stability
12.4.113. A wedge-shaped geological unit comprising halite rock is a component of the Ionakhsh fault (the salt
         wedge) and is situated beneath the Rogun dam wall. There is a risk that as the Rogun reservoir fills
         the hydrostatic head of the less saline reservoir water will result in dissolution of the halite rock
         causing cavity formation and subsidence that could affect the integrity of the dam.
12.4.114. The Rogun dam and reservoir are located over or near several geological faults (Ionaksh, Gulizindan,
         Guissar and Vakhsh faults) that that could potentially increase the risk of substrate movement during
         seismic events
12.4.115. Both aspects present potential dam safety risks that have been addressed in engineering design for
         the Rogun HPP. Failure of the dam would be catastrophic.




          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
          Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                              November 2024
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                              Page | 12-22
        Mitigation and enhancement measures
12.4.116. The risk associated with the salt wedge has been minimised by implementing hydraulic and grouting
        barriers, whose effectiveness is constantly monitored.
12.4.117. Risk associated with the local fault lines and seismicity has been addressed in the dam engineering
        designs that employ an earth and rock construction approach rather than rigid concrete.
