1              Introduction

1.1       Purpose of the Document
Rogun HPP is being constructed at the upstream end of a cascade of Hydropower Plants (HPP) located on the
Vakhsh River in Tajikistan. The reservoirs regulate the seasonal flows in the Vakhsh River to supply domestic
and irrigation water and to generate electricity for international and domestic consumption. The addition of the
Rogun Reservoir, with a volume of 13.3 km³, to the cascade will increase the energy output with the installation
of a 3,780 MW HPP.
The purpose of this Document is, after a short description of the situation, to
      •    address the issue of potential impacts of Rogun HPP on all environmental aspects related to water, and
      •    to develop and discuss mitigation measures that may be required or could be applied in order to reduce
           impacts to an acceptable level.
The present document is based on the following reports and studies:
      •    Coyne et Belier, ELC and IPA, 2014: TEAS (Techno-Economic Assessment Study), Phase II: Project
           Definition Options, Volume 3: Engineering and design, Chapter 5: Reservoir operation study; referred to
           as TEAS 2014.
      •    Pöyry, 2014: Rogun HPP: Environmental and Social Impact Assessment; referred to as 2014 ESIA.
      •    WSP, 2025: Rogun Hydropower Project –Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment;
           referred to as 2025 ESIA; from this, mainly the following Sections:
               o   Volume2, Annex 13-1: Minimum Environmental Flow
               o   Volume2, Annex 13-3: Water Resource Modelling
               o   Volume 2, Annex 7-1: Baseline Conditions Report
The present document is prepared as part of the 2025 ESIA.


1.2       Importance of Water

1.2.1      Potential Impacts of HPPs
An HPP will normally have influence on surface water bodies in the following ways:
      •    Change of water discharge patterns downstream of the dam; here, three possible impacts have to be
           considered, namely:
               o   Change in seasonal flow distribution; large HPPs with a high dam and a large storage reservoir
                   are usually built with the aim to store water during the high flow season (in this case during
                   summer), for using it during the low flow season (here winter) for generating electricity during
                   the period when natural water discharge is lowest and electricity demand is highest. With very
                   large reservoirs in comparison with natural river discharge, this can lead to a complete change
                   in the seasonal flow patten in the downstream area.
               o   Short-term flow changes; this happens when an HPP is operated with a peaking pattern, with
                   the aim of closely following a changing demand, e.g. for making electricity available during daily
                   peak demand hours. Hydropower plants, equipped with turbines that can be turned on and off
                   very quickly, have the flexibility for such short-term changes. For the river downstream of the
                   water outlet of the power plant this will lead to potentially very marked and very rapid changes
                   in water discharge, varying in an extreme case between no water and full capacity of the
                   turbines, potentially several times per day.
               o   In the case of a deviation HPP, where the water taken from the dam will be conducted via
                   channels or tunnels and a high penstock to be turbined and released at a distance downstream
                   of the dam, the river between dam and outlet may remain without water; this stretch of river can
                   be several km long, and it is even possible to divert the water in a way as to release it in a
                   different river (transbasin diversion).
                                                                                                              PUBLIC
Page 2




    •    Creation of a reservoir; in addition to the possibility for shifting water seasonally mentioned above, this
         will have the following effects:
             o    Changing a portion of the river from its natural running water (river, lotic conditions) into a lake-
                  like (stagnant water, lentic condition) water body.
             o    Implications on water quality; the importance of this impact will depend, among other
                  parameters, on the quantity of vegetation that will be submerged during impoundment of the
                  reservoir.
    •    Interruption of the river:
             o    Biological impact: a dam will be a barrier for movements of water organisms, and mainly for fish
                  migrations; in low, weir-type structures this can be mitigated by means of fish ladders or similar
                  structures that will allow fish to pass, bun this is not possible in the case of high dams.
             o    A large reservoir will retain sediments, influencing in this way sedimentation and erosion
                  dynamics in the downstream area.
    •    Potential changes in the local water balance (gains from direct precipitation, and losses from evaporation
         and infiltration).
These are the (theoretical) main changes directly concerning water that will become manifest during the entire
operation period of the HPP. In addition to that, impacts caused and risks present specifically during the
construction period have to be mentioned. These are mainly the risks of water contamination during construction,
as e.g.:
    •    Increased sediment input due to work in the river itself and in its immediate surroundings, which includes
         baring of soils, in this was increasing their exposure to erosion.
    •    Risk of water contamination with concrete waste (increase of water pH), of contamination with fuels and
         lubricants or other hazardous materials due to accidents or inadequate storage and handling.
    •    Risk of water contamination with domestic waste water (untreated waste water from workers’
         accommodations and other facilities.
These risks must be prevented and minimised by developing and implementing the ESMP (including all specific
management plans) for the project; see ESIA Vol. 3. Similar precautions (e.g. for minimising the risk of water
contamination) have also to be applied in the operation phase, especially during major maintenance or
rehabilitation works.

1.2.2    Situation in the Case of Rogun HPP
From the potential impacts mentioned above, the following are relevant in the specific case of Rogun:
    •    Interruption of the river: Vakhsh River was already interrupted, and any fish migrations that may have
         taken place there before were completely blocked by the construction of Nurek dam and the smaller
         dams and weirs of the cascade downstream of it. Rogun dam will be an additional obstacle to fish
         migrations with may take place between Nurek reservoir and the upstream part of Vakhsh River; see
         ESIA Vol. 1, Chapter 15, and discussion of the issue in the CIA,
    •    Creation of a reservoir: the reservoir with a total length of 70 km will change conditions from lotic to
         lentic, which will affect the type of habitat for aquatic organisms. Effects of this on aspects related to
         biodiversity are discussed in ESIA Vol. 1, Chapter 15.
    •    Water quality: the creation of a large reservoir, with a surface of 159 km² and a volume of 13.3 km³, can
         have impacts on water quality, which in turn can affect the situation in the downstream area; see Section
         4.2.
    •    Losses due to evaporation from the reservoir: the TEAS estimated evaporation from an open water
         surface in the project area at 805 mm/year, which would lead to a loss, once the reservoir is full, of 0.144
         km³ per year or 4.6 m³/s. However, this dis not take into account that without a reservoir there is a loss
         due to evaporation from soils and evapotranspiration from plants, which was estimated as being in the
         order of magnitude of 700 to 1000 mm/year, the same order of magnitude (2014 ESIA). This, therefore,
         is not considered as a relevant loss and is not evaluated any further.
    •    Peaking operation: this is a possibility, not yet defined, but likely to be applied at least temporarily. The
         issue is addressed in Section 4.1.4.
                                                                                                                PUBLIC
Page 3




      •    Change in seasonal flow distribution: as every HPP with a high dam and a large storage volume, Rogun
           has the potential to retain water in the high-flow season to release it during the low-flow season (winter),
           with the aim of optimising energy generation. This highly relevant topic is addressed in Section 4.1as
           well as in the CIA.
These potential effects and their implications will have to be addressed.
The most relevant of these potential impacts, in the case of Rogun HPP, is the last one mentioned in the list
above, namely, the potential for changing the seasonal flow pattern in the downstream area by shifting water
from summer to winter. The importance of this is given by the fact that in the Vakhsh-Amu Dary basin, there are
two main, and directly competing, forms of water use, namely, hydropower and irrigation. This is explained
shortly here:
      •    Hydropower uses water for producing electricity. Since river flow is highly seasonal, with high flows in
           summer and very low flows in winter, and since electricity demand is highest in winter, the aim of HPPs
           would be to retain the possible maximum of water in summer in order to use it in winter for generating
           electricity.
      •    Irrigation needs water during the vegetation period, which under the climatic conditions in the basin is
           summer (roughly from April to September), while very little if any water is used for this purpose in winter
           (October to March).
In contrast to irrigation, hydropower is a non-consumptive form of water use: once passed through the turbines,
it is available again for any use in the downstream area. However, shifting a large amount of water from summer
to winter will cause potentially severe restrictions for irrigation in the downstream area, where a higher flow in
winter is not of any practical use. This is a situation of conflicting interests in the Amu Darya basin which is of
high economic relevance and, given the fact that several countries share the river basin, has political implications
at an international level.
For this reason, the potential effect of Rogun HPP on seasonal flow distribution is the main focal point of the
present document. Cumulative and transboundary impacts are dealt with in a separate document, the CIA
(Cumulative Impact Assessment).


1.3       Regulatory Framework
The 2025 ESIA is prepared with taking into account the relevant national legislation and standards as well as
applicable international standards and guidelines, namely those of the World Bank. These applicable standards
are presented in full in Volume 1, Chapter 2 of the 2025 ESIA. This section summarises the regulatory
framework that is relevant to Water.

1.3.1      Tajikistan National Legislation
Water Code of 2000 (as amended in 2020) - The Law governs the use of water and sustainable use of water
resources. The Law is based on principles regarding:
      •    Consideration of the importance of water bodies as the basis of human life and activity;
      •    Basin management of water resources;
      •    Stakeholder participation in the preparation and making of decisions about water resources
           management;
      •    Protection of water bodies;
      •    Avoidance of excessive negative impact on waterbodies;
      •    Regulation of water relations depending on the natural characteristics of waterbodies; and
      •    Accounting of the economic value of water resources.

1.3.2      International Agreements
Water management among Central Asian States is guided by agreements dating in part back to Soviet times,
and additional agreements between the newly independent states reached since then. These instruments
include:

                                                                                                                PUBLIC
Page 4




    •    Protocol 566 of September 10, 1987
    •    October 12, 1991 Declaration
    •    February 18, 1992 Agreement "On Cooperation in the Field of Joint Water Resources Management and
         Conservation of Interstate Sources
    •    By-law of the Basin Water Association "Amu Darya" (6 April 1992, Ashkhabad)
    •    By-law of the Interstate Commission for Water Coordination of Central Asia (ICWC) (5 December 1992,
         Tashkent)
    •    Nukus Declaration of September 20, 1995.
A more complete list of         agreements     can   be   accessed    on   ICWC's    website   (http://www.icwc-
aral.uz/legal_framework.htm).
These agreements form the basis for the current practice of water allocation among the Central Asian states
(Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Uzbekistan and Turkmenistan for the Amu Darya basin). The following institutions are
in charge of water allocation:
    •    Two Basin Water Organisations (BWO, Basseynoe Vodnoe Obedinenie) were created still during Soviet
         times, one for the Amu Darya, and one for the Syr Darya).
    •    The 1992 agreement led to the foundation of the Interstate Commission for Water Coordination (ICWC).
ICWC is the institution in charge of water allocation; the two BWOs are the operative branches of ICWC.
The most recent of these agreements is the Joint Statement of the Heads of Water Agencies of the Republic of
Kazakhstan, the Republic of Tajikistan, Turkmenistan and the Republic of Uzbekistan (27 April 2022, Turkestan),
available on http://www.icwc-aral.uz/statute15.htm, cited here ((emphasis in the web document):
               “We, the Heads of water agencies of Kazakhstan, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan, and Uzbekistan, while
               recognizing the importance of regional water security challenges, express our willingness to take
               further coordinated actions in order to ensure multipurpose and sound use and protection of
               transboundary water resources.
               We believe it important to continue cooperation in the spirit of collective leadership, solidarity
               and collective responsibility as a foundation, on which the regional water cooperation was
               developed and the Interstate Commission for Water Coordination in Central Asia was created.
               We acknowledge our solid commitment to transboundary water management on the basis of
               consensus, to the benefit of all the riparian states and in line with international agreements
               to which the region’s countries are signatories.
               We will continue implementing integrated water resources management at all levels as a key
               tool of green development, adaptation, and water-food-energy-environment nexus.
               We will endeavor, within our mandates, to take feasible measures, with the focus on sound
               water use and water conservation, to build trust and enhance cooperation, transparency and
               sustainability among the Central Asian countries.
               We will make efforts to ensure that qualified personnel, reliable data, advanced knowledge,
               digitalization and innovative development of the water sector become the key factors of
               medium- and long-term economic growth and sustainable development in the region.
               We will encourage development and sharing of knowledge, forecasts and innovations in order
               to improve water use management and planning.
               Having experienced environmental and socio-economic consequences of unsound water use, we
               recognize the need for sustainable use and a reasonable balance between the needs of
               people, economies and ecosystems today and in the future.
               In this context, we will strengthen coordination of actions for the improvement of water-related
               ecosystems, including glaciers, flow formation zones, river deltas and the exposed Aral Sea bed
               and for climate mitigation.
               We attach particular importance to more active work with youth and to encouraging the
               population to value and take care of water.”


                                                                                                          PUBLIC
Page 5




It is important to highlight that while the set of regional agreements may have some shortcoming, they represent
a well-established framework for managing regional water resources. Through the formalization of the institutions
(BWOs, and ICWC, mainly) and the mechanisms by which the countries come together to discuss and agree on
national water sharing allocations, this has proven to be a very robust framework which has been in place for
more than 30 years, and a good system to resolve the water sharing issues among the five countries.
Practical aspects of water regulation in the Amu Darya basin with relevance for Rogun HPP are discussed in
Section 3.1.4.

1.3.3    International Lender Requirements
World Bank Environmental and Social Standards
    •    ESS1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts;
    •    ESS3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution prevention and Management;
    •    ESS4: Community Health and Safety; and
    •    ESS6: Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources.
Hydropower project development guidance documents:
    •    The World Bank Group Good Practice Handbook for Environmental Flows for Hydropower Projects
         (2018); and
    •    European Investment Bank (EIB) Environmental, Climate and Social Guidelines on Hydropower
         Development (2019).




                                                                                                          PUBLIC
Page 6




2              Approach

2.1       Area of Influence
The area of influence can be defined on different levels as follows:
      •    Amu Darya Basin: Vakhsh River is one of the main tributaries of the Amu Darya, and a main source of
           water for the Amu Darya basin. The Vakhsh River's average annual flow is 20 km3, which is about 32%
           of the Amu Darya River's flow, estimated at 61.5 km3 per year (as referenced in Protocol 566,
           Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA), Volume I, Page 23). The Amu Darya river starts
           at the confluence of the rivers Vakhsh and Panj (average of 32.7 km3/year) at the border between
           Tajikistan and Afghanistan, and soon after receives the waters of the Kafirnigan (average of 5 km3/year).
           It will be important to place Rogun and the Vakhsh river in this broader context for its relative importance
           in the basin, in comparison to the much larger Pyanj river and other tributaries. For this reason, a first
           and general level is the Amu Darya basin as whole. Potential downstream and transboundary impacts
           are addressed in the Cumulative Impact Assessment for Rogun HPP.
      •    Vakhsh Basin: since Rogun dam is built on the Vakhsh River, Vakhsh basin has to be taken into
           consideration, starting from Rogun reservoir and including the hydropower cascade downstream of it.
           Rogun HPP does not influence water (flow, seasonal distribution, quality) in the area upstream of Rogun
           reservoir. The risk of backwater effects is not mentioned in the reservoir modelling studies, but it is very
           low, since peak inflows into the reservoir occur mainly in spring and early summer (snow melt and peak
           precipitation, see Figure 3-4), when the reservoir is low and easily able to cope with such inflows. This
           area therefore is not discussed here.
      •    Rogun and Nurek Reservoirs: this is the major part of the area of influence for this assessment, since
           effects of Rogun, first of all, will be felt here. This part includes the stretch of Vakhsh River between the
           outlet of Rogun power house and the Nurek reservoir.
The 2014 studies (TEAS and ESIA) demonstrated that the Vakhsh cascade with Rogun HPP added can be
operated without changing the water regime, and especially without changing seasonal water distribution,
downstream of Nurek HPP. Aligned with this, Tajikistan has agreed to not shift any additional flows from summer
to winter, than the 4.2 km³/year already being shifted by Nurek since its construction, and to not alter the flow
regime downstream. For this reason, the AoI for the updated ESIA was defined as covering Rogun reservoir
area and a buffer zone, reaching as far down as Nurek dam, this AoI is applied here. As mentioned above, for
the purpose of the Cumulative Impact Assessment, which is also a Transboundary Impact Assessment, the AoI
had to be extended as to include the entire Amu Darya basin (see in the CIA).


2.2       Receptors

2.2.1      Main Parameters Affected
The following are the main parameters affected, which are the focal points of this Report:
      •    Hydrology: risk of changes in downstream water flow patterns, and mainly on seasonal distribution of
           water. Highly sensitive issue, since an additional shift (in addition to what was already caused by Nurek
           HPP) from summer to winter, in the interest of maximising winter energy generation, would have very
           negative consequences for water users in the downstream area, mainly concerning irrigation in
           Uzbekistan and Turkmenistan.
      •    Water quality: creation of a reservoir, changing the river characteristics accordingly, can have an impact
           on water quality, which in turn could cause problems for water users downstream of the dam, and could
           also affect aquatic habitats.
      •    Sediment transport: retention of sediments in the reservoir leads to changes in sediment-related
           dynamics downstream of the dam.
      •    Flood risk: floods can be a major risk for the human population along the river, and for man-made
           structures in potentially affected areas.
Changes in these parameters will have an impact on the final receptors. An overview is provided in the following
Section.


                                                                                                                 PUBLIC
Page 7

3             Baseline Situation

3.1      Amu Darya Basin

3.1.1     The Basin
Rogun HPP includes a high dam that is under construction on the Vakhsh River; this is a major tributary of the
Amu Darya, one of the two large Central Asian rivers finally ending in the Aral Sea. Given Rogun’s potential for
change in the downstream area, the Amu Darya basin has to be considered.
The Amu Darya is the largest river in Central Asia by discharge. Its length is 2'415 km and the size of the
catchment area is 534,700 km².




Figure 3-1:     Amu Darya river basin


Starting at the confluence of Vakhsh (20 km3/year) with Pyanj (32.7 km3/year), the river is called Amu Darya;
Pyanj flows along the border between Tajikistan and Afghanistan. Downstream from this confluence, the Amu
Darya is joined by several additional tributaries, the main ones being the Kunduz (from Afghanistan), the
Kafirnigan (from Tajikistan), the Sherabad and the Surkhandarya (from Uzbekistan).
The Amu Darya basin is shared by Afghanistan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan and
terminates in the Aral Sea. Most of the Amu Darya river flow is formed in the upper part of the catchment area,
i.e. in the mountains of Afghanistan and Tajikistan, as shown in the following Table.




                                                                                                         PUBLIC
Page 10




Table 3-1:       Contribution to Amu Darya annual flow by country

  Country                             Annual flow generated (km³)                     %
  Tajikistan                                      62.90                             80.17
  Afghanistan                                      6.18                              7.88
  Uzbekistan                                       4.70                              5.99
  Kyrgyzstan                                       1.90                              2.42
  Turkmenistan                                     2.78                              3.58
  Total                                            78.46                                100
                 Source: Main guidelines of water strategy of the Aral Sea basin 1996 (ICWC)


It should be pointed out that the contribution of Vakhsh River to the part of Tajikistan is 20 km³ (in an average
year). A large part stems from Pyanj (which includes the major part of water originating in Afghanistan, and there
is also the Kafirnigan River, which joins Amu Darya downstream of the confluence of Vakhsh and Pyanj.
Downstream of the Kafirnigan-Amu Darya confluence, there are no more major tributaries. On the way to the
Aral Sea the river forms the boundary between Afghanistan and Tajikistan as well as between Afghanistan and
Uzbekistan, but also crosses the boundaries between Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan.

3.1.2     Amu Darya
The origin of the Amu Darya (which has this name only from the point of the confluence of its main tributary, the
Pyanj, with Vakhsh, 1445 km upstream of the Aral Sea) is in the Pamir, in Afghanistan, close to the Chinese
border, at an altitude of 4900 m asl. The total length of Pyanj-Amu Darya is 2,415 km. The total catchment area
is 534'700 km². Most of its water stems from snow and glacier melt. Two major tributaries come from Afghanistan,
the Kochka (upstream from Pyanj-Vakhsh confluence) and the Kunduz (between Vakhsh and Kafirnigan),
bringing about 7 to 8% of the total flow. Further west, there are no other major tributaries. The Amu Darya has
a highly seasonal flow pattern, as shown in the following Figure.




                                                                                                              PUBLIC
Page 11




                               4000

                               3500

                               3000




                   m³/s м³/с
                               2500

                               2000

                                      J/Я   F/Ф M/М A/А M/М J/И   J/И   A/А   S/С   O/О   N/Н   D/Д


Figure 3-2:      Monthly discharge of Amu Darya at Kerki (1959, 50 year average)
                 Source: 2014 ESIA, based on data from Létolle and Mainguet (1993: 58)
                 Kerki is located in Turkmenistan, about 250 km downstream from the Tajik-Uzbek border.


The water of Amu Darya has high concentrations of calcium carbonate, and its content in organic matter is very
low, due to the low content of humus in the catchment area. Salt content has increased markedly due to drainage
water from irrigated areas and waste water inflow from settlements.

3.1.3     The Aral Sea
The Aral Sea is a large lake without outflow, inflows are balanced by evaporation. Around 1960, it had a surface
of 66’458 km² and was then the fourth largest lake in the world in terms of surface area. Average depth was 16
m, maximum depth was 68 m in the western and 29 m in the central and eastern part; these two parts were
separated by islands which today form an almost complete separation between the two parts.
In historical and prehistorical times, the lake level has known rather important fluctuations, the amount of which
is still a matter of debate. However, for a time span of at least two centuries before 1961 it has been very stable,
with a variation in the order of magnitude of 1 to 2 m.
The Aral Sea has only two tributaries of importance, the Syr Darya in the North and the Amu Darya in the South,
both originating in the high mountain areas to the east of the lake, in Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan and Afghanistan.
Records from the 20th century show that the overall runoff of the Amu Darya at the foothills of the mountains (i.e.
in the region of Termiz, a short stretch downstream of the Tajik-Uzbek border) was 78.6 km³/yr, with high year
to year fluctuations, however without showing a downward trend. Out of this, originally (i.e. before about 1960)
around 60 km³/yr reached the Aral Sea. The fact that in spite of the high annual variation of inflow the lake level
remained rather stable might have been due to the mitigating effect of the groundwater reserves in the soils
along the river plains and the lake.
After the end of World War II, and especially towards 1960, large irrigation projects were implemented in the Aral
basin, along the Amu as well as the Syr Darya. This increased the irrigated area and agricultural productivity
mainly in Uzbekistan and Turkmenistan (for the Amu Darya), but it also had the immediate effect of massively
increasing water abstraction from Amu Darya, thus reducing inflow to the Aral Sea, leading to a situation where
the losses from evaporation were no longer compensated and the Aral Sea started shrinking, and salinity of the
water increased. Around 1990, northern and southern parts of the Aral Sea became separated. The northern
part, the Small Aral Sea, which receives the inflows from Syr Darya, was then isolated from the southern part
(Large Aral Sea) by the construction of the North Aral Dam between the two parts. This lead to a recovery of the
Small Aral Sea; salinity there has dropped from around 30 g/l to pre-shrinking values of 10 g/l or less. The
southern part, however, continues to shrink and salinity is rising accordingly. Today, there is water only in the
former westernmost (deepest) part of the original lake, and, depending on inflow, some water intermittently in
the central part, covering less than 10% of the original surface.

3.1.4     Water Use in the Basin
Up until 1987, the allocation of the Amu Darya waters among the four Central Asian republics (Kyrgyzstan,
Tajikistan, Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan) was based on the water development master plan for the Amu Darya
                                                                                                             PUBLIC
Page 12




basin. In 1987, two important steps were passed: first, based on an analysis of water shortages in 1974–1975,
and especially in 1982, two Basin Water Organizations: BWO “Amu Darya” with headquarters in Urgench, and
BWO “Syr Darya” in Tashkent were created. Secondly, a water allocation plan named "Protocol No. 566" of the
Science and Technological Council of the USSR Water Management Ministry was prepared.
Protocol No. 566, dated September 10, 1987, is still used today as a reference document for discussions on
water allocation between the four formerly soviet countries of the Amu Darya basin: Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan,
Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan.


Table 3-3:      Origin and use of water in the Amu Darya basin

                  Country         Contribution to total flow of     Water allocation according
                                          Amu Darya                      to Protocol 566

                                               %                         %              km³**
                  Kirgizstan                  2.42                      0.6              0.4
                  Tajikistan                  80.15                    15.4              9.5
                  Turkmenistan                3.58                     35.8             22.0
                  Uzbekistan                  5.97                     48.2             29.6
                  Total 1                     92.12                     100             61.5
                  Afghanistan*                7.88                                       2.1
                  Total 2                       100                                         63.6
                * Afghanistan is not an ICWC member. The quantity of water used by Afghanistan included in the Table
                is based on empirical values.
                ** The values in km³ per year reflect the situation in an average year. The actual amount to be used by
                each country is determined by a semi-annual forecast of expected water quantities based mainly on
                assessments of the snow cover in the catchment area.
                Source: Mr. Daler Abdurazoqzoda, Head of Energy and Water Policy Department (pers. comm. 2024-03-
                05).
These volumes of water withdrawals from the Amu Darya River were set against the level of full water
exhaustion, “proceeding from the existing water use practices, irrigated land in use, and based on estimated unit
water use” (Protocol 566). The Master Plans give the following justification of that water distribution criterion:
“According to the USSR water law, for the distribution of limited water resources the priority sectors are: drinking
and municipal water supply and industry, as well as fishery that show high water demand in terms of quantity
and quality and guarantee. In addition, in arid zone, the most demanding water user is the irrigated agriculture.
As a rule, the area of irrigated land is the main guiding factor in setting the volume of water withdrawal.”
After the end of the Soviet Union, when the Central Asian former soviet republics became independent states,
several interstate agreements were reached between them regarding water allocation and use. The International
Water Sharing Agreements of 1992 (Almaty Agreements), formalized the establishment of the International
Commission on Water Coordination (ICWC), the Basin Water Organizations (BWOs) Amudarya and Syrdarya,
and established the main mechanisms through which the countries come together every year to discuss and
agree on water sharing allocations. In a meeting held in Nukus, Uzbekistan, on September 20, 1995, the states
signed the so-called Nukus Declaration, which defined the policy to be taken for the Aral Sea. The Nukus
Declaration does not specifically address water allocation to and distribution among the riparian countries, and
it does not refer to Protocol 566 or any other agreement on water resources. It also does not mention the use of
water resources for hydropower. However, it confirms that previously signed agreements on water resources
would continue to be respected.
This situation was analysed in the 2014 ESIA. The 2014 ESPOE, in its final report, emphasised the concerns
about this situation as follows: “The legal analysis contained in the ESIA (chapter 8) shows that these water
sharing instruments are agreements, declarations or practices rather than treaties, that they lack provisions for
monitoring and enforcement; and provide no dispute resolution mechanisms. The E&S PoE notes that the legal
and technical basis for the BWO and ICWC water quotas to countries lack clarity by not being explicit and that
no documented account of the process and rules applied by these bodies for water allocation between states
has been provided. Although generally appearing acceptable by the countries involved, current annual water
allocation practice thus remains vague and unenforceable and is not amenable to objective resolution in cases
of dispute. It is noted that the current practice does not include Afghanistan that in the future may become a
                                                                                                                PUBLIC
Page 13




more important stakeholder. The Rogun project planning is thus proceeding without transparent verifiable agreed
rules for allocating water between competing seasonal and annual demands.”
While these concerns may be justified from a legal point of view, the practice observed during the last more than
30 years shows that the water management framework arising from Protocol 566 and evolving through the
Almaty Agreements (92), Nukus declaration (95) and through the established practices of yearly water allocation
agreements at the ICWC with the BWOs, is a robust system that works. In addition, since 2010, BWO Amu
Darya makes data on water allocation to and actual water use by the member countries publicly available
(http://sic.icwc-aral.uz/reports_e.htm ) for each half-yearly period (growing season, April 1 to September 30, and
non-growing season, October 1 to March 31). These data are analysed in the Cumulative Impact Assessment
as part of the updated ESIA. The data show that the BWO member countries respect the water allocation, and
that during the period on record Tajikistan on average used 1.3 km³ less water than its allocated share. The
conclusion from that is that in spite of the mentioned shortcomings the system obviously works, and has proven
to be a very robust one.
Currently, the countries meet at least four times per year in the context of the ICWC to define the agreed water
allocations for the vegetation and non-vegetation seasons and the distribution of flows across the season based
mainly on the demand profiles for each country prepared by the BWOs and the forecasted water availability for
the season. Article 8 of the Almaty Agreement gives mandate to ICWC to adjust the water withdrawal limits and
reservoir operation regimes depending on actual water availability and the current water-related conditions. Once
the water withdrawal limits have been agreed upon (with adjustments if needed), the BWOs have the mandate
of implementing the water allocations by operating the diversions for the River’s main stem, and to do the water
accounting. Subsequent meetings during the year are used to make adjustments in the withdrawal limits to
correct for deviations between the forecasts (expectations) and the actual flows.
The following Figure summarizes the water availability and water use over time in the Amu Darya basin, showing
total actual annual flow and total cumulated use.




Figure 3-3:     Total yearly water flow and use in Amu Darya basin
                Data source; http://sic.icwc-aral.uz/reports_e.htm


The Figure also shows something else which is of high relevance in the context with Rogun HPP: while there
seems to be some correlation between balance (i.e. water not used) and inflow to the Aral Sea, these two
parameters are not directly correlated. It cannot be assumed that the total volume of unused water in the basin
would automatically reach the Aral see. This is clearly shown by the data for the last few years, where there was
a slight increase of unused water and at the same time a decrease of water reaching the Aral Sea. This also
means that it cannot be assumed that any change in water released from Rogun would result in a same effect
on inflow to the Aral Sea.
One main concern, for which there is no clear solution yet, is the fact that Afghanistan is still not included in the
water sharing agreements. Presently, it has a project under way, the Qosh Tepa canal, which will have the
                                                                                                              PUBLIC
Page 14




capacity to divert 5 (potentially up to 15 km³ of water annually from Amu Darya, which would change the basin’s
water allocation in a rather fundamental way. Phases 1 and 2 of the project, comprising the construction of the
canal are almost finalized, and Phase 3 the most complex one is to start soon comprising the land reclamation,
administration and the development and organization of the irrigation schemes. See the CIA for a discussion of
the topic which also mentions the need to include Afghanistan.


3.2   Vakhsh Basin
Vakhsh basin is a sub-basin of the Amu Darya basin (see Figure 3-1).
One of the Vakhsh River's sources, its tributary Kizil-Su, lies in a very remote area of southern Kyrgyzstan near
the Chinese border, where it runs westwards for 262 km; it then flows through Tajikistan for a length of 524 km
before joining the Pyanj River to form the Amu Darya at the border of Tajikistan and Afghanistan.
The river gets the name “Vakhsh” at the confluence of Surkhob and Obihingou rivers; the former of these two is
formed by the confluence of Muksu and Kizil-Su rivers; Kizil-Su, which is one of the major spring rivers of Vakhsh,
originates in Kyrgyzstan. Muksu river, which originates from Fedchenko glacier - the largest glacier of Central
Asia - is the main source river of Vakhsh. Vakhsh runoff is approximately 20 km³/yr, to which Muksu contributes
3.53 km³ and Kizil-Su 1.65 km³.
For most of its route, Vakhsh rushes through high mountains, before slowing down in the plains of southern
Tajikistan. The river, which is fed mostly by melting snow and glaciers, achieves maximum flow during the
summer months of July and August. The river's watershed area is 39’100 km², of which 31’200 km² lie within
Tajikistan.
In its upper parts, Vakhsh is a mountain stream with a rather marked gradient, flowing mostly through a rather
narrow valley with steep slopes. On a considerable part of this mountain section it flows in a gorge-like valley
deeply cut into lateral alluvions. Only on a few sections the valley is wider and almost flat, and here the river has
formed extensive floodplains. Two of these, one near Komsomolobod, the other near Novobod, are located
within the area of the future reservoir. Likewise, the confluence of Surkhob and Obihingou will also be submerged
by the reservoir.
Downstream of Nurek, Vakhsh river flows in a south-westerly direction through a gradually widening plain. In its
lowest part it flows in wide meanders in a wide plain; this is the protected wetland area called Tigrovaya Balka.
It then reaches the border with Afghanistan, where it meets Pyanj river. These two then form Amu Darya.
Downstream of the Pyanj-Vakhsh confluence, only one more river of some importance joins the Amu Darya
within the territory of Tajikistan, the Kafirnigan.
Vakhsh river has a seasonal flow pattern, with a discharge maximum in July and a minimum in February, as can
be seen from the following Figure. River flow is mainly influenced by snow melt, since a major part of the annual
precipitation falls during the winter months, in higher areas as snow.




                                                                                                              PUBLIC
Page 15




Figure 3-4:     Monthly average precipitation and average flow of Vakhsh River
                (2025 ESIA Vol. 2, Annex 7-1, Baseline Conditions).


Figure 3-4 presents the average monthly flow pattern at Rogun dam (from a combined flow dataset from Pöyry
(2014) and the World Bank (2023)) and the average monthly precipitation measured at Mehrobod (then known
as Komsomolobod or Komsomolabad) (data obtained from Pöyry (2014)). The graph illustrates that the flows
are not correlated with precipitation but are correlated with seasonal temperature change.




A monthly flow record covering the period January 1932 to December 2022 for the Rogun Dam is available. The
flows are driven by snow melt starting in April with the flows increasing during the summer months. The monthly
flow pattern and the range of monthly flows are shown in the box and whisker plot in Figure 3-5.




Figure 3-5:     Box and Whisker plot of monthly streamflow record at Rogun HPP
                Source: 2025 ESIA, Vol. 2, Annex 13-3, Water Resource Modelling
The average annual flow is 20 km³, with year-to-year variations between a maximum of 27.3 km³ and a minimum
of 14.2 km³ (Figure 3-6).




                                                                                                        PUBLIC

3.3   Vakhsh Cascade
Nurek HPP was commissioned in 1972. It is the largest HPP, and the second largest regulation reservoir in Amu
Darya river basin (after Tyuyamuyun reservoir in Uzbekistan). Until now, it provided around 80% of the electricity
available in Tajikistan. So far, Nurek is the only reservoir in upper Amu Darya basin (i.e. in Kafirnigan, Vakhsh
and Pyanj basins) with an interannual regulation capacity. The other HPPs in the Vakhsh cascade have a
maximum of a weekly (Baipaza) or daily (for peak production) regulation capacity (see Table 3-4).
Figure 3-7 shows the schematic layout of Vakhsh cascade as it was used for modelling Rogun and Nurek
reservoir operation in TEAS (2014) and in the updated ESIA (Vol. 2, Annex 13-3, Water Resource Modelling).
Shurob HPP, which still is a project at the conceptual stage, was not included in the model. In any case, since it
will be a ROR structure, it will not influence seasonal water distribution in any way. Irrigation 1, indicated between
Nurek and Shurob, stands for the irrigation water which is derived from Nurek reservoir.




                                                                                                               PUBLIC
Page 17




Figure 3-7:       Vakhsh cascade
                  Source: TEAS 2014: Phase II - Vol. 3 – Chap.5 – Reservoir operation study.
                  Rogun HPP is under construction, while Shurob is in an early planning stage.


As can be seen from this schematic layout, water is taken from Nurek reservoir and from the downstream part
of Vakhsh River for irrigation within Tajikistan.

The construction of Nurek from 1961 to 1980, the creation of the 10.5 km 3 reservoir and the commissioning of
the hydroelectric station from 1972 to 1979 have resulted in a modification of the Vakhsh River flow pattern to
the following extent:
    •     The filling of the reservoir from 1972 to 1980 resulted in a reduction of downstream flows equal to the
          volume of water stored in the reservoir of 10.5 km3, which over the nine years filling duration, on average,
          meant a flow reduction of 1.16 km³ per year, corresponding to 6.8 % of the Vakhsh River flow or 1.5 %
          of the entire Amu Darya flow.
                                                                                                               PUBLIC
Page 18




            •          The increase of evaporation due to the creation of the reservoir is estimated by HPI (2009) to be
                       0.052 km3/yr, i.e. 0.3% of the Vakhsh River flow.
            •          Modification of the seasonal distribution of Vakhsh river discharge is a result of the reservoir operation.
                       As can be seen from the following Figure, Nurek had a regulating effect on Vakhsh discharge

The main effect of Nurek HPP was the permanent shift of 4.2 km³ of water from summer to winter. The increased
winter flow is used for generating electricity in winter, when demand is highest and natural flow in rivers is lowest.

                                                                                                                           PUBLIC
Page 19




This shift is maintained, and BWO water allocation is based on this changed pattern of Vakhsh discharge. Overall
seasonal flow in the Amu Darya was changed accordingly by this shift.


3.4   Main Characteristics of Rogun HPP
The construction of Rogun Dam started in the 1970s and has been through a number of stop/start cycles with
the project implementation resuming in 2008. As of May 2025, the dam has reached an elevation of 1100 m asl,
and water is impounded to an elevation of 1 075 m asl, and electricity is being generated through the two
preliminary turbines already installed in the power house. Discharges from the reservoir are currently made
through the turbines and bypass tunnels. A further 4 turbines and an upgrade of the two preliminary ones is
planned as the dam embankment will raise and the construction of the associated infrastructure will be
completed. The concurrent installation of the turbines while constructing the dam will allow for the generation of
electricity as the dam is constructed. The construction of the dam to its crest elevation of 1300 m asl is planned
for completion by November 2033.

Dam type: rockfill with central impervious core.

    •     reservoir level 1,075 m asl

    •     2 units @ 200 MW each under operation.
Some additional relevant information on Rogun HPP:
    •     Rogun dam is the second large dam constructed on Vakhsh River, about 70 km upstream of the existing
          Nurek dam.
    •     Its main purpose is the production of electricity, a large part of which shall be exported, mainly to
          Pakistan and Afghanistan, via the 500 kV transmission lines built under the CASA 1000 project.
    •     Filling of the reservoir (13.3 km²) will be done under strict observation of the water allocation rules, which
          means that on average annually not more than 1.2 km² of water will be retained, which corresponds to
          the quantity allocated to Tajikistan under Protocol 566 but so far not used by Tajikistan. The plan is to
          gradually fill the reservoir over the period from 2019 to approximately 2037; see detailed discussion of
          this topic in Section 4.1.3.
    •     The expected life span of Rogun HPP is 115-130 years; the main limiting factor is sedimentation of the
          reservoir.
The 2014 TEAS and ESIA concluded that the Vakhsh cascade, with Rogun HPP in place, can be operated in a
way as not to change the present river flow pattern in the downstream area, and especially not to change the
seasonal distribution of flows. Tajikistan intends to operate the cascade in this way. This means that building
and operating Rogun HPP will not reduce water availability for irrigation by downstream riparians, considering
that Tajikistan intends to use its full water share in any case. This is discussed in detail in Section 4.




                                                                                                                 PUBLIC
Page 21




4              Impact Assessment and Mitigation

4.1       Seasonal Water Distribution

4.1.1      Main Aim: No Change in the Downstream Area
Tajikistan committed itself to respect the two conditions outlined at the end of the previous Section, and by doing
so continue to adhere at all times with the regional water allocation agreements by not using more water than
allocated to it during the filling phase, and by not shifting additional water from summer to winter. This would
mean that there will be no change in seasonal water availability in the downstream area in comparison to the
situation without Rogun, with the only exception that from now on Tajikistan will use the full share of the water
allocated to it. This, however, is something that Tajikistan plans to do in any case, first for filling Rogun reservoir
and then for increasing its irrigation.
In the following Sections, these conditions are analysed in some more detail.

4.1.2      Modelling of Rogun
For the 2014 TEAS, a modelling of Rogun reservoir operation was carried out (Coyne et Bellier et al., 2013:
Reservoir Operation Simulation Studies). This was rechecked and updated as part of the 2025 ESIA (2025 ESIA,
Volume2, Annex 13-3: Water Resource Modelling). The following Sections are based on these two modelling
studies.

4.1.3      Filling of Rogun Reservoir
The guiding principle for the impoundment of Rogun reservoir is that the Government of Tajikistan will impound
the flows that are otherwise not used from its annual water share allocation, as per the water allocations to
countries regionally agreed each year at ICWC meetings. Tajikistan will respect at all times the regional water
sharing agreements during the impoundment of Rogun reservoir, and will not go beyond its allocated water
share.
As outlined in Section 3.1.4, water allocation among the riparian states Kirgizstan, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan and
Uzbekistan is agreed by member states in the context of the ICWC meetings and with the assistance of the
BWO. Based on the forecast on expected water quantity in the following vegetation period derived from
observations of snow cover in the upper reaches of the Amu Darya basin, each country is allocated a certain
amount of water. Figures on this allocation as well as on actual consumption are provided in a BWO home page
(http://sic.icwc-aral.uz/reports_e.htm ). The following Table shows the present use of this water in Tajikistan for
the period 2010 to 2023, the period for which data are available on the internet site mentioned above. The Table
shows the following parameters:
      •    allocated km³: this is the total yearly volume defined by BWO, based on the two forecasts (one growing
           season, summer, and one non-growing season, winter), which Tajikistan is allowed to use;
      •    used km³: shows the water volume that was actually used by the country during this year;
      •    used %: indicates the percentage of BWO allocated water which was used;
      •    balance km³: the volume of BWO allocated water which was not used (- xx) or which was used in excess
           of the allocated volume (+ xx); the Table shows that this latter never occurred during the 15 years on
           record.

Figure 4-1:      Yearly water use by Tajikistan


The same data are shown graphically in Figure 4-1. The graph seems to indicate a trend towards using an
increased share of allocated water, especially in the years 2020 to 2023; according to information received from
the Energy and Water Policy Department (oral communication), this was due to a number of dryer than average
years and the fact that additional water was used for eliminating salts that had been accumulating in irrigated
areas. The latest year on record, 2024 (which includes the first quarter of 2025) shows a portion of unused water
close to the long-range average.
As Table 4-1 shows, during the 15 years on record on average 1.36 km³ of the water allocated to Tajikistan was
not used. In the reservoir simulation study, TEAS (2014) had determined an average volume of 1.211 km³ of
yearly unused water. In the following discussion this value (1.211 km³ per year, corresponding, as a yearly
average, to 38 m³/s) is used, because this is a more conservative assumption than the 1.36 km³, and the TEAS
value is based on a longer time series and therefore less likely to be affected by short-terms fluctuations. This is
the amount of water that is, in an average year, available for filling Rogun reservoir. This corresponds to about
6% of the mean annual Vakhsh runoff (inflow to Rogun reservoir, see Figure 3-6) of 20 km3/year. It is understood
                                                                                                                    PUBLIC
Page 23




that the actual difference will depend on what is agreed in the ICWC meetings of each year, being the difference
between volumes allocated annually and expected use in that year (the point being that the volume of water
available for filling Rogun will vary from year to year, with less water available in dry years and potentially more
in wet years). It is expected that in ICWC negotiations each year, Tajikistan will likely get a greater allocation
during above-average years (water abundant years) and the unused water share will exceed the historical
average; and conversely, during drought years, the unused allocation may be smaller than the historical average.
For this reason, the impoundment of Rogun reservoir will depend mainly on the volume of inflows from the
Vakhsh River during impoundment years. Given the need to respect the regional water sharing agreements at
all times, an exact, fixed impoundment plan is difficult to produce.
It is important to highlight two points here: First, as Tajikistan has not used its full allocation, the ICWC-agreed
allocation has been constant in time, without any increases during wet years. This is subject to change in future
yearly ICWC meeting negotiations given the need to impound Rogun. The basis for making this assumption is
two-fold: (i) Protocol 566 stated: “If water availability in the Amudarya River is above the estimated one, excess
water, i.e. above the established quantities of water withdrawal, must be primarily accumulated by reservoirs”.
This would obviously benefit Tajikistan in the impoundment of Rogun; and (ii) Article 8 of the Almaty Agreement
gives mandate to ICWC to adjust the water withdrawal limits and reservoir operation regimes depending on
actual water availability and the current water-related conditions. This gives room for negotiations if the countries
agree at ICWC. Tajikistan never had an incentive to negotiate for more water during wet years as it never used
its full allocation, but now the incentive is clear and backed by Protocol 566.
Second, it is well known that a significant part of the flows diverted into canals in Tajikistan return back to the
river, and the actual use of the water share is overestimated. Two simple examples are as follows: (i) in winter,
the irrigation canals downstream of the cascade (such as the Vakhsh Main Canal) are used for several HPPs
existing along the canals but there is no consumptive use and the totality of the water diverted returns to the
river; (ii) the conveyance and irrigation efficiency in Tajikistan is low, with numerous return flows to the river. The
Ministry of Energy and Water Resources of Tajikistan is making steps to install hydroposts to measure these
unaccounted return flows to the river, which are significant.
Thus, the above two points indicate that Rogun could potentially be impounded at a slightly faster rate than
anticipated, with the accounting of return flows to the river, and if a few wet years were to occur, specially after
year 2030 (see Table 4-2) when the reservoir construction increases storage capacity at a much faster rate than
1.2 km3/year.
Presently (May 2025) the dam has reached an elevation of 1100 m asl, the water level is at 1075 m asl and
reservoir volume is about 0.5 km³. This volume, which was retained during the last 3 to 4 years, is much less
than the annual available volume, but its retention has possibly contributed to the higher than average water
consumption of Tajikistan illustrated in Figure 4-1. Still, it is not possible, based on the data on water use, to
actually see when how much water was retained. This is also shown by the fact that the water level in the
reservoir was raised by 5 m (from 1070 to 1075 m asl) in August-September 2024, while in that year the amount
of unused water by Tajikistan rose by 1 km³ in comparison with the year before (Table 4-1). At the present still
low water level in the reservoir and the comparatively small surface of only 7 km² the increase in volume by a
rise by 5 m will be in the order of magnitude of 0.035 Km³.
Reservoir capacity, i.e. the volume available for storage, increases as the construction of the dam progresses
(see details in ESIA Vol. 2, Annex 13-3, Water Resource Modelling). However, due to the restriction in water
retention to 1.211 km³ per year, the increase in actual water volume retained is slower, as shown in Table 4-2,
the reservoir will reach its full capacity in 2033, when the dam crest will have reached its final level of 1300 m
asl, while the filling of the reservoir will not be finalised before 2037. As mentioned, the figures presented in the
Table are based on the assumption that the average volume of 1.211 km³ will be available every year. In case
a series of dryer than average years should occur during this period, filling of the reservoir could even take
longer. This slow filling of the reservoir certainly comes at a cost, since increase in electricity generation would
be more rapid with a faster filling regime. However, this process reduces impacts on downstream water users
and is therefore much more favourable than what usually is the case in large storage projects.

m (river elevation, no dam) reservoir volume and area are 0, reaching their maximum once the reservoir will
have reached FSL. This is shown also in the storage-area curve in Figure 4-2.

                    Elevation (m asl)             Volume (km3)                  Area (km2)

Page 25




Figure 4-2:       Rogun reservoir storage-area curve
                  Source: ESIA Vol. 2, Annex 13-3, Water Resource Modelling


With increasing dam height and reservoir volume, electricity generation will also gradually increase. Presently,
two units with a reduced capacity (200 MW each) are installed and operational at Rogun HPP. The runners of
these two turbines will be replaced at a later stage (planned to be done by end of 2025), for them to reach the
final capacity of 630 MW, and the 4 remaining turbines will gradually be installed, bringing the total installed
capacity up to its final value of 3,780 MW. In parallel to this development the operation pattern will gradually
change in the following way:
    •     In the situation without Rogun, seasonal regulation is done entirely by Nurek, whose reservoir oscillates
          between the FSL of 910 m asl and the MOL of 857 m asl, controlling a live storage volume of 4.2 km³.
    •     With increasing dam height and installation of additional generating units, Rogun will gradually take over
          regulation, fluctuating between a spring low and an autumn high.
    •     In parallel to that, reservoir fluctuation at Nurek will gradually decrease.
    •     Once Rogun reservoir will have reached its FSL of 1290 m asl, flow regulation will be done entirely at
          Rogun, the reservoir fluctuating between FSL and the MOL of 1260 m asl and a regulating volume of
          4.2 km³.
    •     Nurek reservoir will then be operating as a near-ROR structure; its reservoir level will be kept more or
          less stable at FSL or a little bit below; small fluctuations in reservoir level will be used for balancing out
          fluctuations resulting from Rogun operation (e.g. by peaking) or to provide some storage space in case
          of a flood.
This will then be the standard operation pattern for the Rogun-Nurek cascade. This development was modelled
by TEAS (2014), and is shown in Figure 4-3. This graph should not be interpreted as precisely reflecting the
development of all parameters illustrated, but it is a useful visualisation of the overall development, whereby j-
16 in the graph would correspond approximately to the year 2037 of the present time scale.




                                                                                                                 PUBLIC
Page 26




Figure 4-3:       Process of Rogun reservoir filling
                  Source: TEAS 2014 (2013 Reservoir Operation Simulation)


4.1.4     Cascade Operation with Rogun

4.1.4.1    Basic Operation Characteristics
When discussing Rogun HPP and cascade operation (the latter including Rogun and Nurek HPPs, taking into
account that the smaller HPPs downstream of Nurek will continue to operate as ROR structures), two patterns
have to be distinguished, namely:
    •     Seasonal: this covers the annual pattern of retaining water in summer for using it for electricity generation
          in winter, which leads to a seasonal fluctuation in reservoir level. As illustrated in Figure 4-1, this is
          presently done in Nurek reservoir, to be gradually taken over by Rogun reservoir until such a time when
          Rogun will assume this role completely, transforming Nurek into a close to ROR structure. It is important
          to point out that the total regulated volume, which is now 4.2 km³ stored in summer and released in
          winter will not change in this process.
    •     Short term, e.g. daily: as a general rule, storage hydropower plants, in addition to seasonal regulation,
          are also very suitable for short-term (daily) regulation. This means that they can closely follow short-
          term variations in demand.
In Tajikistan, electricity demand is highest in winter. Therefore seasonal water storage in reservoirs is essential
for meeting this demand pattern, and this causes the reservoir drawdown illustrated in Figure 4-3. However,
there is also a distinct daily pattern, as shown in Figure 4-4.




                                                                                                                PUBLIC
Page 27




Figure 4-4:       Average daily electricity demand pattern
                  TEAS 2014: Economic Analysis, Fig. 18, p. 33
In addition to the fact that electricity demand in winter (Q1, January to March, and Q4, October to December) is
higher than in summer, the Figure also clearly shows that there are two daily peaks, one in the morning and one
in the evening. Peaking production is now done by Nurek (and to some degree by the downstream HPPs of the
cascade). Once Rogun fully operating, Nurek will still be able to perform in this way, but some of the short-term
regulation is likely to be assumed by Rogun as well. It has to be pointed out that there is no firm and well-defined
regime for the short-term operation of the cascade, since this will be – as Nurek is now – largely demand-driven.
A description of possible and probable patterns is provided in the following Sections.

4.1.4.2    Operation of Nurek HPP
Presently, Nurek provides a large part of the total electricity presently produced and used in Tajikistan. It is
operated in three ways, whereby these three modes are overlapping:
    •     Baseload: 2 to 3 out of the 9 units are always in operation (not including maintenance or emergency
          shutdowns), and water output of each of the units, when working at full capacity, is between 150 to 163
          m³/s.
    •     Seasonal storage: during the high-flow season the reservoir is filled to its FSL of 910 m asl, and during
          winter, when inflow into the reservoir is lowest and electricity demand is highest, it is drawn down to its
          MOL of 857 m asl, in this way using its live storage of 4.2 km³ (2014 ESIA, p. A).
    •     Daily peaking: daily variation in electricity demand has to be met by corresponding short-term changes
          in production. Such a peaking regime means that turbines are turned on and off frequently, which in turn
          leads to important variations of water output from the power plant. Presently, peaking demand is met by
          Nurek and the cascade downstream of it.
Typical daily patterns are shown in Figure 4-5.




Figure 4-5:       Turbining pattern in Nurek HPP
                                                                                                              PUBLIC
Page 28




                  Source; Barki Tojik Dispatch Center, data received 2024-05-08
The theoretical water output from Nurek, with all 9 units operating at full capacity, would be between 1,350 m³/s
(with the reservoir at FSL) and 1,467 m³/s (with the reservoir at MOL). The Figure shows that during the days
illustrated this maximum was never reached. This can be due to the demand level at that time, but also, especially
in winter, due to limited water availability. It also shows that production (and with it use of water) is not constant
during the day. In summer, the hourly differences are rather small, a small peak in the morning is visible in one
curve and one in the evening in both of them. In winter, the situation can be very different. While one of the
curves shows a day with almost constant 24 h output, the other one illustrates a day with a marked peaking
pattern. In the morning, water use raises within 3 h from 250 to 842 m³/s, to drop then from 827 to 266 m³/s
within one hour; it should be noted that the available data are average hourly discharges, the actual change may
be within a much shorter time. The values of roughly 250 m³/s during the day and in the night would mean that
at this time probably only two units were operating at reduced capacity.
This clearly illustrates the fact that in a peaking power plant, the daily pattern can vary greatly from one day to
the next, and that during the day very marked short-term variations are possible.

4.1.4.3    Operation of Rogun HPP During Filling
The general filling process of Rogun reservoir was discussed in Section 4.1.3. Here, operation during the filling
process is discussed. In order to understand this, some information on relevant structures is required. At the
present stage of construction (May 2025), in addition to the dam with a crest elevation of 1100 m asl the following
structures of relevance for this process are in place:
    •     Diversion tunnels: there are three diversion tunnels (DT 1 to 3) in operation, a fourth (DT 4) is under
          construction. when the reservoir will reach a level of about 1110 m asl, sedimentation in the reservoir is
          expected to reach a level that will block DT 1 and 2, which by then will be replaced by DT 4. Capacity of
          the DTs depends on reservoir water level; at al level of 1100 m asl, the four DTs will be able to pass a
          total of about 7,000 m³/s.
    •     Two units are installed already in the power house, with a maximum installed capacity of 200 MW. Each
          of them is able to turbine water at a maximum of 192 m³/s, at the present reservoir level about 156 m³/s
          each; it is planned to upgrade them to the full capacity of 630 MW each, the process foreseen to start
          be the end of 2025.
The following Figure shows the operation pattern of Rogun HPP for the years 2019 to 2023.




                                                                                                               PUBLIC
Page 29




Figure 4-6:       Operation of Rogun HPP during the construction period
                  Source: IADT Report 2025
The Figure shows the following main characteristics:
    •     By far the largest quantity of water passes through the DTs, with a very clear seasonal pattern.
    •     The two turbines already installed are operating with a maximum combined discharge of about 300 m³/s.
    •     During the high-flow season, the turbines are shut down at times, during the low-flow season they
          operate continuously, although with a reduced output; this presumably reflects water availability (i.e.
          inflow, since the aim is to keep the reservoir elevation stable).
    •     During the low-flow season, no water flows through the DTs for extended periods of time. The only
          discharge during that time is the one passing though the turbines.
Some additional information is provided in the next Figure. This shows details of DT operation in 2020, which
was the dryest year of the ones shown in the previous Figure.




Figure 4-7:       Rogun filling phase operation
                  Source: IADT Report 2025
                  DT = Diversion Tunnel (tunnels 1-3). UWL = upstream water level (= reservoir water level)
                  Curves legend : --- Total Outlfow, --- DT1, --- DT2, --- DT3, --- UWL


Main elements:
    •     Till about July, the plant was operated in a way as to keep the reservoir level constant at about 1058 m
          asl, meaning that inflow was equal to outflow..
    •     In August/early September, the reservoir level was risen to 1070 m asl, by which time the total reservoir
          volume reached about 0.44 km³. Note that the amount of water retained here was far below the
          theoretically available 1.2 km³; a reservoir level rise by 12 m, at a reservoir surface of about 7 km²,
          corresponds to a volume increase of about 0.08 km³.
                                                                                                              PUBLIC
Page 30




    •     In the beginning as well as in the end of the year, all DTs were closed completely; discharge during this
          period must represent turbine output (of about 150 – 200 m³/s).

4.1.4.4    Operation of Rogun HPP
Rogun HPP will be operated in the way indicated above, meaning that the annually available volume of water
shifted from summer to winter will be retained there, seasonal cascade regulation to be guided by Rogun and
no longer by Nurek. Figure 4-8 shows the change this will cause: summer flows (= unregulated flow of Vakhsh
River, inflow to Rogun reservoir) will decrease in the section between Rogun and Nurek, while winter flows
(regulated outflow from Rogun reservoir) will increase.




Figure 4-8:       Inflow to and release from Rogun during operation
                  Source: WSP 2025 ESIA: Vol. 1 Annex 13-3: Water Resource Modelling, Figure 9-1


This general operation pattern does not say anything about the short-term (e.g. daily) operation of the power
plant. With all 6 units in operation, the power plant can turbine 1,626 m³/s in total. Assuming a year-round
operation at full capacity, this would result in a total water volume of 51 km³ per year, 2.5 times the available
water. This makes it clear that operation will have to be guided by water availability and power demand. The
most probable scenario is that at least one turbine will be running all the time, the remaining according to
demand, with maximum capacity being reached only during peak hours. The result of this is a at times highly
irregular flow release, with the potential to change between 271 m³/s (one turbine operating) to 1,626 m³/s (power
plant operating at full capacity). Such changes can occur frequently and abruptly.



4.1.4.5    Combined Operation of Rogun and Nurek
As mentioned previously, the guiding principle of the operation of the combined Rogun and Nurek reservoirs is
that there will be no difference in the seasonal flow pattern below Nurek when comparing the situation without
and with Rogun. Simulation results show that this is possible and Tajikistan has made a commitment to not shift
more than 4.2 km3/year from summer to winter. This is the amount historically shifted by Nurek operations
(emptying itself in winter, and being refilled in summer, by the amount of 4.2 km³/year). This commitment from
Tajikistan is reflected in the minutes of riparian consultations as well as in the financing agreement as per the
obligation to comply at all times with the water sharing agreements (which include the national water allocations
and the seasonally agreed deliveries) and avoiding significant harm downstream. Thus, after impoundment, the
presence and operation of Rogun should be invisible to downstream countries, given that the flow regime
downstream of Nurek will not change from the historical period.



                                                                                                            PUBLIC
Page 31




                 1200

                 1000

                  800

   Flow (m3/s)    600

                  400

                  200

                    0
                          Jan    Feb     Mar     Apr     May      Jun   Jul      Aug      Sep    Oct      Nov   Dec
                                                                    Month

                        Release Rogun            Nurek Release No Rogun                Nurek Release with Rogun

Figure 4-9:              Simulated releases from Nurek Reservoir with and without Rogun
                         Source: WSP 2025 ESIA: Vol. 1 Annex 13-3: Water Resource Modelling, Figure 9-2


The Figure shows that, with Rogun operating, the release from Nurek will be the same as it is now, without
Rogun. The pattern is determined by releases from Rogun (Figure 4-8, Rogun outflows), and this pattern is not
changed by Nurek since this will then be operated as a “near ROR” structure. It should be noted that the cascade
downstream of Nurek only has the capacity to modify the pattern on a daily, or at the most on a weekly basis,
since the storage volume of these ROR schemes is too small for having any major influence (see Table 3-4).
The same will be the case for Shurob HPP, which is planned to be built between Rogun outlet and Nurek
reservoir, this however not before Rogun will be finalised and operating.
As mentioned before, the cascade, in addition to seasonal storage and regulation, will also have to respond to
short-term (daily and shorter) fluctuations. The pattern to follow, driven by demand, is illustrated in Figure 4-5;
these short-term fluctuations will be taken over, to some degree, by Rogun HPP. Still, as mentioned, Nurek will
continue to do some of that (whence the precision that it will be operated as a “near-ROR structure”, i.e. without
seasonal, but still with short-term regulation). Nurek will also have to be operated as a re-regulation structure for
Rogun: while Rogun HPP, when at FSL and operating at full capacity, has a total water output of 1626 m³/s, the
maximum output of Nurek is at 1350 m³/s. This means that Rogun cannot operate at full capacity when Nurek
reservoir is at FSL. For this reason, Nurek reservoir will mostly be somewhat below FSL in order to be able to
cope with the output from Rogun. To some extent, daily fluctuations in outflow can also be moderated by the
HPPs downstream of Nurek, which have daily (or in the case of Baipaza) weekly storage.


4.2              Water Quality
The creation of a reservoir with the hydrological changes this brings about, can have an influence on water
quality, and this in turn can cause impacts in the downstream area.

4.2.1             Situation


Some data on water quality are provided in Table 4-4.
