Water quality in Vakhsh River is generally good, mainly since there are no major sources of contamination. The
data also show that Amu Darya shows an increased load of items linked with salinity (carbonate, chloride,
sulphate). A description of the baseline water quality situation is provided in the updated ESIA, Vol. 2, Annex 7-
1, Table 7-10.

4.2.2     Impacts
Changes of water quality, in the context of a dam project, are mainly the result of the transition from a lotic (river)
environment to lentic (lake-like), with stagnant, deep water.

4.2.2.1    Reservoir Stratification
Under the climatic conditions prevailing in the project area (with hot summers and cold winters) the large and
deep Rogun reservoir will develop a thermal stratification with two yearly circulations. A stable thermal
stratification with warmer water at the surface and cooler water in the deeper layers during summer can have
impacts on water temperature in the downstream area. Since the water intake for the turbines is located at
greater depth, i.e. below MOL, colder water will flow through the turbines, to be delivered to the river downstream
of the dam. This can have negative impacts on the river, and mainly on organisms (fish and organisms serving
as food for fish). In the case of Rogun, this impact will be negligible, since the water will flow into Nurek reservoir
a short stretch downstream, and since the river section between these two reservoirs has very little if any
biological importance. Colder water flowing into Nurek reservoir will insert itself into the lower water layers of the
reservoir, without causing any major change in the thermal stratification of Nurek reservoir. Downstream of
Nurek, no change from the present situation will result.

4.2.2.2    Oxygen Content
Reservoir stratification is also important with respect to oxygen content of the water. During a stable thermal
stratification in summer, oxygen in deeper layers can be depleted if large amounts of organic matter have to be
broken down. In the case of a reservoir, this can be the case under two conditions, namely:
    •     after first filling, if large amounts of biomass (vegetation) were submerged and are now being broken
          down, and
                                                                                                                PUBLIC
Page 33




    •     in cases where eutrophication of the reservoir happens, i.e. when large amounts of nutrients are being
          brought into the reservoir, which lead to a high plant (algae) productivity close to the surface, where
          there is enough light for photosynthesis.
The second condition is not considered as a risk in the case of Rogun. Nurek reservoir, after over 40 years of
existence, is still an oligotrophic water body, i.e. with low concentrations of nutrients, and the same can be
expected for Rogun
The first condition (submergence of biomass) can lead to anoxic conditions in the reservoir, and this in turn will
lead to methane (CH4) production; methane is a very powerful greenhouse gas (GHG). This is the case mainly
in tropical reservoirs, where often very large amounts of biomass are submerged. This is not the case for Rogun,
where the vegetation mostly consists of grassland, with very small amounts of forest. In the following calculation,
based on results from other areas with similar (grassland) vegetation, a biomass amount of 10 t/ha is assumed;
in grasslands, the amount of biomass is usually between 0.4 and 8 t/ha, which means that 10 t is a conservative
assumption. In moist tropical forests, this value can reach an amount of 150 to over 200 t/ha, taking into account
only soft, i.e. easily degradable biomass (leaves, herbs etc.), leaving away hard biomass (wood), since this latter
decays very slowly and is not really of relevance for water quality deterioration (Zwahlen, 2022:406). Table 4-5
summarises the situation as it will likely evolve in the case of Rogun HPP.

The calculations provided in Table 4-5 are based on and show the following:
    1. Starting point is 2025, i.e. the present (May 2025) situation, with the dam crest at 1100 m asl, the
       reservoir level at 1070 m asl and a reservoir volume of 0.5 km³.
    2. Reservoir level will gradually increase till reaching FSL of 1290 m asl (note that FSL represents the
       stage when the reservoir is full; as Figure 4-3 shows, there will be a seasonal fluctuation between FSL
       and MOL).
    3. The volume of retained water will gradually increase for finally reaching full storage volume of 13.3 km³
       (with a seasonal fluctuation as indicated for reservoir level) .
    4. Gradual filling of the reservoir (columns 2 and 3) will be done by retaining the water that is allocated to
       Tajikistan, but was not used so far. It is assumed here that this will be done by retaining 1.2 km³ per
       year, which corresponds to the average amount available for this purpose; this would allow for filling the
       reservoir till 2037. The amount actually retained per year will depend on weather conditions, it could be
       more in wet years and less in dry years.

                                                                                                                    PUBLIC
Page 34




      5. With rising water level, reservoir surface will increase. Surface of the reservoir depending on reservoir
         volume and level is derived from the storage-area curve shown in Figure 4-2.
      6. An additional land surface will be submerged every year, and the biomass on this additional surface will
         have to be broken down.
      7. The total annual inflow to the reservoir will be the total flow of Vakhsh River, which is 20 km³ on average.
      8. The total oxygen content in the yearly inflow will be the amount of oxygen available for breaking down
         the newly submerged biomass. The amount indicated here is based on the assumption that average
         oxygen content in the inflowing water is 8 mg/l; this is a conservative approach, actual content is more
         likely to be around 10 mg/l.
      9. A certain quantity of biomass is submerged yearly; the estimate here is that the average amount of
         biomass is 1,000 t/km².
      10. The amount of oxygen required for breaking down the newly submerged biomass is calculated on the
          assumption that 1.07 t of oxygen is required per t of biomass.
      11. Oxygen balance indicates the amount of oxygen per year that would still be in the water after all the
          submerged biomass would have been broken down.
      12. Indicates the percentage of oxygen consumed for braking down all newly submerged biomass.
The calculation shows that there will be a huge surplus of oxygen, even under these somewhat pessimistic
assumptions (to be on the safe side, an oxygen content of 8 mg/l was assumed, althoug given the generally low
wate temperature in the river it is likely to be 10 or more mg/l, and a high estimate for the amount of biomass
was included). The conclusion from this is that there is no risk of anoxic conditions forming in the deeper layers
of the reservoir. One specific condition helps in this respect, namely the fact that the reservoir will be filled over
such a long period, which means a gradual, slow increase in reservoir surface and therefore in submerged
biomass, and a very high turnover rate of the water given the fact that only 6% of the water flowing into the
reservoir will be retained.
Since no anoxic conditions will develop in Rogun reservoir, there is also no risk of oxygen-free water being
released to Nurek reservoir.
Concerning the overall GHG emissions from Rogun HPP see 2025 ESIA Vol. 1, Ch. 9, GHG.

4.2.3     Conclusion
No negative impacts on water quality are expected to result from building and operating Rogun HPP, under the
condition that water contamination during construction is strictly controlled and prevented. Still, precautions are
required during the construction period, which presents a risk of water contamination (e.g. due to accidents with
or inappropriate handling of hazardous material; the measures outlined in the ESMP (2025 ESIA, Vol. 3, B04,
Waste Water Management Plan, and B05, Hazardous Material Management Plan) need to be implemented.
Water quality management is required (see Section 5.5).
Due to the marked seasonal fluctuation, Rogun reservoir is not expected to develop into a good habitat for fish
species; on the other hand, that Nurek will not show such fluctuations any longer, it can be expected that there
conditions for fish species which are adapted to lake conditions will find a better situation (see 2025 ESIA, Vol.
1, Ch. 15, Biodiversity).


4.3     Sediments

4.3.1     Situation

4.3.1.1    Sediment Retention in Nurek Reservoir
Vakhsh river is characterized by a high sediment load. The concentration of suspended solids fluctuates during
the year and reaches its maximum during the high flow season. TEAS (Phase II: Project Definition Options –
Volume 2: Basic Data – Chapter 6: Sedimentation, March 2014) has determined the annual sediment supply
into the reservoir to be in the order of magnitude of 62 to 100 hm³. As a conservative approach, the value of
100 hm³/yr has been considered as a representative assumption of sediment solid runoff.


                                                                                                               PUBLIC

Since the construction of Nurek HPP, the sediments of the upper Vakhsh catchment no longer reach the lower
catchment but instead are deposited in the reservoir of Nurek. To what degree sediment is retained in Nurek
reservoir is shown by the following Figure. The suspended solid still reaching Tigrovaya Balka stems mostly or
completely from the intermediate catchment, as good as no sediments will pass Nurek reservoir.

Nurek reservoir has already lost a part of its capacity due to sediment accumulation. While most of the sediment
is deposited below MOL, i.e. in the dead storage, some of it in the upper parts of the reservoir also accumulates
above MOL (see Figure 4-12). The loss of active storage is estimated at 10 * 106 m³ per year in the situation
without Rogun (HR Wallingford, 2016).
                                                                                                                                                                                                              PUBLIC
Page 36




Figure 4-12:    Sedimentation of Nurek reservoir
                Source: HR Wallingford, 2016


The study concludes that without Rogun, the dead storage of Nurek will be filled by about 2075, the loss of active
storage continuing as indicated above.

4.3.1.2   River between Rogun and Nurek
About 15 km of Vakhsh river remain between Rogun dam and Nurek reservoir. The river there flows in a narrow
valley, with steep mountain slopes on both sides. A number of small creeks with intermittent flow join the river
on this stretch, but the only tributary, with a catchment area of 52 km² and probably permanent flow (for which
however no data are available) joins Vakhsh River on the left side immediately downstream of the dam. This
stream had already in earlier studies been identified as a potential risk for Rogun HPP, since a mudflow from
this small lateral valley could block Vakhsh just downstream of the dam. For this reason, a check dam was built
there, with the aim of retaining such sediments (2014 ESIA). This check dam was finalised in 2016.
On May 16, 2024, heavy rainfall caused a flash flood in Obishur which triggered a large mudflow. By this time,
the reservoir behind the Obishur check dam had had already been filled to about half its capacity with sediments.
The massive mudflow filled the reservoir almost completely, and a large amount of sediment, including large
boulders, was flushed through the openings at the top of the check dam. This material was then carried
downstream to the Obishur confluence with Vakhsh, were it was deposited and blocked Diversion Tunnels 1 and
2 (see Figure 4-13). Fortunately, DT3 was not blocked.




                                                                                                           PUBLIC
Page 37




Figure 4-13:    Situation of mudflow from Obishur blocking Vakhsh, May 2024
                Source: WeBuild, 2024-05-30


The total amount of debris and sediment which was flushed from Obishur to Vakhsh was estimated at about
900,000 m³, 550,000 of which were deposited in the river bed of Vakhsh, while 350,000 m³ were swept into DT
1 and 2. This led to a backwater effect which first resulted in an automatic shutdown of the units 5 and 5 (the
two turbines installed and operating), and finally to flooding of the power house. Over the following few days, the
sediment from the DTs and from Vakhsh bed at the food of the dam could be flushed out. The reservoir level,
which was at 1070 m asl at that time, was lowered to 1062 m in anticipation of the flood that would arrive in the
reservoir due to the rain event (see Figure 4-14).




                                                                                                            PUBLIC
Page 38




Figure 4-14:     Reservoir level and flood evacuation through DTs.
                 Source; Tractebel 2025 (UWL = upstream water level, reservolr level)


As can be seen in this Figure, first only DT3 was available. Gradual opening of the gate of DT1 then allowed
flushing out the sediment which had blocked it.
The flood and mudflow has seriously damaged the left abutment of the check dam. DSPOE (2025) commented
on these events, emphasising the risk of other such incidents in the future, and criticising the unsafe way in
which material that needed to be removed from the plunge pool was disposed of, blocking Obishur River, and
asking for the following measures to be taken immediately:
    •     Stabilize the compromised left abutment of the Obishur Dam.
    •     Remove the deposited material obstructing the confluence of the Obishur and Vakhsh Rivers.
This is an issue that will need continuous attention

4.3.2     Impacts
Once Rogun dam in place, sediments will be retained there, they will no longer reach Nurek reservoir. The
situation downstream of Nurek will not change. TEAS (2024) predicts a sedimentation progress similar to the
one that was observed in Nurek (see Figure 4-12), with fine sediments settling down in the deeper parts of the
reservoir, while coarse material will be deposited at the upper end, forming a delta at the inflow of Vakhsh River.
This would also lead to a gradual loss of regulation volume, i.e. of live storage, which will continue over the life
span of the project. This will considerably increase the life span of Nurek. No sediment flushing is foreseen at
Rogun.
The situation of Obishur remains critical and needs to be surveyed on a continuous basis.

4.3.3     Conclusions
The fact that sediment will be retained in Rogun reservoir will have the following effects:

                                                                                                             PUBLIC
Page 39




      •    Rogun reservoir will be filled gradually. Under the somewhat pessimistic assumptions used in the TEAS
           study, it was estimated that the lifetime of Rogun HPP, i.e. the time till the reservoir will be filled, would
           be 115 years. Updated estimates based on more detailed evaluation of sediment inflow estimate this
           time to be 130 years or more. This might be further influenced by a later construction of additional dams
           upstream of Rogun, which might slow down siltation of Rogun reservoir (see CIA). Once the reservoir
           filled, Rogun HPP can still be operated as a ROR structure.
      •    The life span of Nurek will be extended by the retention of sediments in Rogun. This is important, since
           Nurek there is no provision for safely operating Nurek once the reservoir is full, and in that stage it will
           not be possible to operate it as ROR plant. Adding Rogun to the cascade, therefore, has two positive
           effects:
               o   Increasing the active life span, and with it the duration of electricity generation, of Nurek HPP.
               o   Providing more time (approximately the life span of Rogun HPP) for finding a solution for
                   operating Nurek when its reservoir will be filled.
      •    The existence of Rogun will allow the construction of Shurob HPP, an additional power plant foreseen
           to be built at the upper end of Nurek reservoir, with an FSL that will reach the level of the water outlet at
           Rogun HPP. Since, as mentioned, the part of the river between Rogun and Nurek, a stretch of
           approximately 15 km, has very little if any ecological value, this would be a minimum impact addition to
           the Vakhsh cascade. It is a ROR structure which can only be built with Rogun in place, since otherwise
           the small reservoir would be filled with sediment very rapidly. This is a positive impact of Rogun in terms
           of energy production.
      •    Since under present conditions, without Rogun, sediment is already retained in Nurek reservoir, no
           change concerning sediment load downstream of Nurek will occur.


4.4       EFlow

4.4.1      Definition
A residual flow (ecological or minimum flow, EFlow) is defined as the minimum amount of water which needs to
be kept flowing at all times in order to guarantee a minimum of water in the river on a permanent basis.
The main purpose of defining and maintaining a minimum flow is usually one or both of the two following:
      •    to maintain the affected part of the river as a habitat, mainly for fish, but also for other aquatic life; and/or
      •    to guarantee water availability on a permanent basis for water users along the affected stretch of river;
           this concerns mainly water needs for irrigation or drinking water supply, for maintaining a sufficient
           dilution of waste water being discharged into the river, and/or to maintain the use of the river for boat
           traffic.

4.4.2      Standards and Guidelines
Relevant guidelines are summarised in the 2025 ESIA for Rogun, Vol. 2, Annex 13-1: Minimum Environmental
Flow. Of special importance:
      •    The Good Practice Handbook for Environmental Flows for Hydropower Projects, published by the World
           Bank Group, provides guidance to practitioners on selecting an appropriate environmental lows
           assessment level for hydropower project developments, as well as guidance on taking rigorous and
           consistent approaches to assess and manage hydropower project impacts on downstream river
           ecosystems and people, through the assessment and provision of E-Flow (World Bank, 2018). The
           Handbook defines E-Flow as,” the quantity, frequency, timing and quality of water and sediment flow
           necessary to sustain freshwater and estuarine ecosystems and the human livelihoods and well-being
           that depends on these ecosystems.”
      •    Chapter 3 of the European Investment Bank (EIB), Environmental, Climate and Social Guidelines on
           Hydropower Development, October 2019, deals with environmental issues and impacts. Specific
           reference is made to the EIB requirements/recommendations for Downstream Hydrology and Limnology.




                                                                                                                    PUBLIC
Page 40




4.4.3     Importance of EFlow
In the case of hydropower projects, an EFlow will have to be defined when there is a risk of temporary or
permanent dewatering of a section of a river. This will be the case under the following conditions:
    •     Peaking power plant: when an HPP is used mainly or exclusively for peaking power production; this
          means that it will often or regularly be stopped during non-peak hours. No water will be delivered via the
          turbines during these off-times. EFlow is then needed for preventing the river bed of falling completely
          dry.
    •     Deviation power plant: water from a reservoir can be deviated from the river bed by headrace structures
          (canals, tunnels and penstock) for being turbined in a power house at some distance (up to several km)
          downstream of the dam, in order to gain head for power production. This could mean that the river
          between dam and power house tailrace outlet, or at least till the next tributary, would remain without
          water in the absence of an EFlow.
    •     An extreme case of a deviation power plant is the case where the power house is located on another
          river, e.g. in a neighbouring valley (transbasin project). In such a case, the entire river downstream of
          the dam to the confluence with another (major) river would be affected by this permanent reduction in
          flow.
Additional issues in the case of a peaking operation pattern are often the large amplitude between peaking
operation and EFlow, but also the usually very abrupt increase in water volume when the turbines start operating;
the increase from EFlow to maximum turbine outflow may take as little as a few seconds, and this can happen
several times per day. The surge wave resulting from such an operation pattern will literally flush out the river,
and it may present a risk for people along the stretch of river affected in this way.
Rogun HPP is not a deviation power plant, since the power house is located directly at the foot of the dam,
therefore there is no constantly dewatered part of the river which would require an EFlow. However, as Figure
4-6 shows, in the low flow season during the process of reservoir filling, there are times when the DTs are closed,
water only passing through the turbines. If during such a period the turbines would shut down, no water would
be released. Such a situation will be the normal case under standard operation, i.e. after the reservoir filling
phase. With the exception of spilling (presumably rather rare and limited to the season of maximum flow), the
only water flowing downstream of Rogun will be the volume passing through the turbines. EFlow will have to be
released when all turbines are closed down.

4.4.4     EFlow Modelling
The only part of the river where the flow regime will be directly influenced by Rogun HPP is the stretch of Vakhsh
River between Rogun HPP outlet and the upper end of Nurek reservoir, a river section of about 15 km. For this,
an EFlow modelling was carried out (2025 ESIA, Vol. 2, Annex 13-1, Minimum Environmental Flow). It
determined the minimum flow required based on the natural seasonal distribution of water in the stretch of the
river in question. The results of this modelling are shown in Figure 4-15.




Figure 4-15:      EFlow in Vakhsh River in comparison to actual flows
                                                                                                             PUBLIC
Page 41




Two values were determined for the EFlow, namely DR (the minimum flow required during especially dry
conditions) and ML (maintenance low flow, minimum required during normal conditions). For both values,
seasonal variation was included for mimicking the natural seasonal flow variations. The actual flow conditions in
Vakhsh River are shown without Rogun (natural conditions) and with Rogun (release from Rogun reservoir;
these values are also shown in Figure 4-8.

4.4.5     Prevailing Situation at Rogun HPP Site
In the case of Rogun, a certain number of specific conditions has to be considered, namely:
    •     The affected stretch of river, where discharge could temporarily be reduced to zero, is very short, it is
          only between Rogun dam and the upper end of Nurek reservoir at FSL, a total of approximately 15 km.
          Further downstream, river discharge is regulated by Nurek, Rogun has no direct effect on that.
    •     There are no settlements, i.e. no water users, along this affected part of the river.
    •     The importance of this river stretch for fish is very limited (2014 ESIA, Chapter Error! Reference source
          not found.; 2025 ESIA, 2025 ESIA, Vol 2, Annex 7-1, Baselines Conditions). In any case, even if there
          were fish migrations from Nurek reservoir upstream, these would be blocked by Rogun dam. In addition
          to that, the only tributary with permanent flow, which joins Vakhsh on the left bank immediately
          downstream of Rogun dam, the Obishur, was blocked by the construction of a dam serving for retaining
          sediments stemming from it, since there is a risk that such sediments could block the powerhouse outlet
          of Rogun (see 4.3.1.2). Therefore, this small stream is no longer accessible for fish; there is no
          information on whether it ever did play a role as fish habitat.
    •     The river between Rogun dam and Nurek reservoir is a steep and narrow channel. With the exception
          of Obishur, a small stream reaching Vakhsh immediately downstream of the dam, and which was
          blocked by a check dam for retaining sediments (see Section 4.3.1.2), there are no permanent affluents
          on this part of the river.
    •     There is a plan for an additional HPP, Shurob, between Rogun dam and Nurek reservoir. Its dam will be
          93 m high, in this way using the total difference in elevation between the two large reservoirs. It will be
          a ROR structure, its reservoir reaching the powerhouse outlet of Rogun.



4.4.6     Situation with Rogun HPP in Place
Construction of Rogun HPP is under way. Presently, the dam crest has reached an elevation of 1100 m asl; this
is the height of the upstream coffer dam, which will be integrated into the main dam as dam construction
progresses. The reservoir is presently kept at an elevation of 1075 m asl; its level will gradually increase as
construction goes on (see Section 4.1.3).
The relevant question concerning EFlow concerns the situation in the river, i.e. quantity and distribution of water
flowing in the section between Rogun dam and Nurek reservoir. Three different stages have to be considered,
namely, original (natural) situation, construction period, and operation.
Original situation:
    •     Before the start of the construction of Rogun dam, river flow in the river section in question was the
          natural flow of Vakhsh River, the inflow into Nurek Reservoir.
    •     This flow was characterised by its seasonal pattern, with high flows in summer and low flows in winter,
          with a minimum monthly average flow of about 190 m³/s in February. (see Figure 3-4).
Construction period:
    •     The first decisive part of this period was the construction of the diversion tunnels (or rather, their
          rehabilitation: they had been excavated already during Soviet times and were then blocked during a
          flood event) and the coffer dams were swept away.
    •     Since these structures (DTs) are in place, the river flows through these tunnels (see Figure 4-6 and
          Figure 4-7). Amount of water and its seasonal distribution downstream of the dam remained unchanged.
    •     Once the coffer dams had reached a certain elevation, a first step of reservoir impoundment started;
          presently, the reservoir has reached a level of 1075 m asl, and has a volume of 0.44 km³. This means
          that over the last several years this amount of water was retained in the reservoir which, had this been
                                                                                                              PUBLIC
Page 42




          done in one average year, with a total flow in Vakhsh River of 20 km³, would have meant a reduction in
          flow of 2.2%.
    •     Presently, in Rogun HPP two turbines with reduced capacity (200 MW each) are installed and operating,
          presently turbining about 156 m³/s each, with a maximum of 192 m³/s each.
    •     The plan is to fill Rogun reservoir gradually, to reach its FSL of 1290 m asl in 2037, by retaining, on
          average, 1.2 km³ of water per year (see Section 4.1.3 for a detailed description).
    •     With an average yearly flow of 20 km³ in Vakhsh River, this means that retention for reservoir filling will
          reduce the flow in the river downstream of Rogun dam, and with this inflow to Nurek reservoir, by 6%;
          seasonal distribution will not change, since the remaining quantity of 18.8 km³ (in an average year) will
          flow to the downstream area via turbines and spillways.
    •     As the dam height increases, four more turbines will be installed, and the first two ones will be upgraded
          for all to reach the capacity of 630 MW each, being able to pass 271 m³/s; Rogun HPP will have a total
          capacity of 3,780 MW, maximum water output 1,626 m³/s.
    •     During this period, with the increasing water level in the reservoir and the gradual commissioning of the
          turbines, there will be a transition in operation of the two main power plants of the Vakhsh cascade,
          Rogun and Nurek: Rogun will gradually be taking over the regulatory role, with Nurek being kept at a
          more or less stable level (see Section 4.1.4).
    •     For the river stretch between Rogun and Nurek, this will mean that the seasonal distribution in flow will
          change, summer high flows being reduced while winter flows will be increased (by the, on average,
          seasonal shift of 4.2 km³ of water from summer to winter, see Figure 4-8).
    •     During the filling period, as mentioned, most of the water passes through the DTs. During the low-flow
          season, when inflow is less than the volume that can pass through the turbines, the DTs are closed
          completely. As shown in Figure 4-7, in order to keep the reservoir level stable, the volume turbined is
          restricted to the amount flowing in, which can be less than turbine capacity and reaches about 150 m³/s:
          EFlow determined as required for this period (low flow season) would be about 50 to 75 m²/s. If at such
          a time the turbined would need to be shut down, EFlow release would be required.
Rogun HPP operation:
    •     Once all 6 turbines commissioned and the reservoir at its FSL of 1290 m asl, the operational pattern not
          only of Rogun, but of the combined Rogun-Nurek cascade as shown in Figure 4-3 will be implemented.
    •     No sediment will be brought from Rogun reservoir to this part of the river, the sediment there (with
          potential input form Obishur) will be regularly flushed out.
    •     Rogun HPP will take over the regulating function in the cascade (which, without Rogun in place, was
          done by Nurek), meaning that Rogun reservoir will be filled up to FSL (1290 m asl) in summer and will
          be drawn down to MOL (1260 m asl) in winter, while Nurek will be operated as a type or ROR structure,
          meaning that the fluctuations in water level will be comparatively very small. There is no more detailed
          operation regime for Rogun HPP available as yet; however, it is assumed that one or two turbines in
          Rogun HPP will always be operating, while some peaking will be done according to net requirements.
    •     Under normal operation, at least one, but presumably more than one turbine will be running continuously.
          This means that below Rogun HPP, there will be water flowing all the time, while there will also be some
          short-term fluctuations due to peaking. The only time where EFlow would actually have to be released
          would be in cases when all turbines would be shut down. It has to be taken into account that this, if at
          all, is most likely to happen in winter, when electricity demand is highest, and where therefore every
          effort will be made to have the HPP in good operating conditions.

4.4.7     Conclusions
Concerning EFlow between Rogun HPP and Nurek reservoir, the following conclusion can be reached:
    •     The biological value of this part of the river was low already without Rogun dam in place; it will be further
          reduced with Rogun, at least in part due to peaking, but the impact is considered as minor (not
          significant) for the construction period and as negligible to minor (not significant) for the operation priod
          (2025 ESIA, Vol. 1, Chapter 15, Biodiversity.
    •     There is no human use of the river and river wate along this stretch of the river, therefore no negative
          socio-economic impact.
                                                                                                                PUBLIC
Page 43




      •    As Figure 4-15 shows, also with Rogun HPP operating the average monthly flow will always be higher
           than the required EFlow.
      •    Given the fact that during operation at least one turbine will be operating all the time, there will always
           be a minimum of 271 m³/s flowing, which is higher than the EFlow requitement calculated in the
           modelling study, the highest ML flow determined for August being 213 m³/s.
      •    EFlow will only be required in the rare cases where, for whatever reason, all turbines at Rogun HPP will
           be shut down.
EFlow is an important mitigation measure in many cases of hydropower projects, since with this a minimal
ecological functioning of an affected river can be maintained. However, in the case of Rogun HPP, as discussed
above, there is very little probability that it will ever have to be applied.


4.5       Floods
With respect to flood risks, large storage dams present two main aspects:
      •    Reduction of flood risk: flooding occurs when large amounts of water reach a river in a very short period,
           like e.g. exceptionally strong rains falling in a small area, or strong rain combined with snow melt, which
           can lead to a momentaneous river discharge which cannot be conveyed further downstream within the
           river bed and which therefore leads to flooding of the surrounding area. Large storage dams usually
           have a certain capacity for retaining additional water during such events, which may be sufficient for
           lowering the momentaneous peak flow and in this way can prevent or at least reduce a flooding event
           downstream of the dam. There is also a possibility, if a risk for flooding is recognised early enough, to
           lower the reservoir on beforehand in order to create more space for retaining the flood peak. In this way,
           dams can contribute to flood protection of e.g. inhabited areas.
      •    Risk of dam break: the most serious dam-related accident that can happen is a dam break, which has
           the potential to cause very serios flooding in the area downstream of the dam. The higher the dam and
           the larger the volume of the reservoir, the larger the potential damage such an event could cause. Three
           main measures are usually taken to minimise this risk:
               o   Construction of the dam according to best practice; this, e.g. means that a dam will be built in a
                   way that it is able to withstand the strongest earthquake that could happen in this area. Rogun
                   dam is designed according to international standards taking into account geological and seismic
                   risks (including Maximum Credible Earthquake, MCE).
               o   Resisting flood: a dam and its structures, in this context mainly the structures serving for flood
                   discharge, must be conceived and built for being able to handle an extreme flood event without
                   being damaged. The assumption on which the layout of the structures are dimensioned is the
                   Probable Maximum Flood (PMF), which is calculated based on local conditions and potential
                   hazards including rain storms, snow melt etc. TEAS (2014) determined the following magnitudes
                   of flood events in Vakhsh River:
                       ▪   2-year flood event:                 2,360 m³/s
                       ▪   5-year flood event:                 2,780 m³/s
                       ▪   10-year flood event:                3,070 m³/s
                       ▪   20-year flood event:                3,360 m³/s
                       ▪   100-year flood event:               4,030 m³/s
                       ▪   1’000-year flood event:             4,950 m³/s
                       ▪   10’000-year flood event:            5,970 m³/s
                       ▪   PMF (day):                          7,800 m³/s
                       ▪   PMF (instantaneous peak)            8,160 m³/s
                   The Probable Maximum Flood (PMF) is the theoretical maximum ﬂood that poses extremely
                   serious threats to the ﬂood control of a given project in a design watershed and could plausibly
                   occur under current meteorological conditions.
               o   Dam Failure Analysis: in spite of all safety measures taken, one component of a complete dam
                   study is a Dam Failure Analysis or a Dam Break Study; this analyses what would happen if the
                                                                                                               PUBLIC
Page 44




                dam in question would break, determining e.g. the time span until the flood wave would reach a
                certain point, e.g. the nearest inhabited area downstream of the dam, the area that would be
                flooded, the height the water would reach, and the duration of the flood. Based on this, an
                Emergency Preparedness Plan (EPP) can then be developed, which defines the measures to
                be taken in the case of such an accident (early warning system, evacuation plans including the
                identification of safe muster points, required information, awareness raising and training of the
                potentially affected population, etc. A dam break analysis was undertaken in for the Stage 1
                dam height of 1100 m asl (Gruner, 2018), which showed that a failure of Rogun dam at this
                stage would not lead to an overtopping of Nurek dam. A further dam break analysis will be
                prepared for the final dam height of 1300 m asl. These risks are further considered in 2025 ESIA
                Volume 1, Chapter 23: Major Accidents and Disasters. DSPOE, in all their reports, has pointed
                out the necessity for this final EPP, insisting that it will have to be in line with the current EPP
                study for the Vakhsh cascade.


The Vakhsh cascade as presently operating, including Nurek dam, is not designed to handle a Probable
Maximum Flood (PMF). Nurek’s surface spillway capacity is only of 2020 m3/s, not able to handle the PMF peak
of 8,160 m³/s. Rogun has been designed with a surface spillway able to operate and safely ensure PMF
evacuation. Appropriate operation of Rogun’s spillway facilities will provide protection of Nurek and the
downstream areas, in this way reducing flood risks to a considerable extent. This is a positive impact of Rogun
HPP.




                                                                                                             PUBLIC
Page 45




5              Conclusions
In this Section, the impacts caused on the key receptors listed in Section 2.2 are summarised and discussed.


5.1       Hydrology
      •    Risk: Change in seasonal water availability in the downstream area, which would have a serious
           negative impact on water users especially for irrigation.
      •    Situation: Two stages need to be distinguished:
               o   Reservoir filling: for filling the reservoir, a total of 13.3 km³ will be retained, and this water will
                   not be available in the downstream area. This has a negative impact on irrigation in the
                   downstream riparian countries. However, in order to minimise this impact, the following
                   conditions will be met:
                       ▪   Tajikistan will continue to respect regional water allocations agreed yearly at the ICWC
                           (which according to Protocol 566 amount in average to 15.4% of the water flowing in
                           Amu Darya basin as the share of Tajikistan).
                       ▪   So far, Tajikistan has not used this share completely, leaving a surplus of 1.2 km³ per
                           year on average. This unused part will be used for filling Rogun reservoir; this
                           corresponds to approximately 6% of the flow of Vakhsh river. The exact amount is likely
                           to vary each year depending on the flows of each year and the exact water allocation
                           for Tajikistan agreed each year.
                       ▪   This restriction means that it will take approximately untill about 2037 for filling Rogun
                           reservoir completely, with the caveat that it could occur faster if some wet years take
                           place during the impoundment period, but could also take longer in the case of a number
                           of dry years.
                       ▪   Overall, water availability in the downstream area will be reduced by the amount
                           retained. However, this is according to regional agreements, and Tajikistan will use this
                           water in any case (for irrigation once Rogun reservoir will be filled). For this reason, this
                           cannot be considered as a negative impact of Rogun.
               o   Operation: Rogun HPP adds a very considerable storage volume to the Vakhsh cascade,
                   increasing the potential for influencing seasonal flow distribution in the river. However, Rogun
                   can and will be operated in a way as not to change the seasonal flow pattern downstream of
                   Nurek. The combined operation of Nurek and Rogun will not shift any additional flows from
                   summer to winter over the amount of 4.2 km3/year already shifted by Nurek operations during
                   the historical period, resulting in no negative impact from Rogun.
      •    Measures: Additional mitigation measures to the ones adopted and described (slow reservoir filling, no
           additional shift of water from summer to winter), are not required. However, it is recommended to pay
           attention also to the following points:
               o   Strict adherence to ICWC annual agreements and BWO water accounting as described.
               o   Close monitoring of water use in the Vakhsh cascade, with direct sharing with BWO
                   headquarters, and making the relevant data publicly available (as agreed in the financing
                   agreement) in the website of the MEWR.
               o   Continuing stakeholder involvement, also by means of public hearings/workshops in the riparian
                   countries.
               o   Rogun would also have the possibility of making additional water available for irrigation in
                   especially dry years. This, however, is possible only if specific measures are foreseen to
                   compensate the releases of additional water and to refill the reservoir after such an event. Since
                   multi-year storage and water use between upstream and downstream countries is not clearly
                   defined in the existing legal frameworks or in the experience of current practices, special
                   negotiations each year, and potential agreements among the riparian countries addressing this
                   will be required. It is recommended that the countries develop a dialogue towards finding viable
                   win-win scenarios around this topic.



                                                                                                                  PUBLIC
Page 46




5.2       Water Quality
      •    Risk: Two phases again:
               o   Construction: a large construction site presents the risk of contaminating the water, by
                   increasing sediment input, suspended sediment load, or (accidentally) contaminating it with
                   hazardous substances (fuel, oil, lubricants etc.). Negative impact of potentially important
                   dimensions.
               o   Operation: the formation of a reservoir can change water quality.
      •    Situation:
               o   Construction: given the very large construction site, the risk for water contamination is real. Even
                   if there are no water users directly downstream of Rogun, this risk must be minimised.
               o   Operation: Under the conditions of Rogun HPP, there is no risk of deteriorating water quality
                   due to submerged biomass or of an eutrophication of the reservoir. Thermal stratification will
                   occur, but since the water from Rogun is directly transferred to the reservoir of Nurek via a short
                   stretch of river (and at a later stage via an additional small reservoir) releasing cold water
                   downstream of the dam will not have any negative consequences.
      •    Measures:
               o   Construction: identified and known risks must be met by precautionary measures (pollution
                   prevention, waste water treatment, storage and handling of hazardous material) and by
                   measures ensuring preparedness in case of an accident. The required measures are described
                   in the ESMP.
               o   Operation: pollution prevention measures will also have to be applied in the operation of the
                   HPP. It would also be advisable to have waste water treatment plants in the settlements located
                   in the catchment of the reservoir; this, however, is not the responsibility of the Project. As
                   mentioned, the risk of wate quality degradation due to submerged vegetation is very small; as
                   an additional precautionary measure it is recommended to fell all trees before submersion, and
                   to burn the biomass not used. This would also ensure that wood, a valuable resource in the
                   project area, can be used.


5.3       Sediment
      •    Risk: retention of sediment in the reservoir, causing changes in sediment dynamics in the downstream
           area.
      •    Situation: presently, all sediment from the upper Vakhsh catchment is retained in Nurek reservoir. There
           is a risk of an increased sediment load in the rived due to the construction of Rogun dam. The 2016
           Wallingford Sedimentation Study shows that from 1970 (impoundment) to 2015, the total storage volume
           of Nurek reservoir decreased from 10 km³ to about 6.2 km³; most of this loss was dead storage, active
           storage was reduced from 4.2 to about 4 km³. Without any further measure the dead storage of Nurek
           would be completely filled be 2075, and by then active storage would be reduced to 3 km³.
      •    Development: Once the filling of Rogun reservoir starts, sediment will be increasingly retained there.
           Since sedimentation threatens negatively affect the operation of Nurek HPP, this sediment retention will
           increase the life span of Nurek, which is a positive impact of Rogun. With Rogun dam in place, inflow of
           sediments to Nurek will be reduced by 97%, the remaining 3% being the sediment originating from the
           intermediate catchment between Rogun and Nurek. The modelling shows that in that case there would
           be no relevant reduction in total storage volume till 2115 (end of modelling period). The study concludes
           that it would take 130 years for filling Rogun reservoir with sediments to a level where regulation in
           Rogun is no longer possible. The TEAS (2014) estimate for total life span of Rogun was 115 years.
      •    Measures: additional sediment input to Vakhsh River due to the construction of Rogun dam must be
           minimised by applying measures to limit erosions, as described in the ESMP (ESIA Vol. 3 B08, Erosion
           Management Plan). During operation, a monitoring program providing a follow-up on reservoir siltation
           (Rogun as well as Nurek) is recommended. It will also be necessary to develop a plan that will allow for
           Nurek to continue to be operated safely once it will be filled with sediment.
The currently accepted sedimentation scenarios are from TEAS (RP 43 rev. C). The project has been designed
and is being constructed according to these scenarios. Sediment inflow calculations during TEAS have been
                                                                                                                PUBLIC
Page 47




largely confirmed by the extensive sediment sampling programme that is run by the Lot-2 Contractor. However,
the DSPoE judged that the updated Hydrology and Climate Change Study under execution should also examine
sediment transport into the reservoir, particularly under climate change conditions. The first draft results are
expected in August 2025.
Furthermore, the DSPoE requested the execution of a detailed modelling study for the prediction of the patterns
of sediment deposition within the reservoir, under different sediment management measures. This study is about
to be awarded. Results should be expected in the Summer of 2026.


5.4       Floods
      •    Risk: storage reservoirs present a potential for reducing flood peaks, in this way minimising the risk of
           floods in the downstream area. On the other hand, they also present a risk of a dam break, which would
           lead to major flooding in the downstream area.
      •    Situation: Nurek is not designed for safely handling the Probable Maximum Flood (PMF). Rogun is
           designed in a way as being able to do so, which is a major positive impact on Nurek, the downstream
           cascade and the wider downstream area. Rogun is also built for maximum dam safety, e.g. for being
           able to resist the Maximum Credible Earthquake (MCE).
      •    Measures: in addition to the adopted design criteria for dam safety, it is also important that the Dam
           Break Study for the final dam crest elevation of 1300 m asl will be prepared, along with the corresponding
           EPP, and that this plan will be implemented.


5.5       Monitoring
Close and continued monitoring will be required for these four main issues directly related to water. This is
outlined here shortly.

5.5.1      Hydrology
This is the central subject for operating a hydropower plant or a cascade of such plants. The following parameters
have to be monitored on a continuous basis:
      •    Construction and reservoir filling phase:
               o   Inflow to Rogun reservoir.
               o   Outflow (separately through turbines and diversion tunnels).
               o   Reservoir level and volume.
               o   Amount of water retained, including time/duration of water retention.
      •    Operation phase:
               o   Inflow: inflow to Rogun reservoir as well as inflow to Nurek reservoir (possibly derived based on
                   outflow data from Rogun dam and HPP).
               o   Outflow/release, including amount turbined, spilling and releases through any other ways
                   (bottom or mid-elevation outlets) that may be available; to be done for all dams/HPPs in the
                   cascade.
               o   Development of any fluctuations in water level and associated water volume in the two main
                   reservoirs Rogun and Nurek.
These are data required for the operation of the cascade and the individual power plants and are presently
recorded already. This monitoring program will be continued and adapted to the evolving situation as required.
In addition to their importance for cascade operation, these data are also required for monitoring compliance
with agreed-upon rules for water use in accordance with BWO / Protocol 566.

5.5.2      Water Quality
Here as well, monitoring will be required during the construction and during the operation period, as outlined
below:

                                                                                                              PUBLIC
Page 48




    •     Construction period:
              o    The most basic and important water quality monitoring program, to be carried out at least
                   monthly during the entire construction period, would be a systematic sampling at two sites, one
                   upstream of the construction site (the best location in this case probably being just a short stretch
                   downstream of the confluence of Surkhob and Obihingou) and one downstream of the
                   construction site, if feasible downstream of the diversion tunnels. The most important
                   parameters to be measured would be temperature, DO (dissolved oxygen, in mg/l and %
                   saturation), pH, suspended solids, total dissolved solids, oil and grease, and coliform bacteria.
                   Any major difference between the upstream and the downstream values would then indicate a
                   potential negative effect caused by the construction site and construction site activities.
              o    Waste water treatment on the construction site: three potential sources of waste water need to
                   be monitored:
                       ▪   Domestic waste water from workers’ camps etc. These waste waters need to be treated
                           in a suitably dimensioned wastewater treatment plant before being released to the river.
                           Compliance with legal requirements needs to be checked by regular monitoring, and
                           corrective action must be taken in case of non-compliance (see ESIA Vol. 3 B04,
                           WWMP (outline wastewater management plan).
                       ▪   Runoff water from workshops, fuel storage ares etc. Such sites need to be equipped
                           with a proper drainage and oil separators, which need to be maintained; runoff to be
                           monitored.
                       ▪   Drainage and washing water from crushing plants, batching plants and washing sites
                           for lorries transporting concrete. This water is likely to have a very high pH, and an
                           abrupt raise in pH of the river water can be toxic for fish. Therefore, it needs to be
                           drained though sedimentation ponds before being released to the river, the sediment
                           from these ponds needs to be removed and stored properly, in a way that it will not
                           reach the river.
    •     Reservoir filling: as mentioned in Section 4.2.2, it is expected that Rogun reservoir will develop a
          seasonal thermal stratification, without however developing a layer of anoxic water in the deeper parts
          of the reservoir. In order survey this situation, is is recommended to develop a monitoring program which
          will measure temperature and oxygen content in the water column from reservoir surface to bottom.
          Ideally, this is done four times a year (January, April, July and October) at two sites in the reservoir, one
          being located just upstream of the dam (fixed point to be maintained throughout the filling phase, but not
          in proximity of diversion tunnel or power intakes), and one in the centre of the reservoir (this point
          gradually moving upstream with the increasing impoundment). This monitoring should be continued into
          the operation phase, until a steady pattern will have been established. The main question here is whether
          there is any risk of anoxic water being delivered to the downstream area.
    •     Operation: a water quality program will have to be prepared and implemented during the initial part of
          the normal operation phase, i.e. once reservoir filling will be completed, for controlling the development
          of water quality in the reservoir.

5.5.3     Sediment
As mentioned in Section 5.3, Rogun dam will reduce sediment input into Nurek reservoir by about 97%, leading
to no relevant sedimentation in Nurek till at least 2115, while filling of Rogun reservoir with sediments will take
about 130 years. An ongoing monitoring of sedimentation in Rogun as well as in Nurek is recommended.

5.5.4     Floods
As mentioned in Section 5.4, adding Rogun HPP to the Vakhsh cascade will considerably reduce the flood risk
in the downstream area since Rogun will be able to safely rout the Probable Maximum Flood (PMF).
A Dam Break Study was carried out for Rogun Dam at elevation 1100 m asl, which showed that such an event
would not overtop Nurek dam and would therefore not have any negative impact on the downstream area. A
study, including an Emergency Preparedness Plan (EPP) for the full dam crest elevation at 1300 m is under
preparation.

Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                          May 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ
14      LANDSCAPE AND VISUAL
14.1.1. This chapter identifies the potential impacts of the Project on landscape (landscape character) and
        visual (visual amenity) receptors in line with UK and EU best practice approaches and uses
        international guidelines to support the approach.
14.1.2. The full landscape and visual baseline is presented in Volume 2, Annex 7.1 (Baseline Conditions)
        and a summary is provided in Volume 1, Chapter 7 (Baseline) of this ESIA. An assessment of the
        potential Project impacts on the landscape character and visual amenity baseline is presented in
        Section 14.4. Where necessary, requirements and/or recommendations for the avoidance of impact,
        impact minimisation and/or long-term management, are incorporated.
14.1.3. For the purpose of this assessment the likely effects of the Project upon the two separate (but
        linked) topics of landscape character and visual amenity are considered. These encompass:
         Landscapes: are an important component of the distinctiveness of any local area; they take their
          character from a combination of elements, including landform, land use and pattern, land
          cover/vegetation, open space and cultural heritage influences; and
         Visual amenity: a view, its components and context can have a great effect on the quality of
          peoples' lives.
        SUPPORTING INFORMATION
        This chapter should be read in conjunction with the following associated documents in this ESIA,
        where other aspects of the landscape are discussed in:
           Volume 1, Chapter 12 (Soils and Geology);
           Volume 1, Chapter 15 (Biodiversity);
           Volume 1, Chapter 17 (Socio-economics); and
           Volume 1, Chapter 20 (Cultural Heritage).

14.2    LEGISLATIVE FRAMEWORK, POLICY AND GUIDANCE
14.2.1. This section assesses the relevant legislation, policy and guidance which includes applicable
        national and international conventions and treaties, international guidance and World Bank
        Environmental and Social Standards (ESS). Refer to Volume 1, Chapter 2 (Legislation and
        Standards) of this ESIA.
14.2.2. This assessment follows international standards (and associated guidance), in particular the World
        Bank Environmental and Social Framework. Whilst none of the ESS standards specifically cover
        landscape or visual amenity, the following documents were considered to help inform this chapter:
         World Bank (2017). Environmental and Social Standard 1 (ESS1): Assessment and Management
          of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts.
         World Bank (2017). Environmental and Social Standard 6 (ESS6): Biodiversity Conservation and
          Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources.
         World Bank (2017). Environmental and Social Standard 8 (ESS8): Cultural Heritage.
         Asian Development Bank Environmental Assessment Guidelines (2003).
         European Investment Bank Environmental and Social Standards (2022).


        ROGUN HYDROPOWER PROJECT - UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT                  PUBLIC |
        Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                  May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 14-1
14.2.3. The National Environmental Action Plan of Tajikistan, 2006 does not reference landscape and visual
        effects specifically but some aspects of the document are related, including the sections on land
        degradation and biological diversity.
14.2.4. In the absence of any existing guidance in Tajikistan specifically relating to landscape and visual
        impact assessment (LVIA), this chapter has been undertaken broadly in accordance with the
        following industry accepted best practice guidance in the UK and Europe and in accordance with EU
        requirements:
         ‘Guidelines for Landscape and Visual Impact Assessment’ (GLVIA), third edition, 2013, published
          by the Landscape Institute and Institute of Environmental Management and Assessment;
         ‘An Approach to Landscape Character Assessment’ 2014 published by Natural England;
         World Business Council for Sustainable Development, Guidelines for Environmental and Social
          Impact Assessment, 2016;
         Landscape Institute: Assessing Landscape Value – A Technical Guidance Note, 02/21; and
         Landscape Institute and Institute of Environmental Management and Assessment, Guidelines for
          Landscape and Visual Impact Assessment, 3rd Edition, 2013.
14.2.5. Other international guidelines that have provided points of reference to the approach and method for
        assessing risk and impacts include the United States Department of the Interior, Visual Resource
        Inventory and Visual Resource Contrast Rating Manual.

14.3    ASSESSMENT METHODOLOGY
        AREA OF INFLUENCE
14.3.1. The Area of Influence (AoI) includes the footprint of the Project and associated components,
        including:
              The Rogun HPP dam and associated construction areas;
              The reservoir area at full supply level (FSL) – extending around 60km upstream of the dam
               site;
              Access roads, ancillary facilities and other core infrastructure:
                  • extending c3km downstream (quarry, stockpiles, batching plant, loam plant)
                  • extending c 6.5km upstream (crushing plants, Webuild camp & offices, batching plant,
                     asphalt plant, stockpiles)
              All retained villages and settlements within 1 km of the FSL.
14.3.2. The Area of Influence (AoI) for the purpose of this assessment is considered to be the maximum
        potential area over which impacts from the Project are likely to be experienced. For this landscape
        and visual impact assessment (LVIA), the AoI extends to 1 km from the Full Supply Level (FSL) of
        the reservoir (1,290 m above mean sea level (m asl)) and 5km downstream (i.e. 1km beyond the
        main working area south of the dam site).
14.3.3. The majority of construction activity occurs at the dam site and within c6.5km upstream and 3km
        downstream. The influence of the heavy machinery, construction, processing plants and similar
        therefore reduces the further upstream or downstream you go. Beyond 6.5km upstream, the main
        activity is an increase in water level as the reservoir fills, with consequent relocation of villages.
        Therefore, from around 6.5 km upstream of the dam site, impacts will typically only result from rising
        water levels, with minimal changes downstream.



        ROGUN HYDROPOWER PROJECT - UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT                    PUBLIC |
        Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                    May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 14-2
14.3.4. Topographical containment is provided by the steep valley sides and mountainous landscape
        surrounding the Project site, which helps to limit the area of influence. As such, a 2km buffer from
        the FSL and from the downstream river channel should be sufficient to identity landscape and visual
        receptors most likely to be affected by the Project. This is based on previous professional
        experience, the 2014 ESIA for the Project, the visual containment of the Project site, and
        professional judgement.
14.3.5. The current, partially completed, dam is considered to represent the existing baseline, therefore the
        assessment of impacts that have already occurred (prior to 2023) are not considered within this
        Chapter. It does, however, result in there being considerable construction activity already present in
        the baseline conditions.
14.3.6. This Chapter considers Project impacts up until about 2039, when the full inundation level (1,290 m
        asl) is due to be reached.
        LANDSCAPE AND VISUAL RECEPTORS
14.3.7. To enable an analysis of potential Project-related impacts on landscape and visual receptors, the
        following receptors have been identified:
              Landscape Receptors:
               −   Landscape Character Area (LCA) 1: Disturbed/ industrial landscape - the Project Site;
               −   LCA 2: Enclosed River Corridor / Riverine villages;
               −   LCA 3: Broader alluvial landscape; and
               −   LCA 4: Larger settlements – Rogun City.
              Visual Receptors:
               − Residential receptors:
                 o Residents in Rogun City (west of the river);
                 o Residents in retained villages alongside the river within 6.5 km upstream of the dam
                    site; and
                 o Residents in retained villages alongside the river beyond 6.5 km upstream of the dam
                    site.
               − Recreational receptors:
                 o Users / visitors to gardens, parks, tourist attractions and outdoor recreational sites on
                    the edge of Rogun City;
                 o Users / visitors to gardens, parks, tourist attractions and outdoor recreational sites in
                    retained villages within 6.5 km upstream and 3km downstream of the dam site; and
                 o Users / visitors to Vodoyem and Izba outdoor hiking areas (south west of dam site).
               − Commercial receptors:
                 o Workers in Rogun City;
                 o Workers in retained villages within 6.5 km upstream of the dam site; and
                 o Workers at the existing construction compounds.
               − Transport receptors:
                 o Users of the M41 highway; and
                 o Users of surrounding roads (including unpaved routes)




        ROGUN HYDROPOWER PROJECT - UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT                   PUBLIC |
        Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                   May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page | 14-3
        QUALITATIVE ASSESSMENT
14.3.8. Conclusions about the sensitivity of receptors, the magnitude of impacts and the significance of
        effects are based on professional judgement through review of qualitative baseline data and
        photography from site work.
        RECEPTOR SENSITIVITY
        Landscape
14.3.9. The assessment of landscape effects relates to the existing physical features of the landscape,
        including patterns of land use, land cover and its aesthetic and perceptual qualities.
14.3.10. Assessing landscape sensitivity is determined through consideration of each landscape receptor’s
         value and its susceptibility to change.
         Indicators of landscape value include any designations attached to the landscape, its condition,
          evidence of recreational activity, and cultural association.
         Landscape susceptibility refers to the ability of the landscape to accommodate the Project without
          undue negative consequences to its baseline. Landscape susceptibility relates to the ability of a
          particular landscape to accommodate the Project.
14.3.11. Overall landscape sensitivity is then determined by employing professional judgment to combine
         and analyse the identified landscape value and susceptibility to determine the overall landscape
         receptor sensitivity.
        The categories of relative sensitivity and their typical characteristics are described in Table 14-1.

         Visual
14.3.13. The visual assessment relates to potential changes to existing views from identified receptors
         (people), e.g., residents, recreational routes, or motorists, as a result of the addition or loss of
         features in the existing view.
14.3.14. Visual receptors are also assessed in terms of the value of the view and susceptibility to change, in
         order to determine their overall sensitivity to the Project.
          Indicators of visual value include whether the view is recognised, e.g. via a planning designation
           or within guidebooks, or is a recognised view in art or literature.
          Visual susceptibility relates to the occupation of those experiencing the view and the extent to
           which their attention or interest is focused on the view.
14.3.15. Overall visual sensitivity is then determined by employing professional judgment to combine and
         analyse the identified visual value and susceptibility to determine the overall visual receptor
         sensitivity.
14.3.16. The categories of relative sensitivity and their typical characteristics are summarised in Table 14-3.

        ASSESSMENT OF IMPACT SIGNIFICANCE
14.3.18. Project impacts have been assessed against the baseline landscape and visual conditions.


        ROGUN HYDROPOWER PROJECT - UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT                                 PUBLIC |
        Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                                 May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                             Page | 14-10
14.3.19. The significance of the effects is the outcome of the interaction of the receptor sensitivity and the
         impact magnitude and indicates the requirements for mitigation. The post-mitigation outcome has
         also been assessed to determine the significance of the residual effect.
14.3.20. The landscape assessment has taken account of direct and indirect changes to existing landscape
         elements, features and key characteristics and evaluates the extent to which these would be lost or
         modified, in the context of their importance in determining the existing baseline character.
14.3.21. The visual assessment has taken account of the likely changes to visual composition, including the
         extent to which new features would distract or screen existing elements in the view or disrupt the
         scale, structure, or focus of the existing view.
14.3.22. The descriptions in Table 14-5 summarize the categories used to assess the magnitude of the
         impact in relation to landscape and visual.
         Table 14-5 – Impact Magnitude Categories

          Magnitude      Description of Landscape impact                  Description of Visual impact
