14.3.23. The receptor sensitivity categories in Table 14-3 and Table 14-5 and the impact magnitude category
         in Table 14-7 combine to provide a qualitative assessment of the resulting landscape and visual
         impacts respectively.
14.3.24. The matrix for assessing significant of effects is shown in Volume 1 Chapter 5 (ESIA
         Methodology) of this ESIA, and effects that are classified as moderate or major are considered to
         be significant effects. Effects classified as minor or negligible are considered to be not significant.

14.4     POTENTIAL IMPACTS
14.4.1. Impacts on landscape character and visual amenity are as follows:
          Temporary and Permanent Changes due to Construction Activities as a result of temporary
           uncharacteristic activity due to construction, machinery, workers, artificial lighting, material
           storage piles, worker facilities and movement of materials during the construction of the dam,
           new section of highway, creation of a reservoir, and change in upstream river profile, and;.
           Construction activity is expected to be completed in 2032. FSL is expected to be reached by
           2038.
          Permanent Changes due to Operational Activities including permanent loss of river
           characteristics (such as sandbars) and permanent higher water level.
14.4.2. Figure 14-1 shows an image of the Nurek dam, located downstream of Rogun dam. It is anticipated
        that, on completion of the Rogun dam construction and reservoir filling, the Rogun site may be
        visually similar to the Nurek dam. Figure 14-2 also shows a photograph of the Rogun dam site in
        2023.




         ROGUN HYDROPOWER PROJECT - UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT                            PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                            May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                        Page | 14-12
        Figure 14-1 - Photograph of Rogun dam site in 2023 (left); aerial view of Nurek Dam
                      downstream of the Rogun (right)




14.4.3. As outlined in Chapter 3: Project Description of this ESIA, the dam construction will be complete
        by 2032 and the Project reservoir will reach the FSL 1,290m asl by approximately 2038, flooding an
        area of 170 km².
14.4.4. The land that will be inundated once FSL is reached is mostly village settlements, grassland
        degraded by farming (grazing), arable land and orchards, including the dekhan farms and kitchen
        gardens, and some areas of Juniper forest.
        CONSTRUCTION
        Landscape
14.4.5. During the construction phase, most of the landscape changes are confined to the dam site and its
        allied infrastructure, such as the construction camp, quarries, storage areas, and from construction
        traffic, working, and movement of people and plant.
14.4.6. The construction site covers an area of 20 sq km. Approximately 1.3 km of the river reach has
        already been blocked for construction of the dam. As a result, water has started to accumulate
        upstream of the dam site, with some areas with low elevation already inundated, as can be seen in
        the comparison images of year 2013 and 2022 (Figure 14-2). This has consequently created a
        reservoir in the baseline scenario.




        ROGUN HYDROPOWER PROJECT - UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT                  PUBLIC |
        Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                  May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                              Page | 14-13
        Figure 14-2 - Rogun Dam site at the resumption of construction in 2013 (Left) and during
        construction in 2022 (Right)




14.4.7. Whilst the wider riverine character of the landscape will be largely retained with mountain backdrops
        and settlements along its course, with the river channel remaining similar downstream, the
        landscape and topography around the dam site will become permanently modified.
14.4.8. There is potential for alterations to the existing baseline character of the landscapes present due to
        the following activities:
         Activity a: Ground and Ground-Level Disturbance Activities & Changes (primarily left bank
          works):
           • Loss of existing vegetation to facilitate the construction of access roads/ bridges, relocated
             construction workers’ accommodation/ compounds. demolition of villages, and rising water
             levels. For the purpose of this assessment the main areas of vegetation loss are considered to
             be scrubby vegetation, grassland, arable land and orchards, and some areas of Juniper forest.
             Removal of vegetation may also result in soil erosion and degradation of adjacent landscape;
           • Abandonment of field systems and demolition of villages may degrade local character;
           • Change to the local topography as a result of temporary stockpiling of materials and
             earthworks during construction of new access road and bridges upstream (left bank works);
           • Continued ground disturbance due to construction of the dam, supporting infrastructure and
             associated ancillary facilities;
           • Changes in access routes and road management for new construction elements (such as left
             bank access road and bridges) with loss of existing bridge crossings and permanent diversion
             of access routes, including the M41 changing the character of the local landscape as
             construction progresses; and
           • Temporary change in land use to accommodate relocated construction compounds and
             construction workers’ accommodation; relocation of Project affected people (PAP) which may
             create abandoned or demolished villages on the edge of the FSL; flooding of existing
             construction areas; and construction of new access roads and bridges upstream creating
             uncharacteristic temporary construction activities and presence of workers, machinery,
             materials, and earth movements in areas not currently undergoing such activity.




        ROGUN HYDROPOWER PROJECT - UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT                    PUBLIC |
        Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                    May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page | 14-14
          Activity b: Construction Activities: :
            • Temporary continuation of manmade activity in the landscape due to the construction camp,
              quarries and storage areas around the dam site;
            • New access roads and bridge connections along the left bank for some 55km will extend the
              working area upstream introducing some uncharacteristic and activity from construction traffic,
              and the working and movement of people, machinery and plant.
          Activity c: Presence of the Reservoir:
            • The filling of the reservoir will result in permanent changes to the river channel upstream,
              increasing the level of the water and permanently changing the river channel. The vegetation
              and river meanders will be submerged by the new reservoir.
14.4.9. All of the above construction activities will collectively impact on the baseline of the landscape
        character receptors identified, although many activities are already present in the landscape. The
        construction impacts are therefore considered together and collectively, to create a single Impact at
        Construction, thus representing the ‘worst case’ scenario.
14.4.10. The Project will result in direct impacts to all LCAs identified and result in changes to the existing
         character of the landscape.
14.4.11. In all instances, landscape impacts are considered to be greatest during the construction phase of
         the Project due to the movement, plant, and works occurring, in addition to the construction of the
         structures. Whilst many of these activities already occur in the baseline (due to construction of the
         dam being on-going), some additional activity will occur. Following construction, the Project will be
         largely static except for rising and fluctuating reservoir water levels and consist of a distinctive
         manmade dam wall in the landscape.
14.4.12. Overall, the Project during construction is considered to have a very small to medium magnitude of
         change on LCAs.
14.4.13. The resulting effects are summarised in Table 14-6.
         Table 14-6 – Assessment of Temporary Changes due to Construction Activities on

         Mitigation and Enhancement Measures
14.4.14. Moderate (significant) effects have been identified for LCA 2: Enclosed River Corridor / Riverine
         villages, where the continuation of construction works and expansion of the reservoir will cause
         noticeable impacts by demolishing villages, bridges and access routes, building of new access
         roads and bridges, and the loss of vegetation, including crops associated with agricultural activity.
14.4.15. The key mitigation will be the cessation of construction activity which will restore much of the
         tranquillity and naturalness back to the local landscape. No landscape specific mitigation is therefore
         identified at construction.
14.4.16. However, mitigation identified for other purposes are summarised in Section 14.5.
         Residual Effects
14.4.17. As no mitigation is identified, the residual effects for sandscape receptors at construction remain the
         same.
         Visual
14.4.18. There is potential for alterations to the existing visual amenity of receptors during construction due to
         the following activities:


         ROGUN HYDROPOWER PROJECT - UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT                         PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                         May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                     Page | 14-16
          Activity a: Ground and Ground-Level Disturbance Activities & Changes (primarily left bank
           works):
            • Visual disruption and change in views due to limited but uncharacteristic temporary
              construction activities and presence of workers, machinery, materials, and earth movements in
              areas not currently undergoing such activity (such as upstream access roads/ bridges on the
              left bank) may impair visual amenity, especially for nearby residents;
            • Temporary change to land use due to the flooding of existing construction areas in order to
              accommodate relocated construction compounds and the construction workers’
              accommodation, and relocation of Project affected people (PAP) which may create some
              abandoned and demolished villages on the edge of the FSL.
          Activity b: Construction Activities: Emissions of Dust:
            • New access roads and bridge connections along the left bank for some 55km will extend the
              working area upstream introducing some uncharacteristic activity from construction traffic, and
              the working and movement of people, machinery and plant and changing the experience and
              visual perception of the view;
          Activity c: Presence of the Reservoir:
            • The construction and introduction of a large dam wall and associated earthworks will change
              the riverine environment upstream with rising water levels; and
            • The filling of the reservoir will result in permanent visual changes to the river channel
              upstream, increasing the level of the water and losing the characteristic features of the river
              channel including areas of scrubby vegetation at the margins.
14.4.19. All of the above construction activities will collectively impact on residential, commercial and
         transport receptors. The above impacts are therefore considered together and collectively, to create
         a single impact during Construction, thus representing the ‘worst case’ scenario.
14.4.20. In all instances, visual effects are considered to be greatest during the construction phase of the
         Project due to the uncharacteristic movement, plant, and works occurring, in addition to the
         construction of the structures. Following construction, the Project will be largely static, although
         water levels within the reservoir will fluctuate, and the dam wall will remain a permanent, distinctive
         manmade feature in some views.
14.4.21. Overall, the Project during construction is considered to have a small to medium magnitude of
         change on the visual amenity of residential, commercial and transport receptors. The resulting
         effects are summarised in Table 14-7.

         Mitigation and Enhancement Measures
14.4.22. Significant effects have been identified for residential, recreational and transport receptors due to
         construction activities resulting in changes to views. The effects of construction activity are already
         part of the baseline conditions due to existing dam construction works, so the main mitigation will be
         completion and removal of the construction activity to create views of a more natural, rural
         landscape
14.4.23. Construction activities relating to traffic management, air quality, and site rehabilitation are managed
         through their respective management plans. For example, the Site Rehabilitation Plan will include
         measures to ensure structures are removed once they are no longer required, helping to ensure
         impacts reduce as construction activities wind down. These activities are summarised in Section
         14.5. As a result, specific mitigation for visual receptors is not required.
         Residual Effects
14.4.24. As no mitigation is identified, the residual effects for visual receptors at construction remain the
         same.




         ROGUN HYDROPOWER PROJECT - UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT                          PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                          May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                      Page | 14-20
         OPERATION
         Landscape
14.4.25. There is the potential for permanent alterations to the landscapes once the Project is operational,
         due to the Presence of the Dam and Reservoir. This includes the following elements:
          Presence of the Dam and Reservoir:
            • An area of river channel, settlements, valley and hillsides will become permanently submerged
              below the new higher water level;
            • Permanent presence of a large working dam potentially resulting in uncharacteristic noise/
              water movements in the landscape whilst the hydroelectric dam is operational;
            • Permanent addition of new, built structures within the landscape, including the dam and
              associated buildings, changing the immediate character of the local landscape;
            • Changes in the textures and colours of the landscapes due to the permanent loss of
              vegetation and river corridor to the dam, dam buildings, and reservoir behind. Such textures
              and colours may be different to those in the surrounding landscapes; and
            • Permanent change to the surrounding landscape with amended access roads/ routes and new
              shoreline.
14.4.26. There is the potential for alterations to the landscapes present once the Project is operational, due
         to the permanent change to the nature of the landscape directly below the footprint and the
         permanent presence of the dam with reservoir behind.
14.4.27. Although the impact in terms of loss of land is notable, the wider area from the perspective of
         landscape and scenic value remains relatively unaffected. The landscape character would still be
         broadly the same, having a river ecosystem surrounded by grasslands and mountains.
14.4.28. Overall, the Project at operation is considered to have a very small to medium magnitude of change
         on LCAs as a result of the permanent presence of the dam and reservoir. The resulting effects are
         summarised in Table 14-8.

         Mitigation and Enhancement Measures
14.4.29. No significant effects on any of the four LCAs have been identified once the Project is operational
         and therefore no mitigation is required.
14.4.30. The local landscape will be enhanced by vegetation planting utilised as part of the soil stabilisation
         strategy, whilst removal of structures once they are no longer required is outlined in the Site
         Rehabilitation Plan. Vegetation planting and removal of structures would both contribute to the
         evolved character of the areas, although over time revegetation would occur naturally.
         Residual Effects
14.4.31. At operation, the reservoir with mountain backdrop will appear natural and scenic, once revegetation
         has softened any abandoned or demolished villages on the edge of the FSL. Residual effects during
         operation are expected to remain minor, negligible or positive for all receptors.
         Visual
14.4.32. There is the potential for permanent alterations to existing views and visual amenity once the Project
         is operational. This includes the following elements:
          Presence of the Dam and Reservoir:

              foreshorten views up the river corridor from downstream locations;
            • Permanent loss of meandering river shoreline upstream;
            • Permanent new section of M41 highway and connecting roads/ bridges in views; and
              Permanent presence of reservoir upstream in views.

14.4.33. There is the potential for alterations to the landscapes present once the Project is operational due to
         the permanent change in landform and land use from natural river corridor to a dam with reservoir
         behind.
14.4.34. Although the impact in terms of loss of land is notable, the wider area in terms of backdrop and
         setting remains relatively unaffected, with a river ecosystem surrounded by grasslands and
         mountains. The identified visual receptors (villagers and travellers on the M41) are relocated to the
         higher reaches and thus they will still experience views of the mountain ranges and riverine valley,
         albeit from a higher altitude.
14.4.35. Overall, the Project at operation is considered to have a neutral to small magnitude of change on

        Mitigation and Enhancement Measures
14.4.36. No significant effects have been identified during operation of the Project and therefore no mitigation
         specific for visual receptors is required.
14.4.37. The Site Rehabilitation Plan will ensure removal of structures once they are no longer in use, and
         establishing vegetation for soil stability would enhance views. These are covered under their
         respective technical management plans and as such, no mitigation is required for visual receptors at
         operation.
        Residual Effects
14.4.38. Residual effects during operation are expected to remain minor or negligible for all receptors.

14.5    MITIGATION AND ENHANCEMENT MEASURES
        CONSTRUCTION
14.5.1. Significant effects are identified during the construction phase on both landscape and visual
        receptors, due to the presence of construction workers, plant, materials, compounds, vehicles and
        the activity associated with the building works. Their removal on completion of the construction
        works will therefore largely remove these adverse impacts. Given the extent of the construction
        area, it cannot be screened. Changing the existing river channel to a reservoir also cannot be
        mitigated, but its new character is similar to that of other dams along the same river network and


        ROGUN HYDROPOWER PROJECT - UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT                          PUBLIC |
        Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                          May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                      Page | 14-25
         therefore not unusual in the landscape. In some instances, a change from a riverine landscape
         backdropped by mountains to a lake backdropped by mountains would be neither a positive nor
         adverse change, just different.
14.5.2. No specific landscape or visual mitigation at construction is therefore identified.
            • Minimise drop height of materials; and
            • Switch off equipment when it is not in use.
          Mitigation sections of the Volume 1, Chapter 12 (Geology and Soils), Chapter 15
           (Biodiversity), Volume 2, Annex 15.1 (Critical Habitat Assessment) and Volume 3 Annex A4
           (Biodiversity Management Plan) of this ESIA include:
            • Translocation of individual plants, macro-turfs or viable reproductive material (such as seeds
              or fruit) out of the inundation zone to sites that don’t already hold important plant
              assemblages/protected species;
            • Planting plans for the purpose of soil stabilisation will be informed by biodiversity specialists to
              maximise biodiversity benefits derived from these plans;
            • Planting for the purposes should be done in consultation with the ECoW (and botany
              specialist, as required) and should comprise native species of local provenance, sourced from
              (or in consultation with) Vakhdat Forest Nursery;
            • Monitoring will also be required to ensure ongoing effectiveness of planting undertaken as part
              of the soil stabilisation mitigation. Planting will be a historically and ecologically suitable native
              species mix, informed by the Ecological Clerk of Works (ECoW);
            • No net loss (NNL) of floodplain habitat and Juniper woodland, with options to deliver NNL
              outlined in the No Net Loss Framework (NNLF) plan such as creation of new floodplain habitat
              upstream of the flooding zone;
            • Replacement woodland planting around the flooding zone and/or in off-site locations along the
              Vakhsh River valley;
            • Avoidance of juniper woodland loss in relation to the left bank road through detailed design;
            • ECoW ‘toolbox talks’ including sensitive use of lighting (such as directional lighting); and
            • Creation of woodland/scrub habitat adjacent to the reservoir for soil stabilisation purposes
              which will help regenerate the existing character of the local landscape.
         OPERATIONAL
14.5.3. No mitigation measures have been identified, although the implementation of the Site Rehabilitation
        Plan and Biodiversity Management Plan will include monitoring of sapling success and a program of
        replacing failed saplings
         RESIDUAL EFFECTS
14.5.4. No landscape or visual specific mitigation is identified at construction, although implementation of
        mitigation measures for soil stability, biodiversity, and air quality may reduce the landscape and
        visual effects of the Project. The residual effects may therefore be slightly different to the pre-
        mitigation effects.
14.5.5. As no mitigation is identified at operation, the residual effects remain the same as the pre-mitigation
        effects.




         ROGUN HYDROPOWER PROJECT - UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT                       PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                       May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                   Page | 14-26
14.6     SUMMARY
14.6.1. The construction phase will result in some new and on-going ground disturbance activities
        (earthworks, storage of materials, excavations), and plant movements, and the relocation of
        construction compounds and construction workers’ accommodation following flooding of existing
        sites, as well as the presence of the reservoir and rising water levels changing the character and
        appearance of the alluvial river corridor. This will result in a significant change to the Project area
        and result in some direct impacts to landscape character and visual amenity.
14.6.2. In all instances, landscape and visual effects are considered to be greatest during the construction
        phase of the Project due to the movement, plant, and works occurring, albeit characteristic in the
        local landscape, and in addition to increasing height of the dam wall and rising water levels in the
        reservoir behind. Whilst no landscape or visual specific migration was identified, the mitigation
        identified for other technical disciplines (including biodiversity, and soils) will be of benefit in reducing
        adverse impacts on landscape and visual receptors, such as planting for soil stabilisation alongside
        the reservoir, and removing unused structures to help soften the visual changes and contribute to
        local character, but the scale and nature of the Project means it cannot be screened during
        construction.
14.6.3. Following construction, the Project will be largely static (albeit with fluctuating river/reservoir levels)
        but will consist of distinctive, higher manmade features in the view and considerably higher water
        levels than are seen in the current baseline. The presence of a reservoir and dam along this river
        are already existing features in the wider landscape, and the removal of construction activity will
        restore much of the existing character and visual amenity. The effects of the Project are therefore
        lessened at operation, so no mitigation was identified at operation.
         CONSTRUCTION EFFECTS
         Ground Disturbance Activities; and Presence of the Reservoir

        residual effects following mitigation.

        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                       Page | 15-1
15        BIODIVERSITY
15.1.1.   This chapter presents an assessment of the potential impacts of the Project upon biodiversity. The
          biodiversity baseline is presented in Volume 2, Annex 7.1 (Baseline Conditions Report) of this
          ESIA and a summary is provided in Volume 1, Chapter 7 (Source of Secondary Baseline) of this
          ESIA. This chapter should also be read in conjunction with Volume 2, Annex 15.1 (Critical Habitat
          Assessment) and Volume 3, Biodiversity Management Plan (BMP) and No Net Loss
          Framework (NNLF).
15.1.2.   The baseline assessed in this chapter represents the current condition after existing and approved
          disturbances, such as the partially-completed dam wall with its current inundation level and Project
          impacts to be assessed include the filling of the reservoir up until the maximum inundation level (i.e.
          to 1290m – hereafter referred to as the ‘flooding zone’) along with other Project components such as
          new roads (including the AIIB-funded left bank road) and stockpiling areas.
15.1.3.   Both terrestrial and aquatic biodiversity impacts are considered within separate Areas of Influence
          (AoI), see Section 15.4.
15.1.4.   Associated facilities, as described in Volume 1, Chapter 3 (Project Description) of this ESIA, are
          assessed in accordance with ESS1 and referenced explicitly where relevant. A full assessment of
          impacts predicted to arise from the most significant of these projects, for example the 500kV
          overhead line connection and new major roads outside of the immediate dam site and flooding zone,
          are addressed within the separate ESIAs produced for these developments. Cross-reference to the
          likely risks and impacts associated with these projects is included within this chapter.
15.1.5.   This assessment has been undertaken in alignment with the World Bank Environmental and Social
          Standard 1 Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (ESS1)
          and World Bank Environmental and Social Standard 6 – Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable
          Management of Living Natural Resources (ESS6). The assessment comprises a brief review of
          existing work undertaken in 2014 to support an ESIA for the Project, followed by further assessment
          undertaken in 2023 that was informed by updated information and with contextual reference to the
          2014 ESIA where relevant.

15.2      LEGISLATIVE FRAMEWORK, POLICY AND GUIDANCE
15.2.1.   This section outlines the legislation, policy and guidance considered as part of the assessment,
          which includes applicable national and international conventions and treaties, international guidance
          and World Bank Environmental and Social Standards (ESS). See also Volume 1, Chapter 2
          (Legislation and Standards) of this ESIA.
15.2.2.   The overall strategy with regards to nature conservation in Tajikistan is underpinned by the country’s
          constitution, adopted in November 1994, that guarantees the right of citizens to a healthy
          environment. Since independence in 1991, numerous specific acts of legislation applicable to
          biodiversity have been adopted; those of most relevance to this chapter are as follows:
           Forest Code of the Republic of Tajikistan (2011)
           Water Code of the Republic of Tajikistan (2020)
           Law "On Environmental Protection" (2011) (which importantly includes protection of species listed
            on the Tajikistan Red Book).


          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                             Page | 15-2
           Law "On Ecological Information" (2011)
           Law "On Protection of the Air" (2012)
           Law "On the Protection and Use of Flora" (2004)
           Law "On Fauna" (2008)
           Law "On Soil Protection" (2009)
           Law "On Special Protected Natural Territories" (2011)
           Law "On Environmental Impact Assessment" (2017)
           Law "On Hunting and Hunting Economy" (2014)
15.2.3.   In addition to the above, Tajikistan has ratified or signed the following major environmental
          agreements related to natural resources:
           Convention on Biological Diversity (1992) - Ratified in 1997.
           Convention on Wetlands on International Importance as Waterfowl Habitat (Ramsar) - Ratified in
            2001.
           Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species (CITES) - Ratified in 2016.
           Bonn Convention on the Conservation of Migratory Species of Wild Animals - Ratified in 2000).
15.2.4.   The above conventions are referenced where relevant within this assessment.
15.2.5.   This assessment follows international standards (and associated guidance), in particular the World
          Bank Environmental and Social Framework and the following documents were used to inform this
          chapter:
           World Bank (2017). Environmental and Social Standard 1 (ESS1): Assessment and Management
            of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts.
           World Bank (2017). Environmental and Social Standard 6 (ESS6): Biodiversity Conservation and
            Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources.
           World Bank (2018). Guidance Note for Borrowers. ESS6: Biodiversity Conservation and
            Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources.
           European Investment Bank. (2018). Guidance Note for Environmental and Social Standard 3 on
            Biodiversity and Ecosystems.
           International Finance Corporation (IFC). 2019. International Finance Corporation’s Guidance
            Note 6: Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources.
           Hardner, K., Gullison, R. E., Anstee, S. & Meyer, M. 2015. Good Practices for Biodiversity
            Inclusive Impact Assessment and Management Planning.
15.2.6.   Where appropriate, the most stringent of the relevant Lender requirements is adopted, and this is
          referenced accordingly. Similarly, some Lenders (such as EIB) adhere to European Union
          legislation, even outside of this jurisdiction, where EIB require ‘…projects in other countries must
          provide the necessary assurance that similar outcomes can be achieved to those that would be
          required under EU legislation, using methods or procedures that broadly align with those specified in
          relevant EU instruments.’ Relevant examples include:



          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                 SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                              Page | 15-3
           Council Directive 92/43/EEC of 21 May 1992 on the conservation of natural habitats and of wild
            fauna and flora (the ‘Habitats Directive’); and
           Directive 2009/147/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 30 November 2009 on
            the conservation of wild birds (the ‘Birds Directive’).
15.2.7.   In the cases of the above Directives, their application is considered at a level proportionate to the
          species or receptor in question, recognising how the Project is located outside of geographies for
          which these Directives are intended and therefore not always explicitly transferable. For example,
          the level of protection afforded to a species in Europe will reflect the species’ scarcity or threatened
          status within Europe, which may be very different from the population status elsewhere.
15.2.8.   In the absence of suitable national guidance, the approach to ecological assessment is broadly
          underpinned by the methodology outlined by the Chartered Institute of Ecology and Environmental
          Management (CIEEM, 2018).

15.3      ASSESSMENT METHODOLOGY
15.3.1.   This section provides detail on the approach adopted in assessing potential impacts to biodiversity
          from the Project. This should be read in conjunction with Volume 1, Chapter 5 (ESIA
          Methodology) of this ESIA, which describes the broad approach to impact assessment that has
          been implemented within this ESIA. Impact assessment methods specific to biodiversity also include
          methods described in CIEEM (2018), such as confidence level thresholds, and Hardner et al.
          (2015).
          RECEPTOR SENSITIVITY
15.3.2.   The sensitivity of ecological resources has been valued by considering both resources that have
          been designated for their nature conservation interest and the use of professional judgement to
          determine biodiversity values, including any social, community and economic values of ecological
          resources. The valuation has made use of available guidance and information and considered the
          distribution or status of the species or features. Where uncertainty exists, or where features cannot
          be valued with confidence due to the acknowledged limitations, a higher sensitivity category has
          been applied as a precautionary approach. The categories in Table 15-1 have been used.

          Table 15-1 – Receptor Sensitivity Evaluation Categories

                                                     of sufficient value as to trigger Critical Habitat

15.3.3.   To focus the assessment on those receptors of greatest ecological value, only those receptors that
          are categorised as Low sensitivity and above have been considered through the assessment
          process. Hereafter these are referred to as sensitive receptors.
          IMPACT MAGNITUDE
15.3.4.   The assessment of the impact magnitude considers:
           The type of impact, i.e., positive, negative or neutral.
           The size (spatially), value and sensitivity of the receptor.
           The duration, magnitude and extent of the impacts.
           The timing and frequency of the impacts.
           The ability of the affected receptor to recover from temporary impacts and timescale of recovery.
           The potential for implementation of, and effectiveness of, additional mitigation or enhancement
            measures.
           The level of confidence in these predictions (i.e. certainty over impact materialising as described).
          The criteria used for assessing impact magnitude in the assessment are described in Table 15-2.




          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                      SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                   Page | 15-5
          Table 15-2 – Impact Magnitude Criteria
           Magnitude       Criteria
           Large           Large effect on the nature conservation status of the site, habitats or species,
                           likely to threaten the long-term integrity or viability of the receptor.
           Medium          Medium effect on the nature conservation status of the site, habitats or species,
                           but would not threaten the long-term integrity of the system.
           Small           Noticeable effects, but either of sufficiently small scale or short duration to cause
                           no harm to the conservation status of the site, habitats or species.
           Very Small      If noticeable, then negligible effects, either of sufficiently very small scale or short
                           duration to cause no harm to the conservation status of the site, habitats or
                           species
           No Change       Not expected to affect the conservation status of the site, habitats or species
                           under consideration in any way, therefore no noticeable effects on the ecological
                           resource.
           Beneficial      A positive effect.

          EFFECT SIGNIFICANCE
15.3.5.   The final stage of the assessment uses the value as obtained with reference to Table 15-1 and
          Table 15-2.
15.3.6.    In order to provide a qualitative assessment of the resulting effects. The matrix for assessing
          significance of effects is shown in Volume 1, Chapter 5 (ESIA Methodology) of this ESIA and
          effects that are classified as moderate or major are considered significant effects. Effects classified
          as minor and negligible are not considered significant.
15.3.7.   For effects identified as significant, and where possible, additional mitigation is required to reduce
          residual effects to an acceptable level. In the absence of feasible additional mitigation, the effect will
          remain significant. Mitigation is also described where measures are required to achieve compliance
          with Tajik legislation and/or Lender standards (e.g. EIB’s requirements, see 15.2.6 )
          CRITICAL HABITAT / NATURAL HABITAT
15.3.8.   Adherence to ESS6 mandates that No Net Loss (NNL), and for Critical Habitat a Net Gain (NG),
          must be achieved for any Critical Habitat / Natural Habitat values identified as being adversely
          affected by the Project. Any materially adverse impacts (i.e. those classified as minor and above)
          will be subject to mitigation, as if they were considered significant.
15.3.9.   ESS6 defines Natural Habitat as: ‘Natural habitats are areas composed of viable assemblages of
          plant and/or animal species of largely native origin, and/or where human activity has not essentially
          modified an area’s primary ecological functions and species composition.’ While Critical Habitat is
          defined as: ‘…areas with high biodiversity importance or value, including:
          (a) Habitat of significant importance to Critically Endangered or Endangered species, as listed in the
          IUCN Red List of threatened species or equivalent national approaches;
          (b) Habitat of significant importance to endemic or restricted-range species;
          (c) Habitat supporting globally or nationally significant concentrations of migratory or congregatory
          species;


          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                      SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                   Page | 15-6
          (d) Highly threatened or unique ecosystems; and )
          (e) Ecological functions or characteristics that are needed to maintain the viability of the biodiversity
          values described above in (a) to (d).’
15.3.10. Volume 2- Chapter 15.1 includes the Critical Habitat Assessment (CHA) for the project. This CHA
          has concluded that there are no areas of Critical Habitat within the Project AoI.


          ASSUMPTIONS AND LIMITATIONS
15.3.11. Given that this ESIA is an update to the 2014 ESIA the biodiversity baseline collection for this
          Project has relied more heavily upon desk-based study and consultation than would be the case for
          a ‘new’ ESIA. Field survey effort during 2023 was targeted and does not represent a full suite of
          surveys for any receptor; however, when considered collectively with the extensive desk study
          information available (including that derived from the 2014 ESIA studies) and within the context of a
          heavily modified AoI across large parts of which the biodiversity assemblage is heavily depleted, is
          considered proportionate to the predicted impacts from the Project (in line with ESS1). Additionally,
          ongoing monitoring has been recommended to increase and update the baseline understanding
          going forward, which also helps to manage potential data validity issues associated impacts that will
          occur incrementally over a number of years. Further detail on baseline limitations is provided within
          Volume 2, Annex 7.1 (Baseline Conditions Report) of this ESIA.

15.4      SCOPE OF ASSESSMENT
15.4.1.   Two separate spatial areas are referenced throughout this assessment, reflecting both the extent of
          impacts associated with the Project and the consideration of a landscape or ecosystem-level area,
          within which a broadly uniform biodiversity assemblage could feasibly be present. This ecosystem
          level approach allows for biodiversity receptors to be assessed within the context of an appropriate
          biogeographical range, and it also serves to inform those biodiversity receptors likely to be present
          within the Project Area of Influence (AoI), as part of an initial screening process. The two separate
          spatial areas are described within the following sections.
          AREA OF INFLUENCE
          Terrestrial Biodiversity
15.4.2.   The AoI is a widely used concept to describe the maximum potential area over which impacts from
          the Project will be experienced. For the purposes of this assessment, the AoI initially extended to the
          end of the Vakhsh catchment beyond the flooding zone, within which an impact from displaced
          biodiversity and humans can reasonably be expected to occur; e.g., territorial fauna being displaced
          into neighbouring territories, livestock farmers being displaced into previously unused areas, etc.
          Beyond this distance, and in the absence of any evidence to the contrary, it is assumed that any
          Project influence beyond the AoI will be negligible.
15.4.3.   As the studies and assessment developed and the project description was refined, an updated AoI
          was defined, specific to various receptors/receptor groups. This is defined within Table 15-3 And is
          summarised as follows:




          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                   SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page | 15-7
          Terrestrial Biodiversity
15.4.4.   The terrestrial ecology AoI extends across the Project site, flooding zone to FSL, footprint of the left
          bank road/bridges and 100m ‘disturbance zone’ around these areas (i.e., where fauna may be
          displaced). The AoI is extended to 500m when considering the potential spread of invasive plant
          species.
          Aquatic Biodiversity
15.4.5.   From an aquatic biodiversity perspective, the AoI has been defined as Vakhsh River upstream of the
          Nurek Reservoir, to its full capacity at 1290m asl. (Novabad area) and includes tributaries directly
          impacted by the flooding zone or resettlement. To account for these tributaries, a 2km buffer was
          included. As the project description was refined, an updated AoI was defined, incorporating the
          footprint of the left bank road/bridges and 100m ‘disturbance zone’ to account for any tributary
          crossings.
          ECOLOGICALLY APPROPRIATE AREA OF ANALYSIS (EAAA)
15.4.6.   This assessment follows industry guidance and focuses upon impacts associated with the Project,
          within an area that is ecologically linked to the functioning of that receptor. This is defined as the
          ecologically appropriate area of analysis (EAAA) and in most cases will comprise a continuous
          habitat or ecosystem area, within which the ecology is functionally linked.
15.4.7.   From an aquatic biodiversity perspective, two distinct EAAAs have been defined based on
          connectivity within the Vakhsh River system. The first EAAA (downstream) comprising the Nurek
          Reservoir and the reach of river between the Nurek Reservoir and Rogun Dam, while the second
          EAAA (upstream) compromises the Vakhsh River upstream of the Rogun Dam to Damburacha,
          which is approximately 100 km upstream from full supply level at an elevation of 500 m above FSL.
15.4.8.   For species that were screened as part of the Critical Habitat Assessment, where the Project AoI fell
          outside of the known geographic extant but were included at the request of key stakeholders, the
          EAAA includes the mapped geographic extant for the species as described in Volume 2, Annex
          15.1 (Critical Habitat Assessment).
          SCOPING SUMMARY
15.4.9.   In order to ensure the assessment remains proportionate and fully cognisant of relevant assessment
          conclusions presented within the 2014 ESIA, it is considered appropriate to define a concise scope
          of the assessment for this ESIA. For clarity on this approach, Table 15-3 details those
          receptors/impacts that have been considered and their inclusion/exclusion, together with associated
          justification and detail of the AoI and EAAA being considered.




          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |

15.4.10. Table 15-4 provides receptor evaluation for those receptors confirmed a being present/potentially
        present across the AoI and scoped in as per Table 15-3. This evaluation process should be read in
        conjunction with Volume 2, Annex 15.1 (Critical Habitat Assessment) of this ESIA.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                            Page | 15-17
Table 15-4 – Receptor Evaluation

 Receptor            Value /          Justification
                     Sensitivity

 Floodplain          Medium           Most of the floodplain habitat across the AoI is heavily modified and lacking any notable species or species composition that
 habitat                              would signify Natural Habitat (as per ESS6). Two notable exceptions are found upstream of the confluence of the Vakhsh and
                                      Obikhingou rivers (one within each channel). The example within the Vakhsh River comprises deposited materials that appear
                                      to have altered the braided channel here, creating a ‘new’ floodplain which exhibits far more natural habitat composition. Given
                                      low levels of modification, these floodplain extents area considered to be Natural Habitat and therefore of Medium value.

 Juniper             Medium           The juniper woodland recorded from the AoI is a remnant feature of habitat that would have been extensive in the past. It
 woodland                             shows some signs of modification (from grazing/animal movements and firewood collection), but not to the extent that its
                                      integrity as a functioning habitat is compromised. It is therefore considered to be Natural Habitat as per ESS6 and therefore of
                                      Medium value.

 Sensitive flora     Medium           The baseline studies identified 20 TRB species as being present/potentially present across the AoI (no IUCN Red List species
                                      are considered present within the AoI). From the available literature reviewed, it appears that all of these species have habitat
                                      preferences that are generally lacking within the AoI, and therefore any records of presence/potential presence are likely to be
                                      from remnant populations that would have previously been more widespread (i.e. prior to the extensive habitat modification
                                      that has occurred across the AoI). In particular in relation to the four of the five species identified during field surveys within the
                                      AoI, Fritillaria eduardii (TRB CR) prefers elevations above 1200m asl, and within wooded habitats; Crocus korolkowii (TRB EN)
                                      prefers more natural meadows/grassland without significant modification; Juno Nicolai (TRB EN) prefers mixed forest habitat;
                                      and Tulipa praestans (TRB CR) prefers broadleaved woodland on stony slopes. Vitex agnus-castus (TRB CR) has a more
                                      generalist habitat preference, although is largely lacking from the AoI due to ongoing collection of plants for firewood and
                                      pharmaceutical use; the species has been identified from gravelly slopes where its persistence is presumed to relate to its
                                      relative inaccessibility here. In addition, the most recent distribution maps of these species suggests that the Vakhsh River
                                      valley is not a core area for these plants, with most of their distribution occurring further west and south (as per Nowak &
                                      Nobis, 2020). Two other TRB species, the VU Cousinia leptocampyla and C. corymbose, while not identified within the AoI
                                      during field surveys, are associated with juniper woodland and so assumed as present in these Natural Habitat areas. A total of
                                      seven sensitive species are therefore considered as being present/potentially present within the AoI.
                                      It’s considered likely that the populations within the AoI will not be of significant importance within the context of the wider
                                      population; however, in the absence of robust evidence to corroborate this (these species are all relatively under-studied), a
                                      precautionary value of Medium is considered appropriate here.




Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                                                                                      PUBLIC |
Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                                                                                     SEPTEMBER 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                                                                                  Page | 15-18
 Receptor            Value /          Justification
                     Sensitivity

                                      None of these species are considered to trigger Critical Habitat as per ESS6.

 Notable reptile     Low              Four species of reptile were recorded during baseline studies that are on the TRB (two VU; two EN), including one also listed
 species                              as VU by IUCN (Asian tortoise Testudo horsfieldii). None of these were found within the AoI, although their presence is
                                      considered likely in places given the broad habitat tolerances of these species. There is no indication that the reptile
                                      populations recorded within the AoI are of particular importance within the context of the wider population, with species either
                                      being common and widespread, regionally and beyond, or rare within the AoI. On this basis, a value of Low is considered
                                      appropriate.
                                      None of these species are considered to trigger Critical Habitat as per ESS6.

 Notable mammal      Low              Baseline studies recorded 12 species of mammal that are on the TRB (10 listed as VU; two as EN – snow leopard and least
 species                              weasel; no CR), with one also having IUCN Red List classification (snow leopard: VU). However, only three of these were
                                      encountered during field surveys in 2023 (least weasel, otter and Savi’s pipistrelle), and a number of the more sensitive
                                      species are not considered present within the updated AoI given their respective habitat preferences. None of the species
                                      considered present/potentially here have populations across their EAAA that are considered to be greater than of local
                                      importance and therefore a value of Low is considered appropriate.
                                      None of these species are considered to trigger Critical Habitat as per ESS6.

 Breeding birds      Low              Baseline studies have not identified any particularly important breeding bird populations being supported by habitats within the
                                      AoI, and the habitats do not appear to offer optimal conditions for birds aside from relatively widespread (albeit TRB VU)
                                      species such as blue whistling thrush and Indian paradise flycatcher that inhabit scrubby and rural areas, and white-capped
                                      water-redstart that inhabits the riparian zone around fast-flowing watercourses. Given the abundance of the habitats present,
                                      and the number of territories of these species that may be supported here a value of Low is considered appropriate on a
                                      precautionary basis.
                                      None of these species are considered to trigger Critical Habitat as per ESS6.

 Migrating birds     Low              Baseline studies (undertaken in spring and autumn 2023) have not identified any particularly important migratory bird
                                      populations being supported by habitats within the AoI, and the habitats do not appear to offer optimal conditions for birds that
                                      will stop on their migration through the Vakhsh River valley. However, it is possible that the floodplain habitats and exposed
                                      braided channel in particular, might be of use to roosting/resting birds and value of Low is considered appropriate on a
                                      precautionary basis.

                                      None of these species are considered to trigger Critical Habitat as per ESS6.

 Aquatic             Medium           Given that the Vakhsh River has been fragmented by 5 dams, including the Nurek Reservoir, any long-range fish migrations
 Biodiversity                         that would have occurred between the Amu Darya or the lower parts of Vakhsh River to its headwaters have already been
 (fish)                               interrupted. Given this level of modification to the longitudinal connectivity across the EAAA, and acknowledging the presence
                                      of Amu Darya trout and Turkestan Catfish (TRB: VU) within the Study Area, a value of Medium is considered appropriate.

 Aquatic             Low              The aquatic habitat across the AoI has been given a value of Low as a result of the above-mentioned interruption in
 Biodiversity                         longitudinal connectivity, the presence of non-native species, and there were no IUCN listed aquatic invertebrates identified. It
 (general)                            Vakhsh River within the AoI is therefore not considered to be Natural Habitat as per ESS6.

Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                                                                              Page | 15-20
15.5      POTENTIAL IMPACTS AND EFFECTS
15.5.1.   This section comprises a precautionary assessment of the impacts upon sensitive receptors that
          require consideration through the assessment process.
15.5.2.   The basis for receptor evaluation is derived from ESS6, which requires receptors of increased value
          (Natural Habitats and Critical Habitats) to be identified and appropriately assessed in order that a
          project can demonstrate compliance. These classifications represent the most important biodiversity
          values (in the case of Critical Habitat, the very highest), which require particular conditions to be met
          before projects can proceed in areas where such values are present. A detailed assessment of
          Critical Habitat and Natural Habitat is presented in Volume 2, Annex 15.1 (Critical Habitat
          Assessment) of this ESIA.
15.5.3.   For each impact, the assessment considers the sensitive receptors that will be affected, and how
          this will materialise, before considering the effect that will result. Mitigation is then applied where
          appropriate in order that effects are reduced to a level that is considered acceptable, with
          enhancement measures also described. Measures to deliver compliance for a particular Lender’s
          requirements (such as protection of species listed on EU Habitats Directive – see 15.2.6) are also
          listed.
15.5.4.   Given that there is significant overlap between the construction and operation phases of the Project,
          it is necessary to define activities under each of these phases from which impacts will derive and be
          assessed within the following sections.
          CONSTRUCTION
15.5.5.   The activities that will be considered under the construction phase will be:
             Clearance activities across the flooding zone, left bank road and associated facilities, and
              ongoing inundation up to Full Supply Level (FSL)
             Construction activities at the HPP site and associated facilities
             Current HPP operation and maintenance
          OPERATION
15.5.6.   The activities that will be considered under the operation phase:
             Operational reservoir regime including annual drawdown (c. 30m) and associated facilities
             Reservoir at FSL
          POTENTIAL IMPACTS
15.5.7.   Impacts are defined within the context of the Project as the direct or indirect interferences produced
          by the Project’s actions on the environment, able to influence the state or quality of one or more
          ecological receptors. It should be noted that many impacts have already materialized to a certain
          degree as a result of the existing disturbances, such as the partially-completed dam wall with its
          current inundation level.
15.5.8.   Impacts assessed in relation to the biodiversity receptors (and the phases that they’ll occur) include:




          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                    PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                   SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page | 15-21
          Construction
          Clearance activities across the flooding zone and associated facilities, and ongoing
          inundation up to FSL
           Loss/degradation of habitat and species
           Direct mortality/drowning of sensitive fauna
           Encroachment / introduction of invasive species
          Construction activities at the HPP site and associated facilities
           Disturbance and injury/killing of fauna as a result of construction activities
           Release of pollutants into the river
          Current HPP Operation and Maintenance
           Entrainment and Impingement of fish through the turbines
           Changes to downstream flow regimes
          Operation
          Operational reservoir regime including annual drawdown (c. 30m) and associated facilities
           Mortality/displacement of sensitive fauna from new roads and overhead transmission lines
           Release of pollutants into the river
           Entrainment and Impingement of fish through the turbines
           Changes to downstream flow regimes
          Reservoir at FSL
           Impacts from increased human pressures (e.g. grazing, hunting, firewood collection, fishing, etc.):
           Change in habitat composition
           Loss of tributaries and short-range migration
15.5.9.   The following assessments are made with reasonable confidence unless otherwise stated.
15.5.10. These impacts will be assessed against each relevant sensitive receptor as per the scoping table
          (Table 15-3). Following each assessment, specific mitigation and enhancement measures are
          described before concluding with an updated residual effect.

15.6      CONSTRUCTION
          CLEARANCE ACTIVITIES AND ONGOING INUNDATION
          Loss/degradation of habitats
15.6.1.   The clearance activities across the flooding zone, left bank road and resettlement sites have the
          potential to result in the loss of important habitat and habitat/features relied upon by sensitive
          biodiversity. The ongoing reservoir inundation will result in the complete loss of habitats and features
          across the flooding zone and may alter functionality of neighbouring habitats through fragmentation
          and disruption of current ecological processes, causing habitat degradation in these areas.



          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                 SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                              Page | 15-22
15.6.2.   The State Forestry Agency (SFA) are undertaking an exercise to clear the flooding zone in advance
          of inundation. This will comprise removal of trees and woody scrub, as well as remaining built
          structures (much of these have already been removed as part of the resettlement that has already
          occurred).
15.6.3.   The new AIIB-funded left bank road and associated bridges will extend for approximately 55km and
          will result in the clearance of habitats across its route within an expropriation corridor (not yet
          defined but assumed to be 50m width to allow for the new road and associated working area).
          Additionally, the new road will result in fragmentation and severance of habitats along its route.
15.6.4.   Similarly, associated facilities will require land clearance, which will adversely impact upon the
          habitat assemblage present, and any species supported by these habitats.
15.6.5.   Continued flooding until maximum inundation level of 1290m will result in the loss of terrestrial
          habitats within the flooding zone. Additionally, the flooding will result in the permanent and
          irreversible loss of plants, and features used by sensitive biodiversity across the flooding zone as
          they become submerged. This impact will be realised on an incremental basis, over a number of
          years.
15.6.6.   It is acknowledged that a new reservoir in the landscape is a change in habitat composition with
          respect to the existing river and the surrounding terrestrial habitats and will be an impact that
          materializes incrementally. The filling of the dam will result in the loss of surrounding terrestrial
          habitats as well as a loss in lotic (river) habitat as the Vakhsh River will be transformed into a lentic
          (lake) system, adversely affecting those species reliant on the flowing waters within the AoI. This
          impact is assessed singularly under the ‘Reservoir at FSL’ section (15.7.43 onwards).
          Impact Assessment
          Sensitive Habitats
15.6.7.   The only habitats scoped in for assessment are two discrete floodplains, and juniper woodland
          habitats, with all other habitats being heavily modified and not of requisite value for consideration (as
          a habitat receptor in their own right). Most of the floodplain habitats across the AoI are already
          degraded as a result of ongoing/historic modification. The floodplains at Mehrobod and most of the
          floodplain extent upstream of the confluence of the Vakhsh and Obikhingou rivers have been heavily
          modified as a result of ongoing human influence from settlements, cultivation/localised agriculture
          and widespread grazing use. Two exceptions to this prevalence of degradation are discrete
          floodplain areas; one within the Obikhingou River immediately upstream of the confluence with the
          Vakhsh River, and one approximately 6km upstream of this confluence where it is likely that recent
          geomorphological changes have resulted in a ‘new’ floodplain area that has yet to experience any
          material human influence and therefore supports habitat that appears to be largely unmodified. It is
          therefore considered appropriate to classify both as Natural Habitat as per ESS6 (see Volume 2,
          Annex 15.1 (Critical Habitat Assessment) of this ESIA).
15.6.8.   The clearance activities and ongoing inundation from the Project will result in the permanent and
          irreversible loss of the two Natural Habitat floodplains within the flooding zone footprint; this
          comprises approximately 77ha and a relative loss of 100% of the habitat’s occurrence within its
          EAAA. This is anticipated to occur around 2027 when 1185m inundation level is reached and
          represents a large impact magnitude that is permanent and irreversible and will result in an adverse
          effect that is major (significant). This assessment is made with certain confidence.



          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                     PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                    SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 15-23
15.6.9.   The woodland habitat within the AoI is restricted to remnant areas of juniper woodland on the left
          bank of the Vakhsh River that represent the last remaining fragments of a once common habitat in
          the landscape. This is a habitat that shows some signs of modification through grazing pressure and
          some tree loss but still retains of sufficient natural character (especially in terms of species diversity
          and composition) to be classified as Natural Habitat as per ESS6 at this stage (see Volume 2,
          Annex 15.1 (Critical Habitat Assessment) of this ESIA).
15.6.10. The ongoing inundation and associated clearance activities across the flooding zone will result in the
          permanent and irreversible loss of approximately 160ha of habitat type (when also considering
          removal of trees across an assumed 25m buffer zone from the 1290m level). In addition, the left
          bank road will result in the permanent and irreversible loss of approximately 28ha of this habitat. In
          total, 188ha of this habitat will be lost, which is a relative loss of approximately 18% of the habitat’s
          occurrence within its EAAA. This is anticipated to occur between the years 2034-36 (depending on
          the left bank road construction programme), allowing for pre-flooding clearance and the anticipated
          maximum inundation level being reached around 2038 and represents a medium impact magnitude
          that will result in an adverse effect upon this receptor that is moderate (significant). This
          assessment is made with certain confidence.
          Sensitive Flora
15.6.11. Seven species of sensitive flora (i.e., listed on TRB; there were no species identified that are on the
          IUCN Red List) were recorded during baseline surveys, considered to represent remnant
          populations persisting in sub-optimal habitats for these species.
15.6.12. The ongoing flooding will result in the permanent and irreversible loss of notable plants within the
          flooding zone, while the impact of clearance activities across this area may include loss of Vitex
          agnus-castus plants (TRB CR), given that this species can grow to beyond 2m and therefore
          represents a scrub/small tree that may be included within SFA clearance activities. Additionally, it is
          possible that sensitive flora may be lost during construction of the left bank road.
15.6.13. The EAAA for all notable plants represents sub-optimal habitat conditions (with those plants
          identified considered to be remnant populations), which extends across a much wider area Table
          15-4), and the loss of some specimens within the AoI is not considered to materially affect any
          species’ conservation status beyond this extent. With regards Vitex agnus-castea, a single
          population of this species was recorded immediately upstream of Chorsada, on gravelly slopes,
          which will feasibly be lost in its entirety on the assumption that clearance activities will remove all
          plants within this area. Although this species is listed as CR on the TRB; a more recent review of the
          species’ conservation status (Nowak & Nobis 2020) has classified it as Least Concern (LC) within
          Tajikistan and so there is uncertainty of the species’ genuine conservation status. Additionally, the
          range and distribution of all these species within Tajikistan is predominately outside of the AoI (in
          particular to the west and south of the country), which suggests greater habitat suitability is present
          elsewhere. With this in mind, the impact is considered to be small, resulting in an adverse effect that
          is minor (not significant).
15.6.14. With regards Vitex agnus-castea, given its predicted total loss within the AoI, the Project impact is
          precautionarily considered to be large, permanent and irreversible, resulting in an effect that is
          major (significant).




          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                    PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                   SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page | 15-24
         Sensitive Fauna
15.6.15. Baseline surveys resulted in a range of sensitive fauna species being present/potentially present
         from the AoI (and wider study area), as follows:
          38 species of mammals, 12 of these being of increased conservation interest (e.g., by virtue of
           TRB or IUCN Red List status)
          13 species of reptiles, four species of increased conservation interest. No amphibians of
           increased conservation interest.
          158 bird species, 18 species of increased conversation interest
15.6.16. The main source of potential impact to sensitive fauna (notable mammals, reptiles and breeding
         birds) is predicted to be the ongoing clearance activities within the flooding zone and across the left
         bank expropriation corridor, together with ongoing flooding across the flooding zone which will result
         in the losses of important habitat/features and the displacement of sensitive fauna. Given the
         typically small home ranges of these animals, this may result in displacement and population losses
         at a local scale as suitable habitat/features are lost. However, the animals recorded from the
         baseline studies occupy a varied suite of habitats and are unlikely to be dependent upon any
         particular habitat/feature; it is therefore reasonable to assume that they will be readily displaced
         during clearance activities to other habitats (i.e. outside of the flooding zone).
15.6.17. Reptiles are particularly sensitive to impacts to, and losses of, hibernacula, given the particular
         environmental conditions that these features provide. Such features generally comprise rock piles,
         log piles, burrows, and are required to be offer stable conditions over winter months when reptiles
         are inactive. Similarly, roosting bats are also sensitive to loss of roost features/habitat and there is
         the potential that mature trees (most likely present within the juniper woodland identified), built
         structures and crevices within rocks/cliffs may support bat roosts. In both cases, the clearance
         activities and flooding have the potential to result in losses of these features and displacement of
         animals.
15.6.18. With regards migratory birds, it is feasible that the braided channels are of use to migratory birds as
         a stopover habitat when exposed; however, no observations of such behaviour were made during
         any of the surveys undertaken (during 2014 ESIA surveys, or spring/autumn surveys undertaken in
         2023), and given the predicted lack of much food resource here (see the baseline description within
         Volume 2, Annex 7.1 of this ESIA), it is reasonable to conclude that any such use of these
         floodplains is not considered to be of significant importance.
15.6.19. Although this impact will be constant, permanent and irreversible, all of the sensitive species
         recorded (excluding bats and migratory birds) occupy a varied suite of habitats and are unlikely to
         be dependent upon any particular habitat/feature; it is therefore reasonable to assume that they will
         be readily displaced during construction activities to other habitats (i.e. outside of the clearance
         areas), which would be a medium impact, resulting in a minor effect (not significant).
15.6.20. With regards bats and recognising the relative uncertainty here over the extent of bat roosts present,
         the loss of these supporting features is precautionarily considered to be medium and therefore an
         effect that is minor (not significant).
15.6.21. With regards migratory birds, beyond the flooding zone there is extensive braided channel habitat
         upstream of Novabad, which would require birds to fly an additional 7km+, which given the distances
         travelled by migratory birds is considered feasible. The impact from land-take is predicted to be
         small, permanent and irreversible resulting in an adverse effect that is negligible (not significant).


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                    PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                   SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page | 15-25
         Additional Mitigation and Enhancement Measures
15.6.22. To reduce potential significant effects, and to ensure good practice construction methods relating to
         protection of biodiversity, the following measures shall be implemented.

         Sensitive Habitats
15.6.23. Floodplain and juniper woodland habitat have been defined as Natural Habitat (as per ESS6) and so
         the Project is required to deliver No Net Loss (NNL) for these habitats. An NNL Framework (NNLF)
         has been produced as part of the BMP and this explores various options to delivering NNL, with a
         feasibility appraisal of each to enable a conclusion of those that are feasible and will be taken
         forward for further development.
