15.6.24. The floodplain habitat prevails because of morphological processes as well as through species
         composition, and so it may be difficult to recreate or restore this habitat within the overall Project
         footprint; however, options for on-site mitigation in this regard have been fully explored in line with
         the mitigation hierarchy and considered within the NNLF.

15.6.25. At this stage, other options considered include the following:

          Creation of new floodplain habitat upstream of the flooding zone or within a different catchment;
           and
          Other, out of kind, projects such as supporting creation of another habitat type that will be
           demonstrated to compensate for the loss of floodplain through creation of a sufficient area of
           another sensitive, native habitat type.
15.6.26. With regards juniper woodland, a single avoidance option exists, in relation to the left bank road, and
         the design will be finalised to avoid all areas of juniper woodland.

15.6.27. For areas of woodland that will be lost to clearance activities prior to flooding the options for on-site
         mitigation may comprise replacement planting of woodland at various locations around the flooding
         zone and Project site, which will align with complementary planting requirements such as for soil
         stability purposes or making use of the aforementioned stabilised water levels at Nurek as a result of
         the constructed Rogun dam to undertake planting there.

15.6.28. Regardless of on-site options that may be considered to mitigate for losses to juniper woodland, it is
         considered that off-site options will be required, and so juniper woodland has been included within
         the NNLF. Numerous potential options exist to deliver NNL for this habitat, especially given its
         formerly extensive coverage of the landscape in Vakhsh River valley. Discussions with relevant
         stakeholders on this have identified a number of options that are explored within the NNLF,
         including:

          Expansion of existing remnant areas of juniper woodland through planting and habitat
           management;
          Restoration of remnant areas of juniper woodland to improve overall habitat quality/value;
          Creation of new woodland areas;




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                    PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                   SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page | 15-26
          Collaboration with/contribution to existing juniper woodland management projects, such as those
           within the Nurek Management Area, or currently being delivered by Gesellschaft für
           Internationale Zusammenarbeit (GIZ) in partnership with FFI; and
          Other, out of kind, conservation projects such as supporting planting and management of native
           fruit and nut tree woodlands.
15.6.29. As with the work required to inform floodplain mitigation, an essential requirement will be to
         complete a detailed botanical inventory of the juniper woodland habitat that will be lost. This will be
         completed by a suitably experienced botanist with experience of Tajik flora.

15.6.30. The NNLF also includes monitoring, including establishment of feedback mechanisms to ensure
         successful NNL delivery in perpetuity.

         Sensitive Flora
15.6.31. Mitigation is required to reduce significant effects specific to Vitex agnus-castea, but this extends to
         all TRB plants given the protection they received under Tajik law.

15.6.32. In advance of clearance activities across the flooding zone, a repeat survey for sensitive flora will be
         undertaken within those areas within the flooding zone where species were previously identified,
         along the route of the left bank road and within areas of Natural Habitat. These areas are illustrated
         within the Project Biodiversity Management Plan (BMP). This will be completed by a suitably
         experienced botanist following a method for surveying that has been put forward in the BMP and
         approved by the Lender consortium. The botanist will be experienced in Tajik botany, with at least
         10 years of professional experience and demonstrable experience, and knowledge of the habitat
         types and flora found in the Vakhsh River valley.

15.6.33. Findings from the surveys will be added to a Biodiversity Constraints Tracker (BCT) for the Project
         so that the nature and location of these findings can inform ongoing mitigation and monitoring.

15.6.34. Any rare plants encountered will be collected and moved to receptor sites that will be pre-identified
         in which these plants will remain to mitigate the Project-related impacts. These receptor sites will be
         chosen in consultation with botanical specialists and will consider ongoing risks from the
         anthropogenic impacts currently affecting the species in question (i.e., a particular focus will be in
         areas that will likely become less used by local communities and livestock once local resettlement
         has occurred and inundation is complete). Indicative illustrations have been provided within the
         BMP, which will also describe the survey methodology, responsibilities and experience of the
         specialists, survey results and success criteria.

15.6.35. Given the incremental nature of this impact (i.e., increasing in spatial extent over time), this
         translocation measure will be implemented sufficiently far in advance of land-clearing to allow
         translocation prior to clearing and flooding (under the guidance of the Lead ECoW and associated
         botanist support).

15.6.36. Additionally, should monitoring suggest that the translocation has not been successful, then
         adaptive management in the form of a supplementary planting programme supported by the local
         botanic garden/nursery will be undertaken. With regards Vitex agnus-castus, this is a species that is
         readily propagated, from both seeds and cuttings (as described within Ari 2016); the four other
         species confirmed as being present are also subject to cultivation programmes at Tajik botanical


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                    PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                   SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page | 15-27
         gardens (as per TRB) and so it is expected that should this be required, this would represent a
         viable adaptive management strategy. However, it is still recognised that the Project may result in
         the loss of these local populations given the inherently variable success rate for plant translocations,
         and the numerous factors that will influence whether plants will become established in the receptor
         sites. This has been factored into the residual impact conclusion accordingly.

         Sensitive Fauna
15.6.37. Although no significant effects are predicted from this impact with regards sensitive fauna, measures
         are required to ensure compliance with Tajik law as well as Lender standards.

15.6.38. In advance of clearance activities (and programmed accordingly by the Lead ECoW and detailed
         within the BCT), a walkover will be undertaken of all areas to be cleared, with a particular focus on
         those features used by reptiles for sheltering purposes, such as log piles, rock piles, loose built
         structures, etc. This will be completed by a suitably experienced herpetologist, following a method
         for surveying that is presented in the BMP and approved by the Lender consortium.
15.6.39. Pre-clearance/flooding walkovers for bats will be undertaken that focus on areas that have the
         potential to support bat roosts, such as stable cliff features and buildings, and Natural Habitat juniper
         woodland (including areas within the route of the left bank road); these areas are identified within the
         BMP and will be covered incrementally in line with the proposed clearance programme and flooding
         (i.e., these checks will be done within six months of the predicted impact). The surveys will be
         completed by a suitably experienced bat surveyor and will comprise a mix of daytime roost searches
         and night-time bat activity surveys, with the aid of appropriate bat survey equipment such as high-
         powered torches and handheld and/or static bat detectors.
15.6.40. The import of suitable broadband bat detectors into Tajikistan (assuming that they cannot be
         sourced in-country) may be complicated. On other recent projects in Central Asia the process has
         been lengthy, and the sourcing of field-equipment will be planned at the earliest opportunity to
         ensure that surveying is not compromised. In the worst-case scenario the Lead ECoW would have
         to rely on bat detection equipment travelling out with the bat specialists (i.e. their own personal
         equipment) or searches would have to be entirely manual/visual.
15.6.41. Findings from the surveys will be added to the Project BCT, and this will inform timing of specific
         clearance activities so that clearance of important features can be avoided during the winter months
         when they may be used as hibernacula.
15.6.42. Any reptiles encountered will be moved away from the flooding zone and translocated to an area of
         suitable habitat outside of the inundation zone (within a similar habitat/altitude). Given the generalist
         nature of the species identified, it isn’t necessary to identify specific receptor sites for this
         translocation; the main focus is for the animals to be moved away from clearance activities and
         flooding. The Lead ECoW shall have overall responsibility for the reptile translocation program
         taking expert herpetological guidance as necessary. The reptile translocation process within the
         BMP sets out biosecurity measures to be employed to ensure the safe handling of animals and to
         prevent disease spread and enforced intra- and/or inter-species competition. It establishes criteria
         for the selection of suitable release sites and there is an assumption that given reduced general
         human pressure in the wider area as a result of displaced communities, a relatively higher carrying
         capacity will be realised that will accommodate this predicted small-scale translocation exercise. The
         progress of inundation may, in cases, not be gradual and progressive but (dependent on



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                  SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 15-28
         topography) be more episodic in nature. The translocation methodology considers this so that
         translocation activities are not abortive. Solutions to this are likely to be translocations at greater
         distances than would be normally undertaken to ensure there is no risk of animal returns to the
         flooding zone.
15.6.43. Given the incremental nature of this impact, i.e., increasing in spatial extent over time, this measure
         will be implemented on a regular basis (e.g. annually) but informed by the programme of clearance
         activities across the flooding zone (i.e. checks will only be done within six months of the predicted
         clearance/flooding impact), and reported/tracked within the BCT.
15.6.44. Where bat roosts are identified and not in use during clearance activities, and if adequate alternative
         natural roost sites are available in the wider area, then no further action will be required. If a roost is
         found and no adequate alternate sites are available, the following applies:
          It will be necessary to provide artificial roost sites for use by bats when they return to the roost
           site. The artificial roost shall be suitable for they species/number of bats that use the roost that
           will be lost; expert input shall be provided to inform this. Artificial bat roosts are available
           online. At least five genera of bats (Pipistrellus, Nyctalus, Myotis, Eptesicus, and Barbastella – all
           of which are present in the Project area) are known utilise artificial bat boxes to varying degrees.
           Other encountered species may require more bespoke solutions. It is expected that an artificial
           bat box such as the FSC Triple Cavity Bat Box [for example, https://www.wildcare.co.uk/treble-
           crevice-bat-box-black-10562.html] can be used as a default unit in the Project area for general
           purposes – the unit possesses design features that make it suitable for several of the species that
           may be encountered in the Project area. If the sourcing of bat boxes proves difficult a template
           plan for the units can be provided and they can then be constructed locally – no specialised
           material or equipment are necessary.
          If the roost is in use during construction works, and if these works cannot be delayed until the
           bats have vacated the roost, then a suitably experienced bat specialist shall be consulted to
           formulate an appropriate course of action. For small/single bat roosts of limited conservation
           value it may be appropriate for the bat worker to move the bats to an alternative roost site
           (whether natural or artificial), such as a suitable tree of rock/crevice outside of the affected area
           (to be informed by the bat specialist in the field); whereas for more significant roosts, a more
           robust approach will need to be adopted, following the advice of the bat specialist. It is important
           than an experienced bat worker is engaged to undertake this work given the risk of rabies
           transmission when handling bats.
15.6.45. No additional mitigation measures are required (or possible) to avoid impacts to nesting birds from
         habitat/feature loss given that the area will be flooded to the maximum inundation level. Good
         practice measures are detailed within 15.10 to minimise impacts to nesting birds at all phases of the
         Project in line with Lender requirements.

15.6.46. There will be complementary mitigation measures that will be implemented as part of site
         rehabilitation (see Volume 3, Outline Site Rehabilitation Plan) that will include tree-planting that
         may provide nesting opportunities for a range of breeding bird species, depending on the nature of
         the species planted and with a timeline dependent upon the age of trees planted. This is detailed
         further in 15.10.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                     PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                    SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 15-29
         Residual Effects
15.6.47. Compensation mitigation to deliver NNL is defined in the NNLF. The NNLF provides information on
         the process to be followed to demonstrate successful delivery as required by ESS6 and other
         Lender standards; this will reduce the impact to very small resulting in a negligible effect, at a
         minimum. The outcome will not only achieve NNL, but a net gain for both floodplain and juniper
         woodland habitats.

15.6.48. On the assumption that plant translocation is at least partially successful for each species being
         considered, the residual impact to all sensitive flora will be reduced to small resulting in an effect
         that is minor. In relation to Vitex agnus-castus, this uncertainty will result in a medium impact
         prevailing, resulting in an effect that is moderate, on a precautionary basis. This conclusion should
         be considered within the context of a local population that was likely unviable (given only a single
         stand was recorded) and trending towards local extinction due to the aforementioned pressures
         upon it; additionally it is feasible that the receptor evaluation is unrealistically high that has inflated
         the assessment.

15.6.49. The residual impact to sensitive fauna will be remain to small resulting in an effect that is
         negligible.

         Direct Mortality / Drowning
15.6.50. The clearance activities and ongoing flooding (as described from 15.6.1 onwards) will also result in a
         direct mortality/drowning impact that has the potential to affect sensitive fauna, in particular less
         mobile species of mammals (notably roosting bats), reptiles and nesting birds (or more specifically
         their dependent young).
15.6.51. The effect of direct mortality/drowning associated with ongoing flooding will depend upon the rate at
         which flooding will occur, together with the timing of this impact. A very crude estimate of the latter is
         0.5m per day (based upon filling to the maximum inundation level within the shallowest gradient in
         the flooding zone and therefore the area where ‘quickest’ average flooding will occur at any one
         point).
15.6.52. The main impact pathway will be mainly from flooding (on the assumption that clearance activities
         will be undertaken during spring/summer months, so not affecting hibernating mammals and
         reptiles), although clearance activities do have the potential to impact dependent young of all these
         animals (in particular nesting birds), which will be less able to escape the impact.
         Impact Assessment
15.6.53. With a worst-case flooding impact scenario being realised during winter months when reptiles are
         inactive, and clearance activities during the months when nesting birds will have eggs/dependent
         young the impact magnitude is considered to be medium, permanent and irreversible, resulting in a
         minor adverse effect (not significant).
         Additional Mitigation and Enhancement Measures
15.6.54. No mitigation for significant effects is required, although measures will be adopted to ensure
         compliance with Tajik law and Lender standards. This is described in relation to the loss/degradation
         of habitats impact, at 15.6.37 onwards.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                     SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page | 15-30
        Residual Effects
15.6.55. The impact magnitude will be reduced to small resulting in an effect that is negligible (not
        significant).

        Encroachment / Introduction of invasive species
15.6.56. Vehicle movement associated with clearance activities, and the ongoing flooding across the flooding
        zone have the potential to facilitate the introduction and/or cause the spread of invasive species
        across the wider area, which may have an adverse impact on native biodiversity. This impact can
        materialise as seeds/plant material is transported on vehicles, and/or seeds are carried by
        floodwaters.
15.6.57. The highly invasive Bathurst burr was recorded from numerous locations across the AoI, in
        particular across modified floodplain and braided channel habitats. This species is known to
        adversely affect a wide range of important natural and agricultural habitats, and spreads very easily
        through various sources (including on livestock and during flooding as the seeds floats – both
        pathways that will be very prevalent across the AoI). Clearance activities (movement of vehicles)
        and ongoing flooding may therefore contribute to the further spread and establishment of invasive
        species communities across the AoI, especially given the acknowledged potential for spreading
        across flooded areas.
15.6.58. Of the 21 fish species expected to occur in the Project AoI, twelve (12) have been introduced
        (Volume 2, Annex 7.1 (Baseline Conditions Report). Invasive and non-native species have the
        ability to cause ecological harm by dramatically alter food web structures. Invasive and non-native
        species can prey on or out compete native species and often have high reproductive abilities. Once
        introduced, these species will have the ability to move upstream and extend their distribution
        regionally and permanently.
        Impact Assessment
        Sensitive Habitats
15.6.59. Any new invasive species presence across the AoI will affect disturbed or open areas preferentially
        due to the nature of such plants’ colonisation strategy, and because ecosystem resilience is
        impaired. Both of these considerations apply to the juniper woodland, which is naturally quite open
        but has also experienced a level of modification through sporadic tree removal and the through-
        movement of livestock. With this in mind, it is feasible that Bathurst burr will spread to the juniper
        woodland habitats during ongoing inundation as the reservoir will provide an effective pathway to
        these remnant woodland habitats. This has the potential to significantly alter the woodland species
        composition, which would represent a large impact magnitude that will result in an adverse effect
        that is major (significant). This assessment is made with reasonable confidence.
        Sensitive Flora
15.6.60. With regards sensitive flora, the assessment text presented for sensitive habitats is relevant here,
        and over time it is feasible that introduced invasive species could spread to areas where the
        sensitive flora is present. While there is very little certainty in this regard, it is feasible that such a
        scenario could result in threatened plant species being lost across a large area as invasive species
        colonise and out-compete other plants. Such an impact would likely be irreversible in practice but
        given the limited extent of sensitive flora present within the AoI, and the extensive similar habitat



        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                     SEPTEMBER 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page | 15-31
         across the wider landscape, together with the fact that invasive plant species are already present in
         the wider area, the predicted impact would be small (in terms of an impact attributable to the
         Project), resulting in a minor effect (not significant) for rare plants in general (it is also
         acknowledged that in the case of Vitex agnus-castus, it will be less prone to loss to this species
         given its larger size/more scrubby form). While the Project could ultimately lead to the permanent
         loss of some of these threatened plants from the AoI, it is not thought that this would materially
         affect the conservation status of any species given the small populations considered to be present
         here, and the wider distribution (and more optimal habitat) elsewhere in the country for all species,
         in particular the south and west of Tajikistan, together with their more favourable conservation status
         described within more recent reviews (as illustrated within Nowak & Nobis 2020).
         Aquatic Biodiversity
15.6.61. With regards fish, it should be noted that a number of non-native species are already present within
         the Rogun Reservoir and surrounding waterbodies, and it is likely that non-native species will further
         establish within the reservoir. Non-native species have been introduced into the Nurek Reservoir
         and are reportedly already within the Rogun Project area (2014 ESIA).
15.6.62. In accordance with the Rules of fishery in reservoirs of the Republic of Tajikistan, as approved by
         the Order of the Ministry of Agriculture and Environment of the Republic of Tajikistan of September
         22, 2007, it is forbidden to “make acclimatization and cultivation of new species of fish without the
         corresponding biological reasons and permission of bodies of fish conservation”.
15.6.63. A mix of lentic and lotic non-native fish species are found within the AoI. Many of the species that
         have been introduced into reservoirs and ponds have a preference for standing or slow flowing
         waters, and are unlikely to out compete native species in the fast-flowing waters upstream (e.g.
         Carp, Goldfish). Species such as the Sharpbelly and Common Bleak are found in flowing rivers and
         would be able to move upstream.
15.6.64. As no Critical Habitat for fish species has been identified upstream of Rogun, and it has been
         reported that non-native species are already within the Vakhsh River upstream of the Rogun Dam,
         the impact magnitude of the introduction of non-native species is considered small and therefore the
         added effect of the Project on the Vakhsh River within the Project AoI is considered to be minor (not
         significant).
         Additional Mitigation and Enhancement Measures
15.6.65. To reduce potentially significant effects to juniper woodland, the following measures shall be
         implemented:
          The extent of invasive species present (currently considered to just comprise Bathurst burr) within
           proximity to the juniper woodland habitat will be recorded within the growing season prior to
           predicted flooding at 1290m asl (i.e. the time at which flooding is predicted to reach the juniper
           woodland habitat). This is currently around 2036-2038.
          An Invasive Species Management Plan (ISMP) will be produced in advance of flooding to 1290m
           asl. This will detail measures to control the spread of invasive plant species identified in proximity
           to the juniper woodland habitat, such as plant removal and safe disposal. The ISMP will also
           identify measures for reducing the risk of contaminated materials being imported onto site such
           as wheel-washing and adherence to identified access routes. The DFZ (or their lead contractors)
           will develop the detail of the plan and integrate it into their working practices. The ISMP will be an


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                 SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                              Page | 15-32
            explicit commitment of the Environmental and Social Commitments Plan (ESCP). The
            requirement for separate ISMPs for construction and operation will be at the discretion of DFZ.
          The Lead Ecological Clerk of Works (ECoW) will ensure the delivery of toolbox talks to
           contractors operating outside of the current dam site to raise awareness of invasive species
           identification and good practice to minimise the risk of invasive species spread from the Project.
15.6.66. No additional mitigation measures have been identified for non-native fish species, beyond the
         restriction of their introduction. It is further recommended that any introduction of non-native species
         for aquaculture (if considered) should be limited to the Nurek Reservoir, as this is a contained
         system, already has a high proportion of non-native fish species, is close to market and will have a
         controlled water level. It should be noted that this could be viewed as economically beneficial,
         however is not considered at this stage.
         Residual Effects
15.6.67. Implementation of the additional measures will reduce the impact of invasive species encroachment
         to very small, resulting in a negligible effect (not significant) to habitats and sensitive flora.

15.6.68. With non-native fish species already present within the Vakhsh River and surrounding waterbodies,
         there is little that one can do to eradicate them from the system, and as such the effect will remain
         minor (not significant).

         CONSTRUCTION ACTIVITIES AT THE HPP SITE AND ASSOCIATED FACILITIES
15.6.69. For the purposes of this assessment, this phase comprises ongoing construction activities beyond
         the partially completed dam structure, i.e., construction required to complete the dam structure and
         other Project components, such as construction of the new left bank road and bridges. Associated
         facilities such as construction of new settlements and transmission lines are also considered here.
         Impacts will relate to movement of plant machinery, air quality (including dust), noise and vibration
         as well as pollution impacts.
         Habitat degradation and disturbance and injury/killing of fauna as a result of construction
         activities
15.6.70. Sensitive habitats are at risk of degradation from construction activities where air quality impacts
         occur; most notably from emissions and dust.
15.6.71. Fauna is at increased risk of disturbance and injury/death in areas where construction activities are
         occurring, including from construction machinery, air quality, noise and vibration impacts.
15.6.72. Direct mortality from construction activities may occur from a number of sources including vehicle
         movement, excavation and vegetation clearance across the flooding zone and within the footprint of
         associated facilities (such as stockpiling areas and resettlement sites) and hunting/poaching by
         construction workers. This impact will be most notable in areas where constructions are
         new/infrequent, as opposed to where they have been ongoing (such as the dam site), and is
         predicted to affect sensitive reptiles and mammals.
15.6.73. The left bank road will require ongoing construction activities across a length of 55km and an
         assumed working corridor of 50m. The impact sources here will be numerous, with air quality, noise
         and vibration and pollution impacts all having the potential to disturb sensitive fauna here as well as
         degrade the habitats upon which the fauna depends.



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                     PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                    SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 15-33
15.6.74. Based on ariel imagery, the left bank road will cross numerous watercourses. Impacts associated
         with instream structures and downstream sedimentation have the potential to disturb downstream
         habitats.
15.6.75. It is assumed that construction activities will take place predominately within the spring/summer
         months, especially in relation to the left bank road. With this in mind, sensitive fauna of increased
         sensitivity will comprise roosting bats and nesting birds (other animals will be readily able to
         disperse).
         Impact Assessment
         Sensitive Habitats
15.6.76. The only habitat scoped in and potentially at risk of being adversely affected by construction
         activities is the juniper woodland in proximity to the left bank road. Here, dust produced during
         construction has the potential to degrade the juniper woodland through affecting the physiology and
         processes of the tree components, in particular the leaves and the photosynthesis that they
         undertake. Additionally, the dust may contain harmful compounds that can adversely affect the plant
         chemistry (e.g., as per Carr, 2021).
15.6.77. Given the sensitivity of the juniper woodland habitat, the potential impact of dust pollution has the
         potential to be an impact of medium magnitude, resulting in an effect that is moderate (significant),
         in the absence of demonstrable good practice from the contractors during construction.
         Sensitive Fauna
15.6.78. It is assumed that most animals will be displaced as a result of construction activities and associated
         disturbance, which will reduce the mortality risk and potential for hunting/poaching; however, there
         may be animals that will not be able to relocate (such as roosting bats and the young of nesting
         birds), together with those that will be killed by vehicle movements. Roosting bats are known to be
         susceptible to disturbance from acoustic stimuli, with distances from which this impact is realised
         varying according to the source of disturbance (as per Reason & Wray 2023). Generally, distances
         of >30m are included when it comes to predicting disturbance impacts. With this in mind, the
         construction activities at the left bank road when in proximity of the juniper woodland in particular will
         represent a potential effect pathway.
15.6.79. This impact is considered likely to only affect a small proportion of those animals present (on a
         precautionary basis), with most able to relocate without any discernible medium/long-term impact.
         However, given the relative uncertainty over this (in particular in relation to the left bank road), the
         impact is considered to be medium infrequent, medium-term and local in nature, resulting in an
         adverse effect that is minor (not significant).
         Additional Mitigation and Enhancement Measures
15.6.80. Mitigation is required to reduce effects to juniper woodland in proximity to the left bank road.
         Primarily, this mitigation will be achieved through the design process of the new road whereby the
         juniper woodland is avoided by at least 100m.

15.6.81. Other measures to minimise construction-related disturbance are included within Volume 1,
         Chapter 8 (Air Quality) and Chapter 11 (Noise and Vibration) of this ESIA. These are
         summarised below:




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                     PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                    SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 15-34
          Several bowsers water the road and dampen dust along many sections, but visual observation
           shows this is not entirely adequate and that further effort is required;
          Enforce a speed limit to reduce the dust generated by HGVs;
          Locate stationary machinery (i.e., pumps, compressor, concrete mixing, etc) away from noise-
           sensitive receptors;
          Start-up machinery and vehicles sequentially;
          Select plant and equipment that conforms to Directive 2000/14/EC (noise emission in the
           environment by equipment for use outdoors, or equivalent);
          Reversing alarms without tonal component (i.e., broadband);
          Undertake regular and effective maintenance for the construction machinery and any sound-
           reducing equipment;
          Install temporary acoustic enclosure for noisy equipment;
          Loading and unloading will be done away from noise-sensitive areas;
          Minimise drop height of materials; and
          Switch off equipment when it is not in use.
15.6.82. These measures will also serve to minimise impacts to sensitive fauna, as will general biodiversity
         measures prescribed within the BMP.

         Residual Effects
15.6.83. The impact upon sensitive habitats will be reduced to very small, resulting in an effect that is
         negligible (not significant).

15.6.84. Effects to sensitive fauna will remain minor (not significant).

         Release of pollutants into the river
15.6.85. Pollution into the water environment can derive from a number of sources during construction
         activities and can adversely affect freshwater biota and habitats, through changes in chemical,
         sediment or thermal conditions.
15.6.86. A release of pollutants will encompass deleterious substances, which when released into the
         river/lake would degrade or alter the water quality such that it could directly or indirectly harm fish,
         fish habitat, or the use of fish by humans, and includes petroleum products, chemicals, untreated
         wastewater, sediments, suspended solids and thermally altered water.
15.6.87. This risk is considered minimal at present, pending raising of the reservoir to inundate Construction
         Site 1, where most substandard fuel storage, sewage management, and concrete washing occurs.
         The migration of support activities to the new camps will lead to major improvements (in part through
         implementation of requirements of the new Contractor waste management plans based on the
         waste management frameworks) and the contaminated lands survey will allow identification of
         remedial actions that will be needed before the legacy areas are inundated.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                     PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                    SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 15-35
         Impact Assessment
15.6.88. A risk to water quality exists for the construction phase of the Rogun HPP. There are several
         potential impacts to water quality, including:
          Runoff from oils, lubricants, or other chemicals and substances associated with construction
           activities and vehicles/machines;
          Contamination arising from the use and storage of fuels and oils (hydrocarbons);
          Exposure of water to concrete, which may lead to changes in pH;
          Thermal pollution with cooler waters being released downstream from the Rogun HPP;
          Blasting, which may result in elevated peak pressures and the introduction of nitrate or nitrite
           from the use of explosives; and
          Wastewater discharge from construction camps and the workforce on site during construction.
15.6.89. The release of a pollutants into the river/reservoir would degrade or alter the water quality such that
         it could directly or indirectly have an impact on the ecosystem as a whole, harming fish, aquatic
         macroinvertebrates and their habitats.
15.6.90. The majority of construction activities are taking place within the confinements of the dam and not in
         direct contact with the river, reducing the potential for pollutants to be released directly into the river.
         Based on the scale of the Project, the impact is considered to be medium, local, short-term and
         reversible with an adverse effect that will be moderate (significant).
15.6.91. Additional Mitigation and Enhancement Measures

15.6.92. As outlined in Volume 1, Chapter 13 (Water) and Volume 2, Annex 12.1 (Contaminated Land) of
         this ESIA, Rogun JSC will be required to develop and implement management plans and mitigation
         measures to avoid and minimise construction-related disturbances related to water quality. These
         are summarised below:
          Sources of pollution and contamination during construction must be identified upfront and
           measures established to prevent any pollution into the river systems.
          Contractors shall direct runoff waters from drains and gullies from workshop areas through an
           interceptor prior to discharge into the river or to ground, allowing for the separation of free phase
           Light Non-Aqueous Phase Liquids (LNAPL) from water (significantly reducing the contaminant
           mass being lost to ground or surface water).
          Where workshops do not have a current drainage or gully system, the contractor shall ensure all
           runoff is contained, with all runoffs either being disposed/treated offsite, or onsite via interceptor
           (as a minimum).
          Additional treatment prior to discharge maybe necessary depending on the workshop activities
           and nature of any chemicals utilised.
          At 'general use' washout areas Rogun HPP shall establish an area where wash waters are
           contained and as a minimum undergo basic settlement prior to discharging to the river or
           reservoir.
          No refuelling or washing of equipment will take place within 30 m of a watercourse.



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                     SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page | 15-36
          Where contractors 'wash out' within their own compounds or facilities, each contractor shall
           contain and treat any wash waters prior to discharge. As with the general 'wash out' area as a
           minimum any wash waters shall undergo basic settlement prior to discharge.
          Ensure management plans, such as Sediment Management Plans, Borrow Pit Management
           Plans, as well as procedures on fuel storage and disposal of waste / hydrocarbons are
           developed.
          Contractors shall develop spill response/ cleanup plans.


         Residual Effects
15.6.93. With the application of the above mitigation, the residual impact will be reduced to small, resulting a
         residual effect that is minor (not significant).
         CURRENT HPP OPERATION AND MAINTENANCE
         Entrainment and Impingement of fish through the turbines
15.6.94. The current operation of the existing Rogun HPP has the potential to result in physical injury and
         mortality of fish passing through the two turbines and associated components as water passes
         through the dam.
15.6.95. Entrainment occurs when a fish is drawn into a water intake and cannot escape, while impingement
         occurs when an entrapped fish is held in contact with the intake screen and is unable to free itself.
         Only fish within the local vicinity of the intakes would be exposed to this risk. With the high volume of
         water moving through the turbines, it is likely that fish would suffer entrainment and impingement.
         Impact Assessment
15.6.96. No screening to exclude fish from entering the intakes and passing through the turbines is present at
         the moment. Barnthouse (2013), undertook a review of peer-reviewed literature and concluded that
         any impacts caused by impingement and entrainment are small compared to other impacts on fish
         populations and communities, including overfishing, habitat destruction, pollution, and invasive
         species. Further to this Kemp et al (2005) stated that fish that are good swimmers and exhibit
         behavioural avoidance when encountering velocity gradients (e.g., juvenile salmonids) are less
         susceptible to impingement. Based on the limited fish habitat around the intakes, and with no reports
         of dead fish downstream, the risk of fish mortality from entrainment or impingement during
         construction is considered small, and the resulting effect is minor (not significant).
         Additional Mitigation and Enhancement Measures
15.6.97. As the turbines are already in place and operational, no further mitigation is possible.

         Residual Effects
15.6.98. Under the current design, the residual effect from entrainment and impingement of fish through the
         turbines will remain minor.

         Changes to downstream flow regimes
15.6.99. It is accepted that some effects from the existing dam structure have already been realised, notably
         the barrier effect, which was screened out as the impact has already occurred, and fish migration



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                  SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 15-37
          has already been interrupted. The operation if the existing Rogun HPP has altered the natural flow
          regime of the Vakhsh River between the Rogun and Nurek Reservoir.

          Impact Assessment
15.6.100. A risk to fish, aquatic biota (aquatic macroinvertebrates) and their habitats exists during the
          construction phase of the Rogun HPP as natural flows have been altered. Impacts resulting from the
          change in quantity and timing of flow may negatively impact biota within the downstream reach.
15.6.101. Under current operation, the average monthly streamflow exceeds the minimum environmental
          flows, and simulations presented in the Volume 1, Chapter 13 (Water) showed that the impact on
          the flows in the river reach downstream of Rogun Dam will not be significantly impacted by the dam
          during the filling period when compared to the inflows to the dam.
15.6.102. The Vakhsh River between the Rogun and Nurek Reservoir is a steep gorge with few tributaries that
          would provide refuge or upstream migration.
15.6.103. The change in quantity and timing of flow is constant and certain as the Rogun Dam is permanently
          in place and is currently operating. The magnitude of this impact for aquatic biodiversity is local and
          is considered to be very small. Having already assessed the effects of thermally altered water and
          the effect of pollutants impacting the aquatic ecology during construction, the change is considered
          to be minor (not significant).
          Additional Mitigation and Enhancement Measures
15.6.104. As Rogun Dam is already in place and operational, and the minimum environmental flows for the
          Vakhsh River downstream of Rogun are being met, no further mitigation is required.

          Residual Effects
15.6.105. Under the current operational configuration, the residual effect to Aquatic Biodiversity from changes
          to downstream flow regimes (within the Vakhsh River between the Rogun and Nurek Reservoir) will
          remain minor.

15.7      OPERATION
          OPERATIONAL RESERVOIR REGIME INCLUDING ANNUAL DRAWDOWN (C. 30M)
          AND ASSOCIATED FACILITIES
          Mortality/displacement of sensitive fauna from new roads and overhead transmission lines
15.7.1.   The Project and associated facilities (see Volume 1, Chapter 3 (Project Description) of this ESIA)
          include a number of installations with the potential to cause direct mortality and displacement to
          sensitive fauna. In particular this relates to the following:
           Collision and electrocution risk to sensitive birds (both breeding and migratory birds) from the
            new overhead transmission lines – most notably the two planned 500kV lines (of 400km and
            350km) connecting to Saikhun substation and Khujand substation, respectively).
           The 55km left bank road which will the Project site with a new major bridge over the new
            reservoir at Darband has the potential to result in collision mortality to sensitive fauna, as well as
            severance of local migration.




          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                  SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 15-38
15.7.2.   Collision mortality can pose a significant risk to bird populations where energy infrastructure is
          located within an area that birds (especially those in relatively large numbers) frequently fly through,
          either for seasonal migration or for local migration (i.e. to/from foraging and roosting areas). For the
          former, these locations are normally linked to topography (e.g. river valleys), where the landform
          funnels birds to create ‘hotspots’ of flight activity that if bisected by new energy infrastructure can
          result in significant collision mortality, especially in poor visibility. In the case of the latter, the flight
          activity is less topography-based and more reflective of the shortest distance between two key
          habitats, which if bisected by new energy infrastructure can also result in significant collision
          mortality.
15.7.3.   The potential collision impact is largely influenced by species. Birds with larger body sizes and high
          wing loadings, birds flying in flocks and/or in low light, birds with limited visual capacity, birds
          distracted while engaged in hunting/breeding behaviours, younger and more inexperienced birds
          and migrants not familiar with the landscape may all be at increased collision risk. For example,
          swans and other large waterfowl are of particular concern for collisions (Taylor et al., 2015 in SNH,
          2016).
15.7.4.   Electrocution is generally only a risk in low and medium voltage networks (Birdlife International,
          2015), where bird wingspans are feasibly large-enough to span two points and create a short circuit.
15.7.5.   The sensitive bird species identified as being present/potentially present across the AoI include
          those which will be at risk of impacts from these new installations, in particular larger birds with low
          manoeuvrability such as geese, swans, cranes and large raptors/vultures. There is nothing to
          suggest that the new overhead transmissions lines will extend across any areas of importance to
          significant migratory bird movements (e.g., those associated with the Central Asian Flyway, which is
          a broad north/south front and therefore parallel with the new overhead transmission lines, and for
          which most flight activity is east of Tajikistan). Localised movement of birds was observed
          incidentally when travelling to Rogin from Dushanbe.
15.7.6.   There is one internationally recognised area (designated for birds) that appears to be located in
          proximity to the western of the two new overhead transmission lines - Kattasay and Daganasay
          Reservoirs KBA. This is designated for (amongst other things) it’s importance as a stopover for
          migratory birds and is located at an elevation of 1165m asl.
15.7.7.   The new left bank road introduces a new source of collision mortality impact in an area where
          vehicle movement is currently very limited (and generally at low speed). This has the potential to
          result in death of animals (most notably reptiles and mammals) moving between areas of previously
          connected habitats.
          Impact Assessment
15.7.8.   Given the scale of the new overhead transmission lines being planned, it is entirely feasible that
          their route will take them across areas within which at-risk bird species will be present and therefore
          a collision risk that effects the population a species of high sensitivity cannot be ruled out. With this
          in mind and impact that is large is considered appropriate, when applying the precautionary
          principle. No electrocution impacts beyond small are expected.
15.7.9.   The overall effect to birds from collision risk is therefore considered to be moderate (significant).
15.7.10. The extent of impacts deriving from vehicle movements along the new left bank road (and other
          roads being constructed for the Project) will depend on several factors including the exact location of


          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                         PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                        SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                     Page | 15-39
         new roads, together with the number of vehicles using the roads and the speed of vehicle
         movements. However, there isn’t expected to be a level of vehicle movement that will result in any
         more than a small collision mortality impact, which will result in a negligible effect (not significant).
         Additional Mitigation and Enhancement Measures
15.7.11. In order to mitigate the significant effect deriving from collision mortality, and acknowledging the
         separate baseline and assessment work that will be undertaken specific to the new overhead
         transmission lines, the following is recommended:

          The ESIA(s) for the associated facilities should include consideration of impacts to birds as a
           result of the new overhead transmission lines.
          The new overhead transmission lines should be designed to avoid potential flight activity
           hotspots, such as protected areas/internationally recognised areas, river valleys, ridge lines and
           wetlands. This should include routing at lower elevations and undergrounding where appropriate.
          Industry-standard flight divertors should be installed on new overhead transmission lines around
           any hotpots that cannot be avoided through the design process.
          Fatality monitoring should be undertaken at the locations where bird divertors are fitted (and
           elsewhere, depending upon ESIA findings) to monitor the effectiveness of this mitigation.
          Where fatality monitoring identifies a collision mortality impact greater than expected within the
           relevant ESIAs, then additional mitigation will be required, such as increased divertor installation.
15.7.12. These measures will be included within the BMP(s) for these ESIAs, with a commitment to them
         included within the ESCP for this Project.
         Residual Effects
15.7.13. With the application of the above mitigation, the residual impact will be reduced to small, resulting a
         residual effect that is minor (not significant).

         Release of pollutants into the river
15.7.14. Pollution into the water environment can derive from a number of sources during operation and can
         adversely affect freshwater biota and habitats, whether as a result of changes in chemical, sediment
         or thermal conditions.
         Impact Assessment
15.7.15. During the operation of the Rogun HPP, it is unlikely that pollutants in the form of petroleum
         products, chemicals, untreated wastewater, sediments and suspended solids will be released into
         the lake/river that would have an effect to a regional extent.
15.7.16. Voith Hydro has confirmed that the governor sump tank, governor and ring gate accumulators will be
         enclosed in an oil “bund” (a surrounding wall) such that any oils spills and leaks are contained and
         do not enter the powerhouse drainage system. Furthermore, all generator bearings will be properly
         ventilated, and the oil vapour piped to the outside of the generator housing and condensed. The
         handling of waste oil will be in accordance with environmental standards.
15.7.17. Any spills would be contained locally and would be short in duration.

15.7.18. Under operational conditions the reservoirs may show thermal stratification during the summer
         months where there are high surface temperatures but colder temperatures at depth.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                    PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                   SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page | 15-40
15.7.19. A slight increase to the current levels of thermal pollution has been identified as a risk to fish,
         relative to the current conditions. As the turbine intake structures are located below the surface,
         cooler water is likely to be released into the river downstream of the power plant (section of river
         between Rogun and Nurek). This impact has already been realised with the current turbines, but is
         expected to continue periodically as discharges from Rogun HPP change seasonally. During the low
         flow winter months, discharge will be higher than the natural flows. During the summer months flows
         will be reduced as water is stored in the reservoir to maintain the winter flows. The impact will
         decrease in magnitude as the thermal pollution dissipates into the Nurek Reservoir.
15.7.20. The magnitude of this impact for aquatic biodiversity is considered to be small. The effect of
         pollutants impacting the aquatic biodiversity during operation is considered to be negligible (not
         significant).
         Mitigation and Enhancement Measures
15.7.21. As outlined in Volume 1, Chapter 13 (Water) and Volume 2, Annex 12.1 (Contaminated Land) of
         this ESIA, and similarly to the construction phase, Rogun JSC will be required to develop and
         implement management plans and mitigation measures to avoid and minimise incidents from
         occurring during operation.

15.7.22. Auditing and monitoring as outlined in the BMP will be implemented to identify any potential issues
         and or trends so that corrective measures can be implemented early on.

         Residual Effects
15.7.23. With the application of regular audits and monitoring as well as the implementation of management
         plans and mitigation measures, the residual impact can be reduced to very small, resulting a
         residual effect that is negligible (not significant).

         Entrainment and Impingement of fish through the turbines
15.7.24. The operation if the Rogun HPP will include an additional four turbines, which will have the potential
         to result in physical injury and mortality of fish passing through the turbines and associated
         components as water passes through the dam.
         Impact Assessment
15.7.25. No screening to exclude fish from entering the intakes and passing through the turbines is planned
         for the additional four turbines. Based on the fisheries information obtained from the Nurek
         Reservoir, studies showed that after the filling phase (oligotrophic), most fish species disappeared
         and none of the species still present are found in substantial numbers (ESIA 2014); additionally, no
         Critical Habitat for fish species has been identified upstream of Rogun. It is therefore assumed that
         Rogun will respond in a similar manner and after filling will have a low diversity and abundance of
         fish, thus reducing the risk of frequent entrainment or impingement. With this in mind, and as stated
         in Section 15.6.96, the risk of fish mortality from entrainment or impingement during operation is
         small, the resulting effect is minor (not significant).
         Mitigation and Enhancement Measures
15.7.26. No additional mitigation measures are required as during operations, entrainment and impingement
         of fish through the turbines is considered not significant.
         Residual Effects


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                     PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                    SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 15-41
15.7.27. Under the current design, the residual effect from entrainment and impingement of fish through the
         turbines will remain minor.

         Changes to downstream flow regimes
15.7.28. The operation of the completed Rogun HPP will include four additional turbines and will further alter
         the natural flow regime of the Vakhsh River between the Rogun and Nurek Reservoir.

         Impact Assessment
15.7.29. A risk to fish, aquatic biota (aquatic macroinvertebrates) and their habitats exists during the
         operation phase of the Rogun HPP as the reservoir is managed to regulate water levels within the
         Nurek Reservoir. Impacts resulting from the change in quantity and timing of flow may negatively
         impact biota within the downstream reach.
15.7.30. The simulations presented in Volume 1, Chapter 13 (Water) showed that during operations, the
         flow pattern in the river reach between the Rogun and Nurek Reservoirs will be altered due to the
         HPP operating rules. The flows released from Rogun Reservoir during the summer months will be
         reduced as water is stored in the reservoir to maintain the winter flows. The overall inflow volume to
         the reservoir is released but the release pattern is different with flow from the high summer flows
         released during the winter. The winter flows will be higher and less variable due to the energy
         generation requirements.
15.7.31. The minimum flows required for energy generation are sufficient to meet the environmental flow
         requirements presented in Volume 1, Chapter 13 (Water) of this ESIA.
15.7.32. The change in quantity and timing of flow is constant and certain as the Rogun HPP will be
         permanently in place. Considering the environmental flows will be met as part of the operating rules,
         the magnitude of this impact for aquatic biodiversity is local and is considered to be very small.
         Having already assessed the effects of thermally altered water and the effect of pollutants impacting
         the aquatic biodiversity, the change is considered to be negligible(not significant).
         Mitigation and Enhancement Measures
15.7.33. No additional mitigation measures are required as during operations, release of water is sufficient to
         meet the environmental flow requirements.
         Residual Effects
15.7.34. The magnitude of this impact for aquatic biodiversity remains negligible (not significant)

         RESERVOIR AT FSL
         Impacts from Increased Human Pressures
15.7.35. The new reservoir in the landscape has the potential to displace people/communities into areas
         which are currently not affected by human pressures, which may result in an increase in activities
         which damage resident plants/habitats and animals there. However, it is also acknowledged that the
         general population within the local area has been/will be reduced as a result of resettlement
         activities.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                  SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 15-42
         Impact Assessment
         Sensitive Habitats
15.7.36. The creation of a large reservoir within the landscape will displace people from the flooding zone,
         potentially increasing use of the areas around/within the juniper woodland for grazing, foraging and
         other ecosystem services. It is unlikely that there will be a material increase in the collection of
         firewood based on current levels of firewood collection as well as the reduced access to this location
         given the bridges and paths/rough tracks that will be lost within the flooding zone, together with the
         general reduction in the local population (in close proximity to the flooding zone). Furthermore, given
         this loss of access, it is likely that livestock movement will be displaced altogether from the area
         immediately adjacent to the woodland here. While permanent, loss of woodland to tree clearance
         and grazing as a result of increased human pressures is considered to be reversible and small, on a
         precautionary basis, resulting in an adverse effect that is minor (not significant).
         Sensitive Fauna
15.7.37. With regards fish, the large new waterbody is expected to attract people into the area, increasing the
         harvesting of fish during the initial productive years.
15.7.38. In accordance with Article 25 of the Law of the Republic of Tajikistan (About fish breeding, fisheries
         and about protection of fish resources), there is a restriction on catching species listed as being rare
         and or under the threat of disappearing. Furthermore, Article 63 (Protection of rare plants and
         animals that are in danger of extinction) outlines that species listed in the TRB are protected and
         thus are prohibited from being captured as this would be an activity that would reduce the
         population.
15.7.39. Much like it has been documented and observed at the Nurek Reservoir, it is likely that the targeted
         species will be those that are introduced or common, such as carp and marinka. As detailed in
         Volume 2, Annex 15.1 (Critical Habitat Assessment), no Critical Habitat for fish species has been
         identified upstream of Rogun. Therefore, the impact to the sensitive receptor will be small, resulting
         in a maximum minor effect (not significant).
         Mitigation and Enhancement Measures
15.7.40. The mitigation described within 15.6.23 will extend across an area sufficient to deliver NNL for any
         additional, limited losses to juniper woodland as a result of increased human pressures.

         Residual Effects
15.7.41. Implementation of the additional measures will reduce the impact of increased human pressures to
         juniper woodland to very small, resulting in a negligible effect (not significant).

15.7.42. The residual effect to Aquatic Biodiversity (fish) from increased human pressure will remain minor.

         Change in habitat composition
15.7.43. A new reservoir in the landscape is a change in habitat composition with respect to the existing river
         and the surrounding terrestrial habitats and will be an impact that materializes incrementally
         (although is considered here at FSL). This will adversely affect those species currently reliant upon
         lotic conditions but has the potential to benefit other species that are attracted to lentic habitats.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                  SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 15-43
15.7.44. Regarding terrestrial species, the change in habitat composition has the potential to permanently
         benefit those species that make use of riparian/marginal habitats (such as otters, species of foraging
         bats that utilise such habitats – e.g. Savi’s pipistrelle), which are known to be present within the
         Vakhsh River system.
         Impact Assessment
         Sensitive Fauna
15.7.45. The reservoir will offer a more stable environment for foraging animals (even when considered
         seasonal level fluctuations, when compared to the current meltwater fed Vakhsh system here), and
         will also significantly increase the freshwater resource, meaning that the AoI has the potential to
         support a greater number of otters territories when considered from a spatial perspective. However,
         reservoirs can offer suboptimal habitat conditions for otters when considering other environmental
         factors (Basto et al. 2011), especially when compared with the pre-reservoir watercourse habitat
         (Pedroso and Santos-Reis 2012), especially those with predominately deep water, which the
         species tends to avoid (Melissen 2000); at the same time, studies have also shown that reservoirs
         can offer more favourable conditions to the species in areas with pronounced water level
         fluctuations (Pedroso and Santos-Reis 2012). With this in mind, it is feasible that the upstream
         reservoir extents, where conditions will be shallower, will end up supporting a larger otter population,
         although given the recognised uncertainty discussed (and also limited current plans for
         ongoing/future fisheries management of the new reservoir), it is therefore more appropriate to
         conclude an impact that is beneficial to very small adverse and therefore a negligible effect in
         respect of this impact, with the current small otter population unlikely to be reduced.
15.7.46. Depending upon the terrestrial habitat being submerged, new reservoir habitats in the landscapes
         can support a greater diversity of bird species than the pre-flooding baseline (e.g. Bohada-Murillo et
         al. 2023). Nidup and Dorji (2024) in their study impacts from the Kurichu Dam, Bhutan identified
         increased avian diversity in the new reservoir, in particular for lentic species, while Nasruddin-
         Roshidi et al. (2021) in a study of a Malaysian hydropower project reported increasing species
         richness following a decrease associated with clearance of forest habitat (again, the relative
         importance of the pre-flooding habit is a key consideration here, with this example being a habitat
         that supports a far richer bird assemblage than is currently present within the Rogun flooding zone),
         although it is accepted that this will likely include many common, pioneer species. It is reasonable to
         assume that species with a preference for lentic systems (such as osprey, pochard and other
         waterfowl) may benefit from the new waterbody in the landscape, while species with a preference for
         lotic systems such as white-capped water redstart will likely be adversely impacted. Given the
         relatively uncertainty over how local bird populations will react to the new reservoir in the landscape
         and when considering this assemblage as a whole, it is therefore more appropriate to conclude a
         neutral effect in respect of this impact.
15.7.47. With all the above in mind, it is feasible that any loss in stopover resource will be offset by the
         increased resource provided by the new reservoir, as well as the potential benefits that may arise to
         the wider bird assemblage. The overall impact is therefore predicted to be permanent and
         irreversible, and very small. This will result in an effect that is negligible (not significant).
         Aquatic habitats
15.7.48. The construction of the Rogun Reservoir will result in a change in habitat from a river system (lotic)
         to a lake (lentic), resulting in changes to flow, water depth, water temperature and subsequently


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                    PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                   SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page | 15-44
         species composition, making this a permeant change that is certain and irreversible. Due to the
         similarities with Nurek, the evolution of this change (approximately 16 years) is expected to be
         similar, resulting in a low productivity, oligotrophic lake system once the reservoir has reached its full
         supply level. The difference will be that species within Rogun will have access to upstream habitats
         and tributaries.
15.7.49. Rheophilic species that favour fast flowing waters and shallow rocky areas will likely be displaced
         and therefore an adverse effect is predicted; this applies to Turkestan Catfish (the species’
         population is considered stable and expected to occur in more than 2000 km of river and many more
         than 10 populations (Karimov, 2020)).
15.7.50. The first two years after completion of the Nurek Reservoir (the filling phase), the waters were
         considered relatively sterile, with very low nutrient content (ESIA, 2014). As the reservoir continued
         to fill, the surrounding vegetated landscape became inundated, increasing the nutrient content of the
         water. These nutrients promoted the growth of algae (phytoplankton) and zooplankton, which
         formed the basis of a food web, and supported the growth of fish within the reservoir (ESIA, 2014).
15.7.51. Once the reservoir had reached its full supply level, the input of organic materials from upstream
         was not enough to maintain a productive ecosystem, and many of the fish species within the
         reservoir disappeared. This cycle was demonstrated by the introduction of Northern Whitefish
         (Peled) at the beginning of the filling phase. Northern Whitefish is a pelagic plankton feeder that
         flourished during the first few years after introduction but ceased to exist once the reservoir reached
         full supply level due to a lack of nutrient inputs. Various species of trout and carp were also
         introduced with limited success (ESIA, 2014).
15.7.52. Similar to fish, the change in habitat will affect different aquatic macroinvertebrates in different ways.
         For example, the Trichoptera (caddisflies) and Ephemeroptera (mayflies) species recorded that
         prefer fast flowing riffle habitats will be negatively impacted, while species such as Hemiptera (bugs)
         and Coleoptera (beetles) may thrive. Field sampling and eDNA analysis found that Diptera (flies)
         dominated the aquatic macroinvertebrate community, with non-biting midges (Chironomidae) most
         widely spread and abundant through the AoI.
15.7.53. The magnitude of this impact for aquatic biodiversity in the landscape is considered to be large and
         the adverse effect is considered to be moderate (significant), with approximately 80 km of river (less
         than 15%) lost upstream of the Rogun Dam. The Vakhsh River (main channel) extends a further
         460 km upstream from the Rogun Dam to its headwaters in the Pamirs Mountains.
15.7.54. The change in flows between Rogun and Nurek have been considered under ‘Changes to
         downstream flow regimes’.
         Mitigation and Enhancement Measures
15.7.55. No additional mitigation measures are possible given that the habitat change will be a permanent
         component of the Project.

         Residual Effects
15.7.56. As a maximum, the residual effects from a change in habitat composition will remain moderate (in
         relation to aquatic biodiversity).




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                    PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                   SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page | 15-45
         Loss of tributaries and short-range migration
15.7.57. The Project will result in the loss of access to tributaries and the ability for fish to undertake short-
         range migration.
15.7.58. Fish migration into the tributaries of the Vakhsh River will be limited as a result of the gradient,
         however the lower portions of these inlets are considered to be important for fish as refuge areas off
         the main channel, as they offer clear, cooler waters and are often used for spawning. Further to the
         tributaries impacted by the inundation, this assessment also includes the tributaries downstream
         between Rogun and Nurek, as well as littoral habitats that will be impacted by the fluctuating water
         levels.
15.7.59. Operating the Rogun HPP will result in water level fluctuations in the reservoir, leading to shoreline
         displacement and loss of a productive littoral zone, potentially resulting in sediment deposition and
         inaccessibility. Simulated water levels in the Nurek and Rogun Reservoirs show the variation of the
         water levels due to the regulation of the discharges from the HPP to meet the operating rule
         (Volume 1, Chapter 13 (Water)).
15.7.60. The extent of the littoral zone is regulated by the water level and is of disproportionate importance to
         the food web in Lakes (Hirsch, et.al., 2014). The freshwater littoral zone is the area near the shore
         characterized by submerged, floating, or emergent vegetation and is driven by the presence of
         sunlight at the sediment level (BD Editors, 2017). Littoral plants and algae account for a major part
         of the primary production of lakes and are particularly important in clear high-altitude lakes with low
         nutrient concentration (Hirsch, et.al., 2014). Therefore, any regular and extreme changes in water
         levels are likely to have an adverse effect on ecosystem processes because it affects the littoral
         zone.
15.7.61. The extent of the loss of tributaries and short-range migration as a result of the project will be limited
         to the Project AoI affected by the inundation and/or downstream fluctuations in flow.
         Impact Assessment
15.7.62. Given that inundation is inevitable and the reservoir will be permanent, the loss of tributaries and
         associated short-term migration is considered medium. No Critical Habitat for fish species or
         aquatic macroinvertebrates has been identified upstream of Rogun. Despite these tributaries
