         continuing to discharge into the lake, the fluctuating water level as a result of HPP operating rules
         will likely reduce the refuge potential in the lower portions of these inlets potentially resulting in
         deposition of sediments and inaccessibility. A site visit to the Nurek Reservoir provided an example
         of how the shoreline may look, with a barren littoral zone resulting from the fluctuating water levels.
         To be conservative, the inundation effect of these habitats is likely to be moderate (significant) for
         aquatic biodiversity because of the number of tributaries that will be affected.
         Mitigation and Enhancement Measures
15.7.63. No additional mitigation measures have been identified as HPP peaking will occur, and downstream
         discharges are already occurring.

         Residual Effects
15.7.64. The residual effect to aquatic biodiversity from the loss of tributaries and short-range migration will
         remain moderate.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                     SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page | 15-46
15.8      TRANSBOUNDARY EFFECTS
          TERRESTRIAL BIODIVERSITY
15.8.1.   No transboundary effects upon terrestrial biodiversity have been identified. The only receptor for
          which transboundary effects have been considered is migratory birds, whose conservation is often
          dependent upon suitable habitat/features being maintained across a number of countries. No
          significant adverse effects were identified for migrating birds and therefore transboundary effects are
          not considered any further.
          AQUATIC BIODIVERSITY
15.8.2.   No transboundary effects upon aquatic biodiversity have been identified. The cumulative impact
          assessment concluded that Rogun may have added to a small extent to a further reduction in short-
          range fish migrations from Nurek reservoir upstream. Other than that, the situation in the
          downstream area, especially concerning Tigrovaya Balka and fish migrations, will not be changed
          due to Rogun.
15.8.3.   As per Volume 1, Chapter 13 (Water) the flows downstream from Nurek are not significantly
          impacted by Rogun and the proposed operating rule. The flows during the winter months will be less
          variable with Rogun in place when compared to the releases from Nurek without Rogun. The total
          volume of water released from the Nurek Reservoir is the same as the total volume flowing into the
          Rogun reservoir. The reservoir and the HPP operating rule, changes the pattern of flow. The
          minimum flows required for energy generation are met which are sufficient to meet the
          environmental flow requirements and current irrigation demands.
15.8.4.   The construction of the Project is therefore not expected to have any further adverse transboundary
          effects on aquatic biodiversity.

15.9      MITIGATION AND ENHANCEMENT MEASURES
15.9.1.   Mitigation and enhancement will be secured through adoption and implementation of a Biodiversity
          Management Plan (BMP – see Volume 3, A4 of this ESIA).
15.9.2.   As part of delivery of the BMP, a Biodiversity Constraints Tracker (BCT) will be produced to facilitate
          timely implementation of each required action. The BCT will list all known and emerging biodiversity
          constraints together with actions required to ensure ongoing protection and compliance with the
          BMP. It will be a simple tracker tool that will be managed by the Lead ECoW to help programme all
          elements of the BMP delivery; as such, it will need to be regularly updated with Project programme
          details (such clearance programming, construction activities outside of the HPP site, inundation
          progress, etc.). The BCT will also serve as a useful auditing tool, demonstrating how the
          requirements of the BMP have been delivered.
15.9.3.   Mitigation and enhancement measures are split into two separate categories: those that are required
          to address specific effects identified for a particular receptor as included within section 15.6 and
          15.7; and those which will reduce effects to multiple receptors, as well as ensuring biodiversity
          losses are reduced and animal welfare measures and general environmental good practice is
          adhered to (detailed within this section).
15.9.4.   A Lead Ecological Clerk of Works (ECoW) (or similar title) will be employed to support Rogun JSC
          and DFZ to provide biodiversity support in advance of, and throughout project construction/operation
          (as defined within this Chapter); given the scale of the Project. The scope of the Lead ECoW role


          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                             Page | 15-47
          will vary, depending on programme activities, but will be responsible for ensuring the requirements
          of the BMP are delivered and to facilitate proactive biodiversity protection during construction
          activities (including any clearance activities within the flooding zone).
15.9.5.   Given the scale of the Project the Lead ECoW should possess at least 10 years of experience of
          supervision of larger infrastructure projects and have demonstrable experience of working in Central
          Asia. The Lead ECoW will forecast the need for and be responsible for the recruiting and
          management of a ECoW Support Team who collectively will deliver ECoW supervision at peaks of
          requirement. It is anticipated that the Lead ECoW will be a permanent position throughout
          Construction and into the first three years of Operation. The Lead ECoW will determine and consult
          with other specialists (such as botanists, bat experts, ornithologists, etc.) as required depending on
          the mitigation being delivered. The Lead ECoW will need to be supported by a ring-fenced budget
          for the duration of ECoW services.
          PRE-CONSTRUCTION WALKOVERS
15.9.6.   In order that the mitigation strategy is fully informed and to ensure ongoing validity of important
          biodiversity baseline information in line with GIIP (data are generally considered valid for up to
          between 12-18 months (as per CIEEM 2019)), additional pre-construction survey effort will be
          required. This requirement reflects the staggered time over which impacts will occur (as per the
          reservoir inundation by year level table within Volume 1, Chapter 3 (Project Description) of this
          ESIA), and effects will be realised. Some of these walkovers will also inform receptor-specific
          mitigation measures but these walkover surveys will primarily focus on:
           Floodplain habitat – to fully inform compensation measures for this habitat, as part of the NNLF.
           Juniper woodland habitat – as above for floodplain habitat, as part of the NNLF.
           Pre-clearance biodiversity walkovers of areas where vegetation clearance will be needed,
            including (but not limited to): pre-clearance of the juniper woodland within the maximum flooding
            zone; Forestry Agency land-clearing in advance of pre-inundation land-clearing; and DFZ
            contractor land-clearing in advance of construction of the left bank road and new infrastructure as
            part of the resettlement program. Walkovers will focus on identifying nesting birds and roosting
            bats/bat roosts.
           Flooding zone walkovers of suitable habitat for target species of reptile and TRB plants.
           Surveys of suitable bat roost features – to inform ongoing mitigation for this receptor in light of
            recognised limitations to the baseline data collection for this species group.
           Walkover of areas outside the flooding zone to identify ‘receptor’ sites for the translocation of
            TRB flora species.
15.9.7.   The timing of these walkovers is summarized in Table 15-5. Findings from these surveys will be
          added from existing baseline information to the BCT and schedule of mitigation and will be used to
          update the BMP. The BMP will remain a ‘live’ document throughout its implementation.

15.10 GENERAL BIODIVERSITY
15.10.1. Construction activities will be timed to avoid the breeding bird season where possible. This will be
          informed by pre-construction walkovers and at present is considered most applicable to areas where
          tree/scrub vegetation clearance will be undertaken. Concurrent with this, the ECoW will conduct
          walkovers of ongoing and near-future DFZ areas that are designated for clearance, with the Forestry
          Agency and construction contractor land-clearing representatives; in particular in locations where
          there is potential to harm nesting birds, other wildlife and sensitive flora through ground and
          vegetation clearance. The frequency of these walkovers will be informed by DFZ site clearance
          programmes, with walkovers completed no later than one-month in advance of all planned clearance
          activities or at the discretion of the ECoW. It is expected that this will only be required for areas
          where clearance of more natural vegetation and other habitat features is planned.


          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                       PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                      SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                   Page | 15-49
15.10.2. The Lead ECoW will undertake/manage pre-works checks of suitable areas and advise on mitigation
         to protect any bird nests identified. Specifically, this will comprise avoidance of known nest sites or
         areas of suitable nesting habitat being excluded until such point that the young birds have fledged
         the nest (or the nest has failed for natural reasons). To help minimise the impact of this measure
         upon the overall construction programme where works cannot avoid this period, necessary
         vegetation clearance shall take place in advance of works and outside the breeding bird season so
         that habitat suitability is reduced, and birds are dissuaded from nesting in these areas during
         construction.
15.10.3. The ECoW will upskill Forestry Agency staff in identifying and removing animals such as reptiles and
         amphibians which may not be able to disperse quickly enough and would therefore be at risk of
         mortality during Forestry Agency clearance activities. This will be done as part of the toolbox talks
         that the Lead ECoW will regularly undertake. Any such animals will be moved to nearby suitable
         habitat identified through pre-construction walkovers.
15.10.4. An important component of the pre-construction survey will be management of Bathurst burr spread
         during construction and operation (flooding), and identification of any new invasive plant species in
         the area. An Invasive Species Management Plan (ISMP) will be produced which will detail measures
         to be adopted to stop the spread of such species along the construction corridor and across the
         flooding zone, in particular around/within the juniper woodland habitat. This Plan will be delivered as
         a requirement of the BMP and will be produced prior to flooding at 1150m asl (timing to be
         determined – see Volume 1, Chapter 3 (Project Description) of this ESIA. This will allow time to
         implement any proactive measures required.
15.10.5. The ECoW will deliver a series of ‘toolbox talks’, including site inductions, to relevant Forestry
         Agency staff, DFZ construction workers and supervisors to raise awareness of particular ecological
         issues. This will include, but is not limited to:
          Ecological exclusion zones and working around features of increased ecological sensitivity (e.g.
           nesting birds, mammal burrows, reptile hibernacula, watercourses, etc.);
          Construction site good practice (e.g. keeping the construction site safe for wildlife when not in
           use; covering excavations, sensitive use of lighting, etc.);
          How to recognise particular animal or plant species (including invasive species) or groups;
          What to do if particular animal or plant species are encountered; and
          Awareness-raising in relation to hunting/poaching of sensitive fauna, and prohibition in the
           construction workers Code of Conduct, as required in the ESMP.
15.10.6. The ECoW will also undertake ad hoc monitoring of relevant works areas to check implementation of
         toolbox talk content.
         COMPLEMENTARY MEASURES
15.10.7. There is the potential for a beneficial impact to habitats across the Project through the planting plans
         that will be delivered as part of site rehabilitation (see Volume 3, Site Rehabilitation Plan). Planting
         will comprise native species of local or regional provenance and will be informed by inputs from the
         ECoW with additional specialist inputs sourced as required, such as from botanical expert. Similarly,
         there will be planting required to assist with slope stabilisation and to prevent or minimise soil
         erosion, which is described in Volume 1 Chapter 12 (Geology and Soils) of this ESIA. As above,



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                  SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 15-50
         this planting will comprise native species of local or regional provenance and planting plans will
         include inputs from the Project ECoW to ensure species composition is of optimal value to
         biodiversity while remaining cognisant of its primary function.
15.10.8. Of particular potential benefit from planting plans required to provide soil stabilisation will be creation
         of woodland/scrub habitat adjacent to the reservoir that will provide a nesting and roosting resource
         for birds and bats, respectively as well as shelter for other species that currently use the riparian
         corridor, such as otter. Similarly, the increased habitat structure and heterogeneity that will be
         created will improve conditions for foraging by these species, in particular insectivorous bats given
         the greater abundance of insects supported in woodland habitats compared to unwooded areas.

15.11 MONITORING
15.11.1. An appropriate monitoring programme will be a critical component in ensuring the ongoing success
         of mitigation described above. Monitoring has been referenced within the above sections and has
         been detailed more specifically within BMP. Monitoring requirements and methods shall be reviewed
         and updated periodically, e.g. on an annual basis, to ensure they are still fit for purpose. The overall
         monitoring commitment should be partnered with the Project capacity building objective, with the
         Project identifying individuals to be increasingly involved on the Project’s biodiversity monitoring,
         through a training and mentoring programme overseen and managed by the ECoW.

16      ECOSYSTEM SERVICES
16.1.1. This chapter presents the impact assessment for Ecosystems Services for the Project. Ecosystems
        Services impacts are described in Section 16.4 using the assessment methodology described in
        Chapter 5. A summary of the results of the impact assessment is presented in Section 16.7.
16.1.2. This chapter is organized as follows:
           Legislative Framework, Policy And Guidance
           Assessment Methodology
           Potential Impacts and Effects
           Mitigation Measures
           Residual Effects
           Summary

16.2    LEGISLATIVE FRAMEWORK, POLICY AND GUIDANCE
16.2.1. This chapter follows the same international guidance as the Chapter 15 of this ESIA, namely:
        ▪   World Bank, 2018. Environmental and Social Standard (ESS) 6: Biodiversity Conservation and
            Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources;
        ▪   European Investment Bank, 2018. Environmental and Social Standards: Guidance Note for
            Standard 3 on Biodiversity and Ecosystem;
        ▪   Asian Infrastructure Investment Bank, 2024. Environmental and Social Framework: Biodiversity
            Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources;
        ▪   Asian Development Bank, 2009. Safeguard Policy Statement: Biodiversity Conservation and
            Sustainable Natural Resource Management.

16.3    ASSESSMENT METHODOLOGY
16.3.1. The spatial scope of the assessment includes the Project area and Area of Influence (AoI) and the
        temporal scope includes the Project construction and operational phases.
16.3.2. Ecosystem services are the benefits that the natural environment provides to people. As per the
        WRI guidance, these can be categorised as:
        ▪   Provisioning services: the goods or products obtained from ecosystems;
        ▪   Regulating services: the contributions to human well-being arising from an ecosystem’s control
            of natural processes;
        ▪   Cultural services: the non-material contributions of ecosystems to human well-being; and
        ▪   Supporting services: the natural processes that maintain other ecosystem services.
16.3.3. The ecosystem services assessment is informed by the process outlined by WRI’s guidance: World
        Resource Institute (WRI), 2013. Weaving Ecosystem Services into Impact Assessment. The
        approach follows six steps:
        ▪   Step 1: Identify relevant ecosystem services (scoping stage);
        ▪   Step 2: Prioritize relevant ecosystem services (start of the baseline and impact analysis stage);


        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number Project REF                                                       May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 16-1
         ▪   Step 3: Define the scope and information needs of the ecosystem services assessment;
         ▪   Step 4: Establish the baseline for priority ecosystem services;
         ▪   Step 5: Assess project impacts on priority ecosystem services; (end of the baseline and impact
             analysis stage); and
         ▪   Step 6: Mitigate impacts of the project on priority ecosystem services (mitigation stage).
         ASSUMPTIONS AND LIMITATIONS
16.3.4. This is assessment complements the Biodiversity and Social assessment. Its aim is to provide a
        general overview of ecosystem services that are likely to be impacted by the project and
        demonstrate the link between biodiversity and social impacts.
16.3.5. This assessment was applied retrospectively. The analysis is based on pre-existing data; primary
        data was not collected for the purpose of the ecosystem services assessment.
16.3.6. In light of these characteristics:
             •   The assessment comprehensively investigates provisioning, regulating, cultural and
                 supporting ecosystem services and identifies those that are key to the Project.
             •   To manage uncertainty, which is a known limitation of a high-level and retrospective
                 assessment, the precautionary approach has been applied to the extent that it was deemed
                 appropriate based on professional opinion and experience. The precautionary approach is
                 recommended by ESS6 and follows the principle of, where there is a lack of information, a
                 cautious scenario is applied. In the case of this ESIA, this means that where there is potential
                 for an adverse impact but there is uncertainty regarding the impact likelihood or the
                 significance of the consequence, a significant adverse impact should be assumed and the
                 mitigation should address a significant impact.
         BASELINE ASSESSMENT
16.3.7. The findings of the baseline studies are presented in the Annex 7.1: Environmental and Baseline
        Conditions Report. The Baseline Report also includes gender considerations (section 11.3).
16.3.8. The baseline data was collected from:
         ▪   Previous ESIA documentation;
         ▪   Information from other technical workstreams, specifically Chapter 15: Biodiversity, Chapter
             17: Socio-economics and Chapter 20: Cultural Heritage. This information includes the
             primary data from field surveys and stakeholder engagement for these assessments. Further
             details regarding the baseline data collection exercise are provided within the Chapter 15 and
             Chapter 17 of this ESIA.
         ▪   Academic literature and other documentation available online.
16.3.9. Data was interpreted to describe ecosystem services across the Project’s AoI to understand the
        socio-economic impact on communities across the wider study area.
16.3.10. Ecosystem services can be identified with an appropriate level of confidence through a desktop
         study, since certain natural features (i.e. receptors) are known to provide specific types of
         ecosystem services. Primary data, collected predominantly through the Social and Biodiversity
         Technical workstreams, confirmed the presence of most ecosystem services.


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number Project REF                                                        May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page | 16-2
16.3.11. For ecosystem services which are only potentially present, we have applied the precautionary
         approach which should be applied as recommended by ESS6 (see definition in paragraph 16.3.6).
16.3.12. The identification of ecosystem services followed the methodology outlined by the document
         developed by the World Resource Institute (WRI) in 2013: Weaving Ecosystem Services Into Impact
         Assessment. This involved identifying the main ecosystem and habitat types, protected or important
         areas and species, and key abiotic resources (e.g., watercourses, geological features); then refining
         the possible ecosystem services produced by these elements and used by local or global
         beneficiaries or the Project.
16.3.13. A list of ecosystem services confirmed as being present or potentially present within the scope of the
         project was developed. To ensure rigor, ecosystem services were classified by:
        ▪   The Receptor (i.e., the ecological feature of interest);
        ▪   The receptor’s Location;
        ▪   The Type of Ecosystem Service provided by the ecological feature (General class, CICES
            group, CICES group code). These were identified as per the recognized International
            Classification of Ecosystem Services (CICES): Towards a Common International Classification
            of Ecosystem Services (CICES) for Integrated Environmental and Economic Accounting, V5.2,
            European Environment Agency1;
        ▪   The Beneficiaries of the ecosystem service (e.g. local communities, global communities, the
            Project, tourists, etc);
        ▪   A Short Description of the Benefit of the ecosystem service provided by the receptor;
        ▪   Prioritization, meaning whether or not the ecosystem service was retained for the impact
            analysis;
        ▪   A Reason for Prioritization. Ecosystem services were prioritized as per the best practice ESS 6
            criterium: "Any important ecosystem services that are provided by the biodiversity and living
            natural resources that may be affected by the project, and their value to project-affected and
            other interested parties."; and
        ▪   Source of Information, meaning the data source of the ecosystem service.
16.3.14. The results of the impact assessment are presented in Section 16.4. A summary is also provided in
         Section 16.5.2.
        RECEPTOR SENSITIVITY EVALUATION
16.3.15. The sensitivity of an ecosystem service estimates the value of the ecosystem service to relevant
         beneficiaries. It considers both the level of dependence that beneficiaries have on the ecosystem
         service, as well as the level of importance that ecosystem service is given by beneficiaries (e.g.,
         social, community, cultural, recreational and economic).




        1 https://cices.eu




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number Project REF                                                        May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page | 16-3
16.3.16. The valuation has been made using available guidance and information, and considers the
         distribution, extent and condition of the receptors. The sensitivity categories are provided in Table
         16-1.

         Table 16-1 - Receptor Sensitivity Categories

          Receptor              Description
          Sensitivity

          High                  Beneficiaries are highly dependent on the ecosystem service and it is crucial for their
                                survival and maintenance of an adequate quality of life (i.e. not living in hardship).
                                Impacts on such an ecosystem service must be mitigated as a requirement for the
                                ESIA to ensure that the ecosystem service is not compromised in terms of service
                                provision.

          Medium                The ecosystem service is valuable to beneficiaries, but they are not highly dependent
                                on the ecosystem service, and it is not crucial for their survival and maintenance of
                                an adequate quality of life (i.e. not living in hardship).
                                Mitigation is required in the ESIA if the assessment identifies that the significance of
                                the impact is major or moderate.

          Low                   The ecosystem service is of limited value to beneficiaries. They are not highly
                                dependent on the ecosystem service, and it is not crucial for their survival and
                                maintenance of an adequate quality of life (i.e. not living in hardship).
                                To follow best practice of the ESS 6, these ecosystem services should be mitigated
                                where possible; however, this is not a requirement for the ESIA.

16.3.17. In line with the above definitions, Table 16-2 presents the receptor sensitivity for each receptor
         prioritized in the baseline assessment.

        IMPACT SIGNIFICANCE
16.3.18. The assessment considers the effects of the Project with the application of mitigation linked to
         resettlement. This gives an indication of the need for other forms of mitigation to be implemented.
         The likely effectiveness of that mitigation has then been considered and a residual effect stated.
         Impacts were assessed for both the construction and operational phases.
16.3.19. Each impact is assessed as follows:
        ▪     The Receptor impacted and the Receptor Sensitivity;
        ▪     The Frequency of the impact;
        ▪     The Likelihood and certainty of the impact occurring;
        ▪     The Extent of the impact;



        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                           PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number Project REF                                                                May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                        Page | 16-5
        ▪     The Duration of the impact and whether it is reversible;
        ▪     The Magnitude of the impact. This is determined as per the criteria definitions in Table 16-4;
        ▪     The Effect of the impact (e.g. whether it is adverse or beneficial);
              •     Whether the impact is Direct or Indirect;
              •     The Significance of the effect, based on the results of the above criteria;
              •     Identification of any Mitigation; and
              •     The Residual Significance.

        Table 16-3 - Impact Magnitude

            Magnitude         Criteria

                              Major effect on the ecosystem service, likely to threaten the long-term integrity of the
            Large
                              service provision.

                              Moderate effect on the ecosystem service but would not threaten the long-term integrity
            Medium
                              of the service provision.

                              Noticeable effects, but either of sufficiently small scale or short duration to cause little
            Small
                              harm to the service provided.

            Very Small        Limited effects that cause no harm to the service provided.

                              The ecosystem service is affected positively, increasing the provision/quality of the
            Beneficial
                              ecosystem service.

        IMPACTS
16.3.20. Impacts are defined as the direct or indirect changes produced by the Project activities on the
         environment, able to influence the state or quality of one or more receptors.
16.3.21. The list below provides a summary of the impacts considered in this assessment in construction and
         operational phases. These overlap greatly with those identified in the Chapter 15 of this ESIA,
         which provides more detail as to the nature of these impacts.
16.3.22. Relevant construction phase impacts comprise:
        ▪     I11-1: Land and vegetation clearance within the flooding zone;
        ▪     I11-2: Hunting of local wild mammals and birds and fishing by the construction work force;
        ▪     I11-3: Reductions in water quality due to sediment release into the river system from the
              deposition of construction and tunnelling waste into the river, release of contaminants into the
              river and localized water quality issues due to discharges from construction facilities and
              reduction in air quality due to release of dust and emissions of pollutants;
        ▪     I11-4: Water abstraction for construction activities;
        ▪     I11-5: Injury or killing of fauna as a result of construction activities;
        ▪     I11-6: Disturbance – including noise and vibration and visual disturbance;



        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                                  PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number Project REF                                                                       May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                               Page | 16-6
        ▪   I11-7: Introduction and spread of alien or invasive species.
16.3.23. Relevant operational phase impacts will comprise:
        ▪   I11-8: Flooded area resulting in habitat loss and change in habitat type (new reservoir within the
            landscape)
        ▪   I11-9: Water pollution arising from new reservoir by altering temperature balances, water
            chemistry and sedimentation;
        ▪   I11-10: Barrier effect arising from new reservoir in the landscape.

16.4    POTENTIAL IMPACTS AND EFFECTS
        CONSTRUCTION IMPACTS
16.4.1. Land clearance (I11-1) is a direct adverse impact that will affect several ecosystem services
        present in the Project AoI. Land clearance involves the removal of vegetation in the area which will
        become flooded as the dam is constructed. For all ecosystem services, the impacts are certain to
        occur and are permanent. The following receptors are adversely affected by land clearance:
        ▪   Wild plants (R11-1, of medium sensitivity as per Table 16-2): the impact is of medium
            magnitude, as wild plants are more widely present in the region and could provide the
            ecosystem service in resettled locations.
        ▪   Trees used for memorials and shrines (R11-2, of medium sensitivity): the impact magnitude
            is medium as the community will no longer be present to benefit from this ecosystem service;
            however they will still be able to benefit from the knowledge that these trees are still alive and
            present. It is the individual trees in their specific location that are important to local communities,
            therefore other trees of the same species present more widely in the region could not provide the
            ecosystem service.
        ▪   Trees associated with local stories & taboo (R11-3, of medium sensitivity): same reasoning
            as R11-2 above.
        ▪   Croplands and orchards (R11-6, of high sensitivity): the magnitude of the impact is very
            small. There will be an impact but resettlement as part of this project have organised
            replacement lands and alternative incomes for local communities. There will be a delay between
            the creation on new orchards and the time they can be harvest. Therefore, even though
            agricultural lands will no longer be available in that location, resettlement will provide agricultural
            lands for use and or alternative forms of income. There will therefore be no harm to service
            provision.
        ▪   Pastures (R11-7, of high sensitivity): same reasoning as R11-6 above.
        ▪   Remaining woody areas, including remnants of Juniper Forest (R11-8, of medium
            sensitivity): the magnitude of the impact is small. This is due to several factors: (a) resettled
            communities will be provided with alternative wood resources (see reasoning given for R11-6
            above); (b) resettled communities will have access to managed forest areas for the cultural and
            social benefit; (c) the area occupied by these forests is very limited, therefore carbon
            sequestration benefits are negligible on a global scale; and (d) the erosion service they provide
            will no longer be relevant once the area is flooded.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number Project REF                                                           May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                   Page | 16-7
16.4.2. The construction phase impacts R11-1/2/3/6/7 are local, as the ecosystem services they provide
        benefit local communities within the AoI. Carbon sequestration provides non-localized benefits,
        however these are very minor at a scale due to the small size of the remnant woody areas that are
        flooded.
16.4.3. The significance of land clearance impacts on croplands and pastures will be negligible; impacts on
        wild plants and remaining woody areas will be minor; and impacts on trees used for
        memorials/shrines and local stories/taboo will be minor. As discussed in the Chapter 20 of this
        ESIA, mitigation will be implemented in the form of a Cultural Heritage Management Plan for R11-
        2/3. This lowers the significance of the residual impact of R11-2/3 to negligible.
16.4.4. Fishing and hunting by the construction workforce, the release of contaminants and
        sediments, and water abstraction (I11-2/3/4) are direct adverse impacts that may are likely to
        affect freshwater streams (R11-11). This is because such activities could harm freshwater flora,
        fauna and drinking water quality and quantity. The characteristics of these impacts means that the
        impact would likely occur periodically, could possibly occur, is of regional extent (encompassing
        downstream communities), and is of medium-term duration (during construction phase). The
        magnitude of the impact is small, as the intermittent nature of the impact would cause minimal harm
        the integrity of freshwater streams. It has therefore been concluded that the significance of the
        impact is minor, due to the unlikelihood and medium duration of the impact. No mitigation has been
        planned for this impact.
16.4.5. All construction impacts (I11-1 to I11-7) are direct and indirect adverse impacts that will affect
        species (R11-12) and habitats (R11-9). This is because habitats, fauna and flora will be removed
        through land clearance or could be disrupted by other construction activities. The characteristics of
        these impacts means that the impacts are likely to be periodic (for construction activities) or constant
        (for land clearance). It is certain to occur and is of long-term or even permanent duration. It is
        predominantly local to the AoI, with some benefits provided to the global community (e.g. genetic
        information providing a source for undiscovered treatments/pharmaceuticals/technologies). The
        magnitude of the impacts for both receptors is medium, as (a) habitats and species present outside
        of the AoI will continue providing ecosystem services, or (b) ecosystem services that are lost in the
        cleared area will no longer be relevant when the area is flooded. It has therefore been concluded
        that the magnitude of the impact is moderate.
16.4.6. As discussed in the Chapter 15 of this ESIA, mitigation will be implemented in the form of a
        Resettlement Action Plan, Livelihood Restoration Plan, Biodiversity Management Plan and No Net
        Loss Framework. This lowers the significance of the residual impact to minor, as equivalent habitats
        and species will be enhanced in other areas to provide these broad supporting ecosystem services.
         OPERATIONAL IMPACTS
16.4.7. Flooded area (I11-8) is a direct adverse impact that are a continuation of the land clearance
        impacts (I11-1) in the construction phase. Land was cleared in order for inundation to occur during
        operation. This means that the characteristics of the impacts to receptors R11-1/3/6/7/8 and the
        significance of the impacts remain the same as those described in paragraph 16.4.1.
16.4.8. Trees used for memorials and shrines (R11-2, of medium sensitivity): the impact magnitude is
        large as the flooded landscape will no longer be able to provide this ecosystem service. It is the
        individual trees in their specific location that are important to local communities, therefore other trees
        of the same species present more widely in the region could not provide the ecosystem service.


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                    PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number Project REF                                                         May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 16-8
16.4.9. This is similarly the case for how all operational impacts (I11-8 to I11-10) are a continuation of
        construction impacts on species (R11-12) and habitats (R11-9). During operation the impacts to
        these receptors remain consistent with the construction phase, however during the operation the
        impacts will be constant and permanent. The significance is moderate without mitigation and made
        minor with mitigation.
16.4.10. Flooded area (I11-8) will also directly and adverse impact floodplains (R11-10) and freshwater
         streams (R11-11). This is because these habitats will no longer exist once the area is flooded. The
         characteristics of the impact (frequency, likelihood, extent, duration) are the same as described
         above. The magnitude of the impact on both receptors is small, as although the habitats will no
         longer exists and therefore the ecosystem services will not be provided, (a) these ecosystem
         services will not be relevant at the scale of the flooded reservoir and (b) resettled communities will
         be provided with water and food and recreational opportunities. The significance is minor for both
         receptors. No mitigation has been planned for this impact.

16.5     MITIGATION MEASURES
16.5.1. A list of mitigation plans relevant to the mitigation of ecosystem services is presented in Table 16-4.

16.5.2. As described in the referenced documents in Table 16-4, To reduce potential significant effects, and
        to ensure good practice construction methods, the following measures shall be implemented
16.5.3. Prior to Land clearance (I11-1) across the flooding zone, a repeat survey for notable flora will be
        undertaken within those areas within the flooding zone where species were previously identified,
        along the route of the left bank road and within areas of Natural Habitat.

16.5.4. Findings from the surveys will be added to a Biodiversity Constraints Plan (BCP) for the Project so
        that the nature and location of these findings can inform ongoing mitigation and monitoring. Any rare
        plants encountered will be collected and moved to receptor sites that will be pre-identified in which
        these plants will remain to mitigate the Project-related impacts

16.5.5. To minimize the impacts on Juniper Forest (R11-8), the left bank road design should be finalised to
        avoid all areas of juniper woodland by at least 100m.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                       PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number Project REF                                                            May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                    Page | 16-9
16.5.6. For areas of woodland that will be lost to clearance activities prior to flooding the options for on-site
        mitigation may comprise replacement planting of woodland at various locations around the flooding
        zone and Project site, which should align with complementary planting requirements such as for
        landscaping and soil stability purposes or making use of the aforementioned stabilised water levels
        at Nurek as a result of the constructed Rogun dam to undertake planting there.
16.5.7. Other measures to minimise construction-related disturbance on Species (R11-12) and Habitats
        (R11-9) are included within Volume 1, Chapter 8 (Air Quality) and Chapter 11 (Noise and
        Vibration) of this ESIA. These are summarised below:

          Several bowsers water the road and dampen dust along many sections, but visual observation
           shows this is not entirely adequate and that further effort is required;
          Enforce a speed limit to reduce the dust generated by HGVs;
          Locate stationary machinery (i.e., pumps, compressor, concrete mixing, etc) away from noise-
           sensitive receptors;
          Start-up machinery and vehicles sequentially;
          Select plant and equipment that conforms to Directive 2000/14/EC (noise emission in the
           environment by equipment for use outdoors, or equivalent);
          Reversing alarms without tonal component (i.e., broadband);
          Undertake regular and effective maintenance for the construction machinery and any sound-
           reducing equipment;
          Install temporary acoustic enclosure for noisy equipment;
          Loading and unloading will be done away from noise-sensitive areas;
          Minimise drop height of materials; and
          Switch off equipment when it is not in use.
16.5.8. The removal of Croplands and orchards (R11-6) and Pastures (R11-7) will be mitigated to the
        extent possible through the implementation of RAPs and LRPs. The RAPs and LRPs define and
        quantify the entitlements of PAPs to compensation, livelihood restoration support, and other
        benefits, and are publicized to maximize awareness of these entitlements.
16.5.9. The restoration of livelihoods, social networks and agricultural production takes time, and these
        mitigation measures will take several years to be fully effective. However, if they are implemented in
        full, the impact size will be reduced.

16.6     RESIDUAL EFFECTS
16.6.1. Implementation of the described mitigation will reduce the significance of residual impacts to Minor
        or Negligible for all impacts on ecosystem services. To ensure this is realised, mitigation and
        enhancement measures must be implemented and relevant performance indicators monitored to
        ensure the efficacy of the mitigation measures.

16.7    SUMMARY
16.7.1. A summary of the assessment of Project activities on ecosystem services for the construction phase
        is provided in Table 16-5 and for the operational phase in Table 16-6. The Project will result in
        limited adverse effects to ecosystem services. This is due to the prevailing landscape displaying the
        legacy of ongoing human influence that has resulted in a diminished biodiversity within both
        terrestrial and aquatic habitats.

17      SOCIO-ECONOMICS
17.1.1. This chapter outlines the assessment of potential impacts of the Project on socio-economics, in line
        with the requirements of the World Bank’s Environmental and Social Standard (ESS) 1: Assessment
        and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (2016) and ESS 5: Land
        Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement (2016).
17.1.2. A comprehensive description of social baseline conditions is presented in Annex 7.1: Baseline
        Conditions Report of this ESIA and a summary is provided in Chapter 7: Environmental and
        Social Baseline Conditions of this ESIA. An assessment of the potential Project impacts on socio-
        economics is presented in Section 17.4. Where necessary, recommendations for the avoidance of
        impact, impact minimization and/or long-term management are included.

17.2    LEGISLATIVE FRAMEWORK, POLICY AND GUIDANCE
17.2.1. The socio-economic assessment has considered the relevant legislation, policy and guidance
        framework. The following section sets out the international standards that are relevant to this socio-
        economic impact assessment.
        INTERNATIONAL CONVENTIONS AND STANDARDS
        World Bank Environmental and Social Standards
17.2.2. The following environmental and social standards (ESS) addressing social risks and impacts are
        applicable to this assessment. A full description of each of the standards is included in Chapter 2:
        Legislation and Standards of this ESIA.
           ESS1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts1;
           ESS2: Labor and Working Conditions;
           ESS4: Community Health and Safety;
           ESS5: Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement; and
           ESS10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure.
        TAJIKISTAN LEGISLATION AND REGULATIONS
        Land Ownership
17.2.3. All land within the Republic of Tajikistan is owned by the Government, which is responsible for its
        effective use. The Land Code defines several tenure options for agricultural land, of which the
        primary use rights and secondary use rights are fundamental. Primary use rights include:
         Perpetual use - has no fixed term and is granted to legal entities such as state and cooperative
          agricultural enterprises, public and religious organizations and charities, industrial and
          transportation needs, public enterprises, defense and joint ventures that include foreign entities.




        1 Includes requirements to identify and mitigate impacts on vulnerable groups and definition of vulnerability




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I                |PUBLIC
        Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                                       May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                        Page 17-6
         Limited or fixed-term use - may be granted to legal or physical persons for either a short-term (up
          to 3 years) or long-term (3 to 20 years).
         Life-long inheritable tenure - may be assigned to physical persons or collectives. Physical
          persons must re-register the right in the case of inheritance. This right applies to land-shares
          used to organize a dehkan farm, as well as household (garden) plots.
17.2.4. The only secondary use-right recognized under the Land Code is the right to lease. According to the
        Code, primary rights holders may lease out their plots for a term not exceeding 20 years. The land is
        used in accordance with the state-established land-use standards.
17.2.5. Land Code Article 37 defines the reason under which the right to use land may be terminated:
         Termination of activities by the land user;
         Non-use for two years; and
         Use of the land differing from the use established in the use-rights document.
        Land Acquisition and Resettlement
17.2.6. There is no specific law or policy in Tajikistan that regulates resettlement or land acquisition or
        expropriation of rights to land and immovable property for state or public needs. Moreover, there is
        no single law that comprehensively provides norms and mechanisms for the determination of full
        and fair market value of land or property. The key legislative acts regulating land management
        relations and the ownership rights to immovable properties in the Republic of Tajikistan are:
         Constitution of the Republic of Tajikistan (1994, as amended in 2003): The Constitution of the
          Republic of Tajikistan guarantees citizen’s rights. The legal basis for state acquisition of private
          property for public works is outlined in Article 32 which states “…the property of an individual is
          taken away only on the basis of the law, with the consent of the owner and to meet the
          requirements of the state and society, and with the state paying full compensation.”
         Land Code 1992 amended 2016: The Land Code regulates land relations and provides for "use
          and protection of land and soil fertility ...". Land is subject to rational use and the Code allows
          local authorities to make decisions regarding "rational" land use.
         Civil Code (as amended): The Code regulates the legal status of participants in civil transactions
          and property and non-property relations.
         Civil Procedural Code of the Republic of Tajikistan 2008, as amended: Establishes the
          procedure, rules and conditions for judicial protection in the event of legal proceedings on issues
          related to involuntary resettlement.
         Economic Procedural Code of the Republic of Tajikistan 2008, as amended: Protection in case of
          legal proceedings on matters related to involuntary resettlement.
         Housing Code of the Republic of Tajikistan 2022, as amended: Establishes the right to housing
          and sets out the conditions upon which people can be evicted or deprived of the right to use a
          dwelling.
         Land Valuation Act 2001, as amended: Establishes the legal framework for land valuation and the
          state Land Cadaster.
         Local Government Bodies Act 2004, as amended: Establishes the normative grounds for
          allocation and redistribution of land.
         Land Management Act 2008, as amended: Regulates relations related to the legal basis for
          activities in the field of land management.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I      |PUBLIC
        Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                             May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                              Page 17-7
          Land Administration Act 2016: Obliges the authorities to map and monitor the quality of land,
           including soil contamination, erosion and logging.
          State Registration of and Rights to Real Estate Act 2008 (No. 3), as amended: Establishes the
           legal basis for registration of real estate rights and restrictions on these rights.
          Regulation “about compensation of losses to the land users and losses of agricultural products”
           (approved by the Decree of Government of Republic of Tajikistan, 2000. № 515): Sets out the
           framework for reimbursement for land users’ losses in the case of expropriation.
          Regulation № 641 “On the order of compensation for losses of land users and damage of the
           agricultural production process” (30 December 2011): Sets out an order of compensation of
           losses of land users.
          Resolution of the Government of Tajikistan of January 20, 2009, No. 47, “On the Resettlement
           from the Flood Zone of Rogun HPP of the Population of Rogun City and Nurobod District,” as
           amended: Regulates the resettlement of people living within the flooding zone of Rogun HPP (in
           Rogun city and Nurobod).
          Resolution No. 162 of March 28, 2015, “On modifications and additions to Resolution of the
           Government of Tajikistan No. 47 of January 20, 2009”: Regulates the compensation process for
           resettlement from the Rogun HPP flood zone.
          The National Assessment Standards of the Republic of Tajikistan establish the provisions and
           basic principles of appraisal and set out the requirements for undertaking an appraisal of the
           value of immovable property, movable property, objects of intellectual property, as well as
           requirements for appraisal reports. It is recommended by the law that valuations should be
           carried out no more than 6 months before the transaction for which they are to be used is carried
           out.

17.3     ASSESSMENT METHODOLOGY
17.3.1. The approach to the assessment has been to review the proposed Project and baseline conditions,
        including the population characteristics, followed by the identification of risks related to the sector
        and location of the Project, and the Project activities which could cause an impact.
         RECEPTORS
17.3.2. Receptors have been identified as:
          The economy within the Project Area of Influence (AoI) which includes:
            • Settlements within the resettlement zone;
            • Resettlement sites and nearby settlements;
            • Other settlements within the Flood Zone districts;
          Resettled communities, their assets and livelihoods;
          Community assets and infrastructure; and
          Women and vulnerable groups.
         RECEPTOR SENSITIVITY
17.3.3. To identify the sensitivity of receptors, categories of relative sensitivity are described in Table 17-1,
        with reference to the baseline information.

         Table 17-1 – Criteria for Socio-economic Receptor Sensitivity



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I         |PUBLIC
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                                May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page 17-8
         Sensitivity        Description

         High               A vulnerable2 community, group or aspect of community life that has very limited
                            ability to adapt to Project changes and maintain quality of life, or with very little
                            access to alternative resources of similar quality.
                            Reliance on one principal livelihood which is unsustainable, fragile or lacks legal
                            rights, or is relatively unproductive and/or highly seasonal, such as reliance on
                            migrant labor remittances.
                            Limited ability to pay for food, key services, resources and infrastructure.
                            Receptor will likely struggle to adapt to adverse changes brought about by the
                            Project without extensive support.

         Moderate           A community, group or aspect of community life with some ability to adapt to
                            Project changes and maintain quality of life but has some vulnerability and some
                            access to alternative resources of similar quality.
                            Range of livelihoods present within community which are generally sustainable,
                            relatively productive but which have other risks, such as being highly seasonal or
                            contingent upon other economic sectors.
                            Communities may be able to independently adapt to changes brought about by
                            the Project to a limited extent.

         Low                A community, group or aspect of community life with the ability to adapt to
                            Project changes and maintain quality of life. Receptor has minimal vulnerability,
                            is likely to realize opportunities provided by the Project and has good access to
                            alternative resources of similar quality.
                            Diverse range of livelihoods present within the community which are sustainable,
                            productive and provide consistent income throughout the year.
                            Community is relatively affluent, with a good ability to pay for food, key services,
                            resources and infrastructure.
                            Most member of the community are expected to be able to adapt relatively
                            easily.

        ASSESSMENT OF IMPACT SIGNIFICANCE
17.3.4. The Project impacts have been assessed against baseline conditions that include management
        measures already in place. The significance of the effect is the outcome of the interaction of the
        receptor sensitivity and the impact magnitude and indicates the requirement for mitigation. The post-
        mitigation outcome has also been assessed to determine the significance of the residual effect.
17.3.5. An evaluation of impacts on receptors considers:
           The sensitivity of the impacted receptor;
           The type of impact (positive or negative);
           The timing and frequency of the impacts;
           The likelihood of the identified impact; and




        2 ‘Vulnerability’ is further defined in the social baseline provided in Chapter 7 and Annex 7.1 of this ESIA.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I                  |PUBLIC
        Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                                         May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                          Page 17-9
         The duration and extent of the impact.
17.3.6. The descriptions in Table 17-2 summarize the categories used to assess the magnitude of the
        impact in relation to socio-economics.

        Table 17-2 - Impact Magnitude Categories

         Magnitude          Description

         Large              An extensive change from baseline conditions. An impact expected to affect the
                            wellbeing of many people, social groups and/or businesses over a widespread
                            area, irrespective of duration and reversibility. The scale of the impact may be
                            local, regional or national, or could fundamentally and irreversibly impact
                            significant numbers of individuals. The impact is certain or likely.

         Medium             A distinct change from baseline conditions. An impact expected to affect the
                            wellbeing of a moderate number of people, social groups and/or businesses and
                            which is likely to be temporary (up to five years) and may or may not be
                            reversible. The scale of the impact may be local or regional. More severe impacts
                            on small numbers of individuals cannot be ruled out. The impact is certain or
                            likely. More severe impacts may be possible but are unlikely.

         Small              A perceptible change from baseline conditions. A localized impact expected to
                            affect the wellbeing of a small number of people, social groups and/or
                            businesses, and which is likely to be temporary (up to one year) and
                            reversible. Smaller impacts may be certain or likely. More severe impacts may be
                            possible for a few individuals or in a limited area but are unlikely.

         Very Small         A localized change that is not expected to have a detectable effect on the
                            wellbeing of people or businesses. Change remains within the range commonly
                            experienced by households or the community. More noticeable impacts may be
                            possible for a few individuals or in a limited area. Larger impacts affecting more
                            people are highly unlikely.

         Beneficial         A detectable improvement in the wellbeing or wealth of people or businesses.
                            The scale of the change is not classified for positive impacts.

17.3.7. The receptor sensitivity category in Table 17-1 and the impact magnitude category in Table 17-2
        combine to provide a qualitative assessment of the resulting impact.
17.3.8. The matrix for assessing significance of effects in shown in Chapter 5: Impact Assessment
        Methodology of this ESIA and effects that are classified as moderate or major are considered to be
        significant effects and thus require mitigation to avoid or reduce the impact. Effects classified as
        minor or negligible are considered to be not significant.
        ASSESSMENT OF IMPACT TIMESCALE
17.3.9. Some impacts will be permanent but cause different effects over time. Effects may be initially
        adverse but become positive over time, or vice versa. In other cases, mitigation may become
        effective only over time. In these cases, the impact will be assessed at two points in time
         Year 1 – one year after the commencement of the impact for a given receptor
         Year 5 – five years after the commencement of the impact for a given receptor




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I                   |PUBLIC
        Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                                          May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                           Page 17-10
17.3.10. Impacts that are inherently temporary in nature will not be assessed in this way. It is assumed that,
         after the cessation of a temporary impact, conditions will return to baseline with no significant effects
         remaining, unless otherwise stated.

17.4     POTENTIAL IMPACTS
17.4.1. Based on the criteria set out in Table 17-1, the sensitivity of Socio-economic receptors are
        presented in Table 17-3.

         Table 17-3 - Socio-economic Receptors and Sensitivity

          Receptor             Sensitivity     Rationale

          Economy within       High            The economy is here defined as the total of economic activities taking
          the Project AoI                      place in the area, including business, employment, self-employment,
                                               etc. There are high levels of poverty and unemployment /precarious
                                               employment in the Project AoI. The main source of livelihoods for
                                               many households – the majority in some villages – is remittances from
                                               labor migration, pensions, subsistence agriculture and herding and
                                               public/private sector employment, which could be disrupted by Project
                                               impacts such as resettlement.

          Community            Moderate        Infrastructure in areas that will be inundated by the reservoir will be
          assets and                           permanently lost. Processes for infrastructure replacement are in
          infrastructure                       place, although gaps in provision may occur, e.g., due to construction
                                               delays. Infrastructure that will be lost includes schools, healthcare
                                               facilities, roads, public administration services, and
                                               telecommunications infrastructure, as well as essential businesses
                                               (e.g., shops selling food, household supplies, hardware, car parts,
                                               etc.). Communities will therefore lose access to key services essential
                                               for daily life, wellbeing, and livelihoods.

          Women and            High            Many women in Project-affected communities have no income and
          vulnerable groups                    limited education, limiting their ability to influence Project outcomes, re-
                                               establish their lives after resettlement, and benefit from economic
                                               opportunities. Other groups have been identified that are especially

                                               presented in Table 17-6.

          Resettled            High            Resettlement can increase vulnerability within communities by
          communities,                         temporarily or permanently disrupting livelihoods and social ties. There
          their assets and                     is a particularly high level of sensitivity associated with the
          livelihoods                          resettlement of vulnerable groups such as elderly people, people with
                                               disabilities and households headed by women, whose limited
                                               resources or particular limitations decrease their ability to cope with
                                               disruption and change during resettlement. For example, individuals

                                               will be severely disadvantaged if their communities are separated.

        CONSTRUCTION
        Direct economic impacts of Project employment and procurement
17.4.2. At the peak of the construction phase, later in the 2020s, there will be an estimated total
        construction workforce of 20,000 personnel. Currently there are approximately 17,198 personnel
        working on the Project (as of October 2024). A labor audit in 2023 showed that approximately 23%
        of the workforce come from villages within 50km of the Project. The majority of the remainder come
        from elsewhere in Tajikistan, with only small numbers of expatriate workers engaged. The
        Construction phase is assumed to last approximately 16 years and construction related jobs will
        gradually reduce after their peak.
17.4.3. The Project benefits local business through procurement of goods and services. The Project
        requires construction materials provided by suppliers, and labor which is provided by contractors
        and sub-contractors. Businesses also provide accommodation, food and supplies for workers and
        Project visitors. The Project economically benefits large multi-national and national companies (e.g.,
        construction contractors) as well as small local businesses and sole traders.
17.4.4. The receptor for this impact is the economy within the Project AoI (high sensitivity). This impact is
        expected to be beneficial for the duration of the construction phase. Therefore, the effects
        associated with direct employment and procurement on the Project are Positive.
        Indirect Economic Impacts on the Project AoI
17.4.5. Indirect economic benefits occur when spend by workers employed by the Project, or otherwise
        brought into the area by the Project (e.g., business visitors), generates revenue for other
        businesses. Construction of the Project has already commenced, and the Project is due to be
        complete, with the reservoir at full capacity, in 2038. During the construction period, expenditure by
        Project workers and business visitors provides revenue for the local economy. However, the impact


        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I              |PUBLIC
        Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                                     May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                      Page 17-12
         on the immediate local area is limited, as the majority of workers live on site and return to their
         hometowns when they are not working, reducing their ability to spend money in local shops,
         restaurants, etc.
17.4.6. Of the approximately 17,000 workers currently employed, some 12,500 live in accommodation on
        the construction site (comprised of two rotations of 6,000 people working 15-day shifts, and a further
        500 people working 5-day weeks). A further 250 expat workers reside at the WeBuild camp adjacent
        to Rogun City. The number of workers residing in Rogun City is around 2,100 at any one time;
        comprised of 1,500 office workers (working 5 day weeks), and two shifts of approximately 600
        workers doing 15-day shifts. A further 1,500 workers live in the nearby settlements and commute
        daily. The number of Project workers living in Rogun City is therefore significant, but a relatively
        small proportion of the total workers involved in the Project.
17.4.7. This indirect impact on the economy within the Project AoI (high sensitivity) is expected to be
        beneficial throughout the construction phase. The effect is therefore Positive.
         Physical resettlement and asset compensation
17.4.8. Sixty-nine settlements are scheduled for resettlement by 2032, comprising 6,788 households and
        50,267 people at present population levels. The number of households is not expected to change as
        the construction ban has now been applied to the whole inundation zone, although natural
        population growth means that the total number of affected people is likely to increase over time3.
        RAP 2 provides information on RAP 1 resettlement (which is completed), as well as the ongoing
        resettlement process during the second phase, which involves 1,710 PAHs (16,919) people.
        Another 4,752 households (30,651 people) will be covered by future RAPs (see Annex 7.1:
        Baseline Conditions Report of this ESIA).
17.4.9. All households that are resettled are provided with replacement land and monetary compensation
        for assets, as described in the 2024 Resettlement and Livelihood Restoration Framework (RLRF)
        and 2017-2026 RAP 2.
17.4.10. The Project will result in:
            Loss of all structures in 69 villages;
            Loss of common pastureland;
            Loss of crop lands and orchards;
            Loss of land and livelihoods associated with this land;
            Loss of access to natural resources;
            Loss of ethnographic and intangible cultural heritage;
            Loss of public infrastructure, such as schools, roads and healthcare facilities; and
            Impacts on businesses, including the loss of income in the period between business activities
             ceasing prior to resettlement and income returning to the previous level.
17.4.11. A summary of impacts on land resulting from the various Project components and associated
         facilities (AFs) is presented in Table 17-4.
