17.4.12. In the Flooding Zone within Rogun City and Nurobod District, which has been subject to a
         construction ban since the 1980s, houses are typically crowded and under-maintained. Site visit
         observations confirmed that the quality of housing in resettlement sites is significantly better than in
         the pre-resettlement villages.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I                  |PUBLIC
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                                         May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                          Page 17-14
Figure 17-1 - A Pre-resettlement House in Mehrobod, Nurobod District




Figure 17-2 - A Post-resettlement House in Toichi, Tursunzoda (inhabited since 2017)




Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I     |PUBLIC
Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                            May 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                             Page 17-15
        Figure 17-3 - A street in Navobod, Rasht District




17.4.13. Key findings from an assessment of the ongoing second phase of resettlement confirm that overall,
         the ongoing physical resettlement process is in compliance with the Tajik legislation and largely
         meeting the requirements of the lenders. During the preparation of the RLRF and RAP 2, DFZ has
         worked to improve its delivery of the resettlement program, notably by improving their overall
         systems for tracking the resettlement process, strengthening outreach and the grievance
         mechanism, eliminating the depreciation of assets, assisting PAPs in building their new homes and
         structures, transporting their goods to the resettlement sites and supporting vulnerable families. The
         Project is also benefitting from enhanced efforts at livelihood restoration.
17.4.14. Until July 1, 2024, a 30% depreciation of assets was applied in line with the National Assessment
         Standards of the Republic of Tajikistan (NASRT). Even with the depreciation, most PAPs (73% to
         90%, based on different surveys) reported that the financial compensation for their houses and
         structures was adequate to purchase or build new houses and structures at the resettlement sites.
         Almost all PAPs agree strongly that their new homes are superior in quality to their original homes,
         with important amenities such electricity and water supply, and upgraded or new schools and
         medical facilities to accommodate the resettled people as well as the host communities. PAPs have
         been informed that they can make further claims for compensation through the GRM. Some have
         done so and have received additional support, including in-kind and livelihood restoration measures.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I       |PUBLIC
        Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                              May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page 17-16
17.4.15. To date, PAPs who cannot, or do not wish to, move promptly have been able to remain in their
         previous location for extended periods, as the resettlement program has progressed significantly
         ahead of the rising reservoir level. As the rate of filling the reservoir increases, the speed of
         resettlement will also need to keep pace. The construction of infrastructure at resettlement sites will
         need to progress more quickly than it currently is to ensure that all critical facilities are ready for
         PAPs to relocate. Should delays occur, it is less likely that PAPs will be able to remain longer in their
         current location, and additional transitional support measures (e.g. temporary accommodation) may
         need to be provided to enable PAPs to leave their current location ahead of inundation.
17.4.16. Resettled communities are considered to be of a high sensitivity. The scale of resettlement
         associated with the Project is also very large. The implementation of the 2017-2026 RAP 2, and
         future RAPs prepared in line with the RLRF (2024) will manage ongoing resettlement in line with
         Tajik law and the requirements of the lenders. There is evidence that living standards for PAPs,
         following resettlement, is improving.
17.4.17. Year 1: In the short-term, resettlement causes adverse impacts for the majority of PAPs. Some
         people (especially older people) feel distress at leaving their former homes behind, and the
         experience of moving is often stressful. Households owning assets of low value, and families who
         did not own assets (due to living with extended family), will have limited time to save supplementary
         funds for their house construction, and may need to move into more basic homes initially and
         improve them over time. Establishing social ties with new neighbors in new communities can also
         take time, particularly if there are delays to community infrastructure, such as schools, which can be
         a focus for developing social cohesion. At Year 1, resettlement will result in a Moderate adverse
         impact, causing a Major effect.
17.4.18. Year 5: Adverse impacts lessen over time as new residents settle into their new homes and
         communities become more established. Evidence from previous resettlement shows that living
         conditions typically improve over time as resettlers further develop their homes with extensions or
         upgraded materials, With the full implementation of the RAPs, and the provision of planned
         community infrastructure, the impact of physical resettlement from the villages in the flooding zone
         to the resettlement sites will be reduced. Within 5 years, impacts are anticipated to be Beneficial,
         leading to a Positive effect.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I         |PUBLIC
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                                May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page 17-17
         Livelihood disruption and restoration
17.4.19. The availability of workplaces and productive land at their new location determines the impacts on
         PAPs whose livelihoods are lost due to resettlement. The majority of resettled households have
         primary income sources which are not land-based or dependent on a fixed location and therefore
         will not be directly affected by resettlement. The most common livelihood amongst adults (16+) in
         Project-affected communities is labor migration is the main source of income for 54% of households.
         Pensions are the second most important source of income for 25% of households. Hired labor in the
         public sector provides income for 23% of households. Farming activities, including cattle breeding,
         housekeeping and agriculture, are a source of income for 16-20% of households. Five percent of
         people in PAHs are unemployed and looking for work (exclusive of people who are unemployed but
         not looking for a job, e.g., full-time home-makers), though this number has fluctuated. A small
         proportion of the population have agriculture as their primary livelihood (<10%, predominantly
         arable), although the majority of households practice some subsistence farming around their
         households. Further data on livelihoods is provided in Annex 7.1: Baseline Conditions Report of
         this ESIA.
17.4.20. Primary data compiled in 2023 and 2024 shows little difference between the employment profiles of
         communities before and after resettlement, which supports the conclusion that resettlement is
         having a limited impact on livelihoods for the majority of households, at least in the long term. While
         household members who undertake labor migration are also involved in construct their new house,
         this has not been reported as impacting household income to date. This is likely due to the current
         pace of resettlement being relatively slow, enabling PAPs to align their new house construction with
         their normal labor migration patterns. The number of resettlers self-reporting losses from labor
         migration to date is minimal. However, the pace of resettlement will be increased which could affect
         labor migration income going forward, as labor migrants will either need to stay home to construct
         their new homes within a set timescale or hire builders. There may be instances where households
         could live in temporary accommodation (e.g. with family) in order to accommodate current labor
         migration patterns.
17.4.21. The 0.08 to 0.1 ha household plots enable all households to continue personal small-scale food
         production, as well as allowing those who did not previously have gardens to begin producing their
         own food. The practice of splitting households into families also increases land availability per
         person in the household. Resettled households also have the right to use common pastureland and
         apply for agricultural land from the Ministry of Agriculture (MoA) at their new locations. Livestock can
         be transported to new locations and are included within the transportation support provided by DFZ.
         Although there are detrimental impacts in the short-to medium-term during the transition period, due
         to the time taken to complete formalities on land use, re-establish trees and adapt to new climatic
         conditions, in the long-term households are able to restore, or even increase, their food production
         capacity. Gardens, including those belonging to relatively recent RAP 2 resettlers, were observed to
         be well established at all sites, and increased productivity was reported in some cases.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I        |PUBLIC
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                               May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page 17-18
Figure 17-4 - Gardens of RAP 2 resettlers in Namuna, Tursunzoda (September 2024)




Figure 17-5 - The garden of an early resettler in Toichi, Tursunzoda with re-established trees
(September 2024)




Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I     |PUBLIC
Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                            May 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                             Page 17-19
17.4.22. Among the relocated households surveyed during the preparation of the draft ESIA in 2023, 42%
         reported a perceived loss of income. The June 2024 survey of 363 resettled households indicated
         that, in general, the average annual income of the resettled persons increased from TJS 26,269
         before resettlement to TJS 29,823 after resettlement. This was partly due to PAPs changing
         livelihoods from agriculture to migrant labor and construction. The proximity of some of the
         resettlement sites to a large secondary city also made it easier for people to transition from land-
         based labor to wage-based labor.
17.4.23. For families remaining in commercial agriculture, there have been challenges in obtaining larger
         tracts of farm- and pasturelands close to the resettlement sites. Losses will also occur during the
         time taken to re-establish crops and trees. Trees are being re-grown from seeds as transplantation
         was reported to be ineffective.
17.4.24. Livelihood restoration measures are provided primarily via the Ministry of Labor, Migration and
         Employment (MoLMEP) and MoA. Full details of the program, as well as baseline data regarding
         livelihood and employment amongst Project-affected communities, are presented in Volume 2,
         Annex 7.1: Baseline Conditions Report of this ESIA, as well as in the Livelihood Restoration
         Plan for the second phase of resettlement (LRP 2). Figure 17-6 shows uptake of the livelihood
         restoration measures amongst RAP 2 PAPs, based on a survey of 523 PAP heads of households in
         2023. Some MoLMEP measures are provided automatically to all resettlers – specifically the one-
         time transitional allowance and the preferential – and, as such, uptake is high. Other measures are
         available to all registered unemployed people in Tajikistan and are subject to various conditions,
         leading to lower uptake.

        Figure 17-6 - % of 523 surveyed PAPs receiving individual MoLMEP support (2023)




17.4.25. These livelihood restoration measures have been in place since 2009. In 2024, MoLMEP doubled
         the amount of the one-time transitional allowance to 200TJS per head of household and 100 TJS to



        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I       |PUBLIC
        Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                              May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page 17-20
         each family member. They also increased the preferential loan from 3000 to 5000TJS. Female FGD
         participants in Khakimi (RAP 2, Nurobod), who expressed positive attitudes towards resettlement,
         were aware of the preferential loans and small business micro-loans and anticipated that these
         measures would help them to improve their livelihoods or begin earning an income for the first time.
         Total numbers of resettlers accessing MoLMEP programs (across all RAPs) is presented in Volume
         2, Annex 7.1: Baseline Conditions Report of this ESIA and LRP 2.
17.4.26. The above evidence shows that livelihood restoration to date has been partially successful. Uptake
         of some livelihood restoration measures has been rather low, and 8% of post-resettlement
         households indicated in the 2023 survey that someone within the household who had a permanent
         job prior to resettlement had become unemployed. However, there have been examples of positive
         livelihood outcomes, such as women entering the workforce for the first time. LRP 2 was produced
         in 2025 to mitigate livelihood impacts by increasing awareness of, and facilitating access to,
         programs that are in place in Tajikistan to assist people to gain or improve their livelihoods.
         Additional LRPs will be prepared to accompany each future RAP. The full timeline of preparations of
         future RAPs and LRPs is presented in the RLRF (2024) and RAP 2.
17.4.27. Resettled communities are considered to be of high sensitivity. However, the majority have sources
         of primary income that are not land-based or tied to a particular location, or no income at all, and will
         therefore not be impacted. Disruption to livelihoods is further limited by the implementation of
         existing management measures, including the RAPs, LRPs, and MoLMEP and MoA programs.
17.4.28. Year 1: In the short term, resettlement is highly disruptive to livelihoods even with support measures
         in place. Agricultural activities and businesses take time to re-establish, and increasing the pace of
         resettlement will require more PAPs to reduce time spent on labor migration in order to construct
         houses, which will result in income losses. The impact is assessed to be of Moderate magnitude,
         and the effect is therefore considered Major adverse.
17.4.29. Year 5: In the medium- to long- term it is anticipated that the measures in place, as well as the
         improved infrastructure at new settlements, will lead to a Beneficial impact and a Positive effect on
         livelihoods. The full impact of LRP measures, as well as general social and economic development
         at the resettlement site (e.g., re-establishing community networks with new neighbors and host
         communities, indirect benefits of new businesses developing) will be felt only after several years.
         Loss and Replacement of Infrastructure for Resettled Communities
17.4.30. All community assets and infrastructure within the Flooding Zone will be lost as the reservoir is filled,
         which will affect the economy and social lives of communities. Some of these assets, particularly
         within larger settlements such as Mehrobod and Navobod, are also used by surrounding
         communities that are not scheduled for resettlement.
17.4.31. The large resettlement operation associated with the Project has necessitated the construction of
         new settlements. RAP 2 resettlers have moved to sites across five districts (Nurobod, Faizobod,
         Rogun, Tursunzoda, and Danghara). New settlements provide residents with improved infrastructure
         in terms of new schools, kindergartens, healthcare facilities, and sports and recreational facilities,
         which affected people welcome. Site visits in 2023-4 confirm that the standard of the new facilities at
         resettlement sites such as Darband (Nurobod District) and Yoli Garmoba (Rogun City) is high when
         complete, although in some cases infrastructure completion has been delayed (Namuna,
         Tursunzoda).




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I         |PUBLIC
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                                May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page 17-21
17.4.32. Existing community assets and infrastructure within existing settlements situated close to the
         resettlement sites may be affected by an increase in users, if new infrastructure is not completed in
         time. However, host communities are experiencing benefits from new infrastructure provided at
         resettlement sites, which they will be able to access, even if it is initially delayed.

        Figure 17-7 - School No. 28 at Darband, Nurobod District (September 2024)




        Figure 17-8 - Medical Centre at Yoli Garmoba, Rogun City, (left) and Maternity Unit at
        Darband, Nurobod District, (right) (September 2024)




17.4.33. FGDs and household survey data raised the issue of availability of water in new settlements (in
         Faizobod and Nurobod), but indicated that these issues had been resolved, or will be resolved,
         before resettlers move. Interviews with Jamoat representatives confirmed that upgrades to electricity
         and water infrastructure have been implemented in Faizobod and Tursunzoda. Site visit
         observations in 2024 and 2025 confirmed that water was available in all resettlement sites, but in




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I       |PUBLIC
        Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                              May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page 17-22
         some cases the amount needs to be increased by establishing reservoirs or piping water from areas
         near the resettlement sites.
17.4.34. Infrastructure replacement falls within the remit of DFZ and the above-described evidence shows
         that, overall, this has been implemented to a standard adequate to maintain and improve living
         standards for the majority of resettled households. There have been delays in infrastructure
         replacement, but DFZ has been working with PAPs to ensure that new homes and structures are
         built more quickly. Both household survey and FGD responses confirm that a large majority of
         people are positive about the improvements in infrastructure in new settlements, both in terms of
         what has already been achieved and what is anticipated for the future. There are anecdotal reports
         of people voluntarily moving to the new settlements in order to take advantage of improved
         infrastructure, although not in sufficient numbers to cause a strain on resources.
17.4.35. The impact of resettlement on community infrastructure (moderate-sensitivity) is therefore, overall,
         anticipated to be Beneficial, assuming full implementation of the future RAPs. Effects are
         considered Positive, despite the losses of facilities in the Floozing Zone. In line with the RAPs, all
         essential infrastructure will be in place before resettlement into a site commences, and new
         infrastructure will be maintained once it is handed over to local jamoat control after the completion of
         resettlement.
         Impact of resettlement on non-resettled communities
17.4.36. The majority of settlements within the flooding zone are small, but social and economic ties between
         neighboring villages, and between small settlements and local hubs, is high. The resettlement of
         communities situated within the Flooding Zone will affect the non-resettled communities socially and
         economically. Businesses will lose customers who are resettled far away, and family and friends
         may become separated by large distances. Villages located in the upper reaches of tributary valleys
         will become increasingly remote, although this will be mitigated by the construction of the left- and
         right-bank roads, which will increase connectivity to hub settlements such as Rogun and Darband,
         as well as between tributary valleys (provided that slip roads are constructed to connect each valley)
         (See Volume 2, Annex 17.3 Severance for more details). Some villages may also lose access to
         infrastructure, if facilities such as schools and medical centers are shared with other villages that are
         resettled.
17.4.37. In Rogun City and Nurobod, this impact is being mitigated by the presence of resettlement sites in
         the district. Nurobod will be impacted by the highest proportional population loss (31% of the
         population), but the presence of the large and growing resettlement site at Darband will help to
         retain and attract people and businesses in the area. Proportional population loss due to
         resettlement in Rogun City is much smaller (5%), and resettlement sites are also present at Saidoni
         Nav and Yoli Garmoba. These sites help to add, and retain, potential customers in the area, but
         additional businesses that develop will also compete with pre-existing businesses.
17.4.38. Rasht currently stands to be the most affected district in terms of population loss. The to-be-
         resettled population of Rasht currently stands at 21,048, out of a total population of 139,848,
         equivalent to 15% of its population, with no new resettlement sites currently planned within the
         district. The district capital Gharm, which has a population of 8,857 (2020 census) and is located
         approximately 7.5km from the maximum extent of the flooding zone, stands to be particularly
         impacted. Many residents of the to-be-resettled communities travel regularly to Gharm, some daily,
         for shopping and public services. The resettlement of these communities will impact shops and other



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I         |PUBLIC
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                                May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page 17-23
         businesses that receive custom from these villages. Local taxi drivers who provide services
         connecting the more remote villages to Gharm, and some salespeople who travel to the smaller
         settlements selling goods that are not readily available will be particularly affected.
17.4.39. The non-resettled communities in the wider Project-affected area have a similar profile to the
         resettled communities and are a Moderate-sensitivity receptor. The impact of population changes
         will vary significantly between settlements. Some villages will lose close neighbors and some social
         and other types of infrastructure may need to be adjusted or replaced; more remote and self-
         contained settlements will be minimally impacted, and those close to new resettlement sites such as
         Darband will find the character of the area transformed in both positive and negative ways,
         depending on their perspective. This is considered a Moderate adverse impact, leading to a
         Moderate effect. However, it should be noted that this will not be the case in all settlements and the
         impact will even be beneficial in some cases.
         Demographic and economic impacts on host communities
17.4.40. The in-migration of large numbers of resettlers into an area can have both positive and negative
         impacts on host communities. On one hand, the increased population can bring social and economic
         opportunities, but it can also put pressure on shared resources and reduce community cohesion if
         the new arrivals differ significantly from the host communities. Adverse impacts may also arise if
         land acquisition to make space for resettlers is not implemented in line with good practice. A full list
         and maps of the resettlement sites and surrounding areas can be found in Volume 2, Annex 7.1:
         Baseline Conditions Report of this ESIA.
17.4.41. All of the resettlement sites are located in populated districts. However, with the exception of
         Darband, all of the resettlement sites are located at some distance from existing settlements (100m-
         1km). Darband is contiguous with the previous settlements of Tirgar, Obilurd and Khumdon, which
         will become less remote and more peri-urban in character.
17.4.42. The capacity of the areas to accommodate additional population is a key consideration in the
         selection of resettlement sites. The resettlement sites have been designed to be self-contained in
         terms of critical infrastructure such as schools and medical centers, and to avoid straining the
         resources of host communities. There is no evidence to date of negative impacts on host
         communities’ resources due to additional population pressure. Host communities have been
         consulted through the local jamoats, and FGD participants and jamoat leaders were generally
         positive about resettlement, due to the increased investment in infrastructure in the area. Some
         resources (e.g., the school at Toichi in Tursunzoda) have been planned to be shared with host
         communities. Members of host communities have also begun to find jobs in the new settlements
         (e.g., a nurse and a teacher in Namuna, who were from host communities).
17.4.43. It was reported that there were some integration issues with the community in Tursunzoda when
         resettlement commenced, however engagements were carried out to improve community relations,
         and the issue was resolved. Evidence gathered during the preparation of this ESIA indicated no
         significant issues with community relations, and relations between resettled and host communities,
         where they occurred, were found to be positive.
17.4.44. It has been reported at some sites, particularly Darband, that the improved infrastructure has
         attracted additional households to move voluntarily to the site. While contributing to the economic
         and social life of the new settlements, additional population also adds pressure on shared resources
         such as schools, medical centers and roads. The continued management of these settlements after


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I       |PUBLIC
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                              May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page 17-24
         the completion of resettlement to ensure adequate infrastructure provision for the whole settlement
         will be the responsibility of the local authorities.
17.4.45. Evidence to date shows that the selection of sites for resettlement has been well planned according
         to the protocols in place, including cooperation with local jamoats, and there is no evidence of forced
         eviction. This has been verified to the extent possible. However, the majority of people within the
         Flooding Zone are yet to be resettled. Additional resettlement sites have been identified: Darband,
         Obi Lurd, Tukhchi, Hufak and Roghuni Bolo.
17.4.46. will therefore be required. It is undetermined where these will be located.
17.4.47. This is considered to be a beneficial impact for the host communities, and the effect is Positive. It
         is assumed that all future sensitization works with host communities before the creation of new
         resettlement sites will be completed before in-migration of resettlers commences.
         Economic displacement
17.4.48. Resettlement will lead to the economic displacement of people working within the Flooding Zone
         who live in non-resettled communities. As the majority of affected settlements are small villages
         consisting of residential housing and small facilities (e.g., village schools, medical centers and
         shops) staffed by residents, this impact will be limited in most cases. The heavy reliance on labor
         migration for income amongst the affected communities also mitigates this effect. However, there
         are some larger settlements such as Mehrobod and Navobod, which will likely lead to some job
         losses when they are resettled. Similarly, significant quantities of agricultural land will be lost (see
         Table 17-6), though many resettled PAPs will not resume commercial agriculture. Those who do
         farm commercially have been applying to the MoA for new lands, most of which are available near
         the resettlement sites. In some cases, agricultural land in the affected districts (particularly Nurobod)
         is limited due to the terrain and thus some of the plots will be in areas that are further away from the
         resettlement sites.
17.4.49. Year 1: This impact is considered to be of Medium magnitude as a relatively small number of
         people will be impacted, and these will be spread over a large area rather than concentrated in one
         community. It is not anticipated that any communities will become unviable due to this effect. As the
         affected people are a high-sensitivity receptor, this is a Major adverse effect.
17.4.50. Year 5: The affected communities have some adaptive capacity and it is likely that alternative
         livelihoods will be found over time. The development of Darband may also provide opportunities for
         those able to commute or move there. Those living in large households with several working adults
         may also leave the workforce and rely on wider family income, if they can afford to do so. Others
         may choose to relocate out of the area. It is unlikely that this impact will be a significant driver of
         poverty in the medium- to long-term, although quality of life will decrease for some. This impact is
         therefore considered Small, leading to a Moderate adverse effect in the absence of mitigation.
         Loss of agricultural production in the flooding zone
17.4.51. As the land to be inundated is low-lying, agricultural land will be disproportionately affected.
         Nurobod, which will lose 11,684ha, equivalent to 12.6% of its total area, but this includes 59.7% of
         fields, 46.3% of perennial crop land, and 22.9% of Presidential land, as well as 26.3% of forest and
         scrub land. Rasht and Rogun City will lose a much smaller proportion of their land area, but the land
         that will be lost is disproportionately agricultural. Rogun City has no fields or perennial crop lands but
         will lose 13.6% of Presidential land. Rasht will lose 9.4% of perennial crop lands and 8.2% of fields



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I         |PUBLIC
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                                May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page 17-25
         (but just 1.3% of Presidential land). A full summary of land types to be lost is provided in Volume 2,
         Annex 7.1: Baseline Conditions Report of this ESIA.

17.4.52. This loss of agricultural land could affect commercial food production. As agricultural land is state-
         owned, people losing their commercial plots who wish to continue farming are able to apply to be
         allocated a new plot through the MoA, although, given the geography of the affected area with steep
         mountains surrounding the affected area, some equivalent plots will not be readily available in the
         resettlement areas, but in nearby areas. The reduction of commercial food production may require
         more food to be brought in from other areas, which may incur price rises or a reduction in the
         availability of fresh produce. The profitability of smaller food retailers will also be affected if their
         costs are increased by a reduction in local supply. Resettlement will decrease the population of
         some of the affected districts, and thus total food demand. In Nurobod and Rogun City the loss of
         agricultural land is larger than proportional population loss, even before in-migration to resettlement
         sites is considered. This is a Large impact on a high-sensitivity receptor, leading to a Major adverse
         effect. The change is permanent and irreversible, in the absence of mitigation (see Section 17.5).
         Retrenchment
17.4.53. The Project has been one of the largest employers in the area for many years due to the very large
         construction workforce required. The Project impacts on workers are assessed in Chapter 19:
         Labor and Working Conditions.
17.4.54. Following the employment peak later in the 2020s, the workforce will reduce gradually. This reduces
         the impact of reduced employment opportunities on the community as a whole, as there will not be a
         sudden rise in unemployment, but rather a transition over several years. As the majority of the
         workforce live more that 50km from the Project (77%) the impact will also not be entirely
         concentrated in one geographic area and will be felt to some extent throughout Tajikistan, although
         it will be most acute in the immediate vicinity of the Project.
17.4.55. There may be an increased need for labor migration outside of Tajikistan amongst former Project
         workers if suitable alternative employment opportunities are not available locally. There are
         additional HPPs planned for the Vakhsh cascade (e.g., at Shurob which is planned to commence


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I         |PUBLIC
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                                May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page 17-26
        construction after Rogun), but these plans are in the early stages of development, and the timeline
        and size of the required workforce are yet to be determined.
17.4.56. The receptor sensitivity is moderate-high, and the impact magnitude is assumed to be Large on a
         precautionary basis. The effect is therefore Major adverse.
        Impacts on Gender and Vulnerable Groups
17.4.57. This section presents any differentiated and additional effects (positive and adverse) on women and
         vulnerable groups, resulting from the impacts identified above. The justification for why certain
         groups are considered vulnerable is presented in Annex 7.1: Baseline Conditions Report of this
         ESIA. These can be groups who suffer additional or more severe adverse impacts or are less able
         to benefit from beneficial impacts (even if no adverse impacts occur). Further detail on specific
         issues affecting women, and measures to be put in place to manage these, are found in the Gender
         Action Plan (GAP) and Volume 2, Annex 17.1: Gender of this ESIA.
        Direct economic impacts of Project employment and procurement
17.4.58. Women and other vulnerable individuals will gain some benefits from employment on the Project if
         they are able to find work. Women comprise approximately 5% of the total workforce at the dam, the
         majority of whom work in domestic roles such as cooks and cleaners, as well as a minority in
         professional roles such as nurses or Human Resources (HR) specialists. The Project has also led to
         legal changes to reduce the number of professions which are banned for women, and a training
         centre in Rogun City has opened which has residential accommodation space for women with
         children. Some jobs are available that are suitable for other groups identified as vulnerable, such as
         people with disabilities, the illiterate, and young people lacking skills or education.
        Indirect Economic Impacts on the Project AoI
17.4.59. Women and other vulnerable individuals will benefit from indirect economic impacts. Indirect Project
         spend by workers and visitors supports a range of businesses in Rogun city and nearby settlements,
         including those that are more readily accessible to those unable to undertake heavy manual labor
         (e.g., shops, guest houses). Additionally, all members of a workers’ household benefit from the
         relatively high salaries and reduced need for labor migration due to employment with the Project,
         regardless of whether or not they are themselves working.
        Physical resettlement and asset compensation
17.4.60. All key resettlement decisions are the responsibility of the Head of Household (HoH). Women
         comprise 18% of HoH within the resettled population. For women who are not HoH, their influence
         varies greatly between households, depending on household dynamics, age, and other factors. This
         leaves them vulnerable to resettlement decisions being made that are not in their interest, including
         being separated from friends and family. However, very few instances have been identified of
         women stating that they were resettled to a site chosen by their parents or husbands with no input
         from themselves or against their wishes, beyond a small number of anecdotal reports from the
         household survey. DFZ have also organized consultations with women, although these were
         sometimes informal.
17.4.61. Households with no able-bodied adult men within the household face additional challenges with
         house reconstruction. Typically, women, elderly people and people with disabilities will be unable to
         carry out the reconstruction themselves and have to spend money on laborers. However, it is
         possible for those who apply to their jamoat to be provided with laborers at no cost. The entitlement


        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I       |PUBLIC
        Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                              May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page 17-27
         is formalized in the Entitlement Matrix of the RLRF (2024), which is reflected in the 2017-2026 RAP
         2 and will be included in future RAPs.
17.4.62. A Head of Family (HoF) – male or female – can receive a land plot. This benefit is supported by
         resettled communities and has been confirmed in the Entitlement Matrix (RLRF (2024)). This
         measure benefits female-headed households, granting additional independence and financial
         security, as they can independently construct their new homes and cultivate gardens.
17.4.63. Very poor households will benefit from physical resettlement, as the uniform 0.08h size of plots will
         increase the land available for those who previously had the smallest plots. Households that split
         into several families (each provided with new land plots once resettled) will significantly increase the
         total land available to them.
17.4.64. HoH who face difficulties completing the administrative processes associated with resettlement, for
         example illiterate HoH, recently divorced or widowed women who are newly HoH, or elderly HoH,
         may experience worse outcomes in resettlement, for example if they do not understand the
         compensation calculation process and are unable to negotiate. Support for those who are not legally
         or financially literate is included as an entitlement in the 2017-2026 RAP 2, and the RLRF (2024)
         which will guide the development of future RAPs.
17.4.65. People living in residential care institutions are among the most vulnerable in society, as they are
         unable to live independently and require care from specialist professionals. The Project will require
         orphans and psychiatric in-patients to be resettled. This includes an orphanage in Navobod which
         accepts children from throughout the Rasht District, and the psychiatric hospital in Navobod which
         accepts in-patients from across Tajikistan. Current staff may not be able to continue working at the
         reconstructed facility if they are not also resettled to the same location as the facility. Even if suitable
         replacement staff can be found for a reconstructed facility, the personal bonds between carers and
         the vulnerable residents at these facilities will be lost, which may result in psychological distress and
         a reduced quality of care, at least temporarily.
17.4.66. The effects of physical resettlement will differ significantly between the affected vulnerable groups.
         Some, such as women gaining additional independence by becoming HoF and having their own
         land plot for the first time, or poor households gaining larger plots than they had previously, will
         benefit. Measures are already in place through DFZ, the jamoats and the RAPs to assist certain
         groups requiring additional support. Significant risks are, however, present for residents in
         residential care institutions, whose care will be severely disrupted if not carefully planned.
         Livelihood disruption and restoration
17.4.67. People and families who are already living in poverty are less able to manage any additional
         hardship caused by resettlement or loss of livelihood, even a relatively small or temporary loss of
         income. Physical resettlement can break kinship networks which these households and individuals
         often rely on.
17.4.68. Women, and other groups whose employment options are more restricted, will also take longer to
         restore their livelihoods if they are lost. However, they are less likely to be impacted due to lower
         labor force participation – just 12% of women in the Project AoI have an income, and, of those, more
         than half obtain this income from pensions. Pre-resettlement female Focus Group Discussion (FGD)
         participants, and some men from the communities, expected increased opportunities for women to
         enter paid employment after resettlement, and some post-resettlement FGD participants stated that
         some had succeeded in doing so.


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I           |PUBLIC
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                                  May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                   Page 17-28
17.4.69. LRP 2, and future LRPs, will help women and other vulnerable groups to access support to improve
         their livelihoods. MoLMEP provides targeted support to women and disabled people. National
         welfare programs are also available to the very poor and those unable to work, which can be
         accessed by self-reporting to the jamoat.
         Loss and Replacement of Community Assets and Infrastructure
17.4.70. Where social infrastructure is not replaced in time or adequately, this can result in women
         compensating by taking on additional responsibilities. For example, when schools or residential care
         facilities are not operational, women care for children or sick/elderly relatives at home and are
         prevented from working outside the home. Elderly and disabled people requiring care from outside
         the home will suffer deteriorating health and/or quality of life if these services are not restored
         adequately or on time. DFZ is working with relevant ministries and jamoats to ensure that key
         facilities, such as medical facilities, are established early in the resettlement communities.
         Impact of resettlement on non-resettled communities
17.4.71. Disruption to community networks due to resettlement will have more severe impacts for more
         vulnerable individuals, who are more likely to rely on informal kinship networks for support. Some
         individuals are likely to be impacted in the non-resettled communities identified with close links to a
         resettled village. Over time, social networks can be restored to some extent, and people who cannot
         manage independently may also have the option to voluntarily relocate, for example moving in with
         family members, which will mitigate this impact to some extent.
         Demographic and economic impacts on host communities
17.4.72. No differentiated impacts on women or vulnerable groups within host communities are anticipated.
         As with other groups, any impacts are likely to be beneficial provided good practice in line with the
         RAPs is implemented; employment opportunities and improved infrastructure.
         Economic displacement
17.4.73. Economic displacement is anticipated to impact a small number of people, some of whom will be
         women or members of vulnerable groups. These groups have less adaptive capacity than other
         community members, as they typically have a more restricted range of possible livelihoods.
         Targeted programs for vulnerable groups are included in the LRP.
17.4.74. MoA currently provides the following:
          A seed and seedling program for female-headed households, people with disabilities and the
           elderly.
          Free farming equipment for vulnerable families.
          Support for female-headed households with less the 5ha of land.
17.4.75. MoLMEP also provides support for women, female-headed households and people with disabilities.
         However, this support is predicated on self-reporting to the jamoat. DFZ has been working to ensure
         vulnerable groups are aware of these programs.
         Loss of agricultural production in the flooding zone
17.4.76. Impacts of food availability, price and quality will impact all within the affected districts, but those
         with the fewest resources have the least capacity to adapt and will therefore be worst affected.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I            |PUBLIC
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                                   May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                    Page 17-29
         Retrenchment
17.4.77. The direct impact of retrenchment will be smaller for women and other vulnerable groups that are
         less likely to be employed on the Project (e.g., people with disabilities). However, indirect impacts on
         other household members will occur when employment on the Project is no longer available. This
         will include wives, children and other dependents, such as elderly and disabled family members who
         are unable to work. If large job losses amongst the male population result in increased labor
         migration, adverse impacts will occur for women remaining at home, whose household and
         agricultural responsibilities increase, and whose opportunities outside the home decrease. In the
         absence of new employment sources in the area, this impact could be permanent.
         Summary of effects on women and vulnerable groups
17.4.78. A wide range of impacts will be experienced by women and other groups considered vulnerable
         from the point of view of the Project. These impacts will range from Large to Beneficial, causing
         effects ranging from Major Adverse to Positive.
         OPERATION
         Direct economic impact of Project operational employment and procurement
17.4.79. It is estimated that, after commissioning, Rogun HPP JSC will employ around 800 people at the fully
         operational HPP. Some operational workers (both technical and administrative) are already in place,
         most of whom currently live in on-site accommodation and who are working the same shift pattern
         as construction workers. The operational phase will generate long-term employment opportunities
         mainly for HPP operation and maintenance, and environmental, health and safety, and monitoring
         activities. A significant proportion of these operational roles will be permanent positions. It is unlikely
         that all operational workers will reside on-site and will instead settle permanently in established local
         communities such as Rogun City. Employment on the Project will be managed by JSC Rogun plans,
         policies and procedures.
17.4.80. The Project will continue to procure goods and services from businesses of different scales during
         operation, e.g., parts for maintenance. This will continue to benefit the affected businesses, although
         on a much smaller scale than during construction.
17.4.81. The local economy is a high-sensitivity receptor, and the impact is expected to be beneficial. The
         effect is therefore Positive.
         Indirect economic impacts of the operational HPP
17.4.82. Operational employment will provide indirect benefits to the local economy, although on a much
         smaller scale than provided during the construction phase. Due to the anticipated Beneficial
         impacts arising from the indirect employment, effects are considered Positive.
         Indirect economic impacts of the completion of construction
17.4.83. Rogun City has been economically reliant on the Project since its inception in the 1980s. The HPP is
         the largest employer in the town, and the majority of other businesses and livelihoods are directly or
         indirectly supported by it due to the high wages paid, and workers and business visitors being
         attracted to the town. The construction workforce will reduce gradually up until the Project is
         operational, therefore the impacts of a reduced population size will also affect locally businesses
         progressively. Total spending in the local economy is anticipated to reduce as the Project
         construction comes to a conclusion, which will affect businesses and livelihoods locally.


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I          |PUBLIC
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                                 May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page 17-30
17.4.84. Given the number of people employed on the Project and the long timescale over which people
         have become reliant on it, this impact will be Large. The effect is therefore Major Adverse.
         Improved energy supply in Tajikistan and the wider region
17.4.85. The primary purpose of the Project is to improve energy supply in Tajikistan. Currently, shortages
         are a significant problem for the country and blackouts are a regular occurrence, particularly in the
         winter. This negatively impacts quality of life and economic development. The additional power
         generated by the fully operational Project is expected to resolve these shortages and provide
         additional energy for export in the summer. Sale of excess will also generate revenue for the
         economy of Tajikistan. Furthermore, the GoT has committed to allocate 3% of the revenue from
         energy sales to support measures to assist the poor and vulnerable and improve social safety nets,
         increasing to 5% after the completion of construction, to be allocated at a national level.
17.4.86. The partially-operational Project has already generated sufficient excess to enable international sale
         to nearby countries, including Kazakhstan, which signed a PPA, Uzbekistan, and Afghanistan and
         Pakistan, which benefit from the CASA-1000 project. It is estimated that around 70% of the energy
         will be exported. While this will be sold for profit, it is anticipated that the price will be affordable and
         that polluting, expensive fossil fuel generation will be displaced.
17.4.87. This is a beneficial impact, leading to a Positive effect for both Tajikistan and other countries where
         the power is used.

17.5     MITIGATION AND ENHANCEMENT MEASURES
17.5.1. This section presents measures to enhance positive effects, and mitigate adverse effects, from the
        impacts identified above. Where no mitigation is required, or no enhancement is possible, the impact
        will not be further discussed in this section.
         CONSTRUCTION
         Physical resettlement and asset compensation
         Livelihood disruption and restoration
         Loss and Replacement of Infrastructure for Resettled Communities
17.5.2. All aspects of resettlement and livelihood restoration will be mitigated to the extent possible through
        the implementation of RAPs and LRPs. The RAPs and LRPs define and quantify the entitlements of
        PAPs to compensation, livelihood restoration support, and other benefits, and are publicized to
        maximize awareness of these entitlements.
17.5.3. In order to ensure sufficient budget allocation, DFZ and the GoT are preparing future RAPs and
        LRPs in advance of resettlement commencing in the affected districts, in line with the RLRF (2024).
        When resettlement in Rasht commences, preparations will be made to allocate additional budget for
        asset compensation than in previous RAPs, due to the improved quality of housing in that District
        relative to Rogun and Nurobod.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I            |PUBLIC
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                                   May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                    Page 17-31
         Impact of resettlement on non-resettled communities
         Economic displacement
         Loss of agricultural production
17.5.4. The type and scale of the impact on non-resettled communities will determine the appropriate
        mitigation. People residing in settlements which remain viable overall, but whose livelihoods are
        affected by the loss of productive assets or business customers, will benefit from access to national
        programs which assist people to improve their livelihoods. To mitigate impacts on non-resettled
        communities in the Project AoI, the LRPs will be shared widely with host and nearby communities in
        the Project-affected districts by MoLMEP. This will raise awareness of the programs that are
        available to all Tajik citizens and will assist affected people to adapt or find new livelihoods.
17.5.5. Particular issues exist for some settlements that are losing access to shared infrastructure with
        villages that are being resettled. Ensuring infrastructure provision is the responsibility of the relevant
        Ministries (e.g., Ministry of Health, Ministry of Education) and local authorities. Adequate coverage
        must be ensured by either providing replacement infrastructure at the remaining village, or, if this is
        not practical, increasing capacity at facilities in nearby settlements.
17.5.6. Chapter 12: Soils and Geology of this ESIA contains plans for high-quality agricultural soils that
        will be lost to be stripped and moved further up the slopes. A protocol will be developed to re-
        establish the soils and prevent erosion. It is estimated that productivity will be restored within 3
        years, although there will be a need to ensure that the soils are maintained, e.g., replenishment with
        compost, in the intervening years. As agricultural land belongs to the government, an application to
        the MoA will have to be made by those wishing to commence farming on the new soils.
17.5.7. The restoration of livelihoods, social networks and agricultural production takes time, and these
        mitigation measures will take several years to be fully effective. However, if they are implemented in
        full, the impact size will be reduced to Small by Year 1, leading to a Moderate Adverse effect as
        economically displaced people adapt to new livelihoods, and Very Small by Year 5, leading to a
        Minor Adverse effect. Psychological impacts of the loss of friends and neighbors will remain, and
        farming on the steeper slopes will be more challenging, e.g., due to the increased need for erosion
        prevention. Communities will continue to adapt over time, further reducing the impact.
         Demographic and economic impacts on host communities
17.5.8. To date, resettlement has brought benefits to host communities, with no significant adverse impacts
        identified. Good practice must therefore be maintained when additional sites are identified. The 2024
        RLRF contains requirements for land acquisition and community consultations when new
        resettlement sites are identified. These requirements will be included in future RAPs.
         Retrenchment
17.5.9. A Retrenchment Plan should be completed and implemented to mitigate the impacts of the Project
        reducing its workforce, and the changing skills demand, during the shift to operation.
17.5.10. Retrenched Project workers also have the right to access MoLMEP support available to all Tajik
         citizens. The Retrenchment Plan will include information about these programs as identified in the
         most LRP which is most current at the time when the full Retrenchment Plan is due to be
         implemented. This will enable workers to access training and alternative livelihood opportunities.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I         |PUBLIC
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                                May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page 17-32
         The types of livelihoods that are available will depend on wider efforts to develop Rogun City in the
         absence of Project construction work.
17.5.11. The implementation of a full Retrenchment Plan will reduce the impact magnitude from Large to
         Small, leading to a Moderate Adverse effect.
         Impacts on Gender and Vulnerable Groups
17.5.12. Many of the identified impacts on women and vulnerable groups that have been identified are either
         beneficial or are mitigated to the extent possible by existing management measures or general
         mitigation measures, but a few unmitigated adverse impacts remain. Targeted mitigation measures
         for these specific impacts, as well as additional enhancement measures that can be implemented,
         are presented below.
         Impact from physical resettlement on people living in residential care
17.5.13. The impacts on these groups can be mitigated by minimizing the physical distance over which they
         are moved. This may also enable some key staff to remain working in the institutions, provided that
         they too remain living locally. It is recommended that DFZ consider measures to encourage any
         resettlers working at these facilities to remain local; for example, in the event that a to-be-resettled
         worker is HoF, giving them the option to choose a land plot in the area where the new facility is
         located. Of the current resettlement sites, the most suitable location for the relocated psychiatric
         hospital and orphanage is Darband, given its relatively accessible location (after the completion of
         the new roads), and large size, which will maximize the number of potential replacement workers
         within the community. It is also recommended that DFZ gain external advice from international
         resettlement specialists in order to plan the resettlement of these facilities.
         Impact of resettlement on vulnerable individuals within non-resettled communities, including
         loss of kinship networks and economic displacement
17.5.14. Wider publicizing of the existing support measures through the LRP, to be shared in all potentially
         impacted settlements, will enable individuals or households who are eligible for support to access it.
17.5.15. DFZ, with the help of national and local authorities (Hukumats/jamoats) will conduct an information
         campaign to raise awareness of benefits available to those who are unable to work. Increased
         funding for these programs through the benefits-sharing mechanism will mitigate the budgetary
         impact of increased numbers of applicants.
         Summary of mitigation and enhancement measures for women and vulnerable groups
17.5.16. The targeted mitigation measures to reduce particular additional impacts for women and vulnerable
         groups will reduce the magnitude of these adverse impacts from Large to Small. The range of
         impacts for women and vulnerable groups will therefore be Moderate Adverse to Positive.
         OPERATIONAL
         Indirect economic impacts of the completion of construction
17.5.17. In order to ensure that Rogun, and other settlements that are heavily reliant on Project employment,
         remain viable, the area must be re-oriented towards new economic sectors. This can only be
         achieved with careful planning and investment, led by the GoT, in cooperation with local authorities,
         MoLMEP, and Rogun JSC. Work on the future development of the town must commence as soon as
         possible, in order for plans to be ready to come into effect when the Project construction workforce



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I         |PUBLIC
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                                May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page 17-33
         begins to decrease. The relevant authorities must also consult with affected communities to identify
         their preferences with regard to future development, and possible solutions for implementation. In
         order to maximize long-term resilience of affected communities, any future plans must consider a
         range of possible livelihoods.
17.5.18. It is recommended that planning for the economic re-orientation of the area be combined into a
         comprehensive Regional Development Strategy to be led by the GoT. This should:
            Identify community, local and national priorities for the long-term development of the Project AoI;
            Develop detailed objectives;
            Provide a roadmap of realistic steps to meet these objectives; and
            Allocate funds to support the desired outcomes.
17.5.19. Strategic economic planning for a large population is a complex undertaking to be carried out in
         conjunction with affected communities. However, some recommendations are provided for
         consideration, based on data gathered during the preparation of this ESIA.
17.5.20. The GoT is interested in using the Project to support the development of tourism as an alternative
         source of livelihoods and income after the completion of construction. This was mentioned by
         several FGD participants, some of whom hoped to open hospitality businesses, showing that the
         public is aware of this interest. The LRP and Benefit-Sharing Program provide opportunities to
         develop proposals by the communities around the reservoir.
17.5.21. The GoT lists as its priorities for tourism development:
            Ecological and nature tourism;
            Medical and health tourism;
            Recreational, historical and cultural tourism;
            Mountaineering; and
            Recreational hunting.
17.5.22. Any plan to increase tourism should include, as a minimum, the following elements:
          An assessment of the carrying capacity of the area to support tourists, without causing
           unacceptable disruption to local communities or environmental damage;
          Types of tourism that are most practical and acceptable in the region (e.g., mountaineering, water
           sports);
          Possible hubs for tourist infrastructure (e.g., Obigarm, Darband, Gharm);
          Measures to support equal opportunities and inclusion, particularly for women and vulnerable
           groups;
          Community sensitization to help them to adapt to the changing economy; and
          Required tourist infrastructure (e.g., hotels, chalets, transport infrastructure from Dushanbe, as
           well as basic infrastructure such as constant water supply and good quality roads) and skillsets
           (e.g., tour guides, hospitality work, language skills) to be developed in local communities, and the
           investment that is required to develop these.
17.5.23. While fewer PAPs will engage in commercial agriculture, it is an important part of the Tajik economy.
         It is recommended that MoA plan for future agricultural development in the area. This could include
         plans for modernization and increased efficiency of agricultural techniques, while avoiding
         environmental damage. The remote rural setting, relatively clean environment and availability of
         fresh produce also supports the objective of developing tourism in the area.



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I        |PUBLIC
         Project No.: UK-70097413 | Our Ref No.: Chapter 17-Rev06-English                               May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page 17-34
17.5.24. Many PAPs interviewed during the household surveys expressed an interest in small businesses
         and self-employment. MoLMEP currently offers, and is expected to continue to offer, various
         programs to help people to find new livelihoods and start businesses. It is recommended that
         MoLMEP continue information campaigns in the Project area after the completion of construction,
         resettlement and LRP implementation, in order to assist those remaining in the area to identify
         support to re-orientate their livelihoods.
17.5.25. With sufficient planning and investment, it is anticipated that Rogun could be transformed from a
         town supported by construction, to a more diverse and resilient community with a range of
         livelihoods available. This will be a Positive effect for the community.

17.6     RESIDUAL EFFECTS
17.6.1. Residual impacts are impacts that remain after the implementation of mitigation and reflect the
        efficacy of the specific mitigation measure. The mitigation measures are implemented by the
        relevant party to manage the social performance of the Project and monitoring of the mitigation
        measures provides the data required to report to the regulatory authorities, Lenders and other
        stakeholders.
17.6.2. The periodic review of the mitigation measures must consider the residual impacts of the impact
        assessment to benchmark implementation performance.
         CONSTRUCTION
17.6.3. Most socio-economic construction impacts result in effects that are either positive, or adverse in the
        short term but positive after several years as existing management measures take effect. These
        beneficial impacts do not require mitigation and fall mainly to those working for the Project directly or
        indirectly or resettlers. Adverse impacts could be felt by some non-resettled communities due to the
        loss of social and economic infrastructure located within the flooding zone. These impacts can be
        mitigated and the significance of the effects reduced to Minor-Moderate Adverse.
         OPERATION
17.6.4. The operational impacts of the Project will lead to Positive effects, assuming effective measures can
        be implemented to support the future development of the area.

17.7     SUMMARY
17.7.1. During the construction phase, positive effects are anticipated to arise from direct employment,
        indirect economic impacts, and through the implementation of the RAPs and LRPs. Significant
        adverse effects are expected to be experienced as a result of short-term impacts of resettlement
        and livelihood disruption and impacts on non-resettled communities that are socially and
        economically linked with resettled villages.
17.7.2. Once operational the Project is expected to bring positive effects resulting from direct and indirect
        economic impacts, and improved energy supply for both Tajikistan and nearby countries purchasing
        excess power. Significant adverse effects may occur as Rogun adjusts to the completion of
        construction and associated decrease in employment and spend in the local economy, but this can
        be mitigated with effective planning led by the GoT to re-orient the town towards new economic
        sectors.

18        COMMUNITY HEALTH, SAFETY AND SECURITY
18.1.1.   This chapter outlines the assessment of potential impacts of the Project on community health, safety
          and security (CHSS), in line with the requirements of the World Bank’s Environmental and Social
          Standard (ESS) 4: Community Health and Safety (2016).
18.1.2.   A comprehensive description of social baseline conditions is presented in Annex 7.1: Baseline
          Conditions Report and a summary is provided in Chapter 7: Baseline of this ESIA. An
          assessment of the potential Project impacts on CHSS is presented in Section 18.4. Where
          necessary, recommendations for the avoidance of impact, impact minimization and/or long-term
          management are included.
18.1.3.   Previous assessments of the Project have been undertaken including an ESIA completed in 2014,
          and reports prepared by the Environmental and Social Panel of Experts (ESPoE).

18.2      LEGISLATIVE FRAMEWORK, POLICY AND GUIDANCE
18.2.1.   The CHSS assessment has taken account of the relevant legislative, policy and guidance
          framework. Table 18-1 sets out international guidance documents that are relevant to this CHSS
          impact assessment.

          Table 18-1 – International guidance relevant to the CHSS assessment
