          TAJIK LEGISLATION AND REGULATIONS
18.2.2.   The Constitution of the Republic of Tajikistan guarantees and protects fundamental rights and
          freedoms, including those set out in the following Articles:
          • Safeguarding nature (Article 22) protects the right to a favorable environment and states that
            everyone has a duty to protect nature and the environment.
          • Right to Health (Article 39) recognizes the right to healthcare and medical assistance and
            highlights the importance of providing healthcare services to the population. It also indicates the
            government’s responsibility to promote and protect public health.
18.2.3.   The following laws are applicable to the Project:
          •    Law of the Republic of Tajikistan on Air Protection (2012);
          •    Law of the Republic of Tajikistan on Production and Consumption Waste (2005, last amended in
               2011);
          •    Law of the Republic of Tajikistan on drinking water and drinking water supply (December 29,
               2010);
          •    Healthcare Code of the Republic of Tajikistan of May 30, 2017. No. 1413 (as amended by the
               Law of the Republic of Tajikistan of 29.01.2021 No. 1762);
          •    Law of the Republic of Tajikistan on the protection of population and territories from emergency
               situations of natural and human-made character (July 15, 2004);
          •    Law of the Republic of Tajikistan on the Safety of Hydraulic Structures (2010 as amended in
               2018);
          •    International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights (1976); and
          •    Occupational Safety and Health Convention (1981).

18.3      ASSESSMENT METHODOLOGY
18.3.1.   The approach to the assessment has been to review the proposed Project and baseline conditions,
          including the characteristics of the population, followed by the identification of risks related to the
          sector and location of the Project, and the Project activities which may cause an impact.
          RECEPTOR SENSITIVITY
18.3.2.   To identify the sensitivity of CHSS receptors, categories of relative sensitivity are described in Table
          18-2, with reference to the baseline information.




          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                          PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                                 May 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                             18-7
          Table 18-2 – CHSS Sensitivity Criteria

           Sensitivity      Description

           High             No or limited access to health services and infrastructure due to factors such as limited or no
                            access to bridges or roads, increased travel cost and danger of using reduced quality roads.
                            No or limited access to critical services such as communication, transportation, power
                            supply, water supply, sanitation because of impacts on available infrastructure.
                            Communities where a high proportion constitutes vulnerable groups, e.g., disabled people
                            and elderly people who require community support. Communities being impacted because of
                            reduced road access and extended journey travel.
                            Communities vulnerable to endemic or non-endemic diseases, and other factors for poor
                            health who require access to health centres.
                            Community dependent on resources shared with the Project that would affect the person but
                            also the project (e.g., water, natural resources).
                            Community members who are highly vulnerable to external shocks or stresses, and who
                            would have very limited ability to adapt to Project changes and maintain their health and
                            wellbeing status.

           Medium           The health and wellbeing of the receptor is relatively good but with some sensitivities that
                            make them vulnerable to external shocks or stressors.
                            Health services and infrastructure is accessible and is of medium to good quality.
                            Majority of community have access to critical services such as communication,
                            transportation, power supply, water supply, sanitation, etc.
                            Much of the population is generally in good health and the prevalence of diseases is
                            moderate but vulnerability to diseases is low.
                            Receptors share resources with the Project but are not dependent upon them or alternatives
                            are available.
                            Community members who have some ability to adapt to external shocks or stresses, and
                            maintain their health and wellbeing status, but have some degree of vulnerability.

           Low              The receptor is generally healthy and resilient to external shocks or stressors.
                            Health services and infrastructure is accessible, with available capacity, and is of good
                            quality.
                            Community has access to critical services such as communication, transportation, power
                            supply, water supply, sanitation, etc.
                            The majority of the population is in good health and prevalence and vulnerability to diseases
                            is low.
                            Receptors do not share resources with the Project.
                            Community members with effective coping mechanisms who are likely to comprehensively
                            adapt to any Project-induced changes with minimum long-term impact on their health and
                            wellbeing status.

          ASSESSMENT OF IMPACT SIGNIFICANCE
18.3.4.   The Project impacts have been assessed against baseline conditions. The significance of the effect
          is the outcome of the interaction of the receptor sensitivity and the impact magnitude and indicates



          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                               PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                                      May 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                                  18-9
          the requirement for mitigation. The post-mitigation outcome has also been assessed to determine
          the significance of the residual effect.
18.3.5.   An evaluation of impacts on receptors takes into account:
          •     The sensitivity of the impacted receptor;
          •     The type of impact (positive or negative);
          •     The timing and frequency of the impacts;
          •     The likelihood of the identified impact;
          •     The duration, and extent of the impact; and
          •     The ability of the affected receptor to recover from temporary impacts (resilience).
18.3.6.   The description provided in Table 18-4 summarizes the categories used to assess the magnitude of
          the impact in relation to community health, safety and security.

          Table 18-4 – Impact Magnitude Categories

              Magnitude     Description

              Large         Impact affects the majority of the area or population in the Area of Influence and/or persists
                            over many years. The impact on the natural and socio-economic environment may be
                            experienced over a regional or national area.
                            Fatal accident, caused by the Project to a member of the community.
                            Project security staff engages in serious inappropriate conduct towards local communities
                            such as unsanctioned and/or aggressive use of force. Serious breaches of applicable law
                            and relevant guidelines such as the United Nation’s Code of Conduct for Law Enforcement
                            Officials occur.
                            A large labor force is required which cannot be supplied by the local workforce due to the
                            quantity of workers required, and / or skill levels available locally, therefore the majority of
                            workers are hired from elsewhere.
                            Frequency of conflicts between workers and community members.
                            The overtax of local resources for example medical care, water, sewer and road
                            infrastructure.

              Medium        Change is a clearly evident difference from baseline conditions. Impact affects a substantial
                            area or number of people and/or is of medium duration. Frequency may be occasional, and
                            impact may be regional in scale.
                            Severe injury caused by the Project to a member of the community.
                            Minor abuses of authority by Project security staff occur, and relationships with local
                            communities are poor.
                            The local workforce are able to supply most unskilled labor requirements for the Project,
                            with a smaller number of unskilled and specialized staff hired from elsewhere.

              Small         Change is a perceptible difference from baseline conditions. Impact is local, rare and affects
                            a small proportion of households, and is of a short duration.
                            Minor accident caused by the Project, or potential safety hazard present (e.g. unattended
                            plant, unfenced equipment dump) experienced by a member of the community.
                            Project security staff act within applicable law and relevant guidelines such as the United
                            Nation’s Code of Conduct for Law Enforcement Officials, but the presence/activities of
                            security staff cause inconvenience to local communities.

          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                                 18-10
           Magnitude       Description

                           Workers required for the Project can be found within the existing local workforce, and most,
                           but not all, skill gaps can be filled by training these local workers.

           Very small      No expected measurable effect on the health, safety and security status of people or
                           communities, change imperceptible from baseline situation.
                           No labor migration is required to fill the employment requirements for the Project.

           Beneficial      Improvement on baseline conditions e.g. improved access to healthcare or sanitation.

18.3.7.   The receptor sensitivity category in Table 18-2 and the impact magnitude category in Table 18-4
          combine to provide a qualitative assessment of the resulting impact.
18.3.8.   The matrix for assessing significance of effects is shown in Chapter 5: Impact Assessment
          Methodology of this ESIA and effects that are classified as moderate or major are considered to be
          significant effects. Effects classified as minor or negligible are considered to be not significant.

18.4      POTENTIAL IMPACTS
18.4.1.   CHSS impacts will mainly affect the settlements that are in close proximity to or within the
          construction area for the Project, and roads used to access these areas. Impacts may also be
          experienced by villages affected by resettlement (out- and in-migration, as well as infrastructure
          impacts). Residents of these areas may experience potential health, safety and security impacts
          during the construction period.
18.4.2.   Once operational, health, safety and security impacts may affect settlements in proximity to the edge
          of the reservoir area.
          CONSTRUCTION
          Drowning
18.4.3.   The partially-filled reservoir poses a risk of drowning, primarily to local communities (medium-
          sensitivity receptor).
18.4.4.   Although the reservoir is not in use by communities for daily activities, the risk of community
          members entering the reservoir voluntarily or accidentally may increase as the extent of the
          reservoir expands. The most significant risk at present arises from accidents (fall from a bridge or
          road traffic collision on the right bank road), although it is possible that interest in using the reservoir
          recreationally (swimming, fishing, boating) may increase as it rises and approaches settlements, and
          the volume of construction activities decreases. There is also a risk of drowning for animals, which
          poses a secondary risk to people who may enter the water to rescue pets or livestock. There are no
          known incidents in which members of the public have drowned, although several incidents involving
          drowned livestock are documented.
18.4.5.   There are currently no settlements directly at the reservoir water level. The most likely risk is
          therefore present on the bridges, as well as sections of the right-bank public road which are located
          on steep slopes directly above the reservoir. As the reservoir rises, it will approach and submerge
          the villages to be resettled. The resettlement program is currently progressing significantly above the
          water level. The Resettlement Action Plans (RAPs) set a pace of resettlement which will assume
          that all resettlers vacate their villages long before the water level is close enough to be a hazard.


          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                           PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                                  May 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                             18-11
          Some settlements will, however, be on the shoreline at full operation level (e.g., the resettlement site
          Saidoni Nav).
18.4.6.   Responsibility for reservoir safety lies with the Military Mining and Rescue Service (MMRS), which
          includes monitoring and clearing the reservoir. In line with Order No.161 of the Chairman of the
          Committee for Emergency Situations and Civil Defense of the Republic of Tajikistan (dated 12th June
          2023), the MMRS monitor the reservoir continuously and have a permanent presence on site. The
          MMRS, jointly with Rogun JSC, have also undertaken training sessions with Project workers
          regarding safety, and have installed ‘No fishing or swimming’ signs in dangerous areas.
          Figure 18-1 - Reservoir clearing by the MMRS




18.4.7.   The receptors affected by this impact are likely to be predominantly residents in Rogun City and
          nearby settlements in the Project-affected Districts (Nurobod, Rasht), as well as non-residents who
          are present in the Project AoI (medium/low sensitivity). This risk is assessed to be of Medium
          magnitude, considering the potentially serious consequences of an incident, as well as the
          measures currently in place to manage the risk and the absence of any known human drowning
          incidents to date. The effect is therefore Moderate adverse.
18.4.8.   Discharge patterns from the project and downstream fluctuations in water level also present a
          drowning risk, however this is not considered to be significant given the absence of residential
          settlements, or any other sites likely to attract members of the public, between Rogun and Nurek.
          Discharge patterns from Nurek will not change (see Volume 1, Chapter 13: Water) therefore there
          will be no increased risk of drowning in communities downstream of Nurek as a result of the Project.
          Collision Risk with Construction Vehicles
18.4.9.   Construction vehicles may collide with people, livestock, property, or other vehicles, causing injuries
          and damage. In a serious incident, fatalities or debilitating injuries may occur. Heavy vehicles can
          also cause damage to local roads, making journeys by local route users more difficult and
          hazardous. This risk occurs on local roads shared between construction and local traffic, especially
          within the site for residents of Pasi muhraho.


          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                         May 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                    18-12
18.4.10. Routes to the construction area do not have segregated paths for pedestrians, resulting in roads
         being shared by local road traffic and heavy vehicles accessing the Site. In more built-up areas,
         particularly Rogun City, roads are also used by pedestrians, including young schoolchildren. Within
         the Project construction site, access to Pasi muhraho is also via a road which is shared with
         construction traffic and villagers, both in cars and on foot. Children from this village are most at risk
         from these vehicles.
18.4.11. Roads without paved, or hard-standing, surfaces that are used to access the Site are vulnerable to
         damage, with some currently in poor condition. Poor road conditions result in erratic driving patterns,
         as drivers avoid large dips and bumps.
18.4.12. The receptors affected by this impact include residents in Rogun City and the Project-affected
         districts (medium-sensitivity), non-residents present on transport routes in the Project AoI (low
         sensitivity), and residents of Pasi muhraho (medium sensitivity). The magnitude of this impact is
         assessed as Medium for local communities and road users, due to the consequences of an
         accident and limited safeguards in place (e.g. pedestrian segregation), and Large for Pasi Muhraho
         residents due to their higher exposure. The effect is therefore Moderate adverse, rising to Major
         adverse for residents of Pasi muhraho.
         Unauthorized Access to Construction Areas
18.4.13. Construction sites can attract trespassers from the local community, such as curious children or
         people hoping to steal construction materials. These people could be exposed to hazards such as
         machinery and hazardous substances.
18.4.14. The Project construction site is fenced at accessible points, and there is one entry point to the site
         which includes a checkpoint with security forces. Due to the location of construction works within
         the site, and its distance from most residential areas, the potential for community members to either
         deliberately or spontaneously enter the construction site is limited. There is one exception however,
         as the settlement of Pasi muhraho is situated within the construction site, behind the main entrance
         gate of the construction site. Pasi muhraho is a small settlement comprised of residential properties
         (approximately 50 households) and a community hall. There is no physical barrier between the
         settlement and the wider construction site, or segregated access to the settlement from the main
         entrance gate of the construction site. Children from this village, who have been seen walking to and
         from school alongside the main route out of the construction site, are most at risk of wandering into
         areas which are unsafe.
18.4.15. For all settlements apart from Pasi muhraho, the magnitude of the impact is assessed as Small, the
         effect is therefore Minor adverse. Due to their proximity to construction activity, and the absence of
         any segregation between the settlement and the construction site, for the residents of Pasi muhraho
         the impact is assessed as Medium. The effect on this settlement is therefore Moderate adverse.
         Air Quality
18.4.16. Poor air quality can be caused by dust, generated from driving heavy vehicles/plant along tracks
         with loose and/or dry soil, blasting grounds works and excavations, or by emissions from
         construction vehicles, including idling plant. Poor air quality poses a health hazard to the local
         population, particularly those in close proximity to construction activity, including construction traffic
         routes. Community members who are more vulnerable to poor air quality due to existing health
         conditions, such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) or asthma, are most at risk of
         experiencing impacts to their health from construction activity.


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                             May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                        18-13
18.4.17. Volume 1, Chapter 8: Air Quality of this ESIA has assessed the potential impacts of fugitive dust
         and particulate matter (PM10 and PM2.5) emissions from construction activities and from exhaust
         emissions (NO2 and CO) from on-site plant and traffic.
18.4.18. Due to the distance of community residential settlements from construction activity, and the
         intermittent nature of construction road traffic, the magnitude of this impact is assessed as Small,
         the effect is therefore Minor adverse.
         Water Pollution
18.4.19. Spills and leaks of hazardous substances could affect nearby watercourses, or groundwater used
         for the local water supply. There are no nearby watercourses although a few drainage channels that
         convey runoff from the site exist. The groundwater-to-reservoir recharge would be small and
         insufficient to convey enough pollutants to cause a problem in the reservoir.
18.4.20. The health of local residents could be impacted by drinking contaminated groundwater, which could
         occur at Construction Camp 1, however there is no use of groundwater at this site, and no regional
         aquifer, therefore this risk is negligible.
18.4.21. The magnitude of this impact is assessed as Very small, the effect is therefore Negligible.

         Noise and vibration
18.4.22. Noise can negatively impact community members’ health and wellbeing by disturbing sleep,
         damaging hearing or reducing the amenity of the local environment. Construction activities, including
         vehicle/plant movements, blasting, ground works and excavations, have the potential to generate
         noise disturbance.
18.4.23. Volume 1, Chapter 11: Noise and Vibration of this ESIA has considered construction noise
         impacts on off-site receptors. Due to their distance from the construction Site and its activities, the
         magnitude of impact on receptors is assessed as Very small, the effect is therefore Negligible.
         Labor Influx
18.4.24. Labor influx refers to the large in-migration of workers which often occurs for major projects.
         Typically, this additional workforce consists of young men travelling without their families from other
         regions or countries. Currently, the majority of Project workers are from Tajikistan, with expatriates,
         predominantly from Russia, Iran and Uzbekistan, comprising approximately 5.1% of the workforce.
         23% of the local workforce are sourced from within 50km of the Project, many of whom commute to
         the Project site daily. ‘Labor influx’ in the context of the Project therefore refers both to the non-local
         workers residing in worker accommodation compounds on site or rented accommodation in Rogun
         City, and the large numbers of daily commuters from nearby settlements.
18.4.25. Approximately 14,000 of the current 17,200 workers on site (as of May 2025) work 12-hour shifts in
         construction, on a 15-day or night on/off schedule. Approximately 2,800 office workers work 8am to
         5pm shifts. The majority of construction workers remain on site during their 15 days of consecutive
         12-hour shifts, and return to their homes for their 15 days of rest. Catering, healthcare and
         recreational facilities are provided on site for workers, further reducing their need to travel outside
         the Project site. Around 1500 workers commute daily from the Project site to their homes in nearby
         settlements.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                             May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                        18-14
18.4.26. Labor influx can put pressure on local resources, infrastructure and services, particularly in
         underserved areas. There is currently some evidence that water services in Rogun City are
         overburdened as a result of the activities associated with the Project.
18.4.27. Cultural misunderstandings can occur when an in-migrant workforce comes from abroad, and could
         stem from things such as the consumption of alcohol, hygiene standards, or language barriers. In
         small communities, a large number of construction workers can overwhelm the local population and
         lead to a large over-population of young men. This can have implications for safety and security,
         particularly for local women.
18.4.28. Labor influx can also be associated with violence and crime. Young men away from their families
         are more likely than other populations to engage in violent behaviors, particularly if they are under-
         stimulated or consume alcohol.
18.4.29. Both PMG and JSC report that there have been very few incidents of conflict or other adverse
         interactions between workers and the local communities, including the foreign workers who live in
         communities near the construction site. The current construction workers’ accommodation includes
         amenities for workers including health, catering and recreational facilities. It is not anticipated that
         there may be significant impacts on the existing community facilities and infrastructure, as the
         majority of services for workers are provided within the construction compounds.
18.4.30. The magnitude of the labor influx impact on receptors has been assessed as Medium as while there
         have not been any reported incidents or conflict, there is evidence that the large construction
         population is impacting local services, resulting in a Moderate adverse effect on the local
         community of Rogun City (medium-sensitivity).
         Communicable Disease
18.4.31. Labor influx increases population mixing, which can increase the spread of infectious diseases,
         particularly if large numbers of people are brought in from outside of the local area. Diseases may
         spread through regular interactions (e.g., shops or hospitality venues), sexual relationships, or sex
         work. Overcrowded or unhygienic living conditions in workers’ accommodation can also lead to
         disease outbreaks which spread into the community.
18.4.32. Medical facilities equipped with basic medicines are available on site for workers. Washing facilities
         are provided in worker accommodation, although substandard sanitary conditions have been
         observed on site in previous labor audits (see the Labor Management Procedures, 2024). It
         should be noted that most of the current accommodation provision will be demolished and cleared
         as reservoir levels rise. New accommodation will be provided downstream of the dam and will be
         required to meet international standards (set out in the IFC/EBRD guidance note “Standards and
         processes for workers' accommodation: processes and standards”) from the outset, and any existing
         sub-standard accommodation will need to be improved meet the same standards (see Volume 1,
         Chapter 19: Labor and Working Conditions for a further assessments of worker accommodation).
18.4.33. The majority of workers, who live in on-site accommodation and work 12-hour, 15-day shifts, have
         limited opportunities to interact with the local community. Some workers live in Rogun City where
         there is the potential for interactions with local residents and the possibility for the spread of disease,
         however these constitute a relatively small proportion of the population and pose no greater risk that
         other residents. No significant disease outbreaks have been associated with the Project to date. The
         magnitude of this impact is assessed as Small, and the significance of the effect is Minor adverse
         for the local community of Rogun City (medium-sensitivity).


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                     PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                            May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                       18-15
        Gender-based Violence (GBV)
18.4.34. Possible GBV scenarios impacting local communities have been categorized in Table 18-5.

        Table 18-5 – Potential types of GBV arising from the Project

          Type of GBV          Relevance to and impact of the Project

          A Project worker     The Project has introduced a large and predominantly male workforce to the area,
          targets a member     which will continue to increase for several years before slowly decreasing.
          of the local         Settlements located near to the construction compound are most at risk of impacts.
          community            Similarly, any public spaces where Project workers and community members mingle,
                               such as shops, streets, hospitality venues, and transport routes (including public
                               transport) used by commuting workers, could also be at risk for impacts. As well as
                               harming the individuals who are targeted, GBV perpetrated by Project workers to
                               community members can harm community relations and even make the Project
                               unacceptable to the community.
                               Women and girls who are targeted may also be re-victimized by their husbands or
                               families if it is believed that they provoked the perpetrator. This is a particular risk in
                               conservative, religious communities, such as the Project area, where stigma around
                               experiencing GBV is high.
                               Particular sensitivities exist for underage girls, who are both more vulnerable in
                               themselves, and also have less power within communities and households to raise
                               grievances and influence outcomes. Tajikistan has a young population, and young
                               girls under 20 constitute around 23% of the population.

          Interactions         Project workers may engage in consensual relationships, friendships, or innocuous
          between Project      interactions with female community members which evoke negative reactions
          workers and local    amongst local men. Husbands and fathers may feel jealous or insulted by interactions
          women indirectly     they believe to be inappropriate, and become aggressive or controlling towards the
          leading to GBV       woman in question.
                               Women and girls in conservative communities, such as the Project AoI, are more
                               vulnerable to this impact, as interactions between men and women are more
                               scrutinized and controlled. Women in these areas typically have less power within
                               communities and households.

          A Project worker     Workers may become aware of GBV occurring between community members
          becomes aware of     unrelated to the Project, for example through witnessing events or verbal reports from
          GBV in the           family, friends and acquaintances. This is particularly relevant for community-facing
          community            workers, such as Community Liaison Officers (CLOs), who are in regular contact with
                               the community.
                               Witnessing or becoming aware of GBV can be very distressing, particularly if the
                               witness is unsure how to respond, or concerned that any actions they take may make
                               the situation worse for the victim.

18.4.35. A detailed assessment of GBV risks associated with the Project are presented in Annex A17.1:
        Gender of this ESIA. The GBV risk to women in the local community, who are considered a high-
        sensitivity receptor, resulting from labor influx and the presence of worker accommodation is
        assessed as Medium, resulting in a Major adverse effect before mitigation.
        Violence perpetrated by Security Workers
18.4.36. Security workers are present at the main entrance gate to the Project site and are responsible for
        checking vehicles, passengers and pedestrians to ensure they are authorized to access the



        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                              PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                                     May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                                18-16
        construction site. Armed military personnel and police also provide security at the main gate to the
        construction site, and private security are also present in places. Based on discussions during
        community engagement activities, there is no evidence of, or issues with, the use of security forces
        amongst affected communities.
18.4.37. The majority of local residents do not enter the site except for work or business purposes. For
        residents of Pasi muhraho, who must pass through site security to enter and leave their village and
        interact with security forces, the risk of a potential incident is higher. The presence of children
        amongst the community, who are also subject to security checks when entering the site, further
        increases the risk. Lone children have been observed passing through the security checkpoint and
        undergoing back and pocket searches (non-contact) and ID checks. The searches were conducted
        by teams of security personnel at the checkpoints at the site entrance. No body searches or private
        searches were observed. The children appeared familiar with the process and did not appear to be
        distressed by it.
18.4.38. The magnitude of this impact is assessed as Small, resulting in a Minor adverse effect for the local
        population of Rogun City (medium-sensitivity). For Pasi muhraho, the magnitude is assessed as
        Medium, resulting in a Moderate adverse effect.
        Physical and economic displacement
18.4.39. The Project will result in very large physical and economic displacement impacts:

        •     Over 50,000 Project-Affected People (PAPs) will be physically resettled from the villages within
              the Flooding Zone. Physical resettlement is currently planned to be complete in 2032, with full
              operation being reached in 2038.
        •     People in the non-resettled villages may lose some access to workplaces, agricultural land and
              other facilities located in the Flooding Zone.
        •     Residents of villages close to resettlement sites (host communities) may be impacted by the in-
              migration of resettlers.
18.4.40. An assessment of impacts on the local economy, livelihoods, agricultural production, and access to
        infrastructure for resettled, host, and non-resettled communities are presented in Volume 1,
        Chapter 17: Socio-economics of this ESIA.
18.4.41. From a CHSS perspective, this displacement could result in a number of potential impacts for the
        three community types (resettled, host, and non-resettled) presented in Table 18-6. It should be
        noted that these categories are not mutually exclusive. For example, villages to be resettled towards
        the end of the resettlement period may be temporarily impacted as non-resettled communities if their
        closest neighbors resettle earlier.

        Table 18-6 – CHSS impacts due to physical and economic displacement

            Impact               Description

                                                    Resettled communities

            Natural hazards at   Tajikistan is affected by numerous natural hazards which pose a risk to communities,
            resettlement sites   such as landslides, earthquakes and flooding. The villages within the Flooding Zone
                                 are located at the base of or on steep slopes, where landslides frequently occur after
                                 earthquakes or heavy rain and damage homes and roads.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                           PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                                  May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                             18-17
 Impact                Description

                       Natural hazards, particularly seismicity and flooding, are considered in the selection
                       of sites. 4 of the current sites (Namuna and Toichi in Tursunzoda, Chorsada-2 in
                       Danghara and Sebiston in Faizobod) are located on flat ground where landslide risk
                       is negligible. Newly constructed residential housing may also be more structurally
                       resilient than previous houses, which are old and under-maintained.
                       A full assessment of natural and man-made hazards at the Project site and
                       resettlement sites is presented in Volume 1, Chapter 23: Major Accidents and
                       Disasters of this ESIA. It is considered that the overall risk profile of resettlement
                       sites is, overall, lower than at the pre-resettlement villages.

 Demolition and        Demolition of previous housing is carried out by DFZ contractors and therefore poses
 salvage               no risk to communities. However, PAPs are allowed to gather salvage materials for
                       use in house reconstruction. While this is a benefit that helps communities by
                       reducing the cost of construction, risks are present at the demolition site, in particular
                       due to the disturbance of asbestos, which is legal and widely used in Tajikistan.
                       Asbestos is highly toxic and carcinogenic, and exposure leads to a number of health
                       conditions including asbestosis (scarring of the lungs) and mesothelioma (an
                       aggressive cancer).
                       PAPs may be exposed to asbestos fibers when gathering or transporting asbestos,
                       and suffer negative health impacts. No asbestos surveys have been carried out in the
                       resettled villages, hence it is not possible to quantify this impact. While no cases of
                       asbestos-related illness have been reported, these conditions develop over years or
                       decades and therefore may not be currently in evidence.

 House                 PAPs are responsible for their own house reconstruction. Risks are present on
 reconstruction        construction sites, such as hand and power tools, working at height, etc. A house
                       specification is provided, and the payment of asset compensation to households is
                       linked to achieving construction milestones. Many people (particularly men) in
                       Tajikistan have construction skills and prefer to manage construction themselves,
                       although some hire laborers, and for vulnerable households, laborers can be
                       provided through the jamoat. All construction will therefore be carried out by people
                       with appropriate skills and experience and the risks will be minimal. However, risks
                       arising from the use of asbestos remain, as it is still used in some construction
                       throughout Tajikistan.

 Physical              Resettlement involves the physical transportation of people and their belongings
 transportation of     (livestock, salvage materials, personal belongings, transplanted trees), sometimes
 people and            over long distances. Transportation is provided by DFZ at no cost to PAPs. Currently,
 belongings            the most distant resettlement sites, Namuna and Toichi in Tursunzoda, are over
                       180km from the Project site. Road safety in Tajikistan is rather poor, with road
                       accidents the 5th most common cause of death in the country.
                       As resettlement is occurring relatively slowly over several years, and is spread over a
                       large geographical area, it will not result in a significant increase in traffic in any
                       specific area. The PAPs and DFZ drivers are also accustomed to driving in the local
                       conditions, so will not be exposed to increased risks relative to baseline.

 Infrastructure and    Infrastructure and utility provision at resettlement sites is significantly improved
 utility provision     relative to that at resettlement villages, although historically there have been some
                       temporary gaps in provision (see Chapter 17: Socio-economics of this ESIA). At the
                       time of preparation of this ESIA, there are no resettlement sites lacking provision of
                       basic services (medical center, school, water, electricity). Implementation of the
                       current RAP 2 (2017-2026) and future RAPs will ensure that infrastructure is provided
                       in future to keep pace with ongoing resettlement.

 Impact                Description

 Interactions with     If host communities are opposed to in-migration into their area, there is potential for
 host communities      hostile interactions which in extreme cases may become violent. However, extensive
                       preparation works were undertaken before the commencement of resettlement into
                       the current sites, including an assessment of the ability of the area to accommodate
                       additional population, and consultations with the jamoats. All of the resettlement sites
                       except Darband are also located at some distance (at least 100m, and in some cases
                       more than 2km) from the nearest host communities. Host communities can also
                       benefit from infrastructure and job opportunities at resettlement sites, increasing
                       community cohesion. Some host community members were observed working at
                       resettlement sites and host communities advising resettlers about adapting
                       agricultural techniques to local climatic conditions.
                       Available information suggests limited but positive relationships between resettlers
                       and host communities.

                                             Host communities

 Infrastructure        Host communities benefit from infrastructure improvements in their area. For
 provision             example, the large hospital (including ambulance services) and maternity unit in
                       Darband are available to the surrounding area, and the catchment area for the school
                       in Toichi includes host communities. Improvements have also been made to water
                       and energy infrastructure in areas where resettlement sites are located.

 Interactions with     As stated above, available information suggests limited but positive relationships
 resettlers            between resettlers and host communities.
 communities

                               Non-resettled communities in the Project AoI

 Infrastructure        If non-resettled communities are reliant on key facilities that are lost to inundation,
 provision             they may be negatively impacted by their loss. Some such examples of non-resettled
                       villages that may lose access to critical infrastructure have been identified (see
                       Volume 1, Chapter 17: Socio-economics of this ESIA). While infrastructure is
                       replaced within the Project AoI, for some villages distances to access this
                       infrastructure will be longer, and there may be gaps in provision of services usually
                       provided at village level or for groups of closely-linked villages (primary schools,
                       medical centers), or when regionally- or nationally-important facilities are lost (e.g.,
                       Navobod psychiatric hospital).



 Remoteness and        The non-resettled villages in the Project-affected Districts are located on the upper
 isolation             slopes and in tributary villages, often in rather remote locations. The loss of their
                       nearest neighbors could render these villages more isolated and potentially reduce
                       social contacts, particularly if friends and family are resettled.
                       Not all villages will be rendered more isolated, however. New roads will facilitate
                       access to other settlements, especially for villages located near to them, and several
                       villages (Obilurd, Khumdon, Tirgar) are contiguous or near-contiguous with Darband
                       and will become more suburban in character. These changes will have benefits for
                       residents’ health and wellbeing, such as easier access to medical care, but will also
                       have impacts on those struggling to adapt to the changed conditions.




Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                          PUBLIC |
Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                                 May 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                            18-19
18.4.42. The above-identified impacts are either Beneficial or Very small adverse after the implementation of
          the RAPs (for resettled and host communities) and MoLMEP economic study and its outcomes (for
          non-resettled communities), as well as other mitigation measures identified in Volume 1, Chapter
          17: Socio-economics of this ESIA. These communities are considered medium-sensitivity from a
          CHSS perspective. The effects will therefore be Positive or Minor adverse.
18.4.43. Community members may also be exposed to asbestos if they salvage certain building materials
          from demolished houses and if they install asbestos containing materials in their new house. On a
          precautionary basis, this impact is considered to be Medium given the large number of people
          potentially impacted, though over a short-term period, and the potential for fatalities, and the effect is
          therefore Moderate Adverse.
          OPERATION
          Drowning risk
18.4.44. The reservoir at full operation will continue to present a hazard once the Project is complete. The
          non-resettled villages in the Project-affected Districts (medium-sensitivity) will be closer to the water,
          and some will be on the reservoir shore. Fall risk from the new bridges and roads located on the
          banks may remain, although this will be reduced if they are constructed with safety features such as
          suitable barriers and guard rails. It is understood that that the MMRS will retain responsibility for
          monitoring the reservoir and performing rescue operations during this Project phase. There will be
          continued placement of signs by the MMRS as the reservoir grows and reaches its full extent.
18.4.45. Resident communities will also have had many years to become accustomed to the presence of the
          Project during construction and reservoir filling. However, if tourism increases due to interest in the
          fully operational Project, it is more likely that people from outside the local area (low sensitivity) may
          be present, and may be more likely to engage in reckless behavior, such as swimming or diving in
          the reservoir. The discharge patterns from the Project will not change (see Volume1, Chapter 13:
          Water) so there is likely to be no increased risk of drowning for communities that are downstream of
          Nurek.
18.4.46. This risk is therefore Medium, and the effect is Moderate adverse.

18.5      MITIGATION AND ENHANCEMENT MEASURES
          CONSTRUCTION
18.5.1.   A construction Community Health and Safety Management Plan (CHSMP) has been developed
          for implementation during the remaining construction phase of the Project (Volume 3, A8:
          Community Health and Safety Management Plan of this ESIA). This will address risks identified in
          this assessment, which will also be mitigated through actions set out in other complementary
          Management Plans which include mitigations as described below. The CHSMP contains special
          measures for Pasi muhraho, to reflect the unique nature of this settlement and the specific impacts
          its residence experience due to its location within the site. Affected communities will also be able to
          access to the Project-specific Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) to raise any concerns or
          complaints that they might have related to safety around the construction site.
18.5.2.   Specific mitigation related to individual impacts is presented below and summarized in other
          management plans, such as the ESMP.




          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                     PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                            May 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                       18-20
          Drowning risk
18.5.3.   MMRS will conduct a full safety assessment and prepare a Reservoir Discharges Safety Plan
          (RRDSP), which will be reviewed and updated for each stage of reservoir filling. This will identify the
          areas of highest risk, and the need for safety signage and rescue equipment installation, as well as
          communication channels with local communities, training, and community sensitization, and the
          need for reservoir patrols and monitoring by the MMRS. Safety signage and equipment will need to
          be regularly monitored and moved as the reservoir fills and shoreline shifts.
18.5.4.   Information sessions with local communities which will become (temporarily or permanently)
          shoreline or near-shoreline settlements, including schools sessions for children, will be organized by
          Rogun JSC/PMG and with the involvement of the MMRS. These sessions will cover the hazards
          which are specific to the reservoir and discharge waters and how these differ from the river which
          was present previously, instructions for the use of any rescue equipment that has been installed,
          emergency contact numbers, and a reminder not to enter the water in order to help other people or
          animals.
18.5.5.   Appropriate safety measures (barriers, signs, rescue equipment) will be integrated into the design of
          roads and bridges. The Traffic Management Plan (Volume 3, Annex A9b: Off-Site Traffic
          Management Plan of this ESIA) includes requirements to add safety barriers to the new M41
          highway which has already been constructed.
18.5.6.   The implementation of these measures will reduce the impact of drowning risk to Small, leading to a
          Minor residual effect.
          Collision Risk with Construction Vehicles
18.5.7.   The Contractor(s) will ensure works are planned to enable them to be delivered safely, and in a
          manner which minimizes congestion, road safety risks and disruption for all road users, in
          accordance with a Traffic Management Plans (TMPs) (Volume 3, Annex A9a: On-Site Traffic
          Management Plan, and Annex A9b: Off-Site Traffic Management Plan of this ESIA). This Plan
          includes the following, with relevance to CHSS:
          •   On-site TMP:
              •   A traffic risk and hazard mitigation strategy;
              •   Vehicle and mobile plant standards;
              •   Transport infrastructure design and maintenance, including internal roads, permanent and
                  temporary bridges, signage, safety barriers, speed reduction and traffic calming measures,
                  and dust control;
              •   Traffic regulation and operational procedures, including speed limits, safety rules for drivers,
                  and transportation of hazardous materials;
              •   Pedestrian safety measures;
              •   Security and traffic coordination measures;
              •   Environmental traffic hazard mitigation;
              •   Emergency response; and
              •   Driver and traffic safety training.
          •   Off-site TMP:
              •   A traffic risk and hazard mitigation strategy;


          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                    PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                           May 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                      18-21
              •   Vehicle and equipment specifications;
              •   Community engagement and road safety, including requirements to implement a road safety
                  campaign, implementation of a GRM, and continuous engagement;
              •   Traffic operations and journey management;
              •   Complex, oversized and hazardous loads;
              •   Environmental mitigation measures; and
              •   Contractor and driver management.
18.5.8.   All drivers will be provided with comprehensive training and be subject to competence requirements
          as detailed in the TMPs. Responsibility for implementing driver training and ensuring that drivers
          adhere to it will sit with their direct employer. The Project is to consider, if possible, the
          implementation of tracking software in Project vehicles to monitor speed and compliance with rules
          and regulations.
18.5.9.   The On-site TMP will mitigate risks to residents of Pasi muhraho. Information sessions are to be
          held with this community to share the TMP and the new measures to be brought in, particular those
          affecting the site entrance and the road leading to the village. This will include road safety sessions
          in schools for children. These activities will be led by the Engagement and Grievance Manager
          (EGM) responsible for Rogun town and local areas. Further details of engagement methods are
          detailed in the Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP).
18.5.10. The Off-site TMP will mitigate risks to local communities using the public roads and will lead to
          improvements in road safety. Local communities will not be at risk from on-site traffic.
18.5.11. The Project-specific GRM will be available to all affected communities and stakeholders to enable
          them to raise concerns related to the Project. The EGM, appointed to manage engagement with
          Rogun town and surrounding local areas, will be responsible for managing grievances which can be
          raised by various channels including a paper form, face to face during meetings, online, or via post.
          Local communities can use this GRM to raise concerns or complaints about issues, such as traffic
          and road safety, related to the Project.
18.5.12. The implementation of the TMPs, as well as the construction of new roads, is anticipated to improve
          road safety relative to the current situation and therefore after mitigation this impact will be
          Beneficial. The residual effect is therefore Positive.
          Unauthorized Access to Construction Areas
18.5.13. The implementation of the Security Management Plan (Volume 3, Annex A7: Security
          Management Plan) will mitigate risks from unauthorized access to the site. The following measures
          to prevent unauthorized access are included:
          •   Community engagement and security risk mitigation, including public safety briefings, GRM
              implementation, and awareness campaigns (including schools), as well coordination with Project
              and public authorities
          •   Site security arrangements, including securing the site perimeter, video surveillance, lighting and
              the presence of guard posts
          •   Site access control for people and vehicles
          •   Emergency response procedures to identify, remove and prevent the return of intruders
18.5.14. Construction areas, inclusive of open excavations, stockpiles, steep slopes and construction
          equipment will continue to be surrounded by barricades with appropriate warning signs to protect the


          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                             May 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                        18-22
        public and prevent unauthorized access. This will be in accordance with measures set out in the
        Community Health and Safety Management Plan (CHSMP).
18.5.15. Affected communities will be able to access the Project-specific GRM to raise any concerns or
        complaints that they might have related to safety around the construction site.
18.5.16. Implementation of the CHSMP and SMP will reduce the magnitude of impact to Very small. The
        residual effect is therefore Negligible. For Pasi muhraho, the impact will be reduced to Small. The
        residual effect will be Minor adverse.
        Labor influx and Communicable disease
18.5.17. Rogun JSC, DFZ and all contractors conduct induction training for all their employees and the
        employees of all their sub-contractors, which covers health and safety practices. This induction
        training will be updated to cover community relationships, including acceptable conduct toward local
        community members, specifically women and girls, and details of sanctions for non-compliance.
        Additionally, guidance on the detrimental effects of the abuse of alcohol and drugs and other
        potentially harmful substances, and the risk and concerns relating to HIV/AIDS, COVID-19 and of
        other health risk-related activities will be provided to workers.
18.5.18. The induction will also include training on the CoC. A sample CoC, to be finalized and approved by
        Rogun JSC, is annexed to the Labor Management Procedures (Volume 2, Annex 12) for the Project.
        A CoC will include Project-specific requirements that follow the World Bank-required template.
        Copies of the CoC will be retained by the employers and displayed on notice boards within worker
        accommodation facilities.
18.5.19. Where new worker accommodation is being provided, sufficient medical facilities should also be
        provided to meet the needs of those being accommodated.
18.5.20. Suitable induction training for all workers, alongside the requirement to sign a Workers’ CoC, and
        the enforcement of the CoC will reduce the magnitude of labor influx impacts and potential
        communicable transmission risks to Small in both cases. The residual effect is therefore Minor
        adverse.
        Gender-based Violence (GBV)
18.5.21. The following mitigation measures will be implemented to ensure reduced gender-related risks
        associated with the Project:
        •   A Gender Action Plan (GAP) has been prepared alongside this ESIA to mitigate gender risks on
            the Project, including GBV. Implementation of the GAP, which includes detailed roles and
            responsibilities for managing gender risks and maximizing opportunities, will reduce the
            likelihood of GBV occurring to a minimum.
        •   Gender-related grievances will be managed in a gender-sensitive way, including a Sensitive
            GRM Office that will be applicable to all workers. This will be implemented and widely
            communicated as part of induction and refresher training. An adequately experienced third-party
            Sensitive GRM Officer will establish oversight.
        •   Contractors will be contractually obligated to include provisions against GBV, which will be
            stipulated within the CoC to be signed by all Project workers.
        •   Appropriate and adequately trained staff will be employed to handle reports of misconduct,
            including sensitive grievances.



        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                        May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                   18-23
18.5.22. Further details of mitigation measures to address impacts arising from the Project associated with
        Gender can be found in Annex 17.1: Gender of this ESIA. A full suite of actions to mitigate risks
        arising from the Project associated with gender is set out in the GAP.
18.5.23. The implementation of actions set out in the GAP, alongside the implementation of a Workers’ CoC
        and induction training covering appropriate and acceptable behaviors, will likely reduce the
        incidence of GBV below the current baseline, and the effect will therefore be Negligible.
        Violence perpetrated by Security Workers
18.5.24. The SMP (Volume 03, A7: Security Management Plan of this ESIA) includes competency
        requirements for private security personnel, a CoC for private security personnel, and rules
        regarding the reasonable use of force by private security personnel. The requires all Contractors to
        carry out background checks on newly hired workers, proportionate to their role. Security-related
        training needs are also identified. Military and police personnel behaviors are governed by the Code
        of Ethics for Civil Servant in the Republic of Tajikistan (2015). The Security Management Plan
        contains requirements for interactions between community members and private and public security
        personnel, including special precautions for interactions with under-18s. It is recommended that
        Rogun JSC implement training at schools in Rogun City to ensure that children are aware of their
        rights and responsibilities regarding complying with security searches.
18.5.25. The local community and Project workers each have their own Project-specific GRMs (detailed in
        the SEP and Labor Management Procedures respectively) where concerns or complaints related to
        the Project, including any related to security personnel, can be raised.
18.5.26. The implementation of the SMP will reduce the impact to Very small. The residual effect is
        therefore Negligible.
        Physical and economic displacement – asbestos exposure during demolition and
        construction of resettlers’ houses
18.5.27. An outline Demolition Management Plan (Volume 3, Annex B14: Outline Demolition
        Management Plan of this ESIA) has been prepared, which includes measures for the management
        of waste from demolition activities, including the management of asbestos-containing waste. This
        outline plan has been prepared for use by PMG and JSC, to be completed by EPC Contractors, this
        is also to be adopted by DFZ, and applied to demolition activities associated with resettlement.
18.5.28. Displaced people will be prohibited from salvaging and reusing asbestos from demolished buildings.

18.5.29. Implementation of the Demolition Management Plan should reduce the risk of asbestos exposure to
        Small, resulting in a Minor adverse residual effect.
        OPERATION
        Drowning risk
18.5.30. An operational public safety assessment and RRDSP will be undertaken when the reservoir is at full
        operation. This will identify key risks and hazards, and provide mitigation measures to be
        implemented. This will also take account of long-term development plans for the reservoir and
        surrounding area, and whether any recreational uses of the reservoir have developed either
        spontaneously or as a result of targeted economic development measures. As during construction,
        mitigation will include safety signage and rescue equipment installed at key locations, engagement
        and training with communities, and MMRS patrols.


        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                         May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                    18-24
18.5.31. Drowning risk to be considered in the development of any tourist infrastructure. If any elements are
          constructed to attract tourists into the vicinity of the reservoir (e.g., viewing platforms, photogenic
          signs), safety barriers and signage will be implemented.
18.5.32. The implementation of these measures will reduce the impact of drowning risk to Small, leading to a
          Minor residual effect.

18.6      RESIDUAL EFFECTS
18.6.1.   Residual impacts are impacts that remain after the implementation of mitigation and reflect the
          effectiveness of the specific mitigation measures. The mitigation measures are implemented by the
          responsible parties specified in the ESMP to manage the social performance of the Project and
          monitoring of the mitigation measures provides the data required to report to the regulatory
          authorities, Lenders and other stakeholders.
18.6.2.   The periodic review of the mitigation measures will consider the residual impacts of the impact
          assessment to benchmark implementation performance.
          CONSTRUCTION
18.6.3.   During construction, no significant adverse CHSS effects will remain after the implementation of all
          identified mitigation measures. Positive residual effects will remain for collision risk with Project
          vehicles, GBV risk, and physical and economic displacement. The assessment of these effects as
          positive acknowledges that many of these impacts have already occurred as part of the baseline
          conditions due to the partially-built Project, and the implementation of the mitigation measures
          outlined in this chapter will lead to improvements relative to the current situation.
          OPERATION
18.6.4.   No significant adverse CHSS effects will remain after the implementation of all identified mitigation
          measures when the Project is complete and fully operational. Positive effects arise from improved
          connectivity due to the new roads and bridges, and enhanced protection against PMF.

18.7      SUMMARY
18.7.1.   During the construction phase, positive effects are anticipated to arise from implementation of the
          RAPs and LRPs, GAP and TMPs, and the improvements in community infrastructure constructed to
          replace that lost due to impoundment.
18.7.2.   Once operational the Project is anticipated to have positive CHSS effects arising from protection
          against PMF and infrastructure improvements. No significant adverse effects are anticipated in
          operation.

19       LABOR AND WORKING CONDITIONS
19.1.1. This chapter outlines the assessment of potential impacts of the Project on labor and working
        conditions, in line with the requirements of the World Bank’s Environmental and Social Standard
        (ESS) 2: Labor and Working Conditions (2016).
19.1.2. A comprehensive description of social baseline conditions is presented in Volume 2, Annex 7.1:
        Baseline Conditions Report and a summary is provided in Volume 1, Chapter 7: Environmental
        and Social Baseline of this ESIA. An assessment of the potential Project impacts on labor and
        working conditions is presented in Section 19.4. and where necessary, recommendations for the
        avoidance of impact, impact minimization and/or long-term management are included.

19.2     LEGISLATIVE FRAMEWORK, POLICY AND GUIDANCE
19.2.1. The labor and working conditions assessment has considered the relevant legislation, policy and
        guidance framework.
         TAJIKISTAN LEGISLATION AND REGULATIONS
19.2.2. The Labor Code (2016) of the Republic of Tajikistan is a comprehensive legal framework that
        regulates labor relations and ensures the protection of workers’ rights. The labor code aims to create
        a fair and balanced labor environment focused on social justice and protecting the rights of all
        parties in the labor market.
19.2.3. In Tajikistan, Occupational Health and Safety (OHS) is primarily governed by the Law of the
        Republic of Tajikistan on Labor Safety (2009). Additional legislation applicable to the Project is set
        out in Table 19-1.

         Table 19-1 – Applicable Tajik Legislation

          Tajik Legislation                                      Requirements

          Law on State Social Insurance (No. 517, 1997)          This law outlines the provisions for social insurance,
                                                                 including benefits for sickness, maternity and
                                                                 unemployment.

          Law on Fire Safety (No. 363, 2008)                     This law outlines the general legal, economic, social
                                                                 and organizational basis for ensuring fire safety in
                                                                 the country.

          Law on Labor Protection (No. 517, 2009).               This law establishes the legal framework for labor
                                                                 protection, aiming to create safe working conditions
                                                                 that preserve the life and health of workers.

          Law on Employment of the Population (No. 669,          This law regulates employment policies, including
          1997)                                                  job placement services, vocational training and
                                                                 measures to support employment for vulnerable
                                                                 groups.

          Law on Occupational Safety and Health (No. 517,        This law outlines the responsibilities of employers
          2009)                                                  and employees in ensuring safe and healthy working
                                                                 conditions.

        conditions impact assessment.

        Table 19-2 – Applicable International Standards and Guidance

                                                               worker management relationships and enhance the
                                                               development benefits of a project by treating workers
                                                               fairly, while also providing them with safe and healthy
                                                               working conditions.

                                                               relationships (including the provision of clear, written
                                                               terms of employment, timely payment of wages,
