19.2.5. Good International Industry Practice (GIIP) refers to the standards and practices mostly accepted
        and applied by the international community to ensure safe and efficient operations across various
        industries.
19.2.6. According to the World Bank, GIIP refers to guidelines that are technical reference documents
        focused on industry-specific examples of best practices. These include occupational health and
        safety related to safe facility design and operation, adequate training, personal protective equipment
        (PPE), and management of physical, chemical, biological, and radiological hazards. Specific
        guidelines relevant to this chapter include;
         General Environmental, Health, and Safety (EHS) Guidelines 2.0: Occupational Health and
          Safety (2007), and
         General EHS Guidelines 4.0: Construction and Decommissioning (2007).
19.2.7. There are key International Labour Organization (ILO) conventions which set out basic principles to
        be implemented, including strict requirements on the minimum age for employment, employee
        rights, and the elimination of forced and child labor cases. ILO Conventions that have been ratified
        by the Government of Tajikistan are as follows;
           Freedom of Association and Protection of the Right to Organise Convention, 1948 (No. 87)
           Right to Organise and Collective Bargaining Convention, 1949 (No. 98)
           Forced Labour Convention, 1930 (No. 29) (and its 2014 Protocol)
           Abolition of Forced Labour Convention, 1957 (No. 105)
           Minimum Age Convention, 1973 (No. 138)
           Worst Forms of Child Labour Convention, 1999 (No. 182).
           Equal Remuneration Convention, 1951 (No. 100)
           Discrimination (Employment and Occupation) Convention, 1958 (No. 111)
           Maternity Protection Convention (Revised), 1953 (no. 103)
           Working Environment (Air Pollution, Noise and Vibration) Convention, 1977 (No. 148)
           Employment Policy Convention, 1964 (No. 122)
           Labor Inspection Convention, 1947 (No. 81)

19.3    ASSESSMENT METHODOLOGY
19.3.1. The approach to the assessment has been to review the proposed Project and baseline conditions,
        including the workforce characteristics, followed by the identification of risks related to the sector
        and location of the Project, and the Project activities which may cause an impact.
        RECEPTORS
19.3.2. Receptors have been categorized according to ESS2 as follows:
         Direct Workers involved in the execution and completion of the project on the Project site; Direct
          workers will include PMG, Rogun JSC and DFZ employees and staff. These workers will be hired
          under individual contracts, on a full-time and/or part-time basis, with specific definitions of
          assigned tasks and responsibilities. There will be approximately 1,200 workers across various
          disciplines forming this category on the Project during construction, reducing to 800 once fully
          operational.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: ESIA-Ch19-Labor-Rev04-eng                          May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 19-3
         Contract Workers and subcontractors involved in construction activities for the Project. All
          contract workers involved in the construction will be hired based on a written labor agreement
          aligned with the Tajikistan Labor Code. The number of contract workers will be between 12,000
          and 17,000 as needs and activities on the Project evolve.
         Primary Supply Chain Workers: These workers regularly supply the goods and materials
          required for the Project's construction phase.
         Community Workers: Community workers will not be employed in relation to this Project as
          local community members are hired as the contractor’s labor force (Labor Management
          Procedures, 2023).
        RECEPTOR SENSITIVITY
19.3.3. The table below describes the relative sensitivity categories of receptors based on the baseline
        information to identify their sensitivity.

        Table 19-3 – Labor and Working Conditions Sensitivity Criteria

         Sensitivity     Description

         High            The project is in a location or sector for which there are reports of non-compliance of labor
                         and working conditions with international standards.
                         The workforce is predominantly migrant labor, including workers from low-income
                         countries.
                         Work takes place in high-risk conditions, including hazardous activities, extreme
                         temperatures, long hours or at isolated locations.
                         Workers will have to use worker accommodation which is provided by their employer and is
                         at high risk of non-compliance with applicable standards.
                         High risk that the Government will not enforce labor and working conditions to the
                         applicable standards.
                         Workers will likely struggle to adapt to adverse labor and working conditions without
                         extensive support and/or measures implemented by their employer.

         Medium          The project is in a location or sector for which some reports or anecdotes demonstrate a
                         potential risk of gaps in labor and working conditions and compliance with international
                         standards, but the prevalence of such risks is uncertain.
                         The workforce includes some migrant labor.
                         Work takes place in conditions that present some risk to workers, but activities are common
                         and risks are well understood, e.g. involving heavy machinery and working at heights.
                         Workers may be able to independently adapt to their labor and working conditions to a
                         limited extent, with limited intervention by their employer.
                         The risks identified can be absorbed and addressed by following any proposed mitigation
                         procedures.

         Low             The project location or sector is not known to have poor labor and working condition
                         practices.
                         Workers are not reliant on the accommodation provided by their employers, and the risks of
                         non-compliance with applicable standards for worker accommodation are low.
                         Workers are from the Project country or include only a small expatriate workforce.

          Sensitivity    Description

                         There is a low risk that the government will not enforce labor and working conditions to the
                         applicable standards.
                         The potential risks faced by workers are low and can easily be mitigated.
                         Workers have minimal vulnerability to their labor and working conditions, and are able to
                         realize opportunities provided by their employment without the assistance of their employer.

        ASSESSMENT OF IMPACT SIGNIFICANCE
19.3.4. The assessment will evaluate the potential impacts on labor and working conditions on the Project
        resulting from non-compliance with applicable standards. The significance of the effect is the
        outcome of the interaction of the receptor sensitivity and the impact magnitude, and indicates the
        requirement for additional mitigation. The post-mitigation outcome has also been assessed to
        determine the significance of the residual effect.
19.3.5. The descriptions in Table 19-4 summarize the categories used to determine the magnitude of the
        potential impacts on labor and working conditions for the receptors. The magnitude of potential
        impacts is based on the extent to which measures are in place to ensure that labor practices and
        working conditions are aligned with the applicable standards. As the receptors are defined as the
        workforce, and the aim is to align all labor and working conditions with the applicable standards, the
        standard impact assessment criteria for magnitude (as set out in Volume 1, Chapter 5: ESIA
        Methodology, Table 5-1) of geographical extent, duration, and frequency are not applied in this
        assessment.

        Table 19-4 - Impact Magnitude Categories

          Magnitude         Description

          Large             Few, or no, measures are in place to help ensure alignment of labor practices and
                            working conditions with the applicable standards. This is likely to include the absence of
                            employment contracts, wages which are not fair or paid on time, sub-standard welfare
                            and accommodation facilities, and an absence of human resource programs preventing
                            discrimination in hiring practices and during employment. Occupational Health and
                            Safety (OHS) systems are not established or followed, equipment is not maintained or
                            serviced on a regular basis, no training is provided for workers, and the provision and use
                            of PPE is limited.

          Medium            Some measures are in place to help ensure alignment of labor practices and working
                            conditions with the applicable standards, but there is risk of gaps. This could include
                            incomplete employment contracts, wages which are occasionally not paid on time,
                            inconsistent standards are implemented for welfare and accommodation facilities, and
                            human resource practices ensuring non-discrimination are not applied consistently. OHS
                            systems are not consistently followed or monitored, an inconsistent approach is taken to
                            equipment maintenance, some training is provided but it is not consistent or monitored,
                            and PPE is provided but no checks are in place to ensure it is used or replaced when
                            needed.

          Small             A comprehensive system of measures is in place to help ensure alignment with labor
                            practices and working condition standards, non-compliance is likely to be for minor
                            issues only. Human resources practices are well managed, with the majority of
                            employees having suitable contracts in place and their wages paid on time. Welfare



        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                          PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: ESIA-Ch19-Labor-Rev04-eng                                  May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                       Page | 19-5
          Magnitude          Description
                             facilities and accommodation are maintained to a good standard and discrimination
                             during hiring and employment is unlikely. OHS is well managed and monitored,
                             equipment is maintained on a regular service schedule, training is provided regularly on a
                             range of relevant subjects, with attendance records held, and all workers are provided
                             with appropriate PPE which is replaced as needed.

          Very Small         Robust and comprehensive system of measures are in place which ensure alignment
                             with labor practices and working condition standards. The risk of non-compliance is only
                             likely to be for isolated issues.

19.3.6. The receptor sensitivity category in Labor and Working Conditions Sensitivity Criteria and the impact
        magnitude category combine to provide a qualitative assessment of the resulting impact.
19.3.7. The matrix for assessing significance of effects is shown in Volume 1, Chapter 5: Impact
        Assessment Methodology of this ESIA and effects that are classified as moderate or major are
        considered to be significant effects. Effects classified as minor or negligible are considered to be not
        significant.

19.4     POTENTIAL IMPACTS
19.4.1. Impacts on labor and working conditions are experienced by those working on the Project including
        OJSC Rogun HPP, Project Management Group (PMG), Project Management Consultant (PMC),
        EPC Contractors for Lots 1-4, other non-EPC contractors, State Enterprise, Directorate of the Flood
        Zone of Rogun HPP (DFZ) and consultants. Potential impacts can occur at various stages of a
        project or operation. Addressing these potential impacts at each stage is critical to ensure that the
        well-being of workers is maintained according to the labor standards.
19.4.2. Potential impacts assessed in this Chapter include the topics, and the standards that should be met
        for each;
          Labor management: Workers should have their terms and conditions of employment in writing,
           which is clear and understandable, and include details of their hours of work, wages and any
           benefits. Workers shall be paid on a regular basis, and be provided with adequate periods of rest.
           The recruitment process and employment relationship will be based on the principle of equal
           opportunity, and without discrimination. Workers have a right to form and join workers
           organizations, and legally established worker organizations will be respected. A mechanism for
           raising grievances should be in place for all workers to raise concerns.
          Occupational Health and Safety: An OHS programme should be in place which identifies
           potential hazards to project workers, provides preventive and protective measures for workers,
           ensures applicable training is provided regularly, sets out a procedure for reporting accidents and
           incidents, and specifies remedies for adverse impacts. OHS performance should be regularly
           reviewed, with a system in place to enable continual improvements.
          Underage and forced labor: Workers under the minimum legal age will not be employed. No
           trafficked person will be employed on the Project, and involuntary or compulsory labor is
           prohibited.
          Retrenchment: Significant job losses should be planned for, and a process established to
           provide support for securing future employment.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                         PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: ESIA-Ch19-Labor-Rev04-eng                                 May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                      Page | 19-6
         Worker accommodation and facilities: Workers should be provided with facilities appropriate to
          their circumstances, including canteens, sanitary and accommodation facilities. Policies should
          be in place to ensure accommodation is managed in such a way as to protect the health, safety
          and well-being of workers.
         Inclusion, vulnerability and gender: Vulnerable workers should be provided with appropriate
          measures of protection and assistance.

        CONSTRUCTION
        Labor Management
19.4.4. Current practices on site for construction shift workers are to work a pattern of 15 consecutive
        calendar days (not to be exceeded), with working hours not to exceed 12 hours per day or night
        which includes a one hour meal break and one 15-minute break after every two hours of work.
        Workers are not allowed, or required, to work overtime. Based on figures collected in 2024,
        approximately 85% of Project workers are working this type of shift pattern.
19.4.5. Working hours for office/normal workers will not exceed 40 hours per week. For workers working a
        5-day week, the duration of work will not exceed 8 hours per day. Office workers must have a meal
        break and at least two rest breaks during their working hours. These office workers comprise 15%
        of the workforce based on figures collected in 2024.


        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                     PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: ESIA-Ch19-Labor-Rev04-eng                             May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page | 19-7
19.4.6. A Labor Audit Team conducted a 12-day site visit between the 5-16th of March 2023 to audit all
        active contractors and subcontractors on site (Labor Audit Report, 2023). In addition, the Labor
        Management Procedures was published in February 2024. The Labor Audit found that several
        contractors have provided employment contracts with missing information (e.g. wages, working
        hours, and break and rest periods). There was also evidence of shift workers working longer hours
        than the standard 12-hour shift, which the Labor Code prohibits. However, these findings were not
        consistent across all contractors, many of whom were found to be operating in compliance with the
        Labor Code. PMG and OJSC have been working with contractors to ensure there is compliance
        across the board.
19.4.7. Workers are allowed to join labor unions, and there is evidence of organized on-site labor. A worker
        grievance mechanism is also provided for workers to submit complaints in a formal and documented
        process (Labor Management Procedures, 2024).
19.4.8. Evidence of poor working conditions have been observed, although this is inconsistent across the
        Project. Observations included limited provision of drinking water, no soap or disinfectant in toilet
        cubicles for workers to wash their hands, poorly maintained toilet facilities, and inadequate first aid
        and medical facilities for workers. The November 2024 OHS Progress Audit report identified that
        there are currently insufficient number of toilets available to workers on site and that other measures
        are in place to improve sanitary and medical conditions.
19.4.9. The magnitude of this impact is assessed as medium; the effect is, therefore, major adverse.
         Occupational Health and Safety
19.4.10. The Project requires a large construction workforce that, at its peak in the late 2020s is anticipated
         to require up to 20,000 workers (a full breakdown of Project workers currently on site is provided in
         the Labor Management Procedures of this ESIA).
19.4.11. Common activities undertaken during construction can introduce risks to the health and safety of the
         construction workforce, such as the movement of machinery, demolitions and excavations, electrical
         works, handling of chemicals, and works undertaken at height and within confined spaces. Risks
         are more likely to be apparent for those unfamiliar with the type of work undertaken and the
         associated hazards.
19.4.12. The main potential health and safety construction risks associated with the Project relate to:
            Use of explosives for tunnelling;
            Working at height in the tunnels and dam (including steep, potentially unstable slopes);
            Working in confined spaces;
            Hot work, e.g. welding;
            Repetitive working and hard manual labor;
            Exposure to physical hazards from use of heavy equipment and machinery;
            Working with pressurized equipment;
            Trip and fall hazards;
            Exposure to noise and poor air quality (e.g. dusty activities and emissions in tunnels);
            Exposure to electrical hazards from the use of tools and machinery;
            Carrying heavy loads;
            Exposure to chemicals (e.g., paints, fuels, fresh concrete);
            Traffic and transportation (within the Project tunnels as well as risks to pedestrians);
            Working in both hot and cold conditions due to the climate extremes; and


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                    PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: ESIA-Ch19-Labor-Rev04-eng                            May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 19-8
         Risk of road traffic incidents.
19.4.13. All major contractors on site are supposed to have HSE specialists who are tasked with
         implementing safety programmes, however during a recent audit where seven Contractor HSE
         Managers were interviewed, none had received formal OHS training. There are generally poor OHS
         standards on site, with the exception of one contractor’s work areas, and there have been a series
         of accidents resulting in both serious and fatal injuries. An individual international OHS Specialist
         has been appointed to support PMG to identify risks and provide corrective actions. The first audit
         of contractors was held in November 2023, the second in November 2024, and a third in April 2025.
         Over this period there have been some improvements to OHS practices, but there are still significant
         OHS risks present on site. Key findings from the most recent audit include;
         HSE Manager competence: Managers do not have sufficient training to identify safe/unsafe
          practices and conditions, provide training, or to supervise/enforce OHS performance.
         Traffic management and vehicles: Speed limits are not adhered to and unsafe practices,
          including overtaking, are common practice. Safety belts are rarely worn, and the condition of
          vehicles is often poor and not recorded in pre-start inspections (many with broken lights, cracked
          windscreens, insufficient tread depth, absent reversing alarms and no first aid or fire
          extinguishers). Flagmen are not used effectively when works are being conducted in roads, or
          next to roads. There are also many roads without sturdy barriers to prevent a vehicle from driving
          over the edge.
19.4.14. A corrective action plan has been prepared to address the identified OHS issues. Some actions are
         already being implemented, focussing on improving the use of PPE and improving traffic safety.
         The Employers Representative (PMC) have appointed nine international HSE Managers to improve
         standards on site.
19.4.15. In the absence of mitigation, due to the large scale of construction activities and the potential for
         accidents and incidents in the workplace, the magnitude of this impact is assessed as large, and the
         significance of the effect is major adverse.
        Underage and Forced Labor
19.4.16. The Project has a large workforce with many contractors, and recruitment is ongoing. If controls are
         not properly implemented, there is a risk of underage and forced labor.
19.4.17. The Labor Code (Article 208) prohibits the employment of workers under the age of 18 for heavy,
         underground and harmful or hazardous works. Workers’ age is verified during the onboarding
         process by using legally recognized documents to prove their right to work (e.g. passport, birth
         certificates). WSP undertook a Labor Audit in March 2023 and observed working conditions during
         subsequent site visits. No evidence of workers under the age of 18 years was found on site during
         the March 2023 Labor Audit or any subsequent site visit.
19.4.18. Similarly, no evidence of forced labor was found during the March 2023 Labor Audit. Most workers
         are internal migrants from other parts of Tajikistan or local community members, and when a sample
         population was spoken to, confirmed that they were working willingly. The minority of workers from
         abroad are specialists in professional roles whose risk of exposure to forced labor practices is
         minimal.
19.4.19. Compliance with legislation and the current practices to check legal documents means that the risk
         of child or forced labor is small. Thus, the significance of the effect is moderate adverse.


        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: ESIA-Ch19-Labor-Rev04-eng                         May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                              Page | 19-9
         Retrenchment
19.4.20. At its peak later in the 2020s, the construction workforce is expected to include up to 20,000
         workers. This workforce will then be downsized to match demand as construction activities are
         completed in 2032. The operational workforce of approximately 800 personnel will be much smaller
         than the construction phase workforce.
19.4.21. Due to the length of the construction period for the Project, which first started in the 1980s, it has
         provided long-term employment opportunities for the local and regional population. It is unlikely that
         workers will find another local project of a similar size and scale to provide similar long-term job
         security. Workers may need to re-locate or travel abroad to find employment as a result. The types
         of jobs available during the operational phase will include a slightly higher proportion of highly skilled
         technical roles, with a reduced demand for unskilled labor. These job losses are likely to
         disproportionately impact communities local to Rogun, as approximately 23% of workers come from
         within a 50km radius of the Project site. Most workers originate from elsewhere in Tajikistan and
         may need to travel to other locations both within, or outside of, Tajikistan to find new employment.
19.4.22. There are currently no retrenchment plans or policies for the Project, which is currently still in a
         phase of scaling up the size of the workforce. A Retrenchment Plan should be completed and
         implemented to mitigate the impacts of the Project reducing its workforce, and the changing skills
         demand, during the shift to operation.
19.4.23. The magnitude of this impact is assessed as large due to the absence of a plan or policy for future
         retrenchment, and the significance of the effect is major adverse.
         Worker Accommodation and Facilities
19.4.24. Poor accommodation, sanitary, and catering facilities can occur on construction sites and negatively
         impact workers’ health and the surrounding environment. Overcrowding and poor hygiene can arise
         in substandard accommodation facilities. Poor ablution facilities can result in contamination of the
         local environment (e.g., nearby watercourses) or disease outbreaks among workers. Hygienic food
         preparation and dining facilities, with hand-wash areas, are essential to prevent food poisoning.
19.4.25. Workers also require adequate and suitable food, rest breaks, and rest areas with protection from
         weather conditions to work effectively. Tajikistan has a continental climate and experiences
         seasonal temperature extremes. Workers, therefore, need accommodation or rest facilities that are
         suitably vented, insulated, and heated.
19.4.26. The construction workers’ accommodation camps include catering, recreational, and sports facilities.
         Some on-site facilities are available to all workers, including those whose employers do not have
         such facilities. Many worker accommodation camps have medical facilities with basic medicines and
         equipment staffed by nurses and/or doctors. The closest medical center for workers who do not
         have access to on-site facilities is the Rogun Medical Centre. All serious injuries or operations
         would likely be treated in a hospital in Dushanbe.
19.4.27. Worker accommodation facilities were inspected as part of the Labor Audit in 2023, where it was
         found that most international contractors provide exceptional facilities for workers, including clean
         and well-designed apartments, and hygienic canteens and kitchens. However, there were areas of
         non-compliance limited to specific contractors, where some toilets and sanitary facilities were
         unsanitary or out of use. Inequalities were noted between male and female facilities in some worker




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                     PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: ESIA-Ch19-Labor-Rev04-eng                             May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 19-10
        accommodation, including examples where shower facilities are shared by men and women, and an
        example where women’s toilet facilities lacked appropriate sanitary provisions.
19.4.28. It should be noted that most of the current accommodation provision is in the process of being
         demolished and cleared as reservoir levels rise. New accommodation will be provided in
         Construction Areas 2 and/or 5. From the outset, new worker accommodation will be required to
         meet international standards (set out in the IFC/EBRD guidance note “Standards and processes for
         workers' accommodation: processes and standards”).
19.4.29. The magnitude of this impact is assessed as medium due to the inconsistent standard of facilities
         available, and the significance of the effect is, therefore, major adverse.
        Inclusion, Vulnerability and Gender
19.4.30. Female workers can experience particular risks and impacts when there are culturally biased norms
         and expectations in the workplace. As in many countries, employees in the construction sector in
         Tajikistan are predominantly male and the current Project workforce is predominantly male, although
         female workers are present on site. Social attitudes about ‘women’s work’ can affect women’s
         careers. Both overt discrimination and unconscious bias may negatively impact women’s
         opportunities.
19.4.31. Women currently comprise between 5.2% of the workforce, primarily undertaking domestic work
         such as cleaning, preparing food for workers and washing dishes. A small number also work as
         nurses, engineers and in administrative roles. Tajik legislation prohibits women from undertaking
         heavy work, doing hazardous work, or working underground (Article 216), and women who are
         pregnant or who have children under the age of three are prohibited from working overtime, on
         weekends, or from taking business trips.
19.4.32. Certain other categories of workers have vulnerabilities that may lead to negative treatment in the
         workplace, including bullying, harassment, or discrimination in wages or access to opportunities.
         This may include, inter alia:
         Members of national, ethnic and religious minorities,
         Migrant workers,
         Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Transgender, other identities (LGBT+) people, or those assumed to be
          LGBT+,
         People with disabilities, or
         People with union or political affiliations.
19.4.33. Gender-Based Violence, Sexual Exploitation and Abuse and Sexual Harassment (GBV/SEA/SH), as
         well as other forms of bullying, harassment, or discrimination, may occur within the Project
         workforce. The table below sets out types of harassment, including GBVH, that may impact the
         Project workforce.

        Table 19-5 - Types of Harassment

          Type of

          Project workers

                               could occur where social attitudes are conservative, and people who are not
                               performing traditional gender roles are more likely to attract attention.

19.4.34. While there are particular risks faced by vulnerable groups working on the Project, there have been
         no formally reported incidents of violence, bullying, or harassment. There is currently no separate
         grievance procedure to handle sensitive grievances, such as those relating to GBV/SEA/SH. Many
         contractors rely on ‘open door’ policies for workers to raise concerns to managers, alternatively
         workers can raise concerns with their trade union representative however there isn’t a mechanism to
         do this anonymously. Workers can file anonymous complaints to the workers’ GRM.
19.4.35. The magnitude of this impact is assessed as medium, and the significance of the effect is,
         therefore, major adverse.
        OPERATION
        Labor Management
19.4.36. Current operational employees work similar shift patterns as construction workers; some employees
         (including operators or duty officers) operate on a shift pattern (12 hours on / off) to provide
         continuous operation of the Project, while a smaller number of office workers and managers work a
         five day week (8 hours a day). Most of the current operational employees live in on-site
         accommodation.
19.4.37. The existing operational labor management practices (shift patterns and accommodation provision)
         are anticipated to remain for the duration of the operation of the Project.
19.4.38. The magnitude of this impact is assessed as small, and the effect is therefore moderate adverse.
        Occupational Health and Safety
19.4.39. During the operational phase, OHS risks are anticipated to be reduced for workers due to there
         being fewer risky activities required to operate the Project. However, risks remain for workers who
         undertake underground work, those who work with electricity, and those who work near water.
19.4.40. With the implementation of corrective actions, as identified by the OHS Audits, and the appointment
         of HSE Managers, improvements to the health and safety of working practices are likely to improve
         thought his will depend on a commitment to continuous improvement by the Project.
19.4.41. The magnitude of this impact is assessed as medium, and the significance of the effect is,
         therefore, major adverse.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                        PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: ESIA-Ch19-Labor-Rev04-eng                                May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                    Page | 19-12
19.5    MITIGATION AND ENHANCEMENT MEASURES
        CONSTRUCTION
        Labor Management and Occupational Health and Safety
19.5.1. In summary, the LMP provides mitigation measures for all employers on the Project to minimize the
        potential impacts experienced. Measures include the following:
         Providing non-discrimination and equal opportunity, including fair employment practices and
          proper implementation of a Project Code of Conduct (CoC);
         Implementing and operating a worker GRM with multiple communication channels, including
          allowance for anonymous complaints, will be implemented and well communicated, including as
          part of staff induction and refresher trainings. A separate central grievance procedure will be
          established to handle sensitive grievances such as those relating to SEA/SH, which will allow for
          anonymous and confidential submissions;
         Preventing SEA/SH, including mandatory induction and periodic refresher training on SEA/SH
          policies and integrate gender-responsive H&S measures across the Project site;
         Managing labor-related challenges related to retrenchment, gender equality, discrimination, fair
          wages, job security, working conditions, workplace harassment, overtime, unionization, family
          and medical leave, workplace safety and wrongful termination.
         Enabling organized workers, including allowing trade union membership and advertisement of
          union member rights;
         Preventing forced labor, including prohibited non-voluntary working arrangements and loan
          agreements;
         Setting out terms and conditions for employment, including contracting contents and sharing,
          recruiting from local communities, retrenchment requirements, overtime policy, and all workers’
          rights;
         Providing up-to-standard worker accommodation facilities, including essential requirements,
          inspections, drinking water, food nutritional value, training sessions, laundry services, toilet and
          shower facilities, ventilation, sewage and waste disposal systems and fire protection measures;
         Increasing the number of toilets available to workers (GIIP is one toilet per 20 workers);
         Providing regular and safe transportation, including the provision of transport to and from the site
          to accommodation and to and from the home district to Rogun;
         Complying with the age of employment, ensuring no one under the age of 18 works on site;
         Adhering to terms and conditions of workers, including working hours, overtime, rest days,
          payment days, wage deductions and sick leave for shift workers and office workers;
         Complying with the requirements of the worker grievance mechanism, including information on
          maintaining grievance logs, registering sensitive grievances, and role and responsibilities;
         Maintaining contractor management, ensuring labor standards and H&S are included in the
          tendering process and contracts require contractors to report on issues such as the number of




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: ESIA-Ch19-Labor-Rev04-eng                           May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 19-13
           accident rates, near misses, severity rates, number of recurring non-compliances, violations of
           compliance and safety, fatalities and serious injuries and penalties for non-compliance;
         Continuing semi-annual OHS audits by an international OHS specialist, who will focus on each of
          the contractors, and the Employee Representative (ER), practices. Implement all actions
          identified in the OHS Corrective Action Plan. Contractors are required to develop their own OHS
          Plans;
         Ensuring the ER and Contractors’ HSE and OHS managers have the authority to suspend works
          that they deem to be unsafe;
         Providing training on the Workers Code of Conduct.
        Retrenchment
19.5.1. Employees will be informed at least two months before their expected release date if their contract
        will not be renewed or will be terminated early.
19.5.2. A Retrenchment Policy will be prepared for the Project by Rogun JSC, which will inform the
        development of a Retrenchment Plan for contractors when the final months of their engagement
        approach. This approach will meet the requirements of the Policy, and Rogun JSC will implement
        the Plan following approval by the Project Management Consultant.
        Worker Accommodation and Facilities
19.5.3. New accommodation and facilities will be provided as construction camps are re-located. These will
        be provided in accordance with GIIP and the following measures undertaken:
         Conducting frequent inspections of accommodation facilities to identify and rectify any hazards.
         Developing and regularly updating emergency response plans.
         Ensuring that living quarters, bathrooms and kitchens are cleaned.
         Implementing proper waste disposal systems.
         Implementing measures to control access to accommodation facilities to enhance security.
         Ensuring adequate lighting in and around accommodation facilities to enhance security.
         Offering recreational facilities and activities to promote mental wellbeing and reduce stress.
         Providing separate facilities for men and women.
        Inclusion, Vulnerabilities and Gender
19.5.4. All Plans developed for the Project, for example, the Code of Conduct, will be gender-sensitive and
        sensitive to the needs of vulnerable groups. Specific actions will include, but not be limited to:
         Provision of a sensitive worker GRM with appropriate procedures to protect complainants;
         Delivery of training and regular refreshers on the Code of Conduct for all operational workers;
         Exploring opportunities to maximize opportunities for women and vulnerable groups, for example,
          through training opportunities to fill operational jobs or by making reasonable adjustments to the
          working environment to accommodate worker needs; and
         Implementing the recruitment plan with an unbiased, non-discriminatory onboarding process for
          operation-phase jobs;


        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: ESIA-Ch19-Labor-Rev04-eng                          May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                              Page | 19-14
         Implementing policies that promote diversity and inclusion in hiring practices;

         Creating equal opportunities for training and leadership roles for all genders and vulnerable
          groups;

         Providing separate and safe sanitation facilities for men and women;

         Ensuring facilities are accessible to all workers, including those with disabilities;

         Establishing a zero-tolerance policy for harassment and discrimination;

         Regularly monitoring and evaluating the effectiveness of inclusion and gender-focused policies;
          and

         Providing workers with access to support services such as counselling and childcare.

        OPERATION
        Labor Management and Occupational Health and Safety
19.5.5. To mitigate effects regarding labor management and occupational health and safety, the following
        measures will be set out in the Operational Environmental and Social Management Plan (OESMP)
        and associated action plans:
         Development and implementation of a Human Resources Manual and Operational Workers’
          Code of Conduct;
         Training on the Operational Workers Code of Conduct;
         Worker contracts shall be compliant with the Labor Code and meet the standards set out in the
          Labor Management Procedures document;
         An Operational Health and Safety Plan shall be in place to address potential occupational health
          and safety risks presented by the project's operation;
         Ensure all workers can access and use appropriate Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) such
          as helmets, gloves and safety glasses;
         The worker GRM will continue to be implemented, as will the sensitive GRM;
         Office staff should modify workstations and tools to reduce physical strain;
         Maintain a comfortable working environment by controlling the temperature and ventilation;
         Conduct regular safety inspections to identify and mitigate potential risks; and
         Schedule regular health check-ups to monitor the workers' health and identify any problems early.

19.6    RESIDUAL IMPACTS
19.6.1. Residual impacts are impacts that remain after the implementation of mitigation and reflect the
        efficacy of the specific mitigation measure. The mitigation measures are implemented by the
        relevant party, specifically the EPC contractor and the subcontractors for the construction phase and
        the operator and subcontractors for the operational phase, to manage the project's social
        performance. Quarterly monitoring of the mitigation measures provides the data required to report to
        the regulatory authorities, Lenders, and other stakeholders.


        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: ESIA-Ch19-Labor-Rev04-eng                         May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                             Page | 19-15
19.6.2. The periodic review of the mitigation measures must consider the residual impacts of the impact
        assessment to benchmark implementation performance.
         CONSTRUCTION
19.6.3. For the construction phase, the additional mitigation measures are safeguards to ensure that the
        magnitude of potential impacts that the workforce is exposed to on-site is minimized to the greatest
        extent possible, especially so for the proportion of the workforce that is considered vulnerable, such
        as minority ethnicities and women.
19.6.4. For all receptors, the significance of the residual impact is minor.
         OPERATION
19.6.5. In the operational phase, the additional mitigation measures minimize the magnitude of potential
        impacts on the workforce, especially for vulnerable groups.
19.6.6. For all receptors, the magnitude of the residual impact is minor.

19.7     SUMMARY
19.7.1. For the construction phase, the magnitude of the impact on four of the receptors, namely labor
        management, occupational health and safety, retrenchments and inclusion, vulnerabilities and
        gender is medium and the magnitude of the impact is small for two of the receptors, namely
        underage and forced labor and worker accommodation and facilities.
19.7.2. Given that the sensitivity of all receptors is high, the significance of the pre-mitigation effect is major
        for four receptors (labor management, occupational health and safety, retrenchments and inclusion,
        vulnerabilities and gender) and moderate for two receptors (underage and forced labor and worker
        accommodation and facilities).
19.7.3. The successful implementation of the additional mitigation measures outlined will reduce the
        significance of all residual effects in the construction phase to minor adverse effects for all potential
        Project labor and working conditions impacts.
19.7.4. For the operational phase, the magnitude of the impact on the receptors, namely inclusion,
        vulnerabilities and gender, is small and, given that the receptor sensitivity is high, the significance of
        the effect is moderate. With the effective implementation of the mitigation measures, the residual
        significance is minor for all potential operational phase labor and working conditions impacts.

       Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ
20       CULTURAL HERITAGE
20.1.1. This chapter presents the assessment of potential impacts of the Project on cultural heritage
        resources, in line with the requirements of the World Bank’s Environmental and Social Standard
        (ESS) 8: Cultural Heritage (2016).
20.1.2. The full cultural heritage baseline is presented in Volume 2, Annex 7-1 – Baseline Conditions
        Report and a summary is provided in Volume 1, Chapter 7 of this ESIA. This chapter should also
        be read in conjunction with the Cultural Heritage Management Plan (CHMP).
20.1.3. For the purposes of this assessment, cultural heritage’ is defined in accordance with ESS8 to
        include the full suite of cultural resources, both moveable and immoveable. This encompasses
        physical, archaeological resources, including archaeological sites (which may be sub-surface),
        cultural artefacts, historical monuments and buildings. It also incorporates cultural resources (e.g.,
        sacred and religious places) including natural features of local cultural value, related intangible
        practice, ethnographic heritage and cultural landscape.
20.1.4. In this context the following ESS8 definitions apply:
               “…movable or immovable objects, sites, structures, groups of structures, and natural features
                and landscapes that have archaeological, paleontological, historical, architectural, religious,
                aesthetic, or other cultural significance. Tangible cultural heritage may be located in urban or
                rural settings, and may be above or below land or under the water”; and
               “…practices, representations, expressions, knowledge, skills – as well as the instruments,
                objects, artifacts and cultural spaces associated therewith, that communities and groups
                recognize as part of their cultural heritage, as transmitted from generation to generation and
                constantly recreated by them in response to their environment, their interaction with nature
                and their history”.
                                                                                            (World Bank, 2018).
         SUPPORTING INFORMATION
20.1.5. This chapter is supported by the following associated documents:
               Volume 2, Annex 20.1 – Gazetteer of Archaeological, Historical, Paleontological, Cultural
                and Sacred Sites
               Volume 2, Annex 20.2 – Gazetteer of Villages
               Volume 2, Annex 20.3 – Village Ethnography Data
               Volume 2, Annex 20.4 – M-Vector Household Data
               Volume 2, Annex 20.5 – Cultural Heritage Drawings
               Volume 2, Annex 20.6 – Cultural Heritage Field Reports (June & August-September 2023)
               Volume 3, A6 – Cultural Heritage Management Plan

20.2     LEGISLATIVE FRAMEWORK, POLICY AND GUIDANCE
20.2.1. The following legislation, policy and guidance have been considered as part of the assessment:
               The UNESCO World Heritage Convention (1972; ratified by Tajikistan in 1992);
               Asian Development Bank Environmental Assessment Guidelines (2003);




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                  May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 20-1
               The Law of the Republic of Tajikistan on the Protection and Use of Objects of Historical and
                Cultural Heritage (2017);
               The Law of the Republic of Tajikistan About Culture (1997);
               World Bank ESS8: Cultural Heritage (2016) and associated Guidance Note (2018); and
               European Investment Bank Environmental and Social Standards (2022).
20.2.2. National Law in Tajikistan (the Law on the Protection and Use of Historical Cultural Heritage, 2017)
        governs the protection, use and promotion of cultural heritage. Intangible cultural heritage is also
        protected in the Law About Culture (1997), which covers non-material aspects. Cultural heritage is
        identified as the exclusive property of the State.
20.2.3. The 2017 law states (Chapter 3, article 23) that all related research (excavations and the survey of
        archaeological monuments) must be carried out only with the permission of the authorized state
        body, the Academy of Sciences of the Republic of Tajikistan (the A. Donish Institute).
         INTERNATIONAL LENDER REQUIREMENTS
20.2.4. Cultural heritage is defined in accordance with ESS8 and the other policy and guidance, to include
        both immoveable and moveable and ethnographic elements. ESS8 requires the proponent to
        identify and reduce or avoid adverse impacts upon cultural heritage resources. The ESS provides
        guidance which specifies the participation of Project Affected Persons (PAPs) and other interested
        parties in the identification of, and potential mitigation of, cultural heritage resources, recommending
        appropriate strategies for impact reduction and long-term cultural heritage management (e.g.,
        through development and implementation of a Cultural Heritage Management Plan (CHMP) and a
        Chance Find Procedure (CFP)).
20.2.5. Standard 10 of the European Investment Bank’s (EIB) Environmental and Social Standards (2022)
        concerns cultural heritage. This Standard aligns with ESS8 and requires a “precautionary approach
        to the management and sustainable use of cultural heritage”. With regard to mitigation measures,
        EIB Standard 10 states that, when minimization of adverse impacts is not possible, the restoration in
        situ of the cultural heritage receptor(s) after the adverse impacts have occurred should take place to
        “ensure full restoration of the functionality and significance of the cultural heritage for the affected
        communities”. Where minimization and restoration are not feasible, remediation / compensation for
        the loss of cultural heritage must be agreed with the PAPs before any potentially impactful works
        begin. Standard 10 also states that the views of PAPs on cultural heritage of importance should be
        incorporated into the Project design.
20.2.6. The 1972 United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO) World
        Heritage Convention was ratified by Tajikistan in 1992. Each State Party to the Convention
        recognizes that it has the primary obligation to ensure the identification, protection, conservation,
        presentation and transmission to future generations of the cultural and natural heritage … situated
        within its territory (Article 4). For the purposes of this Convention, "cultural heritage" means:
               Monuments: monumental architectural, sculptural or painting works, elements or structures of
                an archaeological character, inscriptions, caves and groups of elements, which are of
                outstanding universal value from the point of view of history, art or science;
               Ensembles: groups of isolated or united buildings which, because of their architecture, their
                unity, or their integration into the landscape, have outstanding universal value from the point
                of view of history, art or science; and




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                  May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 20-2
              Sites: works of man or combined works of man and nature, as well as areas including
               archaeological sites that have outstanding universal value from the historical, aesthetic,
               ethnological or anthropological point of view.

20.3    ASSESSMENT METHODOLOGY
        AREA OF INFLUENCE
20.3.1. The Area of Influence (AoI) for cultural heritage includes the footprint of the Project and associated
        components, incorporating:
              The Rogun HPP dam and associated construction areas;
              The reservoir area at full supply level (FSL);
              Access roads, ancillary facilities and other core infrastructure;
              Left bank roads and bridges; and
              All villages and settlements undergoing resettlement (under RAP 2 and future RAPs).
20.3.2. A 1 km buffer incorporating the key Project components listed above has been used to draw an AoI
        for cultural heritage to adequately consider both direct and indirect impacts during impact
        assessment (as described in Table 20-5). The AoI is shown on Drawing 20-3 (Annex 20.5). Areas
        of direct and indirect influence are described further in Section 20.4.
20.3.3. This 1 km buffer is refined from the 20 km baseline study area (Drawings 20-1 and 20-2), described
        in Volume 2, Annex 7-1, which has allowed for a detailed appreciation of cultural heritage
        resources found within the Project area, to understand their rarity, historical association and context.
20.3.4. The Project consists of three main components:
              The existing hydropower plant, consisting of 2 x 400MW turbines in operation, the associated
               dam, whose crest is 1,110 meters above sea level (m asl), and the current reservoir, with a
               surface area of approximately 12 km2, plus associated roads and other facilities;
              The completed dam with a crest at 1,300m asl, hydropower plant with 6 x 630MW turbines,
               and reservoir that will cover approximately 170 km2, and progressively raised to 1,290 m asl
               by approximately 2038; and
              The ongoing Resettlement Program affecting 69 villages and phased according to the
               reservoir levels and villages’ relative temporal risk of inundation. Resettlement began in 2014
               and is expected to be completed by 2032.
20.3.5. The current, partially completed, dam is considered to represent the existing baseline, therefore the
        assessment of impacts that have already occurred (prior to 2023) are not considered within this
        chapter. This chapter considers Project impacts up until approximately 2038 when the full supply
        level (1,290 m asl) of the reservoir is due to be reached.
        CULTURAL HERITAGE RECEPTORS
20.3.6. To enable an analysis of potential Project-related impacts on cultural heritage, the full suite of
        resources, comprising archaeological, historical and paleontological resources, natural features that
        embody cultural values and intangible forms of culture, as defined by ESS8, were considered as
        receptors for the assessment.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
        Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                   May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page | 20-3
20.3.7. The archaeological, historical and paleontological baseline resource is selected as a receptor type
        representing the ancient history of the region. Archaeological, historical and paleontological sites
        and artefacts, one of which is of potential national importance, have been identified within the AoI
        during the baseline study.
20.3.8. There is evidence that the area has been utilized since at least the Stone Age period (c. 1,000,000
        Before Common Era (BCE) to c. 2,000 BCE). Additionally, a number of settlements within the wider
        baseline 20 km study area (described in Volume 2, Annex 7-1) are over 300 – 500 years old.
20.3.9. Sacred and cultural resources identified during the baseline ethnographic study include natural
        features embodying spiritual values, including springs and trees, as well as mosques, cemeteries
        and memorial sites / shrines (mazars). Sacred and cultural resources are selected as a receptor
        type, representing the tangible manifestation of local cultural values and identity.
20.3.10. In accordance with ESS8, cultural landscape is also a recognized component of this receptor type,
         representing important watercourses / springs, viewpoints, viewsheds and / or routeways through
         the landscape. In this sense cultural landscape is integral to a holistic understanding of the local
         ‘sense of place’ associated with the current visual setting of this part of the Vakhsh River valley and
         the historical and cultural receptors within.
20.3.11. Intangible cultural heritage practice represents local cultural activities and behaviors, with a number
         of unique practices recognized in the more isolated and historically remote village communities.
         Intangible activities recorded include wool carpet weaving, carpentry painted with regional motifs,
         needlework and embroidery, musicians making their own traditional instruments and playing music
         and well as specific local customs, taboo and ritual, as described in Volume 2, Annex 7-1.
20.3.12. These three receptor types, applicable to the cultural heritage impact assessment, are summarized
         in Table 20-1.

         RECEPTOR SENSITIVITY
20.3.13. To identify the sensitivity of potential cultural heritage receptors, a scale of relative sensitivity
         presented in Table 20-2 has been used, with reference to the information collated in the baseline
         and as further described in Annex 7-1.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                           PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                           May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                        Page | 20-5
Table 20-2 – Criteria for determining Sensitivity of Receptors – Cultural Heritage

 Receptor      Example Receptors
 Sensitivity

 Very high     •   Archaeological, historical and paleontological sites considered to be of international
                   importance with the greatest potential for further, significant associated discoveries to be
                   made. This also includes exceptionally rare and previously unstudied features with a high
                   potential for further research.
               •   Cultural sites which have been frequented by the local community for longstanding
                   cultural purposes and those which attract visitors from further afield. UNESCO World
                   Heritage assets.
               •   Intangible cultural heritage practice considered to be of international importance,
                   including those examples included on the UNESCO Lists of Intangible Cultural Heritage
                   which are particularly vulnerable to societal change, including induced demographic
                   change and economic development. Certain behaviors and knowledge may be entirely
                   lost from one generation to another.

 High          •   Archaeological, historical and paleontological resources considered to be of national
                   importance with the greatest potential for further, significant discoveries to be made; or
                   cultural / sacred resources considered to be of the highest value to the community.
               •   Cultural sites which have been frequented by the local community for longstanding
                   cultural purposes. Sites associated with oral history, and which may be representative of
                   a number which no longer exist. Sites which are non-moveable (e.g., associated with
                   natural landscape features).
               •   Intangible cultural heritage practice considered of national or regional importance, which
                   maybe be closely linked to both individual and regional identity and which is particularly
                   vulnerable to societal change, including induced demographic change and economic
                   development. Certain behaviors and knowledge may be entirely lost from one generation
                   to another.

 Medium        •   Archaeological, historical and paleontological sites considered to be of regional
                   importance with limited potential for further discoveries.
               •   Cultural sites which may be no longer in use but are known to the community and
                   associated with settlement history/oral history. Cultural sites which are common and
                   potentially moveable.

 Low           •   Archaeological, historical and paleontological features considered to be of local
                   importance including features which are very common and well understood, or those
                   which are very poorly preserved and / or badly disturbed, offering limited potential for
                   further research.
               •   Cultural resources which are very common and potentially moveable - in the sense that
                   new buildings (e.g., churches or mosques), without historical value, can be established or
                   cultural sites which are not in current use, with no enduring local importance or historical
                   value.
