        ASSESSMENT OF IMPACT SIGNIFICANCE
20.3.15. The assessment considers the impact of the Project with the application of embedded mitigation (i.e.
         that which is an inherent component of the Project design). This gives an indication of the need for
         additional mitigation to be implemented. The likely effectiveness of that additional mitigation has
         then been considered, and a residual effect stated.
20.3.16. An evaluation of impacts on cultural heritage receptors takes into account the following
         characteristics:



        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
        Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                      May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                   Page | 20-9
               The sensitivity of the impacted receptor;
               The type of impact (positive or negative);
               The duration, and extent of the impact;
               The timing and frequency of the impacts;
               The ability of the affected receptor to recover from temporary impacts (reversibility); and
               The likelihood of the identified impact.
20.3.17. The definitions in Table 20-4 summarize the criteria used for assessing impact magnitude, or
         intensity, in relation to cultural heritage.

         Table 20-4 – Criteria for assessing Magnitude of Impact – Cultural Heritage

          Magnitude     Description of Criteria
          of Impact

          Large         •   Archaeological, historical and paleontological receptors or their settings are altered, and
                            key elements are changed such that the resource value is entirely and permanently
                            altered or lost.
                        •   Intangible cultural heritage receptors, or component parts thereof, are altered; removed;
                            damaged and/or their functionality/setting/accessibility are entirely and permanently
                            changed or lost; their value or traditional beliefs, practices and/or behaviors cannot
                            continue and are lost, or severely inhibited.

          Medium        •   Archaeological, historical and paleontological receptors or their settings are partly altered,
                            and key elements are changed such that the resource value is modified and/or
                            information is permanently lost.
                        •   Intangible cultural heritage receptors, or component parts thereof, are altered such that
                            their value and/or functionality/setting/accessibility are permanently changed. Such
                            actions result in modification of receptor use and/or modification of traditional beliefs,
                            practices or behaviors.

          Small         •   Archaeological, historical and paleontological receptors or their settings are slightly
                            altered but their integrity is maintained, or archaeological receptors are altered but no
                            information is lost (through archaeological excavation and recording).
                        •   Intangible cultural heritage receptors, or component parts thereof, are altered such that
                            their value and/or functionality/setting/accessibility are slightly changed. Such actions do
                            not result in modification of receptor use or traditional beliefs, practices or behaviors.

          Very Small    •   Archaeological, historical and paleontological receptors or their settings are not altered,
                            or changes are so minor that their integrity is maintained, and no information is lost.
                        •   Intangible cultural heritage receptors, or component parts thereof, are not altered or
                            changes are so minor that that their value and/or functionality/setting/accessibility remain
                            unchanged, and no modification of receptor use is required. Traditional beliefs, practices
                            or behaviors are not modified.

20.3.18. The potential impacts on cultural heritage receptors as a result of the Project have been estimated
         qualitatively, according to a methodology that considers the sensitivity of the receptor, the frequency
         of occurrence and the magnitude of the impacts being considered, with reference to Table 20-2 and
         Table 20-4, respectively. Where potential impacts have been identified, these are considered in turn
         and mitigation is set out where necessary to ensure that any potential impacts are reduced as far as
         practicable.



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                            PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                            May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                        Page | 20-10
20.3.19. The matrix for assessing significance of effects is shown in Volume 1, Chapter 5 of this ESIA, and
         impacts that are classified as moderate or major are considered to be significant effects. Impacts
         classified as minor or below are considered to be not significant.

20.4     POTENTIAL IMPACTS AND EFFECTS
20.4.1. Cultural heritage sites have the potential to be impacted by Project-related interactions that cause
        alteration, such as physical disturbance or excavations affecting resource fabric, context or integrity.
        Project activities and ground-related works with the potential to directly and physical interact with
        cultural heritage resources would occur during the construction (including pre-
        construction/preparation) phase. This includes the submergence of resources within the inundation
        zone.
20.4.2. Direct impacts will therefore occur within, or directly adjacent to, the proposed Project footprint,
        including all supporting infrastructure (new-build settlements in support of the RAP, left bank roads
        and bridges and transmission lines).
20.4.3. Secondary, or indirect impacts may also affect cultural heritage resources. For example, Project
        activities may give rise to variations in the local environmental setting (e.g., due to noise, dust,
        vibration or visual impacts). These impacts may occur at both construction and operation phases of
        the Project. The resettlement of PAPs during construction may restrict local access to cultural
        resources and indirectly limit the continuation of normal cultural activities and intangible cultural
        heritage, particularly where these activities are tied to natural landscape features. Indirect impacts
        are therefore likely to occur to tangible receptors in close proximity to the development (depending
        on the nature of the receptor or the combined Project activities in the vicinity), while indirect impacts
        on intangible cultural heritage may occur throughout the AoI.
20.4.4. Any secondary land slippage which may occur, resulting from the progressive flooding / seasonal
        variations in the reservoir water level, may damage resources in the near vicinity. This impact is
        likely to affect resources on the upper terraces (above the inundation zone, over 1,290 m asl) near
        to the reservoir perimeter. These impacts, if they were to occur, could damage resources located
        close to the reservoir edge. Annex 13-2 – Reservoir Slope Stability indicates that these impacts
        will be restricted to a zone about 1 km from the full reservoir edge, with closer areas generally more
        at risk. The risk of landslides occurring is discussed in detail in Annex 13-2. In the context of the
        Project, potential physical impacts to cultural heritage resources on the upper terraces of the
        reservoir as a result of landslip, are considered to be indirect, as they will occur secondary to the
        (direct) impacts upon any resources within the confines of the inundation zone.
20.4.5. Direct impacts are permanent and irreversible. Indirect impacts can be both temporary (e.g., where
        dust during construction is predicted), or permanent. Permanent indirect impacts include landslip
        induced damage and loss of access to traditional local cultural resources through the resettlement of
        PAPs.
20.4.6. These types of potential Project impacts considered for the cultural heritage assessment are
        summarized in Table 20-5.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                    PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                    May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page | 20-11
        Table 20-5 – Types of Cultural Heritage Impact

         Type                 Description

         Direct Impact        Impacts that result from a direct interaction between a planned Project activity and the
                              receiving environment/resource (i.e., destruction or damage of an archaeological
                              feature or locally cultural site through ground disturbance). Direct impacts may also
                              occur cumulatively, because of gradual degradation from overlapping Project activities
                              These ‘direct impacts’ are likely to occur to resources within or adjacent to the
                              proposed Project footprint. They include the progressive inundation of the reservoir
                              (submergence), the development of all ground intrusive supporting infrastructure and
                              the establishment of new-build settlements in support of the RAP.

         Indirect Impact      Indirect impacts will not affect the land surface directly but may alter the environmental
                              setting in which a cultural heritage resource is experienced or accessed (e.g., by
                              related dust and vibration disturbance, or through the resettlement of PAPs). These
                              impacts may occur in combination, and cumulatively, from overlapping Project activities
                              in proximity to resources.
                              Indirect impacts also include possible secondary land slippage, resulting from the
                              progressive flooding and seasonal variations in the reservoir water level, which may
                              damage resources in the reservoir perimeter.
                              Intangible cultural heritage may be impacted indirectly, resulting from Project induced
                              demographic changes and population influx, for example. Impacts to intangible
                              heritage can also occur in combination, due to both induced change and a loss of
                              access to cultural resources through resettlement.

20.4.7. The impact activities assessed in relation to the cultural heritage component include:
        Construction Impacts:
               The introduction of new buildings and infrastructure has the potential to result in direct
                interactions with cultural heritage resources. Sub-surface archaeological resources may be
                physically damaged or destroyed during vegetation clearance or invasive groundworks.
                Discrete cultural sites may also be partially or wholly destroyed during the development of
                new infrastructure. This includes the construction of new-built settlements in support of the
                RAP, the left bank roads and bridges and transmission lines. Burial sites may also experience
                direct physical damage through ground invasive activity, or compaction through the presence
                of heavy construction traffic required to support the development of Project facilities.
               Increases in dust and/or noise levels and the emission of vibration may indirectly change the
                environmental setting in which a cultural resource (e.g., memorial site or cemetery) is
                experienced. The normal sense of place (i.e., tranquil nature) will be interrupted. Similarly, the
                normal cultural activities of PAPs may also be interrupted if exceedances in safe levels
                prevent routine access to cultural sites, preventing the continuation of usual cultural
                behaviors. Vibration emissions may also result in structural damage to archaeological,
                historical and paleontological resources and cultural resources, such as cemetery sites.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                           PUBLIC |
        Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                           May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                       Page | 20-12
        Resettlement Impacts:
              Resettlement activities are related to the relocation of PAPs. This may result in the indirect
               prevention of routine access to cultural resources, including burial grounds, sacred places
               (e.g., shrines) or other places of local cultural and ethnographic value, preventing the
               continuation of usual cultural behaviors and intangible cultural heritage practice. This does not
               include direct ground disturbance activities associated with (physical) resettlement (which are
               assessed as impacts derived from construction activities).
        Inundation Impacts
              Inundation of the reservoir will occur gradually, in phases. Flooding will result in a direct loss
               of access to (and possible degradation of) archaeological, historical, paleontological and
               cultural and sacred resources (including ancient and modern cemeteries) within the reservoir
               area as they are submerged. If it were to occur, landslide movement triggered by the filling of
               the reservoir could indirectly affect archaeological, historical and paleontological resources.
               The presence of the reservoir at FSL will also result in an indirect change to the local sense of
               place and associated viewshed for those who remain in the area.
20.4.8. The impact assessment has been carried out in accordance with the methodology described in
        Volume 1, Chapter 5 of this ESIA, to assess the potential consequence of proposed activities upon
        the baseline cultural heritage environment. A matrix of anticipated interactions between the cultural
        heritage receptor types (Section 20.3) and the relevant impact arising as a result of the proposed
        Project has been developed (Table 20-6). The objective of this matrix is to demonstrate where
        potential consequences may occur, and hence where potential interactions must be explicitly
        considered as part of the impact assessment process.

        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                                           PUBLIC |

        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                                       Page | 20-13
         CONSTRUCTION PHASE IMPACTS
20.4.9. The construction phase of the Project is ongoing (the dam will be completed in approximately 2032).
        During this phase it is anticipated that all groundworks have the potential to disturb both known and
        unknown resources throughout the Project footprint.
         Archaeological, Historical and Paleontological Resources
20.4.10. The introduction of heavy machinery (e.g., bulldozers, excavators, dump trucks, vibrating roller,
         crane and other equipment and machines) could result in the direct destruction of archaeological,
         historical and paleontological sites through a change in the existing land surface and the direct
         destruction of the site’s environmental context, and therefore, its material value. Construction activity
         may also result in secondary ground compaction or vibration (e.g., as heavy equipment is
         transported). As a result of these ground disturbance activities, archaeological, historical and
         paleontological resources in the near vicinity may also experience indirect impacts through vibration
         impacts associated with construction activity (e.g., road haulage of construction material).
20.4.11. The reservoir will progressively rise during the construction phase and archaeological, historical and
         paleontological resources within the maximum inundation level will be submerged (and degraded). If
         it were to occur, landslide movement triggered by the filling of the reservoir could affect
         archaeological and paleontological resources on the upper terraces during the construction phase.
20.4.12. Direct interactions with the archaeological, historical and paleontological resources baseline
         component are therefore anticipated to result from the following construction phase activities:
               Land preparation and construction of project infrastructure (including left bank roads and
                bridges and transmission lines);
               Development of new-build resettlement locations; and
               Progressive reservoir inundation (i.e., flooding).
20.4.13. Indirect interactions with the archaeological, historical and paleontological resources component are
         anticipated to result from the following construction phase activities:
               Any groundworks (land preparation and construction) that may result in a change in local
                environmental setting, including secondary (e.g., vibration) impacts; and
               Progressive reservoir inundation (resulting in erosion and landslip).
         Direct Impacts
20.4.14. A total of 46 archaeological, historical and paleontological resources were recorded during the
         baseline cultural heritage surveys. Direct construction phase impacts may occur to 15 known
         receptors within the AoI. Of these, 13 are located within the inundation zone. These are listed in
         Table 20-7 and illustrated on Drawing 20-4 (Volume 2, Annex 20.5). These include the locations of
         fortresses, abandoned and historical settlements and structures which are considered low to
         medium in terms of cultural heritage value and sensitivity. A set of dinosaur footprints (RHPP-AR-
         36) of high sensitivity is also located within the AoI and included in this table due to their proximity to
         the dam and existing construction infrastructure (they are immediately adjacent to an existing
         construction access road). The Stone Age lithic scatters recorded within the AoI (RHPP-AR-43 –
         RHPP-AR-45) have not been included in the impact assessment, as these were removed from the
         site and retained by the A. Donish Institute when they were identified.

20.4.15. Archaeological, historical and paleontological resources are non-renewable, and on a worst-case
         basis, without the implementation of archaeological mitigation measures, there is potential for
         sensitive materials, as listed in Table 20-7, to be re-deposited, damaged or destroyed as a result of
         the direct (ground disturbance-related) construction phase activities.
20.4.16. Throughout the AoI there is also a potential for archaeological and paleontological resources,
         including monumental assets, surface artefacts or sub-surface sites, to occur that have not yet been
         located during previous survey phases. These ‘chance finds’ may be accidentally disturbed during
         preparatory ground works and construction-related activities. Chance finds are of unknown
         sensitivity and are therefore classified on a conservative basis. The left bank roads and bridges,
         transmission lines, and any other proposed supporting infrastructure sites not yet subject to targeted
         archaeological reconnaissance and receptor mapping, are highlighted in this regard.
20.4.17. The direct disturbance and loss of these resources would result in an impact, prior to the
         implementation of appropriate mitigation, that is permanent, irreversible and with the potential to
         occur locally within multiple discrete locations throughout the proposed Project footprint. These
         impacts would be large in magnitude, upon individual resources that are (on a worst-case basis)
         highly sensitive, resulting in a major impact significance.
20.4.18. Due to their proximity to the dam and existing construction infrastructure, the set of dinosaur
         footprints (RHPP-AR-36) are at risk of being further directly (and indirectly) impacted through during
         the construction phase without the implementation of urgent mitigation measures. Although their
         typology is yet to be determined, the footprints likely date to the Cretaceous period and are
         considered to be of potential national importance (high sensitivity).
         Indirect Impacts
20.4.19. Indirect construction phase impacts may occur to 12 known receptors within the AoI. These are
         listed in Table 20-8 and illustrated on Drawing 20-4 (Volume 2, Annex 20.5). These include the
         locations of fortresses, an abandoned structure and agricultural earthwork remains which are
         considered low to high in terms of cultural heritage value and sensitivity and one paleontological
         resource of high sensitivity.

20.4.20. On a worst-case basis, without the implementation of specific mitigation measures, there is potential
         for archaeologically sensitive materials, of potential national importance, to be re-deposited,
         damaged or destroyed following secondary landslips that may occur in the vicinity of the reservoir
         perimeter, as its water level changes throughout the construction phase. Eleven resources within the
         vicinity of the FSL (shown on Drawing 20-4, Volume 2, Annex 20.5) are highlighted in this regard
         (situated between 30 m and 730 m from the perimeter), and their historically advantageous position
         on the uppermost terraces of the valley is particularly vulnerable to under-cutting and eventual
         slippage (as described in Annex 13-2 – Reservoir Slope Stability).
20.4.21. The extent of this impact is difficult to quantify without sub-surface analysis. If necessary, the
         sensitivity levels applied to these resources will be refined following their archaeological evaluation
         (as described in paragraph 20.4.27). On a conservative basis, this impact is permanent, irreversible
         and with the potential to occur locally within multiple discrete locations throughout the proposed
         Project footprint. These impacts would be large in magnitude, upon individual resources that are
         (accounting for the highest sensitivity receptor and the likelihood for ‘chance finds’) highly sensitive.
         This would result in a major impact significance.
20.4.22. Potential indirect impacts resulting from changes to the environmental setting (noise, dust etc.) of
         the 11 archaeological and historical resources (Table 20-8) are considered to be negligible, with the
         closest receptor over 10 km from the dam construction zone. However, due to their proximity to the
         dam and associated construction infrastructure, the set of dinosaur footprints (RHPP-AR-36) are at
         risk of being indirectly impacted through vibration induced damage from HGV during the
         construction phase, without the implementation of urgent mitigation measures (and presuming the
         avoidance of direct impacts).
20.4.23. This indirect impact would result in irreversible damage to the fabric of the paleontological resource,
         resulting in an impact, prior to the implementation of appropriate mitigation, that is permanent and
         site-specific. This impact would be large in magnitude, upon an individual resource that is highly
         sensitive, resulting in a major impact significance.
         Mitigation: Archaeological, Historical and Paleontological Resources (Construction Phase)
         Impact Avoidance and Minimization – Cultural Heritage Management Plan (CHMP) (AM-20-01)
20.4.24. To reduce potential significant effects, and to ensure good practice construction methods relating to
         protection of cultural heritage, in line with the Mitigation Hierarchy, the following measures (AM-20-
         01 to AM-20-08) shall be implemented, through adherence to the overarching Project CHMP
         (Volume 3, A6).

20.4.25. The CHMP is a “live” document and PMG are responsible for its annual review and any subsequent
         update, during the lifetime of the Project (as outlined in Section 15 of the CHMP and the
         Environmental and Social Management Plan (ESMP; Volume 3, A1).
20.4.26. The preparation and implementation of an on-site cultural heritage awareness training program will
         be made available (during site induction) for all ‘on site’ employees and sub-contractors, to provide
         awareness of the mitigation and management measures applicable to cultural heritage (as outlined
         in Section 6 of the CHMP).




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                     PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                     May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 20-18
         Impact Avoidance - Avoidance Plans (AM-20-02)
20.4.27. Archaeological, historical and paleontological resources are non-renewable, and the primary goal of
         cultural resource management should be their physical preservation in-situ, in accordance with
         World Bank guidance (ESS8, 2018) (i.e., to avoid direct impacts regardless of the protected status
         of the potential receptor). The preferred mitigation for all sites potentially directly impacted by Project
         activities is, therefore, avoidance, particularly in advance of site-specific mitigation measures (see
         AM-20-02). It is recognized that the archaeologically sensitive locations identified within the
         proposed flooding zone cannot be avoided. Impacts on these resources will therefore be effectively
         minimized as discussed below (see AM-20-02).
20.4.28. To facilitate the avoidance of sensitive, potentially non-replicable resources within the remainder of
         the proposed footprint and supporting infrastructure (construction access roads etc.), all known
         resources located outside the inundation zone will be mapped and presented to the Employer’s
         Representative / future PMC and DFZ as areas for avoidance and physical demarcation (with
         barriers) during all ground preparation and construction works (Avoidance Plans).
20.4.29. The thirteen identified archaeological, historical and paleontological resources which can be avoided
         (i.e., those situated outside the inundation zone) will be buffered by an appropriate and practical
         “exclusion zone” (to account for the potential extent of sub-surface resources). The avoidance of
         construction activities in proximity will also help to manage indirect impacts, particularly related to
         vibration, noise and dust. Further detail is provided in Section 8 of the CHMP. The Avoidance Plans
         will be developed through the CHMP implementation process and include the receptors listed below:
               Kalai Bacha fortress site (RHPP-AR-05);
               Kalai Dukhtarak A fortress site (RHPP-AR-06);
               Kalai Dukhtarak B fortress site (RHPP-AR-07);
               Kalai Shohindar fortress site (RHPP-AR-10);
               Potential remains of structures on a plain near Roghuni poyon / left bank roads and bridges
                (RHPP-AR-31);
               Potential remains of multi-room structure near Darband City (RHPP-AR-32);
               Potential earthwork remains of field systems (RHPP-AR-33, RHPP-AR-34, RHPP-AR-35);
               Fossilized dinosaur footprints near Rogun (RHPP-AR-36);
               Gumushkala fortress site (RHPP-AR-41);
               Yapoloki fortress site (RHPP-AR-42);
               Kalai Chungi Zubaid fortress site (RHPP-AR-46); and
               all resources (yet to be identified) within supporting infrastructure locations (e.g., left bank
                roads and bridges).
         Impact Avoidance and Minimization: Ongoing baseline data capture (AM-20-03)
20.4.30. Baseline data collection for archaeological, historical and paleontological resources and cultural and
         sacred resources throughout the AoI is ongoing, as described in Section 7 of the CHMP.
20.4.31. Additionally, preconstruction surveys will be required for elements of the Project whose locations
         have not yet been finalized, such as the left bank roads and bridges and transmission lines. Any
         mitigation measures subsequently deemed necessary must be undertaken prior to the construction
         of these elements of the Project and will form part of the Pre-construction Survey Process (Volume
         3, Chapter 1) and the Management of Change Procedure (Volume 3, Chapter 2), as per the ESMP
         and in conformance with the CHMP.


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                      May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page | 20-19
         Impact Minimization – Evaluation (AM-20-04)
20.4.32. In the event that the avoidance of archaeological, historical and paleontological resources is not
         possible, as in the case of those identified within the proposed flooding zone and near perimeter
         landslip ‘risk zone’, then a phase of archaeological evaluation is required to fully investigate the
         subsurface potential of the locations of heightened archaeological potential and sensitivity. This
         approach is in conformance with World Bank guidance (ESS8, 2018), to minimize and reduce
         impacts on resources where avoidance is not possible.
20.4.33. This evaluation stage must occur urgently, before any ongoing preparatory works or construction
         activities commencing and before the inundation of the receptors. This program will be phased in
         response to the inundation schedule and will determine the sub-surface extent and research value
         of those receptors thus far identified by purely visual means. If necessary, the sensitivity levels
         applied to these resources will be refined following their archaeological evaluation.
20.4.34. Archaeological evaluation typically comprises a scheme of preliminary, shallow and targeted hand-
         dug test pits (e.g., 1m x 1m in size) at sites previously identified through desktop study or non-
         intrusive walkover only. This scheme of sub-surface analysis will seek to eliminate the risk of
         archaeologically induced delays during the construction phase and the loss of sensitive, non-
         renewable resources as they are characterized in better detail. The schedule of archaeological
         surveys and evaluation must be included in the Overall Implementation Program (OIP) prepared by
         the Employers Representative, in consultation with DFZ.
20.4.35. Once the evaluation stage is complete, further measures to minimize impacts to archaeological
         resources will be determined. For example, if the targeted sites yield archaeological material, it may
         be necessary to implement a program of “preservation by record” through systematic recording
         (e.g., archaeological excavation). Such work, where required, will be described in appropriate
         detailed work programs and specifications (known as Written Schemes of Investigation) to be
         prepared by a Cultural Heritage Management Team (CHMT), retained by PMG, comprised of the A
         Donish Institute and external experts familiar with Lender standards and GIIP, as described in
         Section A6.10 of the CHMP.
20.4.36. The scope and suitability of additional work that will be required to further investigate identified sites
         and/or additional areas will be developed as information from any preconstruction evaluation is
         assessed and disseminated. The effectiveness of this next stage will be supported by the continued
         engagement of competent cultural heritage experts including those at the A. Donish Institute
         currently engaged on the Project, supported by experts familiar with international good practice and
         Lender requirements, and adherence to best practice techniques.
20.4.37. A methodology for the archaeological evaluation phase is incorporated into the CHMP and includes
         the receptors listed below:
               RHPP-AR-01 (fortress site);
               RHPP-AR-02 (fortress site);
               RHPP-AR-03 (fortress site);
               RHPP-AR-04 (mound);
               RHPP-AR-11 (watchtower site);
               RHPP-AR-12 (building);
               RHPP-AR-14 (historical settlement);
               RHPP-AR-24 (abandoned settlement);


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                     PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                     May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 20-20
               RHPP-AR-28 (structural remains);
               RHPP-AR-37 (structural remains);
               RHPP-AR-38 (fortress site);
               RHPP-AR-39 (fortress site);
               RHPP-AR-40 (fortress site);
               RHPP-AR-05 (fortress site);
               RHPP-AR-06 (fortress site);
               RHPP-AR-07 (fortress site);
               RHPP-AR-10 (fortress site);
               RHPP-AR-46 (fortress site); and
               all resources (yet to be identified) within supporting infrastructure locations (e.g., left bank
                roads and bridges).
20.4.38. The location of paleontological receptors recorded within the AoI, including those recorded on the
         Mindat.org online database (see Volume 2, Annex 7-1), will need to be verified and interpreted
         during the evaluation stage. This will require engagement with a paleontological specialist (e.g., at
         the A Donish Institute of Geology) and, potentially, consultation with appropriate regional
         stakeholders depending on the assessment of the local paleontological specialist. Further detail is
         provided in Section 8 of the CHMP.
         Impact Minimization: Environmental Monitoring: Upper Terraces Cultural Heritage Monitoring
         Program (AM-20-05)
20.4.39. The phased implementation of cultural heritage monitoring in the upper terraces (over 1,290 m asl)
         of the valley during progressive inundation is required in collaboration with the CHMT. The aim of
         the program is to minimize the impact on archaeological, historical and paleontological resources
         and cultural and sacred resources in areas at risk of landslip. It is proposed that the cultural heritage
         monitoring (and recording) of the upper terraces (from the water or via Unmanned Aerial Vehicles
         (UAV) is informed by the reservoir inundation schedule (phased programme) and as agreed with the
         CHMT. This program will be confirmed and prepared by the CHMT, in continued consultation with
         the Dam Safety Panel of Experts (DSPoE), the Engineering Geology team and PMG.
20.4.40. Further detail is provided in Section A0.16.14 of the CHMP.
         Impact Minimization: Implementation of Project Chance Find Procedure (AM-20-06)
         As described, there is a high potential for further, as yet unknown, archaeological, historical and
         paleontological resources to exist within the Project footprint and surrounding AoI, particularly in the
         locations not previously subject to systematic archaeological investigation where preconstruction
         surveys will be necessary (e.g., supporting infrastructure locations, left bank roads and bridges etc.).
         The cultural heritage baseline is therefore, considered representative of the Project AoI and it is
         possible that other sites of interest exist that are not currently identifiable by purely visual means.
         Other (potentially important) remains may survive in below-ground deposits, or in areas which were
         inaccessible to baseline survey.
20.4.41. In line with World Bank ESS8, the implementation of a Chance Find Procedure (CFP) is required, to
         mitigate any accidental damage of both surface and sub-surface archaeological, historical and
         paleontological resources, i.e., to provide a mechanism to address discoveries during Project
         activities when a Cultural Heritage Expert is not present. The CFP will be a core component of the
         Cultural Heritage Awareness Training program (AM-20-01).


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                      May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page | 20-21
20.4.42. The CFP will apply for the lifetime of the Project, throughout the entirety of the footprint and
         associated infrastructure, and during any / all ground works, including vegetation clearance. The
         CFP will clarify the exact steps to be taken in the event of any potential resource discovery. Further
         detail is provided in Section 11 of the CHMP. Strict adherence to the Project’s Chance Find
         Procedure by all on site construction workers, where groundwork activities are ongoing or planned,
         will help to manage any accidental and unforeseen risks to archaeological resources.
20.4.43. Ongoing external engagement with non-governmental organizations (NGOs) will be facilitated by the
         Project CHMT, in collaboration with PMG and DFZ.UNESCO’s ongoing Silk Roads research
         program, already supported by the A Donish Institute, is of particular relevance, given the proximity
         of the flood zone to the Karategin sub-section of the ancient Silk Road route. If deemed appropriate,
         the CHMT will engage with UNESCO and share relevant data acquired during ongoing baseline
         data capture and/or identified through the CFP process. Further detail is provided in Section 15 of
         the CHMP.
         Cultural and Sacred Resources
20.4.44. There are 37 currently identified cultural and sacred resources located within the AoI. This number
         may be updated as the ongoing baseline data collection and mapping phase continues, particularly
         related to supporting infrastructure locations (e.g., left-bank roads and bridges). The introduction of
         heavy machinery (e.g., bulldozers, excavators, dump trucks, vibrating roller, crane and other
         equipment and machines) could result in the direct destruction of cultural and sacred resources
         (burial grounds, ritual objects, sacred places etc.) through a change in the existing land surface and
         the direct destruction of the site’s material value.
20.4.45. Construction activity may also result in ground compaction or vibration (e.g., as heavy equipment is
         transported), affecting cemeteries and burial sites in particular. As the reservoir will progressively
         rise during and after the construction phase, 33 known cultural resources within the inundation zone
         will be submerged. Furthermore, this will lead to the loss of usual local access to cultural and sacred
         resources (see Table 20-9 below) as communities are moved as part of the resettlement program.
20.4.46. Direct interactions with the cultural and sacred resources component are therefore anticipated to
         result from the following construction phase activities:
               Construction of supporting infrastructure (e.g. left bank roads and bridges and transmission
                lines);
               Demolition and relocation of cultural and sacred resources (e.g. mosques and graves) to
                resettlement villages; and
               Progressive reservoir inundation.
20.4.47. Indirect interactions with the cultural and sacred resources component are anticipated to result from
         the following construction phase activities:
               Any groundworks that may result in a change in baseline environmental setting;
               The RAP resettlement program and related land acquisition; and
               Progressive reservoir inundation.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                   May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 20-22

         surveys. Direct construction phase impacts are predicted to occur to 33 known receptors within the
         AoI, which incorporates the proposed Project footprint. These are listed in Table 20-9 and illustrated
         on Drawing 20-5 (Annex 20.5). These include the locations of cemeteries, tombs, mosques and
         springs which are considered low to high in terms of cultural heritage value and sensitivity.

20.4.49. Prior to the implementation of cultural heritage mitigation, direct impacts on known cultural and
         sacred resources could result in the loss of, or damage to, 33 receptors within the inundation zone
         due to submergence. On a worst-case basis, and without the implementation of mitigation
         measures, this would include 25 highly sensitive community receptors. Without mitigation, these
         impacts would be permanent and irreversible. The significance of the impacts would be major (on
         highly sensitive receptors i.e., cemeteries, burials, tombs and springs) and with the potential to
         disrupt normal cultural activities, such as site visitation and / or related festivities.
20.4.50. Throughout the AoI there is also a potential for as yet unidentified cultural resources, including
         cemeteries, shrines, springs etc., to occur. These locations may be accidentally disturbed during
         and preparatory or construction-related activities. The supporting infrastructure locations (e.g., left
         bank roads and bridges) are highlighted in this regard. These resources are of unknown sensitivity
         and are therefore classified according to the criteria in Table 20-2 on a conservative basis.
20.4.51. The direct disturbance and loss of cultural and sacred resources would result in an impact, prior to
         the implementation of appropriate mitigation, that is permanent, irreversible and with the potential to
         occur locally within multiple discrete locations throughout the proposed Project footprint. These
         impacts would be large in magnitude, upon individual resources that are (on a worst-case basis)
         highly sensitive, resulting in a major impact significance.
         Indirect Impacts
20.4.52. Indirect construction phase impacts may occur to four known cultural and sacred receptors within
         the AoI. These are listed in Table 20-10 and illustrated on Drawing 20-5 (Volume 2, Annex 20.5).
         These include the locations of burials, a cemetery and a mazar which are considered high in terms
         of cultural heritage value and sensitivity. Their exact locations are subject to ongoing verification, as
         detailed in Sections 7, 9 and 12 of the CHMP. This is considered appropriate for a Project of this
         scale, where baseline data capture and characterization will inevitably continue beyond the ESIA
         stage. The baseline dataset incorporated within this Chapter is appropriate for the assessment of
         Project impacts and the design of effective mitigation strategies – within the limitations described in
         Volume 1, Chapter 7 (Cultural Heritage Baseline).

         Table 20-10 – Cultural and Sacred Resources within the AoI: Potential Indirect Construction
         Phase Impacts

          RHPP-CR-09                  Cemetery                   High                    Khumrogh (exact
                                                                                         location unknown)

          RHPP-CR-10                  Burial – Hazrat            High                    Khumrogh (exact

20.4.53. The resources listed in Table 20-10 are over 10 km from the dam construction area (hence indirect
         changes to noise, dust etc., are not assessed), and are located in settlements close to (but outside)
         the maximum inundation zone. Any indirect disturbances related to resettlement (demolition) are
         also not assessed, due to the presumed distance of limited impacts generated during the removal of
         household debris. Unforeseen indirect impacts, such as exceedances in noise and dust, will be
         captured through the implementation of the CHMP. The CHMP sets out a programme for continued
         local engagement for all villages, in alignment with the SEP and the Project Greivance Redress
         Mechanism (GRM).
20.4.54. The planned resettlement program will likely prevent continued, safe, usual local access to these
         resources. On a worst-case basis, and without mitigation, indirect changes to local access will be
         permanent and irreversible and have the potential to result in a major impact significance, with the
         potential to disrupt normal cultural activities, such as site visitation.
20.4.55. Without the implementation of specific mitigation measures, there is also potential for culturally
         sensitive materials (including graves) to be re-deposited, damaged or destroyed if secondary
         landslips were to occur in the vicinity of the reservoir perimeter, as it is gradually filled throughout the
         construction phase. The four resources within the immediate vicinity of the FSL are highlighted in
         this regard (Table 20-10). On a conservative basis this impact is permanent, irreversible and with
         the potential to occur locally within multiple discrete locations throughout the proposed Project
         footprint. These impacts would be large in magnitude, upon individual resources that are (on a
         worst-case basis) highly sensitive. This would result in a major impact significance.
         Mitigation: Cultural and Sacred Resources (Construction Phase)
20.4.56. Tangible cultural and sacred resources are non-renewable, and the primary goal of cultural resource
         management should be their physical preservation in-situ, in accordance with World Bank guidance
         (ESS8, 2016) (i.e., to avoid direct impacts regardless of the protected status of the receptor). As
         described previously (AM-20-01), to reduce potential significant effects, and to ensure good practice
         construction methods relating to protection of cultural heritage, the following measures shall be
         implemented through adherence to the Project CHMP and related staff training. The CHMP is
         presented in Volume 3.

         Avoidance Plans (AM-20-02)
20.4.57. To facilitate the avoidance of sensitive cultural and sacred resources across the Project area,
         avoidance plans will be incorporated into the Project’s CHMP. The avoidance areas will include the
         sensitive receptor and an appropriate buffer and the associated community access route, as agreed
         with the site guardian / representative. The construction phase avoidance plan will include all
         cultural and sacred resources currently identified within the AoI, but outside the maximum inundation
         zone, and it will be updated by PMG (in collaboration with the Project CHMT) as further resources
         are identified in participation with communities (see AM-20-05 below). The avoidance plans will be
         developed through the CHMP implementation process and include:
          Cemetery in Khumrogh (RHPP-CR-09);



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                      May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page | 20-26
          Tomb of Hazrat Shamsiddinkhoja in Khumrogh (RHPP-CR-10);
          Tomb of Sayedawazi Kozi in Khumrogh (RHPP-CR-11); and
          Vakhobiddin mazar in Zarda (RHPP-CR-24).
20.4.58. Where the exact location of a cultural and sacred resource is currently not known, this will be
         determined as a matter of urgency through ongoing community consultation and related mapping
         (Section 7 and .9 of the CHMP). If ongoing consultation determines resources RHPP-CR-09, RHPP-
         CR-10, RHPP-CR-11 and RHPP-CR-24 are within 1 km of the FSL and vulnerable to landslip
         impacts, the program of cultural sites and/or grave relocation (as described below, AM-20-05), may
         apply.
         Minimization: Ongoing Engagement (AM-20-07)
20.4.59. Where indirect environmental effects are identified at cultural and sacred site receptors, additional
         cultural heritage mitigations during construction are recommended, in consultation with site users.
         This may include:
               Plans for environmental screening (e.g., planting or additional noise barriers);
               Plans for continued safe access to specific locations as necessary;
               Plans for ongoing consultation with village representatives yet to be engaged by the A.
                Donish Institute Ethnography Team in relation to cultural and sacred sites (see Section 7 of
                the CHMP); and
               Plans for continued, ongoing consultation with cultural leaders to monitor ongoing residual
                impacts and the effectiveness of proposed mitigations, in alignment with the SEP and GRM.
20.4.60. Where ongoing consultation identifies a need for it, a strategy for maintaining community access to
         sacred resources and facilitating respect for local intangible cultural heritage, tradition and taboo will
         be incorporated in the CHMP to ensure that the negative socio-cultural effects are effectively
         mitigated. For example, it may be necessary to demarcate areas to be avoided by noisy, dust
         inducing construction vehicles at certain times of the week/year so as to avoid disturbance of
         traditional ceremonial activities in close proximity of construction routes; with regular platforms for
         community liaison recommended in this regard. In addition, this is will help to prevent any accidental
         loss of (unknown) sensitive cultural assets throughout the construction phase (and beyond).
         Minimization: Relocation (AM-20-08)
20.4.61. In the event that the avoidance of cultural and sacred resources is not possible, as in the case of
         those identified within the proposed flooding zone (Table 20-9), then relocation of the receptors may
         be the most appropriate form of mitigation, if at all possible. This relocation program will be phased
         in response to the inundation schedule and developed in consultation with the CHMT.
20.4.62. Through close cooperation with the local communities and DFZ, the most appropriate destination for
         receptors to be relocated will be determined. The nature, scale and ‘movability’ of the receptors will
         also need to be taken into account when making this decision. It may be possible for such receptors
         to be relocated to the new villages where local communities have been resettled. This process will
         be led by DFZ in conjunction with the CHMT and the site guardian prior to the commencement of
         Project operations in the area of concern. Further detail of this relocation and ongoing management
         program is outlined in Section 12 of the CHMP.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                     PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                     May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 20-27
20.4.63. Alternatively, it may be considered more appropriate to move the receptor to a local museum where
         it can be easily accessed by local communities. The WSP Cultural Heritage Team visited a small
         museum situated in Tursunzoda School in March 2023. Through careful management, and in
         consultation with local communities, ‘heritage hubs’ or community museums will be created in the
         new built settlements under the guidance and management of professional museum experts.
         Museums like this would provide opportunities to help manage equitable sharing of benefits of the
         Project while maintaining access to local cultural knowledge / associated artefacts.
20.4.64. As a strategy for ‘heritage hubs’ or community museums is defined through CHMP implementation
         (CHMP Section 12), a fair and equitable strategy regarding the acquisition or purchase of artefactual
         ethnographic resources (e.g., traditional musical instruments or representations of local craft) to be
         removed / relocated during resettlement will be prepared, in association with local museums and
         cultural centers. If local grievances are raised, regarding local compensation for artefactual materials
         relocated to museums, the GRD will apply. Further detail is provided in Section 12 of the CHMP.
20.4.65. The current cemetery relocation practices are described and evaluated in Annex 7-1. An Ongoing
         Cemetery Relocation Best Practice Guide will be developed and implemented through the CHMP, in
         close consultation with local communities and other stakeholders. It will outline the necessary steps
         that must be taken for burial and cemetery sites which cannot be avoided, through the ongoing
         relocation program overseen by DFZ. The Ongoing Cemetery Relocation guidelines will be
         compliant with Good International Industry Practices (GIIP). Further detail is provided in Section 13
         of the CHMP.
         Intangible Cultural Heritage Practice
20.4.66. Throughout the ethnographic studies completed in support of baseline data collection, a variety of
         unique local practices were recorded in consultation with PAPs. These include intangible cultural
         heritage that is distinct and integral to the isolated, rural mountain communities within the Project
         AoI, incorporating traditional music, dance, food, crafts and ceremonial activities (as described in
         Volume 2, Annex 7-1). In the context of the Project, intangible heritage is a receptor, defined as the
         normal cultural behavior and knowledge transmitted from one generation to the next, which
         communities or individuals recognize as part of their cultural heritage and local identity.
20.4.67. Intangible cultural heritage has been assigned a value of ‘high sensitivity’. This valuation recognizes
         that intangible cultural heritage is closely linked to both individual and group identity in the study
         area and may be particularly vulnerable to societal change, including induced demographic change
         and economic development as communities are transferred to more urban settings.
20.4.68. Certain behaviors and knowledge, particularly those that are unique to the AoI, may be entirely lost
         from one generation to another. Although it is recognized that some adjustments in cultural
         behaviors are inevitable over time, endurance of traditional activities is likely to be a considerable
         factor in the ability of the community (particularly older generations) to deal with and adapt to,
         changing external circumstances, resolve conflict and maintain resilience.
20.4.69. The construction phase will therefore include a series of activities that have a potential to indirectly
         influence intangible cultural heritage practice across the AoI.
20.4.70. These activities include:
               Progressive reservoir inundation; and
               The resettlement program.



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                     PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                     May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 20-28
20.4.71. In combination, these activities will likely prevent continued, usual local access to cultural and
         sacred resources and disrupt normal cultural activities and festivities, both for those communities
         who are resettled and those who remain in the valley. During the construction phase, the
         combination of these impacts upon intangible cultural heritage practice is considered both
         cumulatively and individually. Selecting the magnitude of impacts from these activities is subjective,
         with change from the local cultural norm perceived as either potentially positive or negative. This is
         also likely to vary by age group. For example, anecdotal evidence gathered during informal
         consultations suggested that older generations feel more ‘attachment’ to their villages within the AoI,
         with younger generations excited to engage with the opportunities afforded by a more urbanized
         setting. In the context of the Project and prior to the implementation of specific mitigation, potential
         loss, deviation, or dilution of normal cultural behavior is considered a negative impact.
20.4.72. If indirect impacts are to occur, they could be of unknown and, therefore, high intensity (on a worst-
         case basis) and throughout the duration of construction. Societal changes are considered
         irreversible in the context of intangible cultural heritage and major in terms of impact magnitude
         (without mitigation). This is impact is also induced, with a high likelihood of demographic change
         (and employment opportunities) throughout construction, which may be compounded by the
         presence of workers and predicted influx (see Volume 1, Chapter 17).
         Mitigation: Intangible Cultural Heritage Practice (Construction Phase)
20.4.73. As described previously (AM-20-01), to reduce potential significant effects, and to ensure good
         practice construction methods relating to protection of cultural heritage, the following measures shall
         be implemented through adherence to the Project CHMP and related staff training. The CHMP is
         presented in Volume 3.
         Avoidance and Minimization: Cultural Heritage Awareness Program (AM-20-01)
20.4.74. A cultural heritage awareness program will be prepared and implemented for specialist on-site staff
         (interacting with PAPs), facilitating respect for local intangible cultural heritage, tradition and taboos,
         as applicable for the construction phase (and throughout Operation). It will include as a minimum:
               Information on ‘no go’ areas demarcated by avoidance plans;
               Relevant information on local cultural activities (e.g., rules governing certain places, or a
                schedule of seasonal ceremonies / festivities);
               Information on local cultural sensitivities and acceptable behaviors; and
               Any other pertinent issues to be discussed / prepared in participation with PAPs.
20.4.75. Further detail is provided in Section 6 of the CHMP (in Volume 3).
         Minimization: Ethnographic Management (AM-20-07 and AM-20-08)
20.4.76. A strategy for maintaining community access to tangible sacred resources and thereby facilitating
         respect for, and continuation of, local intangible cultural heritage (traditions and taboos) will
         developed through CHMP implementation (and as described in AM-20-04 and AM-20-05). For
         example, it may be necessary to demarcate areas to be avoided by noisy, dust inducing
         construction vehicles at certain times of the week/year to avoid disturbance of traditional ceremonial
         activities in proximity to construction routes, with regular platforms for community liaison
         recommended in this regard. In addition, this is will help to prevent any accidental loss of sensitive
         cultural assets throughout the construction phase (and beyond).




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                      May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page | 20-29
20.4.77. If the avoidance of such tangible sacred resources is not possible, as in the case of those identified
         within the proposed flooding zone, then relocation of the receptors may be the most appropriate
         form of mitigation, if at all possible. This would ensure continued access to such sites and effectively
         mitigate negative socio-cultural effects.
20.4.78. The CHMP includes provisions for continued local engagement for all villages in alignment with the
         SEP (including those not yet consulted with) to address any unforeseen issues regarding the
         preservation of intangible heritage throughout the lifetime of the Project, should they arise. This will
         also be aligned with the implementation of the Project GRM.
20.4.79. The CHMP sets out a strategy for linking to the Project Livelihood and Restoration Program (LRP) in
         the resettlement villages. This program will be specific to traditional crafts and skills, with the aim of
         ensuring that such intangible cultural heritage practice is not completely lost as a result of the
         Project. The program will be developed in close consultation with local communities and will be run
         from a central ‘heritage hub’ or community museum in each resettlement area. Community cultural
         heritage ‘champions’ will be identified to organize courses and events.
20.4.80. Through careful management, and in consultation with local communities, ‘heritage hubs’ or
         community museums will be created in the new built settlements under the guidance and
         management of professional museum experts. Museums like this would provide opportunities to
         help manage equitable sharing of benefits of the Project while maintaining access to local cultural
         knowledge / associated artefacts. Further detail is provided in Section 12 of the CHMP (Volume 3).
         OPERATIONAL PHASE IMPACTS
         Archaeological, Historical and Paleontological Resources, Cultural and Sacred Resources
         and Intangible Cultural Heritage Practice
20.4.81. This phase of the cultural heritage impact assessment is completed for the fully operational Project.
         This considers the reservoir to be flooded to the maximum extent (1,290 m asl). As discussed
         above, there is a risk, prior to the implementation of effective mitigation measures, that cultural
         heritage resources within the perimeter of the reservoir will be impacted by landslip during Project
         operation.
20.4.82. Although this potential impact is hypothesized to occur throughout the construction phase (as the
         reservoir is progressively filled), the FSL will vary seasonally, potentially triggering landslide
         movement which could affect physical cultural heritage resources. Any such landslide movement
         would likely occur close to the reservoir perimeter and be triggered by new erosion processes during
         operations, as described in Volume 2, Annex 13-2. The potential for archaeological, paleontological
         or cultural features to be damaged as a result of landslide movement would depend on their
         proximity to the reservoir and the nature of the surrounding geology.
20.4.83. The loss of access to specific (tangible) cultural and sacred resources is not assessed at operations
         phase, as these are permanent indirect impacts which are derived in the construction phase
         (associated with each RAP) and assessed (and mitigated) in the preceding section (paragraphs
         20.4.56 – 20.4.65). Any ongoing physical degradation, triggered by potential landslip at FSL, is
         assessed for this operations phase.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                     PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                     May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 20-30
         Impact Classification: Archaeological, Historical and Paleontological Resources and Cultural
         and Sacred Resources
20.4.84. The Rogun dam will be constructed by about 2032, and the FSL reached by approximately 2038.
         Archaeological, paleontological and cultural remains in the upper terraces (above the inundation
         zone) may survive but are likely to be at a greater risk of damage / loss as a result of landslip
         caused by erosion / undermining from the reservoir during operation.
20.4.85. On a worst-case basis, without the implementation of specific mitigation measures, there is potential
         for sensitive materials, of potential national importance, to be re-deposited, damaged or destroyed
         following any secondary landslips that may occur in the vicinity of the reservoir perimeter once it
         reaches FSL. Assuming their endurance throughout the construction phase, 11 archaeological
         resources and four cultural and sacred resources within the immediate vicinity of the FSL are
         highlighted in this regard. These are situated within 1 km of the FSL and are particularly vulnerable
         to under-cutting and eventual slippage (as described in Volume 2, Annex 13-2 – Reservoir Slope
         Stability). Volume 2, Annex 13-2 also describes the mitigation and monitoring measures applicable
         to the management of shoreline erosion and landslip, the impact significance is evaluated as minor.
20.4.86. The extent of this indirect impact on cultural heritage is difficult to quantify without further analysis of
         the archaeological resources in particular (beyond visual / surface inspection). On a conservative
         basis this impact is permanent, irreversible and with the potential to occur locally within multiple
         discrete locations throughout the proposed Project footprint. If they were to occur, these impacts
         would be large in magnitude, upon individual resources that are (on a worst-case basis) highly
         sensitive. This would result in a major impact significance, pre-mitigation.
         Mitigation: Archaeological, Historical and Paleontological Resources & Cultural and Sacred
         resources (Operational Phase)
20.4.87. To reduce potential significant effects, and to ensure good practice construction methods relating to
         protection of cultural heritage in accordance with ESS8, the following measure shall be implemented
         through adherence to the Project CHMP and related staff training during operations.
         CHMP: Upper Terrace Cultural Heritage Monitoring Program (AM-20-05)
20.4.88. A cultural heritage monitoring program will be put in place during construction (as described in AM-
         20-05), comprising visual reconnaissance from the water or UAV survey, in continued consultation
         with the DSPoE, and the Engineering Geology team, to monitor ongoing slippage in the upper
         terraces of the valley and to assess the effectiveness of existing measures for ensuring slope
         stability (Annex 13-2 – Reservoir Slope Stability). Further detail is provided in Section 14 of the
         CHMP.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                       PUBLIC |
         Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                       May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                   Page | 20-31
         Impact Classification: Intangible Cultural Heritage
20.4.89. During the operations phase, the indirect impact of the presence of the reservoir upon intangible
         cultural heritage practice is considered cumulatively for PAPs. This is impact is also induced, with a
         high likelihood of demographic change (and employment opportunities) because of the fully
         operational Project, which may be compounded by the presence of project workers and predicted
         influx (see Chapter 17). Any deviations from the local cultural norm resulting from induced
         demographic changes may be perceived as either positive or negative, and are also likely to vary by
         age group, with older generations likely to feel more ‘attachment’ to their villages or original and/or
         rural identity within the AoI. In the context of the Project and prior to the implementation of specific,
         long-term, operations-specific mitigation, potential loss, deviation, or dilution of normal cultural
         behavior and experience is considered a negative impact.
20.4.90. Selecting the magnitude of impacts from changes in cultural landscape character is currently poorly
         understood. It is recognized however, that although fundamentally the presence of the project during
         operation / at FSL, will represent a major change – from an isolated, mountainous, braided river
         valley, the pace of change is very gradual. As a consequence, it is predicted that the ‘shock’ from
         impact is reduced (gradual) and the opportunity for adaption and resilience in the face of change in
         the landscape character is high.
20.4.91. If these indirect (and induced) impacts are to occur, they could be of unknown and, therefore, high
         intensity (on a worst-case basis) and throughout the duration of construction. Societal changes are
         considered irreversible in the context of intangible cultural heritage and major in terms of impact
         magnitude (without mitigation).
         Mitigation: Intangible Cultural Heritage Practice (Operational Phase)
20.4.92. To reduce potential significant effects, and to ensure good practice relating to protection of
         intangible cultural heritage, the mitigation measures outlined for the construction phase must
         continue throughout the operations phase of the Project: These include:
         CHMP: Cultural Heritage Awareness Program (AM-20-01)
20.4.93. Further detail is provided in Section 6 of the CHMP and above under construction phase Mitigation
         (20.4.73 – 20.4.80).
         CHMP: Ethnographic Management (AM-20-07 and AM-20-08)
20.4.94. Further detail is provided in Section 12 of the CHMP and above under construction phase Mitigation
         (20.4.73– 20.4.80).

        TRANSBOUNDARY EFFECTS
20.4.95. Based on the information gathered to date, there are no known cultural heritage resources that
         could be affected that are of international value or significance that may trigger the need for a
         transboundary heritage assessment.

20.5    SUMMARY OF MITIGATION AND ENHANCEMENT MEASURES
20.5.1. A number of enhancement measures will be implemented to address the potential changes that will
        take place during the construction and operational phases, potentially adversely affecting cultural
        heritage resources if no intervention measures are put into place. These measures are summarized
        below (Table 20-11) and described in detail in the CHMP.
20.5.2. The CHMP is implemented in accordance with the relevant legislation, policy and guidance, namely
        the UNESCO World Heritage Convention (1972; ratified by Tajikistan in 1992); Asian Development
        Bank Environmental Assessment Guidelines (2003); the Law of the Republic of Tajikistan on the
        Protection and Use of Objects of Historical and Cultural Heritage (2017); the Law of the Republic of
        Tajikistan About Culture (1997); World Bank ESS8: Cultural Heritage (2016) and associated
        Guidance Note (2018); and European Investment Bank Environmental and Social Standards (2022).

        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 20-33
          Receptor Type       Overview of Mitigation Measure

          Intangible           Continued consultation and ethnographic management (AM-20-07 and AM-
          Cultural Heritage     20-08).


20.6    SUMMARY OF RESIDUAL EFFECTS
20.6.1. Impacts are predicted or hypothesized to 26 archaeological, historical and paleontological resources
        and 37 cultural and sacred resources within the AoI. Through the application of the mitigation
        hierarchy, to avoid and reduce impacts on cultural heritage resources, the residual impact
        magnitude will be reduced from ‘major’ to ‘moderate to minor’. This recognises the high sensitivity of
        impacted receptors and the degree of induced change driven by the Project. Successful mitigation
        and management will also require sustained efforts throughout construction and throughout
        operation.

21.     MAJOR ACCIDENTS AND DISASTERS

21.1. INTRODUCTION
21.1.1. This Chapter provides a high-level assessment of likely significant effects of the Project on
        environmental and social receptors, arising from the vulnerability of the Project to risks of relevant
        major accidents and/or disasters due to natural or manmade hazards (hereafter referred to as Major
        Events). In addition, this Chapter considers the selection process for resettlement sites.
        Resettlement sites are handed over to the local authority control after one year. Similarly, as the
        construction of resettlers’ houses is carried out by the resettlers themselves and is not under direct
        Project control, any risks related to house re-construction or normal habitation will not be
        considered.
21.1.2. The Chapter takes into consideration both external and internal factors during the operation and
        construction phases, namely:
         The vulnerability of the Project and resettlement sites to Major Events; and/or
         The Project and resettlement activities’ potential to cause Major Events
21.1.3. Based on professional judgement, Major Events are occurrences or scenarios that have the
        potential to affect the Project or associated receptors, causing immediate or delayed major damage
        to one or more of the following:
         Human health;
         Property; and
         Environment.
21.1.4. This assessment is based on the information available within other chapters of this ESIA, such as
        receptors, likely significant effects, and appropriate mitigation measures. This Chapter is intended to
        be read as part of the wider ESIA, with reference to the following chapters in particular:
           Volume 1, Chapter 03: Project Description;
           Volume 1, Chapter 07: Baseline;
           Volume 1, Chapter 08: Air Quality;
           Volume 1, Chapter 09: Greenhouse Gases;
           Volume 1, Chapter 10: Climate Resilience;
           Volume 1, Chapter 11: Noise and Vibration
           Volume 1, Chapter 12: Geology and Soils;
           Volume 1, Chapter 13: Water;
           Volume 1, Chapter 15: Biodiversity;
           Volume 1, Chapter 17: Socioeconomics;
           Volume 1, Chapter 18: Community Health, Safety and Security;
21.1.5. The potential Major Events to which the Project may be at risk of vulnerability during construction
        and operation phases considered in this Chapter are listed in Table 21-1.
