21.1.6. This Chapter describes the assessment methodology and the baseline conditions relevant to the
        assessment, and a summary of the likely significant effects resulting from the vulnerability of the
        Project to Major Events. The study area comprises:
         The Project site (construction area, HPP and reservoir); and
         Active resettlement sites, including:
          • Saidoni Nav (Rogun District);
          • Yoli Garmoba (Rogun District);
          • Darband (Nurobod District);
          • Chorsada-2 (Danghara District);
          • Sebiston (Faizobod District);
          • Toichi (Tursunzoda District); and
          • Namuna (Tursunzoda District).
21.1.7. The definition of key terms used in this Chapter are provided in Table 21-2. These definitions have
        been developed by reference to the definitions used in EU and Tajik legislation and guidance
        relevant to major accidents or disasters, as well as professional judgement based on knowledge and
        experience of similar projects, in the context of the Project.

        Table 21-2 - Key Terms

          Term               Definition

          Disaster           A naturally occurring phenomenon such as an extreme weather event (for example
                             storm, flood, temperature) or ground-related hazard events (for example subsidence,
                             landslide, earthquake), which may or may not be exacerbated by human-caused
                             factors, which has the potential to cause an event or situation that meets the definition
                             of a Major Event as defined below.

          External           A factor which occurs beyond the Project may present a risk to the Project, e.g. If an
          Influencing        external disaster occurred (e.g. earthquake, major accident at another facility) it would
          Factor             increase the risk of serious harm or damage to a receptor associated with the Project.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I              PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                         SEPTEMBER 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                      Page | 21-4
          Term               Definition

          Hazard             Anything with the potential to cause harm, including ill-health and injury to people,
                             damage to property or the environment; or a combination of these.

          Internal           A factor which occurs within the Project may present a risk to the Project itself.
          Influencing
          Factor

          Major Accident     In the context of the Project, an event that threatens immediate or delayed serious
                             damage to human health, safety, welfare, property or the environment, and requires the
                             use of resources beyond those of the Project implementing agencies or its contractors
                             to respond to the event. Serious damage includes the loss of life or permanent injury to
                             at least one person, and/or permanent or long-lasting damage to a receptor that cannot
                             be restored through minor clean-up and restoration efforts. The significance of this
                             effect considers the extent, severity and duration of harm and the sensitivity of the
                             receptor.

          Major Event        A Major Event is a low-likelihood, high-consequence event, encompassing both the
                             term Major Accident and the term Disaster.

          Risk               The likelihood of an impact occurring, combined with effect or consequence(s) of the
                             impact on a receptor if it does occur.

          Risk Event         An identified, unplanned event, which is considered relevant to the Project and has the
                             potential to be a Major Accident or Disaster subject to assessment of its potential to
                             result in a significant adverse effect on a receptor.

          Significant        Significant in the context of Major Events could include the loss of life, permanent injury
          effect             and temporary or permanent destruction of an environmental receptor which cannot be
                             restored through minor clean-up and restoration.

          Vulnerability      In the context of the EU Directive 2014/52/EU, the term refers to the ‘exposure and
                             resilience’ of the Project to the risk of a Major Accident or Disaster. Vulnerability is
                             influenced by sensitivity and adaptive capacity of the receptor and magnitude of impact.


21.2. LEGISLATIVE FRAMEWORK, POLICY AND GUIDANCE
21.2.1. This assessment has been completed in accordance with paragraph 15 of EU Directive 2014/52/EU
        and the Law of the Republic of Tajikistan on Ecological Expertise (news of Majlisi Oli of the Republic
        of Tajikistan 2003 № 4, art. 150, 2005, № 12, art. 638, 2007, № 7 art. 690). For further information
        on legal requirements, see Volume 1, Chapter 2: Legislation and Standards of this ESIA.
        NATIONAL LEGISLATION
21.2.2. The applicable legislative framework covering the design, construction and operation of the Project
        includes:
         Code No. 1329 of 23 July 2016 Labor Code of the Republic of Tajikistan;
         Code No. 1688 of 2 April 2020 Water Code of the Republic of Tajikistan;
         Law No. 721 of 28 June 2011 on Security;



        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I              PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                         SEPTEMBER 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                      Page | 21-5
         Law No. 518 of 1 October 2007 on the Peculiarities of Licensing Certain Types of Activities;
         Law No. 14 of 28 February 2004 on Industrial Safety of Hazardous Production Facilities (as
          amended 02/01/2020); and
         Law No. 666 of 29 December 2010 on Safety of the Hydraulic Structures (as amended
          17/05/2018).
21.2.3. The State Committee on Land Management and Geodesy is responsible for identifying suitable land
        for resettlement sites under Resolution No. 47 of 20 January 2009, “On Resettlement of the
        Population of Rogun Town and Nurobod District from the flooding zone of Rogun HPP”, with
        amendments to Resolution No. 47 of 20 January 2009 including:
         Resolution No. 395 of August 27, 2011, “On modifications and additions to Resolution of the
          Government of the Republic of Tajikistan No. 47 of January 20, 2009”
         Resolution No. 162 of 28 March 2015, “On modification and additions in the order of the
          Government of the Republic of Tajikistan of No. 47 of January 20, 2009.
        INTERNATIONAL LEGISLATION AND GUIDANCE
21.2.4. The applicable legislative framework covering the design, construction and operation of the Project
        is summarized as:
        Paragraph 15 of EU Directive 2014/52/EU states:
            “In order to ensure a high level of protection of the environment, precautionary actions need to
            be taken for certain projects which, because of their vulnerability to major accidents, and/or
            natural disasters (such as flooding, sea level rise, or earthquakes) are likely to have significant
            adverse effects on the environment. For such projects, it is important to consider their
            vulnerability (exposure and resilience) to major accidents and/or disasters, the risk of those
            accidents and/or disasters occurring and the implications for the likelihood of significant adverse
            effects on the environment.”
21.2.5. There is no published guidance for the application of the legal requirements to the assessment of
        Major Events. However, selected relevant guidance for risk assessment methodologies includes:
         The International Standards Organization’s ISO 31000: 2018 Risk Management;
         IEMA (2020) Major Accidents and Disasters in EIA: A Primer;
         IEMA (2016). EIA Quality Mark Article: Assessing Risks of Major Accidents / Disasters in EIA;
         International Commission on Large Dams (ICOLD) (2016) ICOLD Bulletin 148: Selecting Seismic
          Parameters for Large Dams; and
         ICOLD (2011) Bulletin 137: Reservoirs and Seismicity – State of Knowledge.
21.2.6. Additionally, the following international sources have been consulted to support the identification of
        potential Major Events:
         The International Federation of Red Cross & Red Crescent Societies (2008), Early Warning, Early
          Action (International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies, 2008). This guidance
          looks to other countries including those in warmer climates, thereby identifying risks that
          Tajikistan may encounter in the future considering climate change and global warming.
         The International Disaster Database. This online source contains data covering over 22,000 mass
          disasters in the world since 1900 to the present day and aims to “rationalize decision making for
          disaster preparedness, as well as provide an objective base for vulnerability assessment and
          priority setting”.


        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I          PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                     SEPTEMBER 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page | 21-6
21.3. ASSESSMENT METHODOLOGY
21.3.1. The assessment of Major Events has been undertaken through a review of available documentation,
        including other topic chapters of this ESIA, other risk assessments and studies carried out for the
        Project, and third-party data and documentation. No primary data gathering has been undertaken for
        the purposes of this chapter, as other sources are considered sufficient for risk identification and
        assessment.
21.3.2. The assessment presents any identified risks along with whether these are managed to be ‘As Low
        As Reasonably Practicable’ (ALARP) or require precautionary mitigation actions in addition to the
        management measures already integrated into the design and execution of the Project.
21.3.3. The following process has been applied to each of the Major Event categories studied:
           Identifying risks;
           Screening these risks;
           Defining the impact;
           Assessing the likelihood; and
           Assessing the risk.
21.3.4. Identify risks - this assessment focuses on low-likelihood, but potentially high-consequence, events,
        as illustrated in Figure 21-1. All low-consequence events, whatever their likelihood, do not meet the
        definition of Major Events. For example, minor spills which may occur during construction, but would
        be limited in area and volume and temporary in nature, do not meet the definition of a major
        accident. Such minor events would be dealt with under the Project Environmental and Social
        Management Plan (ESMP) and do not fall within the scope of this assessment.

        Figure 21-1 - Graphical Representation of Major Accidents and Disasters Consequence
        Significance




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I          PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                     SEPTEMBER 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page | 21-7
21.3.5. Low-likelihood is defined for the purposes of this assessment as: May occur during the lifetime of the
        Project, so no more than once in 10 years for the construction phase, and no more than once in 100
        years for the operational phase. This is an upper boundary for low likelihood.
21.3.6. Very low likelihood events will also be included in the assessment, which may only occur at most
        once every 1,000 years. Mitigation measures will reflect what is reasonable for such rare events,
        considering their potential consequence, within the guiding principle of risks being ALARP.
21.3.7. External and internal influencing factors are considered within the study area presented in Table 21-
        3.

        Table 21-3 - Study area for internal and external influencing factors

         Feature      Feature                                 Study area     Justification

                      conditions

        SCREENING OF RISKS
21.3.8. To identify whether a Risk Event has the potential to be a Major Event, and thus potentially have a
        significant adverse effect, a source, a pathway (between source and receptor) and a receptor need
        to be present. As such the assessment uses the following conceptual model:



        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I              PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                         SEPTEMBER 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                      Page | 21-8
          The source is the original cause of the hazard, which has the potential to cause harm;
          The pathway is the route by which the source can reach the receptor; and
          The receptor is the specific component of the human or biophysical environment that could be
           adversely affected if the source reaches it.
21.3.9. The following screening process is then used to identify those Major Events which would require
        further consideration within the assessment:
          Is there a potential source, and/or pathway and/or receptor as defined above? If not, no further
           assessment required;
          Is there a relevant receptor present in the locations where the Risk Event could occur, and a
           pathway whereby the source of harm can reach the receptor? If not, no further assessment
           required;
          Does the potential impact on the receptor meet the definition of a significant adverse effect? If
           not, no further assessment required.
21.3.10. EPPs for Rogun HPP are being developed successively for each impoundment level. Per ESS4
         requirements, an EPP will be developed at least 12 months prior to impoundment, and then
         activated. Following this approach, a construction stage EPP for dam elevation of 1100 masl, which
         is expected to be reached in 2025, shows that a dam break at Rogun with a dam crest at 1110 masl,
         there will be no risk downstream of Nurek, including the Nurek dam. As higher dam stages are
         reached until it reaches 1290 masl in about 2038, there will be successive EPPs in place before the
         reservoir is raised to the elevation for that stage (according to the latest OIP: 1100 masl in 2025,
         1165 masl in 2027, 1230 in 2032, and then annual increased up to 1290 masl in 2038) until a full-
         fledged EPP is prepared. Where potential risks are identified downstream of Nurek, appropriate
         mitigations will be required including activation of warning systems. Local communities will receive
         suitable communication materials, such as posters and brochures, so that they are familiar with
         required actions in emergencies well before occasions warranting such actions might arise. In
         addition, a full-fledged EPP for the Vakhsh HPP cascade is being prepared as part of the Nurek
         rehabilitation project, and is in draft stage for Tajikistan, and the Government is engaging with
         riparian countries on the approach to develop EPPs in riparian countries.
21.3.11. Risk Events which are not screened out during this process are compiled into a short list for further
         assessment.
         RISK ASSESSMENT
21.3.12. Once the short list of potential Major Events is compiled, the reasonable worst consequence and
         receptor(s) is identified for each scoped-in Major Event. Potential impacts have been identified in
         consultation with relevant disciplines for each Major Event type assessed within this ESIA. The
         impacts are identified through a qualitative process which seeks to answer the question ‘could this
         event constitute a major accident or disaster in terms of the definitions provided’ (refer to Table 21-
         2).
21.3.13. The likelihood of the reasonable worst-case effect(s) occurring has been evaluated considering:
          The likelihood of the event occurring considering the measures already embedded into the design
           and execution of the Project; and
          The likelihood that a receptor is affected by the event.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I          PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                     SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page | 21-9
21.3.14. Likelihood assessments evaluate whether the effect (for example, loss of life) is a possible outcome
         of the event.
21.3.15. The assessment of the risk has been carried out using a Major Events assessment tool. Where likely
         significant adverse effects are identified, mitigation measures must be in place, commensurate with
         the likelihood of the event occurring. The assessment considers, in consultation with relevant
         disciplines, whether the risk to the receptor is managed to be ALARP with the existing measures.
21.3.16. If gaps are identified, where the existing measures do not represent management of risks to a
         receptor to be ALARP, then mitigation is required. The Risk Record presented in Section 23.7
         records the outcome of the assessment.
         APPRAISE RISK MANAGEMENT OPTIONS
21.3.17. Risk management options fall into the following categories:
          Eliminate (or ‘avoid’) the risk, by adopting alternative processes in order to eliminate the source of
           the hazard, or remove the receptor from harm;
          Reduce the risk by adapting proposed processes such that either the likelihood or the impact of
           the Risk Event can be reduced;
          Isolate the risk, by using physical measures to ensure that should the Risk Event occur, it can be
           effectively isolated such that there is no pathway;
          Control the risk, by ensuring that appropriate control measures are in place (for example
           emergency response) so that should a Risk Event occur, it can be controlled and managed
           appropriately. The mitigation hierarchy to repair and compensate any significant damage to
           receptors may then apply following a control measure; and
          Exploit the risk if it presents potential benefits or new opportunities.
21.3.18. As safety risks will be required to be adequately addressed within the regulatory framework for the
         Project, it is not anticipated that significant residual effects, in terms of safety risks, will be identified
         as an output of the assessment.
         SIGNIFICANCE CRITERIA
21.3.19. By definition, a Major Event would have a major adverse effect on human health, property or the
         environment. Accordingly, any risks that could result in a Major Event after the application of existing
         management or regulatory controls in place, or mitigation identified in other topic chapters, will be
         assessed as significant.

        21-5.

21.5. MAJOR EVENT TYPES SCOPED IN OR OUT OF FURTHER ASSESSMENT
21.5.1. An initial review of the Major Event categories identified in the study area has been undertaken to
        inform the scoping process. This is summarized in Table 21-6 to show the potential vulnerability of
        the Project to the risk of each Major Event type. The phases are indicated in the table as “C” for
        Construction phase and “O” for Operational phase. For the purposes of this assessment,
        Operational phase’ is considered to commence when construction of the dam is complete and the
        reservoir is at Full Supply Level (FSL) (1290masl), although it is acknowledged that the Project is
        already partially operational and will be generating at full capacity before this point.
21.5.2. For potential Major Events that have been screened out, justification has been provided to support
        this decision.
21.5.3. Where appropriate, this Chapter includes the further mitigation measures required to prevent,
        reduce or offset any significant adverse effects, the preparedness for and proposed response to
        emergencies, and the expected residual effects after these mitigation measures have been
        employed (if any).




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment – Volume I           PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                     SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page | 21-15
Table 21-6 - Elements Scoped In or Out of Further Assessment

                                                                                                                                                                                                                          Scope In?
 Major Event       Major Event       Major Event
 Group             Category          Type            Basis of Decision to Scope In / Out                                                                                                                                  Phase

 Natural           Geophysical       Earthquakes     The Project is located within a seismically active zone. Earthquakes regularly occur in Tajikistan, mostly with magnitudes below 5.5 on the Richter scale.           Y
 Hazards                                             The strongest earthquakes recorded in the wider region were the Karatag earthquake of 1907 with a magnitude of 7.3 and the Khait earthquake in 1949 with
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          C, O
                                                     a magnitude of 7.6. Damage was caused to the Project itself by an earthquake during construction in 1995. Two earthquakes occurred in May and
                                                     September 2002, measuring 4.9 and 5.3 respectively. The latter resulted in 3 deaths.
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          Project site
                                                     A number of tectonic faults are present in the region and close to the Project site. These faults are listed in Table 23-4. At least one active fault (Vakhsh)       Nurobod District (Darband)
                                                     crosses the dam itself. However, the current Project is being designed to withstand the Maximum Credible Earthquake (MCE). Additionally, a                           Faizobod District (Sebiston)
                                                     comprehensive Seismic Hazard Update Study for the Project was completed in November 2024 (Seister Seismic Engineering Solutions, 2024), which                        Tursunzoda District (Toichi,
                                                     includes up-to-date probabilistic and deterministic assessments of seismic risk as well as assessment of fault displacement, reservoir induced seismicity            Namuna)
                                                     (RIS) and provides microzonation and topographic amplification factors. Some seismic monitoring is in place both nationally and at the dam site. The
                                                     Seismic Hazard Study includes recommendations to improve the Project's existing seismic monitoring network.
                                                     Detailed reviews of the design and construction work have also been undertaken by the Dam Safety Panel of Experts (DSPoE). Since 2022, six DSPoE
                                                     missions have been undertaken to review risks and the degree of hazard posed. During these visits, progress on resolving previously identified issues is
                                                     reviewed and any new issues are identified. The dam is being designed in accordance with the recommendations of the ICOLD bulletin 120: “Design
                                                     features of dams to resist seismic ground motion” (2001). The 2014 Techno-Economic Assessment Study, identifies design features to reduce the impact of
                                                     seismic events on the dam these include, for example, foundations being excavated to very dense material or rock, the transverse foundation slope across
                                                     the core zone should be either horizontal or slope gently upstream along the upper 30m of the abutments of high dams, to assure watertight contacts after
                                                     shaking; and/or preclude excessive settlement of the embankment materials. and the provision of a larger freeboard than normal to increase lateral
                                                     dimensions at the maximum water surface. Reservoir-Induced Seismicity (RIS)
                                                     Large dams can trigger earthquakes in two ways: (1) by the added weight of a reservoir; and (2) by the water that seeps into cracks underground or along a
                                                     fault. There are thought to be over 100 instances of dam reservoirs inducing earthquakes. The most recent case of this was the magnitude 7.9 Sichuan
                                                     earthquake in China in May 2008, which some experts believe may have been induced by filling the Zipingpu Dam. To reduce the risk of reservoir-triggered
                                                     seismic events, which is in general higher when filling is done quickly and stresses change more quickly, the Rogun reservoir will be filled slowly, over a
                                                     period of 16 years. Annex 13.2 Reservoir slope stability provides further detail on this risk. The risk of RIS is addressed in the 2024 Seismic Hazard Study
                                                     (Seister Seismic Engineering Solutions, 2024).
                                                     Resettlement Sites
                                                     The Directorate of the Flooding Zone of Rogun Hydro power plant (DFZ) has reported that sites are selected in conjunction with relevant ministries and that
                                                     seismic hazard is a key consideration. However, earthquake risk is high throughout Tajikistan.
                                                     Regarding earthquakes, the most relevant risks are collapsing buildings (residential and public buildings), and the triggering of additional Major Events (e.g.
                                                     landslides and impulse waves, ground collapse). Public buildings remain under Project control where physical resettlement is ongoing – Toichi and Namuna
                                                     (Tursunzoda District), Darband (Nurobod District), and Sebiston (Faizobod District). Partially constructed buildings which are in use are a particular risk as
                                                     they may be less stable than completed buildings.
                                                     The most significant secondary Major Event which could be triggered by an earthquake, in the context of the Project, is a landslide. This is considered
                                                     separately under ‘Landslides and Mudslides.’

 Natural           Geophysical       Volcanic        The Project is not in a volcanically active area, and it is highly unlikely that an ash cloud could significantly impact on any aspect of the Project. The nearest   N
 Hazards                             Activity        volcano is the Tianshan volcano in China, over 1,000km to the west which has not erupted since the 7th century CE. The Dacht-i-Navar and Vakak
                                                     volcanoes in Afghanistan have no confirmed eruptions in the last 10,000 years and are considered dormant. Therefore, this Major Event Type has been
                                                     scoped out from further assessment.

 Natural           Geophysical       Landslides &    Landslide risk is classified as high in Tajikistan, with the highest risk being present in the center and north west of the country, including the area around the   Y
 Hazards                             mudslides       dam site. The highest number of recorded landslides are around the Vakhsh valley (World Bank Group, 2022). Some interviewed Project-Affected People
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          C, O
                                                     (PAPs) mentioned that landslides were a significant risk in their former village, including one resettled PAP whose former home had been destroyed.
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          Project site
                                                     Intense periods of rainfall can increase the risk of landslides and mudslides, in conjunction with terrain slope, geology, soil, land cover and (potentially)
                                                     earthquakes that make localized landslides a frequent phenomenon. The area is prone to erosion which has been made worse by local deforestation                      Nurobod District (Darband)
                                                     caused by logging, agriculture and grazing.                                                                                                                          Rogun City District (Yoli
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          Garmoba, Saidoni Nav)
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          Faizobod District (Sebiston)

 Major Event       Major Event       Major Event
 Group             Category          Type            Basis of Decision to Scope In / Out                                                                                                                              Phase

                                                     Reservoir impoundment and oscillations in water levels can increase landslide risk. The most devastating such event was the Vajont dam disaster in Italy in
                                                     1963, in which reservoir filling triggered a giant rockfall over 2km in length, which caused an 100m high impulse wave that overwhelmed shoreline
                                                     settlements and overtopped the dam, resulting in over 2,000 deaths (Association of Dam Safety Officials, n.d.). A smaller incident occurred in Hoseynabad-
                                                     e Kalpush, Iran, in 2019, in which an impoundment-induced landslide damaged 300 houses (Magdalena Vassileva, 2023). The risk of reservoir-induced
                                                     landslides and impulse waves are relevant to settlements on the reservoir banks and/or close to the water line.
                                                     The DSPoE 6th Report identified landslides impacting the Project in Yakhak and Obishur. The Yakhak mudflow occurred in a tributary valley, in which a
                                                     creek was blocked by a natural dam which then eroded and caused a wave within the reservoir. The Obishur mudflow occurred downstream of Rogun and
                                                     blocked outflows from the tunnels. The Report provided recommendations to prevent recurring risks to the dam from similar events.
                                                     Landslide risk is considered in the resettlement program, with some villages outside the flooding zone being resettled based on land stability considerations.
                                                     The following settlements are undergoing geological studies to determine whether resettlement is required (all of which will be included in future RAPs):
                                                     Dushokha zamin;
                                                     Devlokhak;
                                                     Shule;
                                                     Dehqonobod (formerly Qizrok);
                                                     Labi Jar;
                                                     Shohindara;
                                                     Zarda;
                                                     Qal’ai surkh;
                                                     Dehai Amirbek;
                                                     Khumdon (formerly Navdonak);
                                                     Yusti poyon; and
                                                     Dehai Shokh.
                                                     At full supply level the reservoir will be at a height of 1290 masl and the height of the dam wall is 335 m. A dam break assessment has been undertaken for
                                                     FSL at 1100 masl but no dam break assessment has been undertaken to date for the FSL at 1290 masl. The dam break assessment for FSL will consider
                                                     the risk of a landslide of sufficient scale to generate a wave which could cause overtopping during construction. Once the dam break assessment report is
                                                     available this risk will be reviewed. Table 3-15: Reservoir Inundation Levels of Volume 1, Chapter 3: Project Description outlines the proposed
                                                     incremental filling of Rogun reservoir.
                                                     The suitability of resettlement sites is considered in accordance with regulatory requirements. Before land acquisition for the development of resettlement
                                                     sites can commence, the site must be approved as safe and suitable by the site selection committee and subject to required approvals from appropriate
                                                     governmental agencies (e.g. the Ministry of Environmental Protection should confirm that the site is not vulnerable to, and will not cause, significant
                                                     environmental risks). Site selection is also aligned with central government planning for social and economic development. Sites are selected in areas
                                                     where additional population will not cause strain on local resources, where earthquake, landslide and climate-related risks are low. Mitigation for this risk
                                                     will be provided via the water level monitoring system, which is already in place, as well as the EPRP.
                                                     Landslide risk is considered in Volume 1, Chapter 10: Climate Change and Volume 2, Annex 13.2: Reservoir Slope Stability (which includes an impact
                                                     assessment), as well as EPRP which are in the process of being updated for the construction site and Vakhsh cascade. Slope stability of the reservoir is
                                                     also covered within geotechnical design. This risk is therefore scoped out of the Major Events assessment for the Project site.
                                                     Resettlement sites
                                                     Landslides are not a significant risk at the Danghara and Tursunzoda sites, which are located on flat ground with no mountains, slopes or riverbanks
                                                     adjacent. This risk is scoped out at these sites.
                                                     The sites in Nurobod District, Rogun City District and Faizobod District are located on or adjacent to steep slopes and therefore are in higher risk areas.
                                                     There are three sources of risk: 1. Landslides from uphill (Darband, Saidoni Nav); 2. Ground collapse from below (Darband, Saidoni Nav, Yoli Garmoba,
                                                     Sebiston); and 3. Upstream landslides causing impact waves which flood or overwhelm settlements on the shore (Darband, Saidoni Nav). This third risk is

                                                     no greater at the resettlement sites than at pre-existing settlements at similar elevation and proximity to the reservoir and therefore will be considered
                                                     alongside them.

 Natural           Geophysical       Sinkholes       There is no evidence of karst dissolution on the reservoir banks as limestone geology is not present. A risk may be caused by dissolution of salt deposits.       N
 Hazards                                             The impact of reservoir filling on the stability of the slopes is assessed in Volume 2, Annex 13.2: Reservoir Slope Stability of this ESIA, as well as other
                                                     studies undertaken for the Project such as the DSPoE reports. There is no evidence to suggest sinkhole risk at other sites. This risk is therefore scoped out
                                                     of further assessment from a Major Events perspective.

 Natural           Geophysical       Tsunamis        The Project is located inland, outside a tsunami risk zone. Therefore, this Major Event type has been scoped out from further assessment from a Major             N
 Hazards                                             Events perspective.

 Natural           Hydrological      Coastal         The Project is located inland, outside a coastal area. Therefore, this Major Event type has been scoped out from further assessment from a Major Events           N
 Hazards                             Flooding        perspective.

 Natural           Hydrological      Fluvial         In the Rogun area, river flood hazard is classified as very low. This means that there is a chance of less than 1% that potentially damaging and life-            N
 Hazards                             Flooding        threatening river floods occur in the coming 10 years (return period of c. 1 in 1,000 years).
                                                     The Vakhsh river is a snow- and glacier-melt river. The high flows are related to the thaw season with main peaks in July and August. Discharge is not
                                                     correlated to precipitation. Three major observed flood events of Vakhsh river at Tutkaul gauging station occurred in July 1953, July 1958 and July 1969.
                                                     The Nurek Hydro Power Plant (HPP) is not designed to handle the Probable Maximum Flood (PMF), and this places both the facility and downstream areas
                                                     at risk. Rogun HPP is designed to withstand the PMF and its storage capacity and water regulation capability would allow flow control to protect Nurek and
                                                     the downstream cascade. The Project provides the opportunity to mitigate adverse effects of climate change on downstream areas.
                                                     The mean annual flow in glacier-fed rivers is predicted to increase in the short to medium-term due to climate change, which is causing glaciers to melt
                                                     rapidly, but decrease in the long-term as glaciers melt and disappear. Some glaciers in Tajikistan, including the Fedchenko glacier in Pamir, have been
                                                     observed to be resilient in recent years, however the causes of this phenomenon are unknown and it is therefore unclear how long it will continue.
                                                     Fluvial flooding has been scoped out during Operation as the Project is designed handle the PMF and will reduce potential flooding along the downstream
                                                     cascade. Overtopping in the unlikely event of exceedance of PMF is also addressed in the cascade EPRP. Fluvial flood risk in Rogun, Darband and Gharm
                                                     is very low. One PAP interviewed in a riverside settlement in Rasht confirmed that he had never observed a fluvial flood in his lifetime.
                                                     Resettlement sites
                                                     As identified above, fluvial flood hazard is classified as very low in Rogun, Darband and Gharm. In all other resettlement sites, including Tursunzoda and
                                                     Danghara, river flood risk is very low (less than 1% probability that potentially damaging and life-threatening river floods occur in the coming 10 years). A
                                                     lake (Khavzi Karakul) is present uphill of Darband (Nurobod District); however, this is a small tarn located in a natural depression and is unlikely to present
                                                     significant flood risk.
                                                     Therefore, this Major Event Type has been scoped out from further assessment from a Major Events perspective.

 Natural           Hydrological      Glacial Lake    GLOF occurs where there is a release of meltwater from a naturally formed moraine (ice dam) within a glacial lake. Causes of release can be through               N
 Hazards                             Outburst        natural dam breach, especially in moraine dams which are typically weak in structure, or caused by landslides, earthquakes or avalanches.
                                     Flooding
                                                     In addition, increased global warming is leading to rapid loss of ice resulting in the formation and/or expansion of glacial lakes, which when breached
                                     (GLOF)
                                                     present a high risk to communities downstream.
                                                     According to the Glacier Lake Outburst Flood Database V3.0 (uni-potsdam.de) 17 incidents between 1910 and 2015 have been recorded in Tajikistan, all of
                                                     which occur in the Pamir mountain range. Several incidents of GLOF have occurred which affected the Project, including one in which the cofferdam was
                                                     overtopped early in construction, and two more recent incidents in which the powerhouse was flooded. An early warning system is in place to monitor
                                                     upstream water levels and raise the alarm in case of elevated flow.
                                                     GLOF has been considered within the Climate Risk and Vulnerability Assessment (CRVA) in Volume 1, Chapter 10: Climate Change of this ESIA. A
                                                     standalone GLOF study has also been commissioned for the Project. This risk is therefore scoped out of further assessment from a Major Events
                                                     perspective.

 Natural           Hydrological      Pluvial         Tajikistan’s topography is mountainous; much of the country sits 3,000m above sea level and it has peaks over 7,000m in the Pamir and Alay ranges. The             Y
 Hazards                             Flooding        Fergana Valley to the north and the Kofarnihon and Vakhsh river valleys to the south are the lower elevation areas. This topography influences the country’s
                                                                                                                                                                                                                        C
                                                     hazardscape. Intense rainfall on steep terrain contributes to damaging floods and mudslides on a regular basis.
                                                     In the Rogun area, urban flood hazard is classified as high. This means that potentially damaging and life-threatening urban floods are expected to occur at
                                                     least once in the next 10 years.                                                                                                                                   Tursunzoda District (Toichi)
                                                     Resettlement sites
                                                     As identified above, urban flood hazard is classified as high in the Rogun area. Urban flood risk in Danghara and Tursunzoda is classified as medium (more
                                                     than 20% probability that potentially damaging and life-threatening urban floods will occur in the next 10 years). The rural nature of most of the resettlement
                                                     sites, however, as well as the steep topography in some, make pluvial / urban flooding unlikely. The only site at potential risk, due to being larger, more
                                                     urbanized, and low-lying, is Toichi in Tursunzoda District.
                                                     The Project site and most of the resettlement sites are located in rural areas where the risk of surface water flooding is low and therefore the risk of pluvial
                                                     flooding does not need to be considered further from a Major Events perspective. Furthermore, the risk of pluvial flooding is considered within Volume 1,
                                                     Chapter 10: Climate Change of this ESIA. However, the location of the resettlement site, Toichi, in Tursunzoda District is scoped in for further assessment
                                                     from a Major Events perspective due to its location in a more urbanized and low-lying area.

 Natural           Hydrological      Groundwater     There are no records of groundwater flooding in the Project area. The risk of the reservoir causing groundwater flooding in neighboring areas by changing          N
 Hazards                             Flooding        groundwater volumes and flows is considered in Volume 2, Annex 13-2 Reservoir Slope Stability. Therefore, this Major Event type has been scoped out
                                                     from further assessment from a Major Events perspective.

 Natural           Hydrological      Avalanches      Avalanches occur regularly in Tajikistan due to its mountainous terrain and cold winter climate. The major reason for avalanches in Tajikistan is fresh snow       Y
 Hazards                                             formation. Large amounts of fresh snow not yet consolidated are likely to be set in motion. In addition, the interface between fresh and old snow is unstable
                                                                                                                                                                                                                        C
                                                     and tends to create sliding planes. Most avalanches in Tajikistan are observed in February and March.
                                                     Avalanche risk is considered in Chapter 10: Climate and is therefore scoped out of this Major Events assessment for the Project site.
                                                                                                                                                                                                                        Nurobod District (Darband)
                                                     Resettlement sites                                                                                                                                                 Rogun City District (Saidoni
                                                                                                                                                                                                                        Nav)
                                                     Danghara and Tursunzoda are located outside the area of avalanche risk. Saidoni Nav (Rogun City District) and Sebiston (Faizobod District) are also at
                                                     negligible risk due to the topography of the sites; Sebiston is located on flat ground, and Yoli Garmoba has deep valleys on both sides which would protect it
                                                     from avalanches on nearby mountains. Darband (Nurobod District) and Saidoni Nav (Rogun City District) are at higher risk, due to their location at the base
                                                     of steep slopes where snow accumulates in winter. Darband extends to the base of the mountain and has a relatively large and growing population.
                                                     Therefore, this risk is scoped in for further assessment for Nurobod District (Darband) and Rogun City District (Saidoni Nav).

 Natural           Climatological    Thunderstor     This type of event could result in lightning strikes to temporary elevated structures during construction (e.g. tower cranes); however, the risk is no different to   N
 Hazards           and               ms              other construction projects. No elevated structures will form part of operational Project or resettlement sites. Specific measures are therefore not considered
                   Meteorological                    to be required as part of the Project. Therefore, this Major Event type has been scoped out from further assessment from a Major Events perspective.

 Natural           Climatological    Wave Surges     The Project includes a large reservoir, which will generate waves. The most significant cause of large and potentially dangerous waves is upstream                    N
 Hazards           and                               landslides, which is discussed under the ‘Landslides and Mudslides’ Major Event type above. This Major Event type has been scoped out from further
                   Meteorological                    assessment from a Major Events perspective.

 Natural           Climatological    Extreme         In the Rogun area (including neighboring Nurobod and Faizobod), extreme heat hazard is classified as low. This means that there is between 5% and 25%
 Hazards           and               temperatures    chance that at least one period of prolonged exposure to extreme heat, resulting in heat stress, will occur in the next five years. This risk will increase due to
                   Meteorological    : Heatwaves,    climate change, which will lead to higher average annual temperatures and longer, more intense heatwaves.

 Natural           Climatological    Droughts        Tajikistan has historically experienced drought conditions. Tajikistan’s worst drought was in 2000 and affected about 3 million people (approximately half the       N
 Hazards           and                               country’s population at that time).
                   Meteorological
                                                     In the Rogun area, water scarcity is classified as very low or non-existent, where droughts occur much less than once every 1,000 years.
                                                     The CRVA in Volume 1, Chapter 10: Climate Change of this ESIA considers water stress and drought. Both are considered low risk during construction
                                                     and very low during operation. This risk is therefore scoped out of further assessment for the Project site.
                                                     Resettlement sites
                                                     Drought is considered very low risk in the area around the Project site, including the Rogun District, Nurobod District and Faizobod District sites, as well as
                                                     Danghara District. However, Tursunzoda is a medium risk for drought, which means that there is more than a 25% chance that at least one period of
                                                     drought will occur in the next 10 years. This may reasonably occur while the resettlement sites remain under Project control, especially Tursunzoda which
                                                     continues to receive new resettlers. The Tajik government will provide guidance on the actions to be taken in the event of drought. Therefore, this Major
                                                     Event type has been scoped out from further assessment.

 Natural           Climatological    Fog             Fog, lasting for more than 24 hours, with a visibility of 50 meters and less, are considered especially dangerous. However, it is a rare phenomenon in               N
 Hazards           and                               Tajikistan. Foggy conditions start in November, reach their peak in December-January, and end in March. The exception is some highland areas in the
                   Meteorological                    zones of cloud formation, where fog is near-permanent. In most areas, the total duration of fog does not exceed 50-70 hours a year. On the mountain
                                                     passes, the duration of fog increases to 1,200 hours a year. Normally, fog continues for 3-5 hours. In the period of 1961-1990, the number of days with fog
                                                     increased by 20-50%. In the area of Khujand city, the number of days with fog increased by 80%, however Khujand is located in the north of Tajikistan,
                                                     180km from the Project area.
                                                     In the event of reduced visibility construction and maintenance works would be temporarily suspended until visibility improved. The risks incurred would be
                                                     to Project workers who are covered by occupational health and safety (OHS) legislation and policy and therefore scoped out of this assessment. Risks to
                                                     resettled communities would be the same or lower than pre-resettlement, depending on whether they remain close to the Project area or move to a lower-
                                                     lying site. Therefore, this Major Event type has been scoped out from further assessment from a Major Events perspective.

 Natural           Climatological    Wildfires:      Wildfire hazard is classified as high in Tajikistan, which means that there is greater than a 50% chance of encountering weather that could support a                N
 Hazards           and               Forest fire,    significant wildfire that is likely to result in both life and property loss in any given year.
                   Meteorological    Bush/brush
                                                     However, in the Project AoI, forests are rare as most trees have been cut for firewood. The remaining forests are located at higher elevations, far above the
                                     pasture
                                                     reservoir FSL. Shrubs and bushes are found on the slopes of the reservoir, but these are also decreasing since most are cleared to make way for
                                                     cultivation. Therefore, wildfire is unlikely to be a risk in this location and has therefore been scoped out from further assessment from a Major Events
                                                     perspective.
                                                     Resettlement sites
                                                     Wildfire hazard is classified as high in Rogun, Faizobod, Tursunzoda and Danghara, which means that there is greater than a 50% chance of encountering
                                                     weather that could support a significant wildfire that is likely to result in both life and property loss in any given year. However, no forests that might be the
                                                     source of wildfires are present around any of the resettlement sites. Therefore, this Major Event type has been scoped out from further assessment from a
                                                     Major Events perspective.

 Natural           Climatological    Poor Air        Air quality in Tajikistan is generally poor and is at its worst during the late autumn and winter months, due to the use of the coal-fired power stations for        N
 Hazards           and               Quality         energy generation and wood fires for domestic heating and cooking.
                   Meteorological
                                                     In 2022 the average PM2.5 concentration in Tajikistan was 9.2 times the World Health Organization (WHO) annual air quality guideline value.
                                                     Construction: During the construction phase there will be an increase in dust emissions associated with the use of heavy construction traffic; compacted
                                                     earth access roads; the operation of the crusher facilities and the associated open conveyors; unloading and loading of materials; the operation of the
                                                     concrete batching plants, the operation of the borrow pits and the associated transport of the materials in open trucks.

                                                      Road sprinkling is carried out in the construction site to suppress dust emissions.
                                                      Operation: There will be diesel engine emissions originating from all mobile vehicles, and from back-up generators. During the operational phase there will
                                                      also be emissions of coarse particulate matter from exposed, dried sediments during periodic drawdown of the reservoir for routine inspection and
                                                      maintenance. The Project will also lead to wider improvements in air quality, however, if fossil-fueled energy generation is displaced, and households are
                                                      able to switch to electric power for heating and cooking.
                                                      Air quality impacts during construction and operation are assessed in Volume 1, Chapter 08: Air Quality of this ESIA. The air quality assessment found
                                                      that construction activities have an adverse effect on air quality and mitigation measures are required. For the operational phase, impacts are minor to
                                                      negligible. This risk can therefore be scoped out of this Major Events assessment.
                                                      Resettlement sites
                                                      The presence of additional people (including their heating systems and vehicles) is likely to exacerbate poor air quality in the airsheds around the
                                                      resettlement sites. However, the risks and impacts will be no greater than any other settlement. Therefore, this Major Event type has been scoped out from
                                                      further assessment from a Major Events perspective.

 Natural           Biological        Disease          Tajikistan is a lower-middle income country; however, the population is in generally good health and at least basic medical care is available to most of the       N
 Hazards                             epidemics:       population. The most recent major disease epidemic in Tajikistan was COVID-19, the first cases of which were identified in April 2020. Although no longer
                                                      considered a global health emergency by the WHO, the vulnerability of the Project to a Major Event caused by COVID-19 during construction and operation
                                     Viral
                                                      would be mitigated by the occupational health and safety (OHS) processes that are implemented by both the contractor and government rules and
                                     Bacterial        guidelines. An outbreak of polio was also detected in 2021; however, this was responded to rapidly by a vaccination campaign. The outbreak was declared
                                                      closed in 2022.
                                     Parasitic
                                                      Labor influx is a potential source of infectious disease outbreak. The workforce is very large, the majority of whom work in two shifts of 15 days and reside in
                                     Fungal           worker accommodation, sharing rooms and facilities with other workers. Risks to workers are managed by OHS and labor management measures. Many
                                     Prion            workers also commute daily from nearby villages, or reside within Rogun City District alongside the community, which exposes the community to some risk.
                                                      Risks to the community are discussed in Volume 1, Chapter 18: Community Health, Safety and Security. No evidence of significant disease outbreaks
                                                      associated with the Project has been identified to date.
                                                      It would be unlikely for any workers to contract Weil’s Disease (or leptospirosis) as appropriate PPE will be worn and any risks managed in the
                                                      Environmental and Social Management Plan (ESMP). Risks from Weil’s Disease is a low likelihood, but not of high consequence. The last outbreak of
                                                      leptospirosis in Tajikistan was recorded in April 2006 approximately 50km east of Dushanbe in the Mehrobod area of the Faizobod District.
                                                      The construction and operation of the Project will not give rise to any disease epidemics.
                                                      Resettlement sites
                                                      The Project may contribute to the spread of infectious disease into the resettlement areas because of population movements, however the shift is slow and
                                                      gradual, and is mostly within sparsely populated rural areas. The risk of significant contributions to the spread of infectious disease, beyond baseline
                                                      conditions, is therefore considered to be negligible, and as such this Major Event type has been scoped out from further assessment from a Major Events
                                                      perspective.

                                     Foot and        Livestock will be transported from the Project area to resettlement sites, which has the potential to spread animal disease. However, there are no known
                                     mouth           outbreaks of infectious animal disease in the resettled communities. If a disease were identified, spread would be controlled through containment of infected

 Natural           Biological        Plants          Project construction activities have the potential to introduce/facilitate the spread of non-native invasive plant species to sensitive habitats around the      N
 Hazards                                             Project site, resulting in the deterioration of these habitats. Standard control measures would be implemented by the appointed contractor during
                                                     construction to handle and dispose of any diseased plants and/or injurious weeds and prevent their spread.
                                                     Invasive species are considered in Volume 1, Chapter 15: Biodiversity of this ESIA and therefore are scoped out of the Major Events assessment.
                                                     Resettlement sites
                                                     Invasive species, e.g. Xanthium spinosum, have been identified in the Project area. Resettlement will involve the transportation of plants, as some resettled
                                                     households have reported transplanting trees. This process is managed by the households themselves, who may accidentally transport seeds or spores.
                                                     However, it is considered unlikely that this risk will lead to a Major Event. The potential for Major Events caused by the introduction of harmful invasive
                                                     species is therefore considered to be negligible and scoped out of further assessment.

 Technological     Societal          Extensive       The Project is in a developing country (according to the definition from the International Monetary Fund, Tajikistan is a developing country because of its      N
 or Manmade                          public          lower economic performance) that has steady population growth. International SOS reports that the central government of Tajikistan has extensive influence
 Hazards                             demonstratio    across the country, though the level of effective state control is lower in areas further from the capital Dushanbe, where the security environment is more
                                     ns which        volatile, notably in Gorno-Badakhshan Autonomous Oblast (GBAO). Instances of violence by security forces have occurred, notably incidents in GBAO in
                                     could lead to   2021-22.
                                     violence and
                                                     The Project is controversial internationally, particularly amongst non-governmental organizations (NGOs) and riparian countries. Downstream Uzbekistan
                                     loss of life
                                                     has opposed the Project in the past due to fears regarding water availability, although these disagreements have since been resolved and a power
                                                     purchase agreement (PPA) has been signed.
                                                     Domestically, data gathered during the preparation of this ESIA suggests a high level of public support, including amongst affected communities, who
                                                     reported being generally optimistic about resettlement and improved energy supply. There have been some controversies, for example allegations of forced
                                                     share-buying to fund Project construction in 2009-2010, although this did not result in large-scale protests. A benefits-sharing mechanism, which will invest
                                                     funds from sale of energy from the Project into social programs for the public, has also been agreed. Widespread or violent protests caused by opposition to
                                                     the Project are therefore considered unlikely, assuming the implementation of the agreed measures.
                                                     Labor grievances leading to strikes are another potential cause of demonstrations. The Project has a very large workforce (anticipated to peak at around
                                                     20,000), many of whom reside on-site. The 2023 labor audit found evidence of potentially contentious issues, such as salary delays and unresponsive
                                                     management structures. However, no evidence of strikes on the Project to date was identified. Labor and working conditions surveys have confirmed that
                                                     workers are generally satisfied with their wages, all of which are higher than the national minimum wage and are considered relatively good in the current
                                                     Tajikistan labor market. The implementation of the Labor Management Procedure addresses labor issues.
                                                     Resettlement sites
                                                     The location of resettlement sites is not considered controversial. Resettlement sites are developed to include high-quality new infrastructure, which is also
                                                     available to the local community.
                                                     Individual grievances have been noted amongst resettled communities due to delays in compensation payments and the provision of land-use certificates,
                                                     although this is not a collective grievance. The implementation of the Resettlement Action Plans (RAPs) and Livelihood Restoration Plans (LRPs) will
                                                     address the issue of household grievances regarding resettlement.
                                                     This Major Event type is therefore scoped out of further assessment from a Major Events perspective.

 Technological     Societal          Famine           Tajikistan came close to famine in the early 2000s due to the aftermath of the civil war and a severe drought, however economic conditions and food          N
 or Manmade                                           production have improved significantly in recent decades. Food insecurity, however, remains an issue in the country, which increases the risk of famine
 Hazards                                              occurring. According to 2023 FAO/WFP Crop and Food Security Assessment Mission (CFSAM) to the Republic of Tajikistan, about 1.56 million people were
                                                      facing acute food insecurity, nearly 50,000 of whom were severely food insecure. However, there has been a reduction in households that experience acute
                                                      food insecurity, from 20% in 2023 to 16% in 2024.
                                                      Tajikistan is a highly agricultural economy, where agriculture comprises 22% of GDP and employs 60% of the domestic workforce. Many people also
                                                      partake in small-scale personal subsidiary farming and have the right to use common pasture. However, 75% of commercial foodstuffs, and 50% of
                                                      agricultural inputs such as seeds, seedlings, animal breeds, fertilizers, and farm equipment, are imported. Tajikistan therefore remains vulnerable to
                                                      fluctuations in international food markets. The Project area is also relatively poor, with 19% of household survey respondents indicating that they had
                                                      insufficient money for food.
                                                      The Project will not cause famine. There may be localized impacts on food production and prices due to the loss of agricultural land in the flooding zone,
                                                      however this will be mitigated by the measures outlined in Volume 1, Chapter 17: Socio-economics, as well as reduced population in the Project-affected
                                                      area. Furthermore, in some cases individual households will become more food-secure, as the resettlement plots are of uniform size and therefore all
                                                      resettlers have access to plots large enough for personal food production. All resettlers retain the right to use pastureland if they choose.
                                                      Downstream riparian countries, specifically Uzbekistan, expressed concern over water availability and impacts on agriculture. However, the operational
                                                      modelling of the Vakhsh cascade assumes there is no change to the seasonal operating pattern for flows downstream of Nurek compared with the situation
                                                      that would exist in the No-Rogun case. As such, based on the maintenance of the stipulated operating pattern, operation of Rogun would not result in
                                                      impacts downstream. It is therefore considered that this Major Event type can be scoped out of further assessment from a Major Events perspective.

 Technological     Societal          Displaced       There are 69 settlements within the future footprint of the Project, and over 50,000 people are to be resettled. The progressive filling of the reservoir has       N
 or Manmade                          population      necessitated a staged program of resettlement for villages and settlements within the Flooding Zone. This program is conducted by DFZ and has been
 Hazards                                             divided into 5 phases covered by Resettlement Action Plans (RAPs). RAP 1 is already complete, while RAP 2 is in progress and is under revision as part of
                                                     this ESIA. A Resettlement and Livelihood Restoration (RLRF) has also been prepared which will guide the preparation of RAPs 3, 4 and 5. Each RAP is
                                                     accompanied by a Livelihood Restoration Plan (LRP). PAPs are compensated for lost assets and infrastructure, and evidence to date shows a general
                                                     improvement in living conditions after resettlement and no significant tensions with host communities.
                                                     This Major Event type has been scoped out as impacts from resettlement are fully managed by the RAPs and LRPs.

 or Manmade        urban             accident        further assessment from a Major Events perspective.

 Technological     Industrial and    Major           There are no major accident hazard pipelines within the Project AoI.                                                                                                N
 or Manmade        urban             accident
                                                     Line D of the Central Asia–China Gas Pipeline is planned to run through Tursunzoda and Faizobod but is not yet operational. Therefore, this Major Event
