 Technological     Industrial and    Nuclear         Tajikistan currently has no operational nuclear reactors. There are strong radiation sources on Tajik territory because of Soviet-era industrial applications. In   N
 or Manmade        urban                             its 2012 Environmental Performance Review of Tajikistan, the United Nations Economic Commission for Europe announced that approximately 54.8 million
 Hazards           accidents                         tons of radioactive waste from Soviet-era uranium mining activities (mostly process residue tailings) are housed in unsecured facilities – many of them near
                                                     the large city of Khujand, approximately 170km north of the Project. The United Nations and the International Atomic Energy Agency have expressed
                                                     concern over the detrimental impact of this unsecured waste on the environment and ecology of Tajikistan. In response, in July 2014, the leadership of
                                                     Tajikistan adopted a national concept on the rehabilitation of uranium tailings during the ten-year period from 2014 to 2024.
                                                     This Major Event type has been scoped out as there are no nuclear facilities or radioactive waste storage facilities within 5km of the Project or any of the
                                                     resettlement sites.

 Technological     Industrial and    Fuel storage    Fuel is stored at the Rogun HPP construction site; however, this will be handled using standard control measures.                                                   N
 or Manmade        urban
                                                     Several petrol stations are in Navobod, which is within the Flooding Zone, however these will be removed before the area is flooded and the town resettled.
 Hazards           accidents
                                                     Potential risks associated with the removal of these facilities is identified in Volume 1, Chapter 12: Soils and Geology of this ESIA. The removal of these
                                                     facilities will be undertaken in line with the Good International Industry Practice outlined in the Environmental, Health, and Safety Guidelines for Retail
                                                     Petroleum Networks (2007).
                                                     Resettlement sites
                                                     No bulk fuel storage areas are present in the resettlement sites. Small amounts may be present in individual homes, although it is considered that these are
                                                     unlikely to be large enough to cause a Major Event. As such, the risk is no greater than for any other settlement and therefore this Major Event type has
                                                     been scoped out from further assessment from a Major Events perspective.

 Technological     Industrial and    Dam             Dam breaches in Tajikistan are rare, however a breach of the temporary dam at the Sargazon reservoir occurred in 1986 due to a large mudflow, resulting             Y
 or Manmade        urban             breaches        in the deaths of over 40 people. Overtopping of the temporary cofferdam at Rogun occurred in 1992.
                                                                                                                                                                                                                         Project site
 Hazards           accidents
                                                     There are 5 hydropower dams in a cascade below the Project on the Vakhsh River. These include the Nurek HPP, located 70 km downstream from the
                                                     Rogun HPP and, downstream of Nurek, the Baipaza, Sangtuda 1, Sangtuda 2 and Golovnaya Dams. There are currently no dams upstream of Rogun in the
                                                     Vakhsh cascade, although it is understood that there are plans to develop further upstream dams in the future.
                                                     There are no downstream communities between Rogun HPP and Nurek HPP. However, the failure of the Rogun dam could potentially trigger cascading
                                                     failure of the downstream dams. This could impact up to 1 million people living in the Vakhsh River basin across Tajikistan and riparian countries, as well as
                                                     causing widespread damage to infrastructure and agricultural production.
                                                     The design and construction of the dam has been carried out in line with the best available information on Major Hazards such as earthquakes and PMF.
                                                     The normal operation of the Project will also protect the downstream dams from PMF. The cascade EPRP is already in place to manage risks in the unlikely

 Major Event       Major Event       Major Event
 Group             Category          Type            Basis of Decision to Scope In / Out                                                                                                                                       Phase
                                                     event of overtopping, including communicating risks to downstream countries. This plan will be updated as the dam is raised. DSPoE is also in place to
                                                     provide expert insight and maximize safety.
                                                     External hazards which could potentially lead to overtopping and/or dam failure are discussed under ‘earthquakes’ and ‘landslides and mudslides.’ Failure
                                                     may also be due to internal factors and therefore is scoped in.
                                                     Resettlement sites
                                                     There are a total of 8 major dams in Tajikistan. 5 of these are located within the above-mentioned Vakhsh cascade, 2 are in the Sughd province in the north-
                                                     west (Farkhad, Kayrakkum), and the natural landslide dam Usoi is in GBAO. None of the resettlement sites are located within downstream areas that are
                                                     likely to be severely impacted if one of these dams fail.
                                                     This Major Event type is therefore scoped out of further assessment at the resettlement sites.

 Technological     Industrial and    Mines and       Tajikistan has large reserves of minerals including coal, gold and silver; however, none are present in the Project AoI. There are no mining activities (except           N
 or Manmade        urban             storage         for the aggregates quarry associated with the Rogun HPP construction site) in the Project AoI. No major mining sites are present near the resettlement
 Hazards           accidents         caverns         sites. Therefore, this Major Event type has been scoped out from further assessment from a Major Events perspective.
                                                     There are several caverns associated with the Project which are being used as part of the construction activities. These caverns are only used by workers
                                                     and as such, as workers are excluded from the assessment, do not require further consideration. Therefore, caverns associated with the Project have been
                                                     scoped out from further assessment.

 Technological     Industrial and    Fires           Fires could be initiated by construction related activities which impact areas adjacent to the construction activities. During construction, standard control             Y
 or Manmade        urban                             measures would be implemented by the appointed contractor to manage the risk of fire.
                                                                                                                                                                                                                               C
 Hazards           accidents
                                                     The construction of the dam involves storage, transportation and use of explosives. The potential risk associated with explosives will be assessed further
                                                                                                                                                                                                                               Project site
                                                     and this Major Event type has been scoped in.
                                                     Resettlement sites
                                                     House fires ignited within resettlers' homes could ignite and spread to host communities, however, this risk is no higher than for any other settlement.
                                                     Resettlement sites are usually located at some distance (>1km) from host communities).
                                                     There are no industrial sites near any of the resettlement sites, except the large aluminum plant located approximately 5km from Toichi, Tursunzoda. Given
                                                     the distance from the plant to Toichi, and the largely rural setting, it is unlikely that a fire at the plant would affect resettlers. Therefore, this Major Event type
                                                     has been scoped out from further assessment from a Major Events perspective.

 Technological     Transport         Road            Road traffic accidents are a major cause of death in Tajikistan. Between 2017 and 2022, total incidents decreased from 1,303 to 1,096, and mortality                      N
 or Manmade        accidents                         decreased from 431 to 416. However, road accidents were the fifth most common cause of death in 2019. During construction there will be an increase in
 Hazards                                             heavy construction plant and equipment on the local road network which may increase the risk of accidents however, this will only be on a temporary basis.
                                                     Risks due to road traffic are considered in Volume 1, Chapter 18: Community Health, Safety and Security and Volume 1, Chapter 22: Traffic and
                                                     Transport. Therefore, this Major Event type has been scoped out from further assessment.
                                                     Resettlement sites
                                                     A slight increase in heavy vehicle movements may occur during the construction of resettlement sites, however this will be temporary. Once resettlement is
                                                     complete the risk will be no greater than at any other settlement. Therefore, this Major Event type has been scoped out of further assessment from a Major
                                                     Events perspective.

 Technological     Transport         Rail            There are no railways within the Project AoI.                                                                                                                             Y
 or Manmade        accidents
                                                     Resettlement sites                                                                                                                                                        C
 Hazards
                                                     A railway track passes directly through the Toichi resettlement site in Tursunzoda. A buffer zone of >400m between the track and residential homes is
                                                     present on both sides of the track, and >300m between the track and nearby public paved road. However, satellite imagery shows evidence of informal
                                                                                                                                                                                                                               Tursunzoda District (Toichi)
                                                     paths created by people crossing the track on foot. The paved road crossing the track is not protected by barriers, traffic lights or any other safety features,
                                                     which potentially puts people crossing the track at risk from colliding with trains. The railway line passing through Tursunzoda runs services from Dushanbe
                                                     to Uzbekistan, which runs two passenger services per week (one outwards, and one return), as well as freight services. Rail transport is not extensively

 Hazards

 Technological     Transport         Aviation        There are 12 airports in Tajikistan - 10 civilian and 3 military. The nearest airfields are Kulob International Airport which is approximately 96km to the south   N
 or Manmade        accidents                         of the Project, Dushanbe International Airport which is approximately 110km west of the Project and Jirgatol Airport which is approximately 117km to the
 Hazards                                             east of the Project.
                                                     Resettlement sites
                                                     The nearest airport to any resettlement site is Bokhtar airport, 42km from the Danghara District site.
                                                     As there are no airports within the 13km study area this Major Event type has been scoped out from further assessment in this chapter.


 Technological     Pollution         Land            Volume 1, Chapter 12: Soils and Geology of this ESIA identifies that the contaminants associated with the construction and operation activities include            N
 or Manmade        accidents                         hydrocarbon spills from diesel generators, refueling bays, fuel tank farms, trucks and other equipment. It states that during all phases of the Project, soil
 Hazards                                             contamination can and should be prevented, especially as these contaminants will quickly enter the Vakhsh River and Rogun Dam, and potentially
                                                     groundwater resources. Some soil contamination from earlier Project works has been identified, which has the potential to impact groundwater, although
                                                     this is localized in nature and unlikely to cause a Major Event.
                                                     As this risk is considered in detail elsewhere, it has been scoped out from further assessment in this Major Events chapter.

 Technological     Pollution         Water           During construction water quality may be impacted by:                                                                                                              N
 or Manmade        accidents
                                                      Increase in suspended solids from construction activities such as excavations or quarrying, or from increases in erosion due to earthworks and other
 Hazards
                                                       related activities. This would increase the sediment and silt load downstream of any activities;
                                                      Potential runoff from oils, lubricants, or other chemicals and substances associated with construction activities and vehicles used;
                                                      Interaction of concrete with water may lead to changes in pH which could affect hydrogeochemical processes and/or aquatic life;
                                                      Blasting work may result in the presence of nitrogen-species such as nitrate or nitrite from use of explosives, which may be toxic to aquatic life; and
                                                      Potential of wastewater inputs to the catchment from construction camps and the workforce on site during construction.
                                                     These risks are considered in Volume 1, Chapter 13: Water and that concluded there will be no significant residual effects after mitigation. This risk is
                                                     therefore scoped out of further assessment from a Major Events perspective.
                                                     Resettlement sites
                                                     Resettlement is unlikely to cause a major water pollution event.
                                                     The presence of the TALCO aluminum plant in Tursunzoda is a potential source of a pollution source that may affect resettlers. Liquid waste is stored in
                                                     ponds adjacent to the plant, next to a watercourse.
                                                     Accidents at aluminum plants which affected nearby communities have occurred globally. The most significant incident occurred at the Ajka alumina plant in
                                                     Hungary in 2010. Liquid waste, red mud’, flooded nearby villages after the containment reservoir collapsed. The accident killed 10 and injured 150. About
                                                     40km of land and the River Danube were affected.
                                                     Satellite imagery of the TALCO site suggests its tailings ponds do not contain red mud. The 4 ponds are relatively small; 3 to the west of the plant, each
                                                     approximately 110x240m in surface area, and another to the south-each, 130x190m. All 4 ponds contain dark-colored liquid. TALCO does not disclose
                                                     details of its processes and the level of hazard posed by these ponds cannot be definitively stated. The ponds are next to a watercourse which could spread
                                                     contamination. However, the TALCO site is located 5km from Toichi and the watercourse flows away from the two Tursunzoda District resettlement sites.
                                                     Therefore, this Major Event type is scoped out.

 Technological     Utilities         Electricity     Instances of electricity failure (also referred to as power outages or blackouts) due to severe weather, insufficiency of generation relative to demand, or          N
 or Manmade        failures                          technical faults. In Tajikistan, blackouts are common, particularly in the winter when demand exceeds supply. The primary purpose of the Project is to
 Hazards                                             improve energy generation in Tajikistan and prevent this, as well as to generate excess for export.
                                                     The Project is currently being powered by electricity generated by the two temporary turbine units. There are also back-up diesel generators should there be
                                                     power failure.
                                                     The Project provides a large and increasing proportion of Tajikistan’s electricity, as well as energy for export. Failure of the Project could lead to large-scale
                                                     blackouts across Tajikistan, and potentially internationally. This will be prevented by routine maintenance to prevent technical issues which would impede
                                                     generation. Total failure due to other external events (e.g., destruction of the dam due to an earthquake) are discussed under other Major Event types. This
                                                     Major Event type is therefore scoped out from further assessment from a Major Events perspective.

 Technological     Utilities         Gas             Underground and above-ground gas transmission pipelines are not present across the Project study area. Therefore, this Major Event type has been                     N
 or Manmade        failures                          scoped out from further assessment.
 Hazards

 Technological     Utilities         Water supply    The operation of the Rogun HPP will be consistent with existing agreements for water allocation in the Vakhsh River. Issues relating to water supply for             N
 or Manmade        failures                          reservoir filling are discussed in Volume 1, Chapter 24: Transboundary Cumulative Impact Assessment of this ESIA.
 Hazards
                                                     Workers camps use water from the mains supply for cooking, washing etc. and bottled water for drinking. Mains water is provided from the Rogun City
                                                     Water Treatment Plant (WTP), which has insufficient treatment capacity relative to demand, leading to frequent water outages. Some of the worker camps
                                                     have a potable water treatment plant at the camp, although this is small and only serves a small proportion of the workforce. The failure of this infrastructure
                                                     is unlikely to lead to a Major Event, as the supply of bottled water in the area is generally sufficient to meet basic needs for the workforce and wider
                                                     population.
                                                     Resettlement sites
                                                     Problems with mains water supply are relatively common in Tajikistan. Water supply at resettlement sites is covered by the RAPs and are within the remit of
                                                     DFZ. There were reportedly widespread problems with water at resettlement sites earlier in the Project, however household survey data collected in 2023
                                                     shows that these problems have been largely resolved. It is also unlikely that water outages in a residential area would lead to a Major Event.
                                                     Therefore, this Major Event type has been scoped out from further assessment.

 Technological     Utilities         Sewerage        All contractors will be required to design and operate facilities in line with the agreed Project Standards. Each contractor’s sewage waste is managed in            N
 or Manmade        failures          system          ponds / sumps. Current waste management practices are below international standards. Liquid is generally infiltrated into the ground and solids are
 Hazards                                             reportedly removed by a hauler for treatment in the Rogun City Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP).
                                                     A contractor has installed a treatment system plant to treat sanitary water from Construction Camp 1, although this is very small and only serves a small
                                                     number of workers. It will be removed before flooding in 2026 and relocated for use elsewhere. The HPP also has a small plant that will be sufficient through
                                                     full operation.
                                                     Management measures for liquid waste are presented in Volume 1, Chapter 21: Materials and Waste and are therefore scoped out of further assessment
                                                     from a Major Events perspective.
                                                     Resettlement sites
                                                     The resettlement sites have no connection to a centralized sewerage system, with most households employing septic tanks. This is the norm in rural
                                                     Tajikistan, and the risk is therefore no higher than similar settlements.
                                                     This risk is therefore scoped out of further assessment from a Major Events perspective at both the Project site and resettlement sites.
                                                     Tajikistan inherited an enormous stockpile of aging ammunition, including large-calibre ordnance and other explosives, following the collapse of the Soviet
 Technological     Malicious         Unexploded                                                                                                                                                                           Y
                                                     Union. Due to its porous borders with Afghanistan, large quantities of poorly-secured small arms and light weapons and ammunition continue to present a
 or Manmade        attacks           ordnance
                                                     real threat to national and regional security. Tajikistan also has extensive landmine contamination along its southern, western, and northern borders from           C
 Hazards                             (UXO)
                                                     both its civil war in the 1990s and Soviet and more recent attempts to prevent border crossings by Islamist militants and narcotics traffickers. During its civil
                                                     war fought from 1992 to 1997, Tajikistan’s Central Rasht Valley region was heavily contaminated with landmines and UXO.
                                                     Tajikistan is successfully managing aging stockpiles of weapons and ammunition. The nation has also been adept at clearing landmines and other explosive             Tursunzoda District (Toichi,
                                                     hazards along its borders and within the central Rasht Valley region.                                                                                                Namuna)

                                                     To date and on the Rogun HPP construction site no UXO have been found to be present, however, there is the risk that landmines and/or other explosive                Nurobod District (Darband,

                                                     Resettlement sites
                                                     The risk of encountering unexploded ordnance is significant in some resettlement sites. The Nurobod District and Rogun District sites are located within the
                                                     Rasht Valley which was heavily impacted during the Tajik civil war in the 1990s (U.S. Department of State). The Tursunzoda District Toichi and, especially,
                                                     Namuna sites are located close to the Uzbek border (15km and 1km respectively), which was affected by land mines planted to deter cross-border attacks
                                                     by the Islamic Movement of Uzbekistan. The two states are cooperating on de-mining efforts, which are ongoing, although the level of progress has not
                                                     been officially shared. Mined areas are demarcated by the GoT, although the presence of mines outside of these areas cannot be ruled out. Resettlement
                                                     sites, and, in some cases, associated agricultural areas are constructed on greenfield land.
                                                     UXO risk is much lower in the Faizobod District and Danghara District sites.
                                                     This Major Event type is therefore scoped in only at the Tursunzoda District, Nurobod District and Rogun City District sites.
                                                     Extremists remain interested in Chemical, Biological, Radiological and Nuclear (CBRN) materials, however alternative methods of attack such as employing
 Technological     Malicious         Attacks:                                                                                                                                                                             N
                                                     firearms or conventional explosive devices remain far more likely. The main terrorist risk in Tajikistan arises from Islamist militants operating across the
 or Manmade        attacks
                                     Chemical        Afghan border, where terrorist groups have generally focused on conventional weapons attacks on Tajik military targets. Historical use of CBRN weaponry
 Hazards
                                                     by terrorists has been in closed, densely occupied structures (underground, buildings) or targeted at specific individuals. The Project has security in place to
                                     Biological
                                                     prevent non-authorized personnel from accessing the Project site. In addition, a Security Management Plan is currently under development as part of this
                                     Radiological    ESIA.
                                                     Such attacks may also occur during wartime. It is considered highly unlikely that a major war in which combatants employ CBRN weapons will occur in
                                     Nuclear         Tajikistan. The only one of Tajikistan's neighbors with this capability is China, with which Tajikistan maintains good relations. The 2021-2 conflict with
                                                     Kyrgyzstan (which possesses no CBRN weapons) was limited to conventional weapons attacks in border regions. Such attacks in wartime have also
                                                     historically focused on densely populated areas, unlike the Project area or resettlement sites.
                                                     This risk is therefore scoped out on the basis that: potential malicious attackers within Tajikistan do not have the capability to carry out such attacks and are
                                                     unlikely to gain this capability in the foreseeable future; attackers with the ability to carry out such an attack are unlikely to target the Project; and the
                                                     Security Management Plan will manage this risk. Therefore, this Major Event type has been scoped out from further assessment.

 Technological     Malicious         Transport       Potential systems would include (but are not limited to) railways, buses, passenger ferries, cargo vessels and aircraft. The Project will not impact any major       N
 or Manmade        attacks           systems         transport systems. The reconstructed roads and bridges are unlikely to be a target for malicious attacks due to the low number of exposed targets.
 Hazards
                                                     Resettlement sites
                                                     The resettlement sites do not include the construction of transport infrastructure beyond local roads.
                                                     The Tursunzoda site is adjacent to a railway, however there is no station and services in Tajikistan are highly irregular. This remote section of rural railway is
                                                     unlikely to be a target for malicious attacks. Therefore, this Major Event type has been scoped out from further assessment.

 Technological     Malicious         Crowded         The Project does not fall within the definition of a crowed place, i.e., pedestrian routes and other thoroughfares as well as sports arenas, retail outlets and      N
 or Manmade        attacks           places          entertainment spaces.
 Hazards
                                                     Resettlement sites
                                                     The resettlement sites are small settlements of a rural or pre-urban nature which contain no areas meeting the definition of a crowded place.
                                                     Therefore, this Major Event type has been scoped out from further assessment.

 Technological     Malicious         Cyber           Cyber-attacks occur almost constantly on key national and commercial electronic information, control systems and digital industries. Tajikistan is considered        N
 or Manmade        attacks                           an attractive target for cyberattacks as its defenses against such an attack are thought to be weak.
 Hazards
                                                     The high profile and critical importance of the Project mean that it could, in theory, be a target. Notwithstanding this, the Project is not considered to be more
                                                     vulnerable to attack than similar infrastructure installed and running on other existing hydropower plants in Tajikistan. Cyber security will also be considered
                                                     in the Security Management Plan which is under development as part of this ESIA.
                                                     Resettlement sites
                                                     The resettlement sites have no greater reliance on digital technology than any other residential settlement.
                                                     Therefore, this Major Event type has been scoped out from further assessment.

 Technological     Malicious         Infrastructure   The risk of terrorism in Tajikistan is largely associated with the Afghan border. Armed incursions by terrorist groups across the Afghan border are a periodic   N
 or Manmade        attacks                            occurrence. The main organizations of concern for the Tajik state are ISIS-K and Jamaat Ansarullah, which aim to overthrow the GoT and establish an
 Hazards                                              Islamist regime, and stage attacks across the Tajikistan-Afghanistan border. Active recruitment by terrorist groups (particularly ISIS-K) targeting Tajik
                                                      citizens has increased in recent years.
                                                      The GoT actively participates in domestic and international counter-terrorism efforts and stated that 31 terrorist actions were prevented in 2022.
                                                      Project site
                                                      Malicious attacks on electrical infrastructure have been known to occur globally, for example a series of small-scale attacks in the USA. Such attacks have
                                                      not occurred in Tajikistan. Prevention of access by unauthorized personnel will be within the scope of the Security Management Plan which is under
                                                      development as part of this ESIA and is therefore scoped out of further assessment in this chapter.
                                                      Resettlement sites
                                                      The resettlement sites do not contain critical infrastructure that would be a likely target for malicious attacks. Therefore, this Major Event type has been
                                                      scoped out from further assessment.

 Technological     Engineering       Bridge failure   There are existing bridges present in the Project area which will be removed as a result of inundation.                                                          N
 or Manmade        accidents and
                                                      12 new bridges will be constructed as part of the Project, including the approximately 900m package 3 bridge at Darband. These bridges are funded as
 Hazards           failures
                                                      separate Projects and are therefore scoped out of further assessment in this ESIA.
                                                      No bridges are planned at resettlement sites.

 Technological     Engineering       Flood            Upstream of the Project, the Vakhsh river has formed extensive floodplains in the wider sections of the valley, which provides natural flood mitigation. The     N
 or Manmade        accidents and     defense          Project itself has been designed to withstand PMF. The Project-site and cascade EPRPs are also in place to manage and respond to flooding and are being
 Hazards           failures          failure          continually updated as the Project progresses.
                                                      As outlined in Volume 1, Chapter 3: Project Description of this ESIA, diversion tunnels (DTs) have been constructed to divert and discharge flood water
                                                      from the construction area. Four DTs are currently operational. Two High Level Outlets (HLOs) are also operational, although they will eventually lose
                                                      functionality due to siltation and be replaced by Remote Tunnel Spillways (RTS). The RTS have a higher discharge capacity than the HLOs and are
                                                      designed to cope with PMF.
                                                      An updated hydrological study commenced in September 2024. This study is designed to take account of the most recent climate change predictions and
                                                      models in line with Good Practice. Where any changes to the design or operation of the project are identified as a result of potential hydrological changes,
                                                      these will be incorporated during the construction phase as appropriate through a Management of Change Process which is a requirement of the ESMP.
                                                      This risk is therefore scoped out of further assessment.

 Technological     Engineering       Mast and         There are no towers or masts near the Project or being built as part of the Project. Some small towers associated with utilities will be constructed at the      N
 or Manmade        accidents and     tower            resettlement sites (e.g., low-voltage overhead lines, mobile phone towers), but the risks are no higher than for any other residential settlement. Therefore,
 Hazards           failures          collapse         this Major Event type has been scoped out from further assessment.

 Technological     Engineering       Property or      The Project involves demolition works including taking down buildings, roads and bridges. This is managed in line with national standards by professional        N
 or Manmade        accidents and     bridge           contractors. Resettlers are allowed to retain salvage materials after demolition; risks associated with this, including management of asbestos, are discussed
 Hazards           failures          demolition       in Volume 1, Chapter 18: Community Health, Safety and Security of this ESIA.
                                     accidents
                                                      Resettlement sites are located on greenfield land and no demolition will take place for their construction.
                                                      This risk is therefore scoped out of further assessment from a Major Events perspective.

 or Manmade        accidents and     failure / fire
                                                      Diversion tunnels have been excavated at the left bank, crossing the valley under the dam area and continuing at the right bank. The downstream part will
 Hazards           failures
                                                      be used as tailrace tunnels during operation.
                                                      Six independent headrace tunnels and penstocks which will guide the water from the intakes to the powerhouse at the left bank.
                                                      Several access tunnels to the powerhouse as well as transformer caverns, gate chambers etc.

        below. These tables are a record of all potential Major Events considered as part of the assessment
        process. Where necessary, the assessment has identified precautionary mitigation actions in
        addition to the management measures already integrated into the design and execution of the
        Project to ensure that risks are managed to be ALARP.

Management measures in place: Reservoir slope stability addressed in the geotechnical design. DSPoE missions, seismicity studies, and a slope stability assessment (see Annex 13.2 of this ESIA) are in place to identify
and provide mitigation measures for these hazards. The Cascade EPRP is in place to manage risks in the event of overtopping. Water levels are monitored by the Project to detect anomalies indicative of upstream
landslides.
Could this constitute a Major Event in the context of the Project? Yes
Justification: Could cause loss of life or permanent injury which requires ongoing disability support and/or damage to a receptor.
Is this ALARP with existing management measures? No
Justification: Risk could be further reduced with the implementation of mitigation measures.
Mitigation: The report "Stage 1 Dam. Dam Break Analysis - Methodology, Simulation and Results" provides the basis of the “Main Dam Phase 1 - 1200 Emergency Action Plan” (2021) that details the Early Warning System
to be implemented.
Is this ALARP with the application of the identified mitigation measures? Yes
Justification: Considered to be ALARP if all mitigation measures outlined are correctly implemented.

Management measures in place: The suitability of resettlement sites is considered in accordance with regulatory requirements. Before land acquisition for the development of resettlement sites can commence, the site
must be approved as safe and suitable by the site selection committee and subject to required approvals from appropriate governmental agencies (e.g. the Ministry of Environmental Protection should confirm that the site is
not vulnerable to, and will not cause, significant environmental risks). Site selection is also aligned with central government planning for social and economic development. Sites are selected in areas where additional
population will not cause strain on local resources, where earthquake, landslide and climate-related risks are low. Reservoir slope stability has been addressed in the geotechnical design to the Stage 1 dam design (1100
masl). The dam break assessment for FSL (1290 masl) will consider the risk of a landslide of sufficient scale to generate a wave which could cause overtopping during construction. Table 3-15: Reservoir Inundation Levels
of Volume 1, Chapter 3: Project Description outlines the proposed incremental filling of Rogun reservoir. Once the dam break assessment report is available this risk will be reviewed. DSPoE missions, seismicity studies,
and a slope stability assessment (see Volume 2, Annex 13.2: Reservoir Slope Stability of this ESIA) are in place to identify and provide mitigation measures for these hazards.
Could this constitute a Major Event in the context of the Project? Yes
Justification: Could cause loss of life or permanent injury which requires ongoing disability support and/or damage to a receptor.
Is this ALARP with existing management measures? No
Justification: The Cascade EPRP does not address risks to upstream communities.
Mitigation: The Project must liaise with appropriate authorities (local authorities, Committee for Committee of Emergency Situations and Civil Defence of Tajikistan) to develop plans for settlements which would be impacted
in a reasonable worst-case scenario. Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) to be developed for early warnings to, and evacuation and safe re-occupation of at-risk settlements. This must be incorporated into the Cascade
EPRP. Provision of training to workers and other stakeholders outlining the response to various emergency scenarios including, safe evacuation, inspection of structures after a hazard event, and re-occupancy.
Is this ALARP with the application of the identified mitigation measures? Yes
Justification: Considered to be ALARP if all mitigation measures outlined are correctly implemented, including implementation of mitigation measures identified during ongoing studies.

Management measures in place: Mine clearance works at the border have been undertaken by the governments of Tajikistan and Uzbekistan.
Could this constitute a Major Event in the context of the Project? Yes
Justification: Could cause loss of life or permanent injury which requires ongoing disability support.
Is this ALARP with existing management measures? No
Justification: Risk could be further reduced with the implementation of mitigation measures.
Mitigation: Carry out a UXO study at the affected sites and communicate the results to the public. Namuna and Darband are to be prioritized, as in-migration of new households continues, and more ground disturbance will
occur. If UXO is identified, DFZ are to ensure it is safely removed by trained professionals. UXO study to be carried out at any new greenfield sites in the affected Districts, including the area within Nurobod District dedicated
for additional resettlement plots, including any agricultural areas. No construction or farming to commence until the area has been confirmed to be free of UXO.
Is this ALARP with the application of the identified mitigation measures? Yes
Justification: Considered to be ALARP if all mitigation measures outlined are correctly implemented.

21.7. SUMMARY
21.7.1. This Chapter has identified Major Events relevant to the Project and determined which are ALARP
        considering measures already in place, and which require mitigation. It is deemed that the risks are
        anticipated to be ALARP if the identified mitigation is implemented.

22      TRAFFIC AND TRANSPORT

22.1    INTRODUCTION
22.1.1. This chapter presents the assessment of potential impacts of the Project activities on traffic and
        transport, in line with the requirements of the WB ESS1: Assessment and Management of
        Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts, ESS3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention
        and Management and ESS4: Community Health, Safety and Security.
22.1.2. A comprehensive description of baseline conditions is presented in Volume 2, Annex 7.1: Baseline
        Conditions Report and a summary is provided in Volume 1, Chapter 7 of this ESIA. An
        assessment of the potential Project impacts on traffic and transport and receptors presented in
        Section 22.5. Project impacts relating to Severance are presented in Volume 2, Annex 17-3:
        Severance Impact Assessment of this ESIA. Where necessary, mitigation measures for the
        avoidance of impact, impact minimization and/or long-term management are included in Section
        22.6.
22.1.3. Previous assessments of the Project have been undertaken including an ESIA completed in 2014.
        POYRY (2014) ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT FOR ROGUN
        HYDRO POWER PLANT
22.1.4. The 2014 ESIA report discusses the findings of assessment of the impact of traffic movements
        within the Project site and the Project area of influence (AoI). The conclusions from the assessment
        focused on traffic and transport are summarized as:
        ▪   There is increased traffic passing through the villages travelling to the project site, and prior to
            the construction of the M41 Obigarm-Nurobod Road Realignment Project, there was an
            increased reliance on the local road network in the villages for logistical routes to the project site.
            The 2014 ESIA recommended a Traffic Management Plan to develop procedures for on-site
            traffic movements.

22.2    LEGISLATIVE FRAMEWORK, POLICY AND GUIDANCE
        The Code of Road Transport of 2020, Republic of Tajikistan (Law No. 1689)
22.2.1. The Code of Road Transport of 2020, Republic of Tajikistan (Law No. 1689) provides regulations
        concerning the transportation of goods and passengers and the transportation of hazardous,
        oversized and heavy cargo.
22.2.2. Article 3 of the Road Transport Code sets out the road transport activities that are subject to
        mandatory licensing, this includes:
           outlining a requirement for a permit for transporting hazardous, oversized or perishable goods;
            ensuring compliance with the specific safety and regulatory standards
           licensing requirements are designed to ensure the safety, reliability, and efficiency of road
            transport services within Tajikistan.
22.2.3. Article 5 of the Road Transport Code sets out the responsibilities and requirements for transporting
        hazardous materials in emergency response activities as directed by state bodies.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: DATE                                                   June 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                   Page | 22-1
         Law on Road Safety of the Republic of Tajikistan (2020)
22.2.4. The Law on Road Safety aims to enhance road safety and reduce accidents, as follows:
            The law mandates a range of measures to ensure the safety of road users, including the
             installation of traffic signs, signals, and road markings. It also includes provisions for the
             maintenance and improvement of road infrastructure.
            The law sets out requirements for driver qualifications, vehicle standards, and regular vehicle
             inspections to ensure that all vehicles on the road are safe and roadworthy.
            The law provides for strict enforcement of traffic rules and regulations. It includes penalties for
             violations such as speeding, driving under the influence, and not wearing seat belts.
            The law emphasizes the importance of public awareness and education campaigns to promote
             safe driving practices and inform road users about traffic rules.
         INTERNATIONAL LENDER REQUIREMENTS
22.2.5. World Bank ESS1 sets out the borrower’s responsibilities for assessing, managing and monitoring of
        environmental and social risks and impacts associated with each stage of a project.
22.2.6. World Bank ESS4 requires the avoidance or minimization of potential traffic and road safety risks to
        workers, affected communities and road users throughout the Project lifecycle. Where appropriate,
        measures and plans will be developed to address impacts identified by the assessment.
22.2.7. In general, all lender Environmental and Social Standards/Guidelines, including ADB and AIIB are
        broadly consistent with the World Bank ESS.
22.2.8. Typical Good International Industry Practice (GIIP) is set out in the World Bank EHS General and
        associated Sector Specific Guidelines. Key measures relating to Traffic and Transport are:
            Section 3.4 of the General EHS Guidelines provides detailed GIIP for managing road safety
             initiatives with the goal of preventing traffic accidents and minimizing injuries suffered by project
             personnel and the public.
            Section 3.5 of the General EHS Guidelines provides a detailed description of the transportation
             of Hazardous Materials with compliance to local laws and international requirements.
22.2.9. World Bank EHS Guidelines on Toll Roads set out examples of GIIP tailored to address project
        hazards and risks
            Section 1.3 provides detail on the community health safety issues during construction of roads at
             large construction sites, with particular focus on pedestrian safety, traffic safety and emergency
             preparedness.
         Institute of Environmental Management and Assessment (IEMA) ‘Environmental Assessment
         of Traffic and Movement’ Guidelines (2023)
22.2.10. The Institute of Environmental Management and Assessment (IEMA) ‘Environmental Assessment of
         Traffic and Movement’ (2023) (EATM) Guidelines have informed this assessment. The Guidelines
         provide information on the identification of receptors sensitive to traffic and transport effects, set out
         a list of effects that should be assessed and suggest thresholds for assessing the impact on the
         existing road network.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: DATE                                                   June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                   Page | 22-2
22.3     ASSESSMENT METHODOLOGY
         STUDY AREA
22.3.1. The Institute for Environmental Management and Assessment: Environmental Assessment of Traffic
        and Movement (IEMA EATM) Guidelines on the identification of sensitive locations, defined as
        receptors that are sensitive to traffic include: hospitals, places of worship, schools and historic
        buildings. The Guideline also identify the need to consider the adequacy of footways and road
        crossing facilities.
22.3.2. The identification of the study area and sensitive receptors should be informed by the location of
        populations, facilities, amenities and attractions surrounding the Project. Consideration should be
        given to groups of:
         ▪   People at home;
         ▪   People at work (e.g. construction workers);
         ▪   Vulnerable populations (e.g. older people, younger people);
         ▪   Locations with concentrations of vulnerable users (e.g. schools, hospitals);
         ▪   Routes with existing safety concerns; and
22.3.3. The Area of Influence (AoI) for the traffic and transport study for this ESIA includes:
            M41 Highway between Dushanbe-Obigarm (approx. 97.5 km)
            M41 Highway between Obigarm-Nurobod (approx. 75 km)
            920 m Long Bridge at Darband connecting to M41 Obigarm-Nurobod (and temporary bridge)
            Local Road (Pц001) between Obigarm-Rogun City (approx. 12 km)
            Left-Bank Road between Roghuni Bolo-Darband (approx. 55 km)
            Right-Bank Road between Darband-Kamarob Valley-Gharm.
            Roads at Rogun HPP construction site
            New local roads at resettlement sites




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: DATE                                             June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                             Page | 22-3
        RECEPTOR SENSITIVITY
22.3.4. The guideline approach to assessing the sensitivity of the receptor is to assign the receptor to the
        nearest highway link and examine the relationship of the receptor to the highway link to understand
        the sensitivity to change.
22.3.5. The range of receptors that could be sensitive to changes in traffic conditions include:
        ▪     Pedestrians (including workers at the Rogun construction site)
        ▪     Motorists and freight vehicles;
        ▪     Highways with inadequate infrastructure and high-risk junctions; and
        ▪     People living in communities adjacent to roads in rural and urban areas.
22.3.6. The categories of receptor sensitivity used in this assessment are described in Table 22-1.

        Table 22-1 – Receptor Sensitivity Categories

            Sensitivity    Definition and Examples
            Categories

            High           Communities for which no alternative transport routes are easily available.
                           Transport infrastructure in residential areas or with adjacent land-uses connected to
                           vulnerable groups and/or locations e.g., hospitals, schools, nursing homes or similar
                           facilities.
                           Transport infrastructure with physical restrictions (e.g., limited width, poor visibility) that
                           would not accommodate Project traffic.
                           Transport infrastructure without non-motorised user infrastructure where non-motorised
                           user flows are anticipated, for example within residential areas.




            Medium         Communities for which a limited number of alternative transport routes are available.
                           Transport infrastructure in commercial or industrial areas.
                           Highways and junctions that may experience congestion.
                           Transport infrastructure with physical restrictions such as narrow footways.


            Low            Communities for which convenient alternative transport routes are available.
                           Transport infrastructure that is physically capable of accommodating Project traffic
                            Adequate separation of non-motorised and motorised road users
                           Receptors located sufficiently distant from the highway and junctions affected by
                           changes in traffic volumes.

        IMPACT MAGNITUDE
22.3.7. The categories of traffic and transport impact magnitude and their respective descriptions are
        described in Table 22-2. The category descriptions are informed by the Guideline criteria, the traffic
        and transport conditions described in Volume 2, Annex 7.1: Baseline Conditions Report of this
        ESIA and as seen during in-country visits, to distinguish the magnitude of change for the likely traffic
        and transport impacts.



        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                                 PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: DATE                                                              June 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                              Page | 22-4
Table 22-2 – Impact Magnitude Categories

 Magnitude        Definition / Examples

 Large            Major increase in traffic flow or HGVs
                  Permanent effect on travel times that cannot be mitigated through non-government
                  authority intervention (i.e., strategic improvements are required such as road upgrades,
                  traffic calming measures alternative transport provision requiring governmental support).
                  Deterioration in transport network, requiring government authority maintenance and/or
                  upgrades.
                  Permanent closure of roads or pedestrian routes, with high/pedestrian flows.
                  Unacceptable detrimental impacts on non-motorised users and highway safety,
                  particularly in areas where non-motorised user flows are high and there is not sufficient
                  separation of road user groups, vulnerable groups are present and existing highway
                  safety concerns are present.

 Medium           Moderate increase in traffic flow or HGVs
                  Permanent or long term (>12 months) effect on travel time which can be mitigated
                  without government authority intervention.
                  Deterioration in transport infrastructure conditions, requiring periodic and minor levels of
                  government authority maintenance.
                  Permanent closure of roads or pedestrian routes, with moderate pedestrian flows or
                  temporary closure of roads or pedestrian routes, with high pedestrian flows.
                  Detrimental impacts on non-motorised users and highway safety, potentially in areas
                  where non-motorised user flows are anticipated to be high and/or are anticipated to be
                  low without there is not sufficient road user separation, vulnerable groups are not
                  anticipated to be present and there are no existing highway safety concerns.


 Small            Minor increase in traffic flow or HGVs
                  Temporary effect (between 6 and 12 months duration) on travel time which can be
                  mitigated without government authority intervention.
                  Temporary closure of roads or pedestrian routes, with moderate or low pedestrian flows.
                  Deterioration in transport infrastructure conditions, which can be mitigated without
                  government authority maintenance.
                  Detrimental impacts on non-motorised users and highway safety, in areas where non-
                  motorised user flows are not anticipated and separation between road user types is
                  adequate, vulnerable groups are not anticipated to be present and there are no existing
                  highway safety concerns.

 Very small /     Negligible increase in traffic flow or HGVs.
 Negligible
                  Temporary effect (up to 6 months duration) on travel time which can be mitigated without
                  government authority intervention.
                  Detrimental impacts on non-motorised users and highway safety, in areas where non-
                  motorised user flows are not anticipated and separation between road user types is
                  adequate, vulnerable groups are not anticipated to be present and/or are sufficiently set
                  back and there are no existing highway safety concerns.
