            Magnitude     Definition / Examples

            Beneficial    New or upgraded transport infrastructure.
                          Reduction in traffic flows and/or HGV numbers


        SIGNIFICANCE OF EFFECTS
22.3.8. The overall significance of effects resulting from traffic and transport impacts is determined by the
        interaction of the categories assigned to receptor sensitivity and impact magnitude. The significance
        matrix provided in Volume 1, Chapter 5: ESIA Methodology of this ESIA shows the levels of
        interaction and is reproduced in Table 22-3.

        Table 22-3 – Significance Matrix
            Impact Magnitude     Receptor Sensitivity

                                 Low                       Medium                       High
            Beneficial           Positive                  Positive                     Positive

            Very Small           Negligible                Negligible                   Minor

            Small                Negligible                Minor                        Moderate

            Medium               Minor                     Moderate                     Major

            Large                Moderate                  Major                        Major

22.3.9. For this ESIA, major and moderate impacts are deemed to be significant, while minor impacts are
        not considered significant pre-mitigation.

22.4    SCOPE OF THE ASSESSMENT
22.4.1. Using the IEMA EATM Guidelines, the following potential traffic and movement impacts will be
        assessed:
        ▪     Severance of communities;
        ▪     Vehicle delay;
        ▪     Community safety;
        ▪     Road user and pedestrian safety; and
        ▪     Hazardous/large loads.
22.4.2. Traffic and movement from the Project can potentially impact human health, through impacts on air
        quality, water and noise. Impacts on these factors are considered in Volume 1, Chapter 8: Air
        Quality, Volume 1, Chapter 11: Noise & Vibration, and Volume 1, Chapter 13: Water.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: DATE                                              June 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                              Page | 22-6
22.5     POTENTIAL IMPACTS AND EFFECTS
22.5.1. The assessment of impacts considers the sensitivity of the receptors that have been identified, the
        magnitude of the impact and consolidates these factors to assess the significance of the effect.
        Adverse impacts during the construction phase include driver delay, risks to community safety,
        degradation of road conditions, road safety, and from the movement of hazardous and large loads.
        Impacts during operation would be confined to road condition and driver delay.

         CONSTRUCTION
22.5.2. As described in Chapter 3: Project Description, dam construction resumed in 2016 and is
        scheduled to be completed in about 2032. As the construction process nears completion, it is
        expected that construction-related traffic and transport activities will start to diminish.
         As described in Chapter 3: Project Description, and Chapter 7: Baseline Conditions, all traffic
         into and out of the site comes through the main gate or over a temporary construction bridge (the
         Sicharog bridge), which will be dismantled in late 2025 or 2026. In general, most Project-related
         traffic comes through the main gate and consists of vehicles bringing materials and goods to the site
         and workers being transported to and from the site. In addition, a small number of passenger
         vehicles and pedestrians from two villages on the left bank are allowed to cross the Sicharog bridge.
         Driver Delay
22.5.3. Driver delay occurs due to additional Project-related traffic on the road network delaying non-Project
        traffic. Additional delays caused by Project-related traffic are only likely to be significant if the traffic
        on the network is already at, or close to, capacity.
         M41 Highway Dushanbe-Obigarm
22.5.4. The primary route used by HGVs to deliver materials and goods to Rogun HPP is the M41 Highway
        between Dushanbe-Obigarm. This route is a 97.5 km section of national highway with a travel-time
        of approximately 2 hours from Dushanbe to Rogun HPP. This highway is a free-flowing single
        carriageway with varying pavement quality. It was upgraded and resurfaced in 2014 to
        accommodate the anticipated increase in construction traffic. Sections have been upgraded or
        improved in recent years.
22.5.5. On average, there is about 150 HGVs delivering materials and goods to the site daily, with the
        majority travelling from Dushanbe. According to the Ministry of Transport, the total number of
        vehicles per day is low for the capacity of the highway, which is reported to have an average daily
        traffic count of 3,738 vehicles in both directions.
22.5.6. As transport infrastructure is physically capable of accommodating Project traffic, the sensitivity is
        low. The magnitude of this impact is medium, as there will be a moderate increase in traffic flow or
        HGVs due to the scale of the Project, and therefore the significance of the effect is minor.
         Pц001 Local Road Obigarm-Rogun City
22.5.7. The road connection (known as ‘Pц001’) Obigarm-Rogun City is the main access road between the
        M41 highway and the site entrance gate at Rogun HPP. It is a single carriageway road with steep
        gradients and sharp turning sections and resurfaced in November 2024. This is the primary route for
        access to Rogun City and the site entrance gate and experiences heavy traffic, particularly on a shift
        changeover day.



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                       PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: DATE                                                    June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                    Page | 22-7
22.5.8. During shift changeover day (occurring every 15 days), there are high levels of traffic in Rogun City
        and at the site entrance gate. Dozens to hundreds of taxis and private vehicles drop and pick up
        workers at the site on shift-change day. While the local road has capacity to manage the flows of
        traffic, upon arrival at the site entrance gate, there is limited infrastructure to facilitate drop-off, pick-
        ups and parking.
22.5.9. Traffic flows through the site entrance gate on 15th September 2024 were counted and indicates
        movements of vehicles and pedestrians to/from the site on a shift changeover day.
            1664 cars (including Project vehicles, taxis and private cars entering/exiting the site)
            143 HGVs (including construction vehicles exceeding 3.5 tonnes);
            5671 pedestrians on foot, many of whom had been dropped off outside of the Site entrance gate.
22.5.10. Additional traffic with potential to delay non-Project traffic was observed at the start and end of
         workers shifts. In some cases, workers hitchhiked or relied on taxis for transport from the site to their
         off-site accommodation. Workers are driving personal vehicles and parking near the site entrance or
         in Rogun City. Due to a lack of parking, vehicles park on verges and on pedestrian sidewalks. This
         is exacerbated on shift changeover days due to the volume of traffic arriving and departing the site.
22.5.11. The magnitude of Project-related traffic and transport impact is medium and, as the sensitivity of
         receptors is medium, the significance of the effect is moderate.
         COMMUNITY SAFETY
22.5.12. A change in total volume of traffic, heavy vehicle composition, speed of vehicles, proximity of traffic
         to people and lack of road safety infrastructure can affect the safety of communities.
22.5.13. There are approximately 17,000 workers employed at the Rogun HPP site; to cater for the
         movement of workers between accommodation camps and construction areas, Lot 2 Contractor
         WeBuild operate a shuttle bus. However shuttle buses often stop in hazardous locations, including
         intersections and tunnels, creating risk for pedestrians and other vehicles. Additionally, other
         contractors do not operate transport to/from the site and accommodation.
22.5.14. There is no route reserved for pedestrians entering the main gate, so vehicle-pedestrian interactions
         are not segregated or controlled. At daily and 15-day shift changes, there are significant increases in
         vehicle and pedestrian traffic in the gate area. There is a village (Pasi Muhraho, population of 580 as
         of October 2023) situated within the construction site approximately 300 m from the site entrance
         gate accessible by an unpaved road (Rohe Mazor). The unpaved road is the only access point for
         future Construction Site 2 and the main dam area. Residents of Pasi Muhraho frequently drive and
         walk to Rogun City via the access gate, including lone children walking to/from school.
22.5.15. Once Construction Site 1 is inundated by about 2028, a Contractor will develop accommodations,
         offices, and other facilities in Construction Site 2. there will be an increase in vehicle movements on
         the road that passes through Pasi Muhraho
22.5.16. With transport infrastructure in residential areas connected to vulnerable groups and locations e.g.,
         schools, the receptor sensitivity is high, the impact is large, and the potential significance of the
         effect is major.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                         PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: DATE                                                      June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                      Page | 22-8
         ROAD CONDITIONS
         M41 Highway Dushanbe-Obigarm
22.5.17. Damage to road edges and general ‘wear and tear’ of the road may occur through increased traffic,
         especially HGV movements and the transport of any abnormal loads. Average daily traffic
         movements on the logistic routes between Dushanbe-Rogun is 3,738 total vehicles, of which 840
         were identified as HGVs (22.5% of total vehicles).
22.5.18. It is difficult to identify the extent to which damage to road edges and general wear and tear will
         occur, in addition, to the exact destinations of all HGVs on the logistic route. On average, there are
         150 HGVs delivering materials and goods to the site daily, with the majority travelling on M41
         Dushanbe-Obigarm. As the highway was upgraded in 2014, there is sufficient capacity to support
         traffic volumes, therefore; the magnitude of this impact would be small, and as the sensitivity of
         receptors is low, the significance of the effect is Negligible.
         ROAD SAFETY
22.5.19. The Tajik national mortality rate caused by road traffic accidents is 13.9 per 100,000 population
         (2021). IEMA EATM Guidelines do not recommend the use of thresholds for identifying significance
         of effects relating to the risk of accidents; but the magnitude of change to the mortality rate for road
         traffic accidents can be used as an indicator of road safety.
         M41 Highway (Dushanbe-Obigarm-Nurobod)
22.5.20. Despite the increase in traffic on M41 Highway from the Project, existing transport infrastructure
         between Dushanbe-Obigarm is capable of accommodating Project traffic.
22.5.21. Due to the inundation of the reservoir to 1290 masl, the existing M41 highway between Obigarm-
         Nurobod will be flooded before 2032, and a new realigned section, approximately 75 km, will be
         operational from 2025. The safety for rural roadside community facilities and dwellings will be greatly
         improved, with increased highway vehicle capacity and road safety infrastructure such as lighting,
         safety barriers and emergency stopping areas. In urban areas, there will be pedestrian walkways,
         designated crossing points, and reduced speed limits from 80 km/hr to 40 km/hr in urban areas.
22.5.22. Roads within resettlement areas will also be constructed to this standard with improved road safety
         infrastructure, pedestrian walkways, designated crossing points and speed limits of 40 km/hr. In
         addition, to bus / taxi stops with designated shelters for pedestrians to wait for transport.
22.5.23. With the in-progress separation of non-motorised and motorised road users, the receptor sensitivity
         is low. Considering improved transport infrastructure with new road safety infrastructure, the
         magnitude of the impact is beneficial, and the significance of the effect is positive.
         Rogun HPP Construction Site
22.5.24. Tunnels are used for vehicular access to various zones of the site. OHS audits have found that in
         tunnels lighting is poor, and reflective markers are missing in some high-risk areas, such as sharp
         bends. Flaggers have been assigned in some locations to direct and stop traffic, but they are at risk
         from passing traffic in some areas where they are positioned in areas of low visibility or in the
         roadway.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                    PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: DATE                                                 June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page | 22-9
22.5.25. Many vehicles in tunnels, including HGVs, exceed speed limits and overtake in tunnels or other
         unsafe locations. In addition, there are many roads where there are no barriers to prevent vehicles
         from leaving the roadway and falling down slopes.
22.5.26. At shift changes, workers sometimes walk long distances on site, and they often ride on construction
         equipment or in the back of pickup trucks or other vehicles not designed for passengers.
22.5.27. The magnitude of impact is large, and the potential significance of the effect is major.
         HAZARDOUS AND LARGE LOADS
22.5.28. The transportation of hazardous or abnormal loads by road is required for the Project construction
         phase.
22.5.29. As noted, about 150 HGVs come to the site each day. Relatively small amounts of hazardous
         materials are used, with concrete being the most significant.
22.5.30. In 2024, three “abnormal” (very large) loads of turbine parts were transported to the site. There are
         currently five abnormal loads planned for 2025, and the expectation of further abnormal loads for
         duration of the construction phase until 2032.
22.5.31. A special one-time permit is required to undertake transport of abnormal loads and requires
         authorization by the Ministry of Transport in Tajikistan. All abnormal loads are transported from
         Dushanbe to the site and require a police escort organised by each local authority. This includes
         road closures in sections along the M41 road with advance notification to communities
         communicated to Jamaats with a pre-determined route planned. Oversized loads must adhere to
         reduce speed limits, consideration of weight and dimensions of vehicles complying with bridge,
         overpass and tunnel restrictions. Abnormal load deliveries are done during hours of lowest traffic,
         during daylight hours only and in good weather conditions.
22.5.32. The receptor sensitivity would be considered medium and the magnitude of the impact is small, the
         significance of the effect is minor.

         OPERATION
22.5.33. The Project is partly operational at present, with generation capacity increasing to full capacity by
         2032, and actual generation reaching its capacity by 2038.
         ROAD CONDITIONS
22.5.34. Once the Project construction phase is completed in 2032, Project-related utilization of off-site roads
         will diminish substantially as the majority of the operational workforce of approximately 800
         personnel will live locally in Rogun City or nearby areas.
22.5.35. The proportion of HGV on the highway between Dushanbe and Rogun will be reduced to delivery of
         goods and materials for maintenance purposes, and it is likely that the proportion of HGV will
         decrease to less than 10% of the current level.
22.5.36. The impact of HGV on the highway condition will be minimal and the sensitivity or the receptor is
         low, the magnitude of the impact is small, and the significance of the effect is negligible.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: DATE                                                June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 22-10
         DRIVER DELAY
22.5.37. As the construction phase winds down, even the minor delays caused by construction-related traffic
         will be substantially reduced, including especially at shift changes due to the reduced workforce
         size.
22.5.38. The impact of operational traffic on the sensitivity of driver delay is low, the magnitude of the impact
         is very small, and the significance of the effect is negligible.

22.6     MITIGATION AND ENHANCEMENT MEASURES
22.6.1. Mitigation measures to be implemented for the Project as mitigation is summarised in Table 22-4.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: DATE                                                June 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page | 22-11
Table 22-4 – Mitigation Measures
 AM            Overview of Mitigation Measure
 AM22-1        Implementation of a Traffic Management Plan (TMP)
               ▪   A Traffic Management Plan will be implemented by Rogun JSC to outline requirements of each contractor. The site-wide plan will apply across
                   the site, including areas controlled by Rogun HPP. It will also require Contractors to develop detailed plans for management of traffic in their
                   respective areas and for their respective activities, to be approved by the Employer’s Representative/Project Management Consultant. Plans
                   have to be approved within six months of the effective date for EPC Contractors for Lots 1 and 2 and the Lot 4 “early works Contractors, and by
                   the time future EPC Contractors for Lots 1, 3A, and 4 mobilize to the site. Contractor Plans will be based on detailed traffic surveys and analysis
                   of their own traffic and transport practices and needs. The plans/procedures will include such requirements as:
                   o   Route and traffic planning for tother oversize loads, to include escorts, signals, and other measures to minimize the effects on other traffic.
                   o   Journey plans and details of the materials delivery routes.
                   o   Strict speed limits and speed control measures, including speed humps at the Pasi Muhraho Village near Construction Camp 2, and
                       introduction of 40 km/hr speed limit for the village (30 km/hr for a 300m stretch of road outside the local school). A dedicated pedestrian
                       walkway shall be constructed between the village and the entrance gate with pedestrian crossing points.
                   o   Enforcement of one-way traffic
                   o   Workers to be transported only in vehicles with safety belts, and required to use them
                   o   Drivers to be licensed by Contractors for the vehicles they will operate and trained on the requirements of the TMP.
                   o   All vehicles used by the Project will be fit for purpose based on an assessment of usage and be maintained in safe working order in line
                       with manufacturers’ specifications, UN vehicle safety regulations and all applicable national legal requirements.
                            o Daily pre-start checklists by operators
                   o   Intact glass in windshields and windows, working lights, reverse alarms, working brakes, treaded tyres, charged fire extinguisher, and other
                       safety features Identification and demarcation of approved access routes.
                   o   Segregation of incoming and outgoing traffic to reduce risk of collisions or overuse
                   o   Overtaking and pedestrian access in tunnels will be prohibited
                   o   Automated traffic signalling in some locations
                   o   Enforcement by senior managers, HSE Managers and specialists, ER/PMC, and Traffic Department



 AM22-2        Road Safety Campaign in Rogun City and Pasi Muhraho Village




Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                                                                                PUBLIC |
Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                                                                                June 2025

 AM            Overview of Mitigation Measure
               ▪   For all upgrade / improvement works to the proposed transport route, the Occupational Health, and Safety Plan and TMP will be adhered to. A
                   safety training programme will be in place to manage worker safety during works.

 AM22-3        Vehicle Identification
               ▪   To be implemented by all parties.
               ▪   All parties will assign and mark all of their vehicles and mobile equipment that come on the site with a unique letter-number identifier on front,
                   rear, and both sides. This will allow identification of offending vehicles, which can then be traced to the owner and the driver for further action.
                   Vehicles without such markings, such as delivery vehicles or visitors, will be assigned identifiers before entering the site.

 AM22-4        Development and Implementation of a Worker Transport Plan to establish a regular bus service for worker transportation at construction
               site
               ▪   The ER/PMC and Contractors will collectively develop and implement a Worker Transport Plan for all workers and contractors to provide
                   adequate transportation of workers between accommodations, the main gate, and workplaces, and between the gate and nearby worker
                   residences.
               ▪   This Plan, and Contractors’ individual sub-plans, will identify optimum stop locations, frequency of services, with consideration of shift start and
                   end times per contractors. Formal bus stops will be established at key locations, such as:
                   •   Site entrance gate
                   •   Rogun City
                   •   Accommodation blocks
                   •   Construction area 1
                   •   Construction area 2
                   •   Permanent offices
               ▪   The Plan will include a regular route schedule, with A capacity to accommodate peak demand during shift changes.

 AM22-6        Construction of parking facilities at the Rogun Construction Site Entrance
               ▪   Rogun JSC has announced a plan to develop one or more areas to accommodate short-term parking near the main gate to accommodation
                   shift-change traffic and reduce the need for roadside parking and pedestrian traffic.

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
This is the Transboundary Cumulative Impact Assessment for Rogun НРР ESIA prepared on behalf of the
Project Management Group for Energy Facilities Construction under the President of the Republic of Tajikistan
(PMG). Its aim is to discuss and evaluate cumulative impacts that may arise from interactions of Rogun HPP
with any other project in the wider project area, and, where required and possible, propose appropriate mitigation
measures. It is a part of the updated ESIA for Rogun HPP. Rogun HPP (the Project) is a major hydropower
project presently under construction on Vakhsh River, upstream of the already existing Nurek HPP. Once
finalised, Rogun HPP will consist of a 335 m high rockfill dam forming a reservoir with a surface of 159 km² and
a total volume of 13.3 km³, and a power house with an installed capacity of 3,780 MW.
This TCIA was prepared according to the applicable standards as defined in the TOR, mainly those of the
international agencies involved in funding Rogun HPP or projects closely related to it. Chapter 2 provides an
overview of applicable standards. The main applicable guideline is the Good Practice Handbook on Cumulative
Impact Assessment and Management (IFC 2013).
According to IFC (2013), “CIA is the process of (a) analysing the potential impacts and risks of proposed
developments in the context of the potential effects of other human activities and natural environmental and
social external drivers on the chosen VECs (Valued Environmental Components) over time, and (b) proposing
concrete measures to avoid, reduce, or mitigate such cumulative impacts and risk to the extent possible.”
Chapter 3 shortly outlines the approach chosen for this CIA. It is of importance to note that it does not only focus
on issues where the ESIA has shown that Rogun HPPP will actually have impacts and where therefore
cumulative impacts with other projects are expected or likely to materialize, but has to include aspects where
there is a potential for such cumulative impacts, and/or where relevant stakeholders have concerns that this may
happen. This means that the definition of the potential area of influence (AoI) as well as the selection of potential
receptors (also called VECs, Valued Environmental Components, or VCs, Valued Components) must not be too
narrow, potentially reaching beyond the AoI which was chosen for the ESIA for the project. This is discussed in
Chapter 3. The VC of main importance is water, and, more precisely, water availability, use and distribution in
the Amu Darya basin, this mainly since any project related to water resources in this vast area can potentially
have impacts on water availability in its downstream area. Other VCs which had to be included mainly because
of concerns of certain stakeholders are issues more or less directly related to biodiversity, namely, the situation
in the Aral Sea, fish migrations in the Amu Darya area, and a protected area called Tigrovaya Balka.
It is a usual practice, according to the relevant standards and guidelines, to prepare, usually as part of the ESIA
(Environmental and Social Impact Assessment), a CIA (Cumulative Impact Assessment) for the project in
question. Since in the case of Rogun HPP the main potential (cumulative) impacts would concern questions of
water availability and seasonal distribution in the downstream area of the project, and would therefore have its
main impacts on the downstream countries in the Amu Darya basin, in this case the CIA is necessarily also a
TCIA (Transboundary Cumulative Impact Assessment).
Per the definitions provided in several of the relevant guidelines and standards, a CIA has to include, in addition
to the main project for which it is being prepared, “impacts from other relevant past, present and reasonably
foreseeable developments as well as unplanned but predictable activities enabled by the project that may occur
later or at a different location.” Chapter 4 provides a list and a short description of these developments, which
can be existing structures, projects under construction, in the planning or conceptual stage, here usually called
“other projects”. These are:
    •    Nurek HPP (existing)
    •    Vakhsh HPP cascade d/s of Nurek (existing)
    •    Shurob HPP (early planning stage)
    •    HPP cascades u/s of Rogun HPP on Obihingou and Surkhob rivers (early planning stage)
    •    HPP cascade on Pyanj River (early planning stage)
    •    Irrigation in Tajikistan (planning stage)
    •    Qosh Tepa Canal, Afghanistan (under construction)
    •    International Highway (Vakhdat – Jirgital, M41) (under construction)
    •    500 kV TLs for Rogun (existing / under construction)
    •    Central Asia – China gas pipeline (early planning stage)

                                                                                                          PUBLIC
Page ii




The first seven in this list are projects directly related to water, while the remaining three are projects of different
types with potential cumulative impacts with Rogun HPP. One main environmental driver in addition to impacts
caused by any of these projects is climate change, which in relation to future developments concerning water
availability and distribution plays a major role.
Following the identification of these projects and the main VCs, Chapter 5 defines the AoI for the CIA.
Conclusions here:
    •     The Area of Influence for the CIA is the entire Amu Darya basin, with the Vakhsh catchment and
          the area downstream of the Pyanj-Vakhsh confluence, i.e. the Amu Darya, as the central parts.
    •     The Cumulative Impact Assessment (CIA) is necessarily also a Transboundary Impact
          Assessment (TBIA), called here Transboundary Cumulative Impact Assessment (TCIA)
The main part of the TCIA is the assessment as such of the cumulative impacts. This is done in Chapter 6,
whereby for each of the other projects listed above a description is provided, followed by identification and
discussion of the cumulative impacts, or an explanation why there will be no such impacts. The main conclusions
from the detailed discussions of potential cumulative impacts are summarised shortly in Chapter 7, which is
reproduced here:
Pipeline
Definition: The Central Asia-China Gas Pipeline is planned to pass north of Rogun reservoir. No detailed
information on this project, which is presently on hold, is available.
Interactions and Cumulative Impacts: cumulative impacts with Rogun can be excluded. One information
received is that there would be a possibility for the pipeline to follow more or less the alignment of the replacement
road. Should this be the case, then this latter might provide access to some construction sites of the pipeline, in
this way reducing the overall impact of these structures to some extent.
Conclusions: No relevant cumulative effects, no mitigation measures required.
Transmission Lines and Road Projects
Definition: This includes a number of linear structured of two different types, namely, 500 kV transmission lines
built for Rogun and/or as part of the CASA 1000 Project, and two road projects not related to Rogun HPP; see
Section 6.9; for details.
Interactions and Cumulative Impacts: There was a concern about a potential conflict between resettlement
villages (resettlement triggered by Rogun HPP and therefore part of the Project) and the land requirements for
these linear structures. The main concern was that in an extreme case this could mean that households that had
been resettled because of Rogun HPP would have to be relocated again because of one of these TL or road
projects. As explained in Section 6.9, the two roads in question have been built, and the alignment of the TLs,
as far as they have not yet been built as well, is known. There is no conflict with any of the Rogun-related
resettlement areas.
Conclusions: No cumulative impacts, no mitigation measures required.
New Main Road
Definition: The existing road along Vakhsh River in the area of the Rogun reservoir will be submerged during
the process of reservoir impoundment. Impoundment has started already and is planned to continue till
approximately 2037, when the reservoir will reach its FSL. The road needs to be replaced, and this replacement
consists of the following main parts: (i) new main road which provides access to the Garm region, to GBAO and
to the border with Kirgizstan, (ii) new main bridge crossing the river (or, finally, the reservoir), and (iii) local access
roads to villages on both banks of the river/future reservoir, which lose their present access. The new main road
as well as the new bridge are being developed as separate projects with separate financing and are therefore
considered as separate projects, to be included in the CIA.
Interactions and Cumulative Impacts: Construction of the new main road in the very rugged terrain along the
Northern (right) bank presents a risk of causing additional sediment input into the reservoir. However, since road
construction does not take place in the immediate vicinity of the reservoir, this risk is considered as real, but
minor and manageable. Another issue is accessibility: the existing road has already been affected by the
increasing reservoir water level, and in three places temporary roads had to be built for allowing access to the
upper parts of the valley. The ongoing resettlement process for Rogun depends on this access. Should the
reservoir interrupt the existing road before the new one is available, the resettlement process could not go on.

                                                                                                                PUBLIC
Page iii




On the other hand, reservoir filling would not be possible to continue if resettlement had not been done. A delay
in reservoir filling could be a problem for the Project since the amount of water that can be retained annually is
strictly limited by the relevant water allocation agreements. This means that there is a clear interdependence
between reservoir impoundment, resettlement and road construction.
Conclusions: Construction of the new main road is finalised, the road is open for traffic since early 2025; the
construction of the main bridge will follow later, reservoir impoundment has not reached that level yet. There is
no risk of blocked access to resettlement sites due to submersion of the old road.
Qosh Tepa Canal
Definition: The Qosh Tepa Canal is a project of a large irrigation canal which will derive water from Amu Darya
with the aim of irrigating up to 600,000 ha in Afghanistan. Only limited information on this project is available.
100 out of the planed 285 km are built. The canal is expected to derive about 5 km³ of water per year, but
depending on source it has the capacity to derive as much as 10 to 20 km³ annually; see Section 6.7 for details.
Interactions and Cumulative Impacts: The canal, located downstream of Rogun HPP, has no impact on this
latter, and Rogun HPP, which will be operated under the accepted water allocation rules, will not have any impact
on the canal. However, the canal will interfere with water availability in the downstream area (Turkmenistan and
Uzbekistan). Rogun HPP has the potential to make additional water available in exceptionally dry years, and the
Qosh Tepa canal and the irrigation schemes alimented by it would benefit from this.
Conclusions: Qosh Tepa canal is developed without any relation with Rogun HPP, this latter Is not connected
in any way with the canal, and no cumulative impacts are to be expected between Rogun HPP and the Qosh
Tepa Canal. However, since both projects are related to the very relevant question of water availability and water
allocation in the Amu Darya basin, the canal needs to be included in the discussions on water distribution. In this
context it also has to be pointed out that Afghanistan is not a member of the organisations regulating water
allocation in the basin.
Irrigation in Tajikistan
Definition: So far, Tajikistan does not make complete use of the water allocated to it by the relevant international
agreement, mainly Protocol 566; in an average year, about 1.2 km³ (corresponding to about 6% of the average
annual flow of Vakhsh River or 2% of average annual flow of the Amu Darya) are not used so far and are
therefore available further downstream. Tajikistan plans to use this water for filling Rogun reservoir, a process
which has started already. Once the reservoir is full, expected for 2037, Tajikistan will use this surplus water for
expanding its irrigation activities located downstream of Nurek.
Interactions and Cumulative Impacts: In any case, Tajikistan intends to make use of the full share of water
allocated to it under Protocol 566, and it is committed to remain within the limitations of the protocol also for filling
and operating Rogun HPP.
Conclusions: There will be no change in water availability in the downstream area which would not be within
the limitations defined by Protocol 566.
Pyanj HPP Cascade
Definition: There is a plan for developing a cascade of HPPs on the Pyanj River, which, if implemented
completely, would consist in 15 HPPs with a total capacity of 17,900 MW and a cumulative storage volume of
32.5 km³. None of these potential HPPs is in an advanced planning stage as yet, and it is very unlikely that any
one of these would be developed before the finalisation of Rogun HPP. Pyanj forms the border with Afghanistan,
which means that these projects would have to be developed as bi-national endeavours.
Interactions and Cumulative Impacts: Since Rogun HPP and the Pyanj cascade are on different rivers, there
would not be any direct interactions between the two. However, since they are both located in the headwaters
of the Amu Darya, adding this cascade (or relevant parts of it) to the system would increase the potential for
influencing water availability in the downstream area.
Conclusions: A potential cumulative impact of Rogun HPP with the Pyanj cascade could be an influence on
water availability in the downstream area, mainly in Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan, but also in Afghanistan, once
Qosh Tepa Canal built. This, however, would become manifest only at some not yet definable point in the future.
Still, the fact that such a situation might actually develop just points out the importance of basin-wide agreements
on water use.
HPP Cascade Upstream of Rogun

                                                                                                              PUBLIC
Page iv




Definition: As for Pyanj, there is a plan for developing two additional HPP cascades on Surkhob and Obihingou,
the two rivers which at their confluence form Vakhsh River; combined they would comprise 9 HPPs with a total
installed capacity of 5,800 MW and a total storage volume of 3.0 km³. As for the Pyanj cascade, none of these
is in an advanced planning stage, and it is not envisaged to go ahead with this development before Rogun HPP
will be commissioned and in operation.
Interactions and Cumulative Impacts: Adding these two cascades to the same river system (Vakhsh) would
clearly result in a number of cumulative impacts. Since the planned cascades are upstream of Rogun, this latter
would not influence them in any way. However, a few points have to be mentioned:
    •     Hydrology: Surkhob and Obihingou cascades would add additional storage volume to the Vakhsh
          cascade. It has to be assumed that they would be developed and operated following the same conditions
          as for Rogun HPP, i.e. without any additional seasonal shift of water and by filling them using the
          available water volume according to Protocol 566; therefore, they would not in any way negatively impact
          water availability along the Amu Darya. On the other hand, the additional storage once filled this would
          increase the potential for releasing water in extremely dry summers, which would definitely be a positive
          effect on water use along the Amu Darya.
    •     River habitat fragmentation: The lower reaches of Vakhsh River were already fragmented by Nurek dam
          and the HPPs downstream of Nurek, and Rogun HPP adds to this by creating an additional barrier and
          by fundamentally changing habitat conditions on the entire length of its reservoir. Presently, there is no
          information available on the value of Surkhob and Obihingou as aquatic habitat, but building these
          additional dams would add to river fragmentation.
    •     Floodplain submersion: The filling of Rogun reservoir will submerge two floodplains considered as
          natural habitats. Adding the Surkhob cascade would have a negative impact on the numerous
          floodplains along this river, a cumulative impact with Rogun HPP.
    •     Sedimentation: Additional dams upstream of Rogun would mean additional storage room for sediments.
          This would have the positive effect of reducing the amount of sediment reaching Rogun reservoir,
          thereby increasing the potential life span of Rogun HPP, and indirectly also the potential life span of
          Nurek HPP. The overall cumulative effect on the entire Vakhsh cascade would be positive.
Conclusions: There would certainly be cumulative impacts of Rogun and the two upstream cascades, neutral
(or slightly positive) concerning hydrology, negative concerning biodiversity and aquatic habitats, and positive
concerning sedimentation and overall life span of the Vakhsh cascade.
Shurob HPP
Definition: Shurob HPP is planned to be built once Rogun has reached the operation stage, with a dam and
power house with 850 MW installed capacity at the upper end of Nurek reservoir and a reservoir reaching the
toe of Rogun dam. It would use the water released from Rogun and the remaining head between the two large
HPPs; see Section 6.3 for details.
Interactions and Cumulative Impacts: Shurob cannot be built without Rogun, as otherwise its small reservoir
would be filled with sediment very quickly. Once Rogun in place, the water released from the dam will be almost
free of sediments. Shurob reservoir will impound the remaining 17 river km between Rogun HPP and Nurek
reservoir. However, this impact is considered as very minor, since this is a short and completely isolated river
stretch which in addition to that will be exposed to the regime dictated by the operation pattern of Rogun HPP,
which will at least to some extent be a peaking pattern. One additional at least marginally positive effect would
be that it will offer job occasions during construction, where at least some of the workers who will lose their job
at the end of construction activities for Rogun could find a new source of income. There would be no competition
for workers between the two, since there will be no overlap of the two construction phases.
Conclusions: Shurob, in all likelihood, will be a low-impact addition to the Vakhsh cascade, and it will contribute
substantially to electricity supply. No negative cumulative impacts are expected.
Vakhsh Cascade Downstream of Rogun
Definition: Rogun HPP will be a major addition to the cascade on Vakhsh River, which as of now consists of
one large storage scheme, Nurek HPP, and a cascade of smaller, basically ROR schemes downstream of it;
see Section 6.2 for details.




                                                                                                          PUBLIC
Page v




Interactions and Cumulative Impacts: Given the proximity of Rogun to the downstream cascade (same valley,
same river, short distance), potential cumulative impacts have to be analysed carefully. Main facts and
conclusions are the following:
   •     Hydrology, yearly flow pattern: as all large storage dams, Rogun has the potential to retain water during
         the high-flow season for using it for electricity generation in the low-flow, high-demand season. This
         would be to the detriment of downstream water users who have to rely on water for irrigation, with a high
         demand in the growing season. Tajikistan has committed itself to build and operate Rogun HPP
         respecting the agreed-upon rules for water allocation and water use among the riparian states in the
         Amu Darya basin, and especially Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan. This means that Tajikistan will not use
         more water than allocated to it under Protocol 566, and it will not shift water from summer to winter in
         addition to the 4.2 km³ already shifted by Nurek; this quantity corresponds to the live storage volume of
         Nurek and can be retained only after a winter reservoir drawdown to the Minimum Operation Level (MOL)
         of Nurek reservoir. The change in the cascade with Rogun HPP will be that yearly regulation will be done
         largely by Rogun, while Nurek reservoir will be kept at a more or less stable level throughout the year.
         Overall, this clearly means that for downstream water users the situation with Rogun HPP in place will
         not change; see also ESIA Vol. 1,. Ch. 13, Water.
   •     Hydrology, short-term fluctuations: in addition to seasonal storage, hydropower plants also have the
         potential for changing production very quickly (peaking regime). This allows the utility to very closely
         follow demand, which as a rule is not constant during the day, but usually follows a distinct pattern with
         times of high demand and times of low demand. This plasticity in production can be a problem for the
         river and its surroundings, including people living along and close to the river, since it can mean very
         marked short-term variations in river discharge and flow velocity, which has a potentially very negative
         impact on aquatic habitats and can be a safety risk for people. In the case of Rogun, however, it has to
         be pointed out that the area between Rogun dam and Nurek reservoir is not inhabited or used by people
         in any way and that therefore there is no safety risk for people, and that the value of this part of the river
         as habitat for aquatic organisms is very low (see ESIA Vol. 1, Ch. 15, Biodiversity). Presently, seasonal
         as well as daily peaking is done by Nurek. Once Rogun operational, this will be assumed by Rogun at
         least to some extent. Nurek will still have to do some daily peaking, following the operation pattern of
         Rogun. The smaller schemes downstream of Nurek, which have only small (hourly or at most daily)
         regulating capacities, will in turn follow this pattern, smoothing out the short-term fluctuations in the
         process. At the downstream end of the cascade, river flow is constant with small if any daily fluctuations.
         The situation with Rogun in place will be the same as today.
   •     Riverine and aquatic habitats: Construction of Nurek dam and HPP changed the situation in Vakhsh
         River considerably, mainly in three respects:
             o   As mentioned, Nurek shifted 4.2 out of the 20 km³ flowing on average annually in Vakhsh River
                 from summer to winter, in this way reducing the seasonal difference between the high- and the
                 low-flow seasons, reducing the former and increasing the latter.
             o   Nurek reservoir retains all the sediments transported by the river. The water downstream of
                 Nurek is largely devoid of sediments.
             o   Like all storage reservoirs, Nurek reservoir also has a capacity for flood regulation. This means
                 that flood events are smoothed out a least to some extent, especially by reducing peak flows
                 during floods.
         These changes had a considerable negative impact on one important semi-aquatic habitat, namely the
         floodplain and Tugai vegetation in Tigrovaya Balka, since this habitat type largely depends on seasonal
         changes in water discharge and therefore water level, on sediment dynamics, and on occasional flood
         events. One additional negative impact of Nurek and the downstream cascade on biodiversity was the
         fact that any long fish migrations between the Amu Darya and the upper reaches of Vakhsh River, which
         under natural conditions may have been quite important, were completely interrupted.
         Rogun may have added to a small extent to a further reduction in short-range fish migrations from Nurek
         reservoir upstream. Other than that, the situation in the downstream area, especially concerning
         Tigrovaya Balka and fish migrations, will not be changed due to Rogun.
   •     Floods, PMF: Nurek is not able to safely manage PMF (Probable Maximum Flood), however, Rogun
         HPP was designed in a way as to be able to do so. This is a considerable improvement in risk
         management for the downstream area, reducing the risk of a dam break and its potentially disastrous

                                                                                                            PUBLIC
Page vi




          consequences in this whole area. This is a very relevant positive cumulative impact of Rogun HPP with
          the downstream Vakhsh cascade, which may become even more important in the future, since under
          the conditions of climate change there is a risk of more marked and important short-time changes in
          river flow which may result in floods. Details for this still need to be evaluated and described in an
          Emergency Preparedness Plan (EPP). The draft EPP for the dam with crest elevation at 1110 m asl was
          submitted on February 29, 2024. The EPP for the full dam is in preparation, and it will need to be in line
          with the current EPP study for the Vakhsh cascade (DSPOE presentation, Almaty Riparian Meeting of
          Nov. 8-9, 2023).
    •     Sedimentation: Rogun will retain sediments, in this way avoiding accumulation of additional sediment in
          Nurek reservoir. This will increase the total life span of Nurek HPP, a positive effect on the entire Vakhsh
          cascade.
    •     Joint operation of Rogun and Nurek HPPs will also increase electricity generation in Nurek to some
          extent; this is mainly due to the fact that there will no longer be a marked drawdown of Nurek reservoir
          in winter, thus allowing to benefit from the additional head.
    •     The addition of Rogun HPP to the Vakhsh cascade offers the potential to deliver additional water to the
          downstream area in the case of exceptionally dry years; this would be a considerable positive effect.
Conclusions: As main conclusions, the following can be said:
    •     Operating Rogun and the entire Vakhsh cascade while respecting the conditions defined in Protocol 566
          concerning water allocation, and especially by not shifting additional water from summer to winter will
          prevent any negative cumulative impacts on water users in the downstream area.
    •     The overall joint operation of the cascade will not lead to additional negative impacts on aquatic habitats
          and related biodiversity in the downstream area (especially: Tigrovaya Balka and fish migrations).
    •     The fact that Rogun will be able to handle the PMF is a positive effect for the downstream area by
          reducing the risk of a dam break event.
    •     Adding Rogun to the cascade will have a number of additional benefits, namely (i) increasing the life
          span or Nurek by retaining sediments, (ii) increasing to some extent energy production in Nurek HPP,
          and (iii) giving the possibility of releasing additional water to the downstream area in the case of dry
          summers. Obviously, the main effect of Rogun HPP will be to add a very considerable amount of
          electricity to the grid, without using additional water. This will be a benefit for all consumers of electricity
          in the country, and potentially beyond, by contributing to the Central Asian grid.
Overall, it can be concluded that there will be no negative impacts, but some improvements in the downstream
area, due to the cumulative effects of Rogun HPP with the Vakhsh cascade.
Concerning the three aspects related to biodiversity it can clearly be stated that Rogun HPP will not change the
situation as compared to what it is now. This is shortly discussed in Chapter 8. The main point here is that Rogun
HPP will be operated within the conditions defined in agreement with riparian countries, i.e. without shifting
additional water from summer to winter. It also will not change the short-term flow regime in Vakhsh River
downstream of the existing cascade.
The final part of the report, Chapter 9, discusses the issue that was identified as the main area of concern in
relation to all projects in the water sector in the Amu Darya Basin: availability, use and distribution of water in
this area.
In order to take into account and discuss main concerns pronounced by downstream water users, a number of
scenarios were developed which show potential outcomes of alternative patterns of water use. These are the
following:
    •     Scenario 1: present situation. This is the baseline scenario which assumes that all countries in the
          Amu Darya basin would continue to use their share of Amu Darya water as they have done so far.
    •     Scenario 2: Tajikistan using its full share. The scenario assumes that Tajikistan will use the full share
          allocated to it by ICWC/BVO agreements, with the consequence that the 1.2 km³ of water not used so
          far on average would no longer be available for downstream users. In the scenario it is assumed that
          Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan would continue to use their share as done so far. Tajikistan has clearly
          announced, and is entitled, to use its full share. It intends to do so first for filling Rogun reservoir and
          then for increasing its own irrigated area. This means that this is the situation that will materialise in the

                                                                                                               PUBLIC
Page vii




           future, in accordance with BVO regulations, and independently of whether Rogun HPP will be built or
           not.
    •      Scenario 3: rapid reservoir filling. This scenario assumes that Rogun reservoir would not be filled with
           the water not used so far over a period of up to 16 years, but would be rapidly filled within three years,
           retaining 3.3, 5 and 5 km³ of water in three consecutive years, afterwards returning to a normal operation
           pattern as per scenario 2. This would cause severe water shortage in the downstream areas during the
           three years of filling.
    •      Scenario 4: additional shift of water. Same assumptions as for scenario 3, but in addition to that
           assuming that, once the reservoir filled, Rogun HPP would shift an additional 5 km³ of water per year
           from summer to winter, with the aim of maximising winter electricity generation. In addition to the
           negative effects of the filling period mentioned above, this would result in a situation where summer flow
           conditions in Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan would change to permanently dry to very dry summers, while
           winter flow would be increased, largely without any benefit for the two countries.
    •      Scenario 5: Qosh Tepa. Like scenario 4, but assuming that in addition to that Afghanistan would divert
           a yearly volume of 10 km³ of water through this canal. The result for Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan would
           be that they would have to face a situation of permanently extremely dry summers like never experienced
           before. This is the worst case scenario.
Two main aspects have to be pointed out as a result of this scenario analysis:
    1. The situation as described in scenario 2 is the one which will materialise as soon as Tajikistan will use
       its full share of water. This will happen in any case, with or without Rogun. This situation is clearly
       accepted by and within the ICWC/BVO regulations.
    2. Scenarios 3 to 5 describe situations with a degree of impact on downstream water users (Turkmenistan
       and Uzbekistan) that would clearly not be acceptable. It has to be pointed out that the assumed role of
       Tajikistan within these scenarios, namely, rapid filling of the reservoir and additional seasonal shift of
       water, would cause inacceptable negative environmental and socio-economic impacts on the
       downstream area, with no possibility for mitigation, and that for these reasons none of these scenarios
       had ever been considered for implementation. In addition, these would be clear violations of
       ICWC/BVO agreements, while Tajikistan has clearly committed itself to respecting these
       agreements. The contribution of Afghanistan to the situation as outlined in scenario 5 however cannot
       be influenced by ICWC/BVO regulations, since Afghanistan is not a member of these institutions.
The main conclusions therefore are the following:
    •      Under changing climatic conditions, storage of water for being able to make use of excess water in
           extremely wet years and to face droughts in exceptionally dry year, both of them situations expected to
           become more severe in the future, may get increasingly important.
    •      It may also be required to add additional storage capacity to the system for increasing its flexibility and
           potential to react.
    •      Such increased storage and water regulation capacity would also require agreements for refilling storage
           after an exceptional drawdown.
    •      International regulating bodies, in this case ICWC/BVO, and their agreements will play an increasingly
           important role.
    •      It will certainly get increasingly important to have all interested parties, i.e. all riparian states, included in
           any agreements concerning the use of wate resources.
This last point is of special importance. Presently, ICWC/BVO for the Amu Darya basin includes Kirgizstan,
Tajikistan, Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan. Afghanistan should be included in future agreements on the use of
this important common resource.




                                                                                                                  PUBLIC
1             INTRODUCTION

1.1           Purpose of the Report
The 2014 TEAS and ESIA (Pöyry, 2014) had shown that it is possible to operate Rogun HPP in a way as not to
change flow characteristics in Vakhsh River downstream of Nurek HPP and the existing Vakhsh cascade, and
mainly without shifting additional water from summer to winter. The operation of Nurek HPP had led to a shift of
4.2 km³ of water from the high flow season (summer) to the low flow season (winter). An additional shift would
not have been in the interest of the downstream riparian countries, since they need the water in summer for
irrigation, and the possibility for retaining additional water in summer in Rogun reservoir was a main concern of
the countries who would have been affected in that case. However, the studies mentioned had shown that with
Rogun HPP in place there would be no such change, and therefore no (negative) impact on downstream
conditions and water users. Tajikistan committed itself to operate Rogun HPP according to these conditions,
keeping its water use within the agreement with the riparian states, namely the Protocol 566, which defines the
annual amount of Amu Darya water available to each country. Therefore, the conclusion was that there will be
no impact downstream of Nurek due to Rogun, and therefore no risk of a cumulative impact. The updated ESIA
prepared by WSP is based on this commitment by Tajikistan.
However, it then turned out, especially in the meetings with downstream riparian countries in which several
CSOs and NGOs participated, that this was not considered as satisfactory, since concerns were voiced about
potential cumulative impacts that might materialise in spite of this situation. For this reason it was decided to
extend the scope of the CIA to be prepared as part of the updated ESIA.
It is therefore important for the CIA to focus not only on given or verified project impacts and their possible
cumulative effects with other projects, but also to look at relevant topics where, under changing conditions, there
might be a potential for cumulative impacts, and/or there are concerns uttered by stakeholders that this might
be the case.


1.2           Rationale for Carrying Out a CIA
This Section just provides a short description of the reasons why a CIA has to be prepared; for a list and
discussion of the guidelines and standards to be followed refer to Chapter 2.
IFC (2013) describes and defines the CIA as follows:
      •   CIA is the process of (a) analyzing the potential impacts and risks of proposed developments in the
          context of the potential effects of other human activities and natural environmental and social external
          drivers on the chosen VECs (Valued Environmental Components) over time, and (b) proposing concrete
          measures to avoid, reduce, or mitigate such cumulative impacts and risk to the extent possible (IFC
          2013:21).
      •   The key analytical task is to discern how the potential impacts of a proposed development might
          combine, cumulatively, with the potential impacts of the other human activities and other natural
          stressors such as droughts or extreme climatic events (IFC 2013:21).
      •   Cumulative impact assessment and management is appropriate whenever there is concern that a
          project or activity under review may contribute to cumulative impacts on one or more VECs (IFC
          2013:23).
The World Bank definition is slightly different, since it says the following:
      •   The cumulative impact of the project is the incremental impact of the project when added to impacts
          from other relevant past, present and reasonably foreseeable developments as well as unplanned but
          predictable activities enabled by the project that may occur later or at a different location . Cumulative
          impacts can result from individually minor but collectively significant activities taking place over a period
          of time . The environmental and social assessment will consider cumulative impacts that are recognized
          as important on the basis of scientific concerns and/or reflect the concerns of project-affected parties .
          The potential cumulative impacts will be determined as early as possible, ideally as part of project
          scoping (WB 2017:18).
Both definitions emphasise the fact that cumulative impacts can arise when the development or project in
question has impacts which in turn might interact in any way with impacts caused by other developments or
projects, and that the CIA has to focus on these cumulated effects or, as the WB puts it, on the incremental
                                                                                                            PUBLIC
impacts resulting from these interactions. The 2014 ESIA has shown that there is a possibility for operating
Rogun HPP in a way as not to change water availability and seasonal distribution in the downstream area. This
operating pattern was adopted by Tajikistan, since it means that the international agreements of water use and
allocation in the Amu Darya basin will be respected. The conclusion of this is that Rogun HPP will not have any
negative impact on the regionally most important of all VECs which could be affected, namely, water availability
in the Amu Dary basin. It could therefore be argued that no CIA is required for Rogun HPP.
However, both definitions given above include one additional relevant condition for carrying out a CIA, namely
“concern that a project or activity under review may contribute to cumulative impacts” (IFC) and “cumulative
impacts that are recognized as important … and/or reflect the concerns of project-affected parties” (WB). The
riparian consultations organised by WB in the context of the further development of Rogun HPP have shown
clearly that, in spite of the results of the ESIA, there are concerns about water availability in the downstream
area, since Rogun HPP could potentially also be operated in a different way, meaning that there are potential
impacts which have to be considered. Therefore, a CIA is required for Rogun, and the focus has to be on water.
Since the vast majority of water users downstream of Rogun and the entire Vakhsh cascade are other countries,
namely, Afghanistan, Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan, this CIA necessarily is also a Transboundary CIA.




                                                                                                      PUBLIC
