                                                                                                              PUBLIC
Even if these institutions do not have specific CIA guidelines, they mention the necessity to include cumulative
impacts in a comprehensive environmental assessment, although not necessarily as a separate process.


2.2           CIA: Definitions
Definitions of CIA:
      •   IFC (2013:21), “CIA is the process of (a) analysing the potential impacts and risks of proposed
          developments in the context of the potential effects of other human activities and natural environmental
          and social external drivers on the chosen VECs over time, and (b) proposing concrete measures to
          avoid, reduce, or mitigate such cumulative impacts and risk to the extent possible.”
      •   WB (2016: 23),p. 23: “Cumulative Impact Assessment is an instrument to consider cumulative impacts
          of the project in combination with impacts from other relevant past, present and reasonably foreseeable
          developments as well as unplanned but predictable activities enabled by the project that may occur later
          or at a different location.” p. 18: “The cumulative impact of the project is the incremental impact of the
          project when added to impacts from other relevant past, present and reasonably foreseeable
          developments as well as unplanned but predictable activities enabled by the project that may occur later
          or at a different location. Cumulative impacts can result from individually minor but collectively significant
          activities taking place over a period of time. The environmental and social assessment will consider
          cumulative impacts that are recognized as important on the basis of scientific concerns and/or reflect
          the concerns of project-affected parties. The potential cumulative impacts will be determined as early as
          possible, ideally as part of project scoping”.
      •   AIIB, 2022 ESF, p. 82: “Cumulative impact assessment is the assessment of the impact on the
          environmental or social conditions, which results from the incremental impact of an action when added
          to other past, present or reasonably foreseeable actions, regardless of what agency or person
          undertakes such actions. Cumulative impact can result from individually minor but collectively significant
          actions taking place over a period of time”.
      •   ADB: guidelines do not give a definition of cumulative impact assessment, but in 2022, p. 122, under
          the heading “Cumulative Effects and the Environmental Assessment Process” point out the importance
          of “identifying relevant past, present, and future actions. By evaluating resource impact zones and the
          life cycle of effects, rather than projects, the boundaries of cumulative impact assessment can be
          properly defined.”



2.3           CIA Procedure According to IFC
The IFC CIA Handbook (IFC 2013) lists the following steps as essential for the preparation of a CIA:
Step 1: Scoping Phase I – Identify VCs, Spatial and Temporal Boundaries
The identification of VCs was based on primary and secondary data gathered during the ESIA process, whereby
it has to be noted that both the 2014 (Pöyry 2014) and the updated (WSP 2025) ESIA Reports were considered.
In the Central Asian context, water (water quantity, availability and seasonal distribution; see Section 4.1.1 for a
more detailed discussion) is the one VC of overarching importance. Spatial boundaries for the CIA are not
necessarily limited to the AoI defined for the ESIA; if in a combination with other projects there is a potential for
cumulative effects becoming manifest outside of the ESIA AoI, or if relevant stakeholders utter concerns that
this may happen, it is required for the CIA to expand the area of (potential) influence accordingly. The temporal
boundaries of the CIA were defined by the expected life span of the Project.
Step 2: Scoping Phase II – Other Activities and Environmental Drivers
As defined by WB (2016:23), the CIA, in addition to impacts caused by the Project has to include “relevant past,
present and reasonably foreseeable developments as well as unplanned but predictable activities enabled by
the project that may occur later or at a different location”. It was therefore necessary to extend the scoping for
other such developments (in this report usually referred to as “other projects” independently of their actual stage
of development) or other environmental drivers. The most important of these latter is climate change, including
potential relevant secondary effects this may have in the wider project area, mainly on water quantity and
seasonal distribution as well as on erosion and therefore on sedimentation.



                                                                                                             PUBLIC
Step 3: Establish Information on the Baseline Status of VCs
Baseline Status of the VCs was based on the baseline data that was collected during the ESIAs, supplemented
with additional information, where required and available, that was collected specifically for the CIA.
Step 4: Assess Cumulative Impacts on VCs
Impact assessment focuses on interactions between different projects, or more specifically, between, Rogun
HPP and any of the other projects and their respective impacts. Additional explanations on this point are provided
in Section 3.1.1.
Step 5: Assess Significance of Predicted Cumulative Impacts
The significance of a cumulative impact will be evaluated in a descriptive and qualitative way. It has to be stated
clearly that for the other projects information availability is often restricted, (very) preliminary or at a general level,
and precise (quantitative) information is often lacking; this is the case especially for projects which presently are
in an early planning stage. This does not make them any less relevant for getting a picture for potential future
developments, but there are too many uncertainties as to allow a reasonably precise prediction of a future
situation.
Step 6: Management of Cumulative Impacts – Design and Implementation
Where significant – or, in this case more likely – potentially significant impacts are identified, mitigation measures
will have to be developed. Sinc the CIA is at a different level as the project-specific ESIA, these mitigation
measures will usually not reach the degree of detail that would be expected in a project-specific ESMMP, they
will mostly be at a much more general level. One important difference can be pointed out here already: while in
the case of an ESMMP the responsibility for implementing the mitigation measures will mostly be the Project
Proponent’s, may it be directly or indirectly, e.g. by obliging the contractor to take measures and respect
standards, in all likelihood in the case of mitigation measures for cumulative impacts, implementation will not be
within the capability and authority of the Project Proponent.




                                                                                                                PUBLIC
3             BOUNDARY CONDITIONS FOR THE CIA

3.1           Stepwise Approach Chosen
The list of steps to be taken in a CIA according to IFC as described shortly above is a useful guideline inasmuch
as none of these points can be ignored. In practice, however, it may not always be possible or advisable to take
these steps as a rigid rule which needs to be followed point by point. It is therefore necessary to give some
additional explanations on the approach specifically chosen in the present case.

3.1.1     Basic Considerations
A few basic considerations concerning scope and limitations of a CIA are shortly presented hereunder:
      •   The central part of a CIA is the project which is the subject of the ESIA, in this case Rogun HPP (the
          Project).
      •   The focus of the CIA is on potential mutual interactions of this Project with other projects, activities or
          developments; these interactions can be mainly of the following forms:
              o   the Project has an impact on any of these other developments;
              o   other developments have an impact on the Project, or
              o   impacts stemming from the Project interact with impacts stemming from other developments,
                  mutually reinforcing the effect, reducing it or leading to additional indirect impacts (whereby it
                  should be kept in mind that “impact” can mean negative, undesirable, as well as positive,
                  beneficial effects).
      •   Projects, activities or developments to be included in the CIA (in this report usually referred to as “other
          projects”) cannot be defined too narrowly; in fact, this should include existing structures and projects
          under construction as well as plans, development intentions (in the sense of “projects in the pipeline”)
          etc., i.e. “relevant past, present and reasonably foreseeable developments” as per the WB definition
          given above.
      •   The time span to be considered also needs to be rather long-term. According to IFC (2013: p. 35, Box
          7), the complete life cycle of the proposed development (in this case Rogun HPP) needs to be covered.
          This means that projects which might be developed in the future, i.e. reasonably foreseeable, will have
          to be included, even if there is not much detailed information on them available as yet, and even if it is
          uncertain when, if ever, some of them will potentially be implemented.
      •   This means that some of these other projects may not (or not yet) be well defined, their implementation
          potentially at some as yet unknown point in the future. For such types of developments, there might not
          yet be more than a general idea of what their potential impacts would be. This also limits the assessment
          of magnitude and exact importance of cumulative impacts resulting from them.
      •   Impacts to be considered are those which have been identified and assessed as relevant in the ESIA
          for the Project, as long as there can possibly be any cumulative impacts, but should include, according
          to the WB definition, “concerns of project-affected parties”, even if it seems clear from the start that such
          impacts might be minimal.
      •   Given the scope of projects included, it will often not be possible to propose very well-defined mitigation
          measures, especially if such measures may be required, if ever, only at some point in the future.
      •   It has also to be pointed out very clearly that the proponent of the Project, in many if not in most cases
          of cumulative impacts, will have limited or no possibilities to implement mitigation measures, since
          responsibilities will be shared, or will be on an entirely different level.

3.1.2     Specific Approach
Some general comments on the requirements and limitations for a CIA were made above. Here, specific
conditions for the CIA for Rogun HPP are outlined.
While an ESIA focuses on one specific project and aims at identifying all impacts to be expected and to assess
them in detail, the focus of the CIA is on receptors (also called Valued Components, VCs) which can be affected
by different projects and where the combined impacts from these different projects could have a cumulative
                                                                                                            PUBLIC
effect; such a cumulative effect can potentially be of different types, like mutually increasing, mitigating or
resulting in indirect impacts which would not become manifest without the interaction of different projects.
However, in most cases, as certainly in this one, the central point of attention is still the project in question, i.e.
Rogun HPP (the Project). This clearly means that there can only be cumulative impacts on receptors where the
Project itself has impacts, or has the potential to have impacts. The starting point for assessing cumulative
impacts therefore has to be an analysis and evaluation of the impacts caused by Rogun HPP. The selection of
receptors or VCs to be included, however, should not be too narrow. Specifically, it seems appropriate to include
VCs or impacts which reflect the concerns of project-affected parties, as expressed in WB guidelines, even if the
ESIA demonstrates that such impacts will be minimal or non-existent. At the same time, the focus of the CIA will
usually have to be on categories of VCs which are defined rather broadly and not too specific, mainly since in
most cases there will be no specific impact assessment available for the other projects included in the CIA.
The broad categories of impacts – and therefore of potential cumulative impacts – are shortly discussed here:
      •   Water: an HPP, and especially one with a large storage capacity, will necessarily have an impact on
          water (hydrology), and can influence river discharge pattern downstream of the power plant and
          seasonal distribution of flow. Water availability is a major concern in Central Asia, demonstrated by the
          importance of agreements between riparian states in the Amu Dary basin. For this reason, water
          availability is a VC to be considered.
      •   Biodiversity: every dam project will directly affect a river by creating a barrier, and plants with large
          storage reservoirs will change living conditions in a number of aquatic as well as terrestrial habitats.
      •   Socio-economic impacts: mainly two (potential) impacts of HPPs on local and regional socio-economic
          conditions have to be considered and are often of very high relevance, namely, (i) the effect of improving
          local economic conditions by providing a large number of income-generating jobs, mainly during the
          construction period and to a lesser extent during operation, and (ii) resettlement, i.e. the fact that people
          will suffer physical and/or economic displacement due to the project (ESIA Vol. 1, Ch. 17, Socio-
          Economy). In this context it also needs to be noted that the main socio-economic effect of an HPP – and
          the reason for its development – is to ensure a stable and adequate electricity supply (mainly to the
          population outside of the AOI considered in the ESIA) and, in the case of Rogun, to allow exports to
          neighbouring countries. This is usually not a direct concern or subject of an ESIA or a CIA, although the
          ESIA will have to take that into account, e.g. for discussing (potential) effects on GHG emissions.
      •   Construction-related impacts: a large number of impacts are usually related – and mostly limited – to
          construction activities, as e.g. noise and air contamination, temporary land use and influx of people from
          outside.
      •   Climate change: HPPs can be affected by climate change, usually in the long term, due to changes in
          precipitation and other conditions affecting water availability (glacier melt). On the other hand, they can
          contribute considerably to mitigating climate change by replacing fossil fuels for generating electricity;
          when addressing this, the potential risk of methane production in the reservoir will have to be considered,
          since in an extreme case this could even outweigh the benefit from fossil fuel replacement.


3.2           Definition of VCs

3.2.1     Relevant Receptors
While it may be useful to think about VCs as a starting point of a CIA, such an early selection should in no case
be considered as final and exhaustive. Once the other projects to be included in the considerations have been
identified, it is still possible that an issue – which might have to be considered as an additional, not previously
identified VC – might appear and will have to be dealt with.
As mentioned above, the most important issue in the wider context of environmental and social impacts in the
project area is water. A combined effect of Rogun HPP and other projects related to and influencing water
resources might have an impact on water availability in the downstream area, or it may be required to operate
them jointly in a way as to mitigate such an impact. The VC or the direct receptor, in this case, would be water
(quantity, availability, accessibility), which in turn would affect water uses, mainly irrigation systems in the
downstream area, and the population depending on irrigation and therefore on water resources (indirect
receptors or VCs).



                                                                                                            PUBLIC
This means that the VCs should not be too narrowly defined right from the start, since an open mind must be
kept for cumulative impacts (and “their” VCs) that may be identified during the process of baseline analysis,
project identification and impact assessment.
The following general approach was therefore used for identifying, defining and characterising VCs that might
be exposed to cumulative impacts:
    •   The sensitive receptors (also termed VCs) were identified based on impacts described in the ESIAs, but
        also depending on types of projects included in the evaluation, on area of (potential) influence for the
        specific effects identified, and on the situation to be expected there.
    •   These receptors were not narrowly defined, and they were described to the extent required and possible.
    •   Impacts are described with sufficient detail, making it clear that e.g. “effects on water availability” could
        be negative, if volume would be reduced or seasonal distribution changed in an unfavourable way, or
        could be positive, e.g. by making additional water available for irrigation from storage reservoirs in dry
        years.
As a conclusion of this it can be stated that the main VC is Water Availability in the Downstream Area.

3.2.2   Additional VCs
In the context of developments in the water sector in general, but perhaps regarding the situation in the Amu
Darya basin even more so, three issues related more or less directly with biodiversity are often discussed and
give rise to concerns, namely:
    •   the Aral Sea,
    •   fish migrations, and
    •   floodplain habitats, especially Tigrovaya Balka.
All three depend on water availability, distribution and use and are directly or indirectly influenced by structures
affecting aquatic habitats. For this reason they have to be included among the topics discussed in the CIA.




                                                                                                          PUBLIC
4             PROJECTS INCLUDED
This Chapter provides the list of projects or other developments that are included in the CIA.


4.1           Projects Related to Water

4.1.1     Importance of Water
Water availability, scarcity and distribution are main concerns in the Central Asia Region. An interstate
organisation (ICWC/BVO), including the former Soviet Republics and now independent states Kirgizstan,
Tajikistan, Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan regulates water allocation among the states in the Amu Darya basin.
One issue of increasing importance is the fact that Afghanistan is not part of these organisations. In addition to
hydropower development in Tajikistan, potential increase in water use in the future by Afghanistan could develop
into an issue of very relevant proportions. The situation will also be influenced – and may change to a
considerable extent – due to the mid- and long-term effects of climate change.
As a short summary of the situation, the following points can be mentioned:
      •   As extensively discussed in the 2014 ESIA, one main concern of riparian countries related to the
          development of Rogun HPP was the potential of an additional storage power plant in the upper reaches
          of the Amu Darya basin to shift water from summer to winter in addition to the volume already shifted by
          Nurek HPP. This concern is explained by the fact that storage plants retain water in the high-flow season
          (in this case spring and early summer) for producing electricity in the low-flow season (winter) when the
          demand for electricity is highest. Such a shift would present a problem for the downstream riparians,
          who need water mainly for irrigation, i.e. in summer.
      •   Tajikistan made it very clear that with Rogun HPP in operation the country will continue to respect the
          international agreements, meaning that it will not use water above the quota allocated to it, and that it
          will not shift additional water from summer to winter. This was analysed and described in detail in the
          2014 studies (TEAS as well as ESIA).
      •   The updated ESIA compares the present flow situation downstream of Nurek (without Rogun in place)
          with the situation as it will be with Rogun operating (ESIA, Vol. 1, Ch. 13) and concludes that there will
          be no significant changes.
While for the ESIA this latter position may be justified, for the CIA it is not sufficient. Even if under the present
conditions it is safe to say that Rogun HPP will not have any negative impacts downstream of Nurek HPP, it is
still required to carefully analyse the situation, since concerns still remain and since it cannot be excluded that
under changing conditions, be it with additional projects to be included in the CIA and/or with changing
environmental drivers as e.g. climate change, the situation for Rogun HPP will also change. Therefore it is
necessary for the CIA to put the focus on water, i.e. water quantity, availability and seasonal distribution in the
downstream area.

4.1.2     Relevance for CIA
Hydrology was an important issue in the ESIAs, and this is also true for the CIA, for the following main reasons:
      •   Construction and operation of Rogun will have an effect on Nurek, and therefore on the downstream
          area, by retaining water for reservoir filling and by changing the inflow pattern to Nurk reservoir. What
          the impact will be depends mainly on the combined operating scheme of the Vakhsh cascade, which
          needs to be looked at.
      •   Influence on Amu Darya (d/s of Pyanj/Vakhsh confluence, and to the Aral Sea) is the most important
          issue in the Central Asian/Amu Darya basin context, and a major concern of relevant stakeholders.
      •   To be taken into consideration:
              o   Entire Vakhsh cascade (knowing that the HPPs downstream or Nurek are comparatively small
                  and basically ROR schemes, without much effect on river flow pattern).
              o   Shurob HPP (between Rogun and Nurek, in planning), is a ROR scheme without any influence
                  on flow pattern except in the 17 river km between the two major schemes.
              o   Potential for further HPP development u/s of Rogun reservoir, on Rivers Obihingou and
                  Surkhob.
                                                                                                          PUBLIC
             o   Projects in the Pyanj catchment, i.e. upstream of its confluence with Vakhsh. Several small
                 projects (Pamir 1, and Sebzor, both operating) are pure ROR and/or too small for having a
                 noticeable influence, but there are at least ideas for additional storage schemes in this area
                 which would have an effect.
             o   Irrigation in Tajikistan d/s of Nurek: Tajikistan does not (yet) use all the water that in principle is
                 allocated to it, but intends to do so, with effects further d/s.
             o   The Qosh Tepa Canal, a new irrigation channel in Afghanistan, will take water that will no longer
                 be available for Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan; the role of Rogun might change in the light of
                 that. This 285 km canal project is located in northern provinces of Afghanistan. It proposes to
                 divert water from the Amu Darya River to allow up to 600,000 hectares to be used for agriculture.
                 The proposed location of intake from the river is near Khoshtepa (Afghan/Tajik border).


4.2          Other Projects
A number of other projects, not related to the water sector, may also have, to a degree to be determined, mutual
effects with Rogun HPP. The most relevant of these is certainly the replacement road (and related
developments), i.e. the International Highway (Vakhdat – Jirgital, M41) Project. This project can be seen – and
certainly is – a mitigation measure for Rogun HPP, since the existing main road M41 between Obi Garm (near
Rogun dam site) and Nurobod (in the upper part of the reservoir, including the only bridge crossing Vakhsh
River) will be submerged by the reservoir and therefore needs to be replaced. However, this is developed as a
separate project, with separate financing and under a different organisation (PGM under the Ministry of
Transport), and therefore is treated as an additional project for the CIA.


4.3          List of Projects to Be Included in the CIA
Table 4-1 provides the list of projects which are included in the CIA, along with a brief description mentioning
potential cumulative effects.

                                                                                                                  PUBLIC
5             AREA OF INTEREST FOR THE CIA

5.1           Vakhsh River Catchment

5.1.1     Basic Information
As explained above, the river downstream of Nurek is not considered as an area potentially impacted by Rogun
HPP. The ESIA concludes that Tajikistan’s commitment to keep their water usage within the annual allocations
by ICWC/BVO Rogun HPP will avoid any impacts downstream of Nurek HPP. However, this is the area that has
to be of foremost interest for the CIA for the following reasons:
      •   The river (Vakhsh) between Rogun HPP tailrace and Nurek reservoir will change completely (change in
          river discharge from natural regime to entirely project-related, with flow changing – in potentially short
          cycles – between minimum flow and full turbine output; water almost devoid of sediments). Not an impact
          of relevance, as described in both ESIAs.
      •   Nurek reservoir: operation mode will change from basically seasonal (strong drawdown in winter) to a
          type of ROR scheme, with a more or less stable reservoir level, serving to some degree as re-regulation
          reservoir for Rogun. This can have a positive effect in the downstream area (less fluctuations). TEAS
          and ESIA (2014) showed that it is possible to operate Vakhsh cascade with Rogun added in a way as
          not to change downstream flow conditions, and mainly seasonal water distribution. Still, the fact remains
          that Rogun HPP adds a considerable storage volume to the cascade, which in theory offers the potential
          to increase water retention in summer. This can explain why there are concerns in the downstream area
          on this relevant issue, and why therefore this needs to be discussed in detail in the CIA.
      •   Nurek cannot withstand the PMF; Rogun is able to manage the PMF, in this way protecting Nurek dam.
          This has to be considered as a major positive effect in the downstream area, especially since a dam
          break of Nurek would certainly have dramatic impacts beyond the Tajk border.
      •   Filling Rogun Reservoir (planned for the period of 2019 to approximately 2037) inevitably retains water.
          Even if this is water allocated to Tajikistan, it is water that before 2019 was not retained by Tajikistan
          and was therefore available downstream. There is no reason to assume that Tajikistan would not respect
          international agreements on water allocation, but it is still a change in comparison to the no-Rogun
          situation.
      •   During operation, Rogun HPP will seasonally retain the same amount of water as now already retained
          by Nurek, i.e. 4.2 km³; no additional water will be shifted from summer to winter. In parallel to that, the
          operation of Nurek will be changed from a reservoir with seasonal storage and drawdown to one which
          will be operated as an almost ROR scheme, with very little fluctuations in reservoir volume (see Figure
          6-2).
      •   There is a plan showing the potential further development of hydropower in the part of the Vakhsh
          catchment upstream of the Rogun reservoir, on the rivers Surkhob and Obihingou. While none of these
          HPPs is in a planning stage as to be realised soon, they have to be included since there is a potential
          for their implementation “in the reasonably foreseeable future”.
      •   Water to be used by Tajikistan for filling and operating Rogun HPP: “The Government of Tajikistan’s
          commitments to i) use only the Tajik allocation of water, as fixed by the ICWC in accordance with the
          existing agreements and ii) to maintain summer water transfer at the same level as is currently being
          transferred at Nurek.” (ESIA, Vol. I, Ch. 6, Table 6-2: Summary of Topics Raised in the Fifth Riparian
          Information-Sharing and Consultation Session (July 2014)).

5.1.2     Projects Included
In addition to Rogun HPP the following of the projects mentioned above with potential cumulative effects are
included in this part of the Area of Influence:
      •   Existing Vakhsh cascade downstream of Rogun HPP.
      •   Shurob HPP.
      •   Cascade upstream of Rogun HPP.
      •   Irrigation in Tajikistan downstream of Nurek.


                                                                                                          PUBLIC
5.2          Amu Darya Basin
As discussed in detail in the 2014 ESIA, water is a very important resource in Central Asia. Large storage dams
have the potential to influence seasonal flow distribution, which could lead to conflicts between different water
users because of different requirements for water (mainly in winter for electricity generation, mainly in spring and
summer for agricultural purposes). For this reason, the CIA must put a clear focus on this resource and potential
cumulative effects of different projects in the Amu Darya basin.

5.2.1   Situation in the Amu Darya Basin
The Amu Darya is the largest river of Central Asia, and one of the two main tributaries of the Aral Sea. The river
is formed by the confluence of its two most important tributaries, the Pyanj and Vakhsh rivers. The Vakhsh
contributes an average of about 26 % to the annual Amu Darya flow, and the Pyanj about 40 %. In the Amu
Darya, as in both its main tributaries, the flow pattern is highly seasonal, with high flows in summer due to snow
and glacier melt in the mountains, and low flow in winter since most of the precipitation in the catchment area
falls as snow.
Water abstraction from the Amu Darya, mainly for irrigation, has led to the demise of the Aral Sea during the
second half of the 20th century. By far the largest part of total annual flow in the Amu Darya basin stems from
the high mountains in Tajikistan, while water requirements for irrigation are highest in Uzbekistan and
Turkmenistan (see key figures in Table 5-1). International agreements on water allocation among the countries
in the catchment area are therefore essential. Water use is regulated by the Amu Darya basin organisation
(ICWC/BVO). The main agreement is the so-called Protocol 566, which defines the percentage each country is
entitled to use. Based on this and a forecast of the probable water amount in the next growing season the specific
quota are determined yearly. Afghanistan is not a party to these organisations and agreements.


Table 5-1:      Origin and use of water in the Amu Darya basin

                  Country         Contribution to total flow of     Water allocation according
                                          Amu Darya                  to Nukus Protocol 566

                                               %                         %              km³**
                  Kirgizstan                  2.42                      0.6              0.4
                  Tajikistan                  80.15                    15.4              9.5
                  Turkmenistan                3.58                     35.8             22.0
                  Uzbekistan                  5.97                     48.2             29.6
                  Total 1                     92.12                     100             61.5
                  Afghanistan*                7.88                                       2.1
                  Total 2                       100                                         63.6
                * Afghanistan is not an ICWC member. The quantity of water used by Afghanistan included in the Table
                is based on empirical values.
                ** The values in km³ per year reflect the situation in an avarage year. The actual amount to be used by
                each country is determined by an annual forecast of expected water quantities based mainly on
                assessments of the snow cover in the catchment area.
                Source: Mr. Daler Abdurazoqzoda, Head of Energy and Water Policy Department (pers. comm. 2024-03-
                05).


Nurek HPP, the first large storage hydropower project in the Amu Darya basin, caused a considerable change
in the downstream flow pattern mainly by shifting 4.2 km³ of water from summer to winter, in this way reducing
the difference between the summer high flow and the winter low flow conditions. Rogun HPP was ﬁrst conceived
in the Soviet Union in the 1950s and 1960s as part of the regional Central Asian development, mainly for
providing irrigation water for the cotton producing region in the lower Amu Darya basin, and in addition, like the
already existing Nurek HPP just downstream of Rogun, for generating peaking power for the Central Asian grid.
The political situation has changed since the independence of the former Central Asian Soviet Republics.
Like every large storage reservoir, Rogun HPP will have the capacity to influence seasonal distribution of river
flows in the downstream area. As the 2014 ESIA has shown, it is possible to operate Rogun in a way that will
                                                                                                            PUBLIC
not negatively affect the downstream riparian countries, and Tajikistan has confirmed that it will continue to
respect water distribution according to Protocol 566. A more detailed analysis of this topic is presented in Section
9.7. In addition to that, Rogun improves flood protection in the downstream area, since it is capable to handle
the PMF, while Nurek is not designed to handle such a flood and would be at risk. Rogun HPP has also the
potential to provide additional water in dry years.

5.2.2     Projects Included
In addition to Rogun HPP the following of the projects with potential cumulative effects are included in this part
of the Area of Influence:
      •   Nurek and Vakhsh cascade downstream of Nurek (existing).
      •   Shurob HPP (in planning stage).
      •   HPP cascade upstream of Rogun (in the conceptual stage).
      •   HPP cascade on Pyanj River (in the conceptual stage).
      •   Qosh Tepa Canal in Afghanistan (under construction).



5.3           Rogun Reservoir and Surroundings
Here, in addition to Rogun reservoir and the cascade upstream of Rogun already mentioned above, resettlement
sites within the upper part of Vakhsh valley, construction of the new main road and the pipeline have to be
included.


5.4           Resettlement Sites
According to the ESIA, there is a risk of conflicts of the resettlement program with other projects. Vol. I, Chapter
3 describes this in the following way:
“3.10.9.       People in some villages that will be affected by resettlement for the Project, may also be impacted
by other projects, including:
      •   Elements of the CASA-1000 rural electrification project, which required land to be acquired in, among
          other things, Tursunzoda and Rudaki;
      •   Obi Garm-Nurobod road project – (ADB & EBRD) required land acquisition in Rogun city and Nurobod
          district;
      •   Dushanbe-Tursunzoda-Uzbekistan border road project (ADB) required land acquisition, in particular, in
          Tursunzoda City; and
      •   Dushanbe-Bokhtar road project (ADB & JICA) required land acquisition in Rudaki district. “
This has to be verified as part of the CIA. The transmission lines associated with and required for Rogun HPP
are considered as separate projects. This means that resettlement, be it physical or economic displacement,
caused by a TL would be considered as part of that project, and not as a cumulative impact with Rogun HPP.
However, if a conflict should arise with one of the resettlement villages, in a worst case the need to relocate once
more households which have already been relocated due to Rogun HPP, this would clearly be a cumulative
impact to be looked at in the CIA.


5.5           Overall AoI
From the description of sectoral AoIs above it is clear that in some cases there is an overlap between some of
them, and/or the projects to be considered within them.
As a consequence it can be said that:
      •   The Area of Influence for the CIA is the entire Amu Darya basin, with the Vakhsh catchment and
          the area downstream of the Pyanj-Vakhsh confluence, i.e. the Amu Darya, as the central parts.


                                                                                                          PUBLIC
    •   The Cumulative Impact Assessment (CIA) is necessarily also a Transboundary Impact
        Assessment (TBIA).




Figure 5-1:     Amu Darya basin as AoI for the CIA
                Grey: Amu Darya Basin; red area: Vakhsh catchment. Red triangle: Rogun dam site.


In addition to the Amu Darya basin as such, the area along the Karakum Canal in Turkmenistan, which was built
between 1954 and 1988 and deviates 13 km³ of water from the Amu Darya to supply irrigated areas and major
settlements, including the capital, would also have to be included in the AoI, since changes in water availability
in the river would also have consequences there.




                                                                                                        PUBLIC
6             CUMULATIVE IMPACT ASSESSMENT

6.1           Rogun HPP
As stated above, this is a CIA specifically prepared for Rogun HPP (also mentioned as Rogun or the Project).
The focus will be on interactions between Rogun HPP and the other projects included in the CIA, interactions
meaning effects of Rogun on any of the other projects, effects of any of the other projects on Rogun, and impacts
of Rogun HPP and one or more of the other project on the same receptor (i.e. cumulative impacts in the strict
sense of the term). Here, a short description of Rogun HPP is provided. For the selection and description of the
other projects see Chapter 4.

6.1.1     Main Characteristics of Rogun HPP

Dam type: rockfill with central impervious core.

      •   dam crest 1,110 m asl, height 143 m (upstream cofferdam)

      •   2 units @ 200 MW each under operation.
Some additional relevant information on Rogun HPP:
      •   Rogun dam is the second large dam constructed on Vakhsh River, about 70 km upstream of the existing
          Nurek dam.
      •   Its main purpose is the production of electricity, a large part of which shall be exported, mainly to
          Pakistan and Afghanistan, via the 500 kV transmission lines built under the CASA 1000 project (see
          Section 6.9).
      •   Filling of the reservoir (13.3 km²) will be done under strict observation of the water allocation rules, which
          means that on average annually not more than 1.2 km² of water will be retained, which corresponds to
          the quantity allocated to Tajikistan under Protocol 566 but so far not used by Tajikistan. The plan is to
          gradually fill the reservoir over the period from 2019 to approximately 2037.
      •   The expected life span of Rogun HPP is 115 to 130 years; the main limiting factor is sedimentation of
          the reservoir.


6.2            Interaction with Existing Vakhsh Cascade
In this Section, interactions of Rogun HPP with the existing Vakhsh cascade are described. The Section starts
with a short description of the HPPs forming this cascade, for then discussing cumulative impacts. The two
entities, namely, Rogun HPP on the one hand and the downstream cascade on the other hand are closely related
with each other since they use the same water. Interactions of these two entities therefore determine the future
flow pattern in the river downstream of the entire cascade. It is important to look closely at this situation, since
changes as compared to present conditions would necessarily have effects in the downstream area.

6.2.1     Cascade Description
The existing HPP cascade downstream of Rogun consists of one large storage dam, Nurek, and 6 ROR HPPs
as listed in Table 6-2.


Table 6-2:        Vakhsh Cascade

                   capacity                                                          capacity,     (m)
                                   Total volume     Active storage      Surface        (MW(
                                       (km³)             (km³)           (km²)

 Baipaza           weekly/daily         0.097            0.080            8.04          600         60
 Sangtuda-1        daily                0.258            0.012            9,75          670        64.4
 Sangtuda-2        daily                0.066            0.003              -           220         22
 Golovnaya         daily                0.095            0.018             7.5          210        23.3
 Perepadnaya       daily                  -                 -               -           29.9         -
 Centralnaya      daily                   -                 -               -           15.1         -
Source: Pöyry (2014)


Golovnaya HPP, in addition to producing electricity, also diverts water from Vakhsh River to an irrigation channel,
and the two HPPs downstream of Golovnaya are located on this channel (see Figure 6-1).
                                                                                                             PUBLIC
Nurek has a large storage volume, which enabled it to influence river discharge in the downstream area, shifting
water from the high-flow season (summer) to the low-flow season (winter). The other dams in the cascade have
little or no storage and are operated as ROR schemes. They have no capacity for changing the river regime.




Figure 6-1:     Vakhsh cascade indicating location of dams
                Rogun is under construction, Shurob is in an earyl planning stage, the other HPPs indicated are under
                operation.




                                                                                                             PUBLIC
6.2.2     Potential Impacts

6.2.2.1       Impacts on Rogun HPP
Since this cascade is downstream of Rogun HPP, there is no impact on this latter.

6.2.2.2       Impacts Stemming from Rogun HPP
There are clear impacts of Rogun HPP on Nurek:
    •     During the filling phase of Rogun reservoir, estimated to last from 2019 to approximately 2037, a total of
          13.3 km³ of water which would otherwise have reached Nurek reservoir directly is retained there. With
          an average yearly flow of Vakhsh of 20 km³, retaining about 1.2 km³ per year would mean a reduction
          of water available for Nurek and the downstream cascade by 6%, and in Amu Darya by 2%. It can be
          assumed that generation of electricity would be reduced by roughly the same percentage.
    •     Presently, two units with a reduced capacity (200 MW each) are installed and operational at Rogun HPP;
          these can compensate at least to some extent the reduction experienced at Nurek. The runners on these
          two turbines will be replaced at a later stage (foreseen to take place end of 2025), for them to reach the
          final capacity of 630 MW, and the 4 remaining turbines will gradually be installed.
    •     Tajikistan has committed itself to keep the flow pattern of Vakhsh downstream of Nurek, meaning that
          with the operation of Rogun HPP no additional water will be shifted from summer to winter. This is mainly
          done for avoiding negative impacts, i.e. water shortage during the vegetation period, in the downstream
          area, the irrigation perimeters in Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan. Rogun HPP will at least partially be
          operated as a peaking plant, meaning that net regulation will largely be managed by the operation
          pattern of Rogun; this necessarily means that the operation of Nurek HPP will be changed to a ROR-
          type pattern scheme, by keeping the reservoir level more or less constant throughout (see Figure 6-2).
    •     Nurek HPP is not capable of withstanding the PMF. Rogun HPP was designed in a way as to be able to
          store and safely convey the PMF. This is a very relevant reduction of the risk of dam break and
          catastrophic flooding in the downstream area.
    •     Rogun reservoir will retain most if not all the sediment reaching it from its catchment area, and with this
          it will increase the useful life span of Nurek. Rogun is also designed to safely pass flows once the
          reservoir is full with sediments. Nurek is not, which means some solution has to be found before its
          reservoir fills. Rogun gives an extra 100+ years to come up with solutions.
    •     Operation of the Vakhsh cascade with Rogun HPP in place will also increase electricity production in
          the other HPPs of the cascade (see Table 6-3).
    •     Once fully operational, Rogun HPP will add, on average, 14.4 TWh of electricity to the Tajik grid,
          increasing the production of the entire Vakhsh cascade from 19.9 TWh (before Rogun) to 35.3 TWh per
          year (TEAS 2014: Phase II Report, Vol. 1, p. 243). As mentioned, this will be done without changing flow
          conditions in Vakhsh River downstream of Nurek.
Most of the effects of Rogun on Nurek are directly related to the operation pattern of Rogun HPP. There will be
no direct impacts of Rogun HPP on the other power plants further downstream, since these will just continue to
use the water released from Nurek HPP.




                                                                                                           PUBLIC
Figure 6-2:      Simulation of Rogun reservoir filling
                 Source: TEAS 2014: Phase 2 Summary Report, Figure 50
                 U1 to U6: units (turbines and generators); U5 and U6 are the ones already installed with reduced
                 capacity.


The Figure illustrates the development over the 16 years filling period. The water regime in Nurek reservoir
(dashed blue line) will change from a seasonal pattern with a marked drawdown in winter to a regime with near
constant water level, i.e. close to a ROR scheme, while Roun will be operated under seasonal storage regime;
note that in the graph, seasonal variation in reservoir level is illustrated, but there will also be short-term (daily)
peaking. Under this system, Rogun HPP will not use its total live storage of 10.3 km³, since operation will be
limited to the volume of 4.2 km³ per year as presently regulated by Nurek; this means that a considerable amount
of water will be stored in Rogun reservoir, part of which could be used for making available additional water for
irrigation in dry years.


Table 6-3:       Effect of Rogun HPP on d/s cascade

 Power plant                                   Without Rogun (GWh/y)                      With Rogun (GWh/y)
 Nurek                                                   11,376                                  12,373
 Baypaza                                                 2,657                                    2,636
 Sangtuda 1                                              2,908                                    2,955
 Sangtuda 2                                              1,064                                    1,054

 Total                                                   19,365                                  20,368
Source: TEAS 2014: Economic Analysis, Table 14



                                                                                                               PUBLIC
6.2.2.3       Potential Cumulative Impacts
TEAS and ESIA of 2014 have analysed the hydrological situation in detail. The main conclusion from this analysis
were:
    •     As all large storage reservoirs, Nurek and Rogun reservoirs have the potential to change the seasonal
          flow regime in the downstream area by retaining water in the high flow season and using it for electricity
          generation in the low-flow season. The construction of Nurek HPP led to such a shift of 4.2 km³ from
          summer to winter, corresponding to the live storage of Nurek reservoir.
    •     Rogun HPP would have the capacity to make an additional such shift if it were operated in a way as to
          maximise winter electricity generation. However, Tajikistan is committed not to change the yearly flow
          pattern from the present one, i.e. with Nurek. This limitation to a live storage volume of 4.2 km³ is an
          operational design constraint which had to be respected in the development of the project. This takes
          into account the fact that water allocation is regulated in the ICWC/BVO agreements, and that the
          downstream users need water for irrigation in summer.
    •     In this situation it can be said that Rogun HPP and the Vakhsh cascade downstream of it will not have
          a cumulative impact on water availability in the area along the Amu Darya.
This latter point is illustrated in the following Figure, which is a result of the new water resources modelling (ESIA
Vol. 2, Annex 13-3, Water Resource Modelling), which compares present inflow to and outflow from Nurek
reservoir with the future situation with Rogun HPP in place.




Figure 6-3:       Flow situation downstream of Nurek without and with Rogun
                  Nurek in- and outflow situation without Rogun (left) and with Rogun (right)
                  Source: 2025 ESIA, Vol. 2, Annex 13-3, Water Resource Modelling; Vol. 1, Ch. 13, Water
The inflow to Nurek, which corresponds to the inflow to Rogun, will be changed by the fact that Rogun, as shown
in Figure 6-2, will take over the function as regulator in the cascade. Release from Rogun, the new inflow to
Nurek, will follow the same pattern as was applied in Nurek before, Nurek now operating as a near-ROR scheme,
and as a result the seasonal flow pattern downstream of Nurek will not change.
Due to the marked seasonal fluctuation, Rogun reservoir is not expected to develop into a good habitat for fish
species; on the other hand, since Nurek will not show such fluctuations any longer, it can be expected that there
conditions for fish species which are adapted to lake conditions will find a better situation (see 2025 ESIA, Vol.
1, Ch. 15, Biodiversity).

6.2.3     Cascade Operation with Rogun HPP

6.2.3.1       Electricity Demand
In Tajikistan, electricity demand is highest in winter. Therefore seasonal water storage in reservoirs is essential
for meeting this demand pattern, and this causes the reservoir drawdown illustrated in Figure 6-2. However,
there is also a distinct daily pattern, as shown in Figure 6-4.




                                                                                                           PUBLIC
Figure 6-4:      Average daily electricity demand pattern
                 TEAS 2014: Economic Analysis, Fig. 18, p. 33


In addition to the fact that electricity demand in winter (Q1, January to March, and Q4, October to December) is
higher than in summer, the Figure also clearly shows that there are two daily peaks, one in the morning and one
in the evening.
Hydropower is a very suitable source of electricity for covering differences in demand, since turbines can be
turned on and off rather quickly. As already explained, the seasonal river flow pattern will not be changed, no
additional water will be shifted from summer to winter. Remains the question of daily peaking, i.e. whether there
will be a change in the short-term flow pattern in Vakhsh river downstream of the cascade.

6.2.3.2       Rogun Reservoir Filling
Figure 6-2 illustrates the principal pattern that will be followed for filling Rogun reservoir and for gradually starting
its operation. Here, the water retention process as presently under way is described.
Presently (May 2025) the dam has reached an elevation of 1100 m asl and the water level is at 1075 m asl.
Reservoir capacity, i.e. the volume available for storage, increases as the construction of the dam progresses.
However, Tajikistan has committed itself to respect BVO water allocation rules also for the filling of Rogun
reservoir. This means that the water available for this corresponds to the yearly water volume allocated to the
country, but so far not used. According to TEAS, this amounts to 1.211 km³ in an average year (with yearly
fluctuations due to precipitation and resulting overall water availability. Due to this restriction in water retention,
the increase in actual water volume retained is slower as the increase in reservoir volume, as shown in Table
6-4: the reservoir will reach its full capacity in 2033, when the dam crest will have reached its final level of 1300
m asl, while the filling of the reservoir will not be finalised before 2037. As mentioned, the figures presented in
the Table are based on the assumption that the average volume of 1.211 km³ will be available every year. In
case a series of dryer than average years should occur during this period, filling of the reservoir could even take
longer.


Table 6-4:       Increase of reservoir capacity and water volume stored

 Date                                          Reservoir capacity (km3)                   Water Volume (km3)
 1 Jan 2025                                              0.86                                    0.440
 1 Jan 2026                                              1.317                                   1.317
 1 Jan 2027                                              2.053                                   2.053
 1 Jan 2028                                              3.240                                   3.240
 1 Jan 2029                                              4.713                                   4.450
 1 Jan 2030                                              6.883                                   5.660
