6.2.3.3       Nurek HPP Operation
Presently, an upgrade program is under way for Nurek HPP. The first phase of this project includes replacement
of 3 hydraulic units, replacement and repair of mechanical and electrical equipment and maintenance of main
power plant equipment, and the delivery and installation of 6 units of single-phase 500/220/10 kV
autotransformers. It also includes the implementation of works related to dam safety. The implementation of the
1st phase of the project began on June 2, 2017, and is expected to be completed on December 31, 2026. As of
September 2024, the status of the program is as follows: construction of the first hydroelectric unit (power unit
A) was completed on August 15, 2022, and was officially commissioned on October 24, 2022. Currently, this
hydro unit is operating with a capacity of 375 MW. Renovation and electrification works in all parts of the main
hydroelectric unit No. 4 (power unit D) have been completed and the necessary tests have been carried out.
Currently, the 30-day technical testing program related to the reliability of the use of this unit has started and is
ongoing. Delivery of the materials/parts of the third hydro unit (power unit C) is currently continuing as planned,
and up to now 95% of the main construction materials have been delivered and stored at the power plant. This
hydraulic unit will be commissioned in October 2025. Performance of works within the scope of other tasks of
the project (R2.1 "Dam Safety - monitoring", R2 "Dam Safety - hydromechanical" and R4 "Open-type installation
of autotransformers" continues as per plans.
Nurek provides a large part of the total electricity presently produced and used in Tajikistan. It is operated in
three ways, whereby these three modes are overlapping:
    •     Baseload: 2 to 3 out of the 9 units are always in operation (not including maintenance or emergency
          shutdowns), and water output of each of the units, when working at full capacity, is between 150 to 163
          m³/s.
    •     Seasonal storage: during the high-flow season the reservoir is filled to its FSL of 910 m asl, and during
          winter, when inflow into the reservoir is lowest and electricity demand is highest, it is drawn down to its
          MOL of 857 m asl, in this way using its live storage of 4.2 km³ (2014 ESIA, p. A).
    •     Daily peaking: daily variation in electricity demand has to be met by corresponding short-term changes
          in production. Such a peaking regime means that turbines are turned on and off frequently, which in turn
          leads to important variations of water output from the power plant. Presently, peaking demand is met by
          Nurek and the cascade downstream of it.
Typical daily patterns are shown in Figure 6-5.




                                                                                                           PUBLIC
Figure 6-5:       Turbining pattern in Nurek HPP
                  Source; Barki Tojik Dispatch Center, data received 2024-05-08


The theoretical water output from Nurek, with all 9 units operating at full capacity, would be between 1,350 m³/s
(with the reservoir at FSL) and 1,467 m³/s (with the reservoir at MOL). The Figure shows that during the days
illustrated this maximum was never reached. This can be due to the demand level at that time, but also, especially
in winter, due to limited water availability. It also shows that production (and with it use of water) is not constant
during the day. In summer, the hourly differences are rather small, a small peak in the morning is visible in one
curve and one in the evening in both of them. In winter, the situation can be very different. While one of the
curves shows a day with almost constant 24 h output, the other one illustrates a day with a marked peaking
pattern. In the morning, water use raises within 3 h from 250 to 842 m³/s, to drop then from 827 to 266 m³/s
within one hour; it should be noted that the available data are average hourly discharges, the actual change may
be within a much shorter time. The values of roughly 250 m³/s during the day and in the night would mean that
at this time probably only two units were operating at reduced capacity.
This clearly illustrates the fact that in a peaking power plant, the daily pattern can vary greatly from one day to
the next, and that during the day very marked short-term variations are possible.

6.2.3.4       Peaking Pattern in Rogun and Nurek
The 2014 technical studies concluded that the best way for operating the Rogun-Nurek cascade would be to
mainly use Rogun reservoir for seasonal regulation, i.e. for operating the power plant in a way as to draw down
the reservoir in winter and fill it again in summer; this led to the pattern for reservoir operation shown in Figure
6-2. The study did not explicitly plan daily peaking in Rogun HPP: “It is noteworthy that the daily peaking
possibility is not assessed in this report here because the simulation time step is the month. All variables
presented are monthly data. It is to be noted that operating Rogun in peaking mode could be possible within the
monthly operation studied here but it will not change the average monthly energy production. The peaking
operation can be assessed thanks to the economic analysis by dispatching the total energy within a day to fit
the demand.” (TEAS 2014: Phase II: Project Definition Options . Volume 3: Engineering and design. Chapter 5:
Reservoir operation study., p. 30).
Once Rogun HPP will be fully operational, the two large power plants will be operated together. The following
principles will be applied:
    •     Operation will be made in a way as to optimise the use of the available water.
    •     Rogun will assume the part of seasonal storage, with an FSL at 1290 m asl and an MOL of 1260 m asl;
          this corresponds to a live storage of 4.2 km³ (live storage 2 in Table 6-1; MOL 1, 1185 m asl, and live
          storage 1, 10.3 km³, in that Table will not be applied in operation, this is a project configuration for being
          able to react to increasing reservoir sedimentation in the future).
    •     Nurek will be operated as a “close to ROR” HPP. Its reservoir will be kept at more or less the same level
          throughout, at usually about 5 to 10 m below FSL in order to have sufficient regulating capacity. This is
          required since the maximum water output of Rogun HPP is 1626 m³/s (all 6 units at full capacity), as
          compared to the 1467 m³/s of Nurek.
    •     Both of the large storage plants, Rogun as well as Nurek, can be used for peaking, i.e. for short-term
          variations in energy output. In the case of Rogun, time required for turbines to reach full capacity is a
          few minutes. The 9 turbines of Nurek are presently being replaced by new ones, as part of the Nurek
          Rehabilitation Project. This will increase capacity from now 300 to new 375 MW per unit, due to higher
          efficiency of the new turbines. They will also have a very short reaction time (0 to full capacity in 2
          seconds), and are therefore highly efficient for reacting to short-term variations. A part of the peaking
          now done by Nurek will be taken over by Rogun. This means that peaking will be done by operating the
          entire cascade, including the smaller HPP downstream of Nurek, in an optimal way. A detailed plan for
          operating the entire cascade is not – or not yet – available. In any case, the cascade will be operated as
          a unit, with the aim of optimising water use and electricity output.
    •     In periods where Rogun will be operated as a peaking plant with large short-term differences, Nurek will
          also act in a way as at least dampening the fluctuations in flow volume caused by Rogun.




                                                                                                             PUBLIC
6.2.3.5        Cascade Downstream of Nurek
As mentioned above, the six smaller HPPs downstream of Nurek are operated in very close coordination with
Nurek (and with Rogun in the future). In this way, they can also contribute to covering peak demand. As they
have only small (hourly to daily) storage capacity, they cannot be used for seasonal storage. However, their
small storage allows them to act as re-regulation ponds for Nurek.
Short-term flow variations caused by large peaking HPPs, causing abrupt massive flow changes in the river
downstream of the water outlet, often have very negative effects on biodiversity of the river and its immediate
surroundings. These impacts are mainly the following:
    •     Surge wave: a very rapid (within seconds) massive increase in water volume will cause a surge wave
          which will flush out the river, destroying sensitive habitats (or not allowing their development) and
          negatively affecting most if not all of the species living there. It is also a risk for people who stay near, in
          or on the river.
    •     Sharp increase and important daily changes of flow velocity may make it difficult for many species to
          live there.
    •     Marked changes in water depth will mainly affect slow moving or immobile water organisms, by leaving
          large areas of the river bed suddenly dry for several hours. Fish spawning grounds can be completely
          destroyed by this effect (in addition to the impact caused by the surge wave).
HPPs designed for peaking therefore are often equipped with a re-regulation pond, which will be able to
smoothen these abrupt changes at least to some degree. In the case of the Vakhsh cascade, the downstream
HPPs also have this role to play.
Baipaza HPP, the first one in the cascade downstream of Nurek, has a storage capacity of 0.08 km³. This would
be enough for receiving the volume of water released from Nurek during about 15 h production at full capacity.
This is more than sufficient for attenuating short-term fluctuations. The other HPPs further downstream act in
the same way, with the result that the Vakhsh River downstream of the cascade does not show any of the short-
term fluctuations caused by Nurek HPP.
With Rogun HPP in place, the cascade will be operated in the same way, the situation downstream of the
cascade will not change.

6.2.4     Conclusions
This leads to two important conclusions for the future situation with Rogun HPP:
    1. The yearly water distribution will not change, as no additional water will be shifted from summer to winter.
       This means no negative impact caused by Rogun on downstream water users.
    2. The daily/short term regime in Vakhsh river downstream of the cascade will not change. This means
       that there will be no additional negative impacts on aquatic and riparian habitats in the downstream area
       of Vakhsh River, and mainly on the floodplain habitats in Tigrovaya Balka and the aquatic habitats in
       Amu Darya.



6.2.5     Flood Management
The Emergency Preparedness and Action Plan (EPAP, Grunder 2024) describes the outcome of a dam break
for the situation with the Rogun main dam at la level of 1110 m asl. In this case, a flood wave with a height of 90
m would develop at the foot of the dam, which would be reduced to 60 m at the upper end of Nurek reservoir
and would then be further attenuated in the Nurek reservoir. Nurek dam would not be overtopped during such
an event. Since the Vakhsh valley between Rogun and Nurek is not inhabited, damage to structures (with the
exception of the power house and associated structures just below Rogun dam) and people would not have to
be expected.
An EPAP for the final dam crest elevation of 1300 m asl is under preparation.
As already mentioned, Rogun HPP will be able to safely handle PMF. This is a considerable reduction of flood
risk in the downstream area, since Nurek is not able to safely convey PMF. This effect may become even more
important in the future, since under the conditions of climate change there is a risk of more marked and important
short-time changes in river flow which may result in floods.

                                                                                                               PUBLIC
6.3           Shurob HPP

6.3.1   Project Description
Shurob dam and HPP is planned to be built directly at the upper end of Nurek reservoir; the purpose of this 850
MW HPP is to make use of the remaining approximately 70 m head on the 17 river km of Vakhsh River between
Rogun and Nurek. The reservoir will reach almost to the foot of Rogun dam (see Figure 6-6), and it will use the
water released from Rogun. It is in an early planning stage, design details are not yet available.




Figure 6-6:     Cascade in upper Vakhsh River
                Source: Pöyry 2014, Figure 8-35


The river between Rogun tailrace and Nurek reservoir is narrow, with a rather marked gradient, bordered by
steep slopes (see Figure 6-7). The entire area is difficult to access and not inhabited or used otherwise (Gruner
2024, EPAP) . Available satellite pictures give a good impression of its character.




                                                                                                       PUBLIC
Figure 6-7:     Vakhsh River between Rogun and Nurek


Considering the biodiversity value of this part of the river, the following has to be taken into account:
    •   Its role for fish migration is very limited at best, since fish migrations have been interrupted with the
        construction of Nurek dam. It cannot be excluded that before the construction of Rogun dam started,
        there was some limited fish migration towards upstream from Nurek reservoir. Rogun dam will definitely
        interrupt this, if any remains at all. Any remaining migration in this part of the river is further restricted by
        the fact that its only tributary of some relevance, the Obishur river which joins Vakhsh on the left bank
        immediately downstream of Rogun dam is blocked by a dam which was built to retain sediment from
        Obishur, which otherwise would have threatened to block outlets from Rogun dam and power house.
                                                                                                              PUBLIC
    •     During the filling phase, river flow in this part of the river will be reduced by the volume retained in Rogun
          reservoir; this will be, as mentioned, 1.2 km³ in an average year, i.e. 6% of the average yearly flow
          volume. The remaining volume will be released from Rogun via the diversion tunnels, spillways or
          turbines.
    •     Once fully functioning, Rogun will most probably be operated in a way as to have at least one unit
          running all the time.
    •     In addition to that, Rogun HPP will be operated, at least to some extent, as a peaking plant. According
          to information received, the regime for Rogun HPP would be similar to the one illustrated for Nurek in
          Figure 6-5: one or two units operating all the time, and, depending on demand and water availability,
          peaking production when required. This could mean short-terms flow variations between 400 and up to
          1600 m³/s. With such a regime, this part of the river will lose most if not all of its remaining value as a
          habitat for aquatic organisms.
The updated ESIA (Volume 2, Annexes, Annex 13.1) determined a residual or ecological flow (EFlow) that would
be required for this part of the river in case flow could or would be stopped completely. This EFlow was
determined for the (probably rare) cases where Rogun HPP will be closed down completely; see discussion in
Water Chapter (ESIA Vol. 1, Ch. 13: Impacts on Water).

6.3.2     Potential Impacts

6.3.2.1       Impacts on Rogun HPP
Shurob HPP, being located downstream of Rogun, will not have any direct impact on this latter.

6.3.2.2       Impacts Stemming from Rogun HPP
Shurob will depend completely on the turbine operating regime of Rogun. It will be operated as a ROR project
which invariably will have to turbine the water released from Rogun HPP, with very little regulating storage.
Shurob could not be built without Rogun in place, since its small reservoir would be filled with sediment very
quickly.

6.3.2.3       Potential Cumulative Impacts
Basically, there are the following cumulative impacts of Shurob HPP with Rogun HPP:
    •     Environmental: the cumulative impact on habitat would be to extend the loss of riverine and riparian
          habitat by an additional 17 km since the 17 km river reach between Rogun dam and the upper end of
          the Nurek reservoir would be submerged by the Shurob reservoir. However, as already mentioned it is
          important to note that this reach will have already been affected by the changes in flow pattern, mainly
          peaking, caused by Rogun operation.
    •     Social: there is a possibility that after the construction of Rogun HPP some workers would find new jobs
          on the Shurob construction site.
Considering that the river reach in question will be affected not only by changes in seasonal, but much more so
by changes in daily, short term flow patterns as described above, the impact of impounding it is considered as
negligible. The river section between Rogun HPP outlet and Nurek reservoir will probably be covered by water
all the time, it is not assumed that in a case of complete shutdown of Rogun HPP the Shurob reservoir would be
emptied completely. However, in spite of that, due to high variation of inflow into the comparatively small Shurob
reservoir, this will not have any value as an aquatic habitat. The overall impact can be considered as neutral. If
Shurob is built, there will certainly be job opportunities there; still, it will be a much smaller construction site, and
there will be no time overlap with Rogun HPP.
One additional, and definitely positive cumulative impact seems to be much more important: Shurob will make
use of the head between Rogun and Nurek, and it will use the water released by Rogun once more for producing
electricity. This will increase overall productivity of the Vakhsh cascade. Since it will definitely not affect a habitat
that could be called a natural river, it can be considered as a minimum impact addition to the cascade (see
Zwahlen et al., 2019). The ESIA to be done for Shurob will have to check this.



                                                                                                              PUBLIC
6.3.3   Mitigation
No mitigation required.


6.4           HPPs in the Pipeline Upstream of Rogun

6.4.1   Project Description
As described in Section 6.1, Rogun HPP is the uppermost part of a cascade of hydropower plants on Vakhsh
river. However, there is a plan for further developing hydropower upstream of Rogun, using the two rivers,
Surkhob and Obihingou (see Figure 6-8) which, at their point of confluence, form Vakhsh River. Figure 6-9
illustrates the two additional cascades, and Table 6-5 lists the planned HPPs. None of these is in an advanced
stage of planning, and none of them will be built before the construction of Rogun (and most probably also
Shurob) will be finished. Still, this is at least an outline of a possible further development in the future.




Figure 6-8:     Diagrammatic view of river system in the study area
                Black dotted lines: international borders. Graph not drawn to scale.
                Source: Pöyry 2014, Fig. 4-2




                                                                                                    PUBLIC
Figure 6-9:        Surkhob and Obihingou cascades
                   Source: PMG, Planning Department


Table 6-5:         Planned HPPs upstream of Rogun

 No.     Name                                         Storage (km³)      Capacity (MW)      Output (TWh/y)
 A       Surkhob River (total)                             1.45               2,100               8.7
 1       Dzalbulak                                          1.4                600                2.0
 2       Sairon                                            0.001               500                2.2
 3       Gorgen                                            0.002               600                2.7
 4       Garm                                              0.002               400                1.8
 B       Obihingou River (total)                           1.55               3,700               9.3
 1       Sangvor                                            1.5                800                2.0
 2       Urfatin                                           0.01                850                2.1
 3       Shten                                             0.01                600                1.5
 4       Evtach                                            0.02                800                2.0
 5       Kaftaruzar                                        0.01                650                1.7
         Total both cascades                                3.0               5,800               18.0
Source: PMG, Planning Department




6.4.2    Potential Impacts
Building these two cascades of HPPs, or parts thereof, would certainly cause impacts which would, during
detailed planning, have to be assessed in project-specific ESIAs. Even without any detailed information on any
of these projects it can already be said that two major mitigation measures would be required, namely:
     •   Resettlement: both valleys are populated, and it can be assumed that a number of villages would be
         affected.


                                                                                                    PUBLIC
    •     Road replacement: the international road to Kyrgyzstan runs along Surkhob River, and in the Obihingou
          valley there is a road leading to GBAO area; both would have to be replaced.
However, these would be project-specific impacts, to be dealt with in the framework of the respective projects,
and cannot be considered as cumulative impacts with Rogun HPP. Potential cumulative impacts are discussed
in the following sections.

6.4.2.1        Impacts on Rogun HPP
As Table 6-5 shows, none of the planned HPPs has a very large storage, but even so they would add a total of
3 km³ of additional storage volume to the cascade. Since they will be built later than Rogun, their filling would
not interfere with the filling of Rogun reservoir, and it can be assumed that this would be done under similar
conditions as presently for Rogun, namely, by respecting the water allocation to Tajikistan according to Protocol
566; this especially means that in spite of the increased total storage volume no water would be shifted from
summer to winter in addition to the 4.2 km³ already shifted by Nurek. The impact on Rogun would be negligible,
and an impact on water availability in the downstream area, if any, would be minor (and definitely within
agreements).
One other impact on Rogun, however, would be more important: all the additional dams would retain sediments,
and with this they would have the potential to increase the life span of Rogun HPP. The effect that could actually
be expected would have to be analysed in detail, but a very simple calculation would at least give an Impression
of what it could be. According to the TEAS (2014) calculations, it will take 115 years for filling the 13.3 km³ of
Rogun reservoir with sediment. Adding to this the 3 km³ of the two additional cascades would bring the total
storage volume up to 16.5 km³; with the assumed rate of sediment input, this would mean that total filling would
be reached after about 140 years, a potential additional 25 years of useful life of Rogun HPP. The fact that
Rogun reservoir will retain sediments also increases the life span of Nurek HPP, and an increased life span of
Rogun therefore could also be a benefit for Nurek.
Climate change will certainly affect Rogun HPP, and with it, although less directly, the entire Vakhsh cascade.
As described in the 2014 and 2023 ESIAs, the main effects of climate change on Rogun HPP will be the following:
    •     Temperature and rainfall have already increased regionally during the last century. Temperature in the
          Vakhsh River basin are likely to increase by approximately 1.7°C between 2010 and 2050.
    •     Increasing temperatures also mean that more precipitation will fall as rain instead of snow.
    •     Glaciers already lost 2.5% of volume of ice during the 20th century. It is predicted that glaciers will lose
          another 20% of their volume during the next 30 to 40 years, which will increase water availability, but is
          also likely to cause mudflows and floods, bringing large amounts of sediments into the reservoir.
    •     Increasing temperature and retreat of glaciers leads to an upward shift of permafrost, which in turn will
          mobilise material and increase erosion.
    •     Retreating glaciers also increase the risk of GLOFs (Glacier Lake Outburst Floods).
    •     Under changing climatic conditions, storage of water for being able to make use of excess water in
          extremely wet years and to face droughts in exceptionally dry years, both of them situations expected
          to become more severe in the future, may get increasingly important.
    •     It may also be required to add additional storage capacity to the system for increasing its flexibility and
          potential to react.
    •     Such increased storage and water regulation capacity would also require agreements for refilling storage
          after an exceptional drawdown.
    •     International regulating bodies, in this case ICWC/BVO, and their agreements will play an increasingly
          important role.
    •     It will certainly get increasingly important to have all interested parties, i.e. all riparian states, included in
          any agreements concerning the use of wate resources. This last point is of special importance.
          Presently, ICWC/BVO for the Amu Darya basin includes Kirgizstan, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan and
          Uzbekistan. Afghanistan should be included in future agreements on the use of this important common
          resource.




                                                                                                                 PUBLIC
This shows that under changing climatic conditions water availability may change to some extent, but the main
risk for Rogun HPP is an increase in sediment inflow to the reservoir. Additional sediment retention could
counteract this development to some extent.

6.4.2.2      Impacts Stemming from Rogun HPP
Since these additional HPPs will be located upstream of Rogun reservoir, there will be no impacts on them
stemming from Rogun.

6.4.2.3      Potential Cumulative Impacts
Two cumulative impacts of Rogun HPP and the two additional cascades can be identified:
    1. River habitat fragmentation: as described in both ESIAs, Vakhsh River was fragmented by the high dam
       of Nurek and the cascade downstream of it, and Rogun adds to this effect. Building another 9 dams
       upstream of Rogun will add to this impact. Presently, there is no specific information about the quality of
       Surkhob and Obihingou as river habitat, e.g. for fish migration, but it can be assumed that they have to
       be considered as natural habitat, whose nature would be changed profoundly by the new cascades.
    2. Submersion of floodplains: As the 2014 ESIA identified, and the updated ESIA emphasises, a number
       of floodplains with a total surface of 75 to 77 ha (ESIA, Vol. II, Ann. 15, Section 4.1) will be lost to Rogun
       reservoir impoundment. While Obihingou seems to be too narrow and too steep for having developed
       extensive floodplains, this is definitely not the case for Surkhob, as Figure 6-10 shows. Potential dam
       sites and size of reservoirs of the two cascades are not yet known, but it cannot be excluded that some
       of these floodplains would be affected due to further hydropower development.
    3. The additional storage, even if not very large, would change the possibility for influencing water
       availability in the downstream area. As mentioned above, under the conditions defined by Protocol 566
       there will be no additional shift of water from summer to winter. However, the increased total storage
       volume could enhance the possibility of providing additional water to the downstream area in dry
       summers.




                                                                                                          PUBLIC
Figure 6-10:   Floodplains in Vakhsh and Surkhob Rivers
               Outlined in blue: floodplains that will be submerged in Rogun reservoir.
               Otulined in red: major floodplains along Surkhob river upstream of Rogun reservoir.
               Dark yellow: main roads
               Light yellow: internationa borders




                                                                                                     PUBLIC
Page 35




6.4.3     Mitigation
Concerning the floodplain habitat, the 2014 EISA (p. 144) made the following statement: “The floodplains (two
of which will be affected by the reservoir, a smaller one near Komsomolobod, a larger one further upstream,
near Nurobod) have been identified as important habitats for wildlife. This type of habitat has become extremely
rare in many parts of the world, and especially in highly developed countries, where river training and other
regulation measures, mainly for the purpose of flood protection and for gaining agricultural land, have led to their
almost complete destruction. This is not (or not yet) the case in Tajikistan, where this habitat type can be found
along all major rivers.” This statement (see also Zwahlen, 2022, p. 146) is certainly still valid today, but as the
previous section clearly indicated, there is a growing pressure on this habitat type even in Tajikistan.
The updated ESIA identified the effect of Rogun HPP on floodplain habitats as an impact requiring compensation.
In Vol. I, Chapter 15, Biodiversity, the following measures are proposed for reaching NNL (No Net Loss):
      •   Creation of new floodplain habitat upstream of the reservoir area or within a different catchment; and
      •   Other, out of kind, projects such as supporting creation of another habitat type that will be demonstrated
          to compensate for the loss of floodplain through creation of a sufficient area of another sensitive, native
          habitat type.
Creating new floodplains upstream of Rogun reservoir might be difficult since, as shown in Figure 6-10,
floodplains already exist at suitable locations, and as mentioned in Section 6.4, these might be affected if the
HPP cascade on Surkhob River will be implemented. Creation of such new floodplains in a different catchment
might also be problematic, since natural floodplains (still) exist in considerable numbers along all rivers in
Tajikistan.
However, one compensation measure that is certainly worthwhile considering would be an effort for improving
the situation of the floodplain habitat in the Tigrovaya Balka Nature Reserve. As clearly stated in the ESIA (Vol.
I, Chapter 15, and Vol. II, Annex 15-1), construction and operation of Rogun HPP will not affect this habitat in
any way, meaning that there is no need for mitigation measures of Rogun project in this area. However, it is a
very valuable habitat, which was affected by the construction of Nurek HPP, and where “out of kind”
compensation measures could be implemented as part of a NNL strategy; see Section 8.3 for a discussion of
this point.
The updated ESIA identified 185 ha of juniper woodland and 77 ha of floodplain that are naturel habitat that will
be lost due to reservoir impoundment and for which compensation is required in order to reach No Net Loss
(NNL). The No Net Loss Framework (NNLF; ESIA Vol. 3, A5) prepared as part of the ESIA identified a list of
required actions and potential sites for such compensation and carefully evaluated them. In this process, suitable
sites for compensation of juniper forest and floodplain loss were selected; both of the potential siles mentioned
above, floodplains upstream of Rogun reservoir as well as Tigrovaya Balka, were eliminated as not suitable for
the purpose.


6.5           Pyanj HPP Cascade
Pyanj River is the river which drains the Gorno-Badakhshan area of Tajikistan and adjacent areas of Afghanistan.
It forms the border between these two countries. At its confluence with Vakhsh River the name of the river
changes to Amu Darya. With 36 km³ per year, Pyanj contributes more water to Amu Darya than Vakhsh (20.1
km³). As for Surkhob and Obihingou, there is a plan for developing hydropower along Pyanj.

6.5.1     Project Description
Large storage projects in the Pyanj River basin, whether on Pyanj itself or on a tributary, would have the potential
to influence water discharge in the downstream area. For this reason, it is required to include such projects in
the CIA.
Presently, there is no project in an advanced stage of development on the Pyanj River; however, there is a list
of a cascade of 14 potential HPP schemes at the conceptual stage (see Table 6-6).




                                                                                                           PUBLIC
Page 36



Table 6-6:          Pyanj hydropower cascade

 No.      Name                                             Storage (km³)       Capacity (MW)       Output (TWh/y)
 1        Barhsor                                               1.25                 300                  1.6
 2        Anderov                                                0.1                 650                  3.3
 3        Pish                                                  0.03                 320                  1.7
 4        Khorog                                                0.01                 250                  1.3
 5        Rushansk                                               4.1                3,000                14.8
 6        Yagulemsk                                             0.02                 850                  4.2
 7        Granitnie Vorota                                      0.03                2,100                10.5
 8        Shigrovat                                             0.04                1,000                 9.7
 9        Khostav                                               0.04                1,200                 6.1
 10       Dashtidjum                                            10.2                4,000                15.6
 11       Djumar                                                 1.3                2,000                 8.2
 12       Moskov                                                0.04                 880                  3.4
 13       Koktshin                                               0.2                 350                  1.5
 14       Verkhne Amudarin                                      15.2                1,000                 4.4
 15       Gidrouzel (no further data)
          Total                                                 32.56               17,900               86.3
          Rogun HPP (for comparison)                            13.3                3,600                14.4


As can be seen from this list, a number of these HPPs would be ROR schemes (presumably all with a storage
capacity <1 km³), while two would have very substantial storage. Since these dams would be located on the
Pyanj River, i.e. on the border with Afghanistan, they would have to be developed as binational projects.
According to information received, about 5 years ago Afghanistan made a request to Tajikistan for a joint
development of Dashtijum HPP; this was then rejected by Tajikistan on the grounds that presently no financing
would be available, since the entire focus presently is on constructing Rogun HPP. As already mentioned, none
of these potential HPPs is at any advanced stage, and in the process of detailed planning, some parameters
may still change. The list only serves the purpose of giving a rough idea on what development might be possible
in the future. It can also be assumed that none of these projects would be built before the construction of Rogun
HPP is finalised, and that Shurob HPP (see Section 6.3) would be prioritised. Assuming institutional conditions
concerning water allocation as the ones valid presently and knowing that it takes approximately 16 years for
filling Rogun reservoir with its volume of 13.3 km³ and an annually available water volume of 1.2 km³, it can
easily be calculated that at least 27 years would be required for retaining the total storage volume of the complete
Pyanj cascade.
There might be other HPPs in the Pyanj basin, on tributaries of the main river. In fact, there are the ones
developed and operated by Pamir Energy; however, these are rater small ROR schemes, like Khorog HPP on
Gunt River (in operation, 9 MW, no storage), Sebzor HPP on Shokhdara River (recently commissioned, daily
storage of <0.001 km³, 11 MW, 77.6 GWh/y; Fichtner 2021) and Charsem HPP on Gunt River (0.0006 km³, 15
MW; USAID, n.d.). The largest one is Pamir 1 on Gunt River (28 MW), which uses Lake Yashilkul as storage
reservoir. The regulating structure built there around 2006 allows for the shift of about 10 m³/s from summer to
winter, which is used in Pamir 1 HPP for generating electricity. This adds up to about 0.015 km³ shifted from
summer to winter. Compared to the winter flow of Pyanj river at the confluence with Gunt (180 m²/s,
corresponding to a total winter flow of 2.8 km³), this shift is barely noticeable and well within natural fluctuations.
The effects of all these HPPs are too small to have any noticeable impact on the Amu Darya regime.


                                                                                                            PUBLIC
Page 37



6.5.2     Potential Impacts
The Pyanj cascade, and here especially the two with large storage, if implemented, would have the potential to
very markedly influence the downstream river regime. Actual effects would depend not only on storage capacity,
but, as for Rogun HPP, also on operation mode.

6.5.2.1        Impacts on Rogun HPP
Since this cascade is in a different catchment area, there would be no impact on Rogun HPP

6.5.2.2        Impacts Stemming from Rogun HPP
Here as well it can clearly be stated that Rogun HPP would have no impact on any dam project in the Pyanj
cascade (unless one would count the fact that Rogun construction leads to a delay in implementation of HPPs
elsewhere in Tajikistan as an impact).

6.5.2.3        Potential Cumulative Impacts
As Rogun and Nurek HPPs, projects of similar size would have the capacity to influence the flow pattern, and
namely seasonal distribution of flows, of the Amu Darya, and therefore water availability in the downstream area
of the Pyanj-Vakhsh confluence, which is certainly a cumulative impact. Rogun HPP can – and will – be operated
in a way as not to cause problems downstream, i.e. not reducing water availability for downstream riparians in
the growing season, and it can be used for providing additional water in dry summers, which is a clear benefit
for downstream water users. A detailed assessment of cumulative impacts cannot be made at this point in time,
but it can be assumed that for a combination of the two cascades an overall operating regime would have to be
and could be found and agreed upon which would be to the benefit for all parties involved.

6.5.3     Mitigation
Since the projects under discussion here – the Pyanj River HPP cascade – are not yet well defined and their
possible implementation is still far in the future, precise mitigation measures like specific operating regimes for
specific projects cannot be defined as yet. The importance of basin-wide agreements for regulating such
questions cannot be overstressed.

6.5.4     Discussion
This points out, once more, the importance of international agreements. It is difficult to see how Tajikistan would,
within the Amu Dary Basin organisations, handle binational storage plants on Pyanj River as long as Afghanistan
is not included in these organisations.


6.6            Irrigation in Tajikistan

6.6.1     Project Description
As shown in the data on water allocation and water use (see Section 9.2) Tajikistan does not use the full share
of Amu Darya water allocated to it, but there is an intention to increase irrigation in the area downstream of
Nurek, in this way making use of the difference while still adhering to Protocol 566.
In this respect, the following information was received from Ministry of Energy and Water Resources:
      •   So far, Tajikistan is not using 15-20% of its yearly water allocation, corresponding on average to 1.2 km³
          (with yearly variations ranging from about 0.3 to 2.2 km³, see Table 9-1). This will be used for filling the
          Rogun reservoir, as shown in Table 6-4.
      •   There is an intention to increase irrigation once the Rogun reservoir will be filled, which will not be earlier
          than 2037. There is no detailed plan for this as yet.
      •   Presently, 767,000 ha are irrigated. The intention is to add 100,000 ha to this.
      •   Water saving irrigation technology will be used to minimise water consumption.


                                                                                                              PUBLIC
Page 38



6.6.2     Potential Impacts

6.6.2.1       Impacts on Rogun HPP
All relevant irrigation schemes are located downstream of Nurek, and water supply to them is regulated from
Nurek reservoir as well as in Golovnaya HPP (see Section 6.2).

6.6.2.2       Impacts Stemming from Rogun HPP
Once Rogun reservoir will be filled, the so far unused part of water allocated to Tajikistan will no longer be
required there and will therefore be available for irrigation. This means that reservoir filling at Rogun brings about
a delay in possible increase of irrigation.

6.6.2.3       Potential Cumulative Impacts
The use of the full amount of water allocated to Tajikistan by Protocol 566, which means that this water is no
longer available in the downstream area, can be seen as cumulative impact on the downstream area, since the
water is first used for one, and then for the other purpose. However, it has to be pointed out that this is in
compliance with the mutually accepted rules.
Even if this is in compliance with the agreements, the question about a potential impact of using the complete
share by Tajikistan on the Aral Sea remains. As discussed in detail in the 2014 ESIA and shown in Section 8.1,
the southern part of the Aral Sea has almost disappeared, the very small remaining part having a too high salt
content as to allow any species of fish to live there. The annual inflow to the "Large Aral Sea", the southern part,
was between 0 (in dry years) and about 8 km³ (in wet years), with an average of about 5 km³.” (2014 ESIA, p.
71 and 333). The average inflow of 5 km³ per year, however, did not stop the Aral Seas from further shrinking.
The use of its full allocation by Tajikistan, with a yearly average of 1.2 km³, could increase frequency of zero
inflow to the Aral Sea, but as Figure 8-3 shows, there is no simple direct relationship between flow in Amu Darya
and the volume of water actually reaching the Aral Sea. In any case, inflow volumes are too small as to change
its overall condition (see a more detailed discussion in Section 8.1 and the results of scenario analyses in Section
9.7.4).

6.6.3     Mitigation
Even if Tajikistan will increase its irrigated area, the corresponding increase in water consumption is not
necessarily linear. Much will depend on irrigation technology applied. Modern water saving irrigation technology
could lead to a substantial reduction in water consumption, which in turn would have a positive impact on
downstream water users. Details of this will have to be carefully analysed during future irrigation development,
and this will also have to address the question whether such modern technologies could be applied on already
existing irrigation schemes as well.


6.7           Qosh Tepa Canal

6.7.1     Project Description
The Qosh Tepa Canal is a canal under construction in Afghanistan for deviating water from the Amu Darya to
be used for irrigation in Afghanistan. Its origin is approximately 285 km downstream from Rogun.
Some information was obtained from the following internet site:
https://thediplomat.com/2023/04/what-afghanistans-Qosh Tepa-canal-means-for-central-asia/
An article published there on April 19, 2023 states the following: “Late last month, an Uzbek delegation visited
Kabul in an effort to strengthen ties with the nascent Taliban regime. Among the various infrastructure projects
discussed between the two parties was the ongoing construction of Afghanistan’s new Qosh Tepa canal in Balkh
province. With a plan to divert up to 10 billion cubic meters from the Amu Darya each year, the canal proposes
the utilization of a significant amount of water from a basin that already has a history of intensive use by the
downstream states of Uzbekistan and Turkmenistan. As over 100 kilometers of the 285 km-long canal has
already been dug, the potential impact of such a project is becoming increasingly palpable each day construction
continues.”
The relevant information from this article is that the canal has the potential of diverting 10 km³ of water per year.
                                                                                                           PUBLIC
Page 39



Additional information was received from Mr. Daler Abdurazoqzoda, Head of Energy and Water Policy
Department, Ministry of Energy and Water Resources. This is summarised below:
    •      The plan is to finish the canal in 2028.
    •      The aim is to irrigate 550,000 ha of desert land. The construction of the irrigation system has not yet
           started.
    •      30% of the system would be irrigated by gravity, the remaining 70% require pumping. System envisaged
           to start operation in 2035.
    •      The estimated amount of water required would be 5 km³/y; this is calculated based on the following
           assumptions: cultivated plants will be wheat (with a water requirement of 4,500 m³/ha) and sunflowers
           (6,000 m³/ha); canal efficiency 65%, i.e. 35% loss due to evaporation and seepage.
    •      This is a concern for the other countries in the Amu Darya basin, but they acknowledge the right of
           Afghanistan to use water. The expectation is that Afghanistan will apply modern, water saving irrigation
           technology. Uzbekistan makes an effort to improve its own irrigation system in order to increase
           efficiency.
    •      There is no intention to change the water allocation rules according to Protocol 566.

6.7.2      Potential Impacts
As discussed in detail in the 2014 ESIA, water is a scarce resource in Central Asia. Water use is regulated by
the Amu Darya basin organisation (ICWC/BVO). The main agreement is the so-called Protocol 566, which
defines the share each country is entitled to use. Based on this and a forecast of the probable water amount in
the next season the specific quota are determined twice a year, for the growing season (April to September) and
the non-growing season (October to March).
Afghanistan is not part of these organisations. If now it would start deviating a substantially larger quantity than
it did so far, this would mean that less water would be available for the other countries, and especially for the
two downstream of the new canal, Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan. The following Table shows to what extent the
other water users could be affected, taking the two figures (5 or 10 km³/y) mentioned above as scenarios.


Table 6-7:         Potential consequences on water availability

 Country                  Nukus Protocol 566                       Situation with Qosh Tepa canal
                             (see Table 5-1)              AFG using 5 km³/y             AFG using 10 km³/y
                           %               km³          km³ (1)        km³ (2)         km³ (1)         km³ (2)
 Kirgizstan                0.6             0.4            0.4            0.4             0.3             0.4
 Tajikistan               15.4             9.5            9.0            9.5             8.3             9.5
 Turkmenistan             35.8            22.0           21.0           20.55           19.2            18.4
 Uzbekistan               48.2            29.6           28.2           28.25           25.8            28.0
 Total 1                   100            61.5           58.6            58.6           53.6            56.3
 Afghanistan                               2.1            5.0            5.0            10.0            10.0
 Total 2                                  63.6           63.6            63.6           63.6            63.6



Comments to the Table:
    •      Situation according to Protocol 566: Water allocation in % and in km³ in an average year (Table 5-1).
           61.5 km³/y is the share distributed between the BVO countries with 95% probability (meaning that
           statistically this value will be reached or surpassed in 95 out of 100 years). In such a year, total Amu
           Darya discharge would be 78.5 km³; the difference to the amount used is the volume lost to evaporation
           and infiltration and the volume reaching the Aral Sea.
    •      It is assumed that Afghanistan, not being a member of BVO, is consuming 2.1 km³/y.

                                                                                                          PUBLIC
Page 40



    •     Situation with Qosh Tepa Canal :
             o   (1): assuming distribution among BVO states according to Prot. 566 percentage.
             o   (2): assuming u/s countries using the same quantity as before and Turkmenistan and
                 Uzbekistan sharing the loss to equal parts.
    •     This assumes that Afghanistan would increase its water use from 2.1 to 5 or 10 km³/y; the situation
          would be further aggravated if the Qosh Tepa deviation were in addition to the 2.1 km³.
It has to be pointed out that none of the two “with Qosh Tepa Canal” scenarios are anything official or agreed
upon, these are just hypothetical calculations to show a potential outcome.
A publication by Hydrosolutions (2023: https://www.hydrosolutions.ch/projects/tipping-the-scales-the-future-of-
the-amu-darya-river-amidst-agricultural-ambitions ) provides additional information, which indicates that the
situation may potentially be more serious than outlined above. According to this, Qosh Tepa canal is designed
for deviating up to 20 km³ of water per year, aimed at irrigating up to 600,000 ha. The two following Figures
illustrate the present situation and the potential outcome.




Figure 6-11:     Current mean water balance of the up- and midstream river sections.
                 Source: hydrosolutions GmbH (2023).




Figure 6-12:     Future mean water balance of Amu Darya River with Qosh Tepa
                                                                                                     PUBLIC
Page 41



                 Source: hydrosolutions GmbH (2023).




It is important to understand that the portion labelled “Downstream Amu Darya River” does not mean that this is
water that will reach the Aral Sea. Most of it is used by Uzbekistan as part of its allocated share, only a small
fraction actually reaches the Aral Sea.
The scenario in Figure 6-12 assumes that, once Qosh Tepa canal operating, pre-existing water abstraction in
the upper and central part of Amu Darya would not change, which would mean that the total additional abstraction
by Afghanistan would go to the detriment of the lower Amu Darya, with very severe impacts on irrigation in a
part of Turkmenistan, but mainly in Uzbekistan.

6.7.2.1       Impacts on Rogun HPP
The Qosh Tepa Canal originates far downstream of Rogun, there will not be any direct impact of it on Rogun
HPP. However, the role of Rogun HPP (and potentially other storage schemes to be added in the future), and
mainly its capacity to provide additional water in extremely dry years, might become even more important. This
however would have to be addressed in the framework of water quota allocation in the entire Amu Darya basin.

6.7.2.2       Impacts Stemming from Rogun HPP
According to agreements, Rogun will not diminish the summer flow to the downstream area. In this way, it will
not have any effect on Qosh Tepa. However, since Rogun HPP can be operated to provide additional water in
dry years, irrigation in Afghanistan will benefit as well. This would be a positive impact on the Qosh Tepa project.

6.7.2.3       Potential Cumulative Impacts
Both projects, Rogun HPP as well as Qosh Tepa, have the potential to, or actually do, influence water availability
in the downstream area. Whether or not such a cumulative impact will arise, e.g. Rogun compensating, to the
extent possible, the lack of water due to Qosh Tepa, will depend on specific agreements on operation of the two
schemes. A hypothetical scenario of a “worst case” cumulative impact of Rogun HPP in combination with Qosh
Tepa canal is described and discussed in Section 9.7.

6.7.3     Mitigation
The most important mitigation measure in this situation would be to improve efficiency of existing and new
irrigation schemes, as already mentioned above. No information is available on whether the application of
modern, water saving irrigation technology will be applied in the Afghanistan irrigation scheme. According to
information available in the internet there is concern about the technology applied for building the new main
channel, which does not seem to prevent major losses to infiltration.
Whether Rogun HPP could play any additional role for mitigating negative impacts will depend on the situation
and on future agreements for water use in the Amu Darya basin.

6.7.4     Discussion
It is not possible, at this point in time and based on the available information, to make any more firm statement
on potential outcomes in this situation. The new situation will not materialise before 2037, when according to
plans both projects will be in operation. Still, it could be that the future role of Rogun reservoir for storing water
to be used in dry years would become even more important. The situation will also depend on additional
hydropower projects which eventually will or will not be developed in the Vakhsh and Pyanj catchments – or
potentially elsewhere in the Amu Darya basin.
It has to be pointed out very clearly that the Qosh Tepa Canal is a project developed and built by Afghanistan,
with no relation whatsoever to Rogun HPP, and without the possibility of any party involved in the development
of Rogun HPP to influence it in any way.
In the 2014 ESIA, the necessity of including Afghanistan in the water management agreements in the Amu Darya
basin was mentioned. This has become even more important now.


                                                                                                           PUBLIC
Page 42



The importance of including Afghanistan is stressed by the Hydrosolutions (2023) publication in the following
way: “The potential consequences extend beyond regional economic impacts. By fundamentally altering the
water balance in the Amu Darya River Basin, the Qosh Tepa Canal could stir geopolitical instability and strain
the delicate diplomatic fabric of Central Asia. The absence of Afghanistan in the existing water-sharing
agreements is a key complication, leaving a critical gap in regional water governance.”


6.8           New Road

6.8.1     Project Description
During the filling process of Rogun reservoir, the existing main road between Obi Garm and Nurobod will be
gradually submerged. Since this road, located in the Vakhsh valley between the two settlements, is the only
access to the upper part of Vakhsh valley, is one of two possible roads for reaching GBAO Oblast in the South-
East of Tajikistan, is the international road to Kirgizstan, and is also the only access to the villages to be resettled
in Stages 2 and 3 of the resettlement process, it needs to be replaced before being submerged. The replacement
road network (see Figure 6-13) consists of 6 different parts as follows:
      1. The road Obi Garm – Nurobod, main (part of main/international road).
      2. Main bridge crossing the reservoir (part of main/international road).
      3. Main road on left bank of upper part of reservoir (where a section of the existing road needs to be
         replaced; part of main/international road).
      4. The new road continuing on the right bank further East, to the North and along the upper part of the
         reservoir (local road).
      5. The new road on the left bank of the reservoir, between main bridge and Rogun dam site, providing
         access to the villages there (local road).
      6. The access roads to villages on the right bank of the reservoir which will lose access due to reservoir
         filling, connecting them to the new main road (local roads).




                                                                                                             PUBLIC
Page 43




Source: 2014 ESIA, Figure 3-5




Figure 6-13:   Replacement roads




                                   PUBLIC
                                                                                                             Page 44



The existing M41 road connecting Tajikistan to the Kyrgyz Republic will be replaced by the new Obi Garm –
Nurobod road (No. 1, main road); construction of this part is nearing finalisation. It is located in the mountain
range on the right bank of the lower part of the reservoir at an elevation between 1’305 m and 1’890 m asl. For
construction, the 75 km connecting Obi Garm to Nurobod are divided into two sections: 30 km between Obi
Garm and Tagikamar (including 2 tunnels of 1.6 km and 1.7 km), and 44 km between Tagikamar and Nurobod.
The plan for these two sections was to have them ready and opened for traffic in December 2024 (information
received from PMG 2024-09-18), and they were commissioned in early 2025 (PMG, 2025-06-09). A 760 m long
bridge (main bridge, No. 2) will cross the reservoir, from where the main road will continue on the left bank (No.
3). It is expected that the contractor for this bridge will be selected early in 2025.
The construction of these roads is definitely an inevitable replacement measure for Rogun HPP and could
therefore be considered as part of the mitigation measures for the hydropower project. However, for practical
reasons construction of the main road (No. 1) and the main bridge (No. 2) are handled as independent projects,
managed by a PMG which is part of the Ministry of Transport. Its financing is also not part of the financing
arrangements for Rogun dam and HPP. For these reasons, this is included in the CIA as New Road Project, an
independent project and one of the projects with potential cumulative impact with Rogun HPP. The other roads,
No. 4 to 6, are local access roads which are required as mitigation/compensation measures for Rogun HPP,
since the villages lose their present access due to the impoundment of Rogun reservoir. These facilities will be
funded by the AIIB and constructed by the Ministry of Transport and local contractors. (updated ESIA, Vol. 1,
Chapter 3, Project Description: Table 3-9).
For the CIA, the new main road (i.e. points 1-3 listed above) is of importance, while the other three parts of the
road replacement project are local access roads, whose impacts (and relevance) are dealt with in the ESIA
studies (2014 ESIA, 2019 Road ESIA, 2025 ESIA). Road section No. 1 (from Obi Garm to the existing main
bridge) is the part which is on the critical path, since it will be gradually affected in the early stages of reservoir
filling. The other section, including the existing main bridge, are located at higher elevations and will be affected
only at a later stage.

6.8.2   Potential Impacts
Additional sediment input into Rogun reservoir, mainly during construction, could be a potential impact of the
road project on Rogun HPP; in any case, this would be an impact of minor importance, due to the distance of
the road alignment.
A completely different type of effect, being an interaction between road and hydropower project, is of more
relevance:
    •   The reservoir has by now (May 2025) reached a level of 1075 m asl. At this level, it has already affected
        three sections of the existing road M 41: (i) shortly after Obi Garm, (ii) near Sicharog, and (ii) near
        Chorsada (see Figure 6-15 to Figure 6-17). Temporary access roads had to be built at these three
        locations, the section near Chorsada including two bridges, for allowing access to the upper part of the
        valley (Nurobod and Garm area and beyond). Of importance for the CIA: without access to the Nurobod
        area, it would not be possible to carry out stages 2 and 3 of the resettlement program. The temporary
        roads were built for being used until the reservoir reaches a level of 1110 m asl.
    •   When the level of the reservoir rises above 1110 m asl (see Table 6-8 for an intended schedule), parts
        of these temporary road sections will be submerged. The most critical one is the section near Obi Garm
        where, mainly due to the difficult topography at this precise place, the replacement road runs only slightly
        above the original road and above the water level indicated, and where yet another temporary solution
        would be difficult to implement.
    •   According to information received from DFZ, the replacement of the main road (without main bridge,
        which will be built at a later stage) is scheduled to be finalised by end of 2024.
    •   A delay in road construction would lead to submersion of parts of the existing road before the new road
        provides access. This could mean the following:
             o   If in this situation reservoir filling would go on, access to the Nurobod area would be interrupted,
                 and this would mean a delay in the resettlement program; this in turn could lead to a delay in
                 further reservoir filling.
             o   If priority would be given to resettlement, this could mean that reservoir impoundment would
                 have to be temporarily interrupted; this in turn could lead to a delay in hydropower project


                                                                                                            PUBLIC
                                                                                                           Page 45



                implementation, since with the water resources allocated to the project it might not be possible
                to recover such a delay in a following year.
This means that there is a clear interdependence between the two projects. Coordination between Rogun HPP
and road construction is provided by regular coordination meetings between the two PMGs. Still, the schedule
for road construction is important to be observed.


Table 6-8:      Schedule for filling Rogun Reservoir

 Year              Res. level         Res. volume      Annual retention      Res. surface      Surface increase
                      m asl               km³                 km³                 km²                km²

 2025                 1075               0.440                                     7                   7
 2026                 1110               1.317               0.877                15                   8

 2027                 1150               2.053               0.736                30                  15

 2028                 1170               3.240               1.187                40                  10

 2029                 1200               4.450               1.21                 55                  15

 2030                 1220               5.660               1.21                 76                  21

 2031                 1230               6.870               1.21                 85                   9

 2032                 1240               8.080               1.21                 90                   5

 2033                 1250               9.290               1.21                 100                 10

 2034                 1260               10.500              1.21                 110                 10

 2035                 1270               11.710              1.21                 132                 22

 2036                 1280               12.920              1.21                 145                 13

 2037                 1290               13.300              0.38                 159                 14



The following Figures illustrate the situation. Figure 6-14 shows the alignment of the new main road (under
construction, first part recently finalised) and indicates locations of the details shown in Figure 6-15 to Figure
6-17.
