The elevations shown in the Figures above are taken from the google earth satellite pictures and may therefore
not be very precise. Even so, they clearly illustrate the interdependence of reservoir filling and access to the
upper part of Vakhsh valley.

6.8.3   Other Potential Cumulative Impacts
According to information received from DFZ, the planned gas pipeline from Turkmenistan to China will, to the
extent possible, follow the alignment of the new road. In this way, the new road could provide access to some of
the construction sites to be installed along the pipeline alignment. This could be considered as a positive
interaction between these two projects (and an indirect positive impact of Rogun HPP on the pipeline project).
Since at present no detailed information is available on the pipeline project, no more precise statement on this
can be made at this point in time (see Section 6.10, pipeline project).

6.8.4   Mitigation
The need for minimising the erosion risk related to road construction is discussed in the 2019 Road ESIA; this
can be summarised in the following way.
    •   Erosion is recognised as a major problem in Tajikistan.
    •   The risk of local increase in erosion due to road construction is addressed.


                                                                                                            PUBLIC
                                                                                                            Page 49



      •   Mitigation: preparation and implementation of a Soil, Erosion and Topsoil Management Plan as part of
          the ESMP (2019 Road ESIA paragr. 415).
      •   The ESMP in the ESIA report outlines the Soil, Erosion and Topsoil Management Plan (Point 6 in Table
          53), listing general measures to be applied for minimising erosion related to construction activities. This
          it understood as a basis for the preparation of the CESMP (Contactor’s Environmental and Social
          Management Plan).
      •   Post-mitigation impact considered as of low risk and insignificant.
Road construction along the reservoir in difficult terrain certainly presents the risk of increased erosion, which in
turn could lead to an increased sediment load to the Rogun reservoir. The specific risk of additional sediment
reaching the Rogun reservoir due to the construction of the new road is not addressed in the 2019 Road ESIA.
However, as stated in the 2025 ESIA, it can be considered as minor.

6.8.5         Conclusion
The potentially more serious risk of a conflict between resettlement, i.e. access to the Nurobod area and beyond,
and reservoir filling requires a close coordination of the three activities involved, namely road construction,
resettlement and reservoir filling. This is given by the interactions between the two PMGs.
The construction of the new road along the right bank of the reservoir was finalised by end of 2024 and it was
opened for traffic in early 2025, which means that the risk of cutting the access to the resettlement sites no longer
exists.


6.9           Transmission Lines and Road Projects

6.9.1         Potential Conflicts
According to DFZ, concerns were raised in public hearings about potential conflicts between resettlement
villages and a number of projects for linear structures, i.e. transmission lines and roads. In a worst case, this
could potentially mean that households which had been relocated as part of the Rogun resettlement program
could be affected by such structures and would have to move again.
Projects potentially causing such impacts could be the following:
      •   CASA 1000 and TL projects related to Rogun HPP;
      •   Dushanbe-Tursunzoda-Uzbekistan border road project; and
      •   Dushanbe-Bokhtar road project.
Such cases of multiple resettlement would certainly have to be identified as conflicts between and cumulative
impacts of Rogun HPP and these other projects and therefore have to be included in the CIA. It has to be noted
that, although TLs to be constructed as part of the CASA 1000 program will be used for exporting electricity from
Rogun HPP to neighbouring countries, this latter is not directly related to and not dependent on Rogun HPP (WB
2014)

6.9.2     Transmission Lines
The following transmission lines (see map in Figure 6-18) might create conflicts with resettlement areas of Rogun
HPP:
      •   500 kV TLs specifically built for connecting Rogun HPP to the national grid.
      •   CASA 1000, section in Tajikistan. The Central Asia-South Asia Electricity Transmission and Trade
          Project (CASA 1000) is a project of a TL connecting Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Afghanistan and Pakistan
          with the aim of exporting electricity from Kyrgyzstan and Tajikistan to Afghanistan and Pakistan. A
          Regional Environmental Assessment (REA) was made for this project in 2014 (WB 2014). According to
          this study, this TL is not directly related to or depending on Rogun HPP, and therefore also not a project
          than can be seen as required for Rogun HPP. While construction of the parts of the line in three countries
          are largely finalised, construction in Afghanistan was interrupted in 2021. In February 2024 World Bank
          announced that an agreement was reached which allows to finalise this project
           (https://www.worldbank.org/en/news/statement/2024/02/23/casa1000resumptionafghanistan).             This

                                                                                                           PUBLIC
                                                                                                  Page 50



       was also confirmed in a meeting with Mr. Jamshed Shoimzod, First Deputy Minister, Ministry of Energy
       and Water Resources, on 2024-05-06.




Figure 6-18:   TL network (Rogun HPP and CASA 1000)



                                                                                                 PUBLIC
                                                                                                           Page 51



6.9.3   Conclusions
A discussion of the issues with the DFZ held on 2024-05-01 provided the following information:
    •   Transmission lines: there is no case of a conflict between resettlement areas and CASA 1000 or any
        other TL project. The issue was raised by a participant in a consultation meeting with resettled persons
        where one of the participants asked whether a new transmission line to be built in the area would present
        a risk for himself, his family and his house. This was clarified with the TL project proponents and it was
        confirmed that the distance between new TL and inhabited areas was sufficient and not creating any
        risk (information confirmed by PMG). In the case of any new TLs that might still have to be built, there
        would be enough flexibility in defining the alignment for avoiding conflicts with resettlement sites.
    •   Dushanbe-Tursunzoda-Uzbekistan border road project: this road was built in 2018. It passes at a
        distance of at least 10 km from the nearest resettlement village.
    •   Dushanbe-Bokhtar road project: road built in 2022. It does not pass near any of the resettlement villages.
DFZ was/is aware of these projects and would have brought up the issues, would there have been any risk of
impact on resettlement sites.
As a conclusion, it can be stated that there is no conflict between the resettlement progress for Rogun HPP and
any of these other projects. No mitigation measures are required.


6.10         Pipeline
There is a project for a gas pipeline from Turkmenistan to China (Central Asia – China gas pipeline) which would
pass near the Rogun HPP area. The project is presently on hold, no information on it is available. Two statements
were received on a potential alignment of this pipeline:
    •   The first information was that the pipeline would pass approximately parallel to the Rogun reservoir in a
        distance of about 30 km North of it. In this case, no cumulative impacts would have to be expected.
    •   According to information received from the DFZ, in the proximity of the project the pipeline would roughly
        follow the alignment of the new road (replacement road, see Section 6.8). In this case, it might be
        possible that the new road, at least in places, could serve as access road to some of the construction
        sites of the pipeline. Since this latter in any case will have to pass through rugged mountain territory,
        access to such construction sites might often be difficult, and having a road providing access would likely
        decrease the impact on landscape and habitats caused by such access roads. This would be a positive
        interaction between the two projects, however, with the level of available information it is not possible to
        come to any more precise conclusions.
Negative impacts of the pipeline on Rogun HPP are not expected.




                                                                                                         PUBLIC
                                                                                                                 Page 52



7             CONCLUSIONS
In this Chapter, the main conclusions from the detailed discussions of potential cumulative impacts are
summarised shortly.


7.1           Pipeline
Definition: The Central Asia-China Gas Pipeline is planned to pass north of Rogun reservoir. No detailed
information on this project, which is presently on hold, is available; see Section 6.10 for details.
Interactions and Cumulative Impacts: cumulative impacts with Rogun can be excluded. Two different
indications on potential pipeline arrangements were received, namely, (i) the pipeline to pass about 30 km north
of Rogun reservoir, and (ii) the pipeline to follow more or less the alignment of the replacement road. None of
these should be considered as a reliable plan, however, if the pipeline were to be built in a way as to follow more
or less the main replacement road, this latter might provide access to some construction sites of the pipeline, in
this way reducing the overall impact of these structures to some extent.
Conclusions: No relevant cumulative effects, no mitigation measures required.


7.2           Transmission Lines and Road Projects
Definition: This includes a number of linear structured of two different types, namely, 500 kV transmission lines
built for Rogun and/or as part of the CASA 1000 Project, and two road projects not related to Rogun HPP; see
Section 6.9; for details.
Interactions and Cumulative Impacts: There was a concern about a potential conflict between resettlement
villages (resettlement triggered by Rogun HPP and therefore part of the Project) and the land requirements for
these linear structures. The main concern was that in an extreme case this could mean that households that had
been resettled because of Rogun HPP would have to be relocated again because of one of these TL or road
projects. As explained in Section 6.9, the two roads in question have been built, and the alignment of the TLs,
as far as they have not yet been built as well, is known. There is no conflict with any of the Rogun-related
resettlement areas.
Conclusions: No relevant cumulative impacts, no mitigation measures required.


7.3           New Main Road
Definition: The existing road along Vakhsh River in the area of the Rogun reservoir will be submerged during
the process of reservoir impoundment. Impoundment has started already and is planned to continue till
approximately 2037, when the reservoir will reach its FSL. The road needs to be replaced, and this replacement
consists of the following main parts: (i) new main road which provides access to the Garm region, to GBAO and
to the border with Kirgizstan, (ii) new main bridge crossing the river (or, finally, the reservoir), and (iii) local access
roads to villages on both banks of the river/future reservoir, which lose their present access; see Section 6.8 for
details. The local roads are handled as projects on the local level and as direct mitigation measures related to
Rogun HPP. The new main road as well as the new bridge are being developed as separate projects with
separate financing and are therefore considered as separate projects, to be included in the CIA.
Interactions and Cumulative Impacts: Construction of the new main road in the very rugged terrain along the
Northern (right) bank presents a risk of causing additional sediment input into the reservoir. However, since road
construction does not take place in the immediate vicinity of the reservoir, this risk is considered as real, but
minor and manageable. Another issue is accessibility: the existing road has already been affected by the
increasing reservoir water level, and in three places temporary roads had to be built for allowing access to the
upper parts of the valley. The ongoing resettlement process for Rogun depends on this access. Should the
reservoir interrupt the existing road before the new one is available, the resettlement process could not go on.
On the other hand, reservoir filling would not be possible to continue if resettlement had not been done. A delay
in reservoir filling could be a problem for the Project since the amount of water that can be retained annually is
strictly limited by the relevant water allocation agreements. This means that there is a clear interdependence
between reservoir impoundment, resettlement and road construction.
Conclusions: Construction of the new main road is finalised, the road is open for traffic since early 2025; the
construction of the main bridge will follow later, reservoir impoundment has not reached that level yet. There is
no risk of blocked access to resettlement sites due to submersion of the old road.

                                                                                                                PUBLIC
                                                                                                               Page 53



7.4          Qosh Tepa Canal
Definition: The Qosh Tepa Canal is a project of a large irrigation canal which will derive water from Amu Darya
with the aim of irrigating up to 600,000 ha in Afghanistan. Only limited information on this project is available.
100 out the planed 285 km are built. The canal is expected to derive about 5 km³ of water per year, but depending
on source it has the capacity to derive as much as 10 to 20 km³ annually; see Section 6.7 for details.
Interactions and Cumulative Impacts: The canal, located downstream of Rogun HPP, has no impact on this
latter, and Rogun HPP, which will be operated under the accepted water allocation rules, will not have any impact
on the canal. However, the canal will interfere with water availability in the downstream area (Turkmenistan and
Uzbekistan). Rogun HPP has the potential to make additional water available in exceptionally dry years, and the
Qosh Tepa canal and the irrigation schemes alimented by it would benefit from this.
Conclusions: Qosh Tepa canal is developed without any relation with Rogun HPP, this latter Is not connected
in any way with the canal, and no cumulative impacts are no cumulative impacts to be expected between Rogun
HPP and the Qosh Tepa Canal. However, since both projects are related to the very relevant question of water
availability and water allocation in the Amu Darya basin, the canal needs to be included in the discussions on
water distribution. In this context it also has to be pointed out that Afghanistan is not a member of the
organisations regulating water allocation in the basin.


7.5          Irrigation in Tajikistan
Definition: So far, Tajikistan does not make complete use of the water allocated to it by the relevant international
agreement, mainly Protocol 566; in an average year, about 1.2 km³ (corresponding to about 6% of the average
annual flow of Vakhsh River or 2% of average annual flow of the Amu Darya) are not used and are therefore
available further downstream. Tajikistan plans to use this water for filling Rogun reservoir, a process which has
started already. Once the reservoir full, expected for 2037, Tajikistan will use this surplus water for expanding
its irrigation activities located downstream of Nurek; see Section 6.6 for details.
Interactions and Cumulative Impacts: In any case, Tajikistan intends to make use of the full share of water
allocated to it under Protocol 566, and it is committed to remain within the limitations of the protocol also for filling
and operating Rogun HPP.
Conclusions: There will be no change in water availability in the downstream area which would not be within
the limitations defined by Protocol 566.


7.6          Pyanj HPP Cascade
Definition: There is a plan for developing a cascade of HPPs on the Pyanj River, which, if implemented
completely, would consist in 15 HPPs with a total capacity of 17,900 MW and a cumulative storage volume of
32.5 km³; see Section 6.5 for details. None of these potential HPPs is in an advanced planning stage as yet, and
it is very unlikely that any one of these would be developed before the finalisation of Rogun HPP. Pyanj forms
the border with Afghanistan, which means that these projects would have to be developed as bi-national
endeavours.
Interactions and Cumulative Impacts: Since Rogun HPP and the Pyanj cascade are on different rivers, there
would not be any direct interactions between the two. However, since they are both located in the headwaters
of the Amu Darya, adding this cascade (or relevant parts of it) to the system would increase the potential for
influencing water availability in the downstream area.
Conclusions: A potential cumulative impact of Rogun HPP with the Pyanj cascade could be an influence on
water availability in the downstream area, mainly in Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan, but also in Afghanistan, once
Qosh Tepa Canal built. This, however, would become manifest only at some not yet definable point in the future.
Still, the fact that such a situation might actually develop just points out the importance of basin-wide agreements
on water use.


7.7          HPP Cascade Upstream of Rogun
Definition: As for Pyanj, there is a plan for developing two additional HPP cascades on Surkhob and Obihingou,
the two rivers which at their confluence form Vakhsh River; combined they would comprise 9 HPPs with a total
installed capacity of 5,800 MW and a total storage volume of 3.0 km³; see Section 6.4 for details. As for the
Pyanj cascade, none of these is in an advanced planning stage, and it is not envisaged to go ahead with this
development before Rogun HPP will be commissioned and in operation.
                                                                                                              PUBLIC
                                                                                                            Page 54



Interactions and Cumulative Impacts: Adding these two cascades to the same river system (Vakhsh) would
clearly result in a number of cumulative impacts. Since the planned cascades are upstream of Rogun, this latter
would not influence them in any way. However, a few points have to be mentioned:
      •   Hydrology: Surkhob and Obihingou cascades would add additional storage volume to the Vakhsh
          cascade. It has to be assumed that they would be developed and operated following the same conditions
          as for Rogun HPP, i.e. without any additional seasonal shift of water and by filling them using the
          available water volume according to Protocol 566; therefore, they would not in any way negatively impact
          water availability along the Amu Darya. Still, it needs to be observed that during the filling phase this
          water would not be available for irrigation within Tajikistan. On the other hand, the additional storage
          once filled this would increase the potential for releasing water in extremely dry summers, which would
          definitely be a positive effect on water use along the Amu Darya; concerning this issue see also Section
          9.6.
      •   River habitat fragmentation: The lower reaches of Vakhsh River were already fragmented by Nurek dam
          and the HPPs downstream of Nurek, and Rogun HPP adds to this by creating an additional barrier and
          by fundamentally changing habitat conditions on the entire length of its reservoir. Presently, there is no
          information available on the value of Surkhob and Obihingou as aquatic habitat, but building these
          additional dams would add to river fragmentation. The importance of this would have to be analysed in
          the ESIAs which would have to be prepared for this further development.
      •   Floodplain submersion: the filling of Rogun reservoir will submerge two major of floodplains. These are
          considered as natural habitats, although they do not fulfil the conditions as critical habitat. Adding the
          Surkhob cascade would in all likelihood have a negative impact on the numerous floodplains along this
          river (see Figure 6-10). This cumulative impact on this habitat type is shortly discussed in Section 6.4.2;
          see also the suggestions for a potential biodiversity offset in Section 8.3.
      •   Sedimentation: Additional dams upstream of Rogun would mean additional storage room for sediments.
          This would have the positive effect of reducing the amount of sediment reaching Rogun reservoir,
          thereby increasing the potential life span of Rogun HPP (and with it also of Nurek HPP). The overall
          cumulative effect on the entire Vakhsh cascade would be positive.
Conclusions: There would certainly be cumulative impacts of Rogun and the two upstream cascades, neutral
(or slightly positive) concerning hydrology, negative concerning biodiversity and aquatic habitats, and positive
concerning sedimentation and overall life span of the Vakhsh cascade.


7.8           Shurob HPP
Definition: Shurob HPP is planned to be built once Rogun has reached the operation stage, with a dam and
power house with 850 MW installed capacity at the upper end of Nurek reservoir and a reservoir reaching the
toe of Rogun dam. It would use the water released from Rogun and the remaining head between the two large
HPPs; see Section 6.3 for details.
Interactions and Cumulative Impacts: Shurob cannot be built without Rogun, as otherwise its small reservoir
would be filled with sediment very quickly. Once Rogun in place, the water released from the dam will be almost
free of sediments. Shurob reservoir will impound the remaining 17 river km between Rogun HPP and Nurek
reservoir. However, this impact is considered as very minor, since this is a short and completely isolated river
stretch which in addition to that will be exposed to the regime dictated by the operation pattern of Rogun HPP,
which will at least to some extent be a peaking pattern, i.e. with marked short-term fluctuations in water quantity
and flow velocity. Shurob will depend completely on water released from Rogun. One additional at least
marginally positive effect would be that it will offer job occasions during construction, where at least some of the
workers who will lose their job at the end of construction activities for Rogun could find a new source of income.
There would be no competition for workers between the two, since there will be no overlap of the two construction
phases; note that the two upstream cascades, if actually developed, would have the same effect.
Conclusions: Shurob, in all likelihood, will be a low-impact addition to the Vakhsh cascade, and it will contribute
substantially to electricity supply. No negative cumulative impacts are expected.


7.9           Vakhsh Cascade Downstream of Rogun
Definition: Rogun HPP will be a major addition to the cascade on Vakhsh River, which as of now consists of
one large storage scheme, Nurek HPP, and a cascade of smaller, basically ROR schemes downstream of it;
see Section 6.2 for details.
                                                                                                          PUBLIC
                                                                                                           Page 55



Interactions and Cumulative Impacts: Given the proximity of Rogun to the downstream cascade (same valley,
same river, short distance), potential cumulative impacts have to be analysed carefully. Main facts and
conclusions are the following:
   •   Hydrology, yearly flow pattern: as all large storage dams, Rogun has the potential to retain water during
       the high-flow season (in this case spring and early summer) for using it for electricity generation in the
       low-flow, high-demand season (winter). This would be to the detriment of downstream water users who
       have to rely on water for irrigation, with a high demand in the growing season. Tajikistan has committed
       itself to build and operate Rogun HPP respecting the agreed-upon rules for water allocation and water
       use among the riparian states in the Amu Darya basin, and especially Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan.
       This means that Tajikistan will not use more water than allocated to it under Protocol 566, and it will not
       shift water from summer to winter in addition to the 4.2 km³ already shifted by Nurek; this quantity
       corresponds to the live storage volume of Nurek and can be retained only after a winter reservoir
       drawdown to the Minimum Operation Level (MOL) of Nurek reservoir. The change in the cascade with
       Rogun HPP will be that yearly regulation will be done largely by Rogun, while Nurek reservoir will be
       kept at a more or less stable level throughout the year. Overall, this clearly means that for downstream
       water users the situation with Rogun HPP in place will not change; see also ESIA Vol. 1,. Ch. 13, Water.
   •   Hydrology, short-term fluctuations: in addition to seasonal storage, hydropower plants also have the
       potential for changing production very quickly (peaking regime). This allows the utility to very closely
       follow demand, which as a rule is not constant during the day, but usually follows a distinct pattern with
       times of high demand and times of low demand. This plasticity in production can be a problem for the
       river and its surroundings, including people living along and close to the river, since it can mean very
       marked short-term variations in river discharge and flow velocity, which has a potentially very negative
       impact on aquatic habitats and can be a safety risk for people. In the case of Rogun, however, it has to
       be pointed out that the area between Rogun dam and Nurek reservoir is not inhabited or used by people
       in any way and that therefore there is no safety risk for people, and that the value of this part of the river
       as habitat for aquatic organisms is very low (see ESIA Vol. 1, Ch. 15, Biodiversity). Presently, seasonal
       as well as daily peaking is done by Nurek. Once Rogun operational, this will be assumed by Rogun to a
       large extent. Nurek will still have to do some daily peaking, following the operation pattern of Rogun.
       The smaller schemes downstream of Nurek, which have only small (hourly or at most daily) regulating
       capacities, will in turn follow this pattern, smoothing out the short-term fluctuations in the process. At the
       downstream end of the cascade, river flow is constant with small if any daily fluctuations. The situation
       with Rogun in place will be the same as today.
   •   Riverine and aquatic habitats: Construction of Nurek dam and HPP changed the situation in Vakhsh
       River considerably, mainly in three respects:
           o   As mentioned, Nurek shifted 4.5 out of the 20 km³ flowing on average annually in Vakhsh River
               from summer to winter, in this way reducing the seasonal difference between the high- and the
               low-flow seasons, reducing the former and increasing the latter.
           o   Nurek reservoir retains as good as all the sediments transported by the river. The water
               downstream of Nurek is largely devoid of sediments, whether bedload or suspended solids.
           o   Like all storage reservoirs, Nurek reservoir also has a capacity for flood regulation. This means
               that flood events are smoothed out a least to some extent, especially by reducing peak flows
               during floods.
       These changes had a considerable negative impact on one important semi-aquatic habitat, namely the
       floodplain and Tugai vegetation in Tigrovaya Balka, since this habitat type largely depends on seasonal
       changes in water discharge and therefore water level, on sediment dynamics, and on occasional flood
       events. One additional negative impact of Nurek and the downstream cascade on biodiversity was the
       fact that any long fish migrations between the Amu Darya and the upper reaches of Vakhsh River, which
       under natural conditions may have been quite important, were completely interrupted.
       Rogun may have added to a small extent to a further reduction in short-range fish migrations from Nurek
       reservoir upstream. Other than that, the situation in the downstream area, especially concerning
       Tigrovaya Balka and fish migrations, will not be changed due to Rogun.
   •   Floods, PMF: Nurek is not able to safely manage PMF (Probable Maximum Flood), however, Rogun
       HPP was designed in a way as to be able to do so. This is a considerable improvement in risk
       management for the downstream area, reducing the risk of a dam break and its potentially disastrous
       consequences in this whole area. This is a very relevant positive cumulative impact of Rogun HPP with

                                                                                                          PUBLIC
                                                                                                          Page 56



       the downstream Vakhsh cascade, which may become even more important in the future, since under
       the conditions of climate change there is a risk of more marked and important short-time changes in
       river flow which may result in floods. Details for this still need to be evaluated and described in an
       Emergency Preparedness Plan (EPP). The EPP for the dam with crest elevation at 1110 m asl was
       submitted on February 29, 2024. The preparation of the EPP for the full dam is under way.
   •   Sedimentation: Rogun will retain sediments, in this way avoiding accumulation of additional sediment in
       Nurek reservoir. This will increase the total life span of Nurek HPP, a positive effect on the entire Vakhsh
       cascade.
   •   Joint operation of Rogun and Nurek HPPs will also increase electricity generation in Nurek to some
       extent; this is mainly due to the fact that there will no longer be a marked drawdown of Nurek reservoir
       in winter, thus allowing to benefit from the additional head.
   •   The addition of Rogun HPP to the Vakhsh cascade offers the potential to deliver additional water to the
       downstream area in the case of exceptionally dry years; this would be a considerable positive effect;
       see discussion of this topic in Section 9.6.
Conclusions: As main conclusions, the following can be said:
   •   Operating Rogun and the entire Vakhsh cascade while respecting the conditions defined in Protocol 566
       concerning water allocation, and especially by not shifting additional water from summer to winter will
       prevent any negative cumulative impacts on water users in the downstream area.
   •   The overall joint operation of the cascade will not lead to additional negative impacts on aquatic habitats
       and related biodiversity in the downstream area (especially: Tigrovaya Balka and fish migrations).
   •   The fact that Rogun will be able to handle the PMF is a positive effect for the downstream area by
       reducing the risk of a dam break event.
   •   Rogun HPP will add a considerable amount of electricity to the Tajik, and potentially to the Central Asian
       grid, without changing the water regime in the downstream area.
   •   Adding Rogun to the cascade will have a number of additional benefits, namely (i) increasing the life
       span or Nurek by retaining sediments, (ii) increasing to some extent energy production in Nurek HPP,
       and (iii) giving the possibility of releasing additional water to the downstream area in the case of dry
       summers.
Overall, it can be concluded that there will be no negative impacts, but some improvements in the downstream
area, due to the cumulative effects of Rogun HPP with the Vakhsh cascade.




                                                                                                        PUBLIC
                                                                                                            Page 57



8             MAIN CONCERN 1: BIODIVERSITY
The three aspects more or less directly related to biodiversity mentioned in Section 3.2.2 are discussed here,
with a focus on potential impacts caused by Rogun HPP and the other projects included in the CIA.


8.1           Aral Sea
The situation of the Aral Sea was described in detail in the 2014 ESIA. To summarise it very shortly: the northern,
smaller part of the former Aral Sea was separated by a dam from the much larger southern part. This dam serves
the purpose to keep the northern part, which is alimented by the Syr Darya, as a lake. The southern section has
almost disappeared, only a very small part still exists in the area which was originally the western, deepest part
of the Aral sea (Figure 8-1). There, salinity is so hight that no fish can live in this water any longer. When
mentioning the Aral Sea in this report, this clearly only refers to the southern (originally large) part of the former
Aral Sea.




Figure 8-1:      Situation of the Aral Sea 1984, 2000, 2010 and 2020 (from left to right)
                 Source: Google Earth


This situation is caused by the fact that very little water from the Amu Darya, the only relevant tributary, reaches
the lake any longer, in dryer than average years usually none at all; see details in Section 6.6.2.3. Ways to save
the Aral Sea by adducting water from other sources have been studied, but none of them is feasible.
Under BVO regulations a certain volume of water is allocated to the Aral Sea, but as Figure 8-2 shows, this
amount is often (in 10 out of the 15 years on record) not reached. In any case, the amount is too small to have
kept the Aral Sea from shrinking further, as Figure 8-1 clearly shows.




Figure 8-2:      Water allocated to and reaching the Aral Sea

                                                                                                           PUBLIC
                                                                                                          Page 58



                Data source: BVO (http://sic.icwc-aral.uz/reports_e.htm )
The following Figure gives an impression on water availability and water use in the Amu Darya basin, showing
total actual annual flow and total cumulated use.




Figure 8-3:     Total yearly water flow and use in Amu Darya basin
                Data source; http://sic.icwc-aral.uz/reports_e.htm


The Figure also shows something else which is of high relevance in the context with Rogun HPP: while there
seems to be some correlation between balance (i.e. water not used) and inflow to the Aral Sea, these two
parameters are not directly correlated. It cannot be assumed that the total volume of unused water in the basin
would automatically reach the Aral Sea. This is clearly shown by the data for the last few years, where there was
a slight increase of unused water without a corresponding increase of water reaching the Aral Sea. This also
means that it cannot be assumed that any change in water released from Rogun would result in a same effect
on inflow to the Aral Sea.


Recently, USAID has started two projects in the Aral Sea region (https://www.usaid.gov/central-asia-
regional/fact-sheets/usaid-environmental-restoration-aral-sea-ii ) :
    •   USAID Environmental Restoration of the Aral Sea I: this is a project “to provide environmental restoration
        measures to improve the resilience of landscapes in the Northern Aral Sea zone. The implementation
        of the Environmental Restoration of the Aral Sea I Activity supports regional transboundary cooperation
        for water and environment and enhances resilience to climate change for vulnerable populations and
        ecosystems.” (UDAID Fact Sheet). The Environmental Restoration of the Aral Sea I Activity aims to
        introduce innovative measures to create an “Oasis” using black saxaul (Haloxylon ammodendron) in the
        Northern Aral Sea zone.
    •   USAID Environmental Restoration of the Aral Sea II “aims to employ, demonstrate, and evaluate a
        variety of innovative technologies and planting and irrigation practices that will add new dimensions to
        the regional knowledge base on afforestation of desert biomes” (USAID Fact Sheet).
Both of these projects aim at improving the environmental situation in the area of the former Aral Sea, but it must
be clearly understood that the aim is not (and cannot be) a restoration of the Areal Sea as it existed until about
the 1950s. The Oasis being created by this project is located in Kazakhstan, close to the Northern Aral Sea and
far outside the area that could be reached by water flowing into the lake basin from the Amu Darya. A request
for additional information sent to the e-mail address indicated in the above-mentioned home page was not
answered.
There is no interaction of any kind between Rogun and these two USIAD projects. There is also no cumulative
impact between these two projects and Rogun HPP.
                                                                                                        PUBLIC
                                                                                                            Page 59



As shown in the scenarios in Section 9.7, the fact that Tajikistan will use its full share of water, i.e. on average
1.2 km³ (equal to 2% of the total Amu Darya flow) in addition to the volume used presently, could lead to an
increase in frequency of years when very little or no water will reach the Aral Sea, depending on the consumption
pattern of Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan. However, as shown above there is no direct relationship between the
amount of unused water and inflow to the Aral Sea, and in any case the quantities are too small as to cause a
noticeable change in the situation of the Aral Sea, and it has to be pointed out that this situation will materialise
with or without Rogun HPP, and is in compliance with ICWC/BVO regulations.


8.2           Fish Migration
It has to be assumed that originally there were rather frequent and relevant fish migrations between Amu Darya
and Vakhsh River; a number of fish species as recorded in the 2014 and the 2025 ESIA are in fact migrating
species, usually moving upstream for reproduction. However, no detailed data on the situation before the
construction of Nurek dam are available. A recent overview of the development of the Aral Sea – and the Aral
Sea crisis – mentions three species of fish which originally migrated over a distance of more than 1000 km
upstream from the lake, without however giving any further detail, and especially without mentioning if such
migrations might have reached into Vakhsh River (Plotnikov et al., 2023). Of the two sturgeon species of the
Amu Darya, both critically endangered according to IUCN, one, the Small Amu Darya Shovelnose Sturgeon
(Pseudoscaphirhynchus hermanni), is endemic to the middle and lower reaches of Amu Darya and may never
have reached Vakhsh river, while the second one, the Large Amu Darya Shovelnose Sturgeon
(Pseudoscaphirhynchus kaufmanni), may still occur in the lower parts of Vakhsh river. Around 2012, an attempt
was started for an artificial breeding program for this latter species (https://life-on-earth.ru/sokhranenie-
bioraznoobraziya/amudarinskie-lopatonosy-reka-vakhsh-tadzhikistan), which however does not seem to have
produced positive results. A later project working with specimens from both species from populations
downstream or Tuyamuyun reservoir, Uzbekistan, was stopped in 2021 due to lack of funding and problems
presented by the COVID-19 pandemics (https://life-on-earth.ru/amu-darya-and-syr-darya-shovelnose-
sturgeons/amu-darya-shovelnose-sturgeons-conservation-uzbekistan).
The fact is that all long-range fish migrations in Vakhsh river were completely interrupted by the construction of
Nurek dam. There is no information on whether the Large Amu Darya Sturgeon may have reached the area of
Nurek and beyond before its construction. The additional, smaller dams downstream of Nurek contributed to a
further fragmentation of the lower reaches of Vakhsh River and may have led to interruptions of short-range
migrations.
The construction of Rogun dam does not change this situation. Even if there is a possibility of interrupting some
fish migration from Nurek reservoir further upstream, this is considered as an impact of very minor relevance in
both ESIAs. Since, as shown above, Rogun HPP will not change river flow patterns, neither seasonal nor short-
term ones, downstream of the Vakhsh cascade, it will also not change in any way present conditions for fish
migration in the Amu Darya Basin.


8.3           Tigrovaya Balka
Potential impacts of Rogun HPP on Tigrovaya Balka is an issue of concern among organisations involved in
biodiversity conservation.
Tigrovaya Balka is a nature reserve in the lower reaches of Vakhsh River, at its confluence with Pyanj River. It
protects a Tugai habitat, a type of wetland characteristic for Central Asia, which is getting increasingly rare; see
the 2014 ESIA (Chapter 12) for a detailed description. It is also a UNESCO World Heritage Site (see
https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1685).
Like all such floodplain wetlands, its long-term existence depends on river dynamics, and especially on the
following conditions:
      •   a meandering river which will shift its bed from time to time, creating in the process so-called oxbows,
          i.e. lake-like sections of former river beds cut from the present river flow;
      •   seasonal flooding due to a strong seasonal regime of the river, with a high-flow season (which here is
          the snow melt season, i.e. late spring and summer) and a low flow season (winter);
      •   occasional very high floods, often capable of changing the landscape by leading to shifts in river bed
          and sediment transport; and



                                                                                                          PUBLIC
                                                                                                            Page 60



    •   sediment dynamics, including transport, deposition and remobilisation of sediments depending on river
        discharge and flood events.



 Tugai: The typical floodplain forests (riparian forests) grow on slightly higher areas, which are covered by
 water only for short durations. The soils are alluvial silts or sandy silts. The most important tree species is
 Populus diversifolia (Turanga) with Populus pruinosa (Petta). The trees grow to a height of 13-15 m and reach
 an age of 35-40 years. Timber stock amounts to 90-200 m³ per ha. Undergrowth is formed by willows (Salix)
 with Eleagnus orientalis on dryer places. Tamarix spp. and Halimodendron halodendron occur on slightly
 brackish soils. Lianas are abundant and reach up to the tree crowns (Clematis orientalis, Cynanchium
 sibiricum, Asparagus persicus).
 Source: Walter and Breckle 1986: p. 292 (translated from German)



As described in the 2014 ESIA, water regime and sediment transport in the downstream area of Vakhsh River
were changed fundamentally by the construction of Nurek dam and its operation as a seasonal storage reservoir.
This caused changes in the Tigrovaya Balka Tugai, which still continue.
Rogun HPP will not change this situation (see Section 6.2.3 for a detailed discussion of this issue), and the
habitat dynamics (or its absence) in Tigrovaya Balka will still be determined by Nurek HPP. There will be no
additional negative effect of Rogun HPP on Tigrovaya Balka. This means that a negative impact of Rogun HPP,
or a cumulative impact of Rogun in combination with the Vakhsh cascade, can be excluded, and that therefore
there is no obligation for Rogun HPP to mitigate the impact.
Having said this, however, it has to be pointed out that Tigrovaya Balka is still a very valuable and fragile habitat
type in the wider project area, which could benefit greatly from careful management aiming at improving the
prevailing conditions. In case such an undertaking should be envisaged, the following would need to be done:
    •   Checking whether any of the measures proposed in the 2014 ESIA were implemented; this includes the
        habitat improvement program that was envisaged or under way at that time.
    •   Checking whether in the meantime any other habitat management programs were proposed and
        implemented.
    •   Analysis of the present situation (field work including vegetation, fish and avifauna, potentially other
        groups as well). Description of the development of the situation in comparison with earlier assessments.
    •   Extensive involvement of relevant stakeholders (local inhabitants, local, national and international nature
        conservation organisations which were involved in the protection of the area, etc.).
Based on this, a plan for improving the situation could then be established and implemented. It is probable that
the main contribution of the HPP cascade to an improvement of the situation would be the creation of occasional
floods during the natural flooding season, i.e. between April and July. Under present conditions, i.e. almost
without any sediments being transported, these floods could only serve the purpose of periodically reaching a
normal high water level within the floodplain, with the aim of eliminating plants that do not belong to the floodplain
habitats, but are now invasive in some parts which are no longer covered with water more or less regularly. This
would serve the purpose of maintaining the specific Tugai vegetation. Such seasonal flooding, however, could
not restore the floodplain dynamics as such, which under natural conditions would include the occasional shift
of the river bed as well as local accumulations or removals of sediment. In no case should such floods be
undertaken without a careful preparation, planning and monitoring.
As already mentioned in Section 6.4.3, such a rehabilitation program, especially if it would also include other
sites and types of floodplain habitat, would have to be done independently of Rogun HPP.
Loss of floodplains due to Rogun reservoir is one of the impacts to be addressed for reaching NNL. The BMP
(2025 ESIA, Volume 3, A04: Biodiversity Management Plan, Table 3-3 - BMP Actions) specifies this as follows:
               “1a(i): An off-site area will be managed to compensate for the loss of approximately 77ha of
               floodplain habitat. This compensation will be developed and delivered through a no net loss plan.
               At this stage, a No Net Loss Framework (NNLF) has been produced and is appended to this BMP.
               The NNLF will demonstrate how the requirements of ESS6 will be delivered with regards to losses
               of floodplain Natural Habitat and will also identify key stakeholders required to be engaged
               with/consulted as the NNL process develops. Measures to compensate for losses will comprise:

                                                                                                           PUBLIC
                                                                                                        Page 61



                   •   Restoration and protection of ‘tugai’ habitat
                   •   A contingency restoration and protection action for native fruit and nut woodland
              Both options represent ‘better than like for like’’ measures based upon the higher conservation
              importance of both of these habitat types (compared with the floodplain habitat being lost).
              1a(ii) Upon agreement of the option(s) available to achieve no net loss to floodplain habitat, a
              shortlist of NNL sites will be produced and submitted for Government/Lender approval. A NNL
              management, protection and monitoring plan will be produced that will provide detail necessary
              to deliver the required NNL. This will be an operational document that will be produced and
              implemented by end-2030 (i.e. dam completion date).”
The No Net Loss Framework (NNLF) developed as part of the ESIA (Vol. 3, A5) carefully identifies and evaluates
possibilities for achieving NNL. In this process, Tigrovaya Balka was eliminated as a suitable candidate location
for this purpose.




                                                                                                       PUBLIC
                                                                                                             Page 62



9             MAIN CONCERN 2: WATER AVAILABILITY IN THE DOWNSTREAM AREA

9.1           Water Use and Potential Conflicts of Interest
As mentioned in the Report, water availability, water use and water allocation among riparian states is a major
issue in Central Asia, and specifically in the Amu Darya basin. In terms of quantity of water used, there are two
main types of water uses, namely, hydropower and irrigation. Irrigation is a consumptive type of water use: once
diverted from the river, much of the water is lost through evaporation, evapotranspiration from plants and
infiltration, especially in the case of inefficient irrigation systems where losses due to infiltration are very
substantial. This means that little if any of the water diverted to irrigation systems finds its way back to the river.
Unlike irrigation however, hydropower is a non-consumptive type of water use, meaning that if in the upstream
region water is used for generating electricity it is still available for irrigation in the downstream area. Therefore
the reason for potential conflicts between hydropower and irrigation are not founded in the use of water as such,
but in the different seasonal requirements: irrigation needs water in the growing season, mainly spring and
summer, low flows in winter are not an issue. Hydropower, on the other hand, requires more water during winter,
when electricity demand is highest, but which is also the low-flow season; storage dams with their large
reservoirs therefore aim at retaining water during summer for using it in winter.


9.2           Present Water Use in Tajikistan
As outlined in Section 5.2, water allocation among the riparian states Kirgizstan, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan and
Uzbekistan is defined by ICWC/BVO. Based on the forecast on expected water quantity in the following
vegetation period derived from observations of snow cover in the upper reaches of the Amu Darya basin, each
country is allocated a certain amount of water. Figures on this allocation as well as on actual consumption are
provided in a BVO home page (http://sic.icwc-aral.uz/reports_e.htm ); these figures are also shown in Annex 2.
The following Figures show present use of this water in Tajikistan for the period 2010 to 2024, the period for
which data are available on the internet site mentioned above.




Figure 9-1:      Seasonal and yearly water use by Tajikistan
                 In the BVO homepage, values for the winter 2012-2013 are missing; they were replaced here by the
                 avarage of all 13 winter values on record.


The Figure shows that Tajikistan uses less water than its allocated share, as shown also in the following Table.




                                                                                                            PUBLIC
                                                                                                               Page 63

This shows that on average during the period on record, Tajikistan was not using 1.36 km³ of its allocation per
year. According to information received from the Energy and Water Policy Department (oral comm.), the fact
that recently consumption almost reached allocation is mainly due to the fact that there were some dry years,
but also some major usage of water for flushing fields of salts that had accumulated in past years. However, it
has to be seen that some water has already been retained in Rogun reservoir, and this amount of water is
included here. In the middle and long term, as already mentioned, Tajikistan intends to use its full share, first for
filling Rogun reservoir, later on for expanding its irrigation. The important point here is that this will be done within
the limitations defined by BVO.


9.3            Importance of Electricity
For Tajikistan, and in the context of the conditions in Central Asia, electricity has the following characteristics
and importance:
      •   Abundant water resources in combination with the mountainous terrain provide ideal conditions for
          hydropower in Tajikistan.
      •   Electricity is required for satisfying the increasing domestic demand; until very recently, electricity supply
          was not sufficient for supplying the entire population, especially in winter, and in some remote areas this
          is still an issue.
      •   Given the abundant resources, electricity is also a potential source of income for Tajikistan by selling it
          to neighbouring countries (CASA 1000 project).
      •   Hydropower is very suitable for peaking, i.e. for responding to short-term demand changes in the grid;
          this could be supplied by base-load from thermal power plants in neighbouring countries.
      •   Hydropower also plays an important role in decarbonising the energy sector, i.e. as a measure to reduce
          climate change.


The 2017 Energy Sector Master Plan for Tajikistan lists a potential for hydropower of 28,610 MW (see Table
9-2).
                                                                                                               PUBLIC
                                                                                                                       Page 64




Table 9-2:         Hydropower potential in Tajikistan

 River                    Estimated capacity* MW         Comments
 Vakhsh                             4,450                Capacity includes Rogun HPP (3,600MW) + Shurob HPP (850
                                                         MW)
 Surkhob                            1,800                MoEWR 2024: 2,100 MW**
 Obihingou                          1,750                MoEWR 2024: 3,700 MW
 Kafirnigan                         1,450                Tajik tributary to Amu Darya downstream of Vakhsh
 Zeravshan                          1,260                River in the North of Tajikistan, tributary of Syr Darya, not part of
                                                         the Amu Darya basin.
 Pyanj                              17,900               MoEWR 2024: 17,900 MW
  Total                             28,610           MoEWR data bring total up to 30,860 MW
*Source: Corporate Solutions, 2017: 84
** Data received from MoEWR (Ministry of Energy and Water Resources), see Sections 6.4 and 6.5.


In 2017, installed hydro capacity amounted to 4,926 MW (Corporate Solutions 2017). This indicates that there
is still a very considerable potential for additional capacity.


Comments on the 2014 and the updated ESIA noted that selection of a lower dam would reduce the number of
people who would have to be resettled, causing only a small reduction in electricity generation. It is certainly
correct that a lower dam height would have reduced the need for resettlement, as was discussed in the 2014
ESIA. However, the point of “small reduction electricity generation” needs to be considered with somewhat more
detail. This is done in Table 9-3.


Table 9-3:         Comparison of dam height alternatives

                                                                      Dam height alternatives
 Parameter
                                               FSL 1290                       FSL 1255                       FSL 1220
 Dam height                                       335                             300                           265
 Reservoir area km²                               159                             114                            68
 Reservoir volume km³                             13.3                            8.5                            5.2
 Installed capacity MW                           3,600                           3,200                         2,800
 Resettlement Villages                             77                             36                             25

  Life span electricity TWh                    4,830,000                    1,350,000                      585,000
* Figures from the 2014 ESIA; present estimation is at about 55’000 persons to be relocated in total, and Rogun HPP
installed capacity is 3,780 MW.


Just looking at installed capacity and yearly electricity output, a reduction by 11% and 14% respectively might
be considered as minor. However, the issue is the life span of the alternatives. If electricity output over the life
span is considered, a reduction of the dam height by 35 m would reduce electricity output by 72%. It has to be
assumed that this difference would have to be compensated by building alternative power plants, which would
create impacts, probably in the form of resettlement, as well.

                                                                                                                       PUBLIC
                                                                                                               Page 65



9.4            Potential Future Development
It cannot be the aim of this report to prepare a detailed analysis of the future changes in the water sector in the
Amu Darya basin. However, as shown there is a clear potential – and certainly a requirement – for further
development. The most important items are:
      •   Implementation of additional hydropower schemes, some of which potentially with large storage
          volumes.
      •   Additional water demand for irrigation (in Tajikistan, but to a large extent in Afghanistan, see Section
          6.7); this latter has the potential for greatly increasing water abstraction from the system, to the detriment
          of existing infrastructure.
      •   Efforts in existing irrigation systems for increasing efficiency, in this way reducing water demand.
All this will happen under the influence of climate change (see the following Section).


9.5            Climate Change
Whatever the success – or failure – of measures for reducing GHG output to the atmosphere will be, Rogun
HPP and the further development of water resources in the Amu Darya basin will have to deal with the effects
of climate change. One main concern is that, since water shortage regularly causes severe problems in dry
years, especially in the lower reaches of Central Asian rivers, this situation could worsen in the future, given the
fact that climate change is expected to have some negative effects, although overall precipitation will probably
not diminish. However, rising temperatures will lead to an increase in evapotranspiration, the expected higher
irregularity in precipitation could lead to an increase in the number of extremely wet and extremely dry years,
and earlier snow melt will mean more water in spring and less in summer.


9.6            Role of Rogun
Rogun HPP is only one, although certainly rater important, component of the entire water use systems and
structures within the Amu Dary basin. Since it will be operated in compliance with the water allocation rules
according to Protocol 566, it will not cause any difference in water availability for the downstream water users.
However, as analysed in detail in the 2014 ESIA, Rogun HPP will have the potential to provide additional water
during the growing season of an exceptionally dry year, and this would be a considerable benefit for the irrigation
systems in the entire downstream area. This could only be done under the following conditions:
      •   Releasing additional water from Rogun (and/or Nurek) reservoir in summer would mean less capacity
          for electricity generation in the following winter.
      •   It would also require the possibility for refilling the reservoirs after such a dry year, which would certainly
          need an amount of water which would be outside of current ICWC allocations.
Having said this, it still has to be seen that Rogun HPP certainly has the potential to change the situation if it
would be operated in a way which would not respect the international agreements. The 2014 TEAS and ESIA
studies have demonstrated that Rogun HPP can be added to the Vakhsh cascade and operated in a way that
will not change water availability and seasonal distribution in the downstream area. This is also recognised in
the final report of the Environmental and Social Panel of Experts in its Final Report (E&S POE, 2014), which
nevertheless points out the potential that Rogun HPP would have for being operated in a different way, mainly
by shifting additional water from summer to winter, with potentially very negative impacts on water users,
especially irrigation, in the downstream riparian countries. It is understandable that this is still a concern of
stakeholders who would potentially be affected by such changes. Therefore, this issue needs to be discussed,
and this is done in the following Section, which describes and analyses a number of scenarios, as also asked
for by the Amendments to the CIA TOR dated September 2024 by WB, covering potential outcomes.


9.7            Alternative Operation Patterns of Rogun HPP

9.7.1     Purpose of the Section
The main conditions for operating Rogun HPP, as defined in the 2014 TEAS and ESIA and adopted by Tajikistan
are the following:

                                                                                                              PUBLIC
                                                                                                             Page 66



    •   Filling Rogun reservoir only with the surplus water not used so far from the volume allocated by BVO,
        i.e. 1.2 km³ per year on average (potentially somewhat more in wet years and considerably less in dry
        years). With this amount, the reservoir could be filled in about 11 years, but in reality it might take longer;
        the 16 years foreseen as filling period take potential delays in the process into account.
    •   No additional shift of water from summer to winter.
By respecting these two conditions, which are operative design constraints for developing the project, Tajikistan
will operate Rogun HPP within the limits determined by Protocol 566 and the yearly water allocation by BVO.
Still, the possibility for alternative forms of operation of Rogun HPP remain, a fact that raises concern among
stakeholders. In this Section, such possibilities are developed in the form of a number of scenarios, and their
potential outcome and consequences are discussed.

9.7.2   Available Data
The scenarios are built on the data shown in Annex 2, available from the website already mentioned
(http://sic.icwc-aral.uz/reports_e.htm ). This site provides data for the time span of 2010 to 2024, i.e. for 14 years
(where by “year” here has to be understood as the period from April 1 till March 31 of the following year). For the
purpose of scenario development, this time span was extended to a duration of 25 years in the following way:
    •   The missing data for winter of October 2012 to March 2013 were replaced by the calculated average of
        all available winter values.
    •   The data of a number of years were duplicated in order to simulate a total duration of 25 years.
This means that, while all the data represent real recorded values, the 25 years shown in the graphs in the
following sections do not represent a real 25 years period. Therefore in the graphs no actual years are indicated,
they are just numbered 1 to 25.

9.7.3   Formulation of Scenarios
For the purpose of this report, 5 scenarios were developed, as follows:
    •   Scenario 1: Situation without Rogun HPP. Assumptions:
            o    all countries continue to use water as they did till now;
            o    Tajikistan continuing to not use the full share allocated;
            o    Afghanistan (which is not included in BVO) assumed to use the 2.1 km³ annually as per the
                 standard BVO distribution (see Table 5-1).
    •   Scenario 2: Tajikistan Using Full Allocation. Assumptions:
            o    conditions as in Scenario 1;
            o    starting in year 9, Tajikistan uses its full allocation (whether for filling Rogun reservoir or for
                 irrigation purposes);
            o    this is assumed to be, in addition to the water consumed as per the available data,
                     ▪   +1.2 km³ in a wet year
                     ▪   +1.0 km³ in an average year
                     ▪   +0.8 km³ in a dry year
                     ▪   0.2 out of which in winter.
            o    TKM and UZB keeping their share; in this simplified approach, it is assumed that the difference
                 would represent a direct reduction of water reaching the Aral Sea.
    •   Scenario 3: Rapid Reservoir Filling. Assumptions:
            o    Same conditions as Scenario 2, but Tajikistan filling the reservoir rapidly with the following
                 assumptions:
                     ▪   Filling in years 15 to 17;
                     ▪   3.5 km³ in year 15;
                                                                                                            PUBLIC
                                                                                                            Page 67



                    ▪    5 km³ each in years 16 and 17;
            o   Difference shared by TKM and UZB, with a slightly larger reduction for UZB.
            o   After filling of Rogun reservoir return to same conditions as in Scenario 2.
    •   Scenario 4: Additional Shift. Assumptions:
            o   Same conditions as Scenario 3, but after filling the reservoir, starting in year 18 Rogun HPP is
                operated by shifting an additional 5 km³ of water from summer to winter.
    •   Scenario 5: Qosh Tepa. Assumptions:
            o   Same conditions as Scenario 4, but starting in year 20 Qosh Tepa canal operates by deviating
                10 km³ of water yearly, 8 of which in summer and 2 in winter.
The number of scenarios could be greatly increased just by changing the assumptions made for each one, but
this would only produce gradual and overall rather minor differences in outcome. The main consequences of
these outcomes is shown and discussed in the following Section.

9.7.4   Potential Outcomes
On the following pages, the results according to the 5 scenarios are shown as follows:
    •   All countries: seasonal water consumption of all BVO countries plus Afghanistan is shown, in order to
        indicate the consequences of the different conditions for all affected parties.
    •   Situation for Uzbekistan, seasonal consumption. As mentioned above (scenario 3), it is assumed that
        the loss in available water will be somewhat greater for Uzbekistan than for Turkmenistan; this is done
        because Uzbekistan, being the most downstream of the potentially affected countries, is likely to suffer
        most in case of water shortages. Nevertheless, the situation for Turkmenistan will be similar.
    •   Situation for Uzbekistan, summer (vegetation period, April to September). The critical period for the
        countries depending largely on irrigation, is the summer period, while water flowing in winter cannot be
        used. For a better understanding of the consequences, in these graphs the average values for wet,
        average and dry summers are indicated. The definition of these three groups is based on the data used,
        which show a range for summer values between 17.2 and 11.3 km³, and was made as follows: wet
        summer: >15.3 km³, average 16.52 km³; average summer: 13.3 to 15.3 km³, average 14.27 km³; dry
        summer: <13.3 km³, average 12.21 km³.
    •   Situation of the Aral Sea. This is the Large Aral Sea, i.e. the former larger, southern, part of the original
        Aral Sea, which by now has almost disappeared and receives only the small remaining amount of water
        not used further upstream. Here, it is assumed that a reduction of water due to increased consumption
        in Tajikistan would directly lead to a reduction in inflow to the Aral Sea, which, as was discussed in
        Section 8.1, is not necessarily the case.
