9.7.5   Discussion of Outcomes
As already mentioned, the five scenarios presented in the previous Section show a number of possible
outcomes, but they could be modified. Here, just a few potential alternatives to the ones emphasised are listed:
    •   Situation of the Aral Sea: as shown, under baseline conditions the inflow to the Aral Sea varies from
        17.4 km³ (year 1 in the graphs, 2010, apparently exceptionally humid in the original data) to almost 0
        (five years with an inflow of <0.5 km³ in the data series). All scenarios show an increase in the number
        of years with such a minimum inflow, but in reality, should e.g. scenario 4 or 5 become reality, there is
        a high probability of many more such dry years, while on the other hand, if winter flows should increase,
        there might actually be a possibility for substantially higher total annual inflows, with much of it in winter.
    •   Water distribution between Uzbekistan and Turkmenistan: as indicated, in the scenarios it is assumed
        that the two countries would bear the loss in available water to about similar shares, with however the
        assumption that the losses for Uzbekistan, being the lowermost in the supply chain (with the exception
        of the Aral Sea), would be slightly greater. Still, it could also happen that in the case Tajikistan would no
        longer honour the international agreements, Turkmenistan could decide to act in the same way, taking
        a similar volume of water as before. This would put Uzbekistan in a still much more difficult situation.
    •   Additional shift of water from summer to winter by Tajikistan: the figure of 5 km³ annually was chosen
        for the scenario, but it could be smaller or potentially even somewhat larger, with the corresponding
        differences in outcome.
    •   Qosh Tepa canal deviating 10 km³ of water annually was chosen in order to create a worst case
        scenario. According to estimates, 5 km² per year seems to be a more realistic assumption, but even this
        would have serious consequences.
The five scenarios as described in the previous Sections are shortly evaluated here.
    •   Scenario 1: Situation without Rogun HPP.
            o   This is the baseline case, i.e. the situation before impoundment at Rogun started.
            o   No change in the downstream area.
            o   In conformity with Protocol 566.
            o   No additional generation of electricity, continuing energy shortage in Tajikistan.
            o   Nurek still at risk by a PMF, which it is not able to handle safely; no reduction in flooding risk for
                the downstream area.
            o   Life span of Nurek will not increase, limited by sediment input.
    •   Scenario 2: Tajikistan using its full share.
            o   In the downstream area, water availability will be reduced by on average 1.2 km³ (corresponding
                to 6% of Vakhsh or 2% of Amu Darya flow in an average year) as compared to the baseline
                case.
            o   In the scenario it was assumed that this difference in water availability will not change water
                consumption in Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan, but that the inflow to the Aral Sea would be
                reduced.
            o   This is the situation which will become reality as soon as Tajikistan is using its full share, which
                it will do, first for filling Rogun reservoir and then for increasing irrigation. This situation will arise
                with or without Rogun.
            o   This scenario is in conformity with Protocol 566 (ICWC/BVO regulations).
    •   Scenario 3: Fast filling of Rogun reservoir.
            o   Assumes that the reservoir will be filled within a period of 3 years, by retaining 3.5, 5 and 5 km³
                of water in three consecutive years.
            o   Would cause serious water shortage in the downstream riparian countries during these 3 years.
            o   Procedure in clear violation of international agreements (Protocol 566, ICWC/BVO).
    •   Scenario 4: Additional shift of water.

                                                                                                                PUBLIC
                                                                                                         Page 73



            o    This scenario assumes that after the fast filling of the reservoir Rogun HPP would shift a volume
                 of 5 km³ per year from summer to winter, in addition to the volume already shifted by Nurek
                 HPP.
            o    This would sharply decrease the water volume available in the downstream riparian countries
                 in summer, turning basically all summers into dry to very dry ones as compared to the baseline
                 condition.
            o    At the same time river discharge in winter would increase, i.e. during the non-vegetation period.
            o    Procedure in clear violation of international agreements (Protocol 566, ICWC/BVO).
    •   Scenario 5: Qosh Tepa
            o    Situation like in Scenario 4.
            o    In addition, it is assumed that Afghanistan would divert 10 km³ of water annually via the Qosh
                 Tepa canal.
            o    This would turn the situation for Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan in a state of perpetual extremely
                 dry summers, with far less water available than ever before.
            o    Clear violation of international agreements (Protocol 566, ICWC/BVO) by Tajikistan.
            o    Operation of Qosh Tepa canal would greatly contribute to a deterioration of the situation,
                 but is outside of Protocol 566 and cannot be influenced by ICWC/BVO.
The conclusions from this analysis are rather clear and obvious:
    •   Scenario 2 will be realised in any case, with or without Rogun, since Tajikistan has clearly announced
        its intention to use the water which is allocated to the country by the corresponding international
        agreements. This is a situation which is accepted by BVO/Protocol 566. The question where these on
        average 1.2 km³ per year will actually be missing, in Turkmenistan and/or Uzbekistan or in the Aral Sea,
        may need some further discussions and decisions, but the procedure is in line with international
        agreements.
    •   Scenarios 3 and 4 would mean that Tajikistan would no longer respect its agreements and obligations
        with the other BVO states, but would act in clear violation of these rules. As shown, such a procedure
        would have serious consequences for the downstream riparian states. Given their consequences these
        scenarios are clearly not acceptable.
    •   Scenario 5 would have the same consequences as Scenarios 3 and 4. However, the situation would be
        further aggravated by the fact that a very large volume of water would be diverted by Afghanistan. It has
        to be stated once again that this has nothing to do whatsoever with Rogun HPP, and that it is an activity
        which also cannot be controlled by ICWC/BVO, since Afghanistan is not a member of these
        organisations. This is clearly the worst case scenario.
    •   Scenarios 3 to 5 describe situations with a degree of impact on downstream water users (Turkmenistan
        and Uzbekistan) that would clearly not be acceptable. It has to be pointed out that the assumed role of
        Tajikistan within these scenarios, namely, rapid filling of the reservoir and additional seasonal shift of
        water, would cause inacceptable negative environmental and socio-economic impacts on the
        downstream area, with no possibility for mitigation, and that for these reasons none of these scenarios
        had ever been considered for implementation. In addition, these would be clear violations of
        ICWC/BVO agreements, while Tajikistan has clearly committed itself to respecting these
        agreements. The contribution of Afghanistan to the situation as outlined in scenario 5 however cannot
        be influenced by ICWC/BVO regulations, since Afghanistan is not a member of these institutions.
As a final conclusion to this the following can be said:
    •   Tajikistan has clearly signalled its intention to develop and operate Rogun HPP while respecting
        international agreements on water allocation. This means that it will be done according to Scenario 2,
        while Scenarios 3 and 4, as well as the part of Tajikistan in Scenario 5, are clearly unacceptable.
    •   The part which Afghanistan plays in Scenario 5 is clearly outside of the influence of the BVO states. The
        fact that Afghanistan is building Qosh Tepa canal demonstrates the intention of the country to develop
        its own irrigation. This indicates once again the necessity to integrate Afghanistan in the Amu Darya
        basin water allocation mechanisms, a necessity that was pointed out already in the 2014 ESIA and was
        supported and emphasised in the Final Report of the E&S POE of 2014.

                                                                                                        PUBLIC
                                                                                                                  Page 74



9.8            Recommendations
From all this it results clearly that the situation of water availability and water use in the Amu Darya basin can
only be seen by including all potential cumulative impacts of present projects and future developments. Many of
the new hydropower projects mentioned and discussed shortly in this reports will be implemented, if ever, only
at some time in the future, certainly none of them before 2037, i.e. before the time envisaged for commissioning
Rogun HPP. The following can be assumed:
      •   Overall water availability and seasonal distribution will change to some extent due to the effects of
          climate change.
      •   Demand for water, especially for irrigation, will increase, e.g. due to the creation of additional irrigation
          schemes in Afghanistan.
      •   Modernising irrigation by applying water saving technology would reduce overall water consumption.
      •   Further development of hydropower will cause an additional water demand for reservoir filling, will create
          additional potential for shifting water from summer to winter, but will also increase the potential for
          providing .additional water in summer in the case of dry years, if managed accordingly.
In this situation, the importance of international agreements regulating water allocation cannot be overstressed.
Presently, and probably still for the immediate future, Protocol 566 is the relevant framework for this, since it is
accepted by the parties and has shown its applicability and usefulness in the past. However, under changing
conditions modifications of this agreement might be required; just a few of such potential modifications are listed
here:
      •   Under changing climatic conditions, storage of water for being able to make use of excess water in
          extremely wet years and to face droughts in exceptionally dry years, both of them situations expected
          to become more severe in the future, may get increasingly important.
      •   It may also be required to add additional storage capacity to the system for increasing its flexibility and
          potential to react.
      •   Such increased storage and water regulation capacity would also require agreements for refilling storage
          after an exceptional drawdown.
      •   International regulating bodies, in this case ICWC/BVO, and their agreements will play an increasingly
          important role.
      •   It will certainly get increasingly important to have all interested parties, i.e. all riparian states, included in
          any agreements concerning the use of water resources.
This last point is of special importance. Presently, ICWC/BVO for the Amu Darya basin includes Kirgizstan,
Tajikistan, Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan. Afghanistan should be included in future agreements on the use of
this important common resource.

Date         Persons met                   Institution, contact            Met by                 Main Topics / Observations
                                                                           Bakhtiyor Begmuradov   •   He will send data on daily operation of Nurek HPP.
                                                                                                  •   Nurek has a total of 9 units. In the process of upgrading, 2 units already
                                                                                                      replaced. Old units have a capacity of 300 MW, the new ones will have
                                                                                                      375 MW per unit.
                                                                                                  •   Water output depending on reservoir level between 150 and 165 m³/s
                                                                                                      per unit.
                                                                                                  •   Nurek presently important for peaking. The new units will react faster
                                                                                                      (can be turned to full capacity in 2 sec.), which will allow for more
                                                                                                      flexibility in operation.
                                                                                                  •   A minimum of 3 units are always working, although not necessarily on
                                                                                                      full capacity.
                                                                                                  •   The entire cascade participates in covering peak demand. In the future,
                                                                                                      Rogun HPP will take over much of this role, but the operation regime of
                                                                                                      the cascade will not change fundamentally.
                                                                                                  •   Nurek will transit to a ROR type of operation, but will still do some
                                                                                                      peaking (taking into account water delivered from Rogun): It will be
                                                                                                      operated at a level of 5 to 10 m below FSL in order to have space for
                                                                                                      flow regulation.
                                                                                                  •   Peaking can become more important in the future when Tajikistan rjoins
                                                                                                      the Central Asian grid (downstream countries having mainly thermal
                                                                                                      power with little potential for peaking).
                                                                                                  •   Filling of Rogun reservoir reduces electricity production in the cascade
                                                                                                      to some extent. This can at least partly be compensated by the two
                                                                                                      units working now already in Rogun HPP.
                                                                                                  •   The entire d/s cascade is being or will be upgraded, and this also
                                                                                                      increases efficiency, so that in the future more electricity will be
                                                                                                      produced.

On this ICWC site, yearly data for water forecast and use are provided, separated in summer (vegetation period, Apr. to Sep.) and winter (non-vegetation period,
Oct. to Mar.). Data for winter 2012-2013 are missing. Before 2010, data were provided in a different format and can therefore not be directly compared with the
data shown here.
